abbreviation.definition: A shortened form, acronym, initialism, ticker-like short code, or compact symbolic label that stands in for a longer name, term, procedure, organism, or concept.
include_rules: Annotate explicit shortened forms such as acronyms and initialisms, e.g., 'MRI' and 'MDRA'.
Annotate compact symbolic labels or short codes used in place of a longer name, including hyphenated forms such as 'NET-B'.
Annotate abbreviations whether they appear alone or after their expanded form in parentheses.
Annotate abbreviations from any domain when they denote a longer underlying expression, including medical, scientific, corporate, or financial contexts.
Annotate abbreviated forms composed of capital letters, letters plus hyphens, or similar compact patterns when they clearly function as a short form in context.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate full expanded names, even when they are the source of an abbreviation, e.g., 'multi-drug-resistant Acinetobacter' is not itself an abbreviation entity.
Do not annotate ordinary words or long-form technical phrases that are not shortened forms, even if they are specialized or repeated in the text.
Do not annotate abbreviations that appear only as explanatory expansions in the guideline examples if the task requires explicit abbreviation spans and none are marked positive in that context.
Do not annotate lowercase common nouns or descriptive labels unless they are clearly being used as an established shortened form or code.
Do not annotate surrounding parentheses, commas, or other punctuation unless they are internal to the abbreviated string.
Do not annotate alphanumeric model names or technical terms solely because they are compact-looking if they are presented as the full item name rather than a shortened substitute.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the abbreviated string itself, not surrounding descriptive words or punctuation.
If a full form is followed by an abbreviation in parentheses, annotate only the parenthetical abbreviated form, e.g., 'Acinetobacter (MDRA)' -> 'MDRA'.
Include internal hyphens, letters, or symbols that are part of the abbreviated form, e.g., 'NET-B'.
Do not extend the span to include determiners, verbs, dates, or modifiers outside the abbreviation, e.g., in 'an MRI on Friday', annotate only 'MRI'.
When an uppercase or shortened token appears as part of a larger noun phrase, select the minimal span that corresponds to the abbreviation itself.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate full names or descriptive technical terms as abbreviation merely because they can be shortened; only annotate the shortened form when it appears explicitly.
Distinguish abbreviation from general domain terminology: long medical or technical expressions such as 'descending thoracic aortic aneurysm' are not abbreviation entities.
Do not annotate a token just because it is capitalized or parenthesized; it must function as a shortened form of a longer name, concept, procedure, organism, or label.
Codes, tickers, or compact labels should be annotated when they are used as abbreviated identifiers, as in 'NET-B'.
If both the expanded form and its short form appear together, annotate only the short form, not the expanded form.

ability.definition: A named or referential capability, move, skill, spell, stance, expression, or other usable/performed ability attributed to a person, character, creature, or system.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of abilities, moves, skills, spells, stances, or similar game/mechanical actions, including proper-name-like forms such as "Batter", "Explode", "Aspect of the Pack", and "Cheeta".
Annotate multiword ability names when all words together form the ability mention.
Annotate non-proper-noun phrases when they denote a capability or expressive faculty being evaluated or used, such as "facial expressions".
Annotate ability mentions whether they appear as something needed, used, removed, judged, or compared in context.
Annotate both unique named abilities and common descriptive ability phrases if they function as a concrete ability mention in the sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic actions, verbs, or capabilities when they are not used as a specific ability mention.
Do not annotate effects, outcomes, attributes, or measurements associated with an ability, such as HP values, damage numbers, or removed conditions.
Do not annotate animals, body parts, substances, products, or life functions unless the phrase itself denotes an ability.
Do not annotate titles, document metadata, template text, markup, or software/package names, as in the negative example with "nvalt" and layout markup.
Do not annotate broad abstract concepts or general roles such as "importance", "protection", "condition of the animals", "milk", or "medicines".
boundary_rules: Annotate the full surface name of the ability as it appears in text, including multiword names such as "Aspect of the Pack" and common-noun ability names such as "facial expressions".
Include internal function words or particles that are part of the fixed ability name, such as "of the" in "Aspect of the Pack".
Do not include surrounding determiners, verbs, or descriptive context unless they are part of the ability name itself.
Do not include trailing punctuation, parenthetical statistics, or effect values; for example, annotate "Explode" but not "Explode (560)".
When an ability name is a single token, annotate only that token, e.g. "Batter" or "Cheeta".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate ability when the span names a capability, move, skill, spell, stance, expression, or other action/resource that can be used, performed, activated, or evaluated.
Do not annotate entities that are people, animals, organizations, products, documents, page titles, or markup unless the text clearly uses the span as the name of an ability.

Distinguish ability names from general outcomes or effects: annotate the named move or capability (e.g. "Explode"), not its result such as damage, HP change, daze, milk, medicines, or sustaining life.

Distinguish ability from generic body parts or objects: annotate "facial expressions" only when it refers to the expressive capability being judged or performed, not when mentioning faces or expressions generically without ability meaning.
Distinguish ability from conditions/statuses: in "Remove the daze from Aspect of the Pack and Cheeta", annotate the named abilities "Aspect of the Pack" and "Cheeta", not the status effect "daze".

acronym.definition: A shortened form made from the initial letters or a compact letter-based abbreviation that stands for a longer name, term, or expression, typically written in uppercase or mixed case and used as the abbreviated reference in the text.
include_rules: Annotate compact abbreviated forms that stand for longer technical, scientific, organizational, or domain-specific expressions.
Annotate uppercase or mixed-case letter sequences used in place of a full term, such as "CSCs", "AP", and "ITS".
Annotate acronym mentions even when the full expansion is not given in the same sentence, as long as the token is clearly used as an abbreviation.
Annotate pluralized acronym forms when the acronym itself carries the plural ending, e.g. "CSCs".
Annotate acronyms across domains, including scientific terminology and technical/computing usage, when they function as abbreviated references.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate shortened personal names, pet names, or clipped name forms such as "Raj" from "Rajendra".
Do not annotate full expanded forms of terms when no abbreviated form is present.
Do not annotate generic uppercase words, technical tokens, or software/product names solely because they are capitalized.
Do not annotate single letters or symbols unless they clearly function as an abbreviation of a longer term in context.
Do not annotate surrounding nouns that describe the referent of the acronym, such as "stem cell origin of renal" around "CSCs".
Do not annotate error messages, commands, or function names unless they are genuinely acronymic abbreviations.
boundary_rules: Mark only the abbreviated letter sequence itself.
Do not include surrounding punctuation, parentheses, possessive markers, or plural words unless the letters themselves contain the pluralized acronym form, e.g. annotate "CSCs" as written.
If an acronym appears inside a longer phrase, select only the acronym token, not the full expanded phrase.
Keep internal capitalization exactly as written; include periods only if they are part of the abbreviation form in the text.
For multi-token contexts, do not extend the span to nearby descriptors such as "renal", "genus", "config", or "info".
confusable_type_rules: Do not label shortened personal names or nicknames as acronym; "Raj" from "Rajendra" is a truncated name, not an acronym.
Do not label full names, expanded technical terms, or descriptive phrases when only the abbreviated form is an acronym.

Do not label programming language names, product names, or database terms merely because they contain capitals; uppercase alone is insufficient unless the token functions as an abbreviation, e.g. "C#" and "SQL Server" are not automatically acronym mentions here.
Distinguish acronym from ordinary short words: an acronym stands for a longer name or term and is used as its abbreviated reference in context.
If a token is a code, version, or identifier rather than an abbreviation of a longer expression, do not annotate it as acronym.

action.definition: A word or phrase that explicitly denotes an action, activity, effort, operation, process, or exerted event, typically expressed by a verb-derived phrase or an abstract noun phrase referring to something being done.
include_rules: Annotate phrases that denote a concrete or abstract action, activity, or process performed, occurring, or exerted in context.
Annotate noun phrases that lexicalize effort or exertion, such as "hard work".
Annotate collective or socially exerted acts expressed as noun phrases, such as "mass public pressure".
Annotate process or operation mentions expressed as verb phrases or verb-like compounds, such as "garbaged collect".
Annotate both physical and abstract actions, including labor, pressure, collection, organizing-type acts, and similar eventive/processual expressions, when explicitly named.
Annotate action expressions even when wording is informal, misspelled, or grammatically imperfect, as long as the intended action is clear.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate substances, objects, systems, tools, diseases, drugs, or other non-action entities, even when they are involved in causing or undergoing actions.

Do not annotate generic scientific or technical descriptions of properties, effects, or functions when they are not used as a specific action mention in context, e.g. repeated mentions of "actions" meaning drug effects.
Do not annotate states, qualities, or characteristics that do not denote something being done.
Do not annotate entire clauses or sentences when only a smaller action phrase is present.
Do not annotate arguments of the action such as agents, targets, recipients, or locations unless they are inseparable parts of the action phrase.
Do not annotate broad procedural descriptions if they are expressed only through dispersed verbs across a sentence rather than a clear action span.
Do not annotate mentions of time, rhythm, duration, publication, or composition in the negative examples unless they are part of a direct action phrase.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the action/event expression itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the action phrase when they directly specify the action, such as intensity, scale, or manner: "mass public pressure", "hard work".
Include verbal forms used as event/action mentions, even if ungrammatical or inflected unusually, when they denote an action occurrence: "garbaged collect".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding auxiliaries, determiners, pronouns, or clausal material unless they are part of the action expression itself.
Do not include purpose, result, target, or location phrases attached to the action, such as objects introduced outside the core action phrase.
When an abstract noun plus modifier functions as the action mention, include the full noun phrase but stop before unrelated complements.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate action when the phrase denotes an activity, process, operation, effort, or exerted act; do not annotate entities that participate in the action, such as people, organizations, substances, or objects.

Do not annotate generic mention of "actions" when it refers to effects, properties, or capabilities of something rather than a concrete action/event mention in context, as in biomedical descriptions of antibacterial actions.
Do not annotate static attributes or evaluations unless they lexicalize an activity/effort itself; "hard work" is included because it denotes labor/effort, not merely a quality.
Do not annotate broad domain topics or systems that enable actions, such as music notation, computer systems, or keyboard input devices, unless the text explicitly names the action/process itself.
Prefer the span that names the action expression over larger event descriptions including agents, patients, and outcomes.

activity.definition: A mention of an action, task, practice, pursuit, or set of actions that someone does or that occurs as an ongoing or repeatable human activity, including everyday tasks, computations, and organized pursuits such as sports.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that explicitly name actions, tasks, or pursuits performed by people or agents.
Include everyday or routine action expressions such as "daily activities".
Include cognitive, computational, or work-task nouns that denote doing something, such as "calculations".
Include organized physical or competitive pursuits such as sports or racing activities, e.g., "automobile racing".
Include plural or collective activity nouns when they refer to a class or set of actions rather than objects.
Include modified activity phrases when the modifiers are part of the intended activity mention, such as duration, frequency, or subtype descriptors.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate entities that are persons, organizations, products, devices, software, platforms, or legal institutions rather than activities.

Do not annotate purely abstract business, legal, or technical nouns when they refer to decisions, support, commitments, plans, configuration items, methods, outputs, or features instead of an activity being carried out.
Do not annotate symptoms, conditions, emotions, or states such as "pain" or "numbness".
Do not annotate isolated verbs unless they function as a nominalized activity mention in context; prioritize noun or gerund phrases that name the activity.

Do not annotate broad contextual text that only mentions an activity incidentally as part of another construction, such as purpose clauses or implementation documentation, unless the activity phrase itself is explicitly present and referential.

Do not annotate menu labels, option names, code/configuration tokens, or interface strings as activities when they are merely labels for system settings rather than descriptions of real-world actions.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the activity, action, task, or organized practice.

Include modifiers that are part of the activity name or needed to specify its type, such as frequency, domain, or object when integral to the phrase: "daily activities", "long calculations", "automobile racing".

Exclude surrounding prepositions, determiners, and purpose markers unless they are inside the established activity phrase; for example, annotate "automobile racing" not "for management and promotion of automobile racing at the track".
Exclude trailing location, time, manner, or causal phrases that describe where, when, or why the activity occurs, unless they are part of a fixed activity name.

When an activity is expressed by a head noun with adjectival modifiers, include the modifiers if they narrow the activity category; e.g., include "daily" in "daily activities" and "long" in "long calculations".
Do not extend the span to include agents, participants, tools, systems, or containers around the activity, such as "users", "screen / tmux sessions", or "the track".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the activity itself, not the people or organizations involved in it. In "management and promotion of automobile racing", only "automobile racing" is the activity, while the managerial acts are outside the annotated span unless independently targeted as activity mentions.

Do not annotate products, software development artifacts, or technical options merely because they imply action. For example, "developing software" in a corporate/legal report and configuration labels like "Encryption method" or "debugging" in menu text are not treated as activity mentions here unless they clearly denote an actual activity being referred to in discourse.
Distinguish activity from states, symptoms, or conditions: in "daily activities have become difficult due to the severe numbness", annotate "daily activities" but not "numbness" or "pain".

Distinguish activity from events or decisions centered on a single occurrence or institutional act. Phrases like "Oracle's decision" or "HP filed its lawsuit" are not activity unless the span specifically names the underlying ongoing practice or task.

Distinguish activity from abstract topics or capabilities. Words such as "development", "support", "configuration", or "debug output" are excluded when they refer to technical settings, business functions, or outputs rather than a concrete activity phrase.

address.definition: An address is a specific expression that identifies a physical street/location address or an address-like target/reference in technical context, such as a named value associated with an address in memory.
include_rules: Annotate explicit street addresses and street-name expressions that identify a specific location, such as numbered street addresses and named streets.
Include abbreviated address components when they are part of the written address, such as directional letters and street-type abbreviations.
Annotate a street name alone when it is used as the specific locational destination or address reference in context.
Annotate address-like references in technical or abstract contexts when the text names the specific addressed item/value rather than merely mentioning the concept of an address.
Annotate the smallest contiguous phrase that uniquely provides the address expression in the sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic mentions of the concept of an address when no specific address expression is given.
Do not annotate city, state, region, or country names by themselves unless they are part of the specific address span to be marked.
Do not annotate names of buildings, businesses, courts, or venues unless the text explicitly gives their address.
Do not annotate legal citations, reporter numbers, case numbers, statute references, dates, or other document identifiers.
Do not annotate standalone numbers that are not functioning as a location/address or address-like reference.
Do not annotate surrounding narrative text that merely describes where something is located without itself being the address.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that directly expresses the address-like referent.

For postal/location addresses, include the full street-address string, including house/building number, directional markers, street name, and street-type abbreviation when present (e.g., "510 N. 12th Ave").
For street names used as locations without a number, include the complete street name only (e.g., "Tower Street").
Do not include surrounding prepositions, verbs, or descriptive context such as "at", "located at", "within", "the closest car park being", or similar framing text.

For abstract or programming-related address mentions, annotate only the token or phrase naming the addressed item or memory location content when that item itself is used as the address expression in context (e.g., "value" in "copy value on one address to another address").
Do not extend the span to include nearby organization names, city names, venue names, case text, or punctuation unless they are part of the address string itself.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish address from person or organization names: annotate the location/address string, not the name of the business or individual associated with it.
Distinguish address from broader place names: when a sentence mentions both a city/area and a specific street or street address, annotate only the specific address expression.
Distinguish address from facilities or landmarks: if a phrase names a car park, chapel, court, or other entity but not the actual address string, do not annotate it as address.

Distinguish address from legal citations, document headers, and bibliographic numbers: numbers or formatted references in case text are not addresses unless they function as a physical/postal or memory address.

Distinguish address from generic uses of the word "address": do not annotate the word "address" itself when it refers only to the concept of an address; annotate the actual addressed value, street, or address expression named in context.
Distinguish physical/postal addresses from ordinary numbers: a number is an address only when combined with address structure or context indicating a location/address role.

adjective.definition: An adjective is a word or hyphenated adjectival modifier that expresses a quality, property, state, or descriptive attribute of a noun or pronoun in context.
include_rules: Annotate words that function as adjectives and directly describe a noun's quality, property, condition, or evaluative characteristic.
Include predicative adjectives that follow linking verbs and describe the subject, such as "attractive".
Include attributive adjectives that appear before a noun and specify a descriptive property, such as "anaerobic" in "anaerobic ... bacterium".
Include hyphenated compound adjectives when the whole compound functions as a single descriptor, such as "self-titled" and "spore-forming".
Annotate each separate adjective in a sequence when multiple adjectives independently modify the same noun.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate nouns, even if they appear before another noun as attributive modifiers, such as institution, location, religion, or category words used nominally.
Do not annotate articles, determiners, pronouns, adverbs, verbs, or participles unless they are functioning as adjectives in context.
Do not annotate full noun phrases headed by a noun; only the adjectival descriptor is in scope.
Do not annotate words that are merely part of proper names, titles, or organization names when they are not being used descriptively.
Do not annotate generic content where no clear adjectival quality word is present, even if the sentence contains many descriptive ideas.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the adjective token or hyphenated adjective phrase itself, not the noun it modifies.
Include internal hyphens when the adjective is written as a compound modifier, e.g. "self-titled" and "spore-forming".
When multiple adjectives are coordinated or listed, annotate each adjective separately if each independently describes the noun, e.g. "anaerobic" and "spore-forming".
Do not include preceding determiners, adverbs, conjunctions, punctuation, or following nouns in the span.
If an adjective appears predicatively after a linking verb, annotate only the adjective word, e.g. "attractive" in "a face attractive"-type contexts.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate nouns used as category labels, institutions, ideologies, religions, or demonyms simply because they can modify another noun; only annotate if the word functions as an adjective in context.
Do not annotate proper names or title words unless the token is clearly an adjectival descriptor rather than part of a named entity.
Distinguish adjectives from nouns denoting people, places, organizations, events, or objects: adjectives describe attributes or qualities, while those other types denote entities.
Do not annotate full descriptive noun phrases when only the adjectival head expresses the property; for example, annotate "spore-forming" but not "spore-forming bacterium".
Words like color or nationality terms should be annotated only when they function as adjectival modifiers or predicates, not when they are part of fixed proper names or standalone nouns.

age.definition: A word or phrase that explicitly states, approximates, or categorizes how old a person or animal is, including numeric age expressions and life-stage terms that inherently denote an age group.
include_rules: Annotate explicit age expressions that state how old a person or animal is, such as numeric or written-out forms with units and age markers: "one and a half year old".
Annotate age-descriptive phrases attached to a person or animal mention when the phrase conveys age, as in "22 year old guy".
Annotate lexical life-stage categories that inherently indicate age group, such as "children".
Include approximate, fractional, or compound age wording when it functions as an age statement, e.g. spelled-out numbers and mixed forms like "one and a half year old".
Annotate both human and animal age mentions when the expression denotes how old the entity is.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate numbers or year-like expressions that refer to calendar years, publication dates, versioning, IDs, or other non-age quantities.
Do not annotate generic temporal expressions, time spans, or frequencies that do not describe an entity's age.
Do not annotate nouns that refer to beings without encoding age, such as "mothers", "dog", or "guy" by themselves.
Do not annotate developmental or relational terms unless they denote an age category in context; for example, a family-role term is not age.
Do not annotate technical text, code fragments, standards, model names, or numeric strings unless they clearly express age.
Do not annotate surrounding context that merely introduces the age expression, such as prepositions, articles, or verbs.
boundary_rules: Include the full age-denoting expression when it appears as a descriptive phrase, including numerals, fractions, units, and age markers: "one and a half year old".

When the age expression is realized as an age-based noun phrase referring to a person, include the whole phrase if the noun is part of the age mention, e.g. "22 year old guy" rather than only "22 year old".
Include plural or collective life-stage nouns when they themselves encode age category, such as "children".
Do not extend the span to determiners, auxiliaries, or surrounding context unless they are part of the age expression itself; for example, exclude "a" in "As a 22 year old guy".
Stop the span at the end of the age descriptor or age-category noun; exclude following predicates, clauses, and appositives.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate calendar dates, years, timestamps, or creation dates such as "2003", "2004", "2012", or "21-giu-2005"; those denote points in time, not age.
Do not annotate durations or lengths of time unless they describe how old an entity is. A bare span like "two years" is not age unless it means "two years old" in context.

Distinguish age from general demographic or participant terms: annotate "children" because it encodes a life stage, but do not annotate neutral human nouns like "mothers", "family", or "consumer" that do not themselves specify age.

Distinguish age from physical size or maturity metaphors unless they genuinely describe age. For example, annotate "puppy" only if the task definition extends to life-stage nouns for animals; otherwise rely on explicit evidence like "one and a half year old dog".
When an age phrase modifies an entity, annotate only the age expression, not the full entity mention unless the head noun is part of the conventional age phrase as in "22 year old guy".

age group.definition: A mention of a human population category defined primarily by age or life stage, such as child, newborn, pediatric, or adult.
include_rules: Annotate words or short phrases that explicitly denote a human age category or life stage.
Include standard age-group terms such as "adult", "children", "newborns", and "pediatric".
Annotate age-group mentions whether they are used as nouns or adjectival descriptors of a population, cohort, or patients, as long as they clearly indicate age category.
Include mentions of neonatal, pediatric, child, adolescent, adult, elderly, or similar age-based categories when explicitly present.
Annotate each distinct age-group mention in coordinated expressions separately when multiple age groups are named in the same phrase.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate person names, occupations, titles, or social roles that are not age categories.
Do not annotate generic references to humans without age information, such as "people", "individuals", "population", or "patients" by themselves.
Do not annotate behavioral, clinical-risk, or exposure-based groups such as "men who have sex with men (MSM)"; these are not age-defined groups.
Do not annotate temporal expressions, durations, ordinals, or frequencies such as "2nd term", "2015", or "3 monthly".
Do not annotate age-related context when the actual age-group term is absent.
Do not annotate broad collective phrases when only part of the phrase expresses age; extract only the age term itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that directly names the age group, usually a single word or short noun phrase: "adult", "children", "newborns", "pediatric".
Include plural or singular morphology when it is part of the age-group term: "child"/"children", "newborn"/"newborns", "adult"/"adults".

When an age descriptor appears inside a longer phrase, include only the age-group word(s), not surrounding generic nouns, unless the full phrase is itself the age-group expression. For example, in "adult and pediatric populations", annotate "adult" and "pediatric", not "adult and pediatric populations".

Do not include non-age modifiers, study descriptors, diseases, procedures, or population nouns such as "population", "patients", "individuals", or "group" unless they are necessary to express the age category itself.
If coordinated age-group mentions appear separately, annotate each mention separately: "adult and pediatric" -> two entities.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate demographic or risk groups defined by sex, behavior, exposure, or profession rather than age, such as "men who have sex with men", "health professional", or "people living with HIV".
Do not annotate generic human collectives like "people", "individuals", "population", or "group" unless an explicit age-group term is present.
Do not confuse age group with developmental or family-role terms unless they are used to denote a patient age category. Annotate "children" or "newborns" as age groups, but not unrelated role nouns.

Do not confuse age group with numeric age expressions unless the expression explicitly identifies an age-based category. The target type is the age-defined category itself, not every mention of time or number.
Do not annotate nationality, political role, race, or other social descriptors such as "President" or "black President"; these are not age groups.

agreement.definition: A word or phrase that denotes a contract, formal arrangement, agreement document, or the state/result of parties being in mutual accord.
include_rules: Annotate nouns and noun phrases that explicitly denote contracts or legally/commercially binding arrangements, such as 'contracts'.
Annotate named or typed agreement documents, such as 'credit card agreement'.
Annotate words that denote mutual accord or concurrence when they function as the agreed state or outcome, such as 'consensus'.
Include both formal legal agreements and less formal agreement states if the expression clearly refers to parties being in agreement.
Annotate agreement mentions regardless of singular/plural form.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general actions of selecting, reviewing, discussing, deciding, or negotiating unless the text explicitly names the resulting agreement or accord.
Do not annotate terms, conditions, rates, obligations, or other provisions mentioned in relation to an agreement when they are not themselves the agreement.
Do not annotate unrelated domain text such as medical findings, software code, HTML/CSS classes, or technical instructions.
Do not annotate generic nouns that are not used with an agreement meaning, even if they appear in formal or transactional contexts.
Do not annotate references to parties, reviewers, cardholders, or organizations involved in an agreement unless the agreement itself is named.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest contiguous span that names the agreement itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the agreement name or type, such as domain qualifiers in 'credit card agreement'.
Include plural or singular head nouns when they denote agreements, e.g. 'contract' or 'contracts'.
For agreement-by-understanding expressions, annotate only the noun naming the agreement state, e.g. 'consensus', not surrounding prepositions or participants such as 'through consensus'.
Do not include determiners, possessives, or surrounding descriptive context unless they are part of the fixed agreement expression.
Do not extend the span to terms, conditions, clauses, prices, durations, or other contents governed by the agreement unless the whole phrase itself is the agreement name.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate 'agreement' only when the span denotes a deal, contract, formal arrangement, or mutual accord/result of agreeing.
Do not annotate products, accounts, cards, services, or institutions; in 'credit card agreement', annotate the agreement document/contract, not 'credit card' by itself.
Do not annotate legal or commercial contents such as 'terms', 'rate per day', or 'applicable terms' unless they are part of the agreement name.
Do not annotate social interaction words that merely describe a process unless they denote the achieved accord itself; 'consensus' is included because it names mutual agreement.

Do not annotate technical, scientific, medical, or programming text that contains no contractual or accord meaning, even if it includes words like 'type', 'class', 'fixed', or other superficially similar terms.

aircraft.definition: A named aircraft entity is a specific aircraft, airplane, helicopter, or other flying vehicle identified by a proper name, manufacturer-plus-model designation, or formal model/variant name.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of specific aircraft models and named flying vehicles.
Annotate manufacturer-plus-model expressions that uniquely identify an aircraft, such as letter-number designations with a maker name.
Annotate specific helicopter names and variants, since helicopters are aircraft.
Annotate aircraft designations that include variant suffixes, nicknames, or NATO-style reporting names when they are part of the full aircraft name.
Annotate standalone model names when they themselves clearly identify a specific aircraft model, even without extra context, if written as a formal designation.
exclude_rules: Exclude generic references to aircraft classes or categories that do not identify a specific named craft, such as "plane", "helicopter", or "fighter" used descriptively.
Exclude manufacturers, aviation companies, or brands when mentioned without a specific aircraft designation.
Exclude names of ships, boats, automobiles, or other non-air vehicles.
Exclude military units, missions, programs, or technologies unless the text explicitly names a specific aircraft.
Exclude titles, headings, or descriptive labels appended to the aircraft name when they are not part of the official designation.
Exclude unrelated capitalized text such as person names, organizations, awards, medical terms, and location names.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full aircraft name as it appears, including manufacturer or brand plus model/designation when they form one unit, e.g., "Caproni CA-60", "Kamov Ka-25 Hormone-A", "Fokker D.VII".
Include alphanumeric model codes, hyphens, periods, and letter-number designators that are part of the official aircraft name.
Include attached variant or nickname text when it is part of the specific aircraft designation, e.g., "Hormone-A" in "Kamov Ka-25 Hormone-A".
Exclude surrounding generic nouns that are not part of the proper name, such as "plane", "helicopter", or "fighter" when they merely describe the type rather than belong to the official name.
Do not extend the span to include determiners, verbs, punctuation, or descriptive context outside the aircraft name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate people, companies, or manufacturers alone unless the text names a specific aircraft model; for example, "Caproni", "Kamov", or "Fokker" by themselves are not aircraft if no model is given.
Do not annotate generic vehicle-type words such as "plane", "helicopter", "aircraft", or "fighter" unless they are part of the named model string.
Distinguish aircraft from non-air vehicles or vessels mentioned nearby; in a sentence containing both a ship and a helicopter, only the helicopter model is aircraft.
Do not confuse aircraft with awards, organizations, diseases, places, or professional designations appearing as capitalized strings in other contexts.
When a name contains both maker and model, annotate the aircraft entity as the whole model name, not as separate maker and code entities.

amino acid.definition: A mention of a specific amino acid, including its occurrence as a named residue or residues in a biomolecular context.
include_rules: Annotate names of specific amino acids when they refer to the biochemical molecule, e.g. "glutamine" and "tryptophan".
Annotate amino acid mentions that appear in residue form within proteins or subunits, e.g. "cysteine residues".
Annotate both singular and plural residue expressions when an explicit amino acid name is present.
Annotate amino acid mentions whether they occur in discussions of concentration, modification, oxidation state, or structural context, as long as the span denotes the amino acid itself.
Annotate common lexical forms that directly name an amino acid, including full names used in scientific prose.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text with no biochemical meaning, even if it contains letters or symbols that could match amino-acid abbreviations in another context.
Do not annotate proteins, enzymes, subunits, genes, or other macromolecules that are not themselves amino acids.
Do not annotate generic biochemical concepts without a specific amino acid name, such as "residues" alone, "level" alone, or "oxidized form" alone.
Do not annotate modifications, processes, or events by themselves when the amino acid is not part of the span.
Do not annotate quantities, times, percentages, or experimental context surrounding an amino acid mention.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the amino acid mention.
Include the amino acid name itself, whether used alone or as part of a residue phrase, e.g. include "glutamine", "tryptophan", and "cysteine residues".
Include immediately attached residue terms when they are part of the mention, such as "residue" or "residues".
Do not extend the span to surrounding modifiers that describe amount, state, time, position, or structure, such as "level", "oxidized form", "alpha-subunit", or measurement/time expressions.
Do not include determiners, verbs, or prepositions unless they are inside the fixed amino-acid expression.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate amino acids themselves, not proteins or larger biomolecules; for example, in "aequorin bioluminescence induced by tryptophan modification", annotate "tryptophan" but not the protein name.
Annotate residue mentions when the residue is explicitly an amino acid, e.g. "cysteine residues"; do not annotate broader structural components such as "alpha-subunit".
Do not annotate generic chemical or biochemical state words unless the amino acid name is present; "oxidized form" alone is not an amino acid mention.

Do not annotate non-biological words that happen to resemble symbols or abbreviations used elsewhere; mathematical variables or ordinary names are not amino acids unless the text clearly refers to the biochemical substance.

anatomical entity.definition: A biological body structure or structural component of an organism, including organs, tissues, nerves, vessels, tracts, columns, afferent structures, and other physically identifiable anatomical parts.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that refer to concrete anatomical structures or structural components of a body.
Include named nerves, vessels, tracts, columns, and similar parts of the nervous or circulatory system, e.g. "sciatic nerves" and "infarcted vessel".
Include anatomical microstructures or specialized structural components when they are presented as body parts, e.g. "lagenar afferents" and "octavus cell column".
Include mentions with pathological or descriptive modifiers when the full phrase still denotes an anatomical structure, e.g. "infarcted vessel".
Include singular or plural mentions of anatomical entities whenever they refer to actual body structures in biological or medical context.
Include both common anatomical names and specialized scientific anatomical terminology.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-biological text, metaphorical uses, or terms from unrelated domains such as software, typography, or art.
Do not annotate diseases, syndromes, injuries, or pathological conditions, even when they occur near anatomical mentions, e.g. "myocardial infarction".
Do not annotate medical procedures, treatments, interventions, or experimental actions.
Do not annotate generic organism references, patient groups, or species names unless the text span itself names an anatomical structure.
Do not annotate purely functional, behavioral, or process terms that do not denote a physical structure.
Do not annotate isolated descriptive adjectives if the full anatomical noun phrase is absent or if the adjective alone does not name a structure.
boundary_rules: Include the full noun phrase that names the anatomical structure, including necessary modifiers that identify the structure, such as tissue state or subtype, as in "infarcted vessel" and "octavus cell column".
Include plural and singular forms when they are part of the anatomical mention, e.g. "sciatic nerves".
Include anatomical adjectives or derived forms only when they are integral to the structure name, not when they merely describe location in a broader event or process.

Do not include surrounding determiners, numerals, species names, or experimental context unless they are part of the anatomical name itself, e.g. annotate "sciatic nerves" not "the sciatic nerves of 46 adult dogs".
Do not include verbs, prepositions, or action phrases adjacent to the anatomical term, e.g. annotate only the structure mention in phrases like "terminated throughout the octavus cell column".
When a structure is expressed as a head noun plus a biological modifier, include both words if together they denote the anatomical entity, e.g. "lagenar afferents".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate physical body structures, tissues, nerves, vessels, columns, and afferent structures as anatomical entity; do not annotate diseases, injuries, or pathological events such as "myocardial infarction".
Do not confuse anatomical entity with medical procedures or interventions; in "acute interventions in myocardial infarction", only "infarcted vessel" is anatomical.

Cellular or functional groups are included only when they denote a recognized anatomical structure or structural component, as in "octavus cell column"; do not annotate abstract biological processes or functions.
Anatomical descriptors embedded in non-anatomical expressions should not be annotated separately unless they directly name a structure.
Do not annotate organisms or populations containing anatomy, such as "adult dogs"; annotate only the body part term within the larger phrase if present.
Do not annotate technical, programming, artistic, or other non-biological uses of words that may resemble entity names; anatomical entity is restricted to biological body structures.

anatomical structure.definition: A physical part of an organism's body, including organs, tissues, body regions, vessels, teeth, and named subparts or components of those structures.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of body parts and structural components of an organism.
Annotate named teeth and dental structures, including full descriptive tooth names with location and order, e.g. "upper left first molar" and "maxillary second molar."
Annotate anatomical subparts and components, including plural mentions, e.g. "buccal roots."
Annotate blood vessels and vessel parts when they are referred to as physical structures, e.g. "parent artery."
Annotate anatomical mentions whether they refer to whole structures or substructures, as long as the text denotes a concrete bodily component.
Include modifiers that are necessary to identify the exact anatomical structure, such as side, position, region, and ordinal number.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-biological objects, software terms, mathematical symbols, astronomy terms, or other technical expressions even if they contain words that can appear in anatomy contexts.
Do not annotate procedures, therapies, manipulations, or actions involving anatomy, such as clipping, preservation, or endodontic therapy.
Do not annotate diseases, lesions, or pathological conditions unless the mention clearly denotes the underlying body structure rather than the disorder itself.
Do not annotate generic spatial words or adjectives by themselves, such as "upper left" or "maxillary," when no anatomical head noun is present.
Do not annotate chemicals, biomaterials, devices, or medications such as "MTA."
Do not annotate abstract scientific variables or geometry terms like "beaming angle," "jet opening angle," or similar non-anatomical uses of "angle."
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase that names the anatomical structure, including laterality, positional, ordinal, and regional modifiers when they are part of the structure name: "upper left first molar", "maxillary second molar".
Include internal anatomical qualifiers that specify subtype or location of the structure, such as "parent" in "parent artery" and "buccal" in "buccal roots".

Do not include surrounding procedure, condition, or tissue-state words outside the structure span, e.g. exclude "Pulp of the" and "was exposed and capped with MTA" when annotating "upper left first molar".
When an anatomical term appears inside a larger prepositional phrase, annotate only the structure itself unless the full phrase is the accepted anatomical mention.
Use the smallest complete span that uniquely identifies the body part or anatomical component; do not extend into determiners, verbs, or unrelated descriptors.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate body parts, organs, teeth, vessels, roots, and structural subparts as anatomical structure; do not annotate procedures or treatments performed on them.

Distinguish anatomical structure from diseases, injuries, or abnormalities: in phrases like "giant intracranial aneurysms," the abnormality term is not automatically the same as a normal anatomical structure, whereas "parent artery" is a body structure.

Distinguish anatomical structure from directional or dental descriptors alone: words like "upper left," "maxillary," or "buccal" are included only when attached to a named structure, not by themselves.

Distinguish anatomical structure from biological substances or materials such as "pulp" when the annotation target in context is another structure like a tooth; annotate only the structure explicitly serving as the anatomical referent.

Do not annotate technical, physical, astronomical, software, or geometric uses of words that may resemble anatomy terms, such as "jet angle" or code identifiers, because they do not denote parts of an organism.

anatomy.definition: Anatomical structures and body-related tissues, organs, regions, bones, joints, vessels, nerves, and other physical parts of an organism.
include_rules: Annotate named body structures and tissues such as organs, bones, joints, muscles, vessels, nerves, connective tissues, and related anatomical parts.
Annotate anatomical regions and subregions when they identify a specific body area, including regional modifiers like side or location.
Annotate compound anatomical terms that denote a single structure or tissue type.
Annotate anatomical names that appear in medical, scientific, or pathological contexts when they still refer to the body structure itself.
Annotate the full anatomical phrase even when it is modified by descriptive adjectives that are part of the structure name.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate symptoms, diseases, disorders, injuries, or pathological findings unless the anatomical structure is explicitly named as part of the phrase.
Do not annotate medical procedures, surgical operations, diagnostic tests, or treatment methods.
Do not annotate anatomical locations when they are used only as general spatial descriptors and do not name a body structure.
Do not annotate organs or tissues only when they are mentioned as part of a non-anatomical device, instrument, or metaphorical phrase.
Do not annotate broad scientific or biological terms that are not specific body structures or tissues.
boundary_rules: Include the full anatomical phrase from the first anatomical term to the last anatomical term in the expression.

Include modifiers that are intrinsic to the anatomy and change its anatomical identity, such as location, side, region, tissue layer, or anatomical subtype (e.g., 'lumbar' in 'lumbar spine', 'cervical' in 'cervical discs').
Include compound anatomical names as one span when they function as a single body structure (e.g., 'Collagen fiber', 'connective tissue').
Do not include surrounding disease, procedure, experimental, or descriptive words unless they are part of the anatomical name itself.
Do not split a single anatomical term into separate spans when the words together name one structure.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse anatomy with procedures, interventions, or treatments; only the body structure itself is annotated, not actions performed on it.
Do not confuse anatomy with diseases, injuries, or pathological conditions; if a term names a condition affecting a body part, annotate only the body part portion if present.
Do not confuse anatomy with general biological material or laboratory content unless it is a named tissue, organ, structure, or body-related anatomical component.
Do not annotate generic nouns that refer to non-anatomical objects or devices even if they are mentioned near body-related text.
When an expression could be read as both anatomical and non-anatomical, annotate it only if it clearly refers to a body structure or tissue.

animal.definition: Names of non-human animals or animal species mentioned as organisms, experimental subjects, or biological sources in text.
include_rules: Annotate common names of non-human animals and animal species mentioned in the text.
Annotate animal mentions when they identify the organism used in an experiment, study, or observation, such as laboratory model animals.
Annotate singular and plural animal forms, for example both "rat" and "mice."
Annotate animal names appearing inside larger noun phrases when the animal term itself directly denotes the organism, such as "rabbit" in "rabbit eye."
Annotate animal mentions regardless of whether they appear with genetic, physiological, or experimental modifiers, as long as the span denotes the animal itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-animal biological entities such as proteins, receptors, globulins, antibodies, complement factors, or other molecular substances.
Do not annotate body parts, organs, tissues, or cells, even when preceded by an animal name.
Do not annotate humans or generic subject terms such as "patients" unless they are explicitly animal names.
Do not annotate geographic names, landmarks, or other locations that contain misleading words, such as glacier names.
Do not annotate diseases, procedures, experimental measurements, or physiological processes.
Do not annotate broad non-specific biological words like "organism," "animal," or "species" when they are used generically rather than naming a specific animal kind.
boundary_rules: Mark only the token(s) naming the animal itself, usually the common species name such as "rat," "rabbit," or "mice."
Do not include surrounding determiners, adjectives, or experimental descriptors unless they are part of the animal name itself; for example, in "receptor-deficient mice," annotate only "mice."
In anatomical phrases, annotate the animal word but exclude body-part terms; for example, in "rabbit eye," annotate only "rabbit," not "rabbit eye."
For multiword animal names, include the full contiguous animal name if all words are needed to identify the animal species or kind.
Do not extend the span to include disease names, proteins, procedures, locations, or other non-animal context words adjacent to the animal mention.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate animals only when the text denotes a living animal or animal species used as a subject, model, or organism; do not annotate proteins, globulins, receptors, or other biomolecules even if their names contain letters or terms that might resemble biological nomenclature.
Do not annotate anatomical structures or tissues as animal; in phrases like "rabbit eye," only the organism name is animal, while "eye" is a body part.
Do not annotate places or geographic names that contain strings identical to other biological terms; for example, "Mount Warlow Glacier" and "Mount Fiske Glacier" are locations, not animals.

Do not annotate human patient mentions such as "patients" as animal; humans described as clinical subjects are outside this entity type unless explicitly treated as animals in the annotation scheme, which is not supported by the examples.

When an adjective or modifier describes an animal model property, strain feature, or experimental condition, annotate only the animal term, not the full modified noun phrase unless the modifier is inherently part of the animal's name.

antibody.definition: Mentions of antibodies, including specific antibody molecules, antibody classes or subclasses, monoclonal/polyclonal antibodies, and auto-antibody expressions referring to immunoglobulin-based binding agents or categories.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions containing the word "antibody" or "antibodies", including modified forms such as "auto-antibodies".
Annotate named antibody types or immunoglobulin designations when they refer to an antibody or antibody category, such as "IgE antibody".
Annotate antibody class or subclass mentions when the text explicitly identifies them as antibody categories, e.g. "IgA class" and "subclass" in antibody context.
Annotate descriptive antibody phrases that include functional or production modifiers, such as "neutralizing monoclonal antibodies".
Annotate singular and plural antibody mentions equally when they denote antibody entities rather than broader processes.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate person names, places, bibliographic text, or any non-biomedical content.
Do not annotate drugs, therapeutic regimens, supplementation agents, or chemicals that are not antibodies.
Do not annotate viruses, cell types, cytokines, proteins, or biomarkers unless the expression explicitly denotes an antibody.
Do not annotate diseases, syndromes, or patient groups even when antibodies are discussed in relation to them.

Do not annotate generic immunological processes or outcomes such as "immune response", "antibody responses", or "reactivity" as antibody entities unless there is a distinct antibody term within the phrase.
Do not annotate assay actions or experimental terms such as "determination", "neutralizing" alone, or "subtype classes" unless they are part of a full antibody mention.
boundary_rules: Include the full antibody mention, including specificity or functional/class modifiers that are part of the noun phrase, e.g. "IgE antibody" and "neutralizing monoclonal antibodies".
When the text names antibody classes or subclasses directly, annotate the exact class/subclass term mentioned, e.g. "IgA class" and "subclass".
Include adjectives such as "monoclonal", "polyclonal", "neutralizing", or "auto-" when they modify the antibody mention and are part of the same expression.
Do not extend the span to surrounding disease names, organisms, procedures, or prepositional phrases, e.g. exclude "in Graves' disease and Hashimoto's thyroiditis" from antibody spans.
Do not include determiners, verbs, or broader experimental descriptions outside the antibody noun phrase.
If "antibody" appears as part of a coordinated or plural phrase, annotate only the antibody expression itself, not adjacent non-antibody items.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate proteins, cytokines, receptors, viruses, cells, or other biological entities unless the mention itself denotes an antibody; for example, "TNF-alpha", "IL-8", "HIV-1", and "CD4+ lymphocytes" are not antibody entities.

Do not annotate diseases or conditions associated with antibodies; in "thyroid auto-antibodies in Graves' disease and Hashimoto's thyroiditis", only the antibody mention is annotated, not the diseases.
Distinguish antibody entities from treatments or drugs: compounds such as "N-acetyl-L-cysteine" and treatment phrases such as "anti-retroviral therapy" are not antibodies.

Distinguish antibody entities from general immune responses or measurements: annotate "IgE antibody" but not broader phrases like "IgE antibody responses" unless the antibody term itself is separately spanable.

When a term denotes an antibody category or isotype, annotate it as antibody if it is explicitly framed as a class/subclass or antibody mention, as in "IgA class"; do not annotate isolated laboratory context words that do not denote an antibody.

apparatus.definition: A physical device, machine, or equipment unit named as an apparatus, including specific apparatus types and descriptive apparatus names.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of apparatus as a device or machine, including phrases where 'apparatus' is the head noun.
Annotate specific apparatus names that include descriptive modifiers before 'apparatus'.
Annotate standalone device names that are clearly used as apparatus terms in the text, such as 'ionizer' in a device-description context.
Annotate apparatus mentions whether they appear in patent-style, technical, or descriptive prose, as long as they refer to a physical device/system.
Annotate the full device name when the phrase identifies a particular apparatus subtype or functional apparatus.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate mentions of research studies, clinical trials, therapies, medications, or dosages.
Do not annotate software, code, network exceptions, classes, or other non-physical technical terms.
Do not annotate general-purpose nouns that are not device names, even if they appear in technical or product contexts.
Do not annotate only the use, purpose, or function of a device when the device itself is not explicitly named as an apparatus.
Do not annotate plural or collective references to devices unless the exact span is a named apparatus expression.
boundary_rules: Include the full noun phrase naming the apparatus, including any descriptive modifiers that specify its function, material, method, or subtype (e.g., 'lithographic apparatus', 'warm air supplying apparatus').
Include the head noun 'apparatus' when it is part of the device name; do not trim modifiers that are integral to the device identity.
Exclude surrounding determiners, prepositions, and discourse framing words such as 'a', 'the', 'such', 'according to', or 'has been provided'.
If multiple coordinated apparatus names appear, annotate each distinct apparatus expression separately rather than one combined span.
Do not extend the span to include actions, locations, or uses that are merely described around the apparatus.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse apparatus with a process, method, study, treatment, or regimen; only annotate the physical device/system being named.
Do not annotate generic nouns that merely refer to an item or object unless the phrase explicitly denotes an apparatus as a device or machine.
Do not include functional descriptions that are not part of the name; keep only the expression that identifies the apparatus itself.

If a phrase names what the apparatus does (e.g., 'ionizer', 'warm air supplying apparatus'), annotate it only when it is clearly used as the device name, not when it is just a verbal description of an action or result.
Prefer the smallest complete device-name span over broader context that mentions usage, manufacture, or application.

appliance.definition: A machine or powered device used in household, kitchen, lodging, or commercial settings to perform a practical function such as cooking, brewing, cooling, freezing, or similar everyday utility tasks.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote a household or commercial appliance device.
Include kitchen and food-preparation appliances such as ovens and coffeemakers.
Include cooling or preservation appliances such as freezers.
Include specific appliance subtypes when the modifier is part of the device name, such as 'deck oven'.
Annotate appliance mentions whether singular or plural and whether they appear in domestic, hotel, or restaurant contexts.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate locations, accommodations, or spaces where appliances may be found, such as 'apartment'.
Do not annotate tools, parts, containers, or mechanical components unless the span names a complete appliance.
Do not annotate vehicles, bicycles, tires, valves, tubes, or other transport-related objects.
Do not annotate generic activities, maintenance actions, or usage verbs such as 'cooking', 'fix', or 'change'.
Do not annotate food names or other non-device nouns that appear near an appliance mention.
Do not annotate very broad environmental nouns or furnishings unless they clearly denote an appliance device.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the appliance, including modifiers that are part of the appliance type, such as materialized subtype words in 'deck oven'.
Include adjectival or compound modifiers only when they help specify the appliance itself; for example, include 'deck' in 'deck oven'.
Do not include surrounding verbs, determiners, quantities, or prepositions, such as 'the', 'a', 'uses', or 'in the'.
For plural mentions, annotate the full plural appliance noun, e.g. 'coffeemakers'.
If the appliance is mentioned as part of a longer coordination, annotate only the appliance term itself, not the entire list.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate appliances, not buildings or rooms: 'apartment' is a place to live, not an appliance.
Annotate appliances, not vehicles or vehicle parts: 'bike', 'tire', 'valve', and 'tube' are transportation objects or components, not appliances.

Annotate appliances, not electronics unless they function as household or commercial devices of the appliance kind; use the provided examples as the anchor cases such as 'coffeemakers', 'freezer', and 'oven'.
Annotate the device itself, not the food, output, or process associated with it; for example, 'pizza' is not an appliance, but 'pizza oven' would be if it names an oven type.
Annotate standalone appliance mentions even when used in hospitality or kitchen contexts; do not require ownership or active use language.

application.definition: A software application: a named or specifically referenced program, app, or software product used by people or systems to perform tasks.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of software programs or apps when the text refers to them as an application, app, or software product.
Annotate both proper application names and specific descriptive application mentions, such as product-like names or noun phrases headed by "app" or "application".
Annotate application mentions in contexts of use, execution, integration, installation, or functionality, e.g. being run, integrated, or used for a task.
Include branded or community-specific apps when they clearly denote a software app, e.g. "community app".
Include a specific application name even when followed by a generic descriptor, e.g. "SketchPad" in "SketchPad application".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic mentions of software with no specific application reference, such as bare "application", "app", "program", or "tool" when used only descriptively.
Do not annotate file names, data files, import/export content, code snippets, library functions, APIs, or commands unless they denote an application itself.
Do not annotate hardware products, appliances, part numbers, manuals, or repair instructions.
Do not annotate organizations, communities, or websites unless the mention clearly refers to the software app rather than the broader group or service.
Do not annotate operating systems, programming languages, or development frameworks unless the full phrase denotes an application mention.
Do not annotate incidental technical text where no software product is being named or specifically referenced.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the software product or application mention.
If a generic software head noun is preceded by a specific identifier, include the full phrase, e.g. "Java application" and "community app".
If the application is named and followed by a generic descriptor, annotate only the name when the descriptor is purely explanatory, e.g. annotate "SketchPad" in "SketchPad application".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding verbs, determiners, prepositions, or usage context such as "running it as a" or "integrated into the".
Do not include platform, vendor, or ecosystem words unless they are part of the application mention itself in context.
For shortened forms like "app", include them only when they are part of the application mention, not when they refer generically to software.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate programming languages, platforms, or technical ecosystems by themselves; annotate only when the text refers to an actual software application, as in "Java application" rather than standalone "Java".
Do not annotate generic technical actions, file import operations, or function names such as "read.csv" or "fread"; these are commands or methods, not applications.
Do not annotate physical products, appliances, parts, or model numbers such as dryer parts or repair items; the application type is limited to software.
Do not annotate websites, communities, or tools unless they are explicitly referred to as an app/application and function as software in the sentence.
When a phrase could denote either a software category or a specific app, annotate only if the context indicates a software program being used, run, integrated, or referred to as an app/application.

art.definition: Artistic works, visual representations, decorative designs, or named categories/styles of art, expressed as common noun phrases rather than creator names or container objects.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote a visual or artistic object, such as an "image".
Annotate phrases that denote an art form, art category, or stylistic/period label, such as "contemporary art".
Annotate decorative or commemorative visual creations expressed as common noun phrases, such as "commemorative designs".
Include both singular and plural mentions when they refer to artistic works or design objects.
Annotate common-noun mentions of art even when embedded inside larger collection or usage contexts, as long as the phrase itself denotes the art object or category.
Annotate broad art-category mentions when the context clearly refers to artistic content rather than metaphorical or technical usage.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate scientific, medical, or technical terms, even if they contain words that can occur in artistic contexts, such as "design", "image", or "abstract".
Do not annotate methods, models, architectures, datasets, measurements, or article metadata.
Do not annotate physical containers, media formats, or pages unless the span itself denotes the artwork rather than the container.
Do not annotate generic references to objects, items, or collections unless the phrase explicitly identifies them as art or an artistic representation.
Do not annotate actions of creating, placing, using, collecting, or exhibiting art; only annotate the art object/category itself.
Do not annotate disease manifestations, radiological findings, or biological effects; these are non-art meanings despite surface similarity to visual representation terms.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the artistic object, visual item, or art category.
Include modifiers that are part of the artwork/category name or type, such as style, period, or functional descriptors: "contemporary art", "commemorative designs".
Include the head noun that signals the artistic object, such as "image", "art", or "designs".
Exclude surrounding determiners, pronouns, and quantifiers unless they are part of a fixed title or name: annotate "image", not "this image".
Exclude verbs and event wording around creation or use, such as "place", "making", or "used for".
When a larger phrase contains both a non-art object and an art noun, select only the art-denoting portion.
Do not extend the span to include explanatory context like "for class" or collection descriptions like "items of"; annotate "contemporary art", not "items of modern and contemporary art".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate art when the phrase denotes an artistic work, visual representation, decorative design, or an art category; do not annotate when the same wording refers to a scientific, medical, or technical concept.
Do not confuse art with documents or publications as containers; in "magazine page", the annotated entity is "image", not the page or magazine.

Do not annotate abstract uses of words like "design", "image", or "abstract" when they refer to method structure, data representation, article sections, or general technical description rather than artistic content.

Do not treat descriptive adjectives such as "antiarrhythmic", "radiological", or "self-normalizing" as art-related merely because they modify a noun; only annotate if the full phrase denotes an artistic object or category.
When a phrase names a collection or set that contains art, annotate only the art-denoting expression within it, not the collection entity itself.

artifact.definition: A tangible, produced, or object-like item, including man-made creations, technical/image artifacts, and named discovered physical remains or finds, when mentioned as a discrete entity.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases referring to concrete created items or object-like entities.
Annotate software or code artifacts mentioned as discrete items, such as "script".
Annotate technical or imaging artifact types when explicitly mentioned as entities, such as "unfolded artifacts" and "motion artifacts".
Annotate named archaeological, anthropological, or discovered physical finds when the mention refers to the find as an identified object/entity, such as "Lapedo child".
Annotate both common noun artifact mentions and proper-name artifact mentions, provided they refer to a specific object/entity in context.
Annotate plural artifact mentions when the span refers to a class or set of artifact entities explicitly mentioned in the text.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate abstract concepts, properties, or processes such as "mixing", "ergodicity", or "automorphisms".
Do not annotate organizations, places, addresses, or institutions, even when they appear in metadata or publication text.
Do not annotate communication channels or infrastructure terms such as "emails", "internet", or "on-line".
Do not annotate generic mentions of groups, sets, branches, or measures unless they denote a concrete artifact item.
Do not annotate full descriptive contexts around an artifact mention when only a smaller core noun phrase denotes the artifact.
Do not annotate verbs or event expressions related to creation, discovery, use, or movement of artifacts, such as "writing" or "push code".
Do not annotate document metadata, titles, section headings, or publication-related text unless the mention itself is a concrete artifact entity.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the artifact, object, or created item.

Include modifiers that are part of the artifact mention and help identify the specific item, such as descriptive adjectives in common noun mentions (e.g., "motion artifacts", "unfolded artifacts") or integral name parts in titled/named objects (e.g., "Lapedo child").
For software/code items, include the core artifact noun such as "script"; do not extend the span to surrounding function words or purpose clauses unless they are part of the artifact name.

Exclude determiners, numerals, ages, and other contextual descriptors that describe the artifact externally rather than naming it (e.g., exclude "The" and "A 24 000-year-old early modern human child burial known as" from "The Lapedo child").
When a common noun is modified by a quoted label that refers to something else in context, annotate only the artifact noun if the label is not part of the artifact mention itself.
Do not include surrounding punctuation or coordination unless each coordinated artifact is part of a single continuous mention; separate distinct artifact mentions when listed individually.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate man-made, produced, or object-like entities and named physical remains/items as artifact, not abstract processes or events.
Use artifact for created digital objects such as a "script", but not for actions performed with them, such as writing, pushing, or mixing.

Use artifact for image defects or technical artifacts when the term refers to a concrete type of artifact in the data or image (e.g., "motion artifacts"), not for generic metaphorical uses of "artifact" if no object/entity is being referred to.

Named discovered remains or burial-associated objects such as "Lapedo child" are artifact when treated as a named archaeological/paleoanthropological find, rather than as a living person in discourse.

Do not annotate academic works, document sections, or mathematical concepts unless they are clearly treated as concrete artifacts by mention form; titles and technical abstractions in the negative examples are not artifact mentions here.
Do not confuse artifact with locations, organizations, addresses, or communication media; for example, universities, cities, email, and internet access are not artifact mentions in this type.

aspect.definition: An aspect is a specific feature, dimension, component, or topical area of a broader subject, entity, activity, or experience that is being discussed, evaluated, managed, or described.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote a specific feature, dimension, component, or area of a broader entity or topic.
Annotate topical domains when they are explicitly referred to as an aspect of something, as in constructions like "aspect of X" or "every aspect of X."
Annotate abstract operational or qualitative dimensions when they are the focus of evaluation or necessity, such as "management" in "Proper management of SROs is essential."
Annotate linguistic, technical, artistic, or subject-matter components when they are identified as one of multiple aspects, such as "prosody" in "three aspects of prosody."
Include both concrete and abstract aspect terms, provided they refer to a delimited facet of a larger whole rather than the whole situation itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate entire subjects, entities, or scenarios when the text does not single out a particular feature or dimension of them.
Do not annotate generic trigger nouns such as "aspect," "part," "feature," "success," or "improvement" when they only indicate that some aspect exists but do not themselves name the aspect.

Do not annotate events, actions, or relations unless they function as the specific aspect under discussion; for example, annotate "management" when it is the focal dimension, but not auxiliary verbs or full action phrases around it.

Do not annotate scientific measurements, doses, treatments, anatomical terms, abnormalities, or findings just because they are being studied; they are not aspects unless explicitly framed as a feature/component of a larger subject.

Do not annotate narrative topics or plot details that are merely content of a story or opinion text, such as moving to a new town or liking someone, unless they are explicitly identified as an aspect or dimension.
Do not annotate highly general nouns with no clear referent to a broader subject, such as standalone mentions of "thing," "issue," or "part," when they do not specify an aspect.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the noun or noun phrase that names the aspect/theme itself, not the surrounding trigger words such as "aspect of", "management of", "improvement in", or similar relational language.
When the aspect is expressed as the object of a construction like "aspect of X," annotate X if X is the topical dimension being referred to (for example, "prosody"), not the word "aspect."
Include modifiers that are part of the aspect name when they specify the topical domain, such as adjectives within the phrase (for example, "classical music").
Use the shortest span that still identifies the specific aspect; do not extend the span to include determiners, punctuation, or full clauses unless they are part of the aspect phrase itself.
If an abstract process noun is itself the named aspect under discussion, annotate that noun alone (for example, "management").
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate broad discourse topics unless they are explicitly presented as an aspect, dimension, component, or area of something. A subject mentioned only as story content or general discussion is not necessarily an aspect.

Do not confuse the aspect with the whole entity being evaluated or studied. Annotate the particular feature, domain, or component under focus, not the larger object unless the larger object itself is explicitly framed as the aspect (for example, "classical music" in "every aspect of classical music").

Do not annotate outcome statements, effects, or findings as aspects. In scientific text, items like abnormalities, lesions, or damage are results or conditions unless the sentence explicitly marks them as dimensions/features under consideration.
Do not annotate people, places, works, substances, or organizations merely because they appear near evaluative or descriptive language; only annotate the feature/topic being singled out as an aspect.

Words like "aspect," "part," "feature," or "element" are usually triggers introducing the aspect, not the aspect span themselves, unless the text truly refers to the generic concept rather than the specific content.

athlete.definition: A person or group of people identified as participants in sports or athletic competition or training, including named individual sports players and generic references to such participants.
