include_rules: Annotate full names of individual people when they are referred to as sports participants or players.
Annotate common nouns or noun phrases that refer to athletes as a class or group, such as sport participants in a study or players in a competition.
Annotate athlete mentions whether they refer to professional, amateur, or study-participant athletes, as long as the context indicates athletic or sport participation.
Annotate plural and singular mentions that denote sport participants, including role-neutral terms like "player"/"players" and sport-specific participant terms like "diver"/"divers".
Annotate athlete mentions even when they appear with surrounding descriptive context such as performance, injury, roster moves, or game participation.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-human entities, including software, files, XML content, devices, or technical commands.
Do not annotate organizations or collectives as entities when the mention is the team or institution rather than the people as athletes.
Do not annotate sport positions, job titles, or roles alone unless the mention itself refers to the athlete as a person or athlete group.
Do not annotate generic people mentions that lack athletic meaning in context.
Do not annotate statistics, counts, dates, seasons, injuries, claims, or event descriptions unless the athlete mention itself is present.
Do not annotate adjectives or modifiers that describe athletes if they do not themselves denote the athlete mention.
boundary_rules: Include the minimal span that denotes the athlete: typically the full personal name (e.g., "Thomas Gafford") or the athlete-denoting noun phrase headword (e.g., "divers", "players").

Do not include surrounding appositives, roster roles, or descriptive modifiers unless they are part of the athlete-denoting expression itself; for example, exclude "long-snapper" from "long-snapper Thomas Gafford".
For named individuals, include first and last name together when both are present; exclude surrounding punctuation and relative clauses.

For generic athlete groups, annotate only the noun or noun phrase that refers to the participants in sport or athletic activity, not the quantities or statistics attached to them; for example, exclude numerals in "10,634 players".
Do not extend the span to include team names, seasons, game counts, medical claims, or other event/context information surrounding the athlete mention.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate athlete when the mention refers to a person or group of people in the role of sport participant; do not annotate organizations such as teams, leagues, or clubs.

Annotate athlete names and athlete-denoting group nouns, but not sports, positions, or activities by themselves; for example, a position label like "long-snapper" alone is not an athlete mention unless attached to a person mention, and even then only the person name is tagged.
Do not confuse athlete with general human mentions: only tag nouns like "players" or "divers" when the context indicates athletic/sport participants rather than unrelated people.
Do not annotate equipment, software, technical text, XML tags, or other non-human strings even if they contain words that could appear elsewhere in athlete contexts.
When a mention refers to a role in competition or physical sport participation, it is athlete; when it refers to a non-sport profession, organization member, or abstract category, it is not athlete.

attribute.definition: A named or describable property, parameter, indicator, target, or characteristic of an entity, system, or domain that is referred to as a feature/field/measure rather than as the entity itself or a concrete value.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote a property, parameter, field, metric, indicator, target, limit, or characteristic of something.
Include technical property names used in software, markup, or data-schema contexts, such as field/property labels like "class".
Include domain-specific research or analytical attribute terms that name meaningful characteristics or evaluative measures, such as "prognostic indicators".
Include target-like attribute mentions when they denote a sought or defined characteristic/object of intervention rather than a concrete entity, such as "therapeutic targets".
Include system or configuration characteristics when referred to as a named limit or setting in prose, such as "load order maximum".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate concrete values, settings, or contents assigned to an attribute, such as class values, version numbers, names, paths, booleans, or feature list items.

Do not annotate generic or underspecified mentions of factors, variables, features, issues, or characteristics when they are not used as a specific attribute name, e.g. "different factors", "demographic factors", "variables studied".
Do not annotate entities, objects, services, diseases, people, organizations, or components that merely participate in a relation with an attribute.

Do not annotate procedural, contextual, or outcome phrases that describe effects or usage rather than naming an attribute itself, e.g. generic mentions of utilisation, access issues, or waiting times in narrative study summaries when treated as broad measured phenomena rather than attribute labels.

Do not annotate boilerplate configuration keys or metadata fields in raw config/code snippets when they are simply part of a listing rather than discussed as attributes in context, e.g. TOML keys like "name", "version", "authors", "edition", "path", "features", "default-features" in the provided negative example.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the attribute, indicator, target, parameter, property, or measured/desired characteristic.
Include modifiers that are part of the attribute name when they specify its meaning, such as domain adjectives or function words: "load order maximum", "prognostic indicators", "therapeutic targets".
For schema or markup/property mentions, annotate only the property name itself, e.g. "class", not surrounding words like "with", "in a", or the property value such as "ember-view".
Do not extend the span to include the entity that possesses the attribute unless it is part of the conventional attribute name.
Do not include verbs, full clauses, or explanatory context around the attribute mention.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish attribute from object/entity mentions: annotate the property or characteristic being referred to, not the item that has it. In "load order maximum", the attribute is the maximum load order setting, not "ESL flag".
Distinguish attribute from values: annotate field or property names like "class", but not the specific assigned value such as "ember-view" or numeric/string values in configuration text.

Distinguish attribute from broad study variables or outcomes when the text discusses them generically rather than naming a concrete attribute term. For example, generic mentions like "different factors", "demographic factors", or "variables studied" are too broad.
Distinguish attribute from services, people, diseases, or domains: in "therapeutic targets for gliomas", annotate "therapeutic targets" as the attribute-like research object, not "gliomas".

Distinguish attribute from document structure or configuration keys when they function only as boilerplate metadata fields in listings or code/config blocks; such mentions are excluded unless the text is explicitly referring to the property as an attribute in discourse.

author.definition: A person name mentioned in the role of writing, describing, or otherwise being the credited creator of a written, scholarly, or similar work.
include_rules: Annotate person names that are explicitly credited as having written, described, or authored a work, such as after cues like "written by" or "described by".
Annotate full personal names when provided for the credited author.
Annotate surname-only mentions when the text clearly refers to a cited or credited author, especially in bibliographic or shorthand reference style.
Annotate each individual person in a multi-author mention if each is presented as an author of the work.
Include author mentions tied to books, articles, formal descriptions, answers, or other authored content, as long as the credited entity is a person.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate names of people who are mentioned only as editors, staff members, employees, interview subjects, or other non-author roles, e.g. magazine staff listings.
Do not annotate organization names, publishers, media groups, journals, magazines, or platforms, even if they produce or publish the work.
Do not annotate technical terms, scientific abbreviations, chemicals, diseases, measurements, or other non-person strings.
Do not annotate a person name if the context does not indicate authorship or creator credit for a work.
Do not annotate dates, years, reference section labels, category labels, or other metadata surrounding an author mention.
Do not annotate combined author strings as one entity when they contain multiple distinct names joined by punctuation or conjunctions.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest contiguous span that names the author, including full given names, middle names/initials, surnames, and surname particles when present, e.g. "Per Olof Christopher Aurivillius".

If multiple authors are listed separately, annotate each person name as a separate author entity rather than one combined span; do not include conjunctions or punctuation such as "and", commas, or ampersands.
When an authorship reference is abbreviated or only part of the name is given, annotate only the portion explicitly present in the text, e.g. "Schervish" in "deGroot & Schervish".
Do not include surrounding role words or trigger phrases such as "described by", "written by", "author", or "edited by" in the entity span.
Do not include publication titles, organization names, dates, or publisher names adjacent to the author mention.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate as author only when the person is mentioned specifically as the creator or writer of a work or formal description; a person mentioned only as staff, editor, interviewer, videographer, or other workplace role is not an author.
Distinguish author from publisher or organization: names like "Scholastic" or "Bauer Media Group" are not authors even if they appear in the same sentence as a work.
Distinguish author from generic person mentions: personal names without evidence of authorship in context should not be annotated as author.
Distinguish author from scientific content terms, abbreviations, and technical expressions; these may appear in articles but are not creator names.
Distinguish author from titles of works: books, magazines, articles, channels, and groups are not authors even if they are closely associated with the named person.

bacterium.definition: A mention of a bacterium or bacteria, expressed as a scientific taxonomic name or a generic/descriptive organism term referring to bacterial organisms.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of bacterial organisms, whether singular or plural, when they refer to bacteria as biological entities.
Annotate full scientific bacterial names consisting of genus and species, e.g. "Pseudomonas aeruginosa" and "Corynebacterium parvum".
Annotate generic bacterial common-noun mentions when they directly denote bacteria, e.g. "bacteria".
Annotate descriptive bacterial noun phrases when the descriptor modifies the bacterial organism mention itself, e.g. "plaque-forming bacteria".
Annotate bacterium mentions regardless of whether they appear in clinical, microbiological, or experimental context.
exclude_rules: Exclude mentions of non-bacterial organisms, even if they appear in parallel with bacteria, e.g. "yeast".
Exclude adjectives or nominal modifiers that refer to bacterial properties without naming the organism itself, e.g. "bacterial" in "bacterial sexually transmitted infection".
Exclude generic references to infection, disease, complications, or treatment unless the bacterium itself is named.
Exclude substances derived from or associated with bacteria, such as toxins, cell-wall components, or abbreviations for those substances, e.g. "endotoxin", "lipopolysaccharide", "LPS".
Exclude taxonomic or epidemiological classification labels below or alongside the organism mention when they are not actual bacterium names in context, e.g. "serovars", "serogroup B", "L2a".

Exclude abbreviated mentions when they are not annotated as standalone bacterium names in the target scheme evidence; for example, do not annotate abbreviated forms like "C. trachomatis" based on the provided negative example.

Exclude broad mentions that clearly refer to the bacterial category only as a common noun in explanatory context if no actual organism mention is being targeted by the scheme, e.g. "the bacterium" in the negative example.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest contiguous span that uniquely identifies the bacterium mention.
Include the full scientific binomial name when present, e.g. "Pseudomonas aeruginosa" and "Corynebacterium parvum".
Include descriptive modifiers only when they are part of the organism mention itself, e.g. "plaque-forming bacteria".
Do not include surrounding context words that describe measurement, location, or experimental role, e.g. exclude words such as "density of", "in sputum", or "during therapy" from the bacterium span.
Do not include punctuation outside the name span; exclude trailing commas, parentheses, and sentence punctuation.
For coordinated mentions, annotate only the bacterium term itself, not neighboring non-bacterial organisms; e.g. in "bacteria and yeast", only the bacterial mention is included.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate non-bacterial microorganisms such as yeast; this type is only for bacteria.
Do not annotate diseases, syndromes, infections, or clinical conditions caused by bacteria; annotate the organism mention only.
Do not annotate bacterial products, components, or molecules such as endotoxin, lipopolysaccharide, or LPS; these are substances, not bacteria.
Do not annotate taxonomic subdivisions or strain/typing labels when they are not themselves bacterium mentions in context, such as serovar, serogroup, or lists of serovar codes.
Do not annotate genes or genetic markers associated with bacteria, such as gene names mentioned in typing discussions.
Do not annotate people, places, routes, or other non-biological names that may appear in the same text.

behavior.definition: A behavior entity is a word or phrase that denotes a pattern, mode, tendency, or characteristic way of acting or responding, including behavioral patterns of agents and named behavioral states of systems or materials.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that explicitly denote a pattern of behavior or repeated mode of action.
Annotate domain-specific expressions that name a characteristic behavioral state or response of a system, material, or phenomenon.
Annotate modified pattern expressions when the modifier specifies what behavior is being described, such as activity-specific patterns.
Annotate standalone terms like 'pattern' when they clearly refer to a behavior/configuration rather than a physical design object.
Annotate scientifically meaningful behavior labels even when followed by a generic head like 'behavior', if the core label itself names the behavior, as in 'spin glass'.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary actions, commands, or procedural steps unless they are explicitly framed as a behavior or pattern.
Do not annotate generic nouns that are not used behaviorally, such as search criteria, details, listings, bridges, property submissions, or categories.
Do not annotate whole descriptive clauses when only a compact behavior term is present.
Do not annotate physical entities, substances, people, places, dates, or constructed objects even if the sentence discusses how they act.
Do not annotate broad attribute terms like 'characteristics' unless the phrase as a whole clearly names a behavior or pattern.
Do not annotate contextual descriptors around a behavior term, such as measurement conditions, locations, or temporal qualifiers.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the behavior/pattern itself.

Include modifiers that are part of the named behavior expression, such as the activity noun in 'drinking patterns' or the state descriptor in 'spin glass behavior' when the true behavior term is 'spin glass'.

When a generic head noun like 'pattern', 'patterns', or 'behavior' is accompanied by a modifier that specifies the behavior, include the full phrase if the modifier is required to identify the behavior; e.g., 'drinking patterns' rather than only 'patterns'.
If the sentence uses a generic noun alone to refer to a behavior schema or repeated way of acting, annotate only that noun; e.g., 'pattern'.

Exclude surrounding verbs, adjectives, quantities, temperatures, and discourse markers that merely describe the behavior rather than form its name, such as 'shows', 'below 32 K', or numbering like '3.'.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse behavior with physical objects or structures: words like 'bridge', 'panel', 'form', or 'button' are not behaviors even if they contain design/style terms.
Do not annotate criteria, alerts, searches, or listings as behavior; these are administrative or informational concepts, not patterns of action or response.

When a phrase describes a material or system property, annotate it only if it denotes a behavioral mode or characteristic response, as in 'spin glass', not the material itself like 'sulfide compound'.

Differentiate behavior from events or actions in progress: imperatives such as 'reverse', 'fold', 'click', or 'enter' are actions/instructions, whereas 'pattern' refers to an enduring or identifiable behavioral configuration.

Differentiate behavior from generic scientific characteristics unless the term denotes a specific behavior/state pattern; 'intoxication characteristics' is not clearly a behavior span here, while 'drinking patterns' is.

beverage.definition: A beverage is a named drink or drink type intended for consumption, including alcoholic and non-alcoholic drinks, as well as branded or varietal drink names.
include_rules: Annotate common nouns that directly denote beverages, such as alcoholic drinks and non-alcoholic drinks, when they refer to something that can be consumed as a drink.
Annotate multiword beverage types that include origin, style, or other lexical modifiers when the full phrase names the drink, e.g. 'Brazilian coffee'.
Annotate branded, proprietary, or otherwise specific beverage names when the context indicates they are drink products, e.g. 'Betty Stogs' and 'Lushington's'.
Annotate beverage mentions regardless of whether they appear in serving or inventory contexts, such as being served, stocked, ordered, or consumed.
Annotate both generic beverage names and specific beverage product names, as long as the referent is the drink itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that are not drinks, even if they appear in descriptive or metaphorical contexts.
Do not annotate generic context words about drinking, serving, or taste such as 'booze', 'flavor', 'cup', or 'barrel' unless they are part of the beverage name itself.
Do not annotate ingredients, crops, or foods that are not being referred to as drinks, such as 'corn' or 'cornfield'.
Do not annotate organization names, artist names, media titles, release names, usernames, URLs, or platform names.
Do not annotate adjectives alone when they merely describe a beverage but do not form part of its name; for example, annotate 'Brazilian coffee' but not 'Brazilian' by itself.
Do not annotate highly generic category terms when they are used only as loose slang or commentary rather than as a concrete beverage mention.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the beverage itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the beverage name or type, such as origin/style descriptors in 'Brazilian coffee'.
Include brand or product names when they refer to a beverage, even if they do not contain a generic drink word, as in 'Betty Stogs' and 'Lushington's'.

Exclude surrounding determiners, quantities, serving-container words, and descriptive context unless they are part of the fixed beverage name: annotate 'wine' not 'with wine', and 'Brazilian coffee' not 'a quick and enjoyable cup of supreme Brazilian coffee'.
Do not include generic container or serving words such as 'cup of', 'barrel of', 'glass of', or similar when the beverage name follows them.
Exclude quotation marks and punctuation unless they are internal to the beverage name, such as the apostrophe in 'Lushington's'.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the drinkable substance or beverage product, not the producer, seller, or venue associated with it.
When a phrase names a branded drink product, annotate it as beverage even if it resembles an organization or product title, provided the context indicates it is being served, consumed, or stocked.
Do not annotate words that refer to flavors, ingredients, or food items unless the phrase as a whole denotes a beverage.
Do not annotate non-beverage uses of the same string; the mention must denote something intended to be drunk.
Do not annotate titles, usernames, albums, releases, websites, or other media/product names unless the context clearly shows they are beverage names.
Distinguish beverage from containers or portions: annotate 'coffee' or 'Brazilian coffee', not 'cup' or 'cup of coffee'.

biological entity.definition: A biological entity is a mention of a living biological unit, a biological substance or marker, or a biologically defined cellular/molecular activity or function.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of cells or other living biological units referred to as entities in the text.
Annotate mentions of biological substances, markers, or indicators used to characterize biological state, such as 'biomarkers'.
Annotate biologically defined activities or functions when the full phrase denotes a biological entity-like concept, such as 'natural killer cell activity'.
Annotate both singular and plural mentions when they refer to biological entities.
Annotate common-noun mentions of biological entities even when they are not proper names.
Annotate entity mentions appearing in scientific or biomedical descriptions regardless of whether they are discussed as targets, observations, or mechanisms.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-biological text such as legal citations, court names, software terms, file paths, commands, machines, or web-server terminology.
Do not annotate general actions, procedures, or events that are not themselves biological entities, such as 'detection', 'prevention', or 'augmentation' alone.
Do not annotate environmental, physiological, or descriptive states by themselves, such as 'oxic' or 'hypoxic', unless the full phrase denotes a biological entity mention.
Do not annotate generic abstract nouns that lack biological reference in context, such as 'activity' or 'signal' alone, unless the complete expression names a biological activity/entity.
Do not annotate people, organizations, places, or products even if they appear in the same sentence as a biological discussion.
Do not annotate broad sentence fragments that merely contain a biological entity; only the entity mention itself should be marked.
boundary_rules: Include the full noun phrase that names the biological entity or biological process/activity, including essential modifiers that identify the entity, such as 'natural killer cell activity'.
Include plural or singular head nouns when they denote biological entities, e.g., 'cells', 'biomarkers'.
Do not extend the span to surrounding verbs, determiners, quantifiers, or copular/context words unless they are part of the entity name; for example, annotate 'cells' not 'the majority of the cells'.

Do not include unrelated descriptive context such as experimental role, degree, or state when it is not part of the entity name; for example, annotate 'cells' rather than 'fully oxic or fully hypoxic cells' unless the full modified phrase is needed to denote a distinct biological entity mention.

When the mention is an activity or function tied to a biological component, include the full activity expression if that is the biological entity being referenced, e.g., 'natural killer cell activity'.
Exclude punctuation and coordinating material outside the entity span.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish biological entities from non-biological technical terms: annotate living units, molecular indicators, and biologically grounded activities, but not software, legal, or mechanical terms.

Distinguish biological entities from general scientific actions or procedures: annotate the biological thing or biological activity itself, not the surrounding research action, e.g., annotate 'biomarkers' but not 'detection of biomarkers'.
Distinguish biological entities from diseases, conditions, or environmental states: in 'fully oxic or fully hypoxic' the states are not the entity; the entity is 'cells'.

Distinguish biological entities from generic measurements or abstract roles: annotate the biologically meaningful phrase only when it denotes a biological object or activity, e.g., 'natural killer cell activity' rather than generic 'activity' alone.
If a phrase contains both a biological entity and non-entity context, select only the biological entity span rather than the full larger phrase.

biological molecule.definition: A biological molecule mention referring to a specific molecule, molecular species, or molecular class found or used in biology/biomedicine, including nucleic acids, antigens, antibodies, proteins, and closely related molecular substances.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of biological molecules and molecular classes, including RNA species, antigens, antibodies, proteins, peptides, and similar biomolecular substances.
Annotate molecule mentions that include a specific molecular name plus class label, such as "Egr-1 mRNA levels".
Annotate descriptive molecular noun phrases that identify molecules by source, target, or biological association, such as "Murine monoclonal antibodies" and "cancer cell antigens".
Annotate singular or plural mentions when they clearly denote molecules or classes of molecules.
Include source/species or subtype modifiers when they are part of the molecule mention, such as "Murine" in "Murine monoclonal antibodies".
Annotate molecular mentions whether they occur as endogenous biological substances or therapeutic/experimental reagents.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-molecular biological entities such as cells, tissues, organs, organisms, diseases, or clinical conditions.
Do not annotate biological processes, pathways, mechanisms, or functions, even if they involve molecules.

Do not annotate generic background mentions of molecule categories when they are used only as broad explanatory terms rather than concrete entity mentions in context, as in the negative miRNA passage.
Do not annotate experimental outcomes, measurements, or statistics alone, such as "3.2-fold increase".
Do not annotate modifiers by themselves when the molecular head noun is absent, such as "cancer cell" without "antigens".
Do not annotate non-biomedical named entities or ordinary text from unrelated domains.
boundary_rules: Include the full molecule mention, including essential modifiers that identify the molecular species or class, such as gene/protein names, organism/source adjectives, and molecule-class heads: "Egr-1 mRNA levels", "Murine monoclonal antibodies", "cancer cell antigens".

When a quantity or measurement noun is tightly attached to the molecule mention and the example style treats the full phrase as the entity, include it, e.g. "Egr-1 mRNA levels" rather than only "Egr-1 mRNA".
Include plural forms when they denote molecular classes or multiple molecules, such as "antigens" or "antibodies".
Exclude surrounding verbs, prepositions, and functional context outside the noun phrase, e.g. annotate "cancer cell antigens" not "directed at cancer cell antigens".
Exclude unrelated experimental, clinical, or disease context words that are not part of the molecule name, such as treatment purpose, tissue names, or comparison phrases.
Start the span at the first token that specifies the molecule and end at the last token belonging to the molecular noun phrase.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the molecule itself, not the cell, tissue, organism, or disease associated with it. For example, in "cancer cell antigens", the entity is the antigen phrase, not "cancer cell" alone.

Do not annotate biological processes, functions, or regulatory events even when molecules participate in them, such as gene expression, cytotoxicity, differentiation, inflammation, signalling, or secretion.

Do not annotate generic scientific descriptors unless they are part of a molecule mention; for example, "regulatory networks", "transcription factors" in a general discussion, and "inflammatory proteins" used only as a broad category should not be marked unless a specific molecular mention is made.
Do not annotate people, organizations, political names, or other non-biological content even if capitalized or name-like, as illustrated by the non-biomedical negative example.

Distinguish molecules from assays or measurements: annotate the molecular phrase when it is the object being measured, but not standalone numerical changes or comparison text such as fold increase values.

Distinguish specific molecular mentions from broad reference to a field or molecule type in generic explanatory prose; generic repeated mentions like "miRNAs" in background discussion are not annotated here unless used as a concrete molecule mention under the dataset's scope.

biological process.definition: A biological process is a named biological, physiological, developmental, regulatory, healing, or functional event or series of events occurring in an organism, tissue, cell, or biological system, including broad process terms and effect/process descriptions.
include_rules: Annotate terms that explicitly name a biological or physiological process, developmental program, regulatory activity, healing process, or functional biological event.
Include single-word process names, especially nominalized biological events ending in forms like "-genesis" or other event-denoting nouns, e.g. "myogenesis".
Include multiword process phrases where a modifier plus a process head together denote a biological phenomenon, e.g. "neural control" and "wound healing".
Include effect/process expressions when the text presents them as a biological or therapeutic processual phenomenon, e.g. "therapeutic effect".
Annotate both normal and abnormal biological processes when the span itself names the process.
Annotate process mentions regardless of whether they are asserted, hypothesized, known, or unknown, as long as the phrase denotes the process itself.
exclude_rules: Exclude names of molecules, genes, proteins, antibodies, cell lines, organisms, anatomical structures, and other physical biological objects, even when they participate in a process.
Exclude generic scientific, governmental, or historical events that are not biological phenomena, such as legislative sessions or settlement history.
Exclude purely descriptive adjectives or states that do not themselves denote a process, such as "abnormal" or "unknown" when standing alone.
Exclude experimental actions, reporting verbs, and research procedures unless the phrase denotes the biological phenomenon rather than the investigator's activity.

Exclude long argument structures that add participants or context beyond the process name, such as objects introduced by "of", "in", or similar attachments, unless they are part of a fixed process term.

Exclude quantitative, temporal, or certainty language around the process mention, such as "potential", "normal", "previously", or "during" unless such wording is part of the core named process expression.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the process itself, including the process head noun or deverbal form, e.g. "myogenesis", "neural control", "wound healing".
Include modifiers that are part of the established process expression and are needed to identify the process, such as adjectival or noun modifiers in "neural control" and "wound healing".

Exclude surrounding prepositions, articles, auxiliaries, and framing verbs, e.g. annotate "wound healing" not "in wound healing", and "myogenesis" not "normal myogenesis" unless the qualifier is intended as part of the process mention.
Exclude participants, locations, and affected objects unless they are integrated into the process name. For example, prefer "neural control" over "neural control of neck muscles".

When a noun phrase contains an effect or process noun plus a purely evaluative or contextual adjective, include only the core process phrase if possible; however, include the full phrase when the adjective is tightly bound to the event meaning, as in "therapeutic effect".
If multiple distinct process mentions appear, annotate each separately rather than merging them into one span.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a biological process with a biological entity such as a gene, protein, cell line, immunoglobulin chain, virus, tissue, or body part. In "role of RASSF4 in normal myogenesis", annotate "myogenesis" but not "RASSF4".
Do not annotate diseases, disorders, or abnormalities themselves unless the text names the underlying process. For example, "abnormal" alone is not a process, but "neural control" is.

Do not confuse a process with a molecular substance modification or component mention when the text refers to materials rather than the event; in the negative example, names like "immunoglobulin heavy chain", "mannose", and "fucose" are not biological processes.
Do not annotate organizations, legislatures, sessions, or other non-biological events; generic uses of "session", "history", or governmental actions are outside the biological process type.

Differentiate processes from experimental procedures or study activities unless the mention denotes a biological phenomenon in the system being described. For example, a research action such as "to study" is not a biological process, whereas "secretion" would be if explicitly annotated as the phenomenon.

Do not confuse causal or functional descriptions with entities. Phrases like "therapeutic effect" are included because they denote an effect process, but agent mentions, treatments, or outcomes as objects are not themselves biological processes.

biological sample.definition: A biological sample is a mention of biological material taken, collected, or used as a specimen for observation, testing, or measurement, such as blood, plasma, or tissue in sample context.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of biological material that function as collected, tested, stored, or measured specimens in a biomedical context.
Include common specimen expressions consisting of a biological material alone or a material plus a sample noun, such as "plasma", "blood samples", and "tissue samples".
Annotate singular or plural mentions when they denote actual specimens used in the study or procedure.
Include ex vivo material derived from organisms when the text treats it as a sample for analysis.
Annotate mentions even when embedded in collection or analysis statements, as long as the span itself denotes the biological specimen.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate substances, tissues, or body materials when they are mentioned only as general biological concepts and not as collected or analyzed specimens.
Do not annotate source organisms, patients, populations, or locations from which samples are obtained, such as animals, humans, or geographic regions.
Do not annotate procedures, study actions, laboratory measurements, or experimental conditions surrounding the sample mention.
Do not annotate medications, chemicals, treatments, devices, software, or any non-biological materials.
Do not annotate generic words like "sample" or "samples" by themselves unless the text identifies the biological material being sampled.
Do not annotate modifiers that only indicate sequence, timing, frequency, quantity, or other descriptive context rather than the specimen itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the sampled biological material, such as "plasma", "blood samples", or "tissue samples".
Include the material term and sample-indicating head noun when both are present, e.g. include "blood samples" and "tissue samples" in full.
If the biological material appears alone but clearly denotes the sampled specimen in context, annotate just that term, e.g. "plasma" in "plasma samples" context.
Exclude determiners, quantifiers, and modifiers that only describe amount, order, timing, or study role, such as "Sequential" in "Sequential plasma samples".

Exclude surrounding procedural or experimental context such as collection actions, source organisms, time expressions, and measurements, e.g. do not include "were collected from cattle" or "during the same time period".
Do not extend the span to include generic container words unless they are part of the specimen name itself; prefer the biological material phrase over broader surrounding text.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the specimen itself, not the organism or patient it came from; in "blood samples were collected from cattle", annotate "blood samples" but not "cattle".

Annotate biological material used as a sample, not medical treatments, drugs, or administered substances; medication mentions such as the negative example with PRN medications are not biological samples.
Annotate sampled material, not laboratory procedures or measurements; phrases about studying, collecting, killing animals, or measuring activity are context, not the entity.
Annotate concrete biological specimens rather than abstract biomedical concepts, diseases, software, or technical terms; generic non-biological uses of words in unrelated text are excluded.
When both a biological substance and a broader experimental item are mentioned, choose only the biological specimen phrase if it denotes the sampled material.

biomarker.definition: A biomarker is a measurable biological molecule, marker class, or physiological/clinical measurement used as an indicator of a biological state, disease, prognosis, response, or other clinically relevant condition.
include_rules: Annotate names of specific molecular biomarkers such as genes, proteins, or related biomolecules when they are presented as indicators, e.g. "S100A2".
Annotate explicit mentions of biomarker classes or categories when the phrase itself denotes the marker type, e.g. "circulating biomarkers".
Annotate physiological or clinical measurements that function as measurable indicators, e.g. "heart rate" and "blood pressure".
Annotate biomarker mentions whether they are discussed for diagnosis, prognosis, monitoring, treatment response, or general clinical significance.
Annotate singular or plural biomarker mentions when the text explicitly identifies them as biomarkers or measurable indicator variables.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text unrelated to biomedical measurement or indication.
Do not annotate institutions, organizations, products, places, or other non-biomedical entities.
Do not annotate diseases, cancers, symptoms, or clinical conditions themselves unless the mention denotes a measurable marker rather than the condition.
Do not annotate drugs, doses, therapies, or procedures that may affect a biomarker but are not biomarkers themselves.
Do not annotate generic scientific or discourse terms that do not denote a measurable indicator, such as "topics", "meeting", or "clinical significance" alone.
Do not annotate vague references such as "these", "results", or "significance" when no explicit biomarker name or measurement is given.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal span that names the biomarker itself, without including surrounding experimental framing such as "expression of", "level of", "measurement of", "reduction in", or disease/context phrases.
Include core modifiers that are part of the biomarker mention when they specify the biomarker category or specimen/context, e.g. "circulating biomarkers".
For molecular names, include the full gene/protein/symbol string as written, e.g. "S100A2".
For physiological or clinical measurement biomarkers, include the full measurement name, e.g. "heart rate" and "blood pressure".
Do not extend the span to include outcomes, conditions, treatments, or populations, such as "in gastric cancer" or "Both doses of lidocaine caused reductions in".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate diseases or disorders as biomarkers; in "S100A2 expression in gastric cancer", annotate "S100A2" but not "gastric cancer".

Do not annotate drugs, treatments, or interventions as biomarkers; in "Both doses of lidocaine caused reductions in heart rate and blood pressure", annotate "heart rate" and "blood pressure" but not "lidocaine".
Distinguish biomarkers from general biological entities by annotating them only when they are used as measurable indicators, marker molecules, or clinical/physiological measurements.

Generic methodological or event language is not a biomarker unless the phrase itself denotes the marker class, e.g. annotate "circulating biomarkers" but not surrounding words like "consensus meeting" or "topics".

If a molecular entity appears only as a disease target, reagent, or background biology without indication that it functions as an indicator or measured marker in context, do not treat it as a biomarker.

biomolecule.definition: A biological substance or biomolecular complex, including proteins, immune complexes, and similar biologically derived or biologically active molecular entities, mentioned as a substance rather than as a process, measurement, or non-biological chemical material.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of biological molecules and biomolecular complexes.
Annotate proteins and generic protein mentions when they refer to the molecular substance, e.g., "protein".
Annotate immune complexes and other named biological complexes, e.g., "immune complexes".

Annotate biologically active substance phrases used as administered or studied entities when the phrase itself denotes the substance mention in context, e.g., "intravenous infusions" in the provided examples.
Annotate singular or plural common-noun mentions of biomolecules when they clearly denote a substance class or instance.
Annotate both full names and standard descriptive names of biomolecular entities, whether or not an abbreviation is given nearby.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-biological synthetic chemicals, industrial compounds, polymers, resins, or optical materials.
Do not annotate diseases, conditions, legal terms, organizations, people, courts, places, or other non-molecular entities.
Do not annotate processes, methods, or actions such as producing, polymerization, binding, infusion as an event, or progression.
Do not annotate measurement expressions, quantities, units, or assay context alone, such as "levels", "amount", "28 fmoles/mg", or similar readouts.
Do not annotate generic material terms when they refer to non-biological matter rather than biomolecules.
Do not annotate abbreviations, labels, or isotope markers by themselves unless they independently denote the biomolecular substance in context.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal span that names the biomolecule or biomolecular preparation.
Include the full noun phrase when modifiers are part of the biomolecular name or class, e.g., "immune complexes".
Include the head biomolecule term even when it is broad or generic in context, e.g., "protein".
Exclude surrounding quantification, assay, and measurement language such as "levels of", "amount of", "bound was", units, and numeric values.

Exclude isotope, labeling, or bracket notation attached to the biomolecule when it functions as an experimental modifier rather than part of the biomolecule name; annotate the biomolecule itself when separable, e.g., in "[125I]oPRL ... protein", only "protein" is annotated in the provided evidence.
Exclude parenthetical abbreviations unless the abbreviation itself is the explicit biomolecule mention being annotated.
Do not extend the span to verbs, administration context, or procedural context outside the biomolecular mention.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate biomolecular substances and complexes, not synthetic industrial chemicals or material compounds; for example, polyisocyanate compounds, resins, and optical materials are excluded.

Annotate biological macromolecules, biomolecular complexes, and biologically administered substances when mentioned as substance entities, as in "immune complexes", "protein", and "intravenous infusions".
Do not annotate legal, administrative, or other non-scientific text spans even if they contain capitalized terms or abbreviations.

Distinguish a biomolecule from a process or event: annotate the substance name, not actions such as infusion, binding, polymerization, or production, unless the example usage treats the administered substance phrase itself as the entity span.

Distinguish a biomolecule from measurements or experimental readouts: annotate the molecule name, not associated levels, amounts, radioactivity markers, concentration units, or progression statements.

Distinguish a biomolecule from a general material object: biological substances such as proteins and immune complexes are included, whereas manufactured materials like resin or plastic lens materials are not.

body fluid.definition: A liquid or semiliquid substance naturally present in, produced by, secreted from, or excreted by the body, including clinical fluid specimens and bodily outputs.
include_rules: Annotate named bodily fluids and fluid specimens such as blood-derived or cerebrospinal specimens, e.g., 'serum' and 'CSF'.
Annotate secretions produced by body sites or organs, e.g., 'bronchial secretions'.
Annotate bodily outputs and excreta when referred to as substances being produced, collected, or measured, e.g., 'urinary output' and 'fecal'.
Annotate both full names and standard abbreviations of body fluids when they clearly denote the fluid.
Annotate fluid mentions regardless of whether they are discussed in clinical, laboratory, or physiological contexts, as long as the span denotes the bodily fluid itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate organs, tissues, vessels, membranes, or body systems even if they contain or transport fluids.
Do not annotate cells, cell types, or other particulate biological materials unless the mention is the fluid itself.
Do not annotate proteins, receptors, genes, extracellular matrix components, or other biomolecules.
Do not annotate diseases, developmental processes, physiological processes, or immune/inflammatory events.
Do not annotate medical devices, collection apparatus, laboratory methods, or procedures used to obtain or analyze fluids.
Do not annotate generic references to patients, animals, or specimens unless the span itself is the fluid name.
Do not annotate non-biomedical or unrelated uses of similar words, as in venue names or promotions in the negative examples.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the fluid itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the conventional fluid name or source description when they specify the fluid, e.g., 'bronchial secretions' and 'urinary output'.
For adjective-like fluid mentions used alone as nouns, annotate the surface form as written, e.g., 'fecal' when it refers to fecal output.
Include abbreviations and acronyms when they directly denote a body fluid, e.g., 'CSF'.
Do not include surrounding prepositions, determiners, quantities, or procedural context, e.g., annotate 'serum' not 'the serum' or 'serum from 700 patients'.
When a coordinated list contains multiple distinct fluids, annotate each fluid mention separately, e.g., 'CSF' and 'serum'.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse body fluid with anatomical structures or body parts: annotate the fluid contained in or produced by an organ/system, not the organ/system itself.
Do not confuse body fluid with cells or tissue components suspended in fluids; terms like blood cells, endothelial cells, or basement membrane are not body fluid.
Do not confuse body fluid with proteins, receptors, genes, microbes, drugs, or other molecular/biological substances unless the mention denotes the fluid medium itself.
Do not confuse body fluid with excretion-collection devices, containers, or procedures; in 'collection of fecal or urinary output', annotate only the outputs, not 'pouch', 'wafer', or 'collection'.
Do not annotate general physiological processes or functions involving fluids unless a specific fluid is explicitly named.
If a term can refer either to a material substance or to a fluid specimen, annotate it only when the context indicates an actual bodily fluid or output, as with 'serum' or 'CSF'.

body part.definition: An anatomical structure or bodily substance belonging to a human or animal, including organs, limbs, tissues, cavities, joints, and body fluids when mentioned as parts or materials of the body.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote a physical part of a human or animal body.
Annotate external and internal anatomical structures, including body regions and joints, e.g., "mouths" and "inflamed joints".
Annotate body fluids and similar bodily substances when explicitly tied to an organism, e.g., "human plasma".
Annotate plural and singular body-part mentions equally.
Annotate body-part mentions whether literal, descriptive, or medically modified, as long as the phrase still denotes an anatomical entity.
Annotate body parts mentioned in human or animal contexts, including comparative statements about species.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate whole organisms, people, patient groups, or species names by themselves, e.g., "humans", "dogs", or "RA patients".
Do not annotate non-anatomical biological entities such as cells, enzymes, metabolites, or chemicals unless the text explicitly refers to a body component.
Do not annotate food, restaurant, or product names that use animal body-part words in a culinary or commercial sense, e.g., "chicken wings" on a menu.
Do not annotate generic substances or materials unless they are explicitly presented as bodily substances, e.g., annotate "human plasma" but not an unrelated use of "plasma" if it is not bodily.
Do not annotate verbs, adjectives, or conditions alone when the anatomical noun is absent, e.g., not just "inflamed".
Do not annotate technical strings, filenames, URLs, code identifiers, or other non-biological text even if they contain words that could resemble entity names.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that denotes the anatomical structure or bodily substance mention.
Include descriptive modifiers that are part of the body-part mention when they directly characterize the anatomical entity, e.g., "inflamed joints".
Include organism qualifiers when they are part of the body-part expression, e.g., "human plasma".
Use the full noun phrase for the body-part mention, including the head noun such as "joints", "mouths", or "plasma".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding disease, event, or participant words not part of the anatomical phrase, e.g., exclude "RA patients" from "inflamed joints of RA patients".
Do not split a single body-part phrase into smaller pieces when the modifier and head form one anatomical mention.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate anatomical structures or bodily substances as body part, not whole organisms or people; in "human plasma", annotate "human plasma" but not the implied person.
Do not annotate food items or animal-product terms when they refer to cuisine or products rather than anatomy; for example, "chicken wings" in a menu context is not a body part mention.
Do not annotate technical or software terms that contain words resembling anatomy but refer to files, keys, or code objects; these are not body part mentions.

Do not annotate disease names, symptoms, or medical conditions unless the span itself names an anatomical structure; in "inflamed joints", the body part is the full phrase, but a standalone condition would not be annotated.

Distinguish body part from general biological entities such as cells, enzymes, or metabolites: these are not anatomical structures or bodily substances unless the phrase explicitly names a body component like "plasma".

boolean.definition: A boolean is an explicit literal token representing a logical truth value, typically one of the language- or system-level constants for true or false.
include_rules: Annotate explicit boolean literals that denote logical truth values in code, formulas, commands, configuration, or technical text.
Include canonical truth-value tokens such as "true", "false", "True", and "FALSE" when they function as literal values.
Annotate boolean literals regardless of casing when the token itself expresses the truth value.
Annotate standalone boolean literals whether they appear as function arguments, return values, conditions, assignments, or assertions.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that are not explicit boolean literals, even if they appear in technical text or code.
Do not annotate ordinary prose uses of "true" or "false" when they mean genuine, accurate, or incorrect rather than a literal truth value.
Do not annotate related programming syntax such as condition keywords, function names, statements, or variable names unless the span itself is the boolean literal.
Do not annotate numeric, textual, or symbolic stand-ins for truth values unless they are explicitly written as boolean literals.
Do not annotate any text in general narrative, scientific, or instructional passages that contains no explicit boolean literal.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the exact token(s) expressing the boolean literal, such as "TRUE", "FALSE", "True", or "true".
Do not include surrounding punctuation, parentheses, commas, periods, operators, or assignment symbols; for example, in "if( true )", annotate only "true".
Do not include function names, variable names, or keywords adjacent to the boolean literal; for example, in "Goal True.", annotate only "True" and not "Goal".
Preserve the original casing of the boolean literal as it appears in text.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate boolean only when the token denotes a logical truth value used as a literal or value. Do not annotate ordinary English uses of words like "true" or "false" when they are descriptive adjectives rather than literal values.
Do not confuse boolean literals with numeric values such as 0, 1, week 0, or counts; numbers are not boolean unless explicitly written as a boolean literal.

Do not confuse boolean with software identifiers: function names, commands, types, variables, or macros that merely contain or neighbor boolean words are not boolean unless the token itself is the literal value.
Do not annotate domain text that discusses truth, falsity, validity, or yes/no concepts implicitly without an explicit boolean literal token.

brain region.definition: An anatomical structure, subdivision, cortical area, nucleus, layer-defined area, or hemisphere that is part of the brain or central nervous system and is referred to as a neuroanatomical region.
include_rules: Annotate named anatomical parts of the brain, including major divisions, hemispheres, cortices, nuclei, lobes, and other recognized neuroanatomical regions.
Annotate subregions or layer-specified parts of brain structures when the phrase denotes a distinct anatomical region, e.g., "superficial superior colliculus".
Annotate cortex-based region names, including modified forms such as "cerebellar cortex".
Annotate hemisphere mentions when they refer to the anatomical brain division, including laterality, e.g., "left hemisphere".
Annotate brain region mentions regardless of whether they appear in anatomy, physiology, pathology, or experimental context, as long as the span denotes the region itself.
Annotate singular or plural region mentions if they explicitly name brain anatomy.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-brain anatomical structures, even if located in the head or near the brain, such as bone, sinus, skin, or other cranial tissues.
Do not annotate whole organisms, sexes, strains, or experimental animals, e.g., "mice" or "rats".
Do not annotate cell types, neurons, glia, somata, fibers, or other microscopic cellular structures unless the text explicitly names a brain region.
Do not annotate body fluids, organs, or tissues outside the brain, such as "plasma" or "liver".
Do not annotate diseases, syndromes, injuries, symptoms, or pathological processes.
Do not annotate generic spatial words alone, such as "left", "superficial", or "cortex", unless they are part of a complete brain-region mention in context.
Do not annotate chemicals, genes, proteins, receptors, transcripts, enzymes, hormones, diets, or drugs.
Do not annotate procedures or actions involving a region; annotate only the region name itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the brain region itself.
Include directional, laterality, depth, layer, or subregional modifiers when they are part of the anatomical name, e.g., "left hemisphere" and "superficial superior colliculus".
Include adjectival forms that are part of the region name, e.g., "cerebellar" in "cerebellar cortex".
Exclude surrounding prepositions, determiners, and verbs, e.g., annotate "cerebellar cortex" not "in the cerebellar cortex".
Exclude attached mentions of cells, tissues, or subcellular structures that are not part of the region name, e.g., annotate the region without extending into "Purkinje cell somata".
When a larger and a smaller brain region are both explicitly named, annotate only the span corresponding to the mentioned region in the text rather than expanding to an inferred broader structure.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate only anatomical parts of the brain or central nervous system as brain region; do not annotate non-neural body parts such as "frontal bone", "liver", or "plasma".
Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, or conditions even if they contain anatomical words, such as "frontal sinusitis".
Do not annotate cell types or cellular components as brain region, e.g., "GABAergic neurons" and "Purkinje cell somata" are not brain regions.
Do not annotate chemicals, hormones, enzymes, diets, treatments, or experimental groups as brain region.
A side-designated neuroanatomical division such as "left hemisphere" is a brain region because the core referent is a brain anatomical subdivision, not merely a spatial direction.
Bone, sinus, or other head structures outside the brain are not brain regions even if they use terms like "frontal" or occur in neurological context.

brand.definition: A brand is a commercially or publicly recognized name used to identify a company, product line, service, or marketed platform, when the mention refers to that name as a branded identity rather than as a generic category or a specific product model.
include_rules: Annotate names that function as brand identifiers for marketed goods or services, including company or product-line brand names such as "Crest."
Annotate abbreviated or acronym-like brand names when they are used as the recognizable brand reference, such as "OSB."
Annotate a named platform or service when it is referred to as a branded identity rather than a generic concept, as in "Internet" from the positive examples.
Annotate the brand mention even when it appears next to a specific product or item, if the brand itself can be cleanly separated from the product description.
Annotate the canonical brand token that a reader would use to identify the maker, marketer, or branded platform associated with the surrounding mention.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic common nouns, product categories, or item types that are not themselves brand names, such as "trapper boot."

Do not annotate specific product variants, descriptors, or subproduct names when they follow a brand name unless the full combination is itself clearly the brand; in the examples, exclude "Complete Deep Clean."

Do not annotate organizations, agencies, scientific missions, instruments, or projects that are referenced as institutional or research entities rather than brands, such as "NASA" and "WISE" in the asteroid example.
Do not annotate software architecture terms, programming concepts, markup tags, or technical components such as "controllers," "views," "ng-router," or "<div>s."

Do not annotate technology or software names when they are mentioned purely as tools/frameworks in technical discussion rather than as branded entities for this type, e.g. "AngularJS" in the provided negative example.
Do not annotate generic or lowercase uses of words that can also appear as names if they refer only to a concept, medium, or category rather than a branded identity.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the brand name itself, excluding surrounding product descriptors, model names, and generic nouns: in "Crest Complete Deep Clean," annotate only "Crest."
Include the full surface form of the brand as written when it is a standalone brand token or acronym, e.g. "OSB."
For possessive forms, annotate the brand name without the possessive suffix when separable, e.g. in "Internet's finest..." annotate "Internet," not "Internet's."
Do not extend the span to include determiners, punctuation, slogans, or nearby descriptive text unless they are part of the official brand name.
If a longer expression contains both a brand and a product/item type, select the shortest span that uniquely identifies the brand.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish brand from product: annotate the company/brand label that markets or names a product, not the product variant or item itself. Thus annotate "Crest" but not "Complete Deep Clean" or "trapper boot."
Distinguish brand from organization names used only as institutions, agencies, or projects. Names like "NASA" or "WISE" in a scientific mission context are not brands here.
Distinguish brand from software/framework/product technology terms used generically or as tool names. "AngularJS" in a programming discussion is not treated as a brand in these examples.

Distinguish brand from general platform or medium nouns unless they function as a named commercial/service brand. "Internet" is annotated here because it is used as a branded named entity rather than a generic lowercase medium term.
Distinguish brand from common nouns and categories: words such as "boot," "app," "views," or "controllers" are not brands even when they appear near branded names.

building.definition: A building is a physical constructed structure or premises intended for human use, occupancy, storage, or activity, including generic building mentions and named buildings or facilities when the reference is to the structure itself.
include_rules: Annotate common nouns that directly denote a physical building or buildings, such as "house" and "buildings".
Annotate plural or quantified building mentions when the quantity is part of the mention, e.g. "22 buildings".
Annotate named facilities or institutions when the context makes clear the reference is to the building/premises rather than the organization, e.g. a museum name used as a housed structure.
Annotate residential, commercial, public, or institutional structures when they are mentioned as physical constructions or premises.
Annotate mentions of a building whether generic or proper-named, as long as the text refers to the structure itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate organizations, institutions, or businesses when the context refers to the group or institution rather than the physical building.
Do not annotate addresses, postal codes, city/state lines, or real-estate listing headers that identify location but do not themselves denote the building entity.
Do not annotate land parcels, neighborhoods, districts, clubs, or other areas unless the mention specifically denotes a building.
Do not annotate interior rooms, building parts, fixtures, or amenities such as bathrooms, closets, elevators, garages, pools, floors, ceilings, or walls as standalone building entities.
Do not annotate descriptions of property features or measurements such as square footage, bedroom counts, bathroom counts, acreage, or price.
Do not annotate depictions, publications, or artifacts like cartoons, newspapers, or drawings, even if they mention places indirectly.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase that denotes the building entity, including determiners or numerals when they are part of the referring expression in context, e.g. "the house" if determiners are included by project style, and quantified mentions like "22 buildings" when the number is part of the entity mention.
Include building-type head nouns such as "house" or "buildings" when they are the words that realize the building mention.

Include the complete proper name of a named institution when the mention is being used to denote the building or premises that house it, e.g. "National Museum of Women in the Arts" in "The building currently houses the National Museum of Women in the Arts."

Exclude surrounding verbs, prepositions, and descriptive clauses that are not part of the building mention, e.g. annotate "house" not "the house took shape", and "22 buildings" not "Of the 22 buildings in the district".
Do not extend the span to include street addresses, city/state/postal information, sale descriptions, or room/feature lists unless the text explicitly names the building itself as the mention.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish building from postal address: annotate the physical structure, not its location string. For example, a street address like "11767 Calla Lilly Court" is not a building mention by itself.

Distinguish building from organizations or institutions: when a named institution appears, annotate it only if the context refers to the physical premises/building rather than the organization as a social or administrative entity.

Distinguish building from rooms, parts, and amenities: do not annotate subparts or attached features such as "master bathroom", "closets", "pool", "garages", or "wine wall" unless the target type explicitly includes whole buildings only.

Distinguish building from neighborhoods, districts, and clubs: places such as "Grand Estate neighborhood" or "Old Palm Golf Club" are not building mentions unless the phrase directly names a specific building.

Distinguish building from visual depictions and media content: words like "cartoon", "drawing", or "newspaper" in the provided negative example refer to media artifacts or publications, not structures for occupancy.

business.definition: A named commercial entity: a company, store, shop, restaurant, pharmacy, brand outlet, or similar business organization identified as providing goods or services.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of commercial establishments or companies, including local shops and branded chains.
Annotate business names whether they are formal names, stylized names, or contextually used names of the establishment, as in "Sue Sues embrodery" and "Cinnabon."
Annotate a generic-looking phrase when the sentence uses it as the name of a specific seller or service, as in "our Online Pharmacy."
Annotate business entities mentioned as places of employment, management, purchase, or service provision when the mention identifies the commercial organization.
Annotate both physical establishments and business organizations if the mention refers to the commercial entity rather than merely a building or product.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic references to businesses or services when they are not used as a specific name, such as generic mentions of a web site, app, store, server tool, or pharmacy in the abstract.
Do not annotate products, prescription drugs, software applications, operating systems, development tools, or technical components, even if sold by a business.
Do not annotate locations, addresses, cities, states, or geographic areas that only indicate where a business operates.
Do not annotate persons connected to the business, such as owners, managers, employees, or customers, unless the business name itself is mentioned.
Do not annotate descriptive noun phrases that refer to a business by function alone unless the context clearly uses the phrase as a proper business name.
Do not annotate larger event, task, or activity descriptions that merely mention a business context without naming the business itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest contiguous span that names the business, including its distinctive business name and any integral business designator if it is part of the name.
Include generic business-form words when they function as the actual name in context, e.g. "Online Pharmacy" when it refers to a specific seller/service rather than pharmacies in general.
Exclude surrounding determiners, possessives, and descriptive context not part of the name, such as "a" in "a Cinnabon" or location/context phrases like "in downtown Hiram".
Do not extend the span to include products, services, or actions associated with the business, e.g. only "Sue Sues embrodery," not "they do alot of the clothing work for the football team."
Keep the exact surface form used to refer to the business, even if misspelled or informally written, e.g. "embrodery" as written in the name.
If a business name appears inside a larger non-business title or sentence fragment, annotate only the business name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate a business when the text refers to a commercial establishment, company, shop, restaurant, pharmacy, brand-operated outlet, or similar organization that provides goods or services.

Do not annotate software products, apps, websites, platforms, or technical systems when they are mentioned as software/tools rather than as business organizations, e.g. "iPad app," "App store," "Visual Studio," "IIS," and "ASP.NET config" are not business entities here.
Do not annotate facility or location mentions unless they name the business itself; a city or area such as "Omaha, Nebraska" or "downtown Hiram" is not a business.

Distinguish businesses from products: if the term names the seller or establishment, annotate it; if it names an item being sold or used, do not. For example, annotate "Cinnabon" as the business being managed, but not a drug name merely because it is purchased somewhere.

Distinguish businesses from occupations or roles: words like "pharmacy," "embroidery," or "store" are only annotated when they function as the name of a specific business in context, not when they describe an industry, service type, or job function generically.
Distinguish businesses from organizations such as teams or administrative bodies when the mention is not a commercial entity. In the example, "football team" is not a business.

cardinal.definition: A numeral or number word that denotes a count or other plain numeric quantity, without functioning primarily as a date, time, money amount, percentage, measurement, or identifier.
include_rules: Annotate explicit numerals that express a count of entities, items, or people, such as "5", "48", "16", and "11".
Annotate standalone numeric quantities when they answer 'how many' rather than 'which year', 'how much money', or 'what percentage'.
Annotate both digit-form and word-form cardinal numbers when they denote plain counts, if present in text.
Annotate cardinals modifying a noun phrase when the number itself gives the count, as in "5 girls" or "48 babies".
Annotate multiple separate cardinal mentions in the same sentence when each independently expresses a count.
Annotate approximate or bounded counts only for the numeric token itself when the number remains a plain count, e.g. in contexts like "at least 48" annotate "48".
exclude_rules: Exclude numbers used as years, dates, times, ages in date-like format, addresses, or other calendrical/locational references.
Exclude monetary amounts, including numerals combined with currency symbols or money words such as "million" when expressing price, cost, or value.
Exclude percentages, rates, ratios, and proportions when expressed as percentage constructions.
Exclude numbers that are part of measurements or quantified units, such as dimensions, distances, durations, temperatures, floors, or similar unit-bearing quantities.
Exclude numerals that appear inside proper names, titles, product names, organization names, programme names, or codes if they are identifiers rather than counts.
Exclude spelled-out indefinite quantity words that are not explicit cardinal numerals, such as vague quantifiers like "some", "many", or "several".
Exclude ordinal expressions used for rank, order, or sequence when they do not denote a simple count.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the minimal token(s) expressing the number itself, e.g. "5", "48", "16", "11".
Do not include nearby nouns, units, or descriptors such as in "5 girls" annotate only "5".

For written ordinals or numeric ordinals used as grade/position labels (e.g. "2nd", "5th"), include the full ordinal token only if the scheme treats ordinals as cardinals; otherwise exclude them. Follow corpus policy consistently.
Do not extend the span to include surrounding function words or modifiers such as "about", "at least", "more than", "first", or punctuation.

If a number is part of a larger expression with a dedicated semantic type such as a date, money amount, percentage, address, model name, or measurement, do not annotate the embedded number as a standalone cardinal.
When numbers are written with digits, include all digits that form the numeral; include internal punctuation only when it is part of the numeral format.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate numbers when they function as dates or years (e.g. "1929", "1983", "1963") rather than simple counts.
Do not annotate numbers when they are part of monetary expressions (e.g. "$44 million", "$14 million") because the full expression denotes an amount of money, not a bare cardinal.
Do not annotate percentages such as "70 per cent" when they denote proportion/rate rather than a simple count.
Do not annotate ordinal expressions indicating rank or sequence such as "the first" unless annotation policy explicitly merges ordinals into this type.
Do not annotate numbers that are part of measurements, dimensions, or quantities with units such as "10 stories high" if the corpus reserves those for measurement-like expressions.
Do not annotate numerals embedded inside names, titles, program names, or identifiers unless they independently denote a count in context.

category.definition: A category is a named class, division, department, organizational unit, court type, software class, process type, team type, or similar grouping label used to classify entities, activities, or roles.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that explicitly denote a class or grouping used to categorize an item, especially in software, organizational, legal, or functional contexts.
Annotate software or product classification labels, including hierarchical menu-like categories, such as "Photo & Graphics Tools".
Annotate named institutional or legal court categories when the phrase denotes the court type or division, such as "Court of Chancery".
Annotate business or organizational functional categories that describe processes or teams, such as "sales process" and "sales team".
Annotate category phrases whether they are proper-name-like or common-noun-based, as long as they function as a classification label rather than a mere topic mention.
Annotate both standalone category mentions and category spans embedded inside larger expressions when the embedded span itself clearly denotes the category.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate broad common nouns that refer to industries, commodities, activities, or domains in a purely generic sense, such as "mining industry", "fish farming", or "food diet", when they are not used as explicit classification labels.
Do not annotate names of concrete entities such as products, chemicals, species, countries, regions, publications, or people.
Do not annotate descriptive phrases that mention use, purpose, or function without denoting a stable category or grouping label.
Do not annotate scientific type expressions that classify models, kinetics, or measurements rather than social, organizational, legal, software, or functional categories relevant to the examples.
Do not annotate overly broad nouns like "tool", "team", "process", or "court" by themselves unless the text provides a specific category phrase.
Do not annotate roles or participant groups when they are merely referring to people in context rather than to a named team or organizational category.
Do not annotate full titles of books, reports, articles, or case compilations when only a subspan denotes the category.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase that names the category, class, department, division, process type, or team type, including internal modifiers that are part of the category name (e.g., "Photo & Graphics Tools", "sales process", "sales team").

Include premodifiers that specify the subtype or domain of the category when they are integral to the meaning, such as functional or organizational descriptors like "sales" or institutional descriptors like "Chancery".
Do not include surrounding cue words that merely introduce the category, such as "within", "more precisely", "in", "the software lies within", or similar framing text.
Do not extend the span to include higher-level explanatory structure unless it is itself the category being referred to; annotate only the category phrase, not the full clause.

For names embedded inside longer titles, annotate only the internal span that denotes the category itself, not the entire document or work title (e.g., annotate "Court of Chancery" but not "Reports of Cases in the High Court of Chancery, 13 and 14 Geo.").
Include conjunctions, symbols, and punctuation only when they are internal to the category name, such as the ampersand in "Photo & Graphics Tools".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse category with a specific product, document, case report, or publication title: annotate the class it belongs to, not the named item itself.

Do not confuse category with industry or economic sector mentions used generically in discourse. Generic references such as "mining industry", "fish culture", or "sport fishery" are not categories unless the text presents them as explicit classificatory labels.
Do not confuse category with biological, chemical, or technical type labels describing scientific entities or models, such as species names, insecticides, or kinetic type descriptions.

Do not confuse category with occupations or groups of people mentioned as participants. Annotate expressions like "sales team" only when they denote an organizational team category, not when a phrase merely refers to individuals without categorical function.

Do not confuse category with locations or institutions as real-world entities. Annotate a phrase like "Court of Chancery" only when it functions as a court category/name, not when broader surrounding text refers to a publication or event associated with it.
When a phrase could be read either as a domain/topic or as a category, annotate it only if it clearly serves as a class label under which something is organized, classified, or grouped.
