cause.definition: A mention of a factor, condition, process, exposure, event, or explicitly labeled causal expression that is presented as producing, explaining, or contributing to an outcome or effect.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that explicitly denote a cause or potential cause of some outcome, such as phrases headed by "cause" when they function as the entity mention.
Annotate specific conditions, exposures, toxicities, losses, defects, or processes when the sentence states they cause, explain, or contribute to an outcome.
Annotate factors presented as causal even if the wording is tentative or partial, such as "potential cause" or "major causes".
Annotate the causal factor whether it appears before or after the effect statement, as long as it is the semantic source of the outcome.
Annotate concise causal mentions expressed without the word "cause" when the text clearly assigns them causal force, e.g. "peat loss" in a sentence where it explains a limited response.
Annotate singular or plural causal mentions if each span denotes the factor(s) responsible for an effect.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate entities that are only the effect, outcome, disease, symptom, or response being caused, such as "cancer" or "sensorineural deafness" when they are only the result.
Do not annotate generic statements that an event "may occur" or that something is "causative" unless a concrete causal factor is itself named as a span.
Do not annotate background descriptions of mechanisms, transport processes, device inaccuracies, or events that are not explicitly framed as the cause of a specified outcome in the text.
Do not annotate genes, mutations, haplotypes, population names, screening targets, or clinical panel items unless they are directly mentioned as the causal factor itself.
Do not annotate highly generic causal heads alone such as "cause" or "causes" when they are not part of a fuller noun phrase identifying the cause.
Do not annotate speculative or relational language alone, such as "as a potential" or "one of the major causes of", without the accompanying causal mention.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal span that names the causal factor itself.
Include internal modifiers that are part of the causal mention when they help identify the cause, such as specific material, process, or state terms: "peat loss".
When the text expresses causation with a predicate plus outcome, include the full causal noun phrase if that is the realized mention, e.g. "potential cause of cancer" rather than only "cause".

If a specific condition, exposure, or mechanism is stated as the thing producing the effect, annotate only that factor, not the following disease or outcome: "Ototoxicity" in "Ototoxicity is one of the major causes of sensorineural deafness."
Do not extend the span to include surrounding verbs of explanation or causation such as "explains", "causes", or "occurred" unless they are part of a noun phrase that itself denotes the cause.
Do not include determiners or quantifiers unless they are inside a fixed causal noun phrase; usually exclude words like "the", "a", "one of the major" from the span.

Exclude the effect, result, or target of causation introduced by "of", "for", or similar complements when the cause itself can be marked separately, except when the annotated mention is an explicit causal expression whose head is "cause", as in "potential cause of cancer".
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish cause from outcomes or effects: annotate the factor producing a result, not the resulting disease, symptom, response, or event.

Distinguish cause from general causal relation language. Words like "causative", "explains", or "response" do not by themselves form a cause entity unless they are part of a noun phrase denoting the cause.

Distinguish cause from mutations, genes, or other entities mentioned only as study targets or carriers. A mutation name is not a cause unless the text explicitly uses it as the causal factor and the mention itself denotes that role.
Distinguish cause from mechanisms, devices, or background conditions that merely permit or accompany an event. Annotate them only when the sentence asserts they produce the stated effect.
Distinguish cause from generic risk or screening context. Population descriptors, carrier frequencies, panels, and recommendations are not causes.
When a phrase contains both a cause and an effect, prefer the span referring to the cause-bearing expression, not the entire proposition.

celestial body.definition: A natural astronomical object in space that is itself a body, such as a planet, moon, or other naturally occurring object referred to as an individual celestial object or class of such objects.
include_rules: Annotate names of natural celestial objects such as planets and moons.
Annotate both proper names and common-noun references to celestial bodies when they clearly denote astronomical objects in context.
Annotate singular or plural common nouns for celestial-body classes when they refer to actual astronomical objects, e.g. "moons".
Annotate each separately named celestial body in coordinated mentions, e.g. "Jupiter" and "Saturn".
Annotate well-known conventional names regardless of capitalization when context clearly indicates the astronomical object, e.g. "earth" meaning the planet.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-astronomical uses of the same word form, such as "earth" meaning dirt, ground, or soil.
Do not annotate man-made objects, vehicles, missions, telescopes, or organizations related to space.
Do not annotate landforms or named features located on celestial bodies, such as craters, regions, or limbs.
Do not annotate scientific terms from other domains, including viruses, proteins, diseases, countries, and institutions.
Do not annotate adjectives derived from celestial bodies when the body itself is not named, e.g. "lunar" by itself.
Do not annotate broad environmental or locational phrases unless the celestial body term itself is explicitly present.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the token(s) that name the celestial body itself: "earth", "Moon", "Jupiter", "Saturn", "moons".
Do not include surrounding prepositions, determiners, or possessives unless they are part of the fixed name.
When a common noun denotes a celestial body in context, annotate just that noun, e.g. "moons" in "the moons of Jupiter and Saturn".
For modified mentions, exclude descriptive words that are not part of the body name, such as in "lunar impact crater" annotate "Moon" only, not the crater term.

If a named feature or structure contains a parenthetical qualifier referring to a different object type, annotate only the celestial body mention inside the sentence, not the feature name, e.g. not "Neumayer (crater)".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse celestial bodies with geographic or political entities: annotate "earth" only when it refers to the planet, not when lowercase "earth" means soil or ground.
Do not annotate surface features or impact structures such as craters; these are not celestial bodies themselves.
Do not annotate organizations, missions, or companies associated with space, such as "Thales Alenia Space".
Do not annotate biological, medical, or chemical terms that may appear in scientific text; these are unrelated to astronomical objects.
Distinguish a celestial body from a broader location phrase: in "moons of Jupiter and Saturn", annotate each body term separately rather than the whole phrase.

cell component.definition: A named cellular anatomical structure, compartment, or junction that is part of a cell or serves as a subcellular location.
include_rules: Annotate names of intracellular compartments and cellular structures, such as "nucleus" and "cytoplasm".
Annotate names of cell junctions and other structural interfaces between cells or within cellular architecture, such as "gap junctions".
Annotate component terms that appear as adjectives when they directly denote subcellular localization or compartment identity, such as "nuclear".
Annotate singular or plural mentions of cell components when they refer to a biological cellular part in context.
Annotate cell component mentions whether they occur in descriptions of localization, transport, morphology, or signaling, as long as the span itself names the cellular structure.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate diseases, treatments, drugs, chemicals, clinical outcomes, trial terms, or other non-cellular biomedical concepts.
Do not annotate whole articles, media titles, product names, game names, or other clearly non-biological text.
Do not annotate generic scientific words that do not name a cell structure, such as phases, responses, activity, action, outcome, or treatment.
Do not annotate organism-, tissue-, or organ-level structures unless the mention specifically denotes a cellular component rather than a larger anatomical region.
Do not annotate proteins or protein complexes unless the mention itself is being used as a cell-structure term; for example, "APC" and "beta-catenin" are not cell components.
Do not annotate mere directional or locative phrases if they do not name a recognized cell compartment or structure.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the cellular structure or subcellular location.
Include the full structural name when it is a multiword component, e.g., "gap junctions".
For adjectival forms that directly denote a cell compartment or location and are used as the component mention, annotate the adjective itself, e.g., "nuclear".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding non-component words such as prepositions, determiners, verbs, or unrelated modifiers.
Do not include attached biomolecule names or process words when the component is only the location, e.g., annotate only the compartment term in phrases like "exports ... to the cytoplasm".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate cell component only when the term denotes a structural part of a cell or a subcellular/cellular location; do not annotate molecules, genes, proteins, or chemicals that may occur in the same phrase.

Distinguish cell component from whole-anatomy mentions: structures like "nucleus" or "vascular wall" are annotated only when they refer to cellular architecture; broader tissue or organ context alone is not sufficient.

Distinguish cell component from biological processes or events: words describing transport, cell cycle stages, signaling, or spread are not annotated unless they explicitly name the structure where the event occurs.

Distinguish cell component from general spatial language: only established cellular compartments or junction structures are included, not generic locative expressions without a cell-structure meaning.

If a modifier describes localization to a component rather than naming another entity, annotate the component-denoting modifier itself, as in "nuclear"; if the modifier only qualifies another non-component entity, do not annotate the larger phrase.

cell line.definition: A cell line is a specific named in vitro cell culture lineage or established cellular model used in biological research, expressed as a proper name, abbreviation, or identifier, with or without the accompanying word 'cell' or 'cells'.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of specific named cell lines used as biological materials in experiments or observations.
Annotate abbreviated or coded cell line names, including letter-number combinations and hyphenated identifiers, when they denote a recognized cell line in context.
Annotate cell line names followed by 'cell' or 'cells', e.g. patterns like '[name] cells'.
Annotate cell line names that appear without 'cell(s)' when the surrounding context shows they are cellular models, as in 'transformation of CEF'.
Annotate full noun phrases referring to a specific cell line instance when they include a simple descriptive modifier tied directly to the line, such as 'cultured AT-1 cells'.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic mentions of cells or cell populations that lack a specific line name, such as 'cells' alone or broad descriptions like 'cultured cells'.
Do not annotate non-biomedical uses of similar strings, including software, web, project, or engineering text, even if they contain abbreviations, numbers, or words like 'Phase'.
Do not annotate experimental conditions, procedures, or adjectives by themselves unless they are attached to a specific cell line mention.
Do not annotate genes, proteins, plasmids, clones, strains, or sample IDs unless the context clearly identifies them as a cell line.
Do not annotate organism names, tissue names, disease names, or anatomical sources standing alone as cell line mentions.
Do not annotate partial fragments of a cell line mention when the full named expression is available in the text.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full cell line mention as it appears, including attached alphanumeric identifiers and punctuation that are part of the standard name, such as 'NIH3T3' and 'AT-1'.
Include the word 'cell' or 'cells' when it is part of the mention in text, e.g. 'NIH3T3 cells' and 'AT-1 cells'.
Include descriptive modifiers only when they are part of the noun phrase referring to the same cell line instance, e.g. 'cultured AT-1 cells' includes 'cultured'.
If the cell line name appears alone without 'cell(s)' and clearly denotes the line, annotate only that name, e.g. 'CEF'.
Do not extend the span to surrounding experimental context, verbs, prepositions, or assay descriptions, e.g. exclude words like 'in', 'transformation of', or following procedural details.
Do not include generic plurals or broad class terms unless they are part of a specific named line mention.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish cell line from generic cell-type mentions: annotate only established named lines such as 'NIH3T3', 'AT-1', or 'CEF', not nonspecific mentions like 'cells', 'fibroblasts', or 'cultured cells' without a unique line name.

Distinguish cell line from organism or tissue sources: if the text refers to a species, organ, tissue, or primary cells rather than an immortalized or named experimental line, do not annotate it as cell line.

Distinguish cell line from genes, proteins, and molecular markers: alphanumeric biomedical strings should be annotated as cell line only when they denote a cellular model in context, often followed by 'cell(s)' or used as a known line name.

Distinguish cell line from project names, software terms, codes, or other uppercase abbreviations. Strings such as 'Phase 2', technical product names, or web/software identifiers are not cell lines unless the context clearly indicates a biological cell line.
Distinguish cell line from strain or clone identifiers: annotate only when the identifier names a cell line, not when it refers to a construct, clone, sample, or experimental condition.

cell type.definition: A mention of a biological cell class or subtype, including generic cell categories and more specific cell-type expressions defined by morphology, lineage, taxonomic context, or differentiation state.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that denote biological cells or categories of cells.
Annotate both generic and specific cell-type mentions, including plural and singular forms, e.g., "cells", "cell", and "neurons" when they refer to biological cell types.
Annotate cell-type expressions that include modifiers specifying biology or classification, such as taxonomic context ("eukaryotic cells") or morphology ("spheroid cells").
Annotate established specialized cell-type nouns even without the word "cell" or "cells", e.g., "neurons".
Annotate cell-type mentions whether they describe normal cells, labeled cells, or cells discussed in experimental context, as long as the span still denotes a cell type.
Annotate mentions when the phrase refers to a recognizable class of cells rather than an individual organism, tissue, molecule, or disease.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-biological uses of "cell" or unrelated technical terms.
Do not annotate anatomical structures, organs, tissues, body regions, or joints unless the phrase explicitly denotes a cell type.
Do not annotate diseases, disorders, syndromes, or pathological conditions.
Do not annotate molecules, enzymes, proteins, genes, receptors, or biochemical activities even when they occur near a cell-type mention.
Do not annotate organism names, taxonomic groups, or species names alone unless they are embedded in a cell-type phrase.
Do not annotate purely contextual location phrases attached to a cell type, such as "of crayfish abdominal ganglia", when they identify where the cells are found rather than what type they are.
Do not annotate generic references to people or patients, or other non-cellular entities.
Do not annotate adjectives or descriptors alone when the cell-denoting head noun is absent, unless the resulting word itself is an established cell-type noun such as "neurons".
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase that names the cell type, including descriptive modifiers that are part of the biological type, e.g., "eukaryotic cells" and "spheroid cells".
Include the head term naming the cell class, such as "cells", "cell", or a specific established cell-type noun like "neurons".
Include adjectival descriptors that specify morphology, lineage, taxonomic context, differentiation state, or other type-defining properties when they directly modify the cell term.
Exclude surrounding verbs, determiners, quantifiers, and discourse words unless they are part of the canonical cell-type expression.
Exclude non-type descriptive material that is not part of the cell-type name, such as activity, location, or actions, e.g., in "neurons of crayfish abdominal ganglia", annotate only "neurons".
When a coordinated phrase contains multiple distinct cell types, annotate each cell-type mention separately rather than the whole coordination unless the coordination itself is the named type.
Do not extend the span to include associated substances, markers, processes, or anatomical structures linked to the cell type.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate cell types, not anatomical body parts or tissues; terms like "hand", "joints", "fingers", or "ganglia" are not cell type entities.
Annotate cell types, not diseases or medical conditions; terms like "osteoarthritis" or "degenerative arthritis" are not cell type entities.
Annotate cell types, not general biological entities such as enzymes, proteins, or biochemical activities; for example, "caspases" and "Acetylcholinesterase activity" are not cell type entities.
Annotate mentions referring to a class or type of cells, even when generic or plural, but not mentions of non-biological objects that happen to contain words like "cell" in another sense.

When a phrase mixes a cell type with its location or organism context, annotate only the cell type portion unless the modifier is part of the type itself; for example, "eukaryotic cells" is a cell type, but "neurons of crayfish abdominal ganglia" contributes only "neurons".
Do not annotate broader organism categories or species names by themselves unless they directly form part of the cell-type expression.

characteristic.definition: A word or phrase that describes a quality, trait, property, attribute, or defining feature of a person, object, concept, or system.
include_rules: Annotate adjectives and adjectival phrases that describe a property, such as shape, state, or condition (e.g., 'cylindrical', 'buoyant').
Annotate nouns or noun phrases used to denote traits or features (e.g., 'traits').
Annotate technical or domain-specific phrases when they name a characteristic or functional property (e.g., 'voice production', 'voice conduction').
Annotate both permanent and temporary qualities if they function as descriptors of the subject.
Annotate multiword expressions that together name one characteristic.
Annotate physical, behavioral, functional, and abstract qualities when they are used to describe something.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate the entity being described (the noun that owns the characteristic).
Do not annotate generic verbs, auxiliaries, or sentence structure words used to express description.
Do not annotate pure actions or procedures when they are not presented as qualities or traits.
Do not annotate descriptive context that is not itself the characteristic span.
Do not annotate broad abstract concepts unless they are explicitly used as traits or properties in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest contiguous span that expresses the characteristic itself.
Include the full adjective/noun phrase when it functions as the characteristic (e.g., 'voice production', 'cylindrical', 'buoyant', 'traits').
If a characteristic is expressed by a single word, annotate only that word.
Do not include surrounding articles, prepositions, verbs, or nouns that merely introduce the characteristic.
Do not expand the span to include linked but separate characteristics unless they form one fixed multiword characteristic expression in the sentence.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse characteristic with the thing being described; annotate the property word or property phrase, not the object or entity that has it.

Do not annotate actions, events, or processes unless the phrase is being used as a property/attribute of something (e.g., 'voice production' as a characteristic, not a standalone activity in another context).
Do not annotate general categories or abstract topics when they are not functioning as qualities or traits of an item.
Do not annotate measurements, quantities, or states that are incidental rather than descriptive properties.
When a phrase can be read as either a property or a domain-specific term, choose characteristic only if it directly describes an inherent or observed feature of the subject in the sentence.

chemical substance.definition: A named chemical compound, element, salt, molecular formula, drug, or recognized class of chemical/drug substances mentioned as a material substance.
include_rules: Annotate specific named chemical compounds and substances, such as sugars, salts, solvents, reagents, and molecular compounds.
Annotate chemical formulas and formula-like substance mentions, e.g. "NaCl" and "ZnSO(4)".
Annotate drug names and pharmacological substance classes when they denote the substances themselves, e.g. "SSRIs".
Annotate substance mentions whether written as full names, abbreviations, acronyms, or symbolic formulas.
Annotate the chemical part of modifier constructions when the modifier names a substance, e.g. annotate "sucrose" in "sucrose-induced".
Annotate chemical substances mentioned in experimental, clinical, or biological contexts regardless of whether they are naturally occurring or synthetic.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate people, organizations, places, publications, databases, or technologies.
Do not annotate generic scientific or medical concepts that are not specific substances, such as "emotional distress", "quality of life", or "cancer patients".
Do not annotate broad non-specific material words when they do not identify a particular substance or recognized substance class, e.g. "chemicals" used generically.
Do not annotate preparations, extracts, mixtures, or formulations when the text does not name a specific constituent chemical substance, e.g. "aqueous extract" by itself.
Do not annotate administration context or induced-condition descriptors such as "alcohol-induced" or "sucrose-induced" as a whole; only annotate the chemical part if present.
Do not annotate experimental subjects, biological taxa, or sample descriptors such as "rats", "rabbits", or "ovariectomized rabbits".
Do not annotate brandless intervention descriptions, communication media, or devices such as "telephone", "e-mail", "websites", or "telecommunication technologies".
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that uniquely identifies the chemical substance.
Include the full chemical name, formula, standard abbreviation, or class label when that label itself denotes a substance, e.g. "sucrose", "NaCl", "SSRIs".
Include letters, capitalization, numbers, hyphens, parentheses, and other formula characters only if they are part of the chemical mention itself.
Do not include surrounding dosage, route, preparation, organism, or experimental context words unless they are part of the substance name.
For coordinated mentions, annotate each chemical substance separately when listed as distinct items, e.g. annotate "ZnSO(4)" and "NaCl" separately.
Do not expand abbreviated chemical mentions unless the expanded form appears in the text; annotate the surface form actually written.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, or physiological states as chemical substance, even if chemicals are discussed in relation to them.
Do not annotate organisms, body parts, cells, or anatomical locations as chemical substance, even when they interact with chemicals, e.g. "nerves", "brain", "rats", "rabbits".
Do not annotate treatments, interventions, or procedures unless the mention itself is a substance name; for example, a review of interventions or technologies is not a chemical substance.
When a plural or class abbreviation names a group of compounds or drugs, annotate it as chemical substance if the expression denotes the substances themselves, e.g. "SSRIs".
Do not annotate generic words like "chemicals" unless the annotation policy explicitly allows generic substance category mentions; prefer specific named substances or established substance classes.

Do not confuse food items, media, or vehicles with chemical substance unless the term is used as a named chemical compound, e.g. "sucrose" is a chemical substance but "aqueous extract" is a preparation description, not a specific substance name.

city.definition: A named city, town, or municipality used as a geographic locality reference in text.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of cities, towns, or municipalities that identify a specific populated place.
Annotate a city mention when it appears alone as a location reference, e.g. "Toronto".
Annotate a city mention when it appears with a country, state, or province, but label only the city portion, e.g. "Berlin, Germany" -> "Berlin".

Annotate a city mention when it appears inside a larger place expression that names a broader region, if the city itself is explicitly present, e.g. "Springfield, Missouri Metropolitan Statistical Area" -> "Springfield".
Annotate city names used in locative contexts such as residence, headquarters, origin, destination, or event location, as long as the mention denotes the city itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate countries, states, provinces, counties, or other larger administrative units unless the mention itself is a city.
Do not annotate generic location words or facility/place nouns such as "restaurant", "office", "closet", "kitchens", or "community" when they are not proper city names.
Do not annotate broader geographic or statistical region names such as metropolitan areas when the text refers to a region rather than the city alone.
Do not annotate adjectival or demonym forms like nationality words (e.g., "polish" meaning Polish) when they do not name a city.
Do not annotate biological, technical, legal, or other non-geographic capitalized terms that are unrelated to municipalities.
Do not annotate a city name when it appears only as part of another entity and does not denote the location itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the city name itself: in "Berlin, Germany", label "Berlin" only, not the following country.
In multi-part place expressions, include the city token(s) but exclude larger enclosing regions when they are separate units, as in "Springfield, Missouri" -> annotate "Springfield" only.
If the city name contains multiple words as part of the official city name, include all words of that city name.
Do not include surrounding prepositions, punctuation, or appositive descriptors such as "in", "based in", or "Metropolitan Statistical Area".
When a city is embedded in an organization or facility description, annotate the city only if it explicitly refers to the geographic location, e.g. "Based in Toronto" -> "Toronto".
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish city from country or state/province: annotate the municipal/locality name, not the larger administrative area, even when they appear together.

Distinguish city from metropolitan regions or broader statistical areas: in expressions like "Springfield, Missouri Metropolitan Statistical Area", only the city name is city; the full regional designation is not.
Distinguish city from organizations: if a capitalized token names an institution, company, or group rather than a municipality, do not annotate it as city.
Distinguish city from facilities or venues: words naming a restaurant, office, closet, kitchen, cabinet, or similar place types are not city unless they are actually a municipality name.
Distinguish city from demonyms/adjectival forms: words like nationality or regional adjectives are not city unless they are the proper noun naming the municipality itself.

class.definition: A software class or class-like type mention, including specific class names, abbreviated type names used as class names, and generic object-oriented class role terms when they denote a class in programming context.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of software classes or class-like program types mentioned in text.
Annotate class identifiers that use letters, capitals, and digits, such as bean or component names like "Bean1" and "Bean2", when they refer to software types.
Annotate abbreviated or acronym-based type names used as class mentions, such as "GUI" when the context indicates it is a class.
Annotate generic object-oriented class relation terms when they directly denote a class role, such as "superclass".
Annotate class mentions whether they appear in prose, code-discussion text, or framework/configuration discussion, as long as they refer to a software type.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate entities outside software/type context, such as biological taxa, plant varieties, animals, places, people, or news-caption content.
Do not annotate ordinary nouns that merely describe categories in non-programming domains, even if they resemble classification terms.
Do not annotate programming-related words that are not class/type mentions, such as annotations, methods, resources, references, libraries, or actions.
Do not annotate the literal word "class" when it is only a descriptive suffix after the actual class mention; in "GUI class", only "GUI" is annotated.
Do not annotate modifiers or surrounding phrases that describe a class mention but are not themselves the class/type name.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the class-like type itself, such as "Bean2", "Bean1", "superclass", or "GUI".
Do not include surrounding descriptive words, determiners, or role words unless they are part of the class mention itself; for example, in "GUI class", annotate only "GUI".
When a generic OO type term is used alone to denote a class relationship or class category, annotate that term alone, e.g. "superclass".
Include alphanumeric class identifiers exactly as written, preserving case and digits, e.g. "Bean2".
Do not extend the span to include punctuation or nearby code-context words such as "class", "resource", "reference", or "Autowired" unless those tokens are themselves the class mention.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish class from object instances or resources: annotate the type name of the software construct, not the thing being used or referenced by the code.
Distinguish class from framework features or annotations: terms like "Autowired" are not classes unless the text clearly uses them as class names.
Distinguish class from variables, bean references, or properties: in a phrase like "Bean2 reference into Bean1", annotate "Bean2" and "Bean1" as class mentions, not the word "reference".

Distinguish class from generic technical vocabulary: annotate generic OO terms such as "superclass" only when they denote a class/category in a programming context, not when similar words are used non-technically.

Distinguish class from biological or ordinary-language classifications: words like "lotus", species names, or other scientific/common nouns are not software classes even if they describe kinds or categories.

clothing.definition: Garments, footwear, and worn apparel items that a person puts on the body, including specific article names and garment subtypes.
include_rules: Annotate nouns and noun phrases that directly name articles of clothing or footwear.
Include common everyday garments such as shirts, pants, socks, shoes, coats, and tops.
Include specialized or functional apparel such as "lab coat."
Include footwear as clothing, as shown by "hiking shoes" and "shoes."
Include garment names with descriptive modifiers that specify the item type, material, or use, such as "flannel shirt" and "hiking shoes."
Include both singular and plural mentions of clothing items.
Include swimwear and similar apparel categories, such as "bikini."
exclude_rules: Do not annotate grooming, hygiene, or appearance terms that are not wearable items, such as "clean shave" or "well groomed."
Do not annotate abstract fashion concepts, styling language, or design terminology when they do not themselves name a garment, such as "style" or "design."
Do not annotate non-clothing objects, technical terms, or software-related words, such as "PHP," "MODAL," "FORM," "MYSQL," "REST API," or "zone files."
Do not annotate body parts, actions involving clothing, or states of being dressed/undressed; only the clothing item itself should be tagged.
Do not annotate broad contextual text about data, applications, or other domains unrelated to wearable apparel.
Do not annotate adjectives alone unless they are part of a full clothing name.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the clothing item.
Include modifiers that are part of the garment name or subtype, such as material, style, or function descriptors: "hiking shoes," "flannel shirt," "lab coat."
Include plural and singular garment nouns exactly as written: "pants," "socks," "tops."
Do not include surrounding verbs, determiners, or list punctuation unless they are part of the clothing name.
Do not extend the span to include unrelated appearance descriptions or grooming terms near the clothing mention, such as "clean" in "clean shave" or "well groomed."
When a style word itself refers to a garment category, annotate it, as in "bikini"; when it only describes another garment, annotate only the actual garment term if separately mentioned.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate wearable garments and footwear, not grooming or body-condition terms; for example, "clean shave" and "well groomed" are not clothing.
Annotate clothing items, not software, data, forms, APIs, or other non-wearable objects, even if the word appears in uppercase or technical text.
Annotate garment categories rather than general fashion descriptions; for example, "traditional modern style" is not clothing, but "bikini" is.
Annotate clothing items even when they indicate occupation or activity through the garment name, such as "lab coat" or "hiking shoes"; these remain clothing, not roles or events.
If a phrase refers to a design or style applied to a garment, distinguish the garment from the abstract design wording: "tops" and "bikini" are clothing, while "design" and "style" are not.

club.definition: A named club, team, membership group, or nightclub/entertainment venue referred to by a specific proper name.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of sports clubs or teams (e.g., "Chelsea").
Annotate proper names of membership or youth clubs (e.g., "Chico's Kids Club").
Annotate proper names of nightlife or entertainment clubs/club venues when used as the establishment's name (e.g., "Crazy Horse III").
Include named clubs whether the context is sports performance, promotion, membership signup, or venue reference, as long as the mention denotes the club entity itself.
Annotate shortened or commonly used club names when they function as the entity's name in context.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic common-noun uses of club or team that do not give a specific proper name.
Do not annotate brands, products, inventions, technical devices, media formats, or business names unless the mention specifically denotes a club entity.
Do not annotate names of sponsors, presenters, leagues, cities, or venues that are mentioned in connection with a club but are not themselves the club mention being referred to.
Do not annotate purely descriptive phrases for groups without a stable proper name.
Do not annotate person names, even if they are members, players, or owners of a club.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full official or commonly used name of the club as it appears in text.

Include integral descriptors that are part of the name, such as possessives, numerals, and internal category words like "Club" when they belong to the title (e.g., "Chico's Kids Club", "Crazy Horse III").
Do not extend the span to include surrounding event, time, location, or action words that merely modify the mention (e.g., in "Chelsea goals at Manchester City on Sunday", annotate only "Chelsea").
Exclude trailing sponsorship or presentation phrases unless they are clearly part of the club's name (e.g., annotate "Chico's Kids Club" but not "presented by Southwest Dairy Farmers").
Do not include determiners or generic nouns before the name unless they are part of the official title.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish club from person names: annotate the organization/venue/group name, not individuals associated with it (e.g., "Lampard" is not a club).

Distinguish club from brand or product names: commercial brands and products are not clubs unless they denote a membership organization or named club entity (e.g., "PUMA X Hello Kitty Backpack" is not a club).
Distinguish club from facilities or transport locations when the term refers only to a place rather than a named club entity; annotate only when the name denotes the club/establishment itself.
Distinguish club from generic activity groups: annotate named clubs with a specific title, not generic mentions like "the club", "a kids club", or "football clubs" without a unique name.

Distinguish club from broader organizations or sponsors mentioned alongside the club; only the club entity itself should be tagged, not presenting or sponsoring organizations unless they are themselves the club name.

code.definition: A symbolic identifier, access string, error/diagnostic value, or explicitly named code phrase used to classify, unlock, reference, or identify something as a code.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of codes as noun phrases, especially when modified by a purpose or domain, such as "access code" or "project code".
Annotate literal code values made of letters, numbers, and optional separators when context identifies them as a code, such as "P0135" or an entered access string.
Annotate diagnostic, error, or reference codes when they serve as standardized identifiers for a condition, component, or event.
Annotate code mentions whether they are generic code types or specific code tokens, as long as the text semantically refers to a code.
Annotate codes used for access, project identification, system lookup, or standardized classification if they are explicitly mentioned in the text.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary uses of the word "code" when it refers broadly to software source, coding activity, or implementation rather than a specific code identifier or code type.
Do not annotate technical terms, abbreviations, gene/protein names, assay names, or protocol names that are not functioning as codes.
Do not annotate generic alphanumeric strings unless the context indicates they are a code, such as an access code, project code, or diagnostic/error code.
Do not annotate descriptive explanations surrounding a code, such as "for the heating element inside the O2 sensor"; only the code mention itself qualifies.
Do not annotate UI labels, template block names, function names, or markup/control strings unless they are explicitly presented as codes.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the code itself or the explicit code-designating noun phrase.
Include the alphanumeric token when it is the code value, e.g. "P0135".
Include the head noun "code" together with its immediate modifier when the text refers to a type of code rather than giving the literal value, e.g. "project code", "access code".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding verbs, determiners, punctuation, or instruction text such as "enter:", "the", or trailing sentence punctuation.
If a code value contains internal hyphens that are part of the written identifier, include them; exclude trailing punctuation that is not part of the code format.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate general software or programming references unless they denote a code identifier or a noun phrase explicitly meaning a code; ordinary mentions of code authoring, source code, widgets, blocks, HTML, or form types are not this entity type.

Do not annotate biological, chemical, or technical abbreviations and assay names merely because they contain capital letters or hyphens, such as "TGF-beta", "LTBP", "APAAP", or "RT-PCR"; these are domain terms, not codes.
Distinguish code from product names, project names, and component names: annotate only the code label or code value, not the named object associated with it.
Distinguish code from error descriptions or diagnoses: annotate the identifier itself (e.g. "P0135"), not the explanatory text describing what the code means.

Distinguish code from passwords or keys only when the text explicitly presents them as a code or as a coded identifier; generic numbers, IDs, or strings without such semantics should not be annotated.

color.definition: A word or phrase that explicitly denotes a visual color, hue, shade, or color combination of something.
include_rules: Annotate basic color words used as colors, such as "yellow".
Annotate multiword shade expressions that specify a particular hue, such as "golden yellow".
Annotate hyphenated or compound color expressions, such as "orange-yellow".
Annotate color phrases that include the colored feature when the phrase itself expresses the color description in context, such as "red trim".
Annotate explicit mentions of hue, tint, or shade whether standalone or modifying another element, as long as the span denotes color.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that are not used to denote color in context.
Do not annotate technical, legal, medical, or numerical expressions even if they contain words that could resemble color terms.
Do not annotate generic descriptive adjectives unless they directly express a color or shade.
Do not annotate the names of objects, clothing items, body parts, or plants unless the annotated span is specifically the color expression.
Do not annotate full noun phrases when only the color component is intended.
Do not annotate conjunctions, punctuation, or surrounding list structure unless they are internal to the color phrase.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal contiguous span that expresses the color description.
Include modifiers that are part of the color name itself, such as shade/intensity words or compound color formation: "golden yellow", "orange-yellow".
Include attached color-bearing descriptors when they form the described color expression in context, as in "red trim".
Do not extend the span to the colored object unless that noun is part of the color expression; for example, annotate "red trim" but not "black skooter skirt" in the example provided.
Preserve hyphenated color compounds as a single span: "orange-yellow".
When colors are listed separately, annotate each color term separately rather than the whole list.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate objects, garments, flowers, or other physical items that merely have a color; only annotate the color expression itself.
Do not confuse color with numbers, dates, legal citations, medical terminology, or other non-visual descriptive terms appearing in technical text.
If a word can function as both an object/material and a color, annotate it only when it clearly denotes hue or shade in context.
Shade phrases that describe color are included under color, but broader descriptive language without an explicit color term is not.
When a color term modifies a part or design element, annotate the full color phrase only if that full phrase is how the color is expressed, e.g. "red trim".

column.definition: A column is a database or table field name used to identify one attribute/slot in structured data.
include_rules: Annotate explicit field/column identifiers mentioned in text.
Annotate simple column names such as status, name, var1, var2, var3 when they are used as field labels or schema attributes.
Annotate compound identifiers that serve as column names, including names with underscores or numeric suffixes.
Annotate column names mentioned in instructions, questions, examples, or error descriptions when they refer to a database/table field.
Annotate a column name whether it is discussed directly or appears inside code-like text, as long as it is the field identifier itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate values stored in a column; annotate the column name, not its contents.
Do not annotate generic schema-related words such as field, data, table, record, profile, module, or variable when they are not the actual column identifier.
Do not annotate complete code expressions or statements; extract only the column identifier within them if present.
Do not annotate descriptive phrases that merely mention a column indirectly without naming it.
Do not annotate multiple adjacent identifiers unless they together form a single column name.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the column itself, including the column label or identifier and excluding surrounding descriptive text.
If a column is referenced by a bare word or identifier (e.g., status, name, var1), annotate exactly that token or token sequence.
If the column name contains underscores, numbers, or camel-case parts, keep the full identifier as one span.
Do not include articles, prepositions, punctuation, quotes, parentheses, or surrounding explanatory phrases.

If the text includes a qualified reference like table.column or profile_first_name, annotate only the column name part that functions as the column identifier, not the table/module prefix unless it is part of the identifier itself.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse column names with row values, variable names in code, or general nouns unless the noun is explicitly functioning as a database field/column label in context.
Do not annotate database tables, files, modules, or objects; only annotate the field/column identifier itself.
If a term appears as part of code, function names, or API calls and is not the column identifier being referenced, exclude it.

When a name could be a common noun (e.g., name, status), annotate it only when it clearly refers to a field/column in the data/table context, not when it refers to a general concept or user attribute outside schema context.

command.definition: A command is the name of a software, shell, editor, or system action that can be invoked directly by a user, typically in a command line, terminal, interactive tool, or command-oriented interface.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of executable or invocable commands used in computing contexts.
Include command names mentioned with lexical cues such as "command", "man", "using", "run", "invoke", or similar command-reference context.
Include command names from command-line environments, shells, editors, or command-driven software interfaces.
Include command names even when embedded in explanatory prose or documentation references, as long as the context identifies them as commands.
Include abbreviated or stylized command tokens such as lowercase utility names (e.g. "man", "findstr") and command-like subcomponents identified by context (e.g. "log").
exclude_rules: Exclude words that are not software/system commands in context, even if they resemble command names.
Exclude programming language keywords, operators, variables, function names, or code identifiers unless the sentence explicitly treats them as commands.
Exclude general product names, hardware names, vehicles, engines, tools, and physical equipment mentions.
Exclude command arguments, flags, options, search strings, file names, paths, and output text associated with a command.
Exclude generic references such as "command", "tool", "option", "button", or "menu" when no specific command name is given.
Exclude section headings, prose terms, scientific terminology, gene names, and other non-command technical strings.
Exclude full descriptive phrases when only a single token is the command name.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the command name itself, not surrounding trigger words such as "command", "man page for", "using", or articles/determiners.

When a command is mentioned as part of a help invocation or documentation reference, mark only the executable/subcommand token that names the command, e.g. in "man while" annotate "man", not the full phrase.

If the surface text contains a larger identifier but only one part functions as the command reference in context, annotate only that command-like part, e.g. annotate "log" in "magit-log-wash-rev" when the sentence indicates it refers to a Magit command.
Do not include punctuation, parentheses, quotes, question marks, or surrounding formatting characters unless they are part of the command token itself.
Prefer the minimal span that uniquely names the command as used in context; exclude arguments, options, file names, paths, and descriptive modifiers.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate software, tools, packages, frameworks, or applications unless the text specifically refers to a runnable command within them. For example, Magit is a tool, while "log" is the command mention.

Do not annotate ordinary English verbs or nouns that happen to match command names unless the context clearly indicates command usage, such as references to a "command", command-line searching, help pages, or a specific UI/CLI action.
Do not annotate shell syntax elements, keywords, or control structures unless they are explicitly presented as commands. A word like "while" in programming or prose is not a command here.
Do not annotate machine operations or domain actions such as "drill" when they describe physical activity or equipment rather than a software/system command.
Do not annotate documentation nouns like "man page" or interface labels like "Tools/Options"; only the command name invoked or referenced should be labeled.

commodity.definition: A commodity is a tradable good or standardized asset, especially a raw material, natural resource, metal, energy product, agricultural product, or similar market-traded item, including common references to financial commodities such as stock.
include_rules: Annotate nouns that denote raw materials or natural resources traded in markets, such as metals and energy products: "copper", "oil".
Annotate generic material terms when they clearly refer to a tradable substance or commodity class, such as "metal" in a pricing context.
Annotate financial asset terms when used as the traded item itself, such as "stock".
Include singular, plural, and generic common-noun mentions of commodities when the sentence indicates economic, trading, reserve, or pricing relevance.
Annotate commodity mentions whether they refer to the physical substance or the market-traded good, as long as the mention denotes the commodity itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words from unrelated technical, medical, calendar, software, or location text that do not denote tradable goods.
Do not annotate organizations, brands, products, or devices unless the mention itself is the commodity being traded.
Do not annotate abstract economic concepts, market activities, or measurements such as "price", "reserves", "shares" alone, "funds", or "research".
Do not annotate currencies, monetary amounts, or units of trade unless the asset name itself is present.
Do not annotate descriptive materials when they are only adjectival or incidental and not referred to as a market good.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest span that names the commodity itself, usually the head noun: "copper", "oil", "metal", "stock".
Exclude quantity, ownership, pricing, and trading modifiers unless they are part of the commodity name: in "four thousand shares of the stock", annotate only "stock".
Do not include surrounding verbs or event language such as "reserves", "price", "shares", "per share", "buy", or "sell" when they are not part of the commodity mention.
For coordinated commodities, annotate each commodity mention separately if they refer to distinct items.
Include common noun forms when they clearly denote a tradable material or financial commodity, even if not capitalized.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a commodity with a company, producer, or market actor; annotate the traded good itself, not the organization associated with it.
Do not confuse a commodity with a financial event or transaction term. In trading contexts, annotate the asset noun such as "stock", but not action words or amounts.

Do not confuse a commodity with a location, date, software product, or technical term appearing in unrelated text; only items denoting tradable raw materials, energy resources, metals, agricultural goods, or standardized financial assets qualify.
Generic economic concepts like "price", "market", "reserves", or "funds" are not commodities unless the term itself names the thing being traded.
When a broad material term refers to the substance as an economic good, it is a commodity; when the same word is used only descriptively without denoting a tradable good, do not annotate it.

communication.definition: A word or phrase denoting a human or interpersonal act, medium, exchange, consultation, or content of communication, including direct message forms and communicative interaction concepts.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote interpersonal exchange or discussion, such as "dialogue."
Annotate explicit message forms or transmitted content units, such as "text messages."
Annotate consultation or contact events conducted through a communication medium, such as "telephone consultation."
Annotate terms referring to communicated content or information being exchanged, when used as the object of communication, such as "information."
Annotate interpersonal communicative or relational concepts when they are presented as core components of human exchange, as in the provided example with "trust."
Include both spoken and written/electronic communication expressions when the phrase names the communicative act, channel, or content.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate commercial, institutional, or generic service mentions when they refer to a business offering or industry rather than a specific communicative act, e.g. "translation service" in a business-services discussion.
Do not annotate documents, files, reports, declarations, portfolios, or similar artifacts when they are mentioned as objects, records, or paperwork rather than as communication events.
Do not annotate general activities, processes, or relationships that are not specifically communicative in meaning.
Do not annotate biological, chemical, or technical interactions such as molecular binding, signaling pathways, or immune checkpoint interactions.
Do not annotate verbs alone unless they are part of a fixed noun phrase naming a communication type; for example, exclude "send" and annotate only "text messages".

Do not annotate broad contextual nouns like "business," "experience," "market," or "service suppliers" that may co-occur with communication-related topics but are not themselves communication entities.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the communication act, exchange, or communicative content, excluding surrounding verbs, determiners, and prepositions when they are not part of the fixed expression.
Include modifiers only when they are integral to the communication expression and change its type, as in "text messages" or "telephone consultation".

For multiword expressions, include the full phrase naming the communication event or medium, e.g. "telephone consultation" rather than only "consultation" when the medium is explicitly part of the named communication.
When a noun denotes communicated content in context, annotate just that noun or noun phrase, e.g. "information".

Do not extend the span to include participants, locations, times, or purposes, e.g. annotate "text messages" not "send text messages" and "dialogue" not "authentic dialogue" unless the modifier is necessary to the expression’s identity.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate communication when the expression denotes an exchange of messages, consultation, dialogue, or communicative content; do not annotate entities that are merely organizations, people, places, or biological/technical objects appearing in the same sentence.

Distinguish communication from services or industries: annotate a concrete communicative act or interaction such as "telephone consultation," but not business offerings or sectors such as "translation service" when mentioned as a commercial service category rather than a specific communicative exchange.

Distinguish communication from documents or records: only annotate them if the context treats them as communicative content being exchanged; do not annotate lists of business or legal documents such as reports or declarations when they are referenced as artifacts rather than acts of communication.

Distinguish communication from abstract social qualities: annotate terms like "trust" only when the annotation scheme treats them as part of interpersonal communicative exchange, as shown in the examples; do not automatically annotate all abstract nouns unless they function in this communicative/interpersonal sense.

Distinguish communication from biological signaling or molecular binding: terms describing cellular pathways, ligands, receptors, or molecular interactions are not communication entities in this schema.

competition.definition: A competition is a named or contextually specific organized contest, tournament, league, championship, cup, or similar event structure in which individuals or teams compete.
include_rules: Annotate named competitions such as championships, leagues, cups, tournaments, opens, series, and similar organized contests.
Annotate both fully named competitions and contextually specific common-noun references when they clearly refer to a particular competition being contested or won, e.g. "league".
Include competition names at any scale, including world, state, national, regional, or local contests.
Annotate pluralized official names when they denote the competition title, e.g. "World Championships".
Include modified competition names that identify the domain or type of contest, e.g. "Climbing World Championships".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general language about competing, competition in the abstract, or non-event uses of related words.
Do not annotate sports, disciplines, or game types by themselves unless the phrase specifically names a competition.
Do not annotate participant entities such as teams, clubs, players, or governing bodies.
Do not annotate locations, dates, seasons, or results unless they are part of the competition name itself.
Do not annotate descriptive complements following the competition name that indicate subject matter or sport externally, such as "in Baseball" in "State Championship in Baseball".
Do not annotate unrelated scientific or technical terms that resemble entity-like noun phrases, as in the negative examples involving "S-parameter" or "monoamine transporter".
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the competition itself, e.g. "Climbing World Championships" and "State Championship".
Include core competition designators such as "Championship", "Championships", "Cup", "League", "Tournament", "Open", "Series", or similar when they are part of the competition name.
Include modifying words that specify the competition level, domain, or organizer when they are part of the name, e.g. "Climbing" in "Climbing World Championships" and "State" in "State Championship".

Do not include surrounding prepositions, determiners, or verbs unless they are part of the official or referential competition name; for example, annotate "league" in "win the league" without "the" or "win".
Do not extend the span to include the sport or event introduced outside the competition name, e.g. in "State Championship in Baseball", annotate only "State Championship" and exclude "in Baseball".
When a common noun is used referentially to denote a specific competition in context, annotate only that noun phrase, e.g. "league".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a competition with a sport or activity: annotate the contest entity ("Climbing World Championships", "State Championship", "league"), not the sport itself such as "Climbing" or "Baseball" when mentioned separately.
Do not confuse a competition with an organization or team. In "win the league with Arsenal", annotate "league" as the competition and not "Arsenal", which is a participant.

Do not confuse a competition with an award, title, or outcome. The entity is the event/contest structure being won, not the act of winning or the championship title as an achievement abstracted from the event.

Do not annotate technical, scientific, or product uses of words like "competition", "league", "series", or especially "championship"-like patterns unless they denote a sports, game, or contest event; terms such as "S-parameter" or "monoamine transporter" are unrelated.

If a phrase names a season, match, or single game rather than the larger organized contest, annotate it only if it clearly functions as the competition name itself; otherwise prefer the higher-level competition entity.

component.definition: A component is a physical, mechanical, electrical, or software part/module that serves as a constituent element of a larger device, machine, application, or system.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote a distinct part of a larger physical mechanism or device, such as mechanical pieces like "latch".
Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote a functional physical part used within a system or assembly, such as "cable".
Annotate software/application structural parts or modules when they are referred to as constituent elements of a program or framework, such as "views".
Include both singular and plural mentions when they refer to component instances or component types.
Annotate a component whether it is referenced generically or as a specific instance, as long as it denotes a part/module rather than the whole system.
Annotate standalone component mentions even when the larger system they belong to is only implicit from context.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate whole systems, products, applications, or devices when the term refers to the complete artifact rather than one of its parts.
Do not annotate generic technical actions, processes, or events such as deployment, wrapping text, rendering, or centering.
Do not annotate substances, chemicals, or materials unless they are explicitly named as a structural part of a larger object.
Do not annotate people, organizations, governments, councils, or communities.
Do not annotate code expressions, method calls, variable names, or configuration snippets when they denote executable syntax rather than a system part.
Do not annotate broad textual or content mentions such as text, questions, examples, websites, or statements unless they explicitly denote a software component.
Do not annotate contextual descriptors around a component, such as ownership, position, or function words, if the component noun alone expresses the entity.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the component itself, usually the head noun alone: "latch", "cable", "views".

Include modifiers only when they are necessary to identify the component as a conventional unit or fixed compound name; otherwise exclude descriptive words such as material, state, direction, or function.
Exclude determiners and quantifiers such as "a", "the", "one of my" from the span.
Exclude surrounding action words, prepositions, and relational context, e.g. annotate "cable" not "pulling on a cable".
For multiword component names, include all words that are part of the component name, but exclude attached explanations in parentheses or appositives.
When a plural noun refers to multiple components of the same kind, annotate the plural component term itself, e.g. "views".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate substances, materials, or consumables as components; for example, "fluoride" and "water" are not components because they are not parts/modules of a larger device, system, or software structure in the sentence.
Do not annotate organizations, people, or governing bodies even when they influence a system; these are not internal parts of an artifact or software system.
Distinguish components from actions or operations: annotate the thing being used or acted upon ("cable"), not the operation ("deploy", "pulling").
Distinguish components from documents, statements, or questions; textual content is not a component unless it explicitly names a structural software/interface part such as "views".

Distinguish components from tools or libraries named only as platforms or APIs when the sentence refers to using them generally rather than to one of their internal parts; e.g. do not annotate "pygame.font.Font()" as a component in the negative example context.

A component must function as a part, module, mechanism, or structural element within a larger physical or software system; if the noun refers to the whole system, an external dependency, or generic text, do not annotate it as component.

compound.definition: A chemical substance or material entity, including specific compounds, chemical formulas, polysaccharides, and named natural products, mentioned as the substance itself rather than as a general topic, dosage form, or treatment description.
include_rules: Annotate names of specific chemical compounds or materials, whether expressed as a full name, chemical class phrase, or formula.
Annotate chemical formulas and composition-style identifiers used as material names, e.g. "Y2SiO5".
Annotate multiword compound names that include class and specific designation, e.g. "Triterpene Ginsenoside Compound K".
Annotate substance class mentions when they clearly denote a chemical material rather than a topic, e.g. "bacterial polysaccharides".
Annotate compounds mentioned in scientific titles, methods, production contexts, and biological property studies as long as the span denotes the substance itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-chemical topics, concepts, or everyday nouns, e.g. "climate change", "football", or "book".
Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, patient conditions, or medical procedures, even when a compound is discussed in the same sentence.
Do not annotate treatment descriptions, routes, or dosage forms that include a substance name but primarily denote a product or therapy expression, e.g. "5% Lidocaine medicated plaster".
Do not annotate concentration expressions, percentages, or numeric modifiers by themselves, e.g. "5%" alone.
Do not annotate highly generic words such as "drug treatments", "systemic drugs", or "therapy" when no specific compound is named.
Do not annotate adjectives or contextual words that merely describe source, use, or effect unless they are part of the established compound phrase.
boundary_rules: Select the minimal contiguous span that names the compound and no more.
Include chemical formulas and symbol strings exactly as written, including capitalization, numbers, plus/minus signs, and punctuation that are part of the formula or material name, e.g. "Y2SiO5".
Include descriptive chemical class words when they are part of the named compound mention, e.g. "bacterial polysaccharides" and "Triterpene Ginsenoside Compound K".

Exclude surrounding verbs, prepositions, determiners, dosage markers, and treatment descriptors that are not part of the compound name, e.g. do not extend a span to include "treatment with" or "medicated plaster" when only the active substance is present.
Do not split coordinated multiword compound names if they form one continuous named mention; however, annotate only the specific compound phrase actually present in the text.

Exclude concentration or percentage prefixes/suffixes unless the annotation scheme explicitly treats the concentration as part of the chemical mention; based on the examples, "5%" is not part of the compound span.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the chemical substance/material itself, not the disease, symptom, biological process, or treatment outcome mentioned around it.

A formulation, dosage form, or therapeutic product description is not a compound unless the span itself names the chemical substance; for example, "5% Lidocaine medicated plaster" is not annotated as a compound mention in these examples.

Generic scientific topics or common nouns are not compounds unless they denote a chemical class or substance, e.g. "climate change" and "football" are not compounds, while "bacterial polysaccharides" is.
Do not confuse organism names, cell types, or species context with the compound; in "bacterial polysaccharides", annotate only the substance phrase, not any separate organism mention outside it.
Material formulas and specific named natural products should be annotated as compounds even when they appear in titles or production contexts, e.g. "Y2SiO5" and "Triterpene Ginsenoside Compound K".

concept.definition: An abstract, general, or category-level notion expressed as a noun phrase, including methods, study types, functions, roles, benefits, experiences, or other non-concrete ideas discussed as conceptual entities rather than specific named objects or events.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that denote abstract ideas, methods, strategies, study designs, functional mechanisms, roles, or categories.
Annotate conceptual labels for scientific or technical mechanisms, even when expressed with specialized terminology (e.g., "RNA thermometers").
Annotate phrases describing a type of study or investigation when the phrase names the study design/category rather than a specific experiment (e.g., "controlled screening studies").
Annotate abstract benefit/effect/function expressions when they are presented as discussable entities (e.g., "benefit of the screening method").
Annotate role-like or status-like conceptual expressions that describe an organizational or evaluative category rather than a named place or institution (e.g., "beta test site").
Include multiword concept mentions when all words jointly define the conceptual meaning.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate specific named laws, acts, programs, protocols, mandates, software packages, institutions, or government bodies, even when they relate to a conceptual topic (e.g., a screening act or software name).
Do not annotate concrete biomedical entities such as proteins, lipoproteins, transferrin, globulins, serum, interstitial liquid, tumors, aneurysms, or anatomical measurements.
Do not annotate generic activities or events when they are mentioned only as part of narrative description rather than as a conceptual noun phrase to be discussed in itself.
Do not annotate highly generic field terms such as "study", "practice", "coverage guideline", or "screening" unless the full phrase denotes a distinct conceptual category or abstract notion.
Do not annotate quantitative results, patient groups, dates, percentages, or procedural details.
Do not annotate descriptive scientific statements about growth, increase, decrease, correlation, or variation unless a bounded noun phrase clearly denotes a reusable abstract concept.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase that names the concept, including modifiers that are part of the concept label, such as material/type adjectives or evaluative descriptors when they define the concept itself (e.g., "RNA thermometers", "controlled screening studies", "benefit of the screening method").
Include determiners or prepositional complements only when they are integral to the concept expression, especially in abstract relational concepts such as "benefit of the screening method".

Exclude surrounding verbs, auxiliaries, and event framing language that merely states what happened to the concept (e.g., in "translation ... is controlled by RNA thermometers", annotate only "RNA thermometers").
Do not extend the span to include purpose, outcome, or contextual clauses outside the core concept noun phrase.
When a concept appears inside a larger mention, select the smallest span that still fully expresses the intended abstract/general idea.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate abstract or general ideas, methods, models, roles, study types, or functional categories as concept; do not confuse them with concrete physical objects or specific named entities.

If a phrase names a study design or methodological category in a generic sense, annotate it as concept (e.g., "controlled screening studies"); if it refers mainly to a specific program, act, organization, or software package, do not annotate it as concept.
If a phrase expresses an abstract benefit, function, experience, or role, annotate it as concept (e.g., "benefit of the screening method", "beta test site").

Do not annotate disease names, body substances, proteins, anatomical findings, or measured clinical conditions merely because they are scientifically important; these are domain entities rather than concept mentions in this schema.
Do not annotate acronyms or titles of laws/programs when they denote specific named initiatives rather than a general conceptual category.

condition.definition: A named medical or clinical condition affecting a person, including diseases, syndromes, cancers, pathological states, and symptoms when expressed as a condition mention.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of medical diseases and disorders, such as infectious, oncologic, or systemic conditions, e.g. "sepsis" and "lung adenocarcinoma".
Annotate named syndromes and pathological states, e.g. "multiorgan dysfunction syndrome".
Annotate symptom or pain-condition mentions when they are expressed as clinical conditions, e.g. "referred pain".
Annotate condition mentions whether they are acute, chronic, severe, localized, or systemic, as long as the span names the condition itself.

Annotate the condition term even when it appears inside a larger noun phrase describing patients, cohorts, or effectiveness of treatment, e.g. annotate only the condition within "lung adenocarcinoma patients".
Annotate both primary diseases and secondary complications when each is explicitly named as a condition mention in the text.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate nonmedical conditions, technical states, manufacturing problems, or mechanical configurations, such as text about automobiles, tanks, or assembly processes.
Do not annotate generic references to a person's state when no specific medical condition is named, such as "his condition".

Do not annotate descriptive clauses about impairment or abnormality if they do not provide a discrete condition name, such as "eye muscles never fully developed" or "impossible for him to see clearly".
Do not annotate legal, social, or administrative status expressions, such as "legally blind", when they function as classification/status language rather than a named medical diagnosis in context.
Do not annotate patients, families, doctors, or other person mentions, even if they are closely associated with a condition.
Do not annotate treatments, devices, technologies, insurance matters, fundraising, or costs, even when introduced because of a medical problem.

Do not annotate abbreviations or shorthand unless they are clearly used as standalone condition names in the text; if the text does not establish the abbreviation as the condition mention, leave it unannotated.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the disorder, disease, syndrome, cancer, or symptom, e.g. "sepsis", "multiorgan dysfunction syndrome", "lung adenocarcinoma", "referred pain".
Exclude severity, degree, temporality, and other modifiers that are not part of the canonical condition name, e.g. in "Severe sepsis" annotate only "sepsis".
Include disease-type head nouns when they are part of the name, such as "syndrome" in "multiorgan dysfunction syndrome" and "adenocarcinoma" in "lung adenocarcinoma".
Include anatomical or descriptive modifiers only when they specify the condition itself, e.g. include "lung" in "lung adenocarcinoma" and "referred" in "referred pain".

Do not extend the span to include surrounding patient/group words, abbreviations not clearly used as condition names, treatments, or procedural context, e.g. annotate "lung adenocarcinoma" but not "lung adenocarcinoma patients".
Do not annotate generic trailing references such as "condition" when they merely refer back to an unnamed or previously described state rather than explicitly naming a medical condition.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate medical conditions themselves, not the people who have them: choose "lung adenocarcinoma" but not "lung adenocarcinoma patients".
Do not annotate devices, treatments, or interventions even when discussed in relation to a condition, such as special glasses or technology used to improve vision.

Do not annotate body parts, physiological traits, or developmental descriptions unless they explicitly form a named condition mention; descriptive text like "eye muscles never fully developed" is not a condition mention here.
Do not annotate broad problem, issue, or experience words unless they explicitly denote a medical condition; for example, "challenge", "issue", and generic "condition" are not condition entities.

A condition mention may be a disease, syndrome, malignancy, or symptom/pain state; symptoms such as "referred pain" are included when they denote a clinical state rather than a momentary nonmedical experience.
Do not annotate nonmedical technical or mechanical states in other domains, even if they contain words like "gas", "assembly", or "installation"; only human or animal health conditions qualify.

configuration.definition: A configuration entity is a named system, software, or application setting, option, parameter, or collection of settings that can be specified or changed to control behavior.
include_rules: Annotate named technical settings, options, parameters, or categories of settings that control the behavior of a system, application, service, or component.
Include operating-system or runtime configuration terms such as "run level" when they refer to a controllable system state/setting.
Include individual configurable network/application parameters such as "port" when mentioned as something that can be changed or set.
Include scoped setting groups such as "MySQL settings" when the phrase names a set of configuration options for a specific product or subsystem.
Annotate configuration mentions even when they appear in instructional or procedural text, as long as the span denotes the setting itself rather than the action.
