Include both singular parameter names and plural/grouped setting names when they clearly refer to configurable technical properties.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic uses of words like "level", "port", or "settings" when they do not refer to an actual configurable technical parameter.
Do not annotate values or states assigned to a setting, such as numeric values, modes, or statuses, unless the value expression itself is the established name of the configuration item.
Do not annotate commands, executables, software products, services, or tools unless the mention itself names a configuration setting.
Do not annotate configuration files, environment files, file paths, directories, or code locations where settings are stored.
Do not annotate broad procedural descriptions, actions, or events of configuring something; only the setting/parameter name should be labeled.
Do not annotate non-technical "levels" or similar terms used for curriculum stages, experience scales, complexity ratings, or other conceptual classifications.
Do not annotate physical arrangement descriptions, hardware/device structures, or mechanical positions/opening angles as configuration.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the configurable setting or parameter, such as "run level", "port", or "MySQL settings".
Include modifiers only when they are part of the setting name or scope, e.g. include system/application qualifiers in "MySQL settings".
Do not include surrounding verbs or action words such as "change", "configure", or "serves".

Do not include source/location expressions that merely indicate where configuration is stored or applied, such as filenames, paths, or prepositional phrases like "in .env and config/database.php files".
When a generic head noun is used as the actual parameter name in technical context, annotate just that noun, e.g. "port".
Do not extend the span to include values, states, or ranges unless they are part of the parameter name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the configuration item itself, not the software product or tool associated with it; in "Ember CLI serves the app", "port" is configuration, while "Ember CLI" is a separate software name.

Do not confuse configuration with ordinary uses of similar words that denote rank, amount, or stage rather than a tunable setting; "level" in curriculum or experience descriptions is not configuration, but "run level" in a system administration context is.
Do not annotate physical arrangements, device structures, or mechanical setups as configuration; descriptions of how a door-holding device is arranged are not software/system settings.

A configuration entity is the parameter category being set, not the file, command, or environment in which it is edited. For example, annotate "MySQL settings" but not ".env" or "config/database.php" as configuration.
If the text describes changing, configuring, or setting something, annotate only the named option being changed, not the whole event or instruction.

constant.definition: A constant is a symbol, identifier, or literal used to denote a fixed, predefined value in code, mathematics, or formal scientific notation.
include_rules: Annotate named immutable values in code, especially identifiers introduced or used as constants, such as uppercase underscore-separated names.

Annotate symbolic parameter names used as fixed constants in scientific or mathematical writing, including letter-number combinations like "K5" and "K9" when they denote specific fixed parameters/constants.
Annotate mathematical constants expressed as standalone symbols or numerals when they serve as fixed values within formal notation, such as "1" in a mathematical expression.
Annotate constants whether they appear in declarations, assignments, equations, or descriptive scientific statements, as long as the expression denotes a fixed predefined value.
Include both alphabetic and numeric parts when they form a single constant name.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary words, product names, website text, or narrative prose that do not denote a fixed predefined value.
Do not annotate numeric expressions that merely state measurements, dosages, dates, years, counts, ranges, or percentages unless the number itself is functioning as a named constant.
Do not annotate citation markers, section labels, footnote indices, or document-reference codes such as "CR1" or "Sec1" when they serve structural referencing rather than constant semantics.
Do not annotate units or types attached to constants, such as "microM" or "&str".
Do not annotate keywords or syntax introducing a constant, such as "const", unless the keyword is part of the constant name, which is not shown in the examples.
Do not annotate full equations, integrals, or code statements when only a subpart is the constant.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the minimal token(s) that name the constant itself.
Include alphanumeric characters that are part of the constant name, including attached digits and internal underscores, as in "BUG_WINDOW_SIZE_MESSAGE", "K5", and "K9".
For mathematical constants written as bare symbols or numerals, annotate only the symbol/number denoting the fixed value, e.g. "1" in "B_1" when it functions as the constant.
Exclude surrounding type declarations, operators, punctuation, units, and descriptive text, such as "const", ": &str", "=", "microM", parentheses, or commas.
If a constant appears inside a larger expression, select only the constant portion and not the full formula or term unless the entire expression is itself the named constant.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate variables, placeholders, or quantities whose value is being measured or can vary by instance; annotate only symbols explicitly used as fixed constants or fixed parameter names.
Distinguish constants from ordinary numbers expressing dates, counts, dosages, measurements, or identifiers in prose; numbers like years, mg amounts, and citation indices are not constants.
Distinguish constants from function names, class names, macro keywords, and type names in code; only the immutable named value itself is a constant.

Distinguish constants from domain terms or chemical names: in scientific text, annotate parameter-style fixed symbols such as "K5" and "K9", not the surrounding substance names or experimental descriptions.

Distinguish constants from labels or section markers such as "Sec1" or reference tags like "CR1" unless they are clearly used as fixed mathematical/programmatic constants rather than document structure markers.

contact.definition: A contact is an explicit piece of contact information that identifies a communication endpoint for reaching a person, team, or organization, such as an email address.
include_rules: Annotate explicit email addresses used as a way to contact someone or an organization.
Include role-based or departmental email addresses, such as addresses beginning with terms like support, editor, or compliance, when they are written as full email addresses.
Annotate contact strings that appear after communication cues such as "email", "writing to", or similar instructions, if the string itself is a valid contact endpoint.
Annotate the full contact even when it contains punctuation inside the address, such as hyphens or periods in the domain or local part.
Annotate contacts regardless of whether they refer to an individual, department, or organization, as long as the span is the contact information itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary words or phrases about communication that are not actual contact details, such as "email us", "write to us", or "share their opinion".
Do not annotate scientific terms, biomedical symbols, mutation names, or abbreviations such as "p53", "TP53", "OS", or exon references.
Do not annotate programming code, namespaces, method calls, class names, parameters, or UI identifiers such as "Color", "Windows.UI", or "Color.FromArgb(a,r,g,b)".
Do not annotate malformed or incomplete contact fragments that are missing essential parts of the contact string.
Do not annotate surrounding organization names or role labels unless they are part of the contact string itself; for example, annotate "editor@ilw.com" but not "editor" alone.
Do not annotate generic web or domain mentions unless they are clearly presented as contact information in the text.
boundary_rules: Select only the contact string itself, not surrounding trigger words such as "email us at", "writing to", or "contact".
For email addresses, include the full address from the local part through the domain, including symbols such as @, periods, and hyphens that are part of the address.
Exclude trailing punctuation not part of the contact, such as sentence-final periods, commas, semicolons, parentheses, or quotation marks.
Do not split a valid email address into smaller spans; annotate it as one contiguous entity.
If a contact string is embedded in a sentence, annotate only the exact characters that form the contact information.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate contact only when the text denotes usable contact information, such as an email address people can write to.

Do not annotate technical strings, code tokens, scientific abbreviations, gene names, variable names, or software class/function references merely because they contain symbols or capitalization patterns; examples like "p53", "TP53", or "Color.FromArgb(a,r,g,b)" are not contact.
Do not annotate organization names, person names, or product names unless the span itself is explicit contact information.
Domain-like or address-like strings should be annotated only if they function as contact information in context; ordinary technical namespaces or package/class references are not contact.
This type targets the contact endpoint itself, not the action of contacting, the message content, or the surrounding instruction.

container.definition: A physical receptacle or vessel designed or used to hold, store, collect, transport, or dispense another material or item.
include_rules: Annotate common nouns or noun phrases that denote physical vessels or receptacles used to hold something, such as jars, barrels, and containers.
Annotate container mentions whether empty or filled, and whether used for storage, collection, transport, or dispensing.
Annotate both generic container nouns and more specific container type phrases when the head noun is a receptacle, e.g. "container", "jar", "barrel", "toner container".
Annotate singular and plural mentions of container objects.
Annotate mentions even when the contained substance is mentioned nearby, as long as the entity named is the receptacle itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text with no physical receptacle meaning, including code identifiers, software objects, or technical strings.
Do not annotate places, environments, buildings, or large spaces such as a "school" or "environment", even though they can physically contain people or things.
Do not annotate representations of objects or spaces, such as "physical model" or "VE model", because they are models rather than actual containers.
Do not annotate the contents, amount, or material being stored unless the full phrase denotes a recognized container type; for example, annotate "toner container" but not "toner" alone.
Do not annotate generic event/context words related to use, collection, storage, or transfer when they are not themselves container nouns.
Do not annotate body parts, clothing, or surfaces unless they explicitly function as named receptacles in the text.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the container object itself.

Include modifiers that are part of the container's specific type or material/form, such as contents-as-type or descriptive classifiers when they directly specify the container kind, e.g. "toner container".
Do not include surrounding determiners, quantifiers, or adjectives that do not help name the container type, e.g. exclude "A hard" from "A hard toner container" and annotate only "toner container".
Do not include attached prepositional phrases or usage context outside the container noun phrase, e.g. in "a barrel of Skinner's on tap", annotate only "barrel".
For plural mentions, annotate the full plural noun form, e.g. "jars".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate substances, products, or contents stored inside a vessel unless the phrase denotes the vessel itself; in "barrel of Skinner's", "Skinner's" is content, while "barrel" is the container.
Do not annotate buildings, rooms, schools, or environments that contain people or objects at a large spatial scale; these are places/environments, not container entities.

Do not annotate abstract, virtual, or representational holders such as "models", "source code", "database", or similar non-physical artifacts unless they explicitly denote a physical vessel or receptacle.
Do not confuse packaging/container nouns with general objects or equipment: annotate only when the object's primary semantic role in context is to hold, store, collect, or dispense something.

continent.definition: A named geographic continent or continent-level land area reference, including standard continent names and multiword continent names such as "North America".
include_rules: Annotate standard continent names when they refer to real-world geographic continents, such as "Europe" and "Africa".
Annotate multiword continent names that include directional components as part of the official continent name, such as "North America".
Annotate continent mentions whether they appear alone or inside a larger geographic phrase, as long as the span itself names the continent.
Annotate repeated mentions of continent names each time they occur in text.
Annotate continent names used in locative, comparative, historical, epidemiological, or distributional contexts if they denote the geographic continent.
exclude_rules: Exclude countries, cities, states, provinces, districts, neighborhoods, towns, and roads, even when they appear in geographic context.
Exclude geographic regions that are not continents, such as lakes regions, local regions, and directional subregions when the full phrase is not itself a continent name.
Exclude generic spatial expressions like "the world", "parts of the world", "elsewhere", or "overseas".
Exclude demonyms, adjectival forms, and language names derived from continents unless the noun phrase itself names the continent.
Exclude non-geographic uses of matching words, including fictional, commercial, game, biological, or technical terms that are not references to actual continents.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the words that form the continent name itself, such as "Europe", "Africa", or "North America".
Include directional words only when they are part of a recognized continent-level name or region functioning as a continent reference in context, e.g. "North America".
Do not include surrounding prepositions, determiners, or descriptive nouns, e.g. annotate "Africa" not "in Africa" and not "Africa Great Lakes region".

When a larger phrase contains a continent name plus a smaller regional designation, annotate only the continent token(s), e.g. in "East Africa" annotate "Africa" if the task is strictly continent extraction.
Do not extend the span to include appositive or administrative descriptors such as "region", "part of the world", or country names.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate countries, cities, states, provinces, neighborhoods, or towns; only continent names qualify. For example, "Tanzania", "Boston", "Massachusetts", "Jamaica Plain", and "Cape Cod" are not continents.
Do not annotate broader or vaguer geographic expressions such as "the world", "other parts of the world", or similar non-continent areas.

Do not annotate subcontinental or regional areas unless the span itself is a continent name. For example, in "East Africa", the continent is "Africa"; the directional modifier indicates a subregion, not a separate continent entity.
Do not annotate non-geographic uses of similar words or strings, such as card types, product names, or other domain-specific terms unrelated to physical world continents.
If a location name denotes a landmass smaller than a continent or an administrative area within one, treat it as a different geographic type and exclude it from continent annotation.

country.definition: A geopolitical entity mention that refers to a sovereign country or a commonly recognized country-level state.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of sovereign countries, e.g., "India", "Pakistan", "Russia", "Syria".
Annotate full multi-word country names, including directional distinctions that identify different countries, e.g., "South Korea" and "North Korea".
Annotate country mentions regardless of their syntactic role, such as subject, object, comparison target, or member of an international organization.
Annotate country names when they appear in political, diplomatic, military, trade, or general geographic context.
Annotate repeated country mentions each time they appear in the text.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate subnational administrative units such as states, provinces, regions, counties, or cities.
Do not annotate informal location abbreviations when they refer to non-country places, such as "GA" for Georgia the U.S. state.
Do not annotate organizations, alliances, or institutions even if their names are associated with countries, e.g., "SCO".
Do not annotate people, national groups, or adjectival forms derived from countries unless the country name itself is present as a noun phrase.
Do not annotate capitals or other cities as countries, e.g., "Damascus" is not a country mention in the example.
Do not annotate generic geographic terms like "metro Atlanta county" or non-entity words that merely indicate location.
Do not annotate product names, technical terms, or forum content that coincidentally resembles a place name but does not denote a country.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full country name as it appears in the text, including directional or regional modifiers that are part of the official or conventional country name, e.g., "North Korea", "South Korea".
For multi-word country names, include all words that jointly identify the country.
Do not include surrounding prepositions, conjunctions, punctuation, or appositive context; annotate only the country name itself.
Do not extend the span to include city names, capitals, or metonyms when the text names a different place entity, e.g., in "arms to Damascus", annotate "Syria" but not "Damascus" as country.
Do not split coordinated country mentions; each country name in a list or coordination should be annotated separately, e.g., "India" and "Pakistan".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate countries only when the span denotes a sovereign country or commonly recognized country-level state entity, not a city, province, county, or other subnational region.
Do not annotate demonyms, nationalities, or language names as country mentions unless the country name itself is explicitly present.
Do not annotate abbreviations or codes that are ambiguous and not clearly used as country names in context, e.g., "GA" in the example refers to a U.S. state abbreviation, not a country.
Do not annotate organization names that contain a country name unless the task is to mark the country span itself and it independently refers to the country in context.
When a place name could refer either to a country or to a city/region, use the intended geographic level in context: annotate only if it refers to the country-level entity.

county.definition: A named county-level administrative geographic unit, including jurisdictions explicitly called a county and place names that refer to a county in context.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of county-level administrative divisions.
Annotate names ending with "County" when they denote a specific county, e.g., "Aitkin County", "York County".
Annotate county names that appear without the word "County" when the surrounding context clearly identifies them as a county, e.g., "Kent" in "the borough of Dartford of Kent".
Annotate historical, current, or jurisdictional mentions of counties when they refer to the administrative area itself.
Annotate the county name whether it appears as part of a locative phrase, an origin/source phrase, or an administrative relation phrase, as long as the referent is the county.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic references to counties without a specific name, e.g., "the county" by itself.
Do not annotate administrative units that are not counties, such as parishes, boroughs, territories, cities, towns, or villages, even when they appear near a county name.
Do not annotate larger or different geopolitical entities unless the context makes clear they are being referred to as a county-level unit.
Do not annotate non-geographic uses of words that resemble place names or county names.
Do not annotate technical, scientific, legal, or descriptive text that contains no county-level place name.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full county name as it appears in text, including the administrative designator when it is part of the name, e.g., "Aitkin County" and "York County".

If a county-level jurisdiction is expressed without the word "County" but is still functioning as the county name, annotate the full name token(s), e.g., "Kent" in a context explicitly referring to "the county".
Do not include surrounding prepositions, articles, or higher/lower administrative units outside the county name, e.g., annotate "Aitkin County" not "of Aitkin County".
Do not extend the span to include related localities or sub-county units, e.g., in "borough of Dartford of Kent", annotate only "Kent".
When a county name contains multiple words, include all words that belong to the official county name.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish county from cities, towns, parishes, boroughs, and territories: annotate only the county-level jurisdiction, not nearby lower-level units such as "parish", "borough of Dartford", or "Northwest Aitkin Unorganized Territory".
Distinguish county from larger administrative units such as states, provinces, or countries: only annotate names used as county-level entities in context.
Distinguish county from generic administrative nouns: annotate the specific place name ("York County", "Kent") but not standalone words like "county" when no name is given.
Distinguish county from organizations or facilities: do not annotate institutions, government bodies, or installations that merely contain a place name unless the mention itself denotes the county.
Distinguish county from non-geopolitical homographs or common nouns; if a matching word is used in a technical, scientific, or non-location sense, do not annotate it.

course.definition: A named course, class, instructional module, or formal training program title, including educational offerings and titled training curricula.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of academic courses or class offerings.
Annotate named training programs or laboratory training titles when they function as formal instructional offerings.
Annotate prerequisite or requirement course names mentioned in advising or curriculum context.
Annotate course titles even when they are embedded in longer noun phrases or appear without words like "course" or "class".
Annotate standalone title-like names used to denote an instructional offering, including nontraditional or technical course names.
Annotate each distinct course title separately when several alternatives are listed in one sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic references to education, training, treatment, exercises, or learning activities when no specific titled offering is named.
Do not annotate software products, frameworks, project names, modules, folders, files, or technical artifacts, even if they appear title-like or capitalized.
Do not annotate medical or behavioral interventions, therapies, or rehabilitation approaches unless they are explicitly presented as the title of a training program.
Do not annotate broad programs, fellowships, degrees, departments, or institutions unless the span itself is a specific course or training title.
Do not annotate bare subject areas, skills, or topics without evidence that they are being used as a course title.
Do not annotate descriptive text that merely explains course content, prerequisites, or context rather than naming the course itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the course/training/program title.
Include introductory or descriptive words when they are part of the official or used title, e.g. "Introduction to Logic" and "Longitudinal Microsurgery Laboratory Training".
Include conjunctions, function words, and final descriptors that belong inside the title, e.g. "and Proofs" in "Mathematical Logic and Proofs".
Exclude surrounding sentence context such as requirement markers, connectors, or status words like "finish", "during", "for", "course", or "fellowship" unless they are part of the title itself.
When multiple course names are coordinated with OR/and and each can stand alone as a separate title, annotate each title separately.
Do not extend the span to include degrees, departments, institutions, software-file terms, or project structure text adjacent to the title.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate as course only when the phrase denotes an instructional unit, training offering, or named educational content; do not annotate when it denotes a software project/component, even if capitalized, as in technical text like "Joomla" or Maven module names.

Distinguish course from generic activities or treatments: repeated exercises, rehabilitation, drug therapy, and other intervention descriptions are not course entities unless presented as a named training/program title.
Distinguish course from broader educational stages or positions: terms like "Hand Surgery Fellowship" are not course unless the span specifically names a course or training module within them.

Distinguish course from subjects or disciplines: annotate "Introduction to Logic" as a titled offering, but do not annotate bare field names like "logic", "mathematics", or "microsurgery" when they refer only to topics.
Distinguish course from documents, files, and artifacts: pom.xml, root pom, modules, and similar technical artifacts are not course entities.

court.definition: A court is a judicial body or tribunal identified by an official name or a context-specific referring expression that denotes the adjudicative institution handling legal matters.
include_rules: Annotate official names of judicial bodies at any level, including appellate, district, and jurisdiction-specific courts.
Annotate full named court expressions that identify a specific court, including embedded jurisdiction names and district numbers.
Annotate context-dependent referring expressions that clearly denote the court handling the matter, such as "This Court" when it refers to a judicial body in the document.
Annotate both standalone court names and court names appearing inside larger legal sentences, as long as the span itself denotes the court.
Include court mentions whether they are complete official names or conventional shortened references, provided the reference unambiguously identifies a judicial body in context.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-judicial organizations, agencies, departments, or regulators, even if they are formal government bodies.
Do not annotate legal citations, statutes, case numbers, or references to legal documents instead of the judicial body.
Do not annotate generic common-noun uses of "court" that do not denote a specific judicial body, such as physical places or abstract references.
Do not annotate person names, religious figures, titles of poems/books/articles, or other capitalized expressions unrelated to a judicial institution.
Do not annotate descriptive legal context surrounding the court mention, such as orders, motions, jurisdictional statements, or procedural language, unless the court itself is named within that text.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full court name as a single span, including legal level, jurisdiction, and district/division identifiers when they are part of the official or referenced court name, e.g. "District Court of Appeal of Florida, First District".
Include leading demonstratives or anaphoric modifiers when they are part of the noun phrase explicitly referring to the court in context, e.g. "This Court".
Include internal function words and punctuation that belong to the court name, such as "of", commas, and ordinal district designators.
Do not extend the span to surrounding verbs, legal actions, or case-related text, e.g. in "The Court of Appeals is directed...", annotate only "Court of Appeals".
Do not include trailing statutory citations, parenthetical explanations, or descriptive clauses unless they are part of the court name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate judicial institutions or adjudicative bodies, not laws, statutes, or legal citations; for example, "28 U.S.C." is not a court.
Do not confuse a court with a government regulator or agency; names like "United States Food and Drug Administration" are organizations but not courts.
Do not annotate people, saints, families, or literary titles even if they appear in formal or capitalized language.
When a phrase refers to the deciding judicial body in a case, annotate it as court even if it is expressed anaphorically, as in "This Court".
Only annotate entities whose semantic role is a court as an institution or judicial body; generic buildings, sports courts, or metaphorical uses of "court" are excluded.

creative work.definition: A creative work is a named or contextually identified artistic, musical, literary, or other intellectual creation, including specific titles and, in some contexts, generic labels that directly denote a set of works created or performed.
include_rules: Annotate explicit titles of songs, musical compositions, albums, films, books, or other creative productions.
Annotate named musical works, including classical compositions and similar titles.
Annotate quoted or otherwise marked titles when they refer to a specific creative work.

Annotate generic work-denoting expressions when the context clearly uses them to refer to the creator's actual body of works or categories of works, such as "ballads" or "sacred pieces" in a works list.
Annotate single-word titles when they identify a specific work, such as "Marie".
Annotate work names even when embedded in larger phrases describing performance, use, or medium, as long as the span denotes the work itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-creative objects, products, vehicles, or commercial models, even if they have name-like capitalization.
Do not annotate scientific, medical, biological, or environmental terms that are not artistic or intellectual creations.
Do not annotate creators, performers, publishers, or other agents unless the span itself is the work title.
Do not annotate purely descriptive medium words or artifact types such as "film", "record", "composition", or "piece" when they do not themselves identify a work.
Do not annotate document sections, headings, numbering, citations, or reference markers unless they are actual work titles.
Do not annotate adjectives or genre labels when they only modify another noun and do not themselves stand for a work or set of works in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest span that identifies the creative work itself.
Include the title or work name only, without surrounding quotation marks or italics markup.
Include integral modifiers that are part of the established work name, such as possessive author names inside titles (for example, "Schubert's Trout Quintet").
When a work is referred to by a generic form or genre expression standing in for the work itself, annotate only that expression (for example, "ballads", "sacred pieces").
Exclude surrounding medium or context words that are not part of the work name, such as "film", "record", "soundtrack", "movement", or "by The Four Tunes" unless they belong to the official title.

If a subpart of a work is mentioned descriptively but the named work itself is present, annotate only the named work, not the subpart label (for example, exclude "4th movement" and annotate "Schubert's Trout Quintet").
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate people, groups, or creators associated with a work; in "Marie by The Four Tunes", annotate "Marie" but not "The Four Tunes" as creative work.
Do not annotate vehicles, products, or model names even when capitalized or repeated, such as "Nemo" or "Citroen Nemo Multispace"; these are manufactured objects, not creative works.

Do not annotate diseases, organisms, chemicals, or natural phenomena such as "blast disease", "Pyricularia oryzae", or "acid rain"; these are scientific or real-world entities, not created artistic/intellectual works.
Do not annotate publication structure words like "Introduction" or section labels unless they clearly denote a distinct titled work rather than a document part.
Distinguish a creative work from its format or performance context: "record", "film", or "soundtrack" alone are not creative works unless they are used as the actual title of a work.
Generic artistic categories can be annotated only when they are used in context to denote the creator's actual works or repertoire, not when they merely describe a style, medium, or field.

creature.definition: A mention of a real, mythical, fictional, or hybrid animate being, including an individual creature, a creature species, or a group/class of such beings.
include_rules: Annotate names of individual creatures, including fictional character names, when they denote a creature entity, e.g. 'Meowth'.
Annotate common-noun labels for creature kinds, races, or monster groups when they refer to animate beings, e.g. 'beast-men'.
Annotate plural mentions of creature classes or supernatural beings, e.g. 'Poes'.
Annotate hybrid or non-human humanoid beings if the term denotes a creature type rather than a human social category.
Annotate creature mentions whether they are specific individuals or generic species/group references, as long as the referent is an animate being.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate humans, person names, titles, or human group labels unless the text clearly identifies them as non-human creatures.
Do not annotate organizations, institutions, school districts, courts, or other legal/administrative entities.
Do not annotate medical conditions, tumors, lesions, or biological/pathological entities that are not animate beings.
Do not annotate body parts, anatomy, or physical states of a creature unless the creature itself is named.
Do not annotate generic animal-related adjectives or metaphors unless they directly name a creature entity.
Do not annotate pronouns or anaphoric references such as 'it', 'him', or 'they' even when they refer to a creature.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal noun phrase or name that directly denotes the creature, such as a proper name ('Meowth') or species/group term ('beast-men', 'Poes').
Include internal hyphens and plural markers when they are part of the creature name or class label, e.g. 'beast-men'.
Do not include surrounding determiners, numerals, or quantifiers unless they are part of the fixed creature name; for example, exclude 'Two more of the' and annotate only 'beast-men'.
Do not include verbs, possessives, or descriptive context outside the creature mention; annotate only the entity span itself.
If a multiword expression jointly forms the creature designation, include all words in that designation; if nearby words merely describe state or action, exclude them.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate creatures only when the mention denotes an animate being, monster, animal-like being, or fictional species/individual, not an institution, legal party, or organization.
Do not confuse creature mentions with disease or medical terms ending in words like 'tumors'; these denote pathological conditions, not beings.
A proper name should be tagged as creature only when it refers to a non-human being or fictional creature individual, as in 'Meowth', not when it refers to a person.
Generic role nouns or collectives are creatures only if they denote a being type/species/group, as in 'beast-men'; human social roles, offices, or legal statuses are not creatures.
Terms referring to fictional supernatural beings or monster classes, such as 'Poes', are creatures even when plural or used generically.

crime.definition: A named crime, criminal offense, or legally recognized offense category mentioned in text, including specific offense names and established crime classes.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of criminal offenses, such as "tax evasion".
Annotate legally recognized offense categories or classes when they denote crimes, such as "felonies".
Annotate specific offense names with embedded severity or degree modifiers, such as "second-degree child abuse".
Annotate offense mentions whether they appear in discussion of prosecution, employment, law, or general description, as long as the span itself names the crime.
Annotate both singular and plural mentions of crime names when they refer to offense types rather than abstract legal systems.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic harm, wrongdoing, or violence when no specific legal offense is named.
Do not annotate broad legal terms that are not offense names, such as "laws", "law", "illegal", or "crime" used only generically.
Do not annotate legal procedures, case events, or judicial concepts such as "trial", "charged", "arrest", "conviction", or "sentence".
Do not annotate statutes, regulations, rights, or legal institutions unless the span itself names a crime.
Do not annotate places, media titles, story content, or other non-legal uses of words that may suggest danger or conflict.
Do not annotate implied offenses that require inference from context; the crime must be explicitly named in the text.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full crime name as expressed in the text, including modifiers that are part of the legal offense name, such as degree, victim/class, or offense subtype: "second-degree child abuse".
Include multiword offense terms as a single span when they form one crime concept: "tax evasion", "child abuse".
Include plural or singular noun forms when they directly denote offenses: "felony" / "felonies".
Do not include surrounding trigger words or legal-process words such as "charged with", "convicted of", "trial of", "law which applied for", unless they are part of the offense name itself.
Do not extend the span to include generic tails like "other laws" or broader descriptions outside the offense term.
If an adjective or qualifier is necessary to identify the offense type, include it; if it only describes external circumstance and is not part of the offense name, exclude it.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the offense itself, not the legal proceeding, accusation, punishment, or enforcement action. For example, in "charged with second-degree child abuse", annotate only "second-degree child abuse".
Do not annotate harmful acts or danger described generically when no specific offense is named, such as "cause it harm".
Do not annotate laws, legal systems, or legal categories unless they directly name a crime offense. "felonies" is a crime category and should be annotated; "laws" alone should not.
Do not annotate people, organizations, places, or objects involved in a crime context; only the crime name itself is the target.
Do not annotate regulatory or social misconduct unless the text expresses it as a crime or offense name, as in "tax evasion".

cryptocurrency.definition: A cryptocurrency is a named digital coin, token, or native blockchain currency used as a crypto asset, payment unit, or network asset, such as bitcoin, Grin, or NEO.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of cryptocurrencies, including well-known coin names and blockchain-native assets, such as "bitcoin", "Grin", and "NEO".
Annotate a mention when it refers to a specific digital coin or token as an asset, currency unit, or named blockchain currency.
Annotate both standard-capitalized and lowercase forms of a cryptocurrency name when the context indicates the digital asset, for example "bitcoin".
Annotate ticker-like or abbreviated forms when they are used as the name of the cryptocurrency rather than as an unrelated acronym.
Annotate the cryptocurrency name even when it appears inside technical or financial discussion, such as pricing, wallet usage, syncing, trading, or API access.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic references to cryptocurrencies when no specific coin or token is named, such as "cryptocurrency", "coin", "token", or "digital asset" used descriptively.

Do not annotate software components, services, or infrastructure associated with cryptocurrencies, such as wallets, nodes, APIs, blockchains in general, or projects when the mention is not the coin/token name itself.
Do not annotate biomedical, scientific, or other domain abbreviations that merely resemble ticker symbols, such as "PTHRP" in gene/protein context.
Do not annotate names of people, organizations, websites, documents, or brands unless the mention specifically denotes a cryptocurrency asset.
Do not annotate descriptive phrases around the asset, such as "the price of bitcoin" as a whole; only the asset name itself is eligible.
Do not annotate fiat currencies, units, or unspecified monetary terms unless they are explicitly cryptocurrency names.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the token(s) that form the cryptocurrency name itself, such as "bitcoin", "Grin", or "NEO".
Do not include preceding or following common nouns that describe the asset or system, such as "price of", "wallet", "node", "blockchain project", or similar contextual words.
If the cryptocurrency is referred to by a ticker-style uppercase form, include the full ticker token when it clearly names the cryptocurrency (for example, "NEO").
Do not extend the span to include surrounding punctuation, markup symbols, determiners, or sentence formatting unless they are part of the official cryptocurrency name.
For multiword cryptocurrency names, include all words that are part of the canonical coin/token name and exclude extra descriptive modifiers.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish cryptocurrency from software, infrastructure, or tools: annotate the coin/token name itself, not related products such as wallets, nodes, APIs, or exchanges.

Distinguish cryptocurrency from blockchain projects or platforms when context shows the mention is the name of the tradable crypto asset; in examples like "NEO is a non-profit, community-based blockchain project," annotate "NEO" because it names the cryptocurrency/project token, not the generic phrase "blockchain project".
Distinguish cryptocurrency from genes, proteins, and biomedical abbreviations: uppercase strings like "PTHRP" are not cryptocurrency unless context clearly indicates a digital coin or token.
Distinguish cryptocurrency from person or website names: titles such as "The Official Website of Raina Telgemeier" contain no cryptocurrency entity even though they may look like proper names.
Distinguish cryptocurrency from generic financial concepts: words like "price", "return", or "market" are not cryptocurrency entities unless they are part of the specific coin/token name.

css property.definition: A named CSS styling property: the key in a CSS declaration that specifies what aspect of presentation is being set, usually appearing before a colon or mentioned by name in styling discussion.
include_rules: Annotate names of CSS properties when they are mentioned in code snippets, inline declarations, or prose about styling.
Annotate a property whether it appears with a value assignment or by itself in discussion.
Annotate standard CSS property names, including hyphenated names.
Annotate multiple property names individually when several are mentioned in the same sentence.
Annotate browser-specific or nonstandard property names only if they are being used as CSS property names in the text.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate property values such as keywords, numbers, units, colors, or functions.
Do not annotate complete declarations, rule sets, selectors, or style blocks.
Do not annotate punctuation or operators used with the property, including ':', ';', braces, or commas.
Do not annotate HTML attributes like 'style', 'class', or 'id' unless the token itself is a CSS property name being discussed.
Do not annotate JavaScript object properties, framework props, or configuration keys that are not CSS properties.
Do not annotate generic words that happen to match a CSS property name when they are used in a non-CSS sense.
Do not annotate any text in clearly unrelated domains with no CSS/property meaning.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the property name itself, e.g. 'position', 'flex-wrap', 'flex-direction', 'zoom'.
Do not include the colon, assigned value, semicolon, or surrounding declaration syntax; in 'position: fixed', annotate only 'position'.
Include hyphens that are part of the official property name, as in 'flex-wrap' and 'flex-direction'.
Do not include vendor prefixes unless they are part of the property token being used as the property name in text/code.
If a property appears in prose without a value, annotate just the property token.
When multiple property names are listed separately, annotate each property as its own span.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish css property from CSS values: annotate 'position' in 'position: fixed', but not the value 'fixed'.
Distinguish css property from CSS declarations or full rules: annotate the property name only, not the entire string 'position: fixed' or larger style blocks.
Distinguish css property from HTML/XML attributes or DOM properties unless the mention clearly refers to a CSS styling property.
Distinguish css property from CSS classes, IDs, selectors, pseudo-classes, and pseudo-elements; these are not property names.
Distinguish css property from general technical words that are ordinary English outside CSS context; only annotate when the token is used as the name of a CSS property.
Do not annotate non-CSS uses of similar strings, such as unrelated scientific, medical, personal, or sports text.

culture.definition: A word or phrase that denotes a culture, cultural identity, cultural region, or a manifestation of culture such as a way of life or something explicitly characterized by its cultural role or status.
include_rules: Annotate names of specific cultural regions or communities when they are the source or bearer of a culture, e.g. "Tunisian Sahara" in a phrase about its culture.
Annotate adjectives, demonyms, or group identifiers when they explicitly denote a cultural identity or people in a cultural sense, e.g. "Nepali" in "everyday life of Nepali people."
Annotate common noun phrases that explicitly denote a cultural manifestation or status, e.g. "cultural fixture."
Annotate terms for way of life, customs, or everyday living when they are used as the cultural entity being referred to, e.g. "life" in a phrase about sharing culture and everyday life.
Annotate culture entities whether they are expressed as proper names, adjectives, or common noun phrases, as long as the meaning is cultural rather than purely political, legal, or geographic.
Use local context such as nearby words like "culture", "cultural", "way of life", "everyday life", "tradition", or similar cues to determine that the mention is cultural.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic uses of the word "culture" when no specific culture, identity, region, or manifestation is named.
Do not annotate broad abstract principles, rights, ideologies, or legal concepts, even if they are socially important.
Do not annotate ethnic, national, or language group names when they are mentioned only for politics, demography, conflict, representation, or administration rather than culture.
Do not annotate ordinary place names unless the context makes them the name of a culture or cultural region.
Do not annotate institutions, governments, laws, declarations, media platforms, or technologies by themselves unless the text explicitly frames them as cultural manifestations.
Do not annotate general descriptors of society or everyday activity unless they specifically refer to a cultural way of life, as in the positive example with "life".
Do not annotate surrounding explanatory text that merely describes a culture; only annotate the phrase that directly names the cultural entity or manifestation.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest span that directly names the cultural group, cultural region, cultural identity, or cultural manifestation.
Include modifiers that are part of the cultural referent itself, such as nationality/ethnic adjectives in expressions like "Nepali" or full regional names like "Tunisian Sahara".
Include the head noun when the cultural concept is expressed as a common noun phrase, e.g. "cultural fixture" or "life" when it refers to way of life.
Do not include preceding functional words such as articles, possessives, prepositions, or verbs unless they are inseparable parts of the cultural expression.
In constructions like "culture of X", annotate X if it identifies the culture-bearing people/place/region, not the word "culture" itself unless the phrase naming a cultural manifestation requires it.

When a longer phrase contains both a generic trigger and a specific cultural referent, select only the specific referent or manifestation span, e.g. "Tunisian Sahara" rather than "culture of the Tunisian Sahara".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate legal, political, or moral principles as culture; expressions like "Equality before the law" are abstract doctrines, not cultural groups or cultural manifestations.

Do not annotate ethnic or national group names when they are mentioned only as demographic or political actors rather than as carriers of culture; in the negative example, "Oromo" and "Amharic" refer to ethnic groups in a political context, so they are not culture entities here.

Distinguish culture from locations: annotate a place name only when it stands metonymically for a distinct culture or cultural region, as in "Tunisian Sahara" within a culture context; otherwise geographic mentions are not culture.

Distinguish culture from people/group entities: annotate adjectives or group identifiers such as "Nepali" only when they denote cultural identity or way of life, not merely nationality or ethnicity in a factual listing.
Distinguish culture from generic social conditions or institutions: terms like "law", "government", "political system", or "constitutional order" are not culture even if they shape society.

Distinguish culture from events or media/technology objects: annotate "cultural fixture" because it names a cultural role/status, but do not annotate the underlying object alone unless the expression explicitly presents it as a cultural manifestation.

currency.definition: A word or expression that denotes money, a currency unit, or a specific monetary amount, including generic mentions of money and numeric amounts paired with a currency name, code, or symbol.
include_rules: Annotate generic nouns that directly denote money, such as "money".
Annotate explicit monetary amounts composed of a number plus a currency unit, code, or symbol, such as "30,000 ECU".
Annotate standalone currency units, names, codes, or symbols when they refer to money in context.
Annotate currency expressions whether they describe possession, receipt, cost, payment, or value, as long as the span itself denotes money.
Annotate both specific amounts and nonspecific references to money when the token or phrase itself names the monetary entity.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate numbers that lack any monetary meaning, such as legal citations, dates, episode numbers, or other numeric references.
Do not annotate non-monetary units or rates outside the currency phrase, even if they appear next to a monetary amount.
Do not annotate organizations, products, titles, people, locations, or events that happen to appear in financial or commercial contexts.
Do not annotate generic words like "costs" or "price" unless the token itself is a currency-denoting expression or is accompanied by an explicit monetary amount/unit.
Do not annotate text that only implies finances or payment without naming money or a currency expression.
boundary_rules: Include the full currency expression when an amount and currency unit occur together, e.g. "30,000 ECU".
Include currency-denoting common nouns exactly as written when they stand alone, e.g. "money".
Do not extend the span to surrounding measure or rate context such as "per QALY gained"; annotate only the currency portion.
Do not include nearby verbs, prepositions, time expressions, or transaction context words such as "receive the" or "within 2-4 WEEKS".
If a symbol, code, or unit is attached to the amount and denotes the currency, include it in the same span as the amount.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate currency only when the expression denotes money or a monetary unit/value, not merely any number or quantity.
Plain numbers, identifiers, case citations, episode numbers, dates, and counts are not currency unless they are explicitly marked as money by a currency word, code, or symbol.
Economic or commercial events such as payments, costs, prices, or receiving funds are not themselves currency entities unless a monetary expression is present.
Units from other domains (time, health, legal references, measurements) are not currency even when they follow a number.
Generic financial concepts should be annotated only when they directly name money itself (e.g. "money"), not when they refer to broader financial situations without a currency expression.

cytokine.definition: A cytokine is a named immune or cell-signaling protein or peptide mediator, including specific cytokine family members and generic mentions of cytokines as molecules.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of cytokine molecules, including abbreviated standard names such as interleukin forms like "IL-2" and "IL-17C".
Annotate singular or plural common-noun mentions of the cytokine class when they directly denote cytokine molecules, such as "cytokines".
Annotate cytokine mentions whether they appear as subjects, objects, or measured analytes in experimental statements, including in expression or induction contexts.
Annotate the cytokine mention even when it is embedded in a larger biomedical phrase, as long as the span itself denotes the cytokine molecule.
Include both specific named cytokines and generic class mentions when the reference is to cytokines as signaling molecules.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-cytokine molecules, chemicals, or drugs, even if they modulate cytokine activity or expression.
Do not annotate broad biological processes, disease states, or inflammatory conditions that cytokines influence.
Do not annotate cell types, tissues, organs, or anatomical systems.
Do not annotate receptors, genes, mRNA, or downstream markers unless the span itself is a cytokine name.
Do not annotate generic laboratory or molecular terms such as "messenger", "species", "antioxidants", or "free radicals".
Do not annotate modifiers, determiners, or pronouns around a cytokine mention when they are not part of the cytokine name itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the cytokine itself, e.g., "IL-17C", "IL-2", or the standalone common noun "cytokines" when it directly refers to cytokine molecules.
Include letters, numbers, and attached hyphens that are part of the cytokine name, such as in "IL-2" and "IL-17C".

Do not include surrounding descriptors or experimental context words such as "mRNA expression of", "induced", "human", "epithelial", or disease names; in "IL-2 induced CD23 expression", annotate only "IL-2".
For generic mentions, annotate only the cytokine term itself, not preceding determiners or modifiers unless they are part of the entity name; e.g., annotate "cytokines", not "those cytokines".
Do not extend the span to include receptor, gene expression, cell type, or pathway terms adjacent to the cytokine mention.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse cytokines with other biomolecules or molecular classes such as reactive oxygen species, reactive nitrogen species, free radicals, antioxidants, enzymes, receptors, or neurotransmission-related molecules; these are not cytokines.

Do not annotate genes, mRNA, or expression events when the text refers to transcriptional measurements rather than the cytokine molecule itself; in "mRNA expression of those cytokines", only "cytokines" is the entity.
Do not annotate drugs or chemical compounds even if they affect cytokine levels, such as "JTE-607", donepezil, galantamine, rivastigmine, tacrine, or memantine.

Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, or pathological processes such as psoriasis, eczema, Alzheimer's disease, inflammation, oxidative stress, or neurodegeneration; these may be regulated by cytokines but are not cytokines.
Do not annotate cells, tissues, or anatomical systems such as B cells, neurons, CNS, or PNS; cytokines are signaling proteins, not cell or anatomy entities.
Interleukin names and abbreviations like "IL-2" and "IL-17C" are cytokines and should be annotated when they denote the signaling molecule itself.

data.definition: Information content or an information-holding resource being entered, stored, processed, displayed, or referenced, including textual content, fields, datasets, and databases when mentioned as data-bearing entities.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that directly refer to information content, such as data, text, fields, records, or similar informational units.
Annotate modified data expressions that specify type, scope, source, or temporal property, e.g. "global data" and "time-stamped historical data".
Annotate database-like expressions when they refer to an information repository or stored information resource, e.g. "local health data base".
Annotate interface or form content terms when they denote user-entered or stored information, as in "fields" and "text" in a save/input context.
Annotate both singular and plural mentions when they refer to concrete data-bearing content rather than generic discussion of a domain.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate physical products, media, or materials that are not themselves information content, such as CD-R discs or printable surfaces.
Do not annotate people, organisms, body parts, proteins, chemicals, or other domain objects that may be studied to produce data but are not the data themselves.
Do not annotate generic actions, processes, or events involving data, such as inputting, saving, storing, printing, or analyzing.
Do not annotate system infrastructure terms by themselves when they refer to hardware or software components rather than information content, e.g. "server" alone.
Do not annotate abstract study results, observations, or numerical findings unless they are explicitly presented as a data entity or data resource phrase.
Do not annotate overly generic mentions that do not refer to an actual information object in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase that denotes the data content or data store, including descriptive modifiers that specify type, scope, source, or structure, e.g. "global data", "time-stamped historical data", "local health data base".
Include head nouns such as "data", "database", "data base", "text", and "fields" when they refer to stored, entered, displayed, or managed information.

Exclude surrounding verbs, prepositions, determiners, and event language unless they are part of the conventional data expression; for example, annotate "some data" only if the quantifier is needed by local span policy, but do not include "input", "save", or "for storing".
When a modifier clearly belongs to the data expression, keep it inside the span; for example include "time-stamped historical" in "time-stamped historical data".

Do not extend the span to software, hardware, or system components outside the data expression; in "a server and database for storing time-stamped historical data", annotate "time-stamped historical data" separately from infrastructure terms.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate physical media or storage products as data. A CD-R disc is a physical object, not an information content entity, so expressions about CD-R products are excluded unless the text explicitly names the information stored on them.

Do not annotate biological molecules, proteins, or other scientific objects merely because they are analyzed or measured. In the proteomics example, protein names are study subjects, not data entities.

Distinguish data from systems or devices: servers, applications, printers, and similar tools are not data, though databases may be included when the phrase refers to the stored information resource rather than the software product alone.

Distinguish data from actions on data. Words like "input", "save", "print", "analyzed", or "storing" describe operations and should not be annotated unless they are embedded inside a fixed data noun phrase.

Distinguish data from document-like or interface content only when the content itself is being referred to as information. For example, annotate "text" or "fields" when they denote entered or displayed informational content, not when they refer only to formatting or UI widgets as objects.

data format.definition: A named standard or well-known scheme for structuring, serializing, or encoding data, such as a markup or interchange format, when mentioned as the format itself.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of named data formats used to store, exchange, serialize, or represent data.
Include acronym or full-name mentions when they clearly refer to the format itself, such as "XML".
Annotate the format name whether it modifies another noun or stands alone, e.g., in "XML file", "XML content", and "XML data".
Annotate format mentions in technical discussion about reading, writing, sending, displaying, parsing, or generating structured data.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate abbreviations or capitalized strings that are not data formats, even if they look technical.
Do not annotate generic words for information containers or artifacts such as "file", "content", "data", "report", or "document" by themselves.
Do not annotate programming language names, frameworks, libraries, assemblies, repositories, licenses, companies, or teams.
Do not annotate codes, grades, identifiers, or organizational acronyms such as "GS" when they refer to administrative classifications rather than data representation.
Do not annotate XML-like tags, comment blocks, or other syntax unless the text is explicitly mentioning the format name as an entity.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the token(s) that name the format itself, e.g., "XML".
Do not include following generic nouns such as "file", "content", or "data" unless they are part of the canonical format name.
Do not include surrounding verbs, adjectives, prepositions, or application context, e.g., in "via XML file" annotate only "XML".
If the format name appears as part of a longer established format expression, include the full format name; if only the core acronym is present, annotate only that acronym.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate data representation or serialization formats, not programming languages, software libraries, applications, or platforms.
Do not confuse a data format mention with a file or document instance: in phrases like "XML file" or "XML content", annotate the format name but not the object noun.

Do not annotate organizational abbreviations, job-grade codes, product names, repository names, assembly names, or legal/license text that merely resemble technical strings, such as "GS", "XLabs", or "Apache 2.0" in the negative examples.
Do not annotate markup-like syntax or code comments unless they explicitly name a data format.

data structure.definition: A data structure is a named computational or formal organization for storing, indexing, grouping, or accessing data elements, such as array, set, or hash.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of data structure types used in a technical, programming, algorithmic, or formal data-storage context.
Annotate common canonical structure terms such as 'array', 'set', 'hash', and similar terms when they denote a structure that stores or organizes elements.
Annotate singular or plural mentions when they refer to the structure itself, e.g. 'array', 'Categories' as a set-like collection, or 'hash'.
Annotate a structure mention even when it appears inside a larger noun phrase, provided the phrase refers to a concrete data-holding structure associated with variables, keys, values, or elements.
Annotate user-defined or domain-specific collection names when context shows they function as a data structure rather than merely as semantic categories or labels.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that refer to general data, information, business entities, legal entities, or real-world groups rather than a computational structure.
Do not annotate actions, methods, or procedures applied to a structure, such as 'Explore(v)', 'fill', 'modify', or 'call'.
Do not annotate components stored within a structure unless they themselves are explicitly named as a data structure, e.g. exclude 'key', 'value', and 'string' in the hash example.
Do not annotate generic quantifiers or non-technical uses of collection words when they do not denote a specific data structure type.
Do not annotate document text with no programming or formal data-organization context, such as court opinions, historical narratives, dates, person names, or places.
Do not annotate abstract categories or concepts unless the sentence makes clear they are being treated as a structure or container in a technical sense.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun or noun phrase that directly names the data structure, such as 'array', 'set', 'hash', 'list', 'tree', or similar structure names.

Include modifiers only when they are part of the structure's full mention in context, such as a variable-attached form like 'visited array' if the task annotates complete mentions; otherwise the core structure word must be included at minimum.
Do not include surrounding verbs, determiners, prepositions, or actions, e.g. annotate 'array' not 'the visited array, you have to fill it'.

Do not include contained element descriptions unless they are part of the structure name itself; for example, annotate 'Categories' when it denotes the set contents only if the sentence uses it as the named structure mention.
Preserve capitalization as written, but capitalization alone does not determine whether a span is a data structure.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate ordinary collections mentioned only in a non-technical everyday sense; annotate only when the term denotes a computing or formal data organization object.
Distinguish data structures from variables or object instances: in 'visited array', 'visited' may be a variable-like modifier, but the entity type is the structure term 'array'.
Distinguish data structures from stored values or content items: 'Categories' is annotated when it refers to the set-like structure, not when it refers merely to category labels as business concepts.
Distinguish data structures from algorithms or operations: words like 'fill', 'call', 'modify', 'matches' are actions on a structure, not data structures themselves.
Distinguish data structures from keys, values, strings, or records inside a structure: in the hash example, annotate 'hash' but not 'key', 'value', or 'string'.
Do not annotate legal, historical, or other domain text that lacks a computing/formal-structure meaning, even if words like 'set' or 'order' appear in other senses.

data type.definition: A programming or computational type name that denotes a class/category of values, objects, or collections, such as built-in types, abstract type categories, or language-defined type constructs.
include_rules: Annotate words or phrases that explicitly name a programming datatype or type category.
Include built-in, standard-library, or language-level type names such as collection types and type constructs, e.g. "Array" and "enum".
Include abstract data categories used as type labels in programming discourse, e.g. "iterable" when it refers to the type/category of objects that can be iterated.
Annotate datatype mentions whether they appear in prose, code-adjacent text, or documentation annotations.
Include singular or plural mentions only when the token itself denotes the datatype name rather than an instance or informal description.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary English nouns or domain terms that are not used as programming type names.
Do not annotate names of functions, methods, operators, keywords, variables, parameters, properties, modules, files, or APIs unless the mention specifically denotes a datatype.
Do not annotate literal values, examples of data, or concrete instances; only annotate the type/category name.
Do not annotate generic documentation words such as "path", "value", "map", or "structure" when they describe content or behavior rather than a datatype.
Do not annotate non-technical named entities or titles appearing in the text, even if capitalized.
Do not annotate markup delimiters or code punctuation around a type name.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the token(s) that name the type itself, e.g. "iterable", "enum", "Array".
Do not include surrounding syntax, punctuation, operators, or markup such as "*", parentheses, braces, or annotation prefixes/suffixes; in "{Array}", annotate only "Array".
Do not include determiners, adjectives, or explanatory nouns unless they are part of the canonical type name.
For multiword type names, include all words that together form the datatype name; exclude surrounding context describing usage or behavior.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate datatype names, not variable names, parameter names, field names, or values. In "@param {Array} path", annotate "Array" but not "path".

Do not annotate general technical concepts unless they denote a datatype/class of values. For example, annotate "iterable" when it names a value category, but not generic words like "structure" or "gameplay".
Do not annotate programming operations, syntax elements, or language features unless the mention itself is a datatype name; the "splat operator" is not a data type, while "iterable" is.
Do not annotate organizations, products, events, scholarships, or other non-technical named entities; capitalized words such as "Football Scholarship" or "Market Evaluation" are not datatypes.
Distinguish datatype mentions from code containers or documentation text: the entity is the semantic type being referred to, not the surrounding comment, tag, or code example.

database.definition: A named or specifically described database resource, database system, or database instance, including proper database names, versioned database products, and explicit database noun phrases referring to a concrete database.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of databases, including scientific or domain-specific database resources, e.g. "SYFPEITHI".
Annotate versioned or named database systems/products when they function as database entities in context, e.g. "Views 1" and "Views 2".
Annotate explicit database noun phrases that refer to a concrete database instance or environment, e.g. "production database".
Annotate a mention when the context directly identifies the entity as a database, such as a name followed by the word "database".
Include both standalone names and descriptive database mentions as long as the referent is a database resource/system rather than a general concept.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate mentions of software libraries, APIs, frameworks, or code artifacts unless they are explicitly databases.
Do not annotate unrelated named products, games, apps, companies, or projects that are not presented as databases.
Do not annotate financial funds, government programs, budgets, or grants.
Do not annotate generic technical words like "data", "storage", or other infrastructure terms unless the text explicitly refers to a database.
Do not annotate broad category words such as a bare "database" when it is purely generic and not a specific database resource or instance.
Do not annotate surrounding scientific, legal, or software-development context that merely describes how a database is used.
boundary_rules: Include the full database name when it is expressed as a proper name or versioned product name, e.g. "SYFPEITHI", "Views 1", "Views 2".
For generic mentions modified only by descriptive adjectives, include the complete noun phrase headed by "database", e.g. "production database".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding verbs, prepositions, determiners, or purpose clauses, e.g. annotate "SYFPEITHI" not "the SYFPEITHI database to predict...".
If a database name is followed by the generic word "database", annotate only the specific name when that alone identifies the resource, e.g. "SYFPEITHI" rather than "SYFPEITHI database".
Include version numbers or release identifiers when they are part of the database/system name, as in "Views 1" and "Views 2".
Do not split multiword database/system names when all tokens are needed to identify the resource.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate database resources or database systems, not generic software libraries; terms used in "library version" contexts such as "GCM library" or "InstanceID Library" are not database entities.
Do not annotate government funds, grants, budgets, or financing programs even if they contain words like "Fund" or support technology projects.
Do not annotate game, app, or project titles such as "TownCraft" or "Assault Android Cactus" unless the context explicitly identifies them as a database.

Distinguish a database entity from general data storage mentions: annotate when the expression denotes a concrete database resource/system, not when it refers only to data, finance, software, or an industry product unrelated to databases.

When a named resource is followed by the word "database", treat it as a database entity; when a named resource is followed by words like "library", "fund", or a product genre, it is not this type unless the context clearly says it is a database.

date.definition: A date is an expression that identifies a specific calendar date or named day-based temporal reference, including full dates and day expressions optionally combined with parts of the day.
include_rules: Annotate explicit calendar dates containing month/day/year or subsets of these when they identify a specific date, e.g. "Jan. 1, 2019".
Annotate named weekdays when they refer to when something happened or will happen, e.g. "Friday".
Annotate day-based temporal phrases that combine a weekday or named day with a part of the day, e.g. "Sunday morning".
Annotate abbreviated or punctuated month forms when used in a date expression, e.g. "Jan." within "Jan. 1, 2019".
Annotate the temporal expression regardless of whether it appears with surrounding prepositions or in narrative context.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-temporal text, even if it contains words that resemble date terms or appears in informal discussion about language.
Do not annotate bare numbers, dimensions, page counts, review counts, or other numeric strings that do not denote a calendar date.
Do not annotate addresses, street names, avenue names, or ordinal street numbers as date.
Do not annotate generic time words without a calendar anchor, such as isolated parts of the day like "morning" when no specific day/date is given.
Do not annotate durations, frequencies, ages, or vague temporal descriptions unless they explicitly name a date or named day.
Do not annotate dates that appear only as formatting noise or embedded product/review metadata unless they are still explicit calendar dates intended as text spans for extraction.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal contiguous span that expresses the date or calendrical time reference.
Include month, day, and year together when they form one expression, e.g. "Jan. 1, 2019".
Include standalone named days when used temporally, e.g. "Friday".
Include date expressions with attached time-of-day modifiers when they are part of the same temporal phrase, e.g. "Sunday morning".
Do not include preceding prepositions or determiners such as "on", "in", or "the" unless they are part of a fixed temporal expression.
Do not extend the span to include surrounding event words or clauses, e.g. annotate "Jan. 1, 2019" not "on the morning of Jan. 1, 2019 there was".
Do not split a single continuous temporal expression into smaller entities when the full phrase denotes one date reference.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate calendar-based temporal references as date, not physical locations, even when they appear near addresses or intersections, e.g. in "near 35th and Saint Paul Avenue Sunday morning", only "Sunday morning" is date.
Do not annotate numbers, dimensions, model names, or product sizes as date merely because they contain numerals, e.g. "16x20" is not date.
Do not annotate publication metadata, ratings, counts, or IDs unless they function as explicit date expressions in context.

Distinguish date from general time-of-day-only expressions: annotate when the phrase is anchored to a day or calendar reference, as in "Sunday morning"; a bare "morning" without a date anchor should not be treated as date.
Distinguish date from event descriptions: words naming the event or action are not part of the date entity even if temporally linked.

degree.definition: An academic credential or award title conferred by an educational institution, including full degree names and standard abbreviations for degrees or certificates.
include_rules: Annotate formal names of academic degrees and educational credentials, such as bachelor's, master's, associate, doctoral, and certificate titles.
Annotate abbreviated degree designations when they refer to an earned academic credential, e.g. "MA," "BA," and similar forms.
Annotate full written degree names, including multiword titles such as "Associate of Arts."
Annotate certificate titles when they function as named educational credentials, e.g. "Bible Certificate."
Annotate degree mentions whether or not the sentence also includes the field of study, awarding institution, or graduation date, as long as the credential title itself is explicitly named.
Annotate degree mentions in contexts of receiving, earning, graduating with, holding, or completing a degree.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic educational activities or stages such as "training program," "postdoctoral training," or "academic career" when no specific credential title is named.

Do not annotate fields of study, majors, disciplines, or subjects such as "Garden History," "Acting," "biochemistry," "oncology," or "rheumatology" unless they are part of the formal degree title itself.
Do not annotate institutions, schools, academies, or universities that award the degree.
Do not annotate academic job titles, ranks, or roles such as "Professor," "Associate Professor," "chief," "mentor," or "principal investigator."
Do not annotate honorifics or name suffixes that are not degree mentions in context.
Do not annotate legal abbreviations, court-title abbreviations, citation abbreviations, initials, or similar short forms that may superficially resemble degree abbreviations, e.g. "P.J." or "JJ."
