Do not annotate dates, years, or completion events by themselves.
boundary_rules: Mark only the degree title itself, not surrounding prepositions or verbs: include "MA" in "received an MA in Garden History," not "received an MA".
Include the full official degree name when written out, including level/type words that are part of the title, e.g. "Associate of Arts" and "Bible Certificate".
Include standard abbreviated degree forms as complete spans, including internal periods if present in the text, e.g. "Ph.D." when it clearly denotes an academic degree title.
Do not extend the span to the field of study or specialization introduced by words like "in," "of," or similar: annotate "BA" in "BA in Acting," not "BA in Acting."
Do not include awarding institutions, dates, or completion context: annotate "BA" not "BA in Acting in 2012," and not "MA ... from the University of Bristol."
Exclude adjacent descriptive words that are not part of the formal degree name, such as generic references to training, career stage, or program participation.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate degree names/titles, not educational institutions: in "MA ... from the University of Bristol," only "MA" is the degree, while the university is not.
Annotate degree titles, not academic fields or majors: in "BA in Acting" and "Ph.D. ... in biochemistry," the degree is "BA" or "Ph.D.", while "Acting" and "biochemistry" are not degrees.

Do not confuse degrees with generic educational offerings or activities. A named credential like "Bible Certificate" is a degree-type award, but generic phrases like "training program" or "postdoctoral training" are not.
Do not annotate job titles or academic ranks such as "Professor of Medicine," "Adjunct Associate Professor," or "chief of rheumatology"; these describe positions, not earned credentials.

Do not annotate legal, bibliographic, or numerical abbreviations that resemble degree abbreviations by form. Items like "P.J.," "JJ.," court citations, docket numbers, and reporter abbreviations are not degrees.

When a phrase refers to having received, earned, graduated with, or completed a named credential, annotate the credential only; when the text describes mentorship, appointments, departments, or research areas without naming a credential, do not annotate.

deity.definition: A deity is the name or direct referential title of a divine, godlike, or worshipped supernatural being in a religious or mythological context.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of gods, goddesses, and other divine beings in religious or mythological contexts, such as "Ereshkigal" and "Inanna."
Annotate monotheistic divine names or direct sacred titles when they refer to the deity as an entity, such as "God."
Annotate deity mentions whether they appear in prayer, oath, scripture quotation, myth narrative, or other explicit religious context, as long as the span denotes the divine being.
Annotate both named deities and unique divine referents even if the surrounding text includes descriptive religious wording.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that resemble deity names but refer to non-divine entities, technical terms, businesses, products, places, or ordinary nouns.
Do not annotate generic religious descriptors when they are not themselves the referential deity mention, such as descriptive phrases attached to a named deity.
Do not annotate names of scriptures, citations, religious events, buildings, groups, or practices unless the span itself names the divine being.
Do not annotate pronouns or indirect references to a deity, such as "he," "she," or "them," even if the antecedent is a god.
Do not annotate metaphorical, idiomatic, sarcastic, or exclamatory uses unless the text clearly refers to an actual deity.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the core name of the deity, excluding preceding descriptive titles or roles when the name itself is present: in "Underworld God Ereshkigal," annotate "Ereshkigal" only.
When a deity is referred to by a standalone sacred title used as the proper referent, annotate the title itself, e.g. "God."
Do not extend the span to include surrounding verbs, determiners, prepositions, possessives, or punctuation unless they are part of the deity name.
For multi-word deity names, include all words that are part of the fixed divine name; exclude adjacent appositives or explanatory phrases that merely describe the deity.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish deity from person: annotate supernatural divine beings worshipped or treated as gods, even when they appear in narrative roles similar to humans, such as "Inanna" or "Ereshkigal."

Distinguish deity from religious role or title: words like "god," "goddess," or similar terms are annotated only when they directly function as the deity referent itself, as in "God," not when they are merely descriptive before a named deity.

Distinguish deity from organization, place, product, or common noun uses of the same string: do not annotate words like "God" or similar forms when used in non-religious names, branding, idioms, or technical/business text.
Distinguish deity from broad religious language: annotate the divine being named or directly invoked, not the surrounding religious event, doctrine, scripture citation, oath, or worship practice.

demographic.definition: A demographic entity is a mention of a population characteristic or a population subgroup defined by attributes such as age, sex/gender, or similar person-level background descriptors, rather than a disease, outcome, procedure, or numeric result.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that explicitly name demographic variables or attributes, especially age- and sex/gender-related characteristics, such as "age", "patient age", and "gender ratio".
Annotate mentions of population subgroups defined by demographic descriptors, including age-related or combined descriptive subgroup phrases, such as "older population" and "obese older population".
Annotate demographic characteristic names whether they appear alone or with a population anchor, as long as the phrase denotes a person-level background characteristic rather than a clinical event.
Include descriptors of demographic composition when expressed as a characteristic label, such as sex/gender balance terms like "gender ratio".
Include age-related subgroup mentions even when the precise numeric threshold is given elsewhere in the sentence, if the subgroup phrase itself carries the demographic meaning.
Annotate the demographic phrase when it is the object of comparison, matching, stratification, or data collection in the study context.
exclude_rules: Exclude pure numeric values, ranges, percentages, ratios, and counts when they appear without the demographic characteristic name, e.g. "60 years", "32 cases", "45 females", "70%".

Exclude clinical timing expressions and disease-history variables such as "age of onset", "time course of disease", and "duration of the lesions" when they describe disease course rather than baseline demographics.
Exclude diseases, diagnoses, and clinical classifications, even when attached to patient groups, such as "MG patients", "type I", "type II", and "type III".
Exclude procedures, examinations, biomarkers, laboratory findings, and antibody-status groups such as "cone biopsy", "anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies", "seronegative", and "seropositive".

Exclude general study population mentions with no demographic attribute, such as "patients", "children", or "men in the United States" when they are merely background population references rather than demographic variables under analysis.
Exclude geographic, institutional, or temporal context unless it is part of a demographic subgroup label; for example do not annotate hospital names, years, or study periods.
Exclude modifiers that describe prevalence, severity, progression, or prognosis rather than demographics.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that denotes the demographic characteristic or subgroup, such as "age", "gender ratio", "patient age", or "obese older population".
Include premodifiers that are part of the demographic meaning, such as subject/population anchors and descriptive attributes: "patient age", "obese older population".
Do not include surrounding verbs, reporting phrases, or measurement predicates, such as "were matched from the standpoint of", "Data on", or "is becoming prevalent in".

Do not include trailing numeric values, ranges, symbols, or parenthetical age cutoffs unless they are syntactically part of the demographic noun phrase; for example annotate "obese older population" but not "(> or = 60 years of age)".
When multiple demographic items are listed separately, annotate each item separately, e.g. "age" and "gender ratio" as two entities.
For coordinated demographic descriptors forming one subgroup mention, keep the full combined phrase if it refers to a single population, e.g. "obese older population".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate diseases, disorders, or clinical conditions as demographic even when they describe a patient group; in "obese older population", the demographic cue is the population phrase with age/body-status descriptors, not a diagnosis mention by itself.

Do not annotate clinical outcomes, symptoms, lesions, or disease course variables unless they explicitly function as demographic characteristics. For example, "time course of disease" and "duration of the lesions" are not demographic.

Do not annotate counts or distributions of patients by sex/age when expressed only as study results, such as "32 cases were males and 45 females"; demographic is restricted to the characteristic label or subgroup mention, not the reported statistics.
Do not annotate age values or onset-time expressions as demographic when they refer to clinical timing rather than a demographic variable, such as "The age of onset ranged from 3 months to 16 years".

Do not annotate broad population descriptors that only identify who has a disease without conveying a demographic attribute, such as "men in the United States" in a disease background statement, unless the mention is used as a demographic variable/subgroup in the local context.
Do not annotate procedures, tests, biomarkers, or study design terms even if they are analyzed across demographic groups.

demonym.definition: A word or fixed expression that denotes the people, inhabitants, or nationals of a geographic place, or an adjective expressing such national/regional origin.
include_rules: Annotate adjectives that express national or regional origin of people, e.g. "Italian" in "Italian amateurs".
Annotate plural nouns used to refer to the people or citizens of a place, e.g. "Liberians".
Annotate singular demonym forms used attributively or nominally to indicate nationality or inhabitant status, e.g. "Afghan" in "Afghan troops".
Annotate demonyms when they identify a group by nationality, regional belonging, or inhabitant status in social, political, military, or cultural contexts.
Annotate both adjectival and nominal demonym forms as long as they denote people or national/regional identity rather than the place itself.
Annotate demonyms regardless of capitalization if the context clearly indicates a geographic-origin people term.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate country, city, region, or other place names themselves.
Do not annotate personal names, titles, offices, or nobility designations even when they co-occur with nationality terms.
Do not annotate generic population nouns such as "people", "troops", "politician", or "amateurs" unless the demonym itself is present.
Do not annotate language names when the context is about a language rather than a people/national identity.
Do not annotate adjectives that are not demonyms, even if they describe political, historical, or cultural attributes.
Do not annotate code tokens, technical strings, document headers, or capitalized forms that are not human demonyms.
Do not annotate larger noun phrases when only one token within them is the demonym.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the demonym word or fixed demonym phrase itself, e.g. "Italian", "Liberians", "Afghan".
Do not include surrounding nouns that denote roles, groups, or institutions, e.g. in "Italian amateurs" annotate only "Italian".
When a nationality adjective modifies another noun, include only the adjective if it refers to people/national identity rather than the full noun phrase.
For plural nominal demonyms, include the full plural form, e.g. "Liberians".
If both adjectival and nominal demonym forms appear separately, annotate each form only where it itself functions as the demonym expression.
Do not extend the span to include determiners, verbs, titles, or geographic place names adjacent to the demonym.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish demonyms from place names: annotate "Italian" or "Afghan" when referring to nationality/people, but not country or region names such as "Italy" or "Afghanistan".

Distinguish demonyms from languages: if a word form can denote both a people/nationality and a language, annotate it only when the context refers to people or national identity, not to speaking, writing, or studying a language.

Distinguish demonyms from organizations or political entities: in phrases naming states, governments, teams, or institutions, annotate only the demonym word if it refers to national affiliation, not the larger organization name.
Distinguish demonyms from titles and offices: words like "British" in "British monarch" are demonyms, but titles such as "Lord High Steward" are not demonyms.
Distinguish demonyms from generic adjectives: only annotate adjectives derived from a geographic or national identity; do not annotate non-geographic descriptors such as "ceremonial" or "honorary".
Distinguish demonyms from ethnic or tribal group names when they are not nationality/place-based forms; annotate only forms that denote inhabitants or nationals of a place.

department.definition: An organizational subdivision, bureau, office, or functional unit within a larger organization, institution, company, government body, or school, including full names, abbreviated forms, and contextually clear shorthand references to such units.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of departments or departmental units within larger organizations, such as government, academic, corporate, medical, or nonprofit entities.
Annotate mentions containing head words like "Department" or abbreviations like "dept" when they identify a specific unit.
Annotate academic department mentions, including abbreviated forms, when they denote a university or school subdivision (e.g., "psychology dept").
Annotate functional business units when context indicates they are departments, even if only the functional name appears (e.g., "Sales").
Annotate public-sector or service-unit departments when they are named as organizational entities (e.g., "Public Health Department").
Annotate both formal and informal naming variants if they clearly refer to a department rather than a broader organization or a person’s role.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic common nouns like "department" or "dept" when they do not identify a specific unit.
Do not annotate subject areas, business functions, or domains when they are mentioned only generically and not as a department name.
Do not annotate job titles, occupations, or positions, even if they contain a departmental term, unless the span specifically refers to the department itself.
Do not annotate whole organizations or institutions when the mention is not a subdivision within them.
Do not annotate locations, addresses, cities, states, or other place names attached to a department mention unless they are part of the department's name.
Do not annotate technical, medical, legal, or other non-organizational uses of words that resemble department names.
Do not annotate pronouns or descriptive references like "the unit", "the office", or "their department" unless they function as an explicit named department mention.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the department/unit itself.
Include the core unit-designating word when present, such as "Department" or abbreviations like "dept" (e.g., "Public Health Department", "psychology dept").
If only the functional unit name is used to refer to the department, annotate just that unit name (e.g., "Sales" in a job or organizational context).
Include modifiers that are part of the official or conventional department name, such as subject area or function words before "Department/dept" (e.g., "Public Health", "psychology").
Exclude surrounding verbs, prepositions, determiners, titles, and role phrases that are not part of the department name (e.g., exclude "the", "to accept", "As a", "Planner").
Do not extend the span to include parent organizations, locations, or other attached entities unless they are clearly part of the department name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate as department when the span denotes an internal organizational subdivision or functional unit, not a whole organization.
Do not annotate broader institutions, agencies, companies, universities, or hospitals unless the text specifically names one of their departments.
Distinguish department from job titles or roles: in "Sales Planner", annotate "Sales" only if it denotes the department/function, not the full job title.

Distinguish department from academic disciplines or subject areas used generically: annotate "psychology dept" because it names a department, but not bare "psychology" when it refers only to the field of study.

Distinguish department from government or public-service organizations: annotate the departmental unit itself (e.g., "Public Health Department"), not references to public health as a general domain or service area.
Distinguish department from products, systems, medical conditions, and other common nouns that may appear in technical or news text; only organizational unit names qualify.

description.definition: A span of text that conveys descriptive content about something, including properties, characteristics, status/value expressions, or quoted message content, rather than the thing itself or a metadata field name.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases or adjective-based phrases that state a property, condition, or characteristic, such as "stress concentrations" or "trivial".
Annotate explicit descriptive expressions that characterize a topic, including evaluative or technical descriptors.
Annotate quoted message content when the message itself is the descriptive content, e.g. "Watch out".
Annotate compact value expressions, including measurements, when they are presented as the descriptive content, e.g. "10 cm".
Annotate descriptive spans even when they are embedded in larger clauses, as long as the span itself is a self-contained description.
Annotate both single-word and multi-word spans if they directly express descriptive content.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate the literal word "description" when it is only a label, heading, schema key, or metadata field name rather than descriptive content.
Do not annotate entire product blurbs, manuals, JSON records, or long explanatory passages solely because they contain descriptive language.
Do not annotate entity names, object mentions, or participants unless the mention itself functions as the descriptive content.
Do not annotate actions, processes, or relations unless the span specifically expresses the describable content/value rather than the event.
Do not annotate generic support words, determiners, auxiliaries, or surrounding syntax that do not add to the descriptive content.
Do not annotate reserved notices, database placeholders, or administrative text that are part of record structure rather than a targeted description span.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that expresses the describable content itself, not surrounding function words or framing context.
Include adjectives, modifiers, or quantifiers only when they are part of the descriptive expression's meaning, e.g. "explicit description" and "stress concentrations".
For quoted spoken or written content, annotate only the content inside the quotation if that content is what is being described, e.g. "Watch out" without surrounding punctuation.
For measurement-like or value-like expressions, include the full value expression when it functions as the description content, e.g. "10 cm".
Do not extend the span to include governing predicates or support words such as "is", "has", "was added", "backed up", or "may develop".
Do not include larger document-field structures or labels such as JSON keys, section headings, or schema wrappers around a description field.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate DESCRIPTION when the span gives specific descriptive content, a characteristic, a message, or a value being described; do not annotate the object being described if the object itself is merely the topic.
Do not annotate metadata uses of the word "description" when it names a field, section, or attribute rather than actual descriptive content, as in structured records with a "description" key.

Distinguish DESCRIPTION from product names, object names, or entities: annotate "Watch out" but not "robots"; annotate "stress concentrations" but not "bolt holes" unless the latter itself is presented as descriptive content.
Distinguish DESCRIPTION from events or actions: annotate the content/result expression such as "10 cm" if it is the reported descriptive value, but not the action phrase "backed up 10 cm".

Distinguish DESCRIPTION from broad narrative or promotional text blocks. Long passages that generally market or explain a product are not annotated unless a specific self-contained descriptive expression is the target span.
Distinguish DESCRIPTION from purely referential or classificatory labels. Generic headings like "Product Description" or database labels are not description entities.

design.definition: A design entity is a named or explicitly mentioned designed form, visual/structural artifact, or formal design plan/methodology, including crafted outputs and object forms discussed in terms of their design or shape.
include_rules: Annotate terms that denote a design methodology or design scheme, especially when expressed as a noun phrase headed by "design," e.g., "simulation design."
Annotate crafted visual outputs or media forms when referred to as designed creations, e.g., "animation."
Annotate physical artifacts or components when the context is their designed shape, form, or optimization, e.g., "blade" in discussion of optimizing its shape.
Annotate mentions of designed forms, layouts, structures, or artifacts even when they are common nouns rather than proper names.
Annotate the specific expression naming the design entity, whether it is a standalone noun or a short modified noun phrase.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate proper names, titles, brands, trade names, product names, or app/game names when they are not design entities.
Do not annotate geographic names, prefixes, chemical/drug names, or other lexical entries that merely look like specialized terms.
Do not annotate generic actions, instructions, or events such as "watch this tutorial," "make," "play," "win," or "tests" unless they contain an explicit design entity mention.
Do not annotate abstract goals or properties alone, such as "shape" or "score," unless the phrase names the designed object or design scheme itself.
Do not annotate ordinary object mentions when there is no indication that the reference is to the object's design, form, or crafted structure.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the design artifact, designed form, or design methodology.
Include modifiers that are part of the design expression when they specify the kind of design, as in "simulation design".
For visual or crafted outputs, include the head noun only if that is the full expression used, as in "animation" or "blade".

Do not extend the span to surrounding verbs, determiners, prepositions, or purpose clauses, e.g., annotate "blade" not "the shape of a blade" unless the full shaped object itself is the design mention.
If a larger phrase contains both a process and a design object, annotate only the part denoting the design entity itself.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse design with general activities or events; annotate the designed artifact or design scheme, not the act of testing, watching, making, optimizing, or playing.
Do not annotate evaluation setups, trials, or tests unless the expression explicitly names a design type, as in "simulation design".

Distinguish design from generic physical objects: annotate an object term like "blade" only when it is mentioned in a context of shape, form, construction, animation, or other designed characteristics rather than as an ordinary object reference.
Do not confuse design with product names, game titles, trade names, chemical names, geographic names, or prefixes; the negative examples show such names are outside this type.

When a phrase refers to appearance, structure, layout, shape, animation, or a formal design plan, it is design; when it refers to a brand, organism, place, substance, or unrelated named item, it is not.

device.definition: A physical or engineered instrument, apparatus, machine, electronic product, vehicle-like hardware unit, or hardware assembly that is used to perform a function or operate as equipment.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that refer to concrete man-made or engineered devices, instruments, apparatuses, or machines.
Annotate consumer electronics and hardware products, such as "remote control", "electronic still camera", and "smart phone".
Annotate generic device mentions when they explicitly denote a physical apparatus, even without a brand or model name, such as "imaging device".
Annotate hardware assemblies or grouped hardware units when the phrase denotes a physical device configuration, such as "array of satellites".
Annotate both standalone device names and modified device names when modifiers are needed to identify the device type.
Annotate device mentions regardless of whether they appear in technical, patent, scientific, or general prose contexts.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate abstract concepts, capabilities, or functions such as treatment, imaging, targeting, delivery, control, or resistance.
Do not annotate scientific methods, research approaches, or technological fields that do not name a concrete apparatus, such as "nanotechnology approaches".
Do not annotate software, source code elements, APIs, classes, method names, selectors, imports, or file names.
Do not annotate organizations, systems, protocols, or brands unless the mention itself denotes a concrete hardware device or device assembly.
Do not annotate materials, chemicals, drugs, biological entities, tumors, cells, proteins, siRNA, or imaging agents.
Do not annotate overly generic nouns like "system", "technology", or "equipment" when they are not used to name a specific device or hardware assembly.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the device, including core modifiers that identify its kind, such as "imaging device", "remote control", "electronic still camera", and "smart phone".
Include determiners or numerals only when they are part of the conventional device mention; otherwise exclude leading articles such as "a", "an", and "the".
Include complements introduced by "of" when they are part of the device expression, as in "array of satellites".
Exclude surrounding verbs, prepositions, purposes, and adaptation/use context, e.g., annotate "imaging device" rather than "Such an imaging device is adapted to".
When a coordinated list contains multiple separately named devices, annotate each device mention separately, e.g., "electronic still camera" and "smart phone".
Do not expand the span to include larger systems, organizations, legal references, or code constructs surrounding the device mention.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate physical or engineered apparatuses as device, not abstract technologies, methods, or approaches; for example, do not mark "nanotechnology approaches".
Treat individual hardware items and named collections functioning as equipment as device when the phrase denotes the apparatus itself, as in "array of satellites".
Do not annotate software/library/class/interface names or programming artifacts as device, even if they contain technical nouns, e.g., code identifiers such as "MKTransitArtwork".
Do not annotate actions or functions performed by a device, such as imaging, targeting, delivery, or control, unless the noun phrase explicitly names the apparatus itself.
Do not annotate general scientific or medical materials, drugs, agents, proteins, or cells as device, even when used in technological contexts.

If a phrase refers to a broader system rather than the hardware unit itself, prefer annotating only the explicit device mention within it, not the whole system name unless the system itself is a device mention.

direction.definition: A word or short expression that denotes spatial direction, orientation, side, or a locative/directional relation, such as a basic directional adverb, adjective, or preposition used to indicate where movement or position is oriented.
include_rules: Annotate standalone words that indicate a basic spatial side or orientation, such as "right" when it means the right-hand side.
Annotate prepositions or adverbs that explicitly mark location or direction in context, such as "in" when it indicates being inside or within a place/context.
Annotate directional terms used in motion or position descriptions, including cases like passing, driving, or being located relative to something.
Annotate the directional word even when it appears in a short or informal sentence and is not part of a longer noun phrase.
Include common lexical direction markers expressing side, orientation, or locative relation when they function semantically as direction in the sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that resemble directional terms but are used with non-directional meanings, such as evaluative, discourse, legal, or temporal senses of "right."

Do not annotate technical spatial language in scientific, mathematical, anatomical, or geometric descriptions when it refers to axes, tensors, fields, rows, or other domain-specific structures rather than simple direction terms.
Do not annotate broad spatial descriptions if they are expressed through nouns or full phrases rather than a discrete directional term.
Do not annotate relational phrases when the key word is not itself a direction token of the target type.
Do not annotate sides or locations mentioned only as part of larger named notations or formulaic expressions, such as "<110> axes" or "epsilon(xy)."
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal token(s) that express the directional term itself, such as "right" or "in".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding function words, punctuation, or full phrases when only the directional word carries the direction meaning.
For phrasal expressions, tag only the directional component if that is the directional word used in context (for example, in "pass on the right," annotate "right," not "on the right").
If a word like "right" appears sentence-finally or adverbially and still conveys orientation or directional sense, annotate only that word.
Do not annotate angle brackets, axis labels, coordinate notation, or technical modifiers surrounding non-linguistic orientation references.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate direction only when the word denotes spatial orientation, side, path, or locative/directional relation in the sentence.
Do not annotate words like "right" when they mean correctness, entitlement, emphasis, discourse marker, or temporal immediacy rather than direction.
Do not annotate prepositions such as "in" unless they function as a locative/directional relation; purely grammatical or idiomatic uses without directional/locational meaning should be excluded.

Do not annotate scientific or anatomical descriptors such as "lateral," "contralateral," "along <110> axes," or "across the rows" unless the dataset explicitly treats them as standalone everyday directional terms; based on the examples, such technical spatial descriptions are excluded.
Distinguish direction from full locations or objects: annotate the directional cue word, not the place, body part, field, row, side, or axis being described.

directory.definition: A directory is a filesystem folder location or an explicit textual reference to a folder/container in a file hierarchy, expressed either as a concrete path string or as a noun phrase denoting a specific directory.
include_rules: Annotate explicit filesystem directory paths, including absolute, relative, Windows, and Unix-style directory paths.
Annotate path fragments that identify a directory within an application or archive structure, such as "WEB-INF/lib".
Annotate noun phrases that explicitly denote a specific directory reference in context, even if no literal path is given, such as "containing directory".
Annotate directory mentions when the text refers to the folder as a location for placing, containing, mounting, or accessing files.
Annotate both standalone directory names and multi-component directory paths when the referenced object is a folder rather than a file.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate package names, module identifiers, classpaths, or tool names that merely contain slashes or dots but do not denote a filesystem directory.
Do not annotate file names or file paths that point to files rather than directories.
Do not annotate generic mentions of directories when they are non-specific and not referential, such as "a few directories" or "the home directory" used only as a general concept.
Do not annotate repository metadata or hidden names like ".git" in contexts where they are discussed as project metadata or copied content rather than as a directory mention to extract.
Do not annotate URLs, web addresses, or documentation links.
Do not annotate directory examples appearing only as part of command syntax or configuration blocks when the task context treats them as incidental and not target directory mentions.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that denotes the directory itself.
For filesystem paths, include the full directory path exactly as written, including path separators such as / or \\, when the span refers to a directory.
Do not extend the span to surrounding prepositions, verbs, determiners, or explanatory text unless they are part of the directory mention itself.
If a path contains a file as its final component, do not annotate the whole path as directory; only annotate when the referenced span ends at a directory component.
Generic noun phrases may be annotated when they explicitly denote a directory, such as "containing directory"; include the full noun phrase that names that directory reference.
Do not include trailing punctuation unless it is part of the path string.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish directory from file: annotate container locations such as "WEB-INF/lib" or "C:\\Windows\\Microsoft.NET/Assembly", but not filenames or file paths whose target is a file, such as references to ".git" when used as a file-like repository artifact rather than a directory mention in this scheme.
Distinguish directory from software/package names: do not annotate strings like "@cypress/commit-info" or "OLEVIEW" because they name packages or tools, not directories.

Distinguish directory from commands and configuration syntax: shell commands, mount instructions, sshd_config directives, and code snippets are not directories unless a specific span within them denotes a directory and is being referred to as such.
Distinguish directory from general location descriptions: annotate only the directory-denoting expression itself, not broader statements about access, permissions, or placement.

Distinguish directory from collections or plural references: generic plural mentions like "a few directories" are not annotated unless they identify a specific directory mention or a clearly referential directory phrase.

disease.definition: A disease is a named pathological condition or diagnosis affecting an organism, including disorders, cancers, inflammatory conditions, syndromes, and experimentally induced disease states when they are referred to as the condition itself.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of diseases, disorders, syndromes, neoplasms, and pathological conditions.
Annotate cancer names, including subtype and extent modifiers when they are part of the diagnosis, e.g., localized forms or site-specific cancers.
Annotate inflammatory, autoimmune, infectious, neurological, and other disorder names when presented as a condition affecting the subject.
Annotate experimentally induced or modeled disease names when the phrase denotes the disease itself, e.g., "experimental autoimmune neuritis".
Annotate tumor and neoplasm names as diseases, including terms like "meningioma".
Include clinically meaningful modifiers that narrow the diagnosis, such as anatomical site, pathological subtype, or localization, when attached to the disease name.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate terms from unrelated technical, legal, industrial, or computational domains, even if they contain specialized terminology.
Do not annotate genes, proteins, biomarkers, pathogens, chemicals, drugs, devices, or procedures unless the span itself names a disease.
Do not annotate generic symptoms, findings, or lesions unless they are used as a recognized disease diagnosis.
Do not annotate broad subject areas or research themes such as tolerance, prognosis, treatment response, or prior art unless they explicitly name a disease.
Do not annotate document structure text, citation material, patent information, section headers, or publication descriptors.
Do not annotate animal species or strain names; in phrases like "of the Lewis rat," the species context is excluded from the disease span.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full disease name as a contiguous span, including necessary modifiers that specify the disorder subtype, etiology, site, or clinical extent, e.g., "experimental autoimmune neuritis" and "localized prostate cancer".
Include adjectival modifiers that are part of the established disease mention, such as anatomical location, severity, stage, or pathological subtype when they directly modify the disease noun.

Exclude surrounding non-disease wording such as document genre labels, study descriptors, or discourse text, e.g., exclude "case report" from "Secretory meningioma with lipomatous component: case report."

When a pathology or tumor name is followed or preceded by descriptive findings not forming a standard continuous disease mention, annotate only the disease term itself if that is the clearest disease span, e.g., "meningioma" rather than the larger title string.

Do not include parentheses, abbreviations, or aliases unless they are part of the same explicit disease mention; if an abbreviation appears separately, annotate it only if it independently denotes the disease in context.
Exclude conjunctions, prepositions, and trailing phrases unless they are internal to the disease name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate diseases, disorders, syndromes, cancers, infections, inflammatory conditions, and pathological diagnoses; do not annotate treatments, procedures, or interventions used to study or manage them.
Do not annotate organisms, body parts, cell types, chemicals, drugs, or biomaterials even when they appear near a disease mention; only the pathological condition itself is a disease.

Cancer mentions are diseases when the span denotes the malignancy, including site/specifier combinations such as "prostate cancer" or "localized prostate cancer"; the anatomical site alone is not a disease.
Pathology descriptors or histologic features are part of the disease only when they contribute to the named diagnosis; otherwise they are separate descriptive context and should be excluded.

Experimental models or induced conditions count as disease when the phrase names the disorder being modeled, as in "experimental autoimmune neuritis"; the fact that it is experimental does not exclude it.
Do not confuse disease mentions with general research topics, inventions, software, industrial processes, or technical terms from unrelated domains, which are never diseases.

disorder.definition: A named medical or psychiatric disorder, disease, syndrome, or diagnosable pathological condition affecting an organism.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of medical disorders and diseases, including hematologic, ophthalmologic, psychiatric, and other clinical diagnoses.
Annotate psychiatric diagnosis names when written as formal disorder terms, such as "Dysthymic Disorder" and "Borderline Personality Disorder".
Annotate disorder names whether they are single-word or multi-word mentions, including adjective+noun constructions such as "Autoimmune thrombocytopenia".
Annotate disorder mentions regardless of whether they appear in titles, narrative text, or coordinated lists.
Annotate rare, specific, or subtype-level disorder names when the phrase identifies a diagnosable pathological condition.
Annotate disorder mentions even when they are mentioned as outcomes, comorbidities, or study subjects, as long as the span itself is the disorder name.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-medical uses of adverse or abnormal language, such as social unrest, failure, conflict, or performance problems.
Do not annotate genes, proteins, mutations, chromosomal changes, or molecular mechanisms unless they are part of the disorder name.
Do not annotate diagnostic methods, laboratory techniques, treatments, procedures, or tests.
Do not annotate body parts, cell types, pathogens, or biomarkers by themselves.
Do not annotate generic condition words without a specific disorder mention, such as isolated uses of "disorder", "disease", "defect", or "condition" when they do not name a particular diagnosis.
Do not annotate abbreviations in parentheses when they merely shorten an already annotated full disorder name and are not independently used as the mention.
Do not annotate causes, outcomes, or complications unless they themselves are named disorders.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the disorder, e.g. "Autoimmune thrombocytopenia", "aniridia", "Dysthymic Disorder", "Borderline Personality Disorder".
Include disease/disorder modifiers that are part of the established name, such as severity, subtype, etiology, or pathology terms when they directly form the disorder mention.
Exclude surrounding explanatory or experimental context, such as "determination of", "result in", "frequently co-occur", or other predicate material outside the disorder name.
Exclude parenthetical abbreviations or acronyms unless the abbreviation itself is separately used as the disorder mention. In "Dysthymic Disorder (DD)", annotate only "Dysthymic Disorder".
Do not extend the span to include causal variants, genes, mutations, body parts, or diagnostic methods unless they are part of the disorder name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate genes, mutations, or molecular defects as disorder; in "FOXC1 W152G mutation" and "molecular defects", only "aniridia" is the disorder.
Do not annotate laboratory methods, diagnostic procedures, or measurement techniques as disorder; in "flow cytometry", the method is not a disorder.
Do not annotate symptoms, findings, or biological abnormalities unless the text presents them as a disease/disorder diagnosis by name.

Do not annotate people, populations, occupations, events, or social conditions as disorder, even if they contain emotionally charged or adverse language; examples like "slave revolt", "coach", or "under fire" are not disorders.
When a phrase names a formal psychiatric or medical diagnosis, annotate it as disorder; when it refers only to a non-diagnostic situation, event, or state, do not annotate it.

district.definition: A named administrative, territorial, or institutional subdivision identified as a district or equivalent district-level unit, including local variants such as taluk, and also named district subdivisions within organizations such as courts.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of administrative subdivisions explicitly labeled with district-level terms such as "district" or "District".
Annotate named local administrative equivalents functioning as district-level units, as in "Kolhar taluk".
Annotate named district subdivisions within organizations when the subdivision itself is called a district, as in "Eastern District" in a court name.
Annotate the full named span even when it appears as part of a larger sentence describing location or institutional hierarchy.
Annotate both capitalized and lowercase forms when they are part of a specific proper name, such as "Bijapur district".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic uses of "district", "taluk", or similar terms when they do not form a specific proper name.
Do not annotate broader or different place types that merely contain or contextualize a district, such as states, islands, coastal areas, or countries.
Do not annotate non-entity technical, medical, scientific, or abstract uses of words that resemble place terminology; the long medical example contains no district entities.
Do not annotate institutions, departments, divisions, or other organizational units unless the span itself is the named district-type subdivision.
Do not annotate descriptive geographic phrases without a named district-level unit, such as "coastal zone of Sabah" or "Sabah coastal area".
Do not annotate modifiers, prepositions, or appositive descriptions outside the district name span.
boundary_rules: Include the full district-level place or organizational subdivision name, including the administrative designator when present: "Eastern District", "Kolhar taluk", "Bijapur district", "Hahake District".
Preserve directional or ordinal modifiers that are part of the official subdivision name, such as "Eastern" in "Eastern District".

When a district-type unit appears inside a larger location phrase, annotate only the district-level span, not the larger region: in "Kolhar taluk of Bijapur district in Karnataka", annotate "Kolhar taluk" and "Bijapur district", but not "in Karnataka".

Do not include surrounding institutional hierarchy or subunits outside the district span unless they are part of the district name itself: in "Missouri Court of Appeals, Eastern District, Division Two", annotate only "Eastern District", excluding "Missouri Court of Appeals" and "Division Two".
Use the exact contiguous span forming the district name; do not extend to nearby descriptive location phrases such as "near the east coast of Wallis Island".
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish district from larger geographic units such as states, islands, or regions: annotate "Bijapur district" and "Hahake District", but not broader places like "Karnataka" or "Wallis Island" when they merely locate the district.
Distinguish district from smaller settlements or localities: if a phrase names a village, town, or city without a district-level designator, do not annotate it as district.
Distinguish district from court, agency, or department names: in judicial or administrative titles, annotate only the subdivision labeled as a district-type unit, not the parent body.
District includes administrative subdivisions expressed with equivalent local terms shown by the examples, such as "taluk", when they function as named district-level administrative areas.
Do not confuse the common noun "district" used generically with a named district entity; a capitalized or otherwise specific named subdivision is required.

document.definition: A named or specifically identifiable written, recorded, or codified information resource, including formal statements, declarations, documentation resources, and legal/regulatory citations that refer to a document or textual record.
include_rules: Annotate formal named textual artifacts such as statements, declarations, reports, manuals, or documentation when they are specifically referred to.
Annotate official or structured legal/regulatory document references and section citations when they identify a codified text, such as 'ARS 36-787'.
Annotate named documentation resources tied to a product, platform, or system, such as 'android documentation'.
Annotate document mentions that are specific enough to denote an identifiable text, even if not capitalized or enclosed in quotation marks.

Annotate both standalone document references and multiword phrases where modifiers are needed to specify which document is meant, such as 'parliamentary statement' and 'initial declaration of emergency' when used as the document name.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic plural or nonspecific mentions of documents when they are not individually identifiable, such as 'documents' used descriptively.
Do not annotate creative work titles such as albums, songs, ballets, or similar entertainment/media works.
Do not annotate scientific article titles or publication titles solely because they are text strings, unless the task context clearly treats them as document references of this type.
Do not annotate laws, regulations, or authorities mentioned only as abstract legal concepts without a textual citation or document reference.
Do not annotate document contents, topics, or evidence described by a document unless the mention refers to the document itself.
Do not annotate broad publication containers or brands like magazines, charts, or record labels unless the mention specifically denotes a document resource.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal span that uniquely identifies the document reference.
Include document-type nouns when they are part of the mention, such as 'statement', 'declaration', 'documentation', or similar named textual resources.
Include modifiers that are integral to identifying the document, such as 'parliamentary' in 'parliamentary statement' or 'android' in 'android documentation'.
For legal or regulatory citations, include the full cited code/reference string, such as 'ARS 36-787'.
Do not include surrounding verbs, prepositions, or discourse framing, such as 'said', 'in', 'showing', or 'lists'.
Do not extend the span to include content described by the document unless that content is itself part of the document name or citation.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate creative works such as albums, ballets, suites, magazines, or chart titles as document; these are works/products rather than documentary references in this type's sense.
Do not annotate article or paper titles in running text when they function as publication titles rather than document references, as in the negative biomedical example.
Distinguish document from laws or authorities as abstract legal concepts: annotate only the written citation/reference itself when it denotes the document or codified section, such as 'ARS 36-787'.
Distinguish document from organizations or domains: in 'android documentation', annotate the full phrase referring to the documentation resource, not 'android' alone.

Distinguish document from information content or evidence: annotate the textual artifact ('parliamentary statement'), not the facts or materials mentioned inside it ('documents showing forcible sex slavery').

domain.definition: A domain is a named or clearly delimited domain entity, including an internet domain name or a specific conceptual, application, or biological domain/unit referred to as a bounded area, scope, or structural domain.
include_rules: Annotate specific internet domain names written as host-like strings with a registered suffix, such as "mainDM.com".
Annotate noun phrases that denote a concrete application or scope domain, such as "clinical practice" when it identifies the area of application.
Annotate scientific or biological domain/unit expressions when the phrase refers to a structural or functional domain, such as "heparin-binding domains".
Annotate both singular and plural domain expressions when the plural form is the entity being referred to, not just a generic class.
Annotate domain mentions whether they are alphanumeric strings or natural-language noun phrases, as long as they denote a bounded domain entity rather than a general topic.
Annotate the domain mention even when it appears inside a larger sentence about use, observation, or reporting of that domain.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic, hypothetical, or placeholder domain references that are not specific entities, such as ".whatever domains", ".something domain", or "free domains".
Do not annotate registry, governance, or infrastructure terms related to domains, such as "ICANN", "gTLD", "tld", "NIC", or "Applicant Handbook".
Do not annotate email addresses, even if they contain a domain substring.
Do not annotate company names, product names, publication titles, page names, or social media platforms unless the span itself is a domain entity.
Do not annotate generic uses of "domain" or "domains" when they do not denote a specific bounded domain concept or named domain entity.
Do not annotate surrounding context that merely describes possession, setup, lookup, registration, or use of a domain.
Do not annotate broad technical or scientific phrases unless they specifically denote a domain/unit rather than a general topic or activity.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the domain itself, without surrounding verbs, determiners, punctuation, or explanatory text.
For internet domains, include the full domain string exactly as written, including dots and attached top-level domain labels (e.g., "mainDM.com").
For conceptual or scientific domains, include the full noun phrase that denotes the domain concept when modifiers are part of its identity (e.g., "clinical practice", "heparin-binding domains").
Do not extend the span to include preceding articles or quantifiers such as "a", "the", "own", "free", "two putative".
If a plural common noun is part of the domain expression itself, include it (e.g., "domains" in "heparin-binding domains").
If a larger phrase contains both a domain and material describing actions on it, annotate only the domain portion, not the action or relation phrase.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish domain from a website, email address, or social media handle: annotate the domain string itself (e.g., "mainDM.com"), but not an email such as "BillyTheKidd@aintitcool.com" or platform mentions like "Twitter" or "Facebook Page".

Distinguish domain from top-level-domain policy terms and registry jargon: generic references such as ".whatever domains", ".something", "tld", "gTLD", or "NIC" are not domain entities unless they name a specific domain string or a concrete domain concept.

Distinguish domain from broad fields or topics: annotate a bounded application or functional domain like "clinical practice", but not every subject mention or article topic unless it clearly denotes the area/domain of application.
Distinguish domain from properties or actions: annotate the domain object itself, not processes involving it such as "look up", "register", or "setting up".

Distinguish domain from generic common-noun uses of "domain/domains": only annotate when the phrase refers to a specific internet domain name or a defined conceptual/biological domain unit, not when used generically or hypothetically.

drug.definition: A chemical, biological, or plant-derived substance used or intended for therapeutic, preventive, anesthetic, sedative, or other medicinal purposes, including named medications and medicinal cannabis products.
include_rules: Annotate specific named medications, anesthetics, sedatives, analgesics, antibiotics, chemotherapeutic agents, and other medicinal substances.
Annotate plant-derived or nontraditional medicinal substances when the text uses them as a drug or medical product (e.g., medical marijuana).
Annotate chemical names and hyphenated drug names exactly as written when they refer to a medicine (e.g., 5-fluoruracil).
Annotate each separate drug in a list or coordination as an individual entity.
Annotate the full common or formal drug name when the text clearly identifies a medicinal substance, even if no brand name is given.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, organisms, body parts, procedures, devices, or laboratory materials.
Do not annotate generic words for medication class or treatment if no specific drug/substance is named.
Do not annotate contextual words describing use, administration, policy, or recommendation when they are not part of the drug name.
Do not annotate fictional names, person names, or non-medical entities even if capitalized or mentioned near medical text.
Do not annotate broad products or substances unless the phrase specifically denotes a medicinal drug use or drug product.
boundary_rules: Include the full drug name exactly as it appears in text, including prefixes, numerals, hyphens, slashes, and descriptive modifiers that are part of the drug name (e.g., '5-fluoruracil', 'medical marijuana').
Do not include surrounding punctuation, quotation marks, or unrelated surrounding words.

If a drug mention is part of a longer phrase, include only the words that identify the drug itself, not the broader clinical context or action (e.g., exclude 'infusion of' from 'Continues infusion of 5-fluoruracil').
When multiple drugs are listed separately, annotate each drug mention independently as its own span.
Do not expand the span to include dosage, route, frequency, formulation, or administration verbs unless those words are intrinsic to the drug name.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse drug names with organisms, anatomical terms, or other biomedical entities; only annotate the substance used or intended for treatment, prevention, anesthesia, sedation, or similar medicinal use.
Do not annotate generic procedures, sampling methods, or measurements when they contain words that may resemble biomedical terms; if the phrase is not a drug substance, it is not a drug entity.
Do not annotate person names, nicknames, titles, or fictional character names even if they are capitalized like drug names.

When a phrase can refer either to a medicine or to another concept, annotate it only when the context clearly shows it is the medicinal substance itself (as in the examples of anesthetics and marijuana as a medical product).
Distinguish drug from treatment action: words like 'infusion', 'regulations', 'used', or 'recommended' are not part of the drug entity unless they are part of the drug’s official name.

duration.definition: A duration is an expression that denotes a length or extent of time for which an event, state, activity, condition, or imposed term lasts.
include_rules: Annotate expressions that explicitly state a length of time, including number + time unit combinations such as "two days" and "five years".
Annotate duration phrases modified by approximation words, such as "about 10 years".
Annotate durations describing how long a condition, symptom, action, activity, event, or legal sentence lasts.
Annotate singular, plural, and fractional duration expressions when they denote temporal extent, such as "a day", "an hour", "half a year", or "three and a half weeks".
Annotate duration ranges or coordinated spans when they function as one duration expression, such as "two to three days".
Annotate durations regardless of whether they refer to past, present, future, or hypothetical time spans, as long as the phrase denotes length of time.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate time points, dates, days of the week, months, years, or clock times when they indicate when something happens rather than how long it lasts.
Do not annotate isolated numbers unless they are explicitly tied to a time unit and express a temporal length.
Do not annotate vague sequencing words or adverbs such as "early", "later", "soon", or "already" unless they are part of a concrete duration phrase.
Do not annotate numeric references to episodes, seasons, versions, quantities, or counts that are not durations, e.g. "episode two" or "season eight".
Do not annotate process descriptions or nouns containing words like "process" or "series" unless they include an actual time-span expression.
Do not annotate spans where the time unit refers to schedule or occurrence time rather than elapsed length, such as "Sunday evening".
Do not annotate full clauses that merely imply duration without a bounded duration phrase.
boundary_rules: Include the full duration phrase from its first duration-related modifier or number through the time unit, e.g. "two days", "about 10 years", "five years".
Include approximation or quantity modifiers that directly modify the duration, such as "about", "around", "approximately", or similar words when attached to the time span.
Include articles or determiners only when they are part of a fixed duration expression, such as "a day" or "an hour"; otherwise start at the numeric or approximating word.

Do not extend the span to include surrounding verbs, prepositions, or event descriptions unless they are part of the duration expression itself; for example, annotate "five years" not "to five years at hard labor".
Do not include trailing complements that describe what happened during the duration, such as activities, locations, or sentence outcomes; annotate only the temporal length expression.
For range-like durations, include the full range if it expresses one duration span, such as "two to three days" or "3-4 weeks".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate duration when the phrase expresses how long something lasts, has lasted, or is imposed for, not when it identifies a calendar date or clock time.
Do not annotate ordinal episode or season references such as "episode two" or "season eight"; those contain numbers but do not express temporal length.
Do not annotate frequency or repetition expressions such as "twice a day" unless the span itself denotes a continuous length of time rather than recurrence.
Do not annotate deadlines or relative time points such as "before Sunday evening", "early", or "soon after"; these locate an event in time rather than measure its duration.
Do not annotate age, historical period names, or numbered versions unless the phrase explicitly denotes a length of time.
Annotate sentence-length punishments or terms of service when they are expressed as a period of time, because they denote a duration, e.g. "five years".

education.definition: Any mention of a form of education, schooling level, educational program, or learning/training activity, including general stages of schooling and continuing or professional education.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote schooling levels or educational stages, such as "grade school" and "college".
Annotate phrases that denote ongoing, professional, or continuing learning activities, such as "continuing education" and "Continuing professional education".
Annotate general training activities when they refer to organized learning or instruction, such as "training".
Annotate education mentions whether they refer to formal schooling or professional development.
Annotate both standalone education terms and modified forms when the modifiers are part of the educational concept.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text that is unrelated to education, even if it appears in formal or technical documents.
Do not annotate professions, people, or groups associated with education unless the education term itself is named.
Do not annotate subject areas, research topics, or content domains taught within education unless the education/training expression itself is present.
Do not annotate verbs or events of studying or graduating by themselves, such as "started", "graduated", or "measured".
Do not annotate generic institutional context words unless they directly denote an educational stage or activity in context.
Do not annotate scientific, legal, software, or licensing terminology that does not refer to schooling or training.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal contiguous span that names the educational stage, schooling level, or educational activity.
Include modifiers that are part of the education expression, such as level or purpose descriptors: "grade school", "continuing education", "professional education".
Include coordinated or compound education phrases only when all included words form a single educational concept, e.g. "Continuing professional education".
Do not extend the span to surrounding verbs, determiners, possessives, or prepositions: in "graduated from college", annotate only "college".
Do not include participant or field terms unless they are part of the educational expression itself: in "Psychologists' continuing education", annotate only "continuing education".
When a standalone noun denotes an educational activity in context, annotate just that noun, e.g. "training".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate education for learning processes, schooling levels, or educational programs; do not confuse it with organizations or institutions as named bodies. Generic "college" is education, but a specific institution name would belong to another type if such a type exists.
Do not confuse education with academic disciplines or subject matter. Annotate "training" or "continuing education", but not topic areas such as "sexuality" in "training in sexuality".
Do not confuse education with credentials, achievements, or life events. Words like "graduated" indicate an educational event but are not themselves education entities.
Do not confuse education with professional roles or groups. "Psychologists'" is a profession/group reference, while "continuing education" is the education entity.
Do not confuse education with documents, software, legal text, scientific terminology, or technical processes appearing in non-educational contexts.

educational institution.definition: A named educational organization or institution that provides formal instruction or academic training, such as a university, college, law school, or comparable school-level institution.
include_rules: Annotate named universities, colleges, schools, academies, and similar institutions of formal education.
Annotate full official or commonly used names of higher-education institutions, e.g. "Oregon State University" and "Moritz College of Law."
Annotate institution names that appear as standalone mentions, even if abbreviated to a generic institutional term in context, when they clearly denote an educational institution, e.g. "University."
Annotate named sub-institutions that function as educational entities, such as a college of law within a larger university, when that subunit is explicitly named in the text.
Annotate the institution mention regardless of whether it appears in employment, study, affiliation, or general descriptive context, e.g. "teach at," "students at," or "coach of."
exclude_rules: Do not annotate scientific terms, biological entities, chemicals, mutations, or technical terminology.
Do not annotate game titles, software, websites, products, or entertainment works.
Do not annotate people, including faculty, coaches, students, or administrators, even when they are mentioned together with a school.
Do not annotate generic educational concepts or levels when they do not refer to an institution mention.
Do not annotate facilities, departments, courses, subjects, or programs unless the phrase names an educational institution itself.
Do not annotate broader context words that merely indicate affiliation or location around an institution name.
Do not annotate unnamed or purely descriptive references to schools when no institutional mention is present.
boundary_rules: Include the full institutional name as it appears in context, including distinguishing modifiers and organizational designators such as "University," "College," and "School" when they are part of the name.
Include preceding proper-name elements that identify the institution, e.g. "Oregon State University" rather than only "University."
Include internal subdivisions only when the subdivision itself is the educational institution being referenced as a named school, e.g. "Moritz College of Law."
Do not include surrounding role or relationship words such as "at," "of" when they are outside the institution name, except when "of" is internal to the official name, as in "College of Law."
Do not include descriptive context outside the name, such as job titles, possessives, or associated people, e.g. exclude "men's head coach Wayne Tinkle of" from "Oregon State University."

Annotate generic standalone institutional terms only when they are clearly used to denote an educational institution mention in context, as in "University level" where "University" refers to the institution type.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse an educational institution with a person affiliated with it; annotate the school name, not teachers, coaches, students, or staff.

Do not confuse an educational institution with academic fields, subjects, or levels of study; in "Business, Language, Communication, and Technology," only "University" is the institution-related mention.
Do not annotate research topics, enzymes, mutations, software, games, or products as educational institutions, even if they occur in scholarly or technical text.
Do not confuse an educational institution with a non-educational organization. Only schools, colleges, universities, and comparable learning institutions belong to this type.
When a named subunit such as a law school or college is itself an educational entity, annotate that subunit rather than forcing annotation to a larger unseen parent institution.

effect.definition: An outcome, consequence, or impacted state that results from or is targeted by a cause, exposure, intervention, or process, including named effects and measurable variables or health outcomes that are changed.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that denote a resulting outcome or consequence of some cause, exposure, intervention, or mechanism.
Annotate explicit mentions of effects, including modified forms such as "indirect effects" when the phrase itself names the outcome.
Annotate measurable biological, clinical, or functional variables when they are the thing being affected or targeted, such as concentrations, rates, levels, or other named states.
Annotate adverse or beneficial health outcomes named in the text, such as "morbidity" and "mortality."
Annotate the affected state even when the sentence expresses it through a change relation (e.g., X affects Y); in such cases, Y is the effect.
Annotate outcome terms whether they are concrete physiological quantities or abstract consequences, as long as they denote the result rather than the cause.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic mentions of "effect" or "effects" when they only introduce a causal relation and do not themselves name a specific outcome of interest.
Do not annotate causes, exposures, treatments, agents, or interventions that produce an effect.
Do not annotate diseases, organisms, locations, legal claims, statutes, or other contextual entities unless they are themselves the stated outcome.
Do not annotate verbs or event triggers of change such as "affects," "reduce," "alter," "decrease," or "inhibits".

Do not annotate processes or functions merely because they occur in an "effect of X on Y" construction if they are described as mechanisms, rates, or transport operations rather than outcome entities in this scheme.
Do not annotate broad clauses or propositions describing an entire result when a narrower noun phrase names the outcome.

Do not annotate measurements of experimental validity or assay context (e.g., viability markers, release assays, membrane permeability) unless the text presents them specifically as the outcome/effect being extracted.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal noun phrase naming the outcome itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the outcome expression, such as adjectives indicating type, direction, or subtype (e.g., "indirect effects").
Include full multiword outcome terms when the head noun alone would be incomplete or less specific (e.g., "metabolite concentrations").
When coordinated outcomes are listed separately, annotate each outcome term separately if they are independent nouns (e.g., "morbidity" and "mortality").
Do not include preceding trigger or relation words such as "effect of", "caused by", "due to", "reduce", or "affects" unless they are inside the actual outcome phrase.
Do not include the causal agent, intervention, exposure, or disease name unless it is part of the outcome term itself.
Do not extend the span to measurement context, location, population, or experimental setup words outside the outcome noun phrase.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the result or impacted state, not the cause or source of change. For example, in "Exposure to ionizing radiation affects metabolite concentrations," label "metabolite concentrations," not "ionizing radiation."
Annotate health outcomes such as "morbidity" and "mortality," not the disease or condition producing them (e.g., not "malaria").

Annotate an outcome term when it denotes the effect itself; do not label generic relational uses of the word "effect" in phrases like "the effect of chlorpromazine on..." where the actual outcome is named elsewhere or not stated as an effect entity.
Do not confuse an effect with a biological process, transport function, legal consequence, or action unless the text presents that process/state as the outcome being measured or changed.
If a sentence describes a change in a variable, annotate the variable or resulting state named as the affected outcome, not the verb expressing change (e.g., not "affects" or "reduce").

electronic device.definition: A physical electronic hardware item or piece of electronic equipment used for computing, communication, audio, playback, or similar technical functions.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of physical electronic devices and equipment.
Include computing devices such as computers and PCs.
Include audio and media hardware such as speakers, amplifiers, and radios.
Include common noun mentions of device types even when not branded or model-specific, e.g. "computer".
Include abbreviations and shortened forms when they clearly refer to an electronic device, e.g. "PC" and "amp".
Include modified device mentions when the modifiers describe the hardware being referred to, e.g. "stock speakers".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate software, digital files, games, platforms, operating systems, programming languages, or AI systems.
Do not annotate non-electronic physical objects, general tools, furniture, or locations.
Do not annotate anatomical terms, medical measurement terminology, or biomechanical concepts.
Do not annotate abstract technical concepts such as artificial intelligence, machine learning, strength, torque, or power.
Do not annotate brand, company, lab, or organization names unless the mention itself denotes the hardware device.
Do not annotate generic technical context words that do not themselves name a device.
boundary_rules: Annotate the noun phrase that names the device itself.
Include descriptive modifiers that are part of the device mention, such as built-in type or variant words: "stock speakers".
Include common device nouns and abbreviations when they directly denote hardware, e.g. "PC", "amp", "radio", "computer".
Do not extend the span to surrounding actions, prepositions, possessives, or locations, e.g. in "on your PC" annotate only "PC".
Do not include file names, software names, operating systems, or platform names adjacent to the device mention.
For coordinated mentions, annotate each device separately when they refer to distinct devices, e.g. "amp" and "radio".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate physical hardware devices, not digital artifacts: "PC" is an electronic device, but "playlist.txt" and "batch file" are not.
Do not annotate software, games, AI systems, platforms, licences, programming languages, or operating systems, even when they run on or are compatible with devices.
Do not annotate body parts or biological structures that may appear in technical contexts, such as "hand", "forearm muscles", or "MCP joint".
A generic object word should be annotated only when it clearly refers to an electronic hardware item in context, e.g. "computer" in "personal computer".
Audio equipment and consumer electronics count as electronic devices when they denote the hardware itself, e.g. "speakers", "amp", "radio".

email.definition: An email entity is a complete electronic mail address identifying a mailbox, typically written in the form local-part@domain.
include_rules: Annotate any complete email address that identifies a mailbox for electronic communication.
Include addresses appearing in running text, instructions, requests, or contact statements, such as 'Please email drew@ciienterprises.com'.
Annotate addresses regardless of whether they are surrounded by punctuation or delimiters, provided the address itself is present.
Include institutional, personal, or role-based addresses, including those with subdomains in the domain portion.
Annotate each distinct email address occurrence wherever it appears in the text.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate person names, organization names, usernames, account names, or domain names by themselves unless they appear as part of a full email address.
Do not annotate scientific names, gene symbols, protein names, abbreviations, or technical strings that do not function as email addresses.
Do not annotate surrounding formatting characters or markup, such as '<' and '>' around an address.
Do not annotate generic communication phrases such as 'Please email', 'report to', or 'with any questions' unless they contain an actual email address.
Do not annotate malformed or incomplete address-like strings missing the local part, the '@' symbol, or the domain.
Do not annotate non-email contact information such as URLs, postal addresses, phone numbers, or comment instructions.
boundary_rules: Mark the full email address as one continuous span, including the local part, the '@' symbol, and the full domain.
Include subdomains and the top-level domain when present, as in 'nhspermission@channing.harvard.edu'.
Do not include surrounding punctuation or delimiters such as angle brackets, parentheses, commas, periods, quotation marks, or trailing sentence punctuation.
Do not include preceding cue words such as 'email', 'contact', 'to', or 'at' when they are outside the address itself.
If multiple email addresses appear in a sentence, annotate each address separately.
Only annotate the exact characters that form the address; do not extend the span to surrounding text like 'leave a comment on this post' or similar instructions.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate an expression as email only when it denotes an electronic mail address in address format, not merely a person, organization, gene, protein, or other named item mentioned nearby.
Do not confuse email with web or internet-related text that lacks a true mailbox format; the presence of '@' together with a mailbox name and domain is the key signal.
Terms from scientific or technical text such as gene names, protein names, abbreviations, and chemical notation are not email unless they explicitly appear in email-address form.
If a person or organization name is implied by the mailbox string, annotate only the email address itself, not the underlying person or organization as part of the same span.
