boundary_rules: Include the full folder name or directory path that denotes the folder, preserving internal slashes, hyphens, angle-bracket placeholders, and alphanumeric segments, e.g. "src/Template", "src/View", and "<project-git-repository-root-folder>".
When a common noun modifier is part of the folder mention, include it if it directly names the folder being created or referenced, e.g. "new folder".

Exclude trailing file names or extensions when the span contains a folder followed by a file, e.g. in "<project-git-repository-root-folder>/LICENSE.md", annotate only the folder portion, not "/LICENSE.md".
Do not include surrounding verbs, prepositions, determiners, or explanatory text unless they are part of the folder expression itself.
If a path points to a directory, annotate the whole directory path; if it points to a file, annotate only the directory component when it is explicitly present and separable.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish folder from files: folders are containers/directories such as "src/View" or "project-git-repository-root-folder", while file names like "LICENSE.md" are not folder entities.
Distinguish folder from software/package names: terms such as "Apache2" or "apache2.2-common" refer to software/packages, not directories, even when mentioned near the word "folder".
Distinguish folder from generic technical strings and classifications: category labels, enzyme names, and other domain terms are not folder entities unless they explicitly denote a directory/path.

Distinguish folder from general common nouns: annotate only when "folder" refers to a specific folder instance or named directory, not when it is merely a generic topic word without a clear folder mention.

food.definition: Names of specific edible items, dishes, snacks, desserts, toppings, or other food preparations intended for human or animal consumption.
include_rules: Annotate names of specific foods and edible products, including snacks, desserts, dishes, and toppings.
Annotate multiword food names as a single span when the words together name one item, e.g. "cheese sticks" and "elephant ears".
Annotate edible garnishes or decorations when they are presented as food components, e.g. "rosettes" on a cake.
Annotate food mentions in lists, examples, or comparisons when each mention refers to a concrete edible item.
Annotate common-noun food mentions even when they are not branded or capitalized, e.g. "pudding".
Annotate both simple and compound food terms as long as the referent is something edible and specific.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate broad generic category words that do not identify a specific food item, such as "food" by itself.
Do not annotate non-edible objects, medical terms, anatomical terms, biological materials, or scientific substances, even if they resemble edible words in form.
Do not annotate preparation methods, utensils, containers, events, or actions related to eating or cooking unless the span itself names a food item.
Do not annotate abstract references to meals or cuisine types when no concrete dish or edible item is named.
Do not annotate adjectives or descriptive context alone unless they are part of the actual food name.
Do not annotate food-like shape words when they refer to visual form rather than something edible.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the food item, dish, edible preparation, or edible garnish.
Include modifiers that are part of the conventional food name, such as descriptive nouns or compound terms: "cheese sticks", "elephant ears".
Include plural or singular forms when they are part of the mention: "rosettes", "cheese sticks".
Exclude surrounding category words when they are not part of the item name, such as in "food such as elephant ears" annotate only "elephant ears".

Exclude determiners, quantities, and list markers unless they are part of the established food name: annotate "pudding" not "some pudding"; annotate "cheese sticks" not "some people that put cheese sticks".
When a topping or garnish term directly denotes an edible decoration, annotate just that edible term: "rosettes".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate non-food meanings of similar words; only tag the span when the context shows an edible item. For example, biomedical or technical terms are not food entities.
Distinguish food from cooking actions or serving events: annotate the edible item itself, not verbs like "put" or broader actions involving food.

Distinguish food from general categories or cuisines when no specific edible item is named. Generic mentions like "food" or "ethnic food" are not annotated, but specific items introduced by them, such as "elephant ears", are annotated.
Distinguish food from non-edible decorative shapes or objects. Terms like "rosettes" should be annotated only when they refer to edible cake decoration or similar edible garnish.

Distinguish food from ingredients or substances only by edibility and mention scope: if the text names an edible substance or snack as something consumed or placed in a dish, annotate it; if a chemically similar term appears in scientific or anatomical context, do not annotate it.

format.definition: A word or phrase that denotes a representational, publication, communication, or presentation form in which content, media, or an item exists or is delivered.
include_rules: Annotate terms that name document, markup, media, release, or message formats, such as "html", "EP", and "post".
Annotate phrases that denote a specific presentation or packaging variant of an item, such as cover/color variants like "white cover".
Annotate anaphoric format expressions when the referring phrase still denotes a format or presentation variant, such as "black one" referring to a cover/version form.
Annotate both abbreviated and full lexicalized format names when they function as the type or form of the item rather than the item's specific content.
Annotate format mentions whether they appear in technical, publishing, or conversational contexts, as long as they denote the form/medium itself.
Annotate singular common nouns when they clearly indicate a communicative or publishing format in context, e.g. "post".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general file references when they denote individual files or file objects rather than a format, e.g. "file" or ".edmx file" in source-control discussion.
Do not annotate domain-specific technical or scientific terms that are not forms of representation, publication, or presentation.
Do not annotate generic nouns unless they clearly refer to a recognized form or medium of content, release, or presentation in context.
Do not annotate software names, product names, platforms, or tools used to create, parse, host, or manage a format.
Do not annotate surrounding descriptive context, actions, or ownership phrases such as "released with a", "reply this", or "parse through".
Do not annotate purely visual adjectives alone, such as "white" or "black", unless they are part of a span denoting a presentation form like "white cover".
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the format term itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the expressed format variant or presentation form, as in "white cover" and "black one".
For file or markup formats, include the format token only, e.g. "html" without surrounding words such as "the following".
For publication or communication formats, include the noun naming the form, e.g. "EP" or "post", without attached verbs or arguments.
Do not extend the span to surrounding objects, actions, or software names, e.g. annotate "html" not "parse through Cheerios".
When a pronominal head stands for a format variant, include the full referring phrase if it denotes the format form, e.g. "black one".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate a term as format when it denotes the type, form, or presentation mode of some content or release, not the specific content instance itself.
Do not confuse format with files or documents as physical/digital objects: in "Edmx file", the phrase refers to a file object, so it is not annotated as format.
Do not confuse format with software, platforms, or tools; for example, "Cheerios" is a tool/library context, while "html" is the format.

Do not confuse format with scientific, biological, or technical domain terms that denote entities, processes, or measurements rather than representational forms; e.g. gene/protein and cell terms are not format.

When a word can denote both an object and a format, annotate it only if the context highlights the representational or release form, such as "EP" as a release format or "post" as a communication format.
Color or descriptive modifiers should be included only when they specify a format/presentation variant, not when they merely describe an unrelated object.

framework.definition: A software framework: a named development framework or application framework used to structure, build, or run software applications.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of software frameworks used in programming or application development.
Annotate framework names whether they are standalone single-word names, e.g., "Dropwizard", "Spring", or multiword names, e.g., "Spring MVC".
Annotate framework mentions when they appear in comparison, learning, usage, or implementation contexts, such as being created, studied, used, or configured.
Annotate names of subframeworks or branded framework variants when the text uses them as the framework being discussed, e.g., "Spring MVC".
Annotate framework names even when mentioned alongside other technologies or separated by punctuation, provided the framework name itself is explicit.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic nouns for methodologies or abstract concepts such as "principles", "applications", "treatment protocols", or other non-software uses of "framework"-like language.
Do not annotate licenses, organizations, or legal text such as "GNU General Public License" or "Free Software Foundation".
Do not annotate software projects, games, or programs unless the mention specifically denotes a framework.
Do not annotate programming languages, primitive/object data types, class names, or variable names unless they are part of the framework's official name.
Do not annotate protocols, file formats, APIs, or web-service technologies by themselves, e.g., "SOAP", unless they are embedded in a framework name.
Do not annotate generic category words like "framework", "library", "platform", or "MVC" when used descriptively rather than as part of a specific named framework.
Do not annotate names appearing only in copyright headers, boilerplate license text, or unrelated non-technical prose unless they are actual framework mentions in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the contiguous span that is the official or commonly used framework name.
Include multiword names when all words are part of the framework name, e.g., "Spring MVC".

Include attached abbreviations or product-family tokens that are part of the name as written, e.g., "Spring" in "Spring/EE" but not the slash or the separate paired item unless it is also a framework mention.
Do not include surrounding verbs, determiners, prepositions, or descriptive phrases such as "use", "created as", or "the minimalist counter point to".

Do not extend the span to include technical arguments, APIs, or data types that are not part of the framework name, e.g., exclude "as String" from "In Blueprints, you just specify the key name as String."
Preserve the exact surface form of the framework mention, including capitalization and internal spacing, when they are part of the name.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish framework from general software products or applications: annotate only items referred to as development/application frameworks, not arbitrary software systems.
Distinguish framework from programming languages and data types: names like "String" are not frameworks even when they appear in technical contexts.
Distinguish framework from standards, protocols, or architectures: terms such as "SOAP" are not frameworks unless the text clearly uses them as part of a framework name.
Distinguish framework from broad platform or ecosystem references: annotate the specific framework name mentioned, not generic references to enterprise editions, licenses, or software foundations.
Distinguish framework from libraries or modules by scope: annotate when the term denotes the overarching framework used to structure application development, as in "Spring" or "Spring MVC".

function.definition: A function is a named or clearly identified callable software unit or function-like programming construct referenced in code or programming discussion, including explicit function names and role-based function terms used as callable components.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of software functions mentioned in code or prose, such as standalone identifiers and callable names.
Annotate function names that appear inside invocations or code expressions, even when surrounded by arguments or namespace/class qualifiers.
Annotate named functions introduced by explanatory text such as 'there's a function, EnvelopeAngle'.
Annotate role-based programming terms that denote a specific function-like construct in context, such as 'reducer' in a Redux/programming context.
Annotate function mentions whether they are referenced as definitions, calls, capabilities, or discussed components, as long as they denote a callable software unit.
Include both conventional function names and framework-specific function-like constructs when the context clearly indicates a callable programming role.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic uses of 'function', 'method', 'operation', 'procedure', or similar words when they do not refer to a specific software callable.
Do not annotate non-software meanings of similar words in news, scientific, military, or general technical text.
Do not annotate hardware, aircraft, organizations, products, or other named entities that are not callable software units.
Do not annotate algorithmic techniques, measurement methods, or conceptual processes described in prose unless they are identified as software callables.
Do not annotate namespaces, class names, package names, module names, or library names by themselves when they merely scope a function.
Do not annotate arguments, variables, object properties, return values, or state fields that appear near a function mention.

Do not annotate punctuation or invocation syntax such as parentheses, dots, scope-resolution markers, commas, or semicolons unless they are inseparable parts of the identifier format used by the annotation scheme.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the token(s) that name the function-like unit itself, such as 'getline', 'EnvelopeAngle', or 'reducer'.
Exclude introductory nouns or descriptors outside the name, such as 'function' in 'function, EnvelopeAngle' and similar framing words like 'method' or 'routine' when they are not part of the name.

For qualified calls or namespace/class access, include only the function identifier itself unless the dataset explicitly treats the qualifier as part of the name; based on the example 'std::getline', annotate 'getline' rather than 'std::'.
Do not include parentheses, arguments, commas, semicolons, operators, or surrounding code syntax; annotate 'getline' in 'std::getline(paramFile, tmp);'.
When the function mention is a common noun used as a role name in a programming context, annotate that noun alone if it refers to a specific function-like software component, as in 'Redux reducer'.
Do not extend the span to objects, variables, state paths, or returned values associated with the function mention, such as excluding 'state.foo' from 'reducer has changed state.foo'.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish function from software libraries, namespaces, classes, modules, or objects: annotate the callable or function-like component, not surrounding containers such as 'std'.

Distinguish function from variables, parameters, file handles, and data fields: in a call expression, annotate the callable name ('getline'), not arguments like 'paramFile' or 'tmp' and not fields like 'state.foo'.

Distinguish function from generic process words in non-software text: words like 'operation', 'method', or 'function' in ordinary prose are not function entities unless they denote a software callable or named function-like component.

Distinguish function from algorithm or measurement method descriptions in technical prose: generic references such as 'triangulation method' or 'laser range finding method' are not functions unless presented as callable software units.

Distinguish function from broader software roles: annotate terms like 'reducer' only when the context indicates a function-like programming construct, not when the same word is used in a non-programming or purely conceptual sense.

furniture.definition: A movable household, office, classroom, or similar furnishing item used to support sitting, sleeping, or placing objects, such as chairs, desks, cots, and beds.
include_rules: Annotate nouns and noun phrases that explicitly denote furniture items, including desks, chairs, cots, beds, and similar furnishings.
Include multiword furniture names with descriptive modifiers when the full phrase refers to a specific furniture type, such as "classroom teacher's desk", "office chair", and "dining chair".
Annotate furniture mentions whether they refer to domestic, educational, or workplace furnishings.
Annotate each distinct furniture item mention separately when multiple items appear in the same sentence, such as "cot" and "cotbed".
Include lexical variants and compounds that name furniture items, including closed compounds like "cotbed" and open compounds like "office chair".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text with no furniture meaning, including technical documentation, XML, schema text, or invention descriptions unrelated to furnishings.
Do not annotate non-furniture products, accessories, or soft goods such as quilts, bumper sets, covers, or other bedding unless the furniture item itself is explicitly named.
Do not annotate generic materials, functions, or actions associated with furniture rather than the furniture object itself.
Do not annotate locations, environments, or usage contexts such as "classroom", "office", "dining", or "vehicle" when they appear alone and do not form part of a furniture noun phrase.
Do not annotate abstract references to systems, devices, valves, tanks, humidifiers, or therapy equipment, since these are not furniture.
Do not annotate highly generic object words like "set", "item", or "product" unless they are part of a specific furniture name.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the furniture item, including modifiers needed to identify the item type, such as functional or setting descriptors: "classroom teacher's desk", "office chair", "dining chair".
Include compound furniture names written as one word or multiple words exactly as they appear, such as "cotbed" and "cot".
Exclude surrounding purpose or suitability language such as "suitable for", "for use in", "in a vehicle", or other prepositional context not part of the furniture name.

Exclude quantities, brands, bundled accessories, and non-furniture companion products when they are not part of the furniture item itself; for example, in "Cot Bed Quilt & Bumper Set suitable for cot or cotbed", annotate only "cot" and "cotbed", not the bedding set text.
When a possessive or descriptive modifier is part of the conventional item name in context, keep it inside the span, as in "teacher's desk" within "classroom teacher's desk".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate furniture items, not rooms, buildings, or spaces; for example, annotate "office chair" but not "office" by itself.
Annotate the physical furnishing object, not accessories or textiles associated with it; bedding or covers are excluded unless the furniture item itself is named.
Do not annotate vehicles or other locations even when furniture is mentioned in relation to them; in "in a vehicle, on an office chair", only "office chair" is furniture.
Do not annotate medical, mechanical, or industrial devices that are not furnishing objects, even if they are physical objects discussed in technical text.
Distinguish furniture from general household goods by requiring that the term denote an item primarily used for sitting, sleeping, placing objects, storage, or furnishing an interior space.

game.definition: A named video game, computer game, mobile game, console game, online game, or specifically named minigame/playable title mentioned as a game entity.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of specific games mentioned in text, including commercial, fan-made, or informal game titles.
Annotate named franchises or series when they are used to refer to a game title or game property in context, as with "Pokemon" in a gameplay comparison.
Annotate specifically named minigames or subgames when the phrase functions as a title, as with "crane mini game".
Annotate full multiword game titles, including titles containing conjunctions, abbreviations, or versus forms, such as "Mario vs Pou".
Annotate a game name even when it appears inside a larger sentence about gameplay, levels, or comparison, provided the span itself denotes the game title.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic common nouns that refer to games in general rather than a specific named title, such as "game", "video game", or "mini game" when unnamed.
Do not annotate gameplay-related terms, mechanics, or descriptive phrases such as "gameplay", "level", "clone", or "pass each level of the game".
Do not annotate character names, places, villages, or other non-game entities unless the text clearly uses them as the full game title.
Do not annotate software products, development environments, web projects, installers, algorithms, or technical terms appearing in non-gaming contexts.
Do not annotate franchise-unrelated common words that happen to appear near gaming language unless they specifically denote a named game title.
boundary_rules: Annotate the exact contiguous span that names the game, including full multiword titles such as "crane mini game" and "Mario vs Pou".
Include internal punctuation and connectors that are part of the game name, such as "vs" in "Mario vs Pou".
For possessive forms, annotate only the underlying game title and exclude the possessive suffix when separable (e.g., annotate "Deozoa" in "Deozoa's gameplay").
Do not extend the span to surrounding descriptive context such as "gameplay", "level of the game", "mini", platform mentions, or explanatory verbs unless they are part of the established game name.
When a common noun phrase functions as the actual name of a specific game or minigame, annotate the whole phrase; when it is only a generic reference to a game, do not annotate it.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish a game from a character: annotate the title of the game, not the in-game character name unless the character name is itself the game title in context. In "Help Mario ... in the game Mario vs Pou", annotate "Mario vs Pou", not the earlier "Mario" referring to a character.
Distinguish a game from gameplay/activity terms: words like "gameplay", "level", or generic "game" describe mechanics or a play session, not the named game entity.
Distinguish a game from software, websites, development tools, or technical products. Names such as "Visual Studio", "IISExpress", or hashing algorithms are not games even if they are product names.

Distinguish a game from a genre or comparison target only by name status: annotate "Pokemon" when it refers to the game franchise/title used as a named comparator, but do not annotate generic categories like "clone" or "mini game" unless they form the specific game name.

Distinguish a named minigame from a location or event: in phrases like "at the crane mini game, in the south east part of the village", annotate only the named game phrase, not the surrounding place description.

gender.definition: Words or short phrases that explicitly denote a human gender category or sex-based human group, such as female- or male-denoting terms used to describe people.
include_rules: Annotate explicit human gender category terms such as "women", "men", "females", and analogous male/female group words when they refer to people.
Annotate plural or singular gender-denoting nouns used for human subjects in clinical, demographic, journalistic, or general prose.
Annotate gender terms whether they appear alone or inside a larger descriptive phrase, as long as the annotated span is the gender word itself.
Annotate mentions of human subpopulations defined by gender in study descriptions, reports, or observational statements.
Annotate both common-language and demographic wording when the phrase directly identifies people by gender.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate animal sex terms or livestock categories, even if they imply male or female, when they refer to non-human animals.
Do not annotate generic demographic concepts like "gender" or "sex" when used abstractly as variables, study factors, or labels rather than as a category mention.
Do not annotate age terms, dates, birth-related fields, or life-stage expressions that do not themselves denote gender.
Do not annotate occupations, kinship-neutral person terms, or generic group labels that lack explicit gender meaning.
Do not annotate pronouns, honorifics, or inferred gender unless the dataset explicitly presents a direct gender-denoting noun or phrase.
Do not annotate words that are only part of software, metadata, form fields, or technical discussion unrelated to human gender categories.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the minimal word or phrase that directly expresses gender, such as "women" or "females".
Do not include surrounding age, quantity, nationality, or descriptive modifiers unless they are inseparable parts of the gender expression; for example, in "young women", annotate only "women".
For coordinated or repeated mentions, annotate each explicit gender-denoting mention separately.
Include standalone noun or adjective forms only when they refer to people by gender class, e.g. "females".
Do not extend the span to include non-gender nouns such as "analysis," "patients," or other context words.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse gender with age expressions: in phrases like "older women" or "young women", annotate only the gender term, not the age descriptor.
Do not confuse gender with species or animal sex labels when they refer to animals rather than humans; terms such as "steers" and "heifers" are not gender entities here.
Do not annotate personal roles or population groups unless the word itself encodes gender; for example, annotate "women" but not generic human-group terms like "users" or "patients".
Do not treat biological or demographic variables such as "age" or the phrase "gender analysis" as gender mentions unless an explicit gender category term is present.
Do not confuse person names or titles with gender unless the span itself is a gender-denoting category word.

gene.definition: A mention of a gene or gene-level nucleic acid entity, including a named gene, a named gene locus, or a gene-derived sequence term such as cDNA when it refers to the genetic entity being studied.
include_rules: Annotate specific named genes, including gene symbols followed by an explicit gene-denoting term, e.g. "COI gene".
Annotate named gene loci when a specific gene name is included, e.g. "MEN1 gene locus".
Annotate gene-derived nucleic acid sequence entities when they are referred to as the object of study, such as "cDNA" or "cDNAs".
Annotate mentions where the gene identity is conveyed by a symbol, abbreviation, or standard molecular genetics term in context.
Include singular or plural forms when they denote gene-related sequence entities, e.g. "cDNA" and "cDNAs".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic biomedical terms that do not identify a gene, such as "algorithm", "lesion", "diagnosis", or other non-genetic terminology.
Do not annotate non-biological uses of potentially similar strings, such as software or framework names like "Bootstrap".
Do not annotate isolated chromosomal band or location expressions unless they are part of a gene or gene-locus mention; for example, exclude "11q" alone.
Do not annotate species names, tissue names, disease names, or individual/sample descriptors unless they are inside the actual gene mention.
Do not annotate generic words like "gene" or "locus" alone when no specific gene is identified.
Do not annotate experimental materials or sequence terms when they are purely generic and not used as a gene-level entity mention.
boundary_rules: Include the full gene mention as written when the span names a specific gene or gene-related sequence unit, including attached descriptors that are part of the mention, e.g. "COI gene" and "MEN1 gene locus".
Include the head noun when the text refers to a gene product at the nucleic-acid sequence level rather than a named gene symbol, e.g. annotate "cDNAs" in "rat SAA cDNAs".

Exclude organism names, strain names, and other modifiers that only specify source unless they are inseparable from the conventional gene mention; for example, do not include "rat" in "rat SAA cDNAs".
Include standard suffixes such as "gene", "gene locus", "cDNA", or plural "cDNAs" when they are part of the entity mention in text.
Do not extend the span to surrounding punctuation, determiners, conjunctions, or experimental verbs such as "sequence", "identify", or "determined".
If a gene symbol is immediately followed by a gene-denoting noun phrase, include the combined span rather than only the symbol, e.g. "COI gene" not just "COI".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate gene names and gene-level sequence entities, not proteins or other molecular products unless the text explicitly frames the mention as a gene or nucleic-acid entity.
Distinguish gene from organism mentions: species or taxon words such as "rat" are context for the gene and are not themselves gene entities.

Distinguish gene from chromosomal regions: annotate mentions like "MEN1 gene locus" because they explicitly denote the locus of a named gene, but do not annotate unnamed cytogenetic regions such as "11q" by themselves as gene.
Distinguish gene from laboratory methods or actions: words like "sequence", "isolate", or "diagnosis" are not gene entities even when they occur near a gene mention.

Distinguish gene from generic biomedical vocabulary and non-biomedical uses of similar strings; only annotate when the expression denotes a gene, gene locus, or gene-derived nucleic acid sequence in context.

genetic variation.definition: A mention of a genomic or hereditary abnormality, variant, or alteration, including mutations, polymorphisms, karyotypic abnormalities, and similar terms that denote a change or variant state in genetic material.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that directly denote a genetic change, variant, or abnormal genomic state, such as "mutation", "genetic polymorphisms", and "karyotype" when used to mean an abnormal or variant chromosomal constitution.

Include mentions of named classes of genetic variation, including mutation, polymorphism, variant, deletion, insertion, duplication, translocation, rearrangement, and karyotypic abnormality, when explicitly expressed in text.
Annotate both singular and plural mentions when they refer to genetic variants, e.g. "mutation" and "genetic polymorphisms".
Include generic variation terms when the biomedical context makes clear they refer to a genetic abnormality, e.g. "alteration" in a genetics context if it denotes a genomic change.
Annotate variation mentions whether or not the affected gene, domain, or disease is also mentioned nearby.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text with no biomedical meaning or no reference to heredity, genomics, or variant status.
Do not annotate gene names, protein names, chromosome names, domains, or loci by themselves unless the span explicitly denotes a variation.
Do not annotate diseases, syndromes, traits, or clinical outcomes that may result from a variation.
Do not annotate generic adjectives or discourse words around the entity, such as "novel", counts, determiners, or study-related wording, unless they are part of a fixed variation term.
Do not annotate experimental methods, statistical descriptions, or study metadata that mention genetics but do not themselves name a variant or abnormality.
Do not annotate broad biological concepts like "abnormality" or "alteration" when they are clearly non-genetic or too vague to denote a genetic variant in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that expresses the variation concept itself, usually the head noun or noun phrase, e.g. "mutation", "genetic polymorphisms", "karyotype".
Include essential modifiers that are part of the variation mention when they specify the variation class, such as "genetic" in "genetic polymorphisms".

Exclude surrounding words that describe count, novelty, study design, or clinical consequence, e.g. exclude "Nineteen", "included in this study", and "Novel" when the core entity is "genetic polymorphisms" or "mutation".

Exclude gene/protein names, domains, and diseases that are linked to the variation but are not themselves the variation mention, e.g. in "mutation in the Per-Arnt-Sim domain of KCNH2 causes a malignant form of long-QT syndrome", annotate only "mutation".
When a broader phrase contains both the variation term and additional explanatory context, stop the span at the variation term unless the extra words are required to name the variation type.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse genetic variation with genes, proteins, or genomic loci: annotate the variation term, not the affected biological entity. For example, in a phrase mentioning KCNH2, the variation is "mutation", not "KCNH2".
Do not confuse genetic variation with diseases or phenotypes caused by the variation: annotate "mutation" but not "long-QT syndrome".
Do not confuse genetic variation with anatomical or molecular subregions: domains such as "Per-Arnt-Sim domain" are not variation mentions.

Do not confuse genetic variation with laboratory procedures, studies, or analytic descriptions unless the wording directly denotes a variant class. A phrase like "included in this study" is not part of the entity.
Generic biomedical nouns are only annotated when they denote a heritable or genomic abnormality in context. Non-biomedical uses or unrelated occurrences from other domains should not be annotated.

genus.definition: A biological taxon name denoting a genus, including scientific genus names and genus name strings that include naming authorship as part of the cited form.
include_rules: Annotate scientific names that explicitly denote a biological genus.
Annotate a genus name when it appears after triggers such as "genus", "new genus", or in taxonomic descriptions.
Annotate genus names embedded in parenthetical classification context when the name itself denotes the genus, e.g. before order/family information.
Annotate cited genus name strings that include naming authorship or author surnames when they are written as part of the genus designation, as in "Pyrophagus Remes Lenicov".
Annotate genus mentions in zoological or botanical contexts when the text states or strongly implies genus rank.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate common nouns or generic references to rank without a specific genus name, such as "genus" or "new genus" alone.
Do not annotate names when the mention refers to a different taxonomic rank, even if the surface form is similar or identical to a genus name.
Do not annotate ambiguous strings in disambiguation-style text unless the specific occurrence clearly denotes a biological genus.
Do not annotate higher-level or lower-level taxa mentioned alongside the genus, such as order, family, tribe, or species names.
Do not annotate non-biological text, technical prose, or capitalized words that are unrelated to taxonomy.
Do not annotate surrounding authorship abbreviations, punctuation, or bibliographic material unless they are part of the genus name string itself.
boundary_rules: Mark the span covering the genus name itself.
Include attached authorship or naming attribution when it is presented as part of the genus name string, as in "Pyrophagus Remes Lenicov".
Exclude taxonomic rank words such as "genus", "gen.", "new genus", and higher-level group names that introduce or follow the genus.
Exclude parenthetical taxonomic placement or classification outside the name span, e.g. "(Raphidioptera: Raphidiidae)".
Do not extend the span to surrounding descriptive words such as "snakefly genus" or geographic/source phrases such as "from China" or "from Argentina".
When the same surface form is ambiguous across multiple senses, annotate only the occurrence that explicitly denotes a biological genus, not the entire disambiguation entry.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate higher or lower taxonomic ranks such as order, family, tribe, species, or subfamily when extracting genus; only the genus-level taxon is in scope.

If a name appears in a disambiguation or mixed-sense context, annotate it only when the specific mention is a biological genus; do not annotate the same string when it refers to a non-genus sense such as an order.
Do not annotate generic taxonomic descriptors without a specific name, e.g. "a new genus" or "the genus" alone.
Distinguish the genus mention from surrounding classification context: in "Lucilia (Diptera:Calliphoridae)", only "Lucilia" is genus, while "Diptera" and "Calliphoridae" are other ranks.
Distinguish genus names from non-taxonomic technical vocabulary or ordinary capitalized terms in scientific and patent text; only taxa functioning as genus names should be annotated.

geographic location.definition: A geographic location is a name or direct mention of a physical place, area, landform, region, or other real-world spatial feature on the earth's surface, including both proper place names and contextually geographic common-noun features.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of regions, counties, islands, and other named places, e.g. "South West of England", "Hampshire", and "Long Island".
Annotate named multi-word geographic areas even when they include function words such as "of" or directional terms such as "South West".
Annotate physical geographic features referred to by common nouns when the context clearly uses them as terrain or place features, e.g. "rocks" in a description of where plants can hardly grow.
Annotate both large-scale and small-scale physical places if they denote real-world geography, including natural and administrative areas.
Annotate each explicitly mentioned geographic location in a list or coordination as its own entity span.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate names of people, fictional characters, organizations, products, games, software, operating systems, platforms, or websites.
Do not annotate purely abstract, technical, or programming terms such as "path", "object", or code identifiers.
Do not annotate temporal, numeric, or categorical expressions such as years, scores, versions, or category labels.
Do not annotate adjectives or modifiers derived from locations when they do not themselves denote the place, e.g. "European" in "European regions".
Do not annotate generic spatial nouns that are not used as actual geographic features in context, such as "areas" or "soil" in the example sentence.
Do not annotate locations that are only implied by broader context and not explicitly mentioned as text spans.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full place name that identifies the location, including internal function words and connectives that are part of the conventional name, e.g. "South West of England".
Include geographic designators that are part of the name, such as "Island" in "Long Island".
For multi-part names joined by "of", include the entire expression when it denotes one location, e.g. "Isle of White".
Do not include surrounding prepositions or modifiers that are not part of the name, e.g. annotate "Hampshire" but not "in Hampshire" or "across Long Island".
When a sentence lists multiple locations, annotate each location as a separate span unless they form one conventional place name.
Annotate lowercase common-noun terrain terms only when they directly denote a physical geographic feature in context, e.g. "rocks" in a description of terrain.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate software, games, platforms, products, or websites even if they contain words associated with places; only annotate when the phrase denotes an actual physical location.
Do not annotate demonyms, regional adjectives, or market labels unless they explicitly name a place; for example, "European" alone is not a geographic location, while "Europe" would be.
Distinguish geographic locations from organizations or administrative bodies: annotate the place itself, not institutions associated with it.
Distinguish locations from event venues or events: annotate the place name if mentioned, but not the event name unless the span itself is a geographic place.
Distinguish physical terrain/location terms from abstract or technical uses of the same word; for example, do not annotate words like "path" or "parent" in code or non-geographic contexts.
If a phrase refers to where something happens or exists in the physical world, it is a geographic location; if it refers to a digital, product, or textual artifact, it is not.

geopolitical entity.definition: A geopolitical entity is a name or adjectival/demonymic expression referring to a politically or socially recognized place, region, nation, or its population, including broad political scope terms such as global/worldwide references.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of countries, nations, regions, and politically recognized territorial units, such as "Scotland".
Annotate demonyms and nationality terms that refer to the people of a geopolitical unit, such as "Americans".
Annotate adjectival forms of geopolitical names when they denote regional or national affiliation, such as "European".
Annotate broad geopolitical scope expressions that refer to the world or global human population, such as "worldwide".
Annotate population-denoting nouns when the context explicitly links them to a geopolitical scope, as with "people" in "100 million people worldwide".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate technical terms, scientific names, chemical compounds, abbreviations, or domain-specific phrases that do not denote a political place or population.
Do not annotate organizations, facilities, media, servers, blockchains, newspapers, authorities, or centers, even when they are associated with a place.
Do not annotate ordinary nouns for people without geopolitical reference; generic terms are excluded unless the context explicitly grounds them to a geopolitical scope.
Do not annotate temporal, numerical, procedural, or structural expressions such as timestamps, hash chains, trees, lists, rate constants, or concentrations.
Do not annotate words that only indicate broad extent or direction unless they function as a geopolitical scope term, as "worldwide" does in the positive example.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that expresses the geopolitical reference: "Scotland", "European", "Americans", "worldwide".
Include adjectival or demonym forms when they refer to a country, region, continent, or the people associated with it, as in "European" and "Americans".
Include generic population nouns only when the surrounding expression makes them a geopolitical population reference, as in "people" within "100 million people worldwide".
Include attached directional or scope words only if they themselves carry the geopolitical meaning in context, as in "worldwide".
Exclude surrounding non-entity nouns such as "centers" in "47 European centers"; annotate only the geopolitical modifier.
Do not extend the span to include numbers, quantifiers, prepositions, or larger noun phrases unless they are part of the geopolitical expression itself.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish geopolitical entities from organizations: annotate place or population references like "Scotland" or "European", but not institutions, authorities, centers, or servers located there.

Distinguish geopolitical entities from generic human groups: annotate "Americans" because it denotes a national population, but do not annotate generic people terms unless they are explicitly tied to a geopolitical scope, as with "people worldwide".

Distinguish geopolitical entities from purely geographic or directional language: annotate terms when they denote political/national/regional affiliation or population, not when they only describe physical location without geopolitical reference.

Distinguish geopolitical entities from technical or scientific terminology: words in chemical names, methods, devices, or data structures are not geopolitical entities even if they resemble capitalized names or abbreviations.
Distinguish geopolitical entities from event or study descriptors: in phrases like "European centers", only the geopolitical modifier is annotated, not the facility or study setting.

government.definition: A named or specifically identified governing executive body of a state, nation, or political jurisdiction, including administrations referred to by country, ruling party, or leader name.
include_rules: Annotate mentions that explicitly denote a specific governing executive body of a political unit.
Annotate country- or state-qualified government names, e.g., "Czechoslovak Government".
Annotate governing bodies identified by the ruling party or coalition when the phrase explicitly includes a government term, e.g., "Liberal National Party government".
Annotate leader-based administration names when they refer to the governing executive, e.g., "Obama administration".
Annotate capitalized or conventional forms of government names when the context shows they are the acting state authority.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic common-noun uses that do not identify a specific government entity, such as abstract references to "government" meaning governance in general.

Do not annotate political parties, PACs, movements, or advocacy groups unless the phrase as a whole explicitly refers to the governing body, e.g., annotate "Liberal National Party government" but not "Liberal National Party" alone.
Do not annotate references to democratic systems, governance concepts, or civic processes, such as "self-government".
Do not annotate companies, private organizations, or other non-government institutions, even if they appear in political or public-policy contexts.
Do not annotate titles, roles, or groups of officials that are not themselves a named government entity, such as "Congressmen".
Do not annotate person names alone, even when they imply an administration; require an explicit governing-body term like "administration" or "government".
boundary_rules: Annotate the full government-designating name as it appears in text, including attached political, national, or leader-based modifiers that specify the governing body, e.g., "Liberal National Party government", "Czechoslovak Government", "Obama administration".
Include the head term that signals the entity is a governing body, such as "government" or "administration", when it is part of the entity mention.
For leader-based forms, include the leader name together with the governing-body term, e.g., "Obama administration", not just "Obama".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding verbs, determiners, appositives, or descriptive clauses unless they are part of the conventional government mention.
Do not annotate only a modifier without the governing-body head term when the full mention in context includes that head term.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish government from political parties or movements: annotate only when the phrase denotes the ruling executive or governing body, not merely a party, ideology, or campaign organization. For example, in "Liberal National Party government", the full phrase is government, but "Liberal National Party" alone would be a party, not this type.

Distinguish government from individual politicians: a person name alone is not government. Annotate only combined forms that refer to the governing administration, such as "Obama administration", not "Obama" by itself.
Distinguish government from legislative or democratic concepts: generic references to systems or functions such as "self-government" are not specific governing entities and should not be annotated.
Distinguish government from organizations and companies: corporate or nonprofit entities such as "Enron" or consulting companies are not government even if they interact with public institutions.

Distinguish government from generic officeholders or institutional roles: terms like "Congressmen" refer to people/roles, not a government entity mention of this type unless the text names the governing body itself.

government agency.definition: A government agency is a specifically named public-sector administrative, regulatory, enforcement, or statistical body created by a government at national, state, or local level.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of governmental bodies, departments, bureaus, commissions, and similar agencies.
Include agencies at any government level when they are specifically named, including national and state-level entities.
Annotate multiword names that combine a jurisdiction with an agency term, such as "United States Census Bureau" and "Virginia State Corporation Commission".
Annotate names of agency subunits or departments when they are presented as the named government body, such as "The DEPARTMENT OF ATTORNEY GENERAL".
Annotate agency names even when they appear in legal, administrative, or citation-style text.
Annotate phrases naming a governing body collectively when the phrase denotes the agency institution in context, as in "Commissioners of the Virginia State Corporation Commission".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate diseases, medical conditions, biological terms, or other non-organizational noun phrases.
Do not annotate generic or descriptive mentions of agencies when no specific proper name is given, such as "the bureau", "the commission", or "government officials".
Do not annotate geographic areas, states, countries, or governments themselves unless they are part of the formal agency name.
Do not annotate courts, legislatures, ministries, or elected offices unless the text presents them as the named agency entity of interest and not merely a governmental branch or officeholder.

Do not annotate individual people, even if they work for or lead an agency; exclude names of commissioners, attorneys general, and similar officeholders when the phrase refers to the person rather than the institution.
Do not annotate surrounding legal or case-caption text such as "et al.", party descriptors, or explanatory clauses that are not part of the agency name.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full official or textually presented name of the agency as one contiguous span.

Include organizational designators and internal unit terms when they are part of the name, such as "Department", "Bureau", "Commission", and "Commissioners of the ... Commission" when the whole phrase refers to the governmental body named in text.
Include leading determiners only when they are clearly part of the written name in context, as in "The DEPARTMENT OF ATTORNEY GENERAL"; otherwise do not extend the span to surrounding articles.

Exclude trailing jurisdiction phrases introduced by separate prepositions when they identify a governing region rather than the agency name itself, e.g. exclude "for the STATE of Rhode Island" from "The DEPARTMENT OF ATTORNEY GENERAL for the STATE of Rhode Island".
Exclude adjacent person names, titles of individual officeholders, punctuation, conjunctions like "et al.", and surrounding descriptive text unless they are part of the agency’s stated name.

Do not split a multiword agency name that contains nested place names; include the place name when it is internal to the agency name, e.g. "United States Census Bureau" and "Virginia State Corporation Commission".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate government-created administrative or regulatory bodies, not geographic or political jurisdictions alone; for example, "United States" or "Rhode Island" by themselves are not government agencies.

Annotate the agency body, not individual officials or employees; if a phrase primarily names people rather than the institution, do not mark it unless the whole phrase is being used as the body name, as in "Commissioners of the Virginia State Corporation Commission".

Do not annotate generic references to government functions or offices that lack a specific agency name, such as bare mentions of "the commission", "the bureau", or "attorney general" when they do not identify a named agency.
Distinguish government agencies from non-government organizations, companies, hospitals, journals, and research groups; only entities that are public-sector agencies or commissions qualify.
Do not annotate diseases, anatomical terms, scientific concepts, or study descriptors that may contain words like "state", "commission", or other misleading tokens but do not denote an institution.

government body.definition: A government body is a legislative, judicial, executive, or other public institutional authority that is part of a system of government and exercises official state, regional, or local governmental functions.
include_rules: Annotate names of formal legislative bodies, such as parliaments, assemblies, and congresses.
Annotate names of formal judicial institutions or branches of government when they are referred to as institutions, such as "judiciary".
Annotate both full names and clear shorthand mentions of government bodies when the context indicates an institutional governmental referent.
Annotate national, regional, state, provincial, or local government bodies if they function as official public authorities.
Annotate singular institutional references even when they are generic in form, if they clearly denote a specific governmental organ in context, e.g. "Parliament" or "Congress".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate private, academic, scientific, nonprofit, or commercial organizations that are not part of government.
Do not annotate titles of officials, officeholders, or groups of people unless the text names the institution itself.
Do not annotate laws, licenses, constitutions, legislation, policies, or other legal instruments unless the phrase directly names a government body.
Do not annotate generic nouns for sectors, disciplines, or systems that are not governmental institutions.
Do not annotate locations, buildings, or jurisdictions by themselves unless they are part of the official name of a government body.
Do not annotate text from unrelated technical, copyright, authorship, or scientific descriptions when no governmental institution is mentioned.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the government body itself.
Include jurisdictional or descriptive adjectives when they are part of the body's name, as in "Victorian Legislative Assembly".
Include the full institutional name for multiword bodies; do not split off components such as "Legislative" or "Assembly" when they refer to one body.
Annotate standalone institutional nouns only when they clearly refer to a governmental institution in context, such as "Parliament", "Congress", or "judiciary".

Exclude surrounding prepositions, determiners, possessives outside the name span, and role words not part of the body name, e.g. annotate "Congress" in "Congress's agenda" and not the possessive marker.
Do not extend the span to include actions, functions, or legal instruments associated with the body, such as "through legislation" or "its decisions".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a government body with a person holding office; in "Speaker of the Victorian Legislative Assembly", only "Victorian Legislative Assembly" is the government body, not "Speaker".

Do not confuse a government body with an educational, commercial, scientific, or nonprofit organization; "Imperial College London", "Applied Modelling and Computation Group", and "Free Software Foundation" are organizations but not government bodies.
Do not confuse a government body with a legal document or policy instrument; "GNU Lesser General Public License" and "legislation" are not government bodies.

Do not confuse a government body with a physical place or geographic area; annotate the institution, not the location or jurisdiction unless it is part of the official body name, as in "Victorian Legislative Assembly".
Do not confuse a government body with a biological or technical system; terms like "immune system", "gastrointestinal tract", or "rumen" are not institutions.

Generic civic concepts should only be annotated when they denote an actual branch or organ of government in context; "judiciary" qualifies when referring to the state judicial branch, but generic uses of similar nouns outside government should be excluded.

governmental organization.definition: A governmental organization is a state, legislative, administrative, judicial, or other public-sector body or institution created by and operating as part of a government, including named sovereign/state entities when referenced in their governmental capacity.
include_rules: Annotate named governmental bodies at any level of government, including national, state, provincial, or local public institutions.
Annotate legislative, administrative, judicial, and review bodies that are part of government, such as parliaments and boards created to perform official state functions.
Annotate sovereign or state entities when the wording refers to them as governmental actors or legal/public bodies, e.g., "Commonwealth of Pennsylvania."
Annotate official multiword names of government institutions in full, including jurisdictional complements such as "of the United Kingdom."
Annotate standalone terms that denote a specific government institution or apparatus in context, e.g., "draft" when referring to the state conscription system.
Annotate each distinct governmental organization separately when multiple government bodies appear in the same sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate private organizations, universities, academic departments, mailing lists, newsgroups, or other non-government institutions.
Do not annotate geographic locations, countries, states, or regions when they are mentioned only as places rather than as governing bodies.
Do not annotate laws, acts, bills, regulations, legal citations, case roles, or document identifiers unless the text explicitly names the government body itself.

Do not annotate generic common nouns for government bodies when used nonspecifically, such as "the government," "parliament," or "board," unless they clearly denote a specific organization in context.
Do not annotate historical events, wars, time periods, or dates.
Do not annotate scientific, medical, or technical terms that may superficially resemble institutional language.
Do not annotate personal titles, officials, or groups of people unless the span names the governmental body rather than its members.
boundary_rules: Include the full official or conventional name of the governmental body or state entity, including attached jurisdictional qualifiers, e.g., "Parliament of the United Kingdom" and "Commonwealth of Pennsylvania".
Include organizational designators that are part of the governmental body's name, such as "Board of Review" in "Unemployment Compensation Board of Review".
For standalone institutional terms, annotate the exact span used to refer to the governmental organization, e.g., "draft" when it denotes the state conscription system.
Do not extend the span to surrounding legal, procedural, or case-caption words such as "Act of the," "Respondent," dates, section numbers, or punctuation.
Do not merge adjacent government entities into one span; annotate each separately when they name distinct entities.
Exclude possessive markers, prepositions, and descriptive text unless they are part of the official name.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish governmental organizations from educational or research institutions: universities, departments of biological sciences, and mailing-address affiliations are not governmental organizations unless the text clearly names a state body acting as a government institution.

Distinguish governmental organizations from laws or legal instruments: annotate the institution that issues or enacts the law, such as "Parliament of the United Kingdom," not the act, bill number, or statute citation itself.

Distinguish governmental organizations from places: "Commonwealth of Pennsylvania" is included when it refers to the state as a government entity, but geographic references to Pennsylvania or the United Kingdom as locations alone are not.

Distinguish governmental organizations from government programs, systems, or functions: include terms like "draft" only when they denote a government-run institution or apparatus, not when used generically for a preliminary version of a document.
Distinguish governmental organizations from military conflicts or historical events: "Vietnam War" is not a governmental organization, even if mentioned in the same sentence as one.
Distinguish governmental organizations from medical, scientific, or technical terminology: disease names, brain regions, stimuli, and similar domain terms are never governmental organizations.

group.definition: A mention of multiple people regarded as a collective, category, social unit, or participant set, including cohorts, kinship groups, friendship groups, and generational groups.
include_rules: Annotate common nouns or noun phrases that denote a set of people considered together, such as participant groups, social groups, kin groups, or cohorts.
Annotate family-relation collectives like "family" when they refer to multiple related people as a unit.
Annotate friendship or peer collectives like "friends" when they refer to multiple people.
Annotate generational collectives like "next generation" when they denote a cohort of people.
Annotate study or clinical participant groups such as "patients" when the mention refers to the human subjects collectively.
Annotate both specific and generic human group mentions if the text uses them to refer to a collective of people, whether actual or conceptual.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate singular person mentions or role nouns referring to one individual, such as "user" or "client" in technical instructions.
Do not annotate non-human collections, object sets, file groups, or other technical/system groupings.
Do not annotate bare plural nouns when they do not denote a human collective in context.
Do not annotate numeric expressions, measurements, or study counts by themselves, such as "22" or "Twenty-two".
Do not annotate demographic or descriptive modifiers outside the core group noun when they merely add detail, such as sex counts or medical/history descriptions.
Do not annotate institutions, places, or organizations unless the mention itself denotes the people as a group rather than the entity as an institution.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the group, such as "patients", "family", "friends", or "next generation".
Include modifiers that are part of the group expression when they specify which group is meant, as in "next generation".
Exclude determiners and possessives unless they are inseparable parts of a fixed group name; annotate "family" not "my family" and "patients" not "22 patients".
Exclude surrounding verbs, prepositions, and descriptive context outside the noun phrase naming the group.
When a coordinated list contains multiple distinct group mentions, annotate each separately if each independently denotes a group, as in "family" and "friends".
Do not extend the span to include counts, demographic breakdowns, or appositive details unless they are part of the core group name.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate "group" only when the expression denotes multiple people or a social/cohort collective, not a single person.
Do not annotate role words like "user" or "client" when they refer to an individual participant rather than a collective; plural mentions are only annotated if they function as a human group mention.
Do not annotate generic biological, anatomical, or object collectives unless they clearly denote a human social or participant group.
Distinguish a group from a quantitative description: in "22 patients", the group entity is "patients", while the number is not part of the entity span.

Distinguish a group from broad non-referential class words used only generically in technical exposition; annotate when the text refers to an actual or conceptual set of people, as with "next generation", but not every plural noun in abstract discussion.

group of people.definition: A phrase that refers to a set, category, or cohort of human beings treated as a group in context.
include_rules: Mentions of people treated collectively, such as populations, cohorts, patients, readers, participants, users, or workers, when the phrase denotes humans as a group.
Demographic group phrases that refer to human populations in general or in a study context.
Role-based human groups when the noun clearly denotes people acting in that role (e.g., readers in a study, patients in a medical context).
Nationality-modified or other adjective-modified human groups when the head noun is a people-group noun (e.g., Japanese patients).
Broad collective references to humans in a setting or study, including generic phrases like general population.
exclude_rules: Abstract populations or sample-like mentions that do not clearly denote people in the sentence context.
Non-human groups such as organizations, software classes, materials, or object collections.
Single individuals named or implied as one person rather than a group.
General nouns that are not used to identify people as a group in context.
Occupational, demographic, or nationality words when they are not functioning as a human group mention.
Purely textual or code-related occurrences unrelated to human groups.
boundary_rules: Include the full noun phrase that names the people group, including adjectives and premodifiers that are integral to the group (e.g., general population, Japanese patients).
Include plural head nouns denoting a people group when used in a human-reference sense (e.g., patients, readers).
Do not include surrounding verbs, prepositions, determiners, or clause material.
Do not include appositives, parentheses, or trailing modifiers unless they are part of the group name itself.
If a phrase contains a group plus a role/specifier, keep only the words that directly identify the people group; exclude unrelated setting/location wording.
When multiple people groups are coordinated, annotate each group separately if each is explicitly a group of people.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse with organizations, institutions, or teams unless the phrase explicitly refers to the people as a human group rather than the organization itself.
Do not annotate generic demographic or statistical references to a population unless the phrase specifically denotes people as a group in context.
Do not annotate professions, devices, or textual roles when they refer to non-human entities; only annotate human-role nouns when they clearly refer to people.
Do not annotate race, nationality, or location words by themselves unless they are part of a broader human group phrase (e.g., Japanese patients).
Do not annotate abstract collections, datasets, or study samples unless they are directly described as people.

hardware.definition: Physical computing equipment or tangible electronic/mechanical components used to process, store, transmit, or interface with data, including end-user devices, storage media, and internal parts.
include_rules: Annotate names of physical consumer computing devices, such as tablets, phones, laptops, desktops, and similar equipment.
Annotate physical storage hardware and media, including drives and disks, when referred to as tangible data-storage components.
Annotate internal computer or networking components, such as cards, boards, modules, peripherals, and other replaceable parts.
Annotate both specific product names and common-noun hardware mentions when they denote a concrete physical device or component.
Annotate hardware mentions whether they refer to standalone devices or parts embedded in a larger machine, as long as the text names the physical component directly.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate software, applications, operating systems, code elements, APIs, function names, signals, or user-interface elements.
Do not annotate settings, permissions, privacy options, network layers, or other abstract technical concepts.
Do not annotate generic actions or processes involving hardware unless a concrete device/component is explicitly named.
Do not annotate non-computing objects from other domains, such as medical treatments, body points, chemicals, or drugs.
Do not annotate broad environmental or locational terms that merely surround a hardware mention.
Do not annotate product ecosystems or vendors unless the mention itself names a physical device or component.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal noun phrase that names the physical device or component, including necessary head nouns such as 'drives', 'disks', or 'card'.
Include brand or model names when they are the hardware mention itself, e.g. 'iPad'.
Include modifiers that are part of the hardware expression when they specify the component type, e.g. 'disk drives', 'storage disks', 'switch card'.
Do not include surrounding prepositions, determiners, verbs, or descriptive context, e.g. annotate 'iPad' not 'on the iPad', and 'switch card' not 'as a switch card in one of the routers'.
Do not extend the span to larger host systems unless they are themselves the hardware mention; in 'switch card in one of the routers', annotate only 'switch card', not 'routers'.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate physical computing devices and parts as hardware, not software artifacts such as apps, operating systems, sessions, handlers, signals, or documentation terms.

Do not annotate abstract technical concepts, protocols, layers, settings, permissions, or functions; for example, 'Layer 2', 'Privacy settings', and 'beginBackgroundTaskWithExpirationHandler' are not hardware.
Do not annotate organizations or manufacturers when only the company is mentioned; only annotate the device name if it refers to a physical product.

Do not annotate medical instruments or non-computing treatment terms in unrelated domains unless they clearly denote hardware in the sense of a physical device/component; the clinical example contains no hardware entities.

When a hardware component is mentioned as part of a larger system, annotate the component actually named rather than the broader system unless both are explicitly mentioned as separate hardware items.

header file.definition: A header file is the name of a programming include file, or its explicit include-guard macro identifier, used to declare interfaces or be referenced in include/import directives or discussion of code dependencies.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of programming header files, especially filenames ending in .h, when they are mentioned in code or prose.
Annotate header file names appearing in import/include directives, such as a quoted or bracketed filename in #import or #include.
Annotate header file names mentioned in natural language discussion of including or using headers, e.g. included ntifs.h.
Annotate project- or tool-generated header filenames as long as they denote concrete header files, e.g. Pods-Example-environment.h.
Annotate include-guard macro identifiers when they stand for the header file itself in the code, e.g. AVCODEC_EAIDCT_H in #ifndef/#define for a header guard.
Annotate the entity whether it appears in source code, comments, or explanatory prose, as long as it unambiguously names a header file or its header guard.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary English uses of header, port of entry, article text, or any non-programming document content.
Do not annotate source code symbols that are not header files, such as function names, type names, variables, constants, or APIs.
Do not annotate preprocessor directives themselves, such as #include, #import, #ifdef, #ifndef, or #define.
Do not annotate generic phrases like the header, that header, include that header, or header file unless a specific header name or include-guard identifier is given.
Do not annotate framework names, package names, directories, or module names unless the mention is specifically the header filename.
Do not annotate file names with other roles or extensions unless they are clearly being used as header files in this annotation type.
Do not annotate macro identifiers unless they specifically serve as the header's include guard or otherwise directly name the header in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal exact string that names the header file or header guard macro, with no surrounding keywords or punctuation.
For include/import statements, include only the file name inside quotes or angle brackets if that file name is the target mention; exclude #include, #import, quotes, and angle brackets.
For prose mentions, include just the header name token, including its extension when present, e.g. winternl.h and ntifs.h.
For header guard mentions, annotate the macro identifier only when it is used as the header's include guard name, e.g. AVCODEC_EAIDCT_H; exclude #ifndef, #define, comments, and surrounding syntax.
Include internal punctuation that is part of the file name, such as hyphens, underscores, and the final .h extension.
Do not extend the span to nearby function names, module names, directories, or descriptive words like header, file, included, or import.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate header file names and header guard macros, not source files, libraries, frameworks, classes, functions, or variables.
A token ending in .h is a header file when it denotes an includable file; do not relabel non-file strings simply because they contain punctuation.
Uppercase identifiers like AVCODEC_EAIDCT_H should be annotated only when they function as include guards for a header, not when they are ordinary macros or constants.
Do not confuse the programming concept of a header file with general English uses of header, heading, or article metadata.
Names of APIs or symbols mentioned near a header file, such as ZwCreatFile, are not header files and should not be annotated.
Framework or package names are not header files unless the mention is specifically the concrete header filename, such as Pods-Example-environment.h.

health condition.definition: A mention of a physical, physiological, or general health state or condition of an organism, including broad health-status expressions and specific bodily condition concepts.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote a health state, physiological status, or medical condition.
Annotate broad health-status expressions, including generic mentions of overall health, when they refer to the condition of a person or group.
Annotate condition phrases formed with a body system or body domain plus a health/status term, such as "skeletal health."
Annotate physiological condition concepts even when they are measurable attributes, such as "blood pressure," when they are referenced as the health-related state itself.
Annotate condition mentions whether they describe normal, abnormal, improved, worsened, or unspecified health states, as long as the span names the state or condition.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate organizations, events, locations, legal cases, government actions, contracts, or other non-medical entities.
Do not annotate behaviors, exposures, treatments, or activities by themselves, such as smoking or exercise, unless the span explicitly names a health condition.
Do not annotate generic demographic or lifestyle factors such as age or general lifestyle.
Do not annotate abstract mentions of risk, severity, controversy, interest, capacity, or quality when they do not name a health condition.
Do not annotate isolated anatomical terms unless they are part of a health condition phrase.
Do not annotate purely administrative, bibliographic, or meeting-related text with no health-state meaning.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the health condition concept itself.

Include modifiers that are part of the condition phrase, such as affected body system or status descriptors, when they are needed for the meaning: "blood pressure," "skeletal health," "state of health."

Exclude surrounding triggers, causes, measurements, and context words that are not part of the condition name, such as verbs and prepositions: in "lowers blood pressure," annotate only "blood pressure."
Exclude person or population references attached to the condition unless they are part of a fixed condition expression.
Do not extend the span to include sentence-level context about risk, severity, change, or investigation unless those words are part of the condition mention itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the health condition itself, not an intervention, behavior, or exposure associated with it; for example, in "Cigarette smoking lowers blood pressure," annotate "blood pressure" but not "Cigarette smoking."
Annotate a health state or physiological condition, not a body part alone; body-related words are included only when they form a condition phrase such as "skeletal health."
Annotate the condition phrase, not a general topic, institution, event, legal matter, or policy context; text about meetings, courts, contracts, or organizations is not a health condition.
Annotate general health-state expressions when they refer to a person's or population's condition, even if broad rather than disease-specific, such as "state of health."

Do not confuse qualities or evaluations about products, services, or procedures with health conditions; words like "sub-standard" or "quality" are not health conditions unless they describe a medical state.

highway.definition: A named highway, numbered route, or specifically identified roadway used for vehicular travel, including official route designations and named highway expressions.
include_rules: Annotate official numbered highway and route names, including forms with country or route prefixes, such as "U.S. Route 98" and "US 71B".
Annotate named highways composed of place names plus a road-class term, such as "Bangalore-Mysore highway".
Annotate alphanumeric route designations when they specifically identify a highway or route.
Annotate highway mentions whether they occur as standalone names or inside larger travel/location descriptions, as long as the span itself names the highway.
Include both formal and commonly written variants of highway names when they clearly refer to a specific roadway.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic mentions of roads or routes that lack a specific name or identifier, such as standalone "the highway" or "the route".
Do not annotate non-road transportation structures or related infrastructure, such as bridges, tunnels, exits, or intersections, unless the highway name itself is explicitly mentioned.
