Do not annotate geographic entities such as countries or regions where a language is spoken, e.g. "Brazil", "Mexico", and "Canada" are not language.
Do not confuse language with software products or assistants; in "Cortana will also be coming... and in French", annotate "French" as the language option, not "Cortana".

Do not confuse language with generic technical vocabulary that merely resembles a language name; words like "anomaly", "filter", or domain-specific terms are not language unless they explicitly name a human, programming, or markup language.
Annotate named formal languages such as markup or programming languages when they are referenced as languages used for writing or encoding content, e.g. "html".

law.definition: A law entity is a named or descriptive expression that denotes a legal rule, statute, restriction, prohibition, or other enforceable legal provision.
include_rules: Annotate expressions that denote a law, statute, ban, prohibition, ordinance, regulation, or similar legal restriction.
Annotate common-noun legal expressions even when they are not official titles, such as 'ban', 'statute of limitations', and 'law'.
Annotate descriptive noun phrases referring to a specific law when the modifiers are part of the mention, such as 'controversial new law'.
Annotate legal doctrines or procedural legal rules when they function as established legal concepts, such as 'statute of limitations'.
Annotate laws mentioned through their operative legal form even without a jurisdiction name or formal title, provided the context makes clear they are legal measures.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate organizations, government bodies, officials, or courts that create, sign, enforce, or overturn laws.
Do not annotate legal actions or processes by themselves, such as signing, overturning, extending, enforcing, or prosecuting, unless the span explicitly names the law being acted upon.
Do not annotate generic references to time periods, penalties, cases, rights, or obligations unless the expression itself denotes a legal rule or legal doctrine.
Do not annotate scientific studies, medical protocols, treatment guidelines, or monitoring procedures; these are not laws even when they impose procedural requirements.
Do not annotate ordinary bans or restrictions that are purely practical, institutional, or medical unless the context indicates they are legal/public-rule prohibitions.
Do not annotate topics or objects affected by the law when they are separate from the legal term itself, such as 'headscarves in public places' in a sentence about a ban.
boundary_rules: Annotate the noun phrase that denotes the legal rule or legal restriction itself.
Include core modifiers that are part of the referring expression for the law, such as descriptive adjectives in 'controversial new law'.
Include attached 'of' complements when they are part of the legal term, as in 'statute of limitations'.

For common legal measures, annotate the legal head noun and only the modifiers needed to identify that law or rule; do not extend the span to surrounding actions, dates, officials, durations, or affected subjects unless they are inside the noun phrase.
Do not include verbs describing enactment, repeal, extension, enforcement, or violation of the law, such as 'overturned' or 'signed'.

Do not include broader event or policy context outside the legal-expression span, e.g., in 'a ban on headscarves in public places', annotate 'ban' rather than the full affected-topic phrase unless the topic phrase is part of the official law name.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate legal rules, restrictions, or statutes as law, not the political process or event surrounding them; e.g., 'law' or 'ban' is law, while the signing or overturning is not.
Do not annotate studies, protocols, monitoring procedures, or research programs as law, even if they use formal terminology such as 'protocol-driven' or 'required'.
Do not annotate medications, treatment regimens, or clinical management rules as law; these are medical entities or practices, not legal instruments.
Distinguish law from general policy or social practice: annotate only when the expression denotes an enforceable legal restriction, statute, or formally described legal rule.
Distinguish law from duration or penalty details: in phrases about extending or changing a legal rule, annotate the rule itself ('statute of limitations'), not the time values attached to it.

league.definition: A named sports league, competition, or formally recognized class/tier of play used to organize competitive participation in a sport.
include_rules: Annotate official names of sports leagues and competitions, e.g. "FA Futsal League".
Annotate common abbreviations or acronyms that unambiguously refer to a sports league or competition, e.g. "NFL".
Annotate formally recognized class or division labels when they denote a competition level within a sport, e.g. "first-class" in cricket.
Annotate league mentions whether they refer to domestic, national, regional, or professional competition structures, as long as the expression identifies the competition itself.
Annotate the mention even when it appears inside a longer phrase, provided the span corresponds exactly to the league name.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate mentions that are only generic references to a league or competition without a specific name, such as "league system" or "contest".
Do not annotate sports, events, or trophies that are not league/competition names in context.
Do not annotate governing bodies, clubs, teams, countries, or athletes unless the mention itself denotes the league.
Do not annotate non-sports uses of similar words or abbreviations, including biomedical terms such as "lymphatics" or unrelated common nouns.
Do not annotate descriptive modifiers of play level unless they are an established competition/class label in context.
boundary_rules: Include the full league or competition name when it appears as a proper name, including governing-body or sponsor prefixes that are part of the name, e.g. "FA Futsal League".
Include standalone abbreviated league names when they clearly denote the competition or league system, e.g. "NFL".
Include class or tier labels only when they function as the formal competition/class designation in the sport context, e.g. "first-class" in cricket.

Do not include surrounding descriptive words that are not part of the league name, such as "system" in "national league system" or explanatory nouns like "Bowl" when not part of the annotated league mention.
Do not extend the span to include teams, seasons, locations, or sentence punctuation unless they are part of the official league name.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the competition or league itself, not the governing organization unless the mention is used as the league name; for example, "FA Futsal League" is a league, while "FA" alone would be an organization if it refers only to the governing body.
Do not confuse league names with teams or national sides; in "appeared for England", "England" refers to a team/nation, not a league.
Do not confuse league mentions with sports themselves; "cricket" is the sport, while "first-class" is the competition/class level.
Do not annotate generic structural terms like "national league system" unless a specific named league is present.
Do not annotate unrelated lowercase words that merely resemble the string "lymph" or similar orthography; only sports-league semantics qualify.

legal case.definition: A reference to a legal proceeding, lawsuit, civil action, appeal matter, or category of judicial cases, expressed by a case title, docket-style designation, or a noun phrase denoting legal cases.
include_rules: Annotate explicit references to specific legal proceedings, including named cases such as "Gold Reserve case".
Annotate docket-style or formal proceeding identifiers when they denote a legal matter, such as "Civil Action No. 16053".
Annotate common-noun phrases that denote a recognizable category or class of legal proceedings, such as "child care cases".
Include singular or plural mentions when the phrase refers to legal disputes or judicial matters rather than to people, documents, or abstract law.
Annotate both official-sounding and descriptive case references if the text uses them to identify a legal matter being discussed.
Annotate case mentions introduced by legal context words like "appeal", "civil action", or similar wording when the full phrase denotes the proceeding itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-legal uses of "case" or "cases", including ordinary situations, examples, containers, grammatical case, or medical/scientific case descriptions.
Do not annotate disease names, scientific terms, gene/protein names, organisms, or article titles that contain the word "case" or appear in unrelated technical text.
Do not annotate standalone legal actions or filings such as "appeal motions" unless the text also names the underlying legal case and that case reference is the entity.
Do not annotate party-role phrases like "plaintiff Womble", "defendant", or "Mrs." unless they occur inside the case name itself.
Do not annotate generic legal vocabulary like "plaintiff", "defendant", "civil action", or "appeal" when used without a specific case-denoting reference.
Do not annotate statutes, regulations, contracts, court decisions in the abstract, or legal doctrines unless they are referred to as a specific case or case category.
Do not annotate isolated docket numbers or document numbers unless the full expression explicitly identifies them as a legal case, action, or similar proceeding.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full case-designating span, including the specific case name or docket-style identifier and the legal proceeding head word when it is part of the expression, e.g., "Gold Reserve case" and "Civil Action No. 16053".
Include legal numbering markers that are part of the case reference, such as "No. 16053" in "Civil Action No. 16053".
For common-noun case references, include the modifier(s) that specify the case type together with the head word, e.g., "child care cases" rather than only "cases".
Do not include surrounding prepositions, determiners, or reporting/context words such as "in the", "appeal motions in the", or "which involves plaintiff".
Do not extend the span to include participant descriptions unless they are part of the official case reference.
When a legal case mention appears in plural form, annotate the full plural expression if it denotes a class of legal cases being discussed, e.g., "child care cases".
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish legal case from laws, regulations, or legal doctrines: annotate the proceeding or dispute being referenced, not the governing rule or principle.
Distinguish legal case from court names or legal institutions: a court is the adjudicating body, while a legal case is the matter before it.

Distinguish legal case from people in the case such as plaintiffs, defendants, judges, or appellants: annotate the case reference, not the participant names, unless those names are part of the case title itself.
Distinguish legal case from motions, appeals, or filings: annotate the case that such motions belong to, not the procedural document alone.
Distinguish legal case from non-legal uses of "case" meaning instance, situation, grammatical case, storage case, or medical/patient case; only legal proceeding senses should be annotated.
Numeric or alphanumeric identifiers are legal cases only when the surrounding expression explicitly marks them as a case/proceeding, such as "Civil Action No. 16053"; isolated numbers are not enough.

legal citation.definition: A formal reference used to identify a legal case or judicial source, typically appearing as a reporter citation, docket/case number, or specific court designation in legal case text.
include_rules: Annotate standard reporter citations consisting of a volume number, reporter abbreviation, and page number, e.g. patterns like "11 N.Y.3d 854" and "492 F.2d 1244".
Annotate docket or case numbers that serve as formal legal identifiers for a case, including hyphenated number strings such as "73-1838" or "97-4246".
Annotate specific court designations when they function as part of identifying the legal authority or case source, e.g. "Eleventh Circuit".
Include citations even when they appear on separate lines, in all caps, or embedded in court-document formatting.
Annotate the citation regardless of whether the full case title, court, date, or disposition also appears nearby, as long as the target span is the formal legal reference itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate party names or case captions by themselves, such as names before and after "v.".
Do not annotate standalone dates, years, or parenthetical decision years when they are separate from the core citation string.
Do not annotate generic legal procedural text such as dispositions, court actions, venue references, or headings that do not function as a citation.
Do not annotate non-legal numeric strings such as phone numbers, product numbers, scientific values, or biological/chemical abbreviations.
Do not annotate organization names, websites, media brands, or person names unless they are part of a legal citation form.
Do not annotate section-free narrative references to legal material if they lack a formal citation pattern.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the citation string itself, not the surrounding case caption, court heading, disposition, or date unless that element is itself a cited legal identifier included by these rules.

For reporter citations, include the full volume-reporter-page sequence, e.g. "11 N.Y.3d 854" or "492 F.2d 1244"; exclude following parenthetical years such as "(2008)" unless the annotation task explicitly treats the year as part of the citation format.

For docket or case numbers, include the complete identifier including internal hyphens and any leading label only if it is part of the cited form; e.g. annotate "73-1838" and "97-4246", but exclude surrounding text such as "NO." when it functions only as a label.

For court identifiers used as legal citation components, include the specific court name or numbered court designation, e.g. "Eleventh Circuit"; exclude broader surrounding text like "United States Court of Appeals," unless the full court name is the cited legal reference to be captured as one unit in context.
Do not extend the span to party names such as "PEOPLE v DUNN" or "Welch v. Gilligan" when the annotation target is the citation rather than the case title.
Exclude punctuation, line breaks, and formatting artifacts before or after the citation unless they are internal to the citation string itself.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish legal citation from a case name: annotate formal numeric/alphanumeric legal references such as reporter cites, docket numbers, and court designations used to identify a case source, not the litigant names joined by "v.".

Distinguish legal citation from dates: years or filing/decision dates like "(2008)" or "2/8/74" are not legal citations unless they are inseparably embedded in the citation string under project-specific formatting.

Distinguish legal citation from court headings or organizational names: annotate a court term like "Eleventh Circuit" when it functions as the identifying legal court reference, but not generic institutional text or headers that merely describe the issuing body.

Distinguish legal citation from ordinary numbers or codes: phone numbers, article identifiers, scientific notation, biological nomenclature, and other non-legal alphanumeric strings are not legal citations.

Distinguish legal citation from document metadata: dispositions such as "VACATED AND REMANDED", venue abbreviations, or filing labels are not citations unless they are part of a formal cited identifier.

legal document.definition: A legal document is a named or generic document or formal written instrument that records, establishes, or governs legal rights, obligations, terms, transfers, or agreements, such as contracts, deeds, or terms-and-conditions documents.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of legal or contractual documents, whether generic or titled, such as contracts, agreements, deeds, and terms-and-conditions documents.
Annotate formal written instruments that establish or evidence legal rights, obligations, consent, transfer, or governing terms.
Annotate both full multiword document names and generic singular document nouns when they clearly refer to a legal document in context.

Annotate a document mention even when it is referred to anaphorically or generically, if the surrounding context makes clear it is a legal instrument, e.g., "Agreement" in a service-acceptance context.
Annotate legal document titles introduced by determiners or references, but select only the document title itself as the entity span.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate court cases, docket numbers, court names, reporters, appeal information, or judicial dispositions such as "U.S. v. Townsend", "United States Court of Appeals", or "AFFIRMED".
Do not annotate statutes, procedural rule citations, or abbreviated legal references when they appear as citations rather than document mentions, e.g., "Fed.R.App.P. 34(a)" or "5th Cir.R. 34.2".
Do not annotate section headings, article structure markers, footnotes, bibliography markers, or non-legal document formatting text.
Do not annotate general references to services, benefits, use, acceptance, transfer, or other events unless the phrase explicitly names the legal document involved.

Do not annotate adjectives, pronouns, or determiners surrounding the document mention when they are not part of the document title, e.g., exclude "these" in "these Terms and Conditions" and "the changed" in "the changed Agreement".
Do not annotate non-legal documents or general written materials unless the context clearly indicates a legal instrument.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the legal document itself, including internal modifiers that are part of the document name, e.g., "Terms and Conditions".
Include the full title when the document is referred to by a conventional multiword name; do not split parts of the title into separate entities.

For generic single-word document references, annotate just the document noun, e.g., "deed" or "Agreement", without surrounding determiners or descriptive context such as "these", "the changed", or "a" unless they are part of the fixed title.
Do not include nearby verbs, acceptance/use language, numbering, punctuation, or section markers that are not part of the document name.

If a legal document mention appears inside a larger citation or procedural string, annotate it only if the span itself denotes a document rather than a court, case, rule citation, or proceeding reference.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate legal instruments or contractual documents, not legal cases, court names, or judicial decisions. For example, a case citation or court heading is not a legal document mention of this type.
Distinguish the document from legal procedures or acts: annotate "deed" as the instrument itself, but not actions involving it such as recording, filing, signing, or appealing.

Do not annotate rules or citation abbreviations used as procedural references in case headers when they function as legal authorities or court rules rather than a concrete document mention in running text.
Distinguish legal documents from ordinary documents or scientific articles: only annotate texts that create, record, or state legal rights, obligations, or formal legal terms.
When a word like "Agreement" refers to a binding legal arrangement/document, annotate it; when similar words are used abstractly or non-legally without denoting an actual document, do not annotate.

legal term.definition: A word or phrase that denotes a legal concept, doctrine, status, procedure, penalty, jurisdictional category, or formally recognized legal classification used in legal or regulatory contexts.
include_rules: Annotate phrases that directly name legal concepts or recognized legal categories, including procedural terms, penalties, and jurisdictional or regulatory classifications.
Annotate sentencing and punishment terms when expressed as legal penalties, e.g. "life sentences".
Annotate court procedure and adjudication terms, e.g. "summary judgment".

Annotate legal-regulatory status or classification phrases, especially those modifying jurisdiction, trade, registration, or similar formally defined categories, e.g. "foreign commerce" and "foreign registry".
Annotate both singular and plural forms when they denote the legal concept.
Annotate the term whenever it is used with legal meaning in context, even if it also has a broader non-legal sense outside that context.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general non-legal technical, medical, scientific, or everyday vocabulary, even if it appears in formal writing.
Do not annotate names of people, countries, organizations, courts, government bodies, or other actors involved in a legal matter.

Do not annotate legal actions expressed only as verbs or event descriptions unless the phrase itself names a legal concept or procedure. For example, exclude "arrested" and "convicted" but include "life sentences".

Do not annotate entire motions, claims, or case descriptions when only a subpart is the legal term. For example, exclude "motion for summary judgment" if the target concept is only "summary judgment".
Do not annotate generic words like "court", "case", "law", "support", "risk", or "application" unless they are part of a specific legal term expression.

Do not annotate references from unrelated domains that merely resemble formal terminology, such as software installation, research administration, or public health text without a legal concept mention.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that expresses the legal term itself, usually a noun phrase such as "summary judgment" or "life sentences".
Include modifiers that are part of the fixed legal expression or are necessary to specify the legal concept, e.g. "foreign commerce" and "foreign registry".

Exclude surrounding function words, possessives, and syntactic context such as articles, prepositions, verbs, and party names: annotate "summary judgment", not "for summary judgment" or "the United States' motion for summary judgment".
When a legal term appears inside a larger non-entity phrase, mark only the legal term portion, not the broader event or clause.
Keep plural marking when it is part of the expressed term, e.g. "life sentences" rather than "life".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse legal terms with organizations, courts, agencies, or parties. In "The district court granted the United States' motion for summary judgment", only "summary judgment" is the legal term; "district court" and "United States" are not.

Do not confuse legal terms with legal events or actions. Words like "arrested", "convicted", or "granted" describe acts in a case, while the annotatable entity is the doctrinal or procedural term such as "summary judgment" or the penalty term "life sentences".

Do not confuse legal terms with ordinary domain vocabulary. A phrase should be annotated only if it denotes a recognized legal status, process, penalty, right, jurisdictional category, or regulatory concept, not merely a general topic.

Do not confuse legal terms with document titles or laws unless the mention is the legal concept itself. Here, the target type covers terms like "foreign registry" and "summary judgment", not named statutes or conventions mentioned as titles.

Economic or administrative phrases should be annotated only when used as legal/jurisdictional categories, as in "foreign commerce"; ordinary commercial discussion without legal meaning should be excluded.

letter.definition: A character-level symbol mention denoting an individual alphabetic letter used as a variable, token, or textual symbol, or a word naming a class of letters such as consonants or vowels.
include_rules: Annotate standalone single letters when they are explicitly mentioned as symbols, variables, or textual elements, e.g. "a", "b", "s".
Annotate letter names or category terms when they denote sets or types of letters, e.g. "consonants" and "vowels".
Annotate letters that appear in mathematical, programming, or string-description contexts when the letter itself is the referenced object.
Annotate quoted or formatted letters if the quotation or formatting only highlights the letter and the semantic referent is still the letter itself.
Annotate plural/common-noun references to letter classes when they directly identify kinds of letters rather than broader language concepts.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary words that happen to contain letters; the mention must refer to the letter itself or a letter category.
Do not annotate multi-letter abbreviations, acronyms, product names, chemical/material names, or technical identifiers such as "ABS", "SAN", "ASP.NET", or "MySql".
Do not annotate numbers, operators, punctuation, or full mathematical expressions when they are not themselves letter mentions.
Do not annotate generic nouns unrelated to letters, such as "user", "account", "database", or "property".
Do not annotate alphabetic text embedded in larger identifiers when the text is functioning as a code, variable name, or compound token rather than as a standalone letter mention.
Do not annotate pronouns, articles, or single-character text unless the context shows it is explicitly being used as a letter/symbol.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the minimal span that denotes the letter expression itself.
For single symbolic variables, mark just the symbol token, e.g. "a", "b", "s".
When the mention is a category word referring to sets of letters, include only that noun, e.g. "consonants", "vowels".
Do not include surrounding punctuation, quotation marks, math delimiters, articles, or nearby descriptive words.
If a letter appears inside a larger alphanumeric or technical token, do not extract the larger token unless the isolated letter itself is explicitly the referenced item.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish letter from general words: annotate only when the token denotes a letter symbol or a class of letters, not when the same character sequence is used as an ordinary word or abbreviation.
Distinguish letter from variables or mathematical expressions with broader scope: annotate the individual symbolic letter itself, not the full formula, equation, number, or expression around it.

Distinguish letter from strings, codes, usernames, product names, or acronyms: do not annotate multi-character technical tokens such as "ASP.NET", "ABS", or "MySql" unless the text explicitly refers to a single letter or a letter class.
Distinguish letter from phonetic or linguistic descriptions: annotate the explicit letter/category term, not surrounding explanations about selection, rules, or grammar.
Distinguish letter from numbers and units: in mixed expressions like "a_number" or "$4$", annotate the letter if it is separately referenced, but not the numeric part.

library.definition: A named software library or reusable code package/component referenced as a software dependency, implementation library, or code library.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of software libraries or reusable code packages/components.
Include names referred to in software-development context such as discussing source code, implementation choice, usage, or dependency selection.
Annotate both single-word and multi-word library names when they function as proper names, e.g., 'Libevent' and 'Query Builder'.
Annotate the name even when followed or preceded by a generic descriptor like 'library', as long as the descriptor is not part of the name.
Annotate library names regardless of whether they are open source, proprietary, or mentioned only once in context.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic mentions of a library when no specific name is given, e.g., 'a library', 'this library', or 'source code'.
Do not annotate generic technical phrases that are not clearly proper names of libraries, even if they look software-related.
Do not annotate names of drugs, chemicals, biomedical treatments, or formulations.
Do not annotate people, places, organizations, vehicles, institutions, or news entities.
Do not annotate protocols, flags, constants, methods, file names, or operating-system features used by a library.
Do not annotate broader project descriptions or documentation topics unless they directly name a library.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the library name itself, excluding surrounding descriptors such as 'library', 'source code of', 'about', 'use', or articles/determiners.
If a common noun follows the name as a descriptor, exclude it when it is not part of the official name: annotate 'Redemption' in 'the Redemption library'.
Include capitalization and internal spacing that are part of the name, e.g., 'Query Builder' and 'Libevent'.
For multi-word names, include all contiguous words that form the library name; do not split the name unless only a subpart is explicitly used as the standalone library name in context.
Do not extend the span to nearby technical terms, features, protocols, or APIs that the library uses or discusses, such as 'EPOLLONESHOT'.
Do not annotate bracketed or quoted context unless the bracketed/quoted text itself is the library name.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate software features, modules, tools, or functions unless the text indicates they are a library. 'Query Builder' should be annotated only when used as the name of a software library/component, not as a generic capability.
Do not annotate programming concepts, APIs, constants, or system facilities referenced by a library, such as 'EPOLLONESHOT'.
Do not annotate products, applications, or platforms unless they are specifically referred to as a reusable software library.
Do not annotate biomedical substances, drugs, or chemical compounds such as 'mebendazole' or 'albendazole'; these are not software libraries even if the surface form resembles a product name.
Do not annotate places, organizations, transport services, or institutions such as 'Eurotunnel', 'Met Police', or 'Scotland Yard'; these are non-software entities.
When the same string could denote either a generic phrase or a library name, rely on software-development context and naming usage; annotate only the library interpretation.

license.definition: A named software, content, or legal distribution license referenced by its specific title or conventional license name.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of licenses used to specify legal terms for software or content distribution.
Include well-formed official or conventional license titles, including expanded names and abbreviated names when they function as the license name.
Include license names that contain organization names, version numbers, or family terms as part of the title, such as "Apache 2.0 License" and "GNU General Public License".
Annotate the license name whether it appears in prose, comments, notices, or documentation-style text.
Annotate a license mention when it appears after licensing cues such as "under", "distributed under", or "see", but annotate only the license name itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text with no license mention, even if it discusses access, content, rights, inventions, or legal/technical matters.
Do not annotate generic terms such as "license", "software license", or "open-source license" when no specific named license is given.
Do not annotate descriptive context around a license mention, such as "distributed under", "for more details", or references to files, directories, or software components.
Do not annotate non-license legal documents, policies, patents, inventions, or technical descriptions.
Do not annotate adjectival or clause-level descriptors that are not part of the license name span, such as "3-clause" in the provided BSD example boundary.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the license itself.

Include the core license name and its identifying modifiers when they are part of the official or conventional name, such as version/number designators and family names: "Apache 2.0 License", "GNU General Public License".

If a longer phrase contains descriptive material plus the license name, include only the actual license name span. Example: in "distributed under the 3-clause BSD License", annotate "BSD License", not "the 3-clause BSD License".
Exclude preceding determiners, prepositions, and licensing verbs such as "the", "under", "distributed under", "licensed under", unless they are inseparable parts of the formal name.
Include the word "License" when it is part of the named form appearing in text.
Do not extend the span to trailing explanatory phrases such as "for more details" or to surrounding punctuation.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate named licenses, not general mentions of legal permission or the common noun sense of "license/licence".
Do not annotate generic software distribution statements unless they contain a specific license name.
Do not annotate organizations, projects, products, or software components that may appear near a license name; only the legal license title is the target.
Do not annotate technical, legal, or patent-document text that lacks an actual license mention, even if it contains words like "access", "rights", "provided", or other permission-related language.
When a phrase mixes a license family descriptor with a named license, annotate only the portion that functions as the recognized license name in context.

list.definition: A named or clearly referential list-like collection, registry, register, playlist, queue, or other ordered/grouped container of items, including formal titled lists and technical identifiers used as list stores.
include_rules: Annotate formal names of registries, registers, and official lists of entities or items.
Annotate common nouns that explicitly denote list-like collections in context, such as 'playlists'.
Annotate technical or system identifiers when they name a collection used to hold, queue, or manage items for future use, such as 'MASTER'.
Annotate both singular and plural mentions when the referent is a list-type container rather than the items themselves.
Include named collections regardless of domain, including cultural registries, media playlists, and software/data-management lists.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary code, shell commands, paths, filenames, variables, or constants unless they specifically denote a list-like collection.
Do not annotate general medical, scientific, or narrative nouns that are not list structures, such as body parts, cases, treatments, or abstract concepts.
Do not annotate mentions of individual items that may appear on a list; only the list or registry itself is annotated.
Do not annotate generic grouping language when no specific list-like entity is referred to, such as vague mentions of items, records, or data without a collection name or clear list semantics.
Do not annotate surrounding actions or relations involving the list, such as 'listed on', 'copy metadata', or 'push this number back onto'.
Do not annotate legal licenses, headers, or document boilerplate unless they themselves are list-like collections, which the examples do not support.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the list-like collection itself.
Include all words that are part of the official or lexicalized name of the list, such as 'National Register of Historic Places'.
For common-noun mentions, include the head noun and necessary modifiers only if they are part of the collection name; e.g. annotate 'playlists' but not surrounding verbs or affected objects.
Do not include preceding determiners, possessives, or quantifiers unless they are part of the formal title.
Do not include trailing temporal, locative, or descriptive context outside the list name, such as 'in 1978' or 'affected'.
For code or technical text, annotate only the identifier naming the list-like store or queue itself, e.g. 'MASTER', without adjacent operators, punctuation, or variable syntax.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish list from organizations or institutions: if a phrase denotes a maintained registry, register, playlist, queue, or named collection of items, annotate it as list even if the title contains words often associated with institutions, as in 'National Register of Historic Places'.
Distinguish list from locations or facilities: when a place name appears inside the title of a registry or list, annotate the whole registry name as list, not the embedded place alone.
Distinguish list from products, documents, or publications: annotate the collection/container of entries, not an individual entry, article, case report, or file.

Distinguish list from software variables in general: only annotate technical identifiers when they function semantically as a list, queue, registry, or collection for storing items; ordinary variables, paths, commands, and code tokens are excluded.
Distinguish list from generic plural objects: annotate common nouns like 'playlists' only when they refer to list-type collections, not merely any set of things described loosely as multiple items.

living organism.definition: A living organism is any individual or group name referring to a living biological entity, including animals, humans, plants, fungi, lichens, and other organisms mentioned as living beings.
include_rules: Annotate names of living beings, including humans, animals, plants, fungi, lichens, and similar organism groups.
Annotate common or collective nouns that directly refer to organisms in general or a class of organisms.
Annotate organism mentions whether singular, plural, or collective when they refer to living entities.
Annotate taxonomic or everyday organism names when they identify a living entity in context.
Annotate organism mentions in scientific, medical, ecological, or general prose when they denote the living entity itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-living entities such as code, configuration values, file paths, copyrights, software, or other technical text.
Do not annotate generic biological terms that name processes, substances, or properties rather than organisms.
Do not annotate anatomical parts, cells, molecules, hormones, or other components of organisms.
Do not annotate abstract categories or descriptors unless they directly name an organism group in the text.
Do not annotate phrases where the apparent organism word is used metaphorically or in a non-biological sense.
boundary_rules: Include the full common noun phrase for the organism name when it is the head of the mention (e.g., "animals", "humans", "flowers", "lichens").
Do not include surrounding determiners, adjectives, quantifiers, or descriptive modifiers unless they are an inseparable part of the organism name itself.
Do not extend the span to include verbs, prepositional phrases, habitat words, or other context following or preceding the organism mention.
If the mention is plural or collective, annotate exactly the plural/collective form appearing in text.
For binomial or scientific names, annotate the organism name itself if present, but do not add taxonomic rank labels or surrounding explanatory text.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse living organism with body parts, cells, tissues, organs, substances, or biological molecules; only annotate the organism as a whole living entity.
Do not annotate broad ecological or biological categories that are not actual organisms in context unless they clearly name a living taxon or organism group.
Do not annotate human-made objects, materials, software terms, file names, code tokens, or documentation text even if they contain common nouns that resemble organism names.
Do not annotate descriptive words about living things when they are used attributively rather than as the organism mention itself.
When a term can refer either to an organism or to a non-living concept, annotate only the reading that clearly denotes a living entity.

location.definition: A location is a place, area, region, or site that denotes where something is situated, happens, or can be visited, including named geographic areas and place-denoting common-noun venues or shopping places used as destinations.
include_rules: Annotate named geographic places such as cities, districts, states, regions, and other administrative or natural areas when they denote a place: e.g., "Divisoria", "Michigan".
Annotate common-noun place expressions when they refer to physical destinations or sites people go to or are located at: e.g., "thrift shops", "garage sales".

Annotate locations introduced by spatial or motion context such as "in", "at", "on", "to", "from", or verbs like "visit", "established", "driving", when the phrase denotes where an action occurs or where someone goes.
Annotate both proper names and descriptive place expressions if they identify a place in the sentence.
Annotate repeated mentions independently whenever each mention denotes a location.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate organizations, companies, institutions, or products, even if their names contain place words or are associated with a place.
Do not annotate named establishments when they are presented primarily as business or venue names rather than as generic place expressions, such as nightclub or lounge names in news text.
Do not annotate URLs, website names, repositories, usernames, hashtags, or other technical strings.
Do not annotate generic event nouns, activities, or objects that are not place expressions.
Do not annotate demonyms, nationality terms, or population references unless they clearly denote the geographic place itself.
Do not annotate broad contextual text around a place mention, such as "the Arkansas capital" when no standalone location span is being targeted under these examples.
Do not annotate locations that appear only inside larger excluded mentions if the larger expression functions as a non-location entity in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the location expression itself, excluding surrounding prepositions, determiners, verbs, and punctuation: e.g., "in Divisoria" -> annotate "Divisoria" only.

Include all words that are part of the place name or place-denoting expression when they function together as one location mention: e.g., multiword common-noun places such as "thrift shops" and "garage sales" are annotated as full spans.
For administrative or geographic names, include the full place name but not larger surrounding descriptive context unless it is part of the name.
Do not extend the span to include event, organization, building, or venue names unless the expression is being used strictly as the location mention and the full name is the place expression.
Do not annotate URLs, handles, document formatting, or other non-running-text strings even if they contain place-like substrings.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish location from organization: annotate a place where something happens, exists, or is visited, not the institution or business operating there. For example, generic shopping places like "thrift shops" are locations when mentioned as places to visit.

Distinguish location from facility/venue-like names used as named establishments: if an expression primarily refers to a specific business, club, bank branch, or lounge as an institution rather than a place category, do not annotate it as location under these guidelines.

Distinguish location from geopolitical references used only adjectivally or metonymically for governments, organizations, or populations; annotate only when the term denotes the physical place or region itself, as in "Michigan winters."
Distinguish location from addresses, links, and technical strings: repository URLs, site paths, and similar text are not location mentions even if they look like names.
Distinguish location from events: words like "concert" are not locations unless the text names the place where the event occurs.

machine.definition: A physical machine or mechanical device used to perform a practical task, including industrial equipment, agricultural machinery, and similar apparatus referred to by a common noun or machine-type phrase.
include_rules: Annotate common nouns or noun phrases that denote physical machines or mechanical devices.
Annotate industrial, agricultural, or processing equipment used to carry out a specific mechanical task.
Annotate both singular and plural mentions of machine-denoting nouns, e.g. "machine" and "machines".
Annotate multiword machine-type expressions when the full phrase names the device, e.g. "self-propelling harvester thresher".
Annotate device names even when they are generic rather than branded, as long as they refer to a concrete machine or apparatus.
Annotate simple apparatus terms that function as equipment in context, such as "sieves" in a processing operation.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate abstract concepts, processes, tasks, or functions, even if machines perform them.
Do not annotate software, code elements, database functions, GIS tools, or other computational artifacts.
Do not annotate buildings, production lines, road networks, or other non-machine physical structures.
Do not annotate materials, parts, outputs, or substances handled by a machine unless the text explicitly names the whole machine.
Do not annotate mentions of people or occupations associated with machinery.
Do not annotate overly broad environmental nouns that only provide context, such as "manufacturing line," when they are not themselves machines.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal noun phrase that names the machine, including descriptive modifiers that are part of the machine name or type, e.g. "self-propelling harvester thresher".
Include common adjectives, participles, and compound modifiers when they specify the kind of machine rather than an incidental property.
Include the head noun denoting the device, such as "machine", "sieve", "harvester", or "thresher".
Exclude surrounding determiners, quantifiers, and discourse words unless they are inseparable parts of the machine expression.
Exclude verbs and action phrases outside the machine noun phrase, e.g. in "used for this separating and washing process," only annotate "sieves".
When a coordinated phrase names multiple machines, annotate each machine mention separately unless the coordination forms one fixed machine type name.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate people, organizations, places, artworks, software, databases, or technical functions; the entity type is limited to physical machines or mechanical equipment.
Do not annotate processes or operations performed by a machine, such as "separating and washing process"; only the device itself is a machine.
Do not annotate infrastructure or network objects such as "road network" or "roads" unless they are explicitly described as machines or equipment.
Generic manufacturing context alone is not enough: annotate "machines" in "machines on a manufacturing line," but not the manufacturing line itself.

A tool, apparatus, or equipment item should be annotated only if it functions as a machine/device, as shown by examples like "sieves"; abstract system components or computational methods are not machine entities.

marker.definition: A marker is a named substance, biological factor, structural component, or reference element that functions as an indicator, signal, landmark, or pointer within an experimental, biological, mechanical, or conceptual system.
include_rules: Annotate names of chemicals, drugs, or compounds when the context explicitly uses them as indicators or assay markers, e.g. "tolbutamide" in "using tolbutamide as a marker".
Annotate named biological molecules, genes, proteins, or signaling factors when they function as reference markers or biologically salient labeled factors, e.g. "Sonic hedgehog".
Annotate component names used as reference points or pointer-like elements within a described system or analogy, e.g. "head" in a Turing machine comparison.
Annotate both proper names and contextually specific common nouns when they denote a marker entity rather than a generic concept.
Annotate a marker mention whether it appears in scientific, technical, or conceptual contexts, as long as it identifies the indicator/reference entity itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic uses of words that can also mean communication or content, such as "message", when they refer to textual meaning, titles, or discourse rather than an indicator or pointer.
Do not annotate book titles, translations, commentaries, religious texts, or other document names even if they contain words like "message" or other potentially marker-like terms.
Do not annotate software/build/configuration identifiers, environment variables, image names, stage labels, commands, paths, or code tokens.
Do not annotate broad experimental descriptions, actions, or outcomes when only a specific indicator entity inside them could qualify as a marker.
Do not annotate generic body-part or component nouns unless the context uses them as a named reference element or pointer within a system, as in the Turing machine example.
Do not annotate surrounding role phrases such as "as a marker", because the entity type is the marker item itself, not the metalinguistic description of its role.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the marker itself, without surrounding explanatory words such as "as a marker", "using", "results in", or "the ... of".
Include all tokens that are part of the marker name, including multiword names such as "Sonic hedgehog".
For common-noun markers used as named components or pointers in a specific system or analogy, annotate only the noun naming the marker, e.g. "head" in "the head of a Turing machine".
Do not include determiners, prepositions, punctuation, or appositive description unless they are part of the established marker name.
If a marker mention appears inside a larger noun phrase, select only the substring denoting the marker, not the full experimental or descriptive phrase.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a marker with a disease, defect, process, or outcome: in "posterior skeletal defects", the defects are consequences, while "Sonic hedgehog" is the marker-like entity to annotate.
Do not confuse a marker with a general message, theme, translation, or textual content; abstract discourse items such as "message" in book-related text are not markers.
Do not confuse a marker with software variables, image names, Docker stages, file paths, commands, or configuration tokens; technical identifiers such as "image_php" or "app-base" are not markers.

When a substance, named biological factor, or system component is mentioned because it serves as an indicator, signal, landmark, or reference point, annotate it as marker; when it is mentioned only as a generic object with no marker/reference role, do not annotate it.
A marker may be a chemical/drug name, a biological signaling name, or a structural/component label if the context presents it as an indicator, landmark, or reference element.

material.definition: A term that names a physical substance, composition, or material formulation used as a constituent or medium, including technical material names and named material layers/coatings.
include_rules: Annotate named substances, formulations, and composition-based terms used as materials.
Annotate technical material expressions that may include multiple modifiers and punctuation when they function as the material name.
Annotate materials specified by function or phase if the phrase is the recognized name of the material (e.g., 'phase change material').
Annotate named layer/coating material phrases when the phrase identifies the material itself (e.g., 'nitride cap layer').
Annotate specialized model or formulation names for particles or solids when they denote a material class rather than a mere generic object.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary nouns that merely describe an object made of material without naming the material itself.
Do not annotate abstract concepts, performance properties, or manufacturing processes unless they are part of a conventional material name.
Do not annotate broad category words such as 'material' by itself when used only generically and not as a specific named material.
Do not annotate context words like 'layer', 'cap', or 'particles' unless they are part of the established material designation in the span.
Do not annotate full sentences, clauses, or surrounding descriptive text containing a material mention.
boundary_rules: Include the full material name as it appears, including modifiers, compound descriptors, slashes, hyphens, and technical qualifiers that are integral to the material name (e.g., 'Lennard-Jones/spline particles', 'phase change material', 'nitride cap layer').
Do not truncate material names by removing required modifiers such as composition, phase, structure, function, or layer descriptors when they are part of the conventional material term.
Exclude surrounding determiners, quantities, verbs, prepositions, and general phrase context; annotate only the material expression itself.
If a material term is embedded in a longer noun phrase, include only the words that directly name the material, not unrelated application or device context.
When a material name contains punctuation that is part of the term (slashes, hyphens), keep it within the span.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse material with objects, devices, systems, or components that are made from a material unless the material name itself is being named.

Do not annotate generic scientific nouns such as 'particles' when they are not serving as a specific material name; in 'Lennard-Jones/spline particles', the full technical material expression is the target because it names the particle material/model.
Do not annotate process names, properties, or states (e.g., phase-related wording) unless they are part of a named material expression such as 'phase change material'.
Do not annotate structural layer labels, coatings, or caps unless the phrase specifically denotes a material name rather than a physical part in context.
Do not confuse material with organization, person, location, or event names; only annotate terms that denote a substance, composition, or material formulation.

mathematical concept.definition: A mathematical concept is an abstract mathematical or statistical notion, method, relation, operation, or object named in text, including standard techniques and formal terms used in mathematics, statistics, or related quantitative analysis.
include_rules: Annotate names of statistical, mathematical, or quantitative methods when they function as abstract concepts, such as 'statistical analysis', 'Pearson correlation', 'Bayesian methods', and 'Gibbs sampling'.
Annotate formal mathematical objects or terms, such as 'polynomial', when used as named mathematical concepts.
Annotate multiword named concepts even if they are common phrases, when the phrase denotes a specific mathematical or statistical construct.
Annotate concepts from applied statistics or computation when the entity is the method/relationship itself, not the software or code that implements it.
Annotate concept names whether they appear in explanatory prose, parenthetical glosses, or methodological descriptions.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general-purpose words that are not mathematical concepts on their own, such as 'analysis' when it is not specifically statistical or mathematical.
Do not annotate application contexts, experimental procedures, or outcome descriptions that merely mention a mathematical concept.
Do not annotate programming functions, hashing algorithms as software usage, or other implementation details unless the text names the mathematical concept itself as the target.
Do not annotate abbreviations or acronyms when they are only shorthand labels and the full mathematical concept is not present or not the intended span.
Do not annotate non-mathematical entities such as medical conditions, treatments, organizations, locations, or general topics.
Do not annotate examples, datasets, equations, or values unless the abstract concept name itself is explicitly present as text.
boundary_rules: Include the full mathematical term as written, including modifiers that make it a specific concept (e.g., 'statistical analysis', 'Pearson correlation', 'Bayesian methods', 'Gibbs sampling').
If the concept is a noun phrase with an adjective or proper-name modifier, include the modifier when it is part of the concept name.
Do not include surrounding punctuation, parentheses, or introductory wording unless they are part of the term itself.
For multiword concepts, keep the span contiguous and include all words that form the concept.
If a concept appears inside an abbreviation explanation, annotate the full expanded concept only if it is explicitly written as the mathematical term.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate ordinary scientific or technical procedures unless they are mathematical/statistical concepts in themselves (e.g., 'analysis' alone is too generic, but 'statistical analysis' is a mathematical concept).
Do not annotate software, programming constructs, file-handling actions, or implementation details even if they use mathematical functions or names (e.g., 'sha1()', 'md5()', 'store image').
Do not annotate domain topics, diseases, treatments, or other non-mathematical subjects that may appear in the same text.
Do not annotate abbreviations by themselves unless they clearly name a mathematical concept in context; prefer the full concept when present.
Distinguish from generic process phrases: annotate the named mathematical/statistical method or relation, not the surrounding verb phrase or application context.
Distinguish from pure numbers, measurements, or data values: only annotate abstract mathematical concepts, methods, relations, or objects.

measure.definition: A measure is a named metric, index, rating, amount, score, variable, or standardized quantitative/qualitative assessment used to evaluate, compare, or describe a phenomenon, state, or outcome.
include_rules: Annotate named variables or metrics used to quantify or characterize a phenomenon, including abstract measures such as "roaming entropy".

Annotate formal or semi-formal assessment names containing measurement nouns such as "index", "rating", "amount", "score", "level", or similar when the full phrase denotes a specific measure, e.g., "appropriateness index", "median rating", "amount of disagreement".
Annotate standard abbreviated economic, clinical, behavioral, or research measures when the abbreviation itself names the metric, e.g., "GDP".
Annotate both standalone measure names and measure phrases embedded in larger clauses, as long as the phrase itself denotes the variable being measured or analyzed.
Annotate a phrase as measure when it is the object of verbs such as assess, rate, quantify, correlate, compare, or develop and the phrase names the metric rather than the underlying entity.
Include text-specific constructed measures if they function as explicit variables in the study or statement, even if they are not globally standardized.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic words for measurement without a specific named metric, such as "measures", "levels", "results", "analysis", or "activity" when used descriptively rather than as a measure name.
Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, biological processes, treatments, genes, proteins, pathways, or cell markers unless the phrase explicitly denotes a measure rather than the underlying entity.
Do not annotate numeric values, percentages, units, or change expressions by themselves, such as "0.4 per cent growth", unless the entity is the named measure independent of its value.
Do not annotate broad clinical or experimental observations like "periodontal status", "cartilage nodules", or "chondrogenesis" when they are outcomes or states rather than explicit metrics.

Do not annotate assay target names or substances in constructions like "hemoglobin A(1c) (HbA(1c)) measures" when the phrase refers generally to measurement of that analyte rather than a named measure concept.
Do not annotate statistical procedures or outputs such as "logistic regression", "matched-pair analysis", or "P = 0.007" as measures.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase naming the measure itself, including head nouns such as "index", "rating", "amount", "entropy", or standard abbreviated measure names such as "GDP".
Include modifiers that are part of the conventional or text-specific measure name, e.g., "appropriateness index", "median rating", "amount of disagreement", "roaming entropy".
For acronyms or abbreviations that themselves denote a measure, annotate only the acronym/abbreviation span, e.g., "GDP".

Do not include surrounding verbs, determiners, comparison language, or values unless they are part of the measure name itself; for example, in "0.4 per cent growth", annotate neither the numeric value nor the larger phrase as a measure unless the target is the named metric alone.
Do not extend the span to attached explanatory context such as "measures", "levels of", "history of", or clause material outside the measure name.
confusable_type_rules: Measure refers to a quantified, rated, indexed, scored, counted, or otherwise defined variable/metric used to assess or describe something; annotate the metric name, not the phenomenon being studied.
Do not annotate diseases, conditions, traits, or outcomes as measure merely because they are assessed by a measure; for example, "diabetes" or "periodontal disease" are not measures.

Do not annotate genes, proteins, pathways, cell types, chemicals, or biomarkers unless the text refers to the measurement construct rather than the biological entity itself; for example, "BMP-2" and "ERK" are biological entities, not measures.

Do not annotate raw laboratory values or assay readouts when presented only as measured substance names plus generic wording such as "HbA(1c) measures"; the example indicates this usage is not treated as a measure mention.

Do not confuse a measure with a statistical result or significance statement; p-values, regression analyses, and comparative statements are not measure entities unless a named metric itself is mentioned.
A process or property can be a measure only when the text uses it as a defined metric or variable name, as in "roaming entropy"; otherwise general processes or phenomena are not annotated.

measurement.definition: A word or phrase that denotes a measurable property, dimension, parameter, or variable of an entity, process, or system, including technical measurement names and their standard abbreviations.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that explicitly name a measurable property or parameter, such as pressure, mass-related measures, width, dimension, duration, or similar technical variables.
Annotate standard abbreviations or symbols when they are used as the name of a measurement variable, such as "Ti".
Annotate specialized compound measurement phrases when the modifiers are necessary to identify the property, e.g., "mass per electron transferred" and "lateral spacer width dimensions".
Annotate measurement terms whether they refer to physical, spatial, temporal, mechanical, or other scientific/technical properties, as long as the phrase denotes what is being measured.
Annotate the property name even when it appears without an explicit numeric value or unit.
Annotate the measurement term embedded in descriptive scientific text if it functions as the variable of observation or control.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate source code identifiers, class names, package names, method names, or programming-language tokens, even if they contain words like "version" or "algorithm".
Do not annotate person names, organizations, places, events, or general prose with no measurement semantics.
Do not annotate generic nouns that are not used as measurable properties in context.
Do not annotate values, counts, percentages, dates, durations, or units alone; these express measured amounts, not the measurement type.
Do not annotate instrument names, sensors, materials, or components unless the text names the property being measured rather than the object itself.
Do not annotate broad scientific processes or states when the measurable attribute is only implicit.
Do not annotate isolated modifiers such as "larger", "low", or "good" without the accompanying measurement noun.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal contiguous span that names the measured variable or dimensional property itself.

Include core modifiers that specify the kind of measurement when they are part of the measurement name, e.g., "inspiratory pressure", "mass per electron transferred", "lateral spacer width dimensions".
Include abbreviated symbols when they denote a measurement variable, e.g., "Ti".

Exclude surrounding determiners, adjectives of degree, and discourse words not part of the measurement name, e.g., exclude "a brief" from "a brief inspiratory pressure plateau (P)" and keep only "pressure" if the broader phrase describes an event rather than the measured property.
Do not extend the span to include values, units, formulas, parenthetical explanations, or surrounding process descriptions unless they are part of the established measurement term itself.

When both a general head noun and a more specific full measurement phrase appear, prefer the full specific phrase if it forms a coherent property name; otherwise annotate only the measurement word actually used as the variable mention.
confusable_type_rules: Do not label physical objects, substances, devices, or structures as measurement merely because they have measurable properties; annotate only the property name, not the thing being measured.

Do not label events, procedures, or states of a system as measurement. For example, a "pressure plateau" is an observed phenomenon; annotate "pressure" as the measured variable, not the full event phrase unless the whole phrase is clearly used as the measurement name.

Distinguish measurement from numeric quantities: numbers, ranges, units, and value expressions are not measurement by themselves. The entity type targets what is being measured, not the amount measured.
Distinguish measurement from scientific methods or algorithms. Terms like "compressionAlgorithm" in code are technical identifiers, not measurement variables.
Distinguish measurement from generic descriptive language. Words such as "larger" or "good" are not measurements unless attached to an explicit measurable property name.

Variable names and abbreviations count as measurement only when they denote a measurable parameter or dimension in context, not when they are arbitrary code variables, class fields, or non-scientific abbreviations.

mechanism.definition: A named mechanism is a specific process, operative component, technical scheme, or functional means by which an effect is produced, a device operates, or information is encoded or handled.
include_rules: Annotate expressions that name a specific causal or operative process responsible for an observed effect or behavior, such as "Surface fouling".
Annotate expressions that name a functional component or operative assembly of a device, such as "keyboard mechanism".
Annotate abbreviated technical schemes or methods when they are used as the mechanism itself in context, such as "SR" in an encoding context.
Annotate mechanism mentions in scientific, engineering, or technical contexts where the span denotes how something works, functions, or is implemented.
Annotate both full-form and abbreviated mechanism names when they refer to a concrete process, component, protocol-like scheme, or encoding method rather than a general topic.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text that contains no technical, functional, causal, or operative mechanism.
Do not annotate broad phenomena, goals, or outcomes when the mechanism causing them is named separately; for example, exclude the effect term if only the causal means is the mechanism.
Do not annotate whole products, toolkits, services, or systems unless the span itself specifically denotes their operative mechanism or component.
Do not annotate generic nouns like "mechanism" alone when they are not part of a specific named expression.
Do not annotate administrative, legal, copyright, blog, family, or other non-technical text such as licenses, notices, or personal posts.
Do not annotate numeric identifiers, figure references, or formatting artifacts attached to a mechanism mention, such as "41" or "FIG.".
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the mechanism, process, device component, or technical scheme itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the established mechanism name, such as material, functional, or domain-specific descriptors: "Surface fouling", "keyboard mechanism".
Include abbreviated names when the abbreviation itself denotes the mechanism or technical method, as in "SR" when it refers to a specific encoding-related mechanism or scheme.

Exclude surrounding determiners, prepositions, conjunctions, figure numbers, and other context words not part of the mechanism name, e.g. annotate "keyboard mechanism" not "the keyboard mechanism 41".
Exclude trailing explanatory context about use or implementation unless it is part of the name, e.g. annotate "SR" not "SR encoding" unless the full phrase is the mechanism name used in context.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a mechanism with a general activity or result; annotate the specific causal, operational, or functional means by which something works or is achieved.

Do not confuse a mechanism with a full system, toolkit, product, or service. In "The DICOM toolkit is reused for SR encoding and DICOM services", annotate "SR" as the specific scheme/mechanism, not "DICOM toolkit" or "DICOM services".
Do not confuse a mechanism with a broad scientific topic or field. "chemotaxis" is the phenomenon; "Surface fouling" is the mechanism for it and is the annotated span.

Do not confuse a mechanism with a generic object unless the text explicitly presents it as a functional mechanism or component, e.g. "keyboard mechanism" qualifies because it names the operative component.
Do not annotate legal, social, narrative, or website text that lacks a technical or functional mechanism, even if it contains nouns describing actions or policies.

media.definition: Content items or content units in audio, visual, or audiovisual form, including named or generic media objects and their directly referenced component units.
include_rules: Annotate generic or specific references to audiovisual or audio content items, such as "movie", "videos", and "music".
Annotate direct subunits of media content when they are treated as selectable or addressable content units, such as "frames".
Annotate named or labeled takes, tracks, or media assets when the label functions as the item name, such as "MUSIC1" and "MUSIC2".
Annotate media mentions whether singular or plural.
Annotate descriptive multiword mentions that identify a particular media subset or installment, such as "day 3 videos".
Annotate media mentions in creation, playback, editing, loading, or labeling contexts as long as the span denotes the media object itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate source code, programming identifiers, namespaces, class names, package names, comments, or configuration terms.
Do not annotate ordinary files, licenses, documentation, or generic technical artifacts unless they denote actual audio/visual media content.
Do not annotate non-media subject matter such as medical conditions, populations, procedures, outcomes, or other domain concepts.
Do not annotate generic temporal expressions, numbers, or ordinals by themselves; annotate them only when they are part of a media mention, as in "day 3 videos".
Do not annotate actions on media, such as "load", "select", or "labelled", unless the word itself is the media entity.
Do not annotate abstract events or sessions unless the text explicitly refers to the recording/content item itself.
Do not annotate standalone mention of a system label unless it names a media item or take in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the media item or media unit itself, such as "movie", "music", "frames", or "day 3 videos".
Include descriptive words that are part of the media mention when they specify the item, collection, or installment, as in "day 3 videos".
Include alphanumeric labels when they directly name a media take or media asset, such as "MUSIC1" and "MUSIC2".
Do not include surrounding verbs, determiners, or task language unless they are part of the media name; for example, annotate "movie" in "load in a movie" but not "load in a movie".
For plural mentions, include the plural noun when it refers to multiple media items or units, such as "videos" or "frames".
When a sentence contains multiple separate media mentions, annotate each mention separately, e.g. "movie" and "frames".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate media content or media units, not software, source code, packages, classes, configuration options, or file-format-related technical text.

Do not confuse media with general documents or legal/code references such as "LICENSE file" or "source code file" in technical boilerplate; those are files or documentation, not media content in this sense.
Annotate content-bearing audiovisual or audio items and their meaningful subunits, such as movies, videos, music, takes, and frames.
Do not annotate scientific, medical, or other topical prose unless it explicitly refers to media content; domain text about pregnancy, thrombophilia, or trials is not media.
If a term refers to a label assigned to a recorded take or track, treat it as media when it functions as the name of that media item, as with "MUSIC1".
If a term refers only to a process, action, or metadata operation involving media, annotate only the media object itself, not the action or surrounding procedure.

medical.definition: A medical entity is a span that denotes a disease or disorder, a therapeutic or treatment-related substance, a clinical or healthcare process, or a medically defined study/procedure used in patient care, treatment, or clinical research.
include_rules: Annotate names of diseases, symptoms, disorders, and other health conditions, including specific conditions like "migraine" and broader condition categories like "disorders" when they clearly refer to medical conditions.
Annotate therapeutic or treatment-related substances when they are presented as medically useful agents, such as "active compounds" in a treatment context.
Annotate clinical or healthcare procedures, care processes, and medication-management activities, such as "medication reconciliation".
Annotate medically scoped studies or research activities when the phrase itself denotes a clinical/medical study type, such as "clinical pharmacokinetic studies".
Annotate medical concepts even when they are somewhat generic, if the sentence places them in a healthcare or treatment context, e.g., a disorder being treated or a clinical process being reviewed.
Annotate noun phrases that directly name the medical concept, rather than requiring a formal ontology-style term.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate purely molecular, biochemical, or laboratory entities that are not presented as medical conditions, interventions, or clinical processes, such as proteins, protein complexes, amino acids, residues, peptides, reagents, or databases.

Do not annotate general scientific or technical methods used for basic research when they lack explicit medical or clinical meaning, e.g., crosslinking, mass spectrometry, transcription/translation systems.
Do not annotate anatomical structures, tissues, or organism-source terms by themselves, such as "human brain", unless they are part of a larger medical condition name.
Do not annotate generic research objects or materials such as cDNA libraries, clones, transcripts, insert sizes, probes, or sequence tags.

Do not annotate highly generic nouns like "information technology" unless the full phrase denotes a medical process or intervention; in "information technology in medication reconciliation", annotate only "medication reconciliation".
Do not annotate surrounding action words or context phrases such as "treating", "use of", "developed for being used in", or "present invention".
Do not annotate broad scientific function statements or organismal importance phrases unless they directly name a medical concept.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that expresses the medical concept, condition, intervention, or medically defined study/process.
Include core modifiers that are part of the established medical expression, such as disease descriptors or study-type descriptors: "clinical pharmacokinetic studies", "medication reconciliation".
For disease mentions, include the full disease/disorder term but not surrounding verbs, prepositions, or purpose clauses: annotate "migraine" in "treating migraine".
For broad medical condition nouns used as targets of treatment or discussion, annotate the noun itself when it denotes a health condition category, e.g., "disorders".

For therapeutic or biomedical substances, include the noun phrase naming the substance itself, e.g., "active compounds", but exclude external framing such as "The", "of the present invention", or verbal context.

Do not extend the span to include non-medical context such as document section headings, methodological descriptions unrelated to medicine, or general scientific qualifiers unless they are part of the medical term itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate as medical when the span denotes a disease, disorder, treatment-related substance, clinical/medical process, or medically scoped study; do not annotate purely chemical or biochemical reagents when they are discussed only as laboratory materials.

Distinguish medical from general biology/protein terminology: proteins, amino acids, residues, cells, cDNA libraries, transcripts, and similar molecular biology entities are not medical unless they are explicitly named as a disease, therapy, clinical procedure, or medical study target.

Distinguish medical from generic scientific methods: methods such as mass spectrometry or crosslinking are excluded unless the phrase specifically denotes a clinical or medical study/process, as in "clinical pharmacokinetic studies".

Distinguish medical from anatomical or organism references: terms like "human brain" refer to anatomy/specimen source rather than a medical condition or intervention and should not be annotated as medical.

Distinguish medical from invention/legal or discourse context: in phrases like "active compounds of the present invention", annotate only the medically relevant substance term, not the patent-style framing.

When a term could be both general and medical, use context: annotate broad terms like "disorders" only when they clearly denote health conditions, such as being treated, diagnosed, prevented, or discussed in clinical context.

medical concept.definition: A medical concept is an abstract biomedical or clinical notion, property, mechanism, outcome dimension, or named measurement concept used to describe, explain, or evaluate medical phenomena, rather than a concrete disease, treatment, person, organization, or general non-medical topic.
