include_rules: Annotate abstract biomedical or clinical notions that describe mechanisms, properties, or evaluative dimensions, such as "pathophysiology", "efficacy", and "safety".
Annotate named biomedical measurement or index concepts, such as "washout index", when the phrase denotes a recognized medical/biological metric or analytical concept.
Annotate concept mentions that function as outcomes, endpoints, assessment dimensions, or scientific properties in medical discussion.
Annotate single-word or multi-word noun phrases that refer to medical reasoning constructs rather than concrete entities.
Include concepts used to explain disease mechanisms, treatment effects, clinical evaluation, or biomedical interpretation, provided the mention itself is the abstract concept.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate concrete disorders, infections, symptoms, pathogens, or other medical conditions when they are mentioned as the subject matter rather than as abstract conceptual categories.
Do not annotate names of drugs, biologics, devices, procedures, surgeries, or treatment regimens.
Do not annotate institutions, agencies, professions, patients, providers, or other people/organization mentions.
Do not annotate purely mathematical expressions, formula parts, ratios, variables, units, or calculation steps unless the full phrase names a biomedical measurement concept.
Do not annotate broad generic words that are not specifically used as biomedical concepts in context, especially in non-medical documents.
Do not annotate financial, policy, engineering, energy, or other non-medical concepts even if they are abstract nouns similar in form to medical concepts.
Do not annotate modifiers or contextual phrases around the concept, such as duration, frequency, certainty, or evaluative framing, unless they are part of the fixed concept name.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that expresses the concept itself, excluding surrounding function words and syntax, e.g. annotate "washout index" without preceding articles or following explanatory phrases.
Include core modifiers only when they are part of the established concept name, such as the head noun plus its concept-defining modifier in "washout index".
For abstract concept nouns, annotate just the noun or noun phrase naming the concept, e.g. "pathophysiology", "efficacy", "safety".
Do not extend the span to include associated diseases, interventions, formulas, time periods, populations, or qualifiers unless they are part of a single fixed concept mention.
Do not include abbreviations, symbols, or variable names that merely appear near the concept unless they are part of the concept string itself.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate diseases, infections, syndromes, or clinical conditions as medical concept merely because they are medical in topic; the negative example shows that mentions like healthcare acquired infections are not this type.
Do not annotate treatments, drugs, procedures, devices, or interventions; annotate only the abstract medical notion or measurement concept if explicitly named.
Do not annotate organizations, agencies, or people in medical text; entities such as CDC or health care workers are not medical concept.

Do not annotate general non-medical abstract goals or system properties such as emissions, reliability, or cost in non-medical domains; medical concept is restricted to concepts used in biomedical or clinical reasoning.

When a phrase combines a medical concept with another entity, annotate only the conceptual component if it is separately expressed; for example, in discussion of treatment outcomes, "efficacy" and "safety" are concepts, while the treatment name is not.

Measurement-related concept names such as "washout index" are included when they denote a biomedical metric or analytical concept, but raw formula components or arithmetic expressions used to calculate them are not.

medical condition.definition: A medical condition is a mention of a disease, disorder, lesion, abnormal clinical finding, symptom-like physiologic state, medically relevant specimen/finding expression, or explicitly medical screening/treatment term referring to a health-related condition or clinical concept.
include_rules: Annotate named diseases, cancers, disorders, lesions, and other abnormal pathologic findings, such as "Mucinous epithelial lesions".
Annotate clinically relevant physiologic abnormalities or health-state terms, including monitored vital-state expressions used medically, such as "blood pressure".
Annotate medication or treatment terms when they are presented as standalone clinical entities in the text and are treated as positive examples in this schema, such as "steroids".
Annotate medical screening or procedure expressions when they form a specific health-related concept, such as "breast cancer screening".
Annotate medically meaningful specimen/material expressions when the phrase itself denotes a clinical/pathology concept, such as "endometrial curettage material".
Annotate multiword medical expressions including their necessary descriptive modifiers when those modifiers specify the condition or finding.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate nonmedical uses of words that can also appear in medicine, such as geological, chemical, acoustic, or engineering "reaction" in nonclinical text.
Do not annotate programming code, file headers, APIs, function names, compiler directives, or other software artifacts.
Do not annotate generic actions, events, or verbs surrounding a condition mention, such as "controlled," "put on," "recorded," or "determined."
Do not annotate broad contextual phrases that merely describe a study, article, or hospital setting unless they are part of the medical term itself.
Do not annotate body parts alone unless the full span denotes a clinically meaningful condition, specimen, lesion, or medical procedure term.
Do not annotate ordinary physical objects, devices, measurements, or signals unless they directly name a medical concept in context.
Do not extend the span to include surrounding punctuation, section headings, or bracketed publication framing unless those tokens are part of the medical name.
boundary_rules: Select the shortest span that fully names the condition, lesion, abnormal finding, symptom, physiologic abnormality, or medical procedure/treatment mention when such items are treated as the target entity in this schema.
Include core modifiers that are part of the medical expression, such as severity, histologic, anatomic, or descriptive adjectives: "Mucinous epithelial lesions".
Include the full multiword expression for named screening, disease, or procedure terms when the words function together as one medical concept: "breast cancer screening".

For body-measure or physiologic-state mentions, annotate only the medical expression itself, not surrounding verbs or management language: in "his blood pressure controlled," annotate "blood pressure" only.
Do not include determiners, possessives, prepositions, or trailing action words unless they are inside the conventional medical term.
When an anatomic/material phrase is used as the full name of a clinically relevant specimen or finding mention, keep the full phrase together: "endometrial curettage material".
If a broader noun phrase contains both a medical term and nonessential context, annotate only the medical term, not the extra context.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish medical conditions from medications or therapeutic substances: annotate disease/finding/state mentions, not the drug name; however, if the schema examples treat a therapeutic item as in-scope, capture only the medication term itself and not administration context, as in "steroids".

Distinguish medical conditions from anatomy-only mentions: annotate an anatomic phrase only when it is part of a clinically meaningful lesion, specimen, or procedure expression, not when it merely names a body part.

Distinguish medical conditions from procedures in general: include procedure or screening names only when they are explicitly medical and presented as the target entity type in this schema, such as "breast cancer screening"; do not annotate unrelated technical or nonmedical processes like geological "reaction".
Distinguish medical conditions from general scientific or physical phenomena: words like "reaction," "signal," or "boundary region" in nonmedical contexts are not medical entities.
Distinguish medical conditions from software/code tokens, headers, function names, and file content; programming text is never annotated.

Distinguish medical conditions from generic clinical actions or management language: annotate the condition term "blood pressure," not contextual verbs like "controlled" or treatment framing like "put on".

medical device.definition: A physical medical instrument, apparatus, implant, shunt, or branded device used to diagnose, monitor, treat, support, or replace a bodily function or condition.
include_rules: Annotate names of physical devices used in medical care, diagnosis, monitoring, treatment, or bodily support.
Annotate implanted devices and prosthetic-like objects, including generic mentions when they clearly denote a medical device, e.g., "implant".
Annotate device types named by technical noun phrases, e.g., "ventriculoperitoneal shunts".
Annotate branded or proprietary device names when they refer to the medical device, e.g., "HemoCue".
Annotate singular or plural mentions of devices when the phrase denotes the device class or specific device used on patients.
Annotate device mentions whether they appear in treatment context, performance evaluation context, or patient history, as long as the referent is the physical medical device.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, anatomical sites, or clinical conditions that the device is used for or associated with.
Do not annotate procedures, therapies, or actions involving the device, such as implantation or treatment, unless the device object itself is explicitly named.

Do not annotate generic hospital equipment references when they are too vague to denote a device mention in context, such as broad mentions like "equipment" or "tool" without clear medical-device reference.
Do not annotate durations, measurements, patient counts, study outcomes, or performance descriptions surrounding the device mention.
Do not annotate legal, geographic, historical, or other non-medical uses of words that could resemble device terminology; unrelated text contains no medical device entities.
Do not annotate manufacturer/company mentions unless the text uses the name to refer to the device itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the shortest span that uniquely identifies the device mention in context.
Include the core noun naming the device, even when generic, if it clearly refers to a medical device in the sentence, e.g., "implant".
Include modifiers that are part of the established device name or device type, e.g., "ventriculoperitoneal shunts".

For branded or proprietary device mentions, annotate the brand/name itself when that is the device reference, e.g., "HemoCue"; do not force inclusion of a trailing generic word like "device" unless it is part of the name being used as the device mention.
Exclude surrounding verbs, procedures, durations, counts, and patient descriptors, e.g., in "implanted for at least 2 years," annotate only "implant".
Exclude disease, body-site, and treatment-purpose phrases that are not part of the device name, e.g., exclude "for hydrocephalus" from "ventriculoperitoneal shunts for hydrocephalus".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a medical device with a medical procedure or treatment action: annotate the physical object used or implanted, not the act of implantation, treatment, evaluation, or monitoring.
Do not confuse a medical device with a medication or chemical substance; only physical instruments, implants, shunts, meters, or similar apparatus qualify.

Do not confuse a medical device with an anatomical structure or pathology. If a phrase names a body part or disease indication rather than the object itself, do not annotate it unless it is part of the device name.

Do not confuse a medical device with an organization, manufacturer, or study term. A name like "HemoCue" is annotated only when it refers to the device/apparatus, not to a company or unrelated proper noun.
Generic technical words are annotated only when the context makes them a device mention, as with "implant"; generic non-device nouns in unrelated text are not annotated.

medical equipment.definition: Physical medical tools, devices, instruments, implants, prosthetic items, or apparatus used for diagnosis, treatment, surgery, rehabilitation, or other clinical care.
include_rules: Annotate specific names of physical medical devices or instruments used in clinical or biomedical settings.
Annotate implants, prosthetic devices, or restorative items placed in or on the body, such as "ceramic crowns".
Annotate tubes, cannulas, needles, and similar procedural instruments, such as "gastrointestinal cannulas" and "needle".
Annotate both singular and plural mentions when they refer to concrete equipment items.
Annotate equipment mentions whether they appear in treatment, surgery, rehabilitation, or experimental/technical medical contexts, as long as the mention denotes a physical device.
Include modifiers that are necessary to identify the specific equipment type, such as material, function, or target body system.
exclude_rules: Exclude broad or non-specific category labels that do not name a particular device, such as "technical aids" when used as a general class.
Exclude procedures, methods, tests, and algorithms, even if they are used with equipment.
Exclude consumable biological or chemical substances, including antibodies, viruses, proteins, and similar laboratory or biomedical materials.
Exclude clinical outcome measures, scores, costs, lengths of stay, and other abstract study variables.
Exclude hospitals, clinics, study phases, and organizational units.
Exclude anatomical locations, patient groups, and disease names when they are not part of a device name.
Exclude purely descriptive words that do not themselves denote equipment.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase naming the equipment, including descriptive material or body-site terms that are part of the device name, e.g. "gastrointestinal cannulas" and "ceramic crowns".
Include the head noun that identifies the physical device or implant, such as "needle", "cannulas", or "crowns".

Include modifiers that specify the equipment's material, design, or intended anatomical use when they directly modify the device name, e.g. "ceramic" in "ceramic crowns" and "gastrointestinal" in "gastrointestinal cannulas".
Exclude surrounding prepositions, determiners, quantities, and measurement expressions unless they are part of the canonical equipment name.
Exclude verbs, procedures, and event language adjacent to the device mention; annotate only the equipment term itself.
When a generic plural or singular noun directly refers to concrete equipment in context, annotate only that noun phrase, e.g. "needle".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate diseases, injuries, or clinical conditions as medical equipment; only physical tools, implants, or apparatus qualify.
Do not annotate procedures, tests, algorithms, rehabilitation activities, or treatment processes even when they involve equipment.
Do not annotate biological, chemical, or immunological reagents such as antibodies; these are substances, not equipment.
Do not annotate anatomical structures or body parts unless they are part of the equipment name, as in "gastrointestinal cannulas" where "gastrointestinal" modifies the device.
Do not annotate generic administrative or umbrella terms for equipment provision, such as "technical aids", when they refer to broad categories rather than specific devices.

If a term denotes a manufactured object used in medical care or research on a patient or specimen, annotate it as medical equipment; if it denotes a molecule, pathogen, score, or clinical parameter, do not.

medical facility.definition: A medical facility is a place, unit, or care site where medical treatment, emergency care, clinical services, or medical research involving patient care is provided.
include_rules: Annotate names and descriptive mentions of places where medical care is delivered, such as clinics, emergency rooms, hospitals, centers, and similar care sites.
Annotate abbreviated or shorthand facility mentions when they clearly denote a medical care location, such as "ER".
Annotate generic facility mentions when they refer to a specific real site in context, even if unnamed, as in "a local clinic".

Annotate research or trial site expressions when they denote medical centers or care sites participating in a study, including compact forms like "multicenter" when used to indicate multiple medical centers/sites.
Annotate both full proper names and common-noun facility descriptions if the phrase denotes a medical facility.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate medical procedures, treatments, injections, surgeries, medications, or tests, even if they occur at a facility.
Do not annotate general medical concepts, diagnoses, symptoms, anatomy, or devices.
Do not annotate non-medical buildings, ordinary locations, or organizations that are not functioning as healthcare sites in the mention.
Do not annotate adjectives or context words that merely describe healthcare activity unless they are part of the facility mention itself.
Do not annotate incidental words that resemble facility language but are used in non-medical, religious, metaphorical, or unrelated contexts.
Do not annotate references to patient care events without an explicit facility/site mention.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names or denotes the facility, unit, or site of care: e.g., "local clinic", "ER".
Include generic facility nouns when they clearly refer to an actual place of medical care in context, such as "clinic" or "ER".
Include modifiers that are part of the facility mention, such as locality or descriptive adjectives that help identify the site: "local clinic".
For study-site language, annotate the site-denoting term itself when it is used to indicate treatment or research centers, as in "multicenter".
Do not extend the span to surrounding prepositions, articles, times, or events: in "in the ER at 11:00 PM", annotate only "ER".
Do not include procedures or reasons for being at the facility; annotate only the facility phrase.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a medical facility with a medical procedure or treatment. Terms like "epidural", "liposuction", "sedation", or "surgery" are interventions, not facilities.
Do not confuse a medical facility with a disease, symptom, injury, or body condition; those describe medical states, not places of care.
Do not confuse a medical facility with organizations or sports teams unrelated to healthcare, even if mentioned in medical news context.
Do not confuse a medical facility with religious, legal, or abstract uses of similar words; only physical or institutional sites of medical care qualify.
When a term can refer either to a facility or to a non-facility concept, annotate it only if the sentence uses it as the place/site where care is delivered or a study is conducted.

medical measurement.definition: A medical measurement is a named clinical, physiological, biochemical, or outcome parameter that can be assessed, monitored, or quantified in a medical context, independent of its specific numeric value.
include_rules: Annotate terms that name a measurable medical or biological parameter, outcome, or index in a clinical/research context.
Annotate outcome measures used to assess prognosis or treatment effect, such as "actuarial survival" and "event free survival".
Annotate physiological or bodily output measures, such as "urine output" and "basal acid output".
Annotate laboratory or chemical measurement parameters expressed as standard measurement names or symbols, such as "pH".
Include modifiers that are necessary to identify the exact measurement being referenced, including descriptive qualifiers like "basal", "actuarial", or "event free".
Annotate the parameter name even when the sentence also contains its value, unit, or time frame; only the measurement term itself is the entity.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate numeric results, percentages, scores, units, or value expressions alone, such as "74%" or "0.9 ml/kg/min".
Do not annotate time points, durations, ages, or frequencies unless they are part of the fixed measurement name, such as "9 years" or "24 h" by themselves.
Do not annotate procedures, treatments, devices, or interventions that may precede or affect a measurement, such as "balloon catheter insertion".
Do not annotate verbs or general assessment language describing that a measurement was taken or changed, such as "evaluated", "increased", or "profile" when used outside the measurement name.
Do not annotate broad medical contexts or document-level phrases that are not themselves measurable parameters.
Do not annotate non-medical technical terms, software strings, paths, UI labels, or unrelated uses of words that resemble measurement terminology, as in the negative examples.
boundary_rules: Annotate the noun phrase naming the measured clinical/biological parameter, ending at the head term of the measurement name: e.g., "urine output", "basal acid output", "actuarial survival", "event free survival".

Include essential modifiers that specify the measurement concept, such as temporal, physiological, or outcome qualifiers when they are part of the standard measurement name: e.g., "basal acid output", "event free survival".

Exclude numeric values, percentages, units, and ranges associated with the measurement: in "0.9 ml/kg/min", annotate only "urine output"; in "89 and 74%", annotate only the measurement names, not the values.
Exclude surrounding verbs and assessment phrases that merely describe evaluation or change, such as "increased", "was evaluated on", or "were".
When a symbolic measurement term appears as the name of the parameter itself, annotate only that term: e.g., "pH" in "24 h pH profile".

If the measurement appears inside a longer study phrase, select only the measurement term, not the broader procedural or report phrase: e.g., annotate "pH", not "24 h pH profile", unless the full phrase itself is clearly the measurement name.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a medical measurement with its recorded value. The measurement is the parameter being assessed ("urine output"); the value is the result ("0.9 ml/kg/min"). Annotate only the parameter name.
Do not confuse a medical measurement with a medical procedure or intervention. Phrases like "balloon catheter insertion" are actions/treatments, whereas "urine output" is the measured outcome.

Do not confuse a medical measurement with a time expression. "At 9 years" and "24 h" provide timing or duration, not the measurement itself, unless they are embedded inside the conventional measurement name.

Do not confuse a medical measurement with a general clinical event or condition. "survival" and "event free survival" are annotated when used as outcome measures, not when referring generically to a patient's state without measurement context.
Do not annotate laboratory devices, software terms, file paths, or technical strings from non-medical contexts; only biomedical parameters or outcome measures qualify.

Do not confuse a medical measurement with an evaluation activity or study endpoint description as a whole. Annotate the specific measurable construct (e.g., "basal acid output"), not the full clause describing its assessment.

medical procedure.definition: A medical procedure is a specific diagnostic, imaging, surgical, therapeutic, or reconstructive intervention performed in medical care, expressed as the name of the procedure or technique.
include_rules: Annotate names of specific medical diagnostic procedures, including imaging studies, such as "Single photon emission computed tomography".
Annotate names of surgical procedures or operations, including technique-based surgery names, such as "arthroscopic knee surgery".
Annotate names of reconstructive or flap procedures when the phrase denotes the intervention itself, such as "free jejunal flap".
Include multiword procedure names that specify modality, technique, or operative method.
Annotate procedure mentions whether they are used as the subject of discussion, as an intervention being evaluated, or as the source/context of a complication.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-medical technical procedures, device-adjustment actions, engineering operations, or measurement setup descriptions.
Do not annotate mathematical expressions, calculations, symbolic operations, or other non-clinical procedural language.
Do not annotate diseases, disorders, injuries, symptoms, findings, or complications unless the span itself names a procedure.
Do not annotate body parts, tissues, or materials when mentioned only as anatomy and not as part of a procedure name.
Do not annotate generic care verbs or vague actions such as "diagnosed", "treated", or "adjusting" unless they are part of a specific established procedure name.
Do not annotate patient groups, clinical indications, or outcome statements surrounding the procedure mention.
boundary_rules: Select the minimal contiguous span that names the procedure itself.
Include modality or technique words that are part of the procedure name, such as imaging or surgical approach terms: "Single photon emission computed tomography", "arthroscopic knee surgery".
Include anatomical or graft/flap terms when they are integral to the named operation or reconstructive procedure, as in "free jejunal flap".
Exclude surrounding prepositions, determiners, and context words such as "benefit of", "in children with", or "has been questioned".
Exclude complications, outcomes, or diagnostic timing terms adjacent to the procedure, such as "Anastomotic leakage from the free jejunal flap, if diagnosed late"; only annotate "free jejunal flap".
If a longer noun phrase contains both the procedure and a separate disease or patient descriptor, annotate only the procedure portion.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a medical procedure with a disease, symptom, or complication; in "Anastomotic leakage from the free jejunal flap", the leakage is not the procedure, while "free jejunal flap" is.
Do not confuse a medical procedure with an anatomical body part alone; annotate anatomy only when it is part of the procedural name, such as "jejunal" in "free jejunal flap".
Do not confuse a medical procedure with a medical device, instrument, or fixture; terms like "measuring instrument", "dial bore gage", "gaging device", and "setting fixture" are not procedures.
Do not confuse a medical procedure with a general scientific, mathematical, or technical operation outside medicine; formulas, factoring steps, and calculations are not medical procedures.
Do not confuse a medical procedure with a broad clinical activity mention unless a specific named test, imaging study, surgery, repair, flap, biopsy, or similar intervention is stated.

medical term.definition: A word or phrase that names a medical concept used in clinical or biomedical context, including diseases or conditions, procedures, examinations, assessments, reports, findings-related concepts, and clinical indications.
include_rules: Annotate terms that denote clinical examinations, diagnostic procedures, or medical report types, such as 'mammography reports'.
Annotate terms for clinical or biomedical assessment methods or scoring concepts, such as 'visual scoring'.
Annotate terms that denote clinical indications, reasons for treatment, or medically relevant decision concepts, such as 'indications'.
Annotate both specific and generic medical nouns when the surrounding context clearly makes them medical.
Annotate medical concepts appearing in research or clinical writing even when they are embedded in longer descriptive sentences.
Annotate multiword noun phrases when the combined phrase expresses one medical concept.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate terms from non-medical domains, even if they are technical or abstract.
Do not annotate general academic or scientific words that lack specific medical meaning in context.
Do not annotate numerical values, coefficients, or statistical measures by themselves.
Do not annotate patient groups, demographic descriptions, or social categories unless the span itself names a medical concept.
Do not annotate full clauses or actions describing what clinicians or researchers do; only the term naming the medical concept is included.
Do not annotate programming, database, mathematical, or data-manipulation terminology.
Do not annotate titles, discourse words, or bibliographic text unless they contain a distinct medical term.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that expresses the medical concept, procedure, assessment, report type, finding-related term, or clinical indication.
Include essential modifiers that are part of the established medical expression, such as modality or descriptive words in 'mammography reports' and 'visual scoring'.
Exclude surrounding function words, determiners, conjunctions, and prepositions unless they are part of the fixed term.
Exclude patient-group or demographic modifiers when they are not part of the medical term itself, e.g. annotate 'indications' but not 'specific to women with ID'.
Do not extend the span to include verbs or larger event descriptions; capture only the noun phrase naming the medical concept.
When a generic noun is used in a clearly clinical sense, annotate only that noun if no more specific medical wording is present, e.g. 'indications'.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate person names, author names, or other human references; the entity must denote a medical concept, not an individual.
Do not annotate general scientific, linguistic, or cognitive concepts with no medical meaning in context, such as 'symbolization', 'language', 'emotion', or 'information processing'.
Do not annotate software, programming, mathematical, or data-analysis terms such as 'index values', 'vector', 'code', 'sort', or 'order', even if they resemble technical terminology.
Distinguish medical terms from document metadata: annotate the medically meaningful report type in 'mammography reports', but not unrelated title text or publication framing.
Distinguish medical terms from population descriptors: 'women with ID' is a patient group mention, while 'indications' is the medical term.
Distinguish medical terms from purely methodological statistics: annotate a clinical assessment term like 'visual scoring', but not agreement measures such as 'kappa = 0.48'.

medical test.definition: A named medical diagnostic, laboratory, imaging, or physiological test/procedure used to detect, measure, or assess a biological or clinical condition.
include_rules: Annotate specific named diagnostic tests, including imaging tests such as CT, MRI, and ultrasound when used as test names.
Annotate specific laboratory assays and experimental/clinical measurement methods such as ELISA and plethysmography when they refer to the test performed.
Annotate abbreviations, acronyms, and full names of tests when they are explicit test mentions.
Annotate compound test names with their modifiers when the modifier is part of the official or descriptive test name (e.g., anatomical site in "abdominal CT scan").
Annotate a test mention whether it appears in a comparative list or in passive constructions describing that it was used or performed.
exclude_rules: Exclude diseases, syndromes, disorders, and conditions even when they are medically relevant or mentioned near testing language.
Exclude test results, findings, interpretations, or measured values; only the test name itself should be annotated.
Exclude generic words for clinical processes or actions (e.g., diagnosis, measurement, evaluation) when they do not name a specific test.
Exclude treatment names, medications, and procedures whose primary purpose is therapy rather than testing.
Exclude anatomical structures and biological markers unless the text names a specific test or assay involving them.
Exclude narrative context, report phrasing, and other words adjacent to a test mention that are not part of the test name.
boundary_rules: Include the full test name as it appears in text, including modifiers that specify the test itself (e.g., anatomical site in "abdominal CT scan").
Include common abbreviations and acronyms when they directly name a test (e.g., "CT", "MRI", "ELISA").
Do not include surrounding verbs, prepositions, articles, or result-reporting words (e.g., exclude "showed", "measured by").
If a test is written as a compound term, include all words that together form the test name.
If a parenthetical abbreviation is the test name, annotate the abbreviation only when it is itself a test mention; do not annotate unrelated explanatory text inside parentheses.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse medical tests with diseases, disorders, syndromes, or pathological conditions; those are not annotated even if they are the reason for testing.

Do not annotate body signs, measurements, or clinical observations unless they are themselves the named test procedure or assay (e.g., "blood pressure" alone is a measurement, but "tail plethysmography" is a test method).
Do not annotate generic medical actions such as diagnosis, evaluation, screening, or examination unless they are expressed as a specific named test.
Do not annotate results, findings, or outputs of a test (e.g., mass lesion, enhancing mass) because the entity is the test used to obtain them.
Prefer this type for named imaging modalities, laboratory assays, and specialized measurement procedures when they are used as the test name.

medical treatment.definition: A medical treatment is a named intervention, procedure, regimen, preventive measure, or therapeutic/clinical training undertaken to prevent, manage, or treat a health-related condition or risk.
include_rules: Annotate named medical or health-related interventions that are administered, undergone, or used to prevent, manage, or treat a condition or risk.
Annotate medical procedures and surgeries, e.g., "liver transplantation".
Annotate preventive or reproductive interventions when they function as health management measures, e.g., "contraception".
Annotate formal therapeutic, clinical, or safety-related training when it is presented as a treatment-like intervention that a person undergoes, e.g., "formal radiation safety training".
Annotate both single-word and multi-word treatment names as long as the phrase directly denotes the intervention itself.
Include treatments even when mentioned in retrospective or descriptive contexts, such as after-effects or patient history, as long as the treatment name is explicitly present.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general health products, supplements, or commercial brand names mentioned as consumer goods or advertisements, e.g., promotional supplement text.
Do not annotate insurance, reimbursement, or policy expressions such as "abortion coverage", "health care plan", or "standard package of benefits".
Do not annotate general healthcare systems, debates, legislation, or institutional actions unless a specific treatment is explicitly named as the intervention span.
Do not annotate diseases, disabilities, symptoms, body parts, or diagnoses by themselves, e.g., "hepatic retinopathy" or "intellectual disability".
Do not annotate outcomes, effects, or clinical states resulting from treatment, such as "recovery" or "regain the energy".
Do not annotate vague references to care or treatment that do not name a specific intervention, procedure, regimen, or preventive practice.
Do not annotate mentions where the term refers only to a social, ethical, or political issue rather than the medical intervention itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the treatment itself.

Include modifiers that are part of the established treatment name or specify the treatment type, such as procedural adjectives or formality descriptors, e.g., "formal radiation safety training" and "liver transplantation".
Include the full treatment phrase when the head noun alone would be underspecified in context, e.g., include "liver" in "liver transplantation".
Do not include surrounding verbs, auxiliaries, or event framing words such as "had undergone", "recovery from", or "coverage of".
Do not include patient groups, conditions, or outcomes attached by prepositions unless they are part of the treatment name itself.
For single-word treatment names, annotate just that word when it directly denotes the intervention, e.g., "contraception".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse medical treatment with diseases, disorders, injuries, or symptoms; annotate the intervention used to prevent, manage, or treat them, not the condition itself.

Do not confuse medical treatment with healthcare policy, insurance, or benefit language; mentions such as "abortion coverage", "health care plan", or "benefits" are not treatments unless the text directly names the intervention itself as the target span.

Do not confuse medical treatment with training, education, or safety programs unless the training itself is presented as a formal medical or health-related intervention to be undergone, as in the example "formal radiation safety training".
Do not confuse medical treatment with products or commercial supplements when they are mentioned as marketed goods, brands, or promotional items rather than as a named therapeutic intervention.
Do not confuse medical treatment with medical procedures' outcomes or episodes; annotate "liver transplantation" but not surrounding result phrases like "Recovery from ... after".
When a term can denote both a procedure and a political or ethical topic, annotate it only when used as the intervention itself, not when discussed solely as part of debate, coverage, or legislation.

medication.definition: A medication is a drug, pharmacologic agent, therapeutic substance, or placebo that is administered, prescribed, studied, or mentioned as a treatment or medical intervention.
include_rules: Annotate specific drug names, e.g., "azathioprine" and "tamoxifen".
Annotate pharmacologic classes or therapeutic substance categories when they are used as administered treatments, e.g., "oral corticosteroid", "intralesional corticosteroid", and "local anesthetic".
Annotate placebo when it is mentioned as a treatment arm, comparator, or administered substance in a medical context.
Annotate medication mentions whether they appear in clinical care, treatment descriptions, or research/trial contexts.
Annotate both single-word and multiword medication mentions, including route/form qualifiers that specify the medication mention.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, body parts, or diagnoses that are targets of treatment rather than the treatment itself.
Do not annotate procedures, administration methods, or devices unless the span itself names the substance; for example, exclude "injections" when only the injected material is the medication.
Do not annotate general treatment words that do not identify a specific substance or drug class, such as "treatment" or "therapy" by themselves.
Do not annotate non-medical products, consumer goods, or technical items such as printers or ink cartridges.
Do not annotate people, characters, organizations, places, or historical events appearing in unrelated text.
Do not annotate chemical or descriptive modifiers alone when they do not by themselves denote a medication mention in context.
boundary_rules: Use the shortest span that fully names the medication mention in context.
Include dosage-form or route modifiers when they are part of the medication phrase, e.g., "oral corticosteroid" and "intralesional corticosteroid".
Include multiword therapeutic class names when they denote the administered substance, e.g., "local anesthetic".
Do not include surrounding treatment verbs or framing nouns such as "treated with", "trial of", "treatment of", or "injections" when the medication itself is a smaller span.
Do not extend the span to include diseases, symptoms, or trial context, e.g., annotate "tamoxifen" in "tamoxifen versus placebo".
When a coordinated list contains multiple medications, annotate each medication mention separately.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the drug or therapeutic substance as medication, not the disease or condition it is used to treat; e.g., "acute herpes zoster" and "pain" are not medication.
Annotate medication names and therapeutic substance classes, not medical procedures or delivery actions; e.g., in "local anesthetic injections", annotate "local anesthetic" but not "injections".
Annotate control substances used as comparators in treatment studies when they function as administered agents, e.g., "placebo".
Do not annotate non-medical product names or commercial goods even if they share lexical patterns with substances, e.g., "ink cartridges" is not medication.

Do not annotate person names, fictional entities, organizations, or other proper nouns appearing in non-medical text; these are not medication even if the surrounding text contains action verbs or product references.

medium.definition: A material, substrate, carrier, or growth/support substance in or on which something is recorded, transported, cultured, contained, or conveyed, including physical sheets and biological or chemical media such as serum.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote a physical or biological material serving as a carrier, substrate, or support.
Annotate recording or storage carriers explicitly named as media, e.g. "recording medium".
Annotate physical transport or handling materials such as "sheet" when they are the object being moved, held, printed on, or used as a substrate.
Annotate biological or chemical growth/support materials such as "serum" and "serum-free medium" when they denote the culture or support medium.
Annotate both natural and manufactured media if the phrase refers to the material itself rather than to a method, device, organism, or location.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate living entities such as humans, animals, microbes, cell lines, lymphocytes, or tumor cells.
Do not annotate body parts, organs, tissues, or specimen types unless they are explicitly referred to as a material medium; e.g. "skin biopsy" is not medium.
Do not annotate diseases, diagnoses, pathological findings, or lesions such as melanoma, gastritis, papules, patches, or metastases.
Do not annotate procedures, routes, or methods such as injections, endoscopy, printing method, or nano-imprinting method unless the phrase specifically names the material medium itself.
Do not annotate generic contextual words that do not denote a material/substrate, such as "growth", "source", "site", or "examination".
Do not annotate analytes, antibodies, or other measured substances merely because they are associated with a medium term; only the medium term itself is eligible when used as the carrier/material.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest contiguous noun phrase that names the material/substrate/medium itself.
Include head nouns such as "medium", "sheet", "serum", and necessary modifiers that specify the medium type, e.g. "recording medium" and "serum-free medium".
Do not include surrounding verbs, determiners, prepositions, or purpose clauses, e.g. annotate "recording medium" not "and recording medium", and "sheet" not "the sheet held by the rollers".

When a modifier describes a property of the medium and is part of the standard expression, include it, e.g. "serum-free medium"; if only a constituent material is mentioned independently, annotate only that material term, e.g. "serum".
Do not extend the span into downstream biological targets, devices, or actions, e.g. annotate "serum" not "serum H. pylori IgG antibodies".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate medium only when the term denotes a carrier, substrate, growth material, or physical material through/onto which something is recorded, transported, or cultivated.
Do not annotate organisms, cells, tissues, or anatomical sites even when they interact with a medium, e.g. "mice", "skin biopsy", "hair follicle", and "cells" are not medium.
Do not annotate diseases, findings, or procedures that may occur in or on a material, e.g. "tumor growth", "injection", and "biopsy" are not medium.

Do not annotate chemical, immunologic, or diagnostic expressions unless they directly denote the medium/material itself; for example, in "serum H. pylori IgG antibodies", the full phrase is a test-related expression rather than a medium mention, while standalone "serum" can be medium.

Do not confuse a communication/storage medium with abstract channels or methods unless a physical recording or carrier material is explicitly named; "recording medium" qualifies because it names the physical carrier.

metabolite.definition: A metabolite is a chemical substance involved in metabolism, including small molecules and biochemical compounds that serve as substrates, intermediates, products, storage molecules, or nutrients in biological systems.
include_rules: Annotate names of chemical substances that participate in metabolism as substrates, intermediates, products, or stored/used nutrients.
Annotate specific biochemical compounds mentioned in metabolic context, such as "acetyl-CoA", "L-alanine", and "L-lactate".
Annotate common metabolite names even when they are broad compound classes if they are referred to as substances in metabolism, such as "lipid" and "glucose".
Annotate metabolite mentions whether they appear in process descriptions, substrate statements, or regulation-of-metabolism contexts, as long as the span itself denotes the substance.
Annotate standard abbreviated metabolite names when they directly denote a metabolite, including forms with capitalization, hyphens, and attached chemical abbreviations.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate biological processes, pathway names, or physiological functions, such as "metabolism" or "gluconeogenic rate".
Do not annotate proteins, genes, enzymes, receptors, or other non-metabolite biomolecules, even if they regulate metabolism, such as "transthyretin".
Do not annotate anatomical locations, tissues, organelles, organisms, or sample sources, such as "rat brain mitochondria", "blood", "hair", or "brain".
Do not annotate non-chemical clinical, genomic, or technical terms, such as "copy number imbalances", "DNA", or disease names.
Do not annotate role words or assay context alone, such as "substrate", unless a metabolite name is explicitly present.
Do not annotate unrelated proper nouns, organizations, sports teams, people, or places, as illustrated by the basketball example.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest contiguous span that names the metabolite itself.
Include chemical prefixes, stereochemical markers, and letter-number modifiers that are part of the metabolite name, such as "L-" in "L-alanine" and "L-lactate".
Include punctuation and symbols internal to the metabolite name when they are part of standard naming, such as the hyphen and capitalization in "acetyl-CoA".
Do not include surrounding context words such as "metabolism", "substrate", "rate", "using", "on", or species/anatomy terms.
For coordinated mentions, annotate each metabolite separately when they are distinct spans, e.g. "lipid" and "glucose" separately in "lipid and glucose metabolism".
Do not extend the span to include broader process phrases like "acetyl-CoA metabolism"; annotate only "acetyl-CoA".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate small-molecule biochemical substances that function as metabolic substrates, intermediates, products, or nutrient compounds; do not annotate metabolic processes or pathways such as "gluconeogenic rate" or "metabolism".
Do not annotate genes, proteins, enzymes, or other macromolecular biomolecules unless the mention is a metabolite; for example, "transthyretin" is not a metabolite.
Do not annotate tissues, body parts, organisms, or cellular components such as "rat brain mitochondria", even when metabolites are mentioned nearby.
Do not annotate experimental materials or generic sample substances unless they denote a metabolite; for example, "blood DNA" and "hair DNA" are not metabolite mentions.

Generic biochemical compound classes can be annotated when used as metabolite substances in context, as with "lipid" and "glucose metabolism"; however, abstract biological categories or non-chemical entities should not be annotated.
In substrate/product context, annotate the chemical entity serving that role, e.g. "L-alanine" in "using L-alanine as substrate", not the role word "substrate" itself.

method.definition: A method is a specific procedural technique, experimental assay, analytical procedure, manufacturing process, or administration approach used to perform, measure, produce, or deliver something.
include_rules: Annotate named or clearly specified procedures used in experiments, analysis, synthesis, fabrication, or treatment delivery.
Annotate laboratory and analytical techniques, e.g. assay or detection methods such as "Western blotting".
Annotate production or growth processes used to create materials or structures, e.g. "self-catalyzed molecular beam epitaxy".
Annotate treatment or delivery approaches when the route or procedure is explicitly named, e.g. "nasal administration".
Include multiword technical method names with their essential modifiers when those modifiers distinguish the specific technique.
Annotate a phrase as method when it answers the question "by what procedure/technique was this done?" rather than naming an object or result.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic actions or everyday verbs that are not names of specific techniques, such as "build", "convert", "test", or "install".
Do not annotate software tools, platforms, version names, command names, plugins, source files, code identifiers, or programming constructs, even when they describe technical workflows.
Do not annotate biological entities, chemicals, materials, devices, or samples that participate in a procedure but are not themselves the procedure.
Do not annotate outcomes, effects, measured properties, or experimental results, such as "tolerance" or "efficient".
Do not annotate broad activity nouns without method-specific qualification, such as isolated "application" or "administration" when no specific route or named procedure is given.
Do not annotate explanatory surrounding text that merely states that a method was used if the method name itself is absent.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full contiguous name of the method, including descriptive modifiers that are part of the established technique name, e.g. "self-catalyzed molecular beam epitaxy".
Include the head noun that signals the procedure or assay, such as "administration", "blotting", or "epitaxy", together with its method-specific modifiers.
For delivery or treatment methods, include route adjectives when they specify the method, e.g. "nasal administration".
Do not include surrounding verbs, auxiliaries, or result phrases, e.g. exclude words like "documented by", "mediated", "grown by", or outcome descriptions around the method.
Do not include objects, reagents, or samples unless they are syntactically part of the conventional method name itself.
When a generic action noun appears with a specific modifier that identifies a technique, annotate the combined phrase rather than the generic noun alone.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate a method when the phrase denotes how something is done; do not annotate entities that denote what is used, what is produced, or what is being studied.

Substances, materials, peptides, proteins, samples, and biological objects mentioned as inputs or targets of a procedure are not method entities; for example, in "P2 peptide mediated tolerance", the peptide is not a method.

General software, project versions, commands, libraries, filenames, code symbols, and configuration terms are not methods in this sense, even if they describe ways to build or run something; examples like "maven 1.x", "pom.xml", or "tag_of" should not be annotated.

A generic process word by itself is not a method unless the context names a specific technique; annotate "Western blotting" but not an isolated generic word like "application" or "build" unless it refers to a recognized procedure.
If a phrase refers to an experimental, analytical, manufacturing, or administration technique, annotate it as method; if it refers to a result, property, tolerance, or efficiency, do not annotate it.

methodology.definition: A named or specifically identifiable method, technique, analytical procedure, framework, or systematic approach used to carry out a task, investigation, measurement, design, teaching practice, or other organized activity.
include_rules: Annotate phrases that name a specific analytical, experimental, computational, instructional, or operational method.
Annotate recognized technique names used to perform analysis or measurement, such as "fuzzy cluster analysis" and "liquid chromatography."
Annotate named frameworks or design approaches used as organized methodologies, such as "universal design for learning."
Include methodology mentions when they appear after cue phrases such as "by," "using," "via," "based on," or "uses," if the following phrase denotes the actual method.
Annotate methodology names whether they are used in scientific, educational, clinical, or technical contexts, as long as the phrase denotes how something is systematically done.
Annotate both full names and established multiword method expressions when the wording identifies a distinct approach rather than a general activity.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate broad domains, specialties, or areas of practice that are not methods, such as "primary health care."
Do not annotate generic mentions of "method," "technique," "analysis," "practice," "provision," or "standards" when no specific named methodology is given.

Do not annotate activities, goals, or problem areas that a method is used for, such as monitoring, teaching, manufacturing, mounting, or care delivery, unless the phrase itself names the actual methodology.
Do not annotate physical devices, equipment, materials, substrates, chips, solder balls, or other tangible objects even if they are involved in a method.
Do not annotate legal/patent boilerplate, section headers, or descriptive narrative about an invention unless a specific methodology term is explicitly named.

Do not annotate overly broad process descriptions like "printed substrate manufacturing" or "flip chip bonding method" when they refer to an application process rather than a distinct named methodology.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full contiguous name of the methodology, including descriptive modifiers that are part of the established method name, e.g., "fuzzy cluster analysis" and "universal design for learning".

When a larger phrase contains both an action word and the named method, select only the methodological term itself, not the surrounding task wording, e.g., in "Therapeutic drug monitoring ... by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry," annotate "liquid chromatography" rather than the monitoring task.
Exclude generic support words before the method unless they are part of the canonical name, such as "principles of" in "principles of universal design for learning."
Do not extend the span to include application context, study objects, goals, devices, or result descriptions attached by prepositions or clauses.

If multiple coordinated method names are present, annotate each methodology term separately when they denote distinct methods; do not force inclusion of punctuation or conjunctions unless they are internal to a single established method name.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate methodology when the phrase denotes a procedure, analytical technique, design framework, or systematic approach used to perform research, measurement, teaching, manufacturing, or analysis.

Do not annotate subject areas, service domains, or fields of practice that describe what kind of care or work is being discussed rather than how it is done; for example, "primary health care" is a care domain, not a methodology.
Do not annotate generic process nouns such as "manufacturing method" or "bonding method" when they merely state that some method exists without naming a specific technique or framework.

Distinguish methodology from tasks or activities: annotate the named technique used to carry out the task, not the task itself. For example, "therapeutic drug monitoring" is an activity, while "liquid chromatography" is the methodology used within it.
Distinguish methodology from equipment or physical objects: if a phrase names machinery, substrates, chips, solder balls, or other artifacts rather than a procedural approach, do not annotate it.

Distinguish methodology from broad conceptual standards or attitudes unless they function as a named framework or approach; named instructional or analytical frameworks such as "universal design for learning" are methodology, but general "standards," "provision," or "practice" are not.

microorganism.definition: A microorganism is a mention of a microscopic biological infectious agent or microbial organism/group, including generic categories and specific scientific names such as viruses, bacteria, and named microbial species.
include_rules: Annotate names of microorganisms at any taxonomic specificity when the text refers to the organism itself.
Annotate specific scientific organism names, including binomials such as "Salmonella typhimurium".
Annotate generic microorganism nouns and plural forms such as "virus", "viruses", and "bacteria".
Annotate modified microorganism group mentions when the modifier helps specify the microbial group, e.g., "atypical bacteria".
Annotate microorganism mentions whether they appear as the detected agent, suspected agent, or general biological concept.
Annotate mentions in clinical, laboratory, or descriptive context as long as the span denotes the microorganism itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate disease names, infections, or syndromes, even if they are caused by or named after a microorganism.
Do not annotate non-microbial organisms such as animal hosts or humans.
Do not annotate laboratory tests, diagnostic procedures, treatments, or mechanisms related to microorganisms.
Do not annotate anatomical sites, body fluids, environmental materials, or containers where microorganisms are found.
Do not annotate biochemical substances, enzymes, genes, proteins, or trace elements.
Do not annotate abbreviations or descriptors unless they directly denote a microorganism mention.
Do not annotate text where a microorganism-like string is absent or where the term refers to something unrelated to biology.
boundary_rules: Annotate the contiguous span that names the microorganism, including genus/species words in scientific names, e.g., "Salmonella typhimurium".
Include generic head nouns when they themselves denote microorganisms, e.g., "virus", "viruses", "bacteria".
Include modifiers that are part of the microorganism mention, such as descriptive subtype adjectives in phrases like "atypical bacteria".
Do not extend the span to surrounding specimen, host, location, or test context, e.g., exclude words in "in both the turtle's feces" or "examined by polymerase chain reaction".
Do not include disease names or condition descriptors even when morphologically derived from a microorganism name.
For coordinated mentions, annotate each microorganism expression as it appears; if a single coordinated phrase shares one microorganism head, include the full microorganism phrase only.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate diseases or syndromes caused by microorganisms; annotate the organism itself only. For example, "malignant theileriosis" is a disease mention, not a microorganism mention.
Do not annotate hosts, body sites, environmental sources, or specimens that may contain microorganisms, such as animals, feces, water, or nasopharyngeal washes.
Do not annotate laboratory methods, assays, or biomedical processes used to detect microorganisms, such as "polymerase chain reaction".
Do not annotate proteins, enzymes, chemicals, or trace elements mentioned in biomedical context; these are substances, not microorganisms.
Differentiate microorganism names from taxonomic or disease-derived adjectives when the text refers to the disease or condition rather than the organism itself.

Annotate both generic microorganism categories and specific organism names when they refer to microbial entities, but not broader non-microbial biological entities unless the text explicitly names a microorganism group.

military unit.definition: A military unit is a named or specifically referenced military organizational body, formation, branch, base-level command, or service division within armed forces.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of military formations or commands, such as numbered armies, divisions, brigades, fleets, wings, or similar units.
Annotate abbreviations and acronyms that refer to a military organizational entity in context, such as a base or command abbreviation used as the acting organization.
Annotate named branches or service divisions of the armed forces when the phrase denotes the military organizational category itself, as in "branch of service".
Annotate both formal and semi-formal military organization names when they function as the actor, subject, or referenced organization in the sentence.
Annotate references to military entities even when they are broad organizational categories rather than combat formations, provided the phrase denotes a military service organization.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-military organizations, academic departments, colleges, research programs, or scientific groups.
Do not annotate biomedical terms, cell lines, diseases, chemicals, or clinical study language.
Do not annotate generic nouns such as "army", "service", "base", or "branch" when they are used nonspecifically and do not identify a particular military organization.
Do not annotate military-related activities, campaigns, operations, contracts, jobs, flights, or safety procedures.
Do not annotate locations, facilities, or regions unless the expression is being used as the name of a military organizational entity.
Do not annotate broad references to the armed forces or military service when they describe a concept rather than a unit-level or organizational entity.
boundary_rules: Include the full contiguous name of the military unit, including numeric/designator elements and the unit term itself, as in "7th Army".
Include official abbreviations or acronyms when they directly refer to a military unit or installation treated as a military organizational entity in context, e.g. "JBER".
Include generic organizational phrases only when they denote a military organizational division rather than a general concept, e.g. "branch of service" in a military-service context.
Do not include surrounding determiners, prepositions, punctuation, or appositional role titles, e.g. annotate "JBER" but not "the JBER" or nearby text such as "public affairs director Maj.".
Do not extend the span to include actions, locations, time expressions, or descriptive clauses attached to the unit name, e.g. annotate only "7th Army" in "the 7th Army was in eastern France".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a military unit with a geographic location: annotate the organizational entity "JBER" or "7th Army", but not locations associated with it such as "eastern France".
Do not confuse a military unit with a person or rank title: military ranks, officers, and job titles are not military units.
Do not confuse a military unit with an event or operation: campaigns, wars, or flights are activities, not organizational units.
Do not confuse a military unit with a job, occupational specialty, or contractual/service descriptor; only the military organization itself should be annotated.
Do not confuse a military unit with a general institutional or academic organization unless the text specifically indicates a military organizational body.

mineral.definition: A naturally occurring mineral, chemical element, or inorganic substance mentioned as a material or dissolved constituent, such as quartz, calcium, or magnesium.
include_rules: Annotate specific named minerals such as crystalline or geological substances, e.g., "quartz".
Annotate chemical elements when they are mentioned as substances or dissolved constituents, e.g., "calcium" and "magnesium".
Annotate mineral names whether they occur in biomedical, environmental, or materials-science contexts, as long as the mention denotes the inorganic substance itself.
Annotate standalone mineral names inside coordinated lists, e.g., both "Calcium" and "magnesium" in a conjunction.
Annotate the substance name even when it appears as part of a larger descriptive phrase about levels, surfaces, or media.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate organic compounds, vitamins, proteins, drugs, or other biomolecules, even if they interact with a mineral.
Do not annotate generic words like "mineral", "metal", "surface", or "water" unless they are part of a specific mineral name.
Do not annotate properties, states, or measurements of minerals, such as levels, concentrations, dominance, or risk associations.
Do not annotate brand names, organizations, software terms, network terms, or other non-substance text that merely resembles a chemical or technical token.
Do not annotate body parts, cell types, diseases, or environmental media surrounding the mineral mention, such as "human lymphoid cells" or "drinking water".
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the mineral, element, or inorganic substance itself.
Include the full lexical name of the substance, whether capitalized or lowercase, e.g., "Calcium", "magnesium", "quartz".
Do not include surrounding determiners, quantities, concentrations, or measurement phrases, e.g., annotate "calcium" not "intracellular calcium level".
Do not include adjoining context words that describe location, source, or use, e.g., annotate "magnesium" not "magnesium in drinking water".
When the substance name appears inside a longer noun phrase, select only the substance term unless the entire phrase is the conventional mineral name.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate minerals and chemical elements used as substances; do not confuse them with biological molecules or vitamins. For example, "calcium" is mineral, but "Vitamin C" is not.
Do not annotate physical properties, biomedical measurements, or outcomes associated with the mineral, such as "calcium level" or disease-risk phrases; only the substance name is mineral.

Do not annotate materials only when they are mentioned as part of a product, instrument, code sample, network log, or unrelated technical text unless a true mineral/substance name is explicitly present.
If a word can denote both a substance and some other concept, annotate it only when the sentence uses it as a mineral/material entity, such as a surface material or dissolved substance.

model.definition: A named analytical, statistical, mathematical, computational, kinetic, or theoretical model: a formal framework or representation explicitly referred to as a model in the text.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that explicitly name a model, typically ending with or centered on the word "model".
Include statistical model names, such as effect-based or distribution-based models, when presented as a specific model type.
Include scientific or engineering model names that describe mechanistic or process representations, such as "kinetic model".
Include modifiers that identify the subtype of model, including mathematical, probabilistic, methodological, or descriptive qualifiers.
Annotate the model mention whether it is being developed, validated, set, used, compared, or analyzed, as long as the span itself denotes the model.
Include both common descriptive model names and formalized model-type expressions when they function as the name of a modeling framework.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate domain objects, devices, materials, or treatments that are not formal models, even if they occur in technical writing.
Do not annotate generic uses of "model" without a specific model type or name when the mention does not identify a distinct modeling framework.
Do not annotate related research activities, such as analyses, procedures, experiments, validations, or conversions, unless the span itself names a model.
Do not annotate descriptive context about what the model is used for, applied to, or validated on if that context is outside the model name.
Do not annotate terms that describe side effects, infections, pharmacokinetics, surgical procedures, or other scientific concepts unless they explicitly form part of a model name.
Do not annotate abbreviations or shorthand references unless they clearly and explicitly denote a model mention in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase naming the model, including descriptive modifiers that are part of its established or local name, e.g., "kinetic model", "random effects model", "negative binomial model".
Include adjectival, statistical, mathematical, or methodological qualifiers immediately attached to "model" when they specify the model type.
Exclude surrounding determiners, verbs, and non-naming context, e.g., annotate "random effects model" not "a random effects model was set a priori".
If the model name contains multiple premodifiers, include all of them if they jointly identify the model type.

Do not extend the span to following purpose or application phrases unless they are syntactically part of the model name itself; for example, exclude trailing phrases like "for sorghum" or "for cellulosic conversion" from "kinetic model".

Annotate only the explicit model mention; do not annotate implied models when only an analysis, procedure, or distribution is mentioned without the word "model" or a clearly model-denoting construction.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish model from general methods or procedures: annotate the specific conceptual representation or formal framework named as a model, not broader activities such as "Bayesian analysis" or "meta-analytic procedure".

Distinguish model from mathematical distributions or effects when they are not presented as models; annotate "negative binomial model" but not "negative binomial" alone unless the text clearly uses it as the full model name.
Distinguish model from physical devices, apparatuses, materials, and biomedical treatments; technical nouns like "apparatus", "wire", "antibiotics", and "therapy" are not model entities.

Distinguish model from generic uses of the word "model" meaning example, version, or product line; only annotate when the term denotes an analytical, statistical, computational, kinetic, or theoretical model.
Distinguish model from variables, parameters, and assumptions used within a model; annotate the model name itself, not its components or settings unless they are part of the model's name.

module.definition: A software component or code package mentioned as a module, plugin, toolkit, library, or similar reusable unit of functionality, including both named modules and generic references to such software components.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of software modules, plugins, toolkits, libraries, or packages.
Annotate named code modules or packages used in programming contexts, including names with namespace syntax such as "Data::Dumper".
Annotate generic mentions of software components when they clearly denote module-like units, such as "plugins".
Annotate toolkit-style software resources when they are referred to as reusable software components, such as "DICOM toolkit".
Include mentions whether singular or plural if they refer to software component entities rather than abstract concepts.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate organizations, companies, brands, cryptocurrencies, tokens, or marketplaces, even if they are technology-related.
Do not annotate hardware devices, apparatuses, record/replay devices, or other physical equipment.
Do not annotate broad software platforms, systems, or applications unless the mention specifically refers to a module-like component within them.
Do not annotate technical services, protocols, standards, encodings, or service categories unless they are explicitly packaged as a software module or toolkit.
Do not annotate purely descriptive phrases about functionality, implementation, or usage when no module/component is named.
Do not annotate incidental capitalized strings or product-like names that lack evidence of being a software component in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the module itself.
Include the full official module or package name, including namespace separators or punctuation that are part of the name, such as "Data::Dumper".
Include a descriptive software-type noun when it is part of the entity mention and helps identify the specific module, as in "DICOM toolkit".
For generic plural mentions used as software components, annotate the noun itself when it clearly refers to modules/plugins, as in "plugins".
Exclude surrounding determiners, verbs, and explanatory context such as "other", "a", "the", "module", or trailing phrases unless they are part of the module name.
Do not extend the span to include unrelated organization, platform, document, or device descriptions surrounding the module mention.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate software components, add-ons, libraries, toolkits, or named code modules; do not annotate companies, products, currencies, or marketplaces unless they are specifically referred to as modules.
A module is a functional software unit used within software development or computing contexts; hardware devices, apparatuses, and physical systems are not module entities here.
Documentation, links, standards, or services are not modules unless the mention explicitly names a software component, as with "DICOM toolkit" rather than "DICOM services".
Generic technical nouns should be annotated only when they denote software modules/plugins in context; ordinary uses of words like "platform" or "device" are not module entities.
When a phrase contains both a broader system and a module-like component, annotate only the component-level software unit, not the larger business or product context.

molecule.definition: A molecule is a mention of a chemical or biochemical substance or molecular unit, including named biomolecules and generic references to molecular entities, when the text refers to the substance itself rather than a process, disease, or other non-molecular concept.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of biomolecules and chemical substances, such as nucleic acids like "RNA".
Annotate molecular units or components when they are referred to as entities, including sequence-unit expressions like "nucleotide 70".
Annotate generic references to molecular entities when they clearly mean actual molecules, such as "molecules" in a scientific context.
Annotate molecule mentions whether singular or plural.
Annotate molecule mentions embedded in larger scientific phrases if the span itself denotes the molecular entity, e.g. "RNA" inside "RNA synthesis".
Annotate mentions that identify a molecule by indexed or numbered specification when the full expression denotes the molecular entity or unit.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-scientific text, software/version strings, organizations, or general prose with no chemical or biochemical meaning.
Do not annotate biological processes, reactions, or activities, such as "synthesis", even when they contain a molecular participant.
Do not annotate mutation descriptions, substitution events, or experimental findings as molecule mentions unless a specific molecule or molecular unit is explicitly named within them.
Do not annotate isolated letters, bases, or numbers by themselves when they are only part of a change description and not a standalone molecular mention in context.
Do not annotate descriptive modifiers or purpose clauses around the molecule, such as "new" or "for targeting", unless they are part of the molecule's name.
Do not annotate broad abstract terms like "process" or "identification" even if they occur near molecule mentions.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest span that directly names the molecule or molecular unit, e.g. "RNA", "molecules", "nucleotide 70".
Include integral type words when they are part of the molecular mention, such as "nucleotide" in "nucleotide 70".
Include attached identifiers, numbers, or symbols that specify the molecule instance or position when they form one expression, e.g. "nucleotide 70".
Do not extend the span to surrounding process, action, or descriptive words, e.g. annotate "RNA" not "RNA synthesis", and annotate "molecules" not "new molecules for targeting".
Exclude determiners, adjectives, and quantifiers unless they are part of the established molecular name or formula.
When a molecular mention appears inside a larger noun phrase, select only the molecular term itself unless the modifiers are necessary to identify the molecule.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse molecules with biological processes or events: in "RNA synthesis", only "RNA" is a molecule, while "synthesis" describes a process.

Do not confuse molecules with mutations or sequence changes: in "a base substitution G for A at nucleotide 70", the mutation event is not the molecule mention; "nucleotide 70" is the molecular unit/location-like molecule mention.

Do not confuse molecules with genes, proteins, or diseases unless the text explicitly names a molecular substance or molecular unit. Annotate the chemical/biochemical entity itself, not the associated condition or organismal concept.
Do not annotate purely numerical positions or coordinates alone; annotate them only when attached to a molecular unit term, as in "nucleotide 70".
Generic molecular class terms can be annotated when they clearly denote molecular entities, as with "molecules", but not when the word is used metaphorically or outside a biochemical/chemical sense.

money.definition: Expressions that denote money as a currency unit, cash-like medium, or a specific monetary amount, including numeric amounts with currency markers and literal references to money itself.
include_rules: Annotate explicit monetary amounts containing a number plus a currency symbol, currency code, or currency unit word.
Annotate written currency names or units when they refer to money, even without a numeral, such as "dollar".
Annotate noun phrases that literally denote money or a money medium, such as "paper money".
Annotate monetary amounts with magnitude modifiers such as thousand, million, or billion when they are part of the same money expression.
Annotate both prefixed and suffixed currency formats, such as "$1.5 billion", "USD80 billion", or "80 dollars".
Annotate plural or singular currency terms when they denote the currency itself rather than something metaphorical.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate plain numbers without any currency meaning, even if they appear near financial context.
Do not annotate non-monetary measurements, technical specifications, frequencies, file formats, or quantities such as "44kHz" or ".wav".
