Do not annotate names of plans, packages, services, programs, or budgets by themselves, even when they involve spending.
Do not annotate generic financial actions or concepts without an explicit money expression, such as "investment", "budget", "funding", or "prosperity".
Do not annotate vague quantifiers unless they are part of a monetary phrase with a currency marker or money noun.
Do not annotate commodities, cards, or paper unless the phrase explicitly denotes money, as in "paper money".
boundary_rules: Include the full monetary expression when a number and currency marker form one unit, including currency symbols/codes and magnitude words: "USD80 billion", "$3 million", "10 dollars".
Include currency unit nouns when they are the referent themselves, even without a number: "the dollar", "euros", "yen".
Include generic money-denoting nouns only when they literally refer to currency/cash or a money medium: "paper money", "cash".
Do not include surrounding verbs, prepositions, or measure contexts outside the money phrase: in "investment of more than USD80 billion", annotate only "USD80 billion".
For symbol-prefixed amounts, include the symbol as part of the span if attached to the number: "$1.5 billion".
Do not split a single continuous monetary amount into smaller parts; annotate the complete amount expression as one span.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate money only when the expression denotes currency, cash, or an amount of money; do not annotate other numeric quantities, sizes, counts, dates, times, or technical values such as "44kHz".
Distinguish money from organizations, government programs, or budgets: in text about funding, annotate only the monetary amount, not the funded initiative or policy name.
Distinguish money from economic events or actions such as "investment", "budget", "price rise", or "devalue" unless an explicit money amount or currency term is present.
Distinguish money from products or physical objects unless the object is itself a money medium, as in "paper money".
If a currency word is used in a non-monetary or idiomatic way and does not denote actual money/currency, do not annotate it.

month.definition: A named calendar month mention, referring to one of the twelve months of the year in a temporal expression.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of calendar month names such as "January", "February", "March", "April", "May", "June", "July", "August", "September", "October", "November", and "December".
Annotate a month name when it appears alone or as part of a larger temporal expression, such as "in May" or "July 2000".
Annotate month names regardless of whether they indicate past, present, or future time.
Annotate month mentions in any syntactic role, including after prepositions or within narrative time references, as long as the word denotes a calendar month.
Annotate standard month abbreviations if they clearly refer to a calendar month.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-temporal words or technical terms that are not month names, even if they appear in contexts discussing time or frequency.
Do not annotate years, full dates, days of the week, seasons, times of day, or generic temporal nouns such as "month", "summer", or "yesterday".
Do not annotate relative or vague temporal phrases that do not explicitly name a month, such as "last time", "beginning of the year", or "recently".
Do not annotate numbers attached to dates or years unless the number itself is part of a separate annotation scheme; for this type, only the month name is annotated.
Do not annotate month-like strings when they are used as names of people, places, organizations, products, or other non-calendar entities.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the month word itself, e.g., "August", "July", "May".
Do not include adjacent years, dates, day numbers, or modifiers; in "July 2000", annotate only "July".
Do not include preceding prepositions or determiners such as "in", "of", "the beginning of"; in "at the beginning of August", annotate only "August".
For multi-word calendar expressions, include only the token that names the month, excluding surrounding temporal context unless it is part of the month name itself.
Use the exact text span corresponding to the calendar month mention, whether it appears capitalized or not.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate month names only when they denote a calendar month, not when similar-looking strings function as personal names, organizations, titles, or other non-temporal uses.
Do not annotate broader time expressions as a whole; if a larger date or time expression contains a month, only the month token is the month entity.
Do not confuse months with seasons, days of the week, or generic time units such as "year" or "week"; only named months belong to this type.
Do not confuse month mentions with domain-specific terms or abbreviations that resemble words but are not calendar months, such as medical or technical terminology.

municipality.definition: A named local administrative territorial unit at the municipality level, typically a city-, town-, or district-level civil division, referred to by its proper name.
include_rules: Annotate proper names that denote a municipality-level administrative unit.
Annotate municipality names whether they appear alone or after a descriptor such as "municipality of" or "Municipality of".
Annotate multiword municipality names in full when all words are part of the official name.
Annotate municipality names used as geographic endpoints or containers in locative phrases, e.g. places something is in, from, or part of, when the name refers to the municipal unit.
Annotate municipality names in historical or administrative statements, such as when an area was formerly part of a named municipality.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic administrative nouns without a specific name, such as "municipality" by itself.
Do not annotate non-administrative place features such as lakes, rivers, shores, or other natural landmarks, even when they occur near municipality names.
Do not annotate broader or different administrative levels unless the text specifically names a municipality.
Do not annotate descriptive location phrases or relational expressions that are not proper municipality names.
Do not annotate non-entity text, technical terms, or capitalized phrases unrelated to places or administration.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the municipality name itself, excluding administrative descriptors such as "municipality of", "Municipality of", or similar governing-unit labels when they are not part of the proper name span.
Include the full official multiword municipality name, including internal particles or articles, e.g. "Rideau Lakes", "Tay Valley", and "Isabela de Sagua".
Do not extend the span to surrounding prepositions or location-introducing phrases such as "in", "at", "from", or "part of".

If a place name appears inside a larger phrase naming a municipality, annotate just the place-name span when that is the municipality name, e.g. in "the municipality of Sagua la Grande", the span is "Sagua la Grande".
Preserve the exact surface form used in text, including capitalization, but exclude punctuation not part of the name.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish municipality from broader administrative regions: annotate the local municipal unit name, not a larger province, state, county, or country that may contain it.

Distinguish municipality from settlements or populated places: if the text refers to a city, town, or village as a settlement rather than as a municipal administrative unit, do not annotate it as municipality. Annotate when the name denotes the municipality itself, as signaled by context such as "municipality of" or by known municipal names like "Rideau Lakes" and "Tay Valley".
Distinguish municipality from physical geographic features: lakes, rivers, shores, and similar natural features are not municipality even when adjacent to municipal names in the same sentence.
Distinguish municipality from organizations or government bodies: the administrative area name is municipality, but a council, office, or governing institution associated with it is not.
Distinguish municipality from generic location references: words like "west shore", "mouth", or "part" describe positions or relations, not municipal entities.

music group.definition: A named musical ensemble or performing act consisting of multiple members, such as a band, vocal group, idol group, orchestra, choir, or similar collective identified by a proper name.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of musical ensembles or performing acts made up of multiple members.
Include named bands, vocal groups, idol groups, orchestras, choirs, and similar collectives when the text refers to them as a unit.
Annotate the group name whether it appears after a role noun or membership phrase, such as in contexts like "leader of" or "member of".
Annotate the official name even when it contains apostrophes or multiword constructions, as in "Johnny's West".
Annotate historical or less common ensemble names when they clearly denote a music-performing group, as in "Grodeck Whipperjenny".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic references to groups without a specific proper name, such as "the musical group", "band", "choir", or "ensemble" alone.
Do not annotate titles of musical works or other creative works, even when they occur in music contexts or with words like "oratorio" or "debut".
Do not annotate names of individual musicians, composers, conductors, or other persons unless the name itself is the group name.
Do not annotate film titles, lists, websites, software/library names, corporations, licenses, packages, or code identifiers.
Do not annotate broad categories or descriptors such as "Punjabi films", "films of 2012", or similar non-group class labels.
Do not annotate partial fragments of a group name when the full name is present and recoverable in the text.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest span that uniquely identifies the music group, without including preceding descriptors such as "the musical group", "member of", or "leader of".
Include integral articles or possessives only when they are part of the official name; otherwise exclude them. For example, annotate "Grodeck Whipperjenny" rather than "the Grodeck Whipperjenny".
Include internal punctuation, apostrophes, and capitalization that are part of the name, as in "Johnny's West".
When a name appears inside a larger noun phrase describing role or membership, select only the group name span, e.g. "Johnny's West" in "a member Johnny's West".
Do not extend the span to surrounding titles, media descriptions, list headings, or sentence context that merely mentions music-related content.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a music group with a musical work title. Titles of songs, albums, operas, oratorios, or other compositions are not music groups even if they appear in music-related context.
Do not annotate names of individual people associated with music, such as performers, composers, or leaders, unless the name denotes the group itself.
Do not annotate genre labels, language/cultural labels, or industry categories such as "Punjabi films"; these describe categories of works, not a group entity.
Do not annotate companies, publishers, software packages, websites, or organizations unrelated to a performing music ensemble, even if they occur in entertainment or media text.

If an expression refers to a collective performing act or band/ensemble/group as the entity being joined, led, or performing, treat it as a music group; if it refers to a created work that debuted or was composed, treat it as a work, not a group.

musical instrument.definition: A musical instrument is a physical device or instrument type used to produce musical sound, such as string, keyboard, percussion, wind, or electronic instruments.
include_rules: Annotate common names of musical instruments, such as keyboard, string, and percussion instruments.
Annotate both single-word and multiword instrument names when the phrase denotes a specific instrument type, such as "electric piano".
Annotate plural as well as singular instrument mentions, such as "drums".
Annotate instrument names whether they appear in descriptions of performance, recording, composition, or equipment.
Annotate generic instrument type names when they clearly denote a musical instrument, such as "piano", "violin", "organ", "guitar", and "drums".
Annotate the instrument mention even when preceded by technical or descriptive modifiers that are not included in the span, provided the core instrument term is explicit.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that are not musical instruments, even if they are common nouns appearing near music-related text.
Do not annotate software, code identifiers, class names, property names, technical components, or game items.
Do not annotate musicians, performers, bands, or other people associated with music.
Do not annotate musical genres, compositions, songs, albums, or performances.
Do not annotate generic collection words or packaging nouns unless the instrument name itself is present; exclude words like "set" when the true entity is "drums".
Do not annotate descriptive tuning, model, or version prefixes when they are not part of the instrument name itself, such as "15-ET" in "15-ET guitar".
Do not annotate weapon names, fictional items, or other non-musical objects that may resemble instrument-like nouns in unrelated contexts.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the instrument itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the conventional instrument name, as in "electric piano".
Exclude surrounding articles, quantifiers, and conjunctions, such as "an", "a set of", and "and"; annotate only the instrument term, e.g. "drums" not "a set of drums".
Exclude nearby context describing ownership, use, tuning, or accompaniment unless it is part of the instrument name; for example, in "15-ET guitar", annotate only "guitar".
When multiple instruments are listed separately, annotate each instrument as its own span, e.g. "piano" and "violin".
Do not extend the span to include performers or event context, such as "musicians", "suite", or "recorded".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the physical musical instrument, not the musician who plays it; "violin" is an instrument, while "musicians" is not.
Annotate instrument names even when they appear in music-performance contexts, but do not annotate musical works, compositions, or performances such as "suite".
Do not confuse instrument names with unrelated technical or product terms that happen to be nouns in other domains; only annotate terms that denote devices used to produce music.
If a word can refer to a non-musical object in context, annotate it only when the context indicates a musical instrument sense.
Annotate the instrument type rather than descriptive packaging around it; for example, choose "drums" rather than a collection phrase like "set of drums".

mutation.definition: A mutation is a textual mention of a specific genetic or protein sequence alteration, expressed either as a standardized variant code or as an explicit phrase describing the alteration event.
include_rules: Annotate standardized mutation or variant strings that specify an alteration, such as amino-acid substitutions or deletions.

Annotate explicit mutation descriptors that name the alteration event, including phrases containing mutation-type terms such as "missense substitution" when they refer to a concrete alteration mention.
Annotate both shorthand protein/DNA change formats and HGVS-like forms when they appear as the mutation mention.
Annotate mutation mentions even when they are modified by attributes such as "novel" or "somatic"; include the attribute only if it is part of the mutation phrase span.
Annotate mutation mentions whether they appear in positive, negative, comparative, or historical statements, as long as the text explicitly names the alteration.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text unrelated to biological sequence alterations.
Do not annotate generic mentions of mutation that lack a specific alteration identity, e.g. "a second mutation" by itself.
Do not annotate gene names, protein names, or loci alone unless the span explicitly denotes the mutation.
Do not annotate descriptive context such as "novel", "identified", "analysis revealed", or sample-level context unless they are part of the mutation phrase itself.
Do not annotate diseases, cell types, families, populations, or cases mentioned alongside the mutation.
Do not annotate software, security, technical, or other non-biomedical uses of words that resemble mutation notation or codes.
Do not annotate negated context words alone; in phrases like "without the R117H mutation", annotate only the mutation mention "R117H".
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that explicitly denotes the mutation.
For standard mutation codes, include the full code exactly as written, including prefixes and suffixes when present, e.g. "N21L", "R117H", "F417del", "p.Val116Glu".
If a descriptive mutation phrase is used as the mention, include the full phrase naming the alteration, e.g. "somatic HIF1A missense substitution".
Include mutation-type words such as "mutation", "substitution", "deletion", "insertion", or "missense" only when they are part of the explicit mutation mention being referred to.
Do not extend the span to include surrounding trigger words or syntax such as "identified", "a novel", "in the WFS1 gene", "without the", or disease/family context.
Do not include the gene name outside the mutation mention unless it is embedded inside a contiguous descriptive mutation phrase, as in "somatic HIF1A missense substitution".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate gene or protein names by themselves; annotate only the alteration affecting them. For example, annotate "p.Val116Glu" and "somatic HIF1A missense substitution", but not "WFS1" alone.
Do not annotate diseases, phenotypes, families, samples, or organisms even when they co-occur with a mutation mention.

Distinguish a mutation mention from a general statement about genetics: generic references such as "a second mutation" are not annotated unless a specific mutation identity or explicit alteration phrase is given.
Variant nomenclature strings are mutation entities, whereas surrounding genomic location, gene context, or experimental interpretation are not.
Broader descriptive phrases can be annotated when they themselves denote a specific alteration event, but not when they only describe study findings or analysis results.

name.definition: A word or phrase used as a specific name, label, alias, or designated appellation for an entity, person, place, or lexical item, typically in naming contexts such as being called, having a name, or being identified by a particular term.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names used to identify something by a particular appellation, especially in patterns such as "called X", "my name is X", or "the word X".
Annotate aliases, alternate names, screen names, nicknames, or self-identified name strings, including informal online name forms.
Annotate names of places, persons, or terms when the sentence presents the expression as the name itself rather than merely referring to the entity.
Annotate single-word and multiword names, including transliterated or non-English lexical names such as "Amethystos".
Annotate name strings that contain internal punctuation or separators when they function as one written name, such as "mia/mimi".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate common nouns or descriptive phrases that refer to a type, class, function, or object rather than a specific name.
Do not annotate technical terminology, equations, variable names, ratios, or abbreviations used only as scientific or mathematical notation.
Do not annotate standalone adjectives, product attributes, or marketing descriptors unless the text explicitly uses them as the entity's name.
Do not annotate full surrounding clauses that merely introduce a name; only the name token span should be tagged.
Do not annotate pronouns, determiners, or relational phrases adjacent to a name unless they are part of the actual name string.
Do not annotate mentions that are only references to brands, products, or items in generic discussion when the text does not clearly present them as names under this type.
boundary_rules: Annotate the exact token span of the name itself, without including surrounding trigger words such as "called", "name is", "word", "term", or quotation marks unless the quoted content itself is the name.
Include all words that are part of a multiword name, e.g. "Mystery Island" as a single span.
Include punctuation or separators only when they are part of the presented name form, e.g. the slash in "mia/mimi".
Do not extend the span to descriptive modifiers or appositives that are not part of the name, such as "The Greek word" before "Amethystos".
If a name appears inside parentheses or quotation marks, annotate only the name content, not the brackets or quotes, unless the punctuation is clearly part of the written name string.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate names themselves, not the underlying object described by common nouns. For example, tag "Mystery Island" but not generic nouns like "island", "palette", or "power amplifier" when they are merely object types.

Do not annotate technical formulas, abbreviations, variable names, or symbolic identifiers as names unless the text presents them as a naming expression. Items like "PAeff", "Pout", or "PAgain" in formula context are not names here.

Do not annotate product descriptions or title-like marketing phrases unless they function as explicit names. In the negative example, descriptive mentions such as "new Matte Chocolate Chip and White Chocolate palettes" are not names to tag under this type as given.

Distinguish names from ordinary lexical content: a word is annotated when it is used as a proper label, alias, username, personal self-identifier, or named term, not when it is just a common descriptive word.

Distinguish names from mentions of people or places as entity categories in their own right: this type captures the textual name string when used as a name/alias expression, regardless of whether it names a person, place, or term.

namespace.definition: A namespace is a programming or markup identifier that names a scope or naming container used to organize and qualify other identifiers, such as a C++ namespace name or an XML namespace prefix.
include_rules: Annotate identifiers that explicitly declare a namespace in source code, especially after the keyword namespace.
Annotate identifiers that act as namespace qualifiers before :: in programming languages when they denote the containing scope for another symbol.
Annotate XML namespace prefixes introduced by xmlns: declarations.
Annotate short or dotted-free namespace labels used to scope code symbols or markup names, when the context clearly shows namespace semantics.
Include the entity when the identifier's role is to provide naming scope rather than to name a callable, type, value, package, or URI.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic technical terms, platform names, or product names unless they explicitly function as namespace identifiers in code or markup context.
Do not annotate package names, module paths, filesystem paths, URLs, or URIs, even if they appear near namespace syntax.
Do not annotate class names, function names, method names, variables, constants, enum values, or other non-namespace code symbols.
Do not annotate namespace-like text appearing only in explanatory prose or questions when it is not actually used as a namespace declaration, qualifier, or XML namespace prefix.
Do not annotate scientific, geographic, organizational, or biological names that are unrelated to code/markup namespaces.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the namespace identifier itself, not surrounding syntax such as namespace, xmlns:, ::, braces, quotes, angle brackets, or punctuation.
For C++ namespace declarations, select just the declared name after the keyword namespace, e.g. cricket in namespace cricket {.
For qualified names using ::, annotate only the namespace portion when it functions as the container qualifier, e.g. std in std::runtime_error.
For XML namespace declarations, annotate the prefix bound by xmlns:, e.g. android in xmlns:android=..., not the URI value.
Do not extend the span to include nested members, classes, functions, package names, or paths that follow the namespace identifier.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a namespace with a package or application identifier: in package="com.yourcompany.t23podfile", the package value is not a namespace under this type, while android in xmlns:android is a namespace prefix and should be annotated.

Do not annotate ordinary words that match common namespace names when they are used in natural language rather than as code or markup identifiers, e.g. android in prose discussing Android-related code is not a namespace.

Do not confuse namespace qualifiers with class, function, enum, or constant names: in Attr::kInput, Attr is not evidenced here as a namespace declaration or namespace prefix example for this type, and kInput is a member/constant, not a namespace.
Do not annotate biological taxonomic namespaces or scientific categories such as genus names; these are domain terms, not programming or markup namespace identifiers.
A namespace is the naming container label used to qualify other identifiers or declared as such in code/markup; annotate the container label only, not the contained symbol.

nationality.definition: A word or phrase that denotes a person's or group's national identity, citizenship, or demonym, typically expressed as a nationality adjective or noun such as 'German', 'Japanese', or 'Americans'.
include_rules: Annotate demonyms and nationality adjectives/nouns that refer to people or groups by national identity, citizenship, or origin.
Annotate singular or plural nationality forms when they stand alone as person-denoting nouns, e.g., 'Americans'.
Annotate nationality adjectives when they modify a noun referring to people or human groups, e.g., 'German' in 'German soldiers'.
Annotate nationality terms used in identity descriptions of a person, even when embedded in longer explanations, e.g., 'Japanese' in a description of someone being biracial.
Annotate each distinct nationality mention separately when multiple nationalities appear in the same sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate country, city, or region names when they refer to places rather than people or national identity, e.g., 'Japan' in 'In Japan'.
Do not annotate nationality-like words when they clearly refer to a language, script, or other non-person sense.
Do not annotate adjectives inside product descriptions, brand names, or commercial text when they describe ingredients, styles, or marketing language rather than nationality.
Do not annotate technical strings, code, file paths, licenses, emails, or other non-linguistic text.
Do not annotate broad identity labels that are not nationalities, such as racial, ethnic, or mixed-heritage descriptors unless they are explicitly national demonyms.
Do not annotate common nouns modified by a nationality term; only the nationality itself is the target span.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the nationality word or phrase itself, including inflectional endings when they are part of the nationality form, e.g., 'American'/'Americans'.
When a nationality adjective modifies a common noun, annotate only the nationality term, not the following noun, e.g., annotate 'German' in 'German soldiers'.

When a nationality word appears as part of a descriptive phrase for a person or group, select just the nationality token, e.g., 'Japanese' in 'a Japanese term' only if it denotes nationality/ethnic identity rather than language; use context to decide.

Do not include surrounding determiners, articles, quotation marks, conjunctions, or punctuation, e.g., annotate 'British' not 'the British' unless the article is inseparable in the dataset conventions.
For coordinated nationalities, annotate each nationality separately, e.g., 'British' and 'German' as separate spans.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish nationality from location: annotate demonyms referring to people or national identity ('Americans'), but do not annotate country/place names themselves unless they function as nationality expressions. For example, 'Japan' is a location, while 'Japanese' is nationality.

Distinguish nationality from language: words like 'Japanese' can name a language or a nationality. Annotate only when the word refers to people, citizenship, national origin, or ethnic/national identity, not when it clearly names a language.
Distinguish nationality from organization or brand names: do not annotate words embedded in product names, company names, or titles unless they genuinely denote nationality in context.
Distinguish nationality from personal descriptors unrelated to national identity: terms such as 'biracial' describe identity categories but are not nationalities.

Distinguish nationality from generic cultural or geographic adjectives: only annotate adjectives/demonyms tied to a nation or its people, not other origin descriptors like regional, technological, or stylistic modifiers.

natural disaster.definition: A naturally occurring hazardous event or destructive environmental condition, referred to by the word or phrase naming the phenomenon itself.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that directly name a natural disaster or hazardous natural phenomenon, such as earthquakes, tornadoes, storms, floods, hurricanes, wildfires, landslides, droughts, tsunamis, or similar events.
Annotate severe weather or environmental condition expressions when they are used as the harmful natural event or cause of disruption, as in "bad weather".
Annotate both singular and plural mentions of disaster types, including generic event mentions when they clearly refer to actual natural disaster occurrences.
Annotate the disaster term even when it appears inside a larger sentence about impacts, casualties, reports, or rescue efforts.
Annotate disaster mentions regardless of whether the event is ongoing, past, forecast, or described abstractly, as long as the phrase denotes the natural hazard itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary non-disaster nouns, historical text, software strings, metadata, or any content that does not denote a natural hazard event or condition.
Do not annotate locations, buildings, organizations, or other entities associated with a disaster rather than the disaster itself.
Do not annotate consequences or response terms such as deaths, damage, rescuers, problems, reports, or warnings unless the disaster phenomenon itself is explicitly named.
Do not annotate generic environmental or climate terms when they do not refer to a specific hazardous event or harmful condition.
Do not annotate purely figurative or metaphorical uses of disaster terms if they do not describe an actual natural phenomenon.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the disaster phenomenon itself, such as "earthquake", "tornadoes", or "bad weather".
Include descriptive adjectives only when they are part of the disaster expression, as in "bad weather"; do not extend the span to surrounding effects, counts, locations, or time phrases.

Exclude determiners, numerals, and event-context words when they are outside the disaster name, e.g. annotate "tornadoes" not "173 tornadoes", and "storm system" only if it is itself being referred to as the disaster.
Do not include verbs or consequence phrases such as "has killed more than 4,000 people"; only the hazard term is annotated.
If multiple disaster mentions appear separately in a sentence, annotate each mention independently.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the hazardous natural event itself, not the human impact or outcome; for example, choose "earthquake" rather than casualty counts or rescue problems caused by it.
Do not confuse a natural disaster with a location affected by it; place names such as "Nepal" are not natural disaster mentions.

Do not confuse a natural disaster with weather-related background unless the phrase denotes harmful conditions as an event or hazard; in the provided examples, "bad weather" is included because it functions as the problematic natural condition.
Do not annotate built structures, organizations, products, software text, dates, or other non-event material even if words like "storm" or "weather" are absent from irrelevant text.

Different disaster types are all included under this type when they denote naturally occurring destructive phenomena, regardless of whether they are geological, meteorological, or related severe weather events.

neighborhood.definition: A named neighborhood, ward, district, or similarly localized sub-area within a city or town, used as a proper place name for a specific urban or community area.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of neighborhoods within a city or town, such as residential, cultural, or historic neighborhood names.
Annotate named intra-city districts or wards when they function as recognized sub-city areas, e.g., "Third Ward".
Annotate neighborhood names whether they appear alone or inside a larger locative phrase, e.g., "in the Third Ward" or "the Short North".
Annotate multi-word neighborhood names in full, including ordinal or directional words that are part of the name, e.g., "Grant City", "Short North", "Third Ward".
Annotate the name when it identifies a specific localized community area rather than a general geographic region.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate person names, organizations, legal case text, or any non-location expressions.
Do not annotate countries, islands, provinces, regions, or other large geographic areas such as "Philippines" or "Central Luzon".
Do not annotate generic descriptors of area type without a specific proper name, such as "rural area", "urban men", or "campus" used generically.
Do not annotate streets, roads, intersections, or transit lines unless the expression itself is a neighborhood name.
Do not annotate references to a neighborhood when only the larger place is named and no specific neighborhood name is given.
Do not annotate directional descriptions that merely describe relative position, such as "south of Grant City", unless they are part of a formal neighborhood name.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full neighborhood name as a contiguous span, including directional or descriptive components that are part of the proper name, e.g., "Short North" and "Third Ward".
Include administrative neighborhood designators when they are part of the official or conventional name, such as "Ward" in "Third Ward".
Do not include surrounding prepositions or locative phrases such as "in the", "near", or "south of"; annotate only the name itself.
Do not include larger enclosing places, street intersections, or institutions mentioned nearby unless they are part of the neighborhood name.
Preserve the surface form used in the text, including articles only if they are conventionally part of the name; in "the Short North", annotate "Short North" only.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate cities, towns, states, countries, or broad regions as neighborhood; a neighborhood is a sub-city or district-like area within a larger settlement.
Do not annotate street names, intersections, or addresses as neighborhood, even when they help locate one, e.g., "Scott Street" and "Elgin Street" are not neighborhoods.
Do not annotate campuses, institutions, or landmarks as neighborhood; in "near Ohio State's campus, the Short North", only "Short North" is the neighborhood.
Do not annotate generic land-use descriptions or demographic area descriptions unless they function as a proper neighborhood name.

Administrative subdivisions can be annotated as neighborhood when they refer to named intra-city districts, as in "Third Ward"; however, larger administrative regions such as provinces or regions are not neighborhood.

network.definition: A communication, computer, or telecommunications network referred to as an infrastructure or system for connecting devices, systems, or users.
include_rules: Annotate common-noun or descriptive mentions that denote a communication or telecommunications network, even when not a proper name, e.g., "local area network".
Annotate generic technical network mentions when the context indicates actual network infrastructure or systems, e.g., "network", "networks".
Annotate network mentions with functional or hierarchical modifiers, e.g., "upper network", "core network".
Annotate singular and plural forms when they refer to one or more networks as entities.
Annotate network mentions appearing in examples, appositives, or parenthetical explanations if they independently refer to a network.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that merely describe connectivity or communication without naming a network.
Do not annotate devices, equipment, or system components unless the span itself denotes a network.
Do not annotate non-technical or metaphorical uses of network-related language that do not refer to a communication or telecom network.
Do not annotate unrelated scientific terms containing similar wording, such as chemical, electrical, biological, or physiological processes, when they are not networks.
Do not annotate generic organization/group references or geographic/social collectives, even if information is shared among them.
Do not annotate modifiers by themselves when the full network phrase is present, e.g., do not annotate only "local area" from "local area network".
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase that names the network, including descriptive modifiers that are part of the network expression, e.g., "local area network", "upper network", "core network".
Include the head word "network" or "networks" in the span; do not annotate modifiers alone without the network head.
When a parenthetical example contains a separate network mention, annotate that inner mention separately if it independently names a network, e.g., annotate both "upper network" and "core network".
Exclude surrounding determiners, prepositions, verbs, and punctuation unless they are part of the network name itself.

For coordinated mentions, annotate each network expression separately if each has its own explicit network head; annotate a shared plural head once when the text gives only one combined mention, e.g., "networks".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate physical devices, components, or hardware that connect to or operate on a network; only the network system itself is of this type.
Do not annotate communication techniques, protocols, or methods used by networks, such as technical approaches mentioned alongside networks; only the network entity is included.

Do not annotate broad social or geopolitical collectives such as "users", "nations", or groups of people, even if they are connected by communication; this type is limited to communication/computer/telecom networks.

Do not confuse this type with generic scientific or biological uses of "network" unless the text refers to an actual communication or telecom network. The examples support technical communication infrastructure, not unrelated metaphorical or domain-specific structures.

If a phrase names a layer, role, or position within infrastructure and still denotes a network, annotate it, e.g., "upper network". If it denotes only a surrounding system without identifying a network, do not annotate.

newspaper.definition: A named newspaper publication title, including daily, weekly, or other newspaper names used to identify a specific newspaper as a news outlet.
include_rules: Annotate the proper name of a specific newspaper publication when it appears in text.
Include newspaper names whether they appear with or without an initial article that is part of the title, e.g., "The Guardian".
Annotate regional, city, or local newspaper titles, including multiword names with place names, e.g., "Boston Herald" and "Green Bay Press-Gazette".
Annotate newspaper names when they are mentioned as sources, publishers, endorsers, or subjects of commentary.
Include official title punctuation and compound forms that are part of the newspaper's name.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic references to newspapers or journalism when no specific newspaper title is given, e.g., "the newspaper" or "journalism".
Do not annotate magazine, journal, book, website, broadcast, or unspecified media outlet names unless they are clearly newspaper titles.
Do not annotate legal citation sources, court reporters, docket text, or case-document publication strings such as those appearing in judicial opinions.
Do not annotate company names, software package names, domains, licenses, or technical text that are not newspaper publications.
Do not annotate adjectives, slogans, quoted opinions, or article titles that merely appear near a newspaper name but are not themselves the newspaper title.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full newspaper title exactly as it appears in the text, including leading articles that are part of the official name, e.g., "The Guardian".
Include distinguishing geographic or organizational modifiers that are part of the title, e.g., "Boston Herald" and "Green Bay Press-Gazette".
Include punctuation and hyphenation that belong to the official title, e.g., the hyphen in "Press-Gazette".
Do not include surrounding determiners, prepositions, or verbs unless they are part of the official newspaper name.
Do not extend the span to include descriptive context such as "the newspaper", "story from", "reported by", or editorial actions like "advocated".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate newspaper titles as publications, not as organizations in a general sense, even if they perform actions such as endorsing or reporting.
Do not annotate generic media references such as "journalism", "the press", or "daily paper" unless they are part of a specific newspaper title.

Do not annotate legal reporters, case citations, or publication-like strings in judicial text unless they clearly refer to a newspaper; for example, citation abbreviations such as "Cal. Daily Op. Serv." are not newspapers here.
Do not annotate software package names, websites, licenses, repositories, or technical project names that may resemble named entities but are not newspapers.
If a span names a publication title specifically identifiable as a newspaper, annotate it; if it names a broader media company, legal source, or technical source instead, do not annotate it.

nickname.definition: An alternate informal, descriptive, or online name used to refer to a person, typically introduced as what someone "goes by," is "aka," or is otherwise called instead of their formal name.
include_rules: Annotate a word or phrase when it is explicitly presented as a name a person is also known by, such as after cues like "aka" or "go by".
Annotate informal aliases, online monikers, gamer tags, or descriptive sobriquets used to identify a person.
Annotate multiword nickname phrases when the full phrase functions as the person's alternate name.
Annotate each distinct nickname mentioned for a person, even if several appear in the same sentence.
Annotate nicknames even when they are stylistically unusual, capitalized irregularly, or resemble usernames, as long as they identify a person by an alternate name.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary person names when they are not being used as alternate names or aliases.
Do not annotate names of organizations, events, products, drugs, diseases, technical terms, code identifiers, or abbreviations.
Do not annotate generic praise, descriptions, or epithets unless the text shows they function as a name for a specific person.
Do not annotate quoted strings, labels, or screen text that are not tied to a person identity.
Do not annotate contextual cue words introducing the nickname, such as "aka", "go by", "my name is", or "most commonly".
Do not annotate text from code, configuration files, commands, or other technical content unless it explicitly identifies a person's nickname.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the nickname string itself, not the introducer words such as "aka", "go by", "called", "named", or similar cues.
Include all contiguous words that make up the nickname, including multiword nicknames such as "Insidiously Mad" and "Good Guy Zac".
When multiple nicknames are listed for the same person, annotate each nickname separately as its own span.
Do not include surrounding punctuation or parenthetical text unless the punctuation is part of the nickname itself.

Do not extend the span to include the person's legal/given name unless that full expression is being used as the nickname; in "Good Guy Zac", include the full phrase because the whole phrase is the nickname.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish nickname from a person's real or formal name: annotate the alias used to refer to the person informally or online, not the underlying legal/personal name.

Distinguish nickname from usernames, handles, or account names only when they are clearly presented as what the person is called or goes by; a bare string in technical text or code is not a nickname without person-reference context.
Distinguish nickname from titles, roles, or descriptors: phrases like "Editor" or occupational labels are not nicknames unless used as an alias for a specific person.
Distinguish nickname from organization names, event names, product names, and abbreviations such as "SPARTAN" or drug names; these refer to non-person entities, not alternate names for a person.
Distinguish nickname from memes, slogans, or quoted phrases unless the phrase is explicitly used to identify a person by that name.

norp.definition: A word or phrase referring to a nationality, religious group, political group, or ethnic/cultural identity of people, including adjectival forms used to denote such groups.
include_rules: Annotate terms that denote ethnic or cultural identities, including adjectival forms used for people, e.g. "African American" and "Indigenous."
Annotate nationality-related labels when they refer to people or a national/political grouping rather than a place.
Annotate political or ideological group labels, including adjectival forms, when they identify a group of people.
Annotate religious group names and their adjectival forms when they denote adherents or identity groups.
Annotate standalone modifiers when the context shows they function as the group identity label rather than as a general adjective, as in the provided example "National".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate medical, scientific, technical, or procedural terms, even if capitalized or adjectival, such as disease names, drug names, treatment regimens, or clinical acronyms.

Do not annotate generic demographic or descriptive words such as "male," "female," "singer," "poet," or similar role terms unless they are part of the nationality/religious/political/ethnic label itself.
Do not annotate common nouns that refer to people collectively without expressing a nationality, religion, politics, or ethnicity, such as "groups," "patients," or "media figures."
Do not annotate adjectives that indicate scale, jurisdiction, or institutional scope when they do not denote a people-group identity.
Do not annotate percentages, statistics, time spans, or other non-entity expressions.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that expresses the nationality, religious, political, or ethnic/cultural group identity.
Include all words that are part of the group label, including multiword identities such as "African American".
Do not include adjacent words that describe gender, occupation, age, number, or other non-group attributes, e.g. in "African American male rapper" annotate only "African American".
When a modifier itself is used as a standalone group label, annotate that modifier alone, e.g. "Indigenous" and "National" in the provided examples.
Do not extend the span to include surrounding nouns like "groups," "figures," "bandwagon," or other head/common nouns unless they are part of the established group name.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate geographic places or regions merely because they resemble nationality words; annotate only when the term denotes people or a sociopolitical/ethnic/religious group identity.

Do not annotate organizations, institutions, media outlets, churches, parties, or governments; if a word modifies an organization rather than naming a people/group identity, exclude it unless the text is clearly using it as a group label.
Do not annotate languages unless the mention refers to the people or identity group rather than the language itself.

Do not annotate medical, scientific, or technical adjectives such as disease descriptors, treatment categories, or clinical abbreviations; the negative examples illustrate that domain terminology is not norp.

Do not annotate ordinary adjectives of scope or administration unless they explicitly denote a political/national grouping in context; words like "National" should be tagged only when they refer to a sociopolitical identity/group, as in the provided example.

noun.definition: A common noun or noun phrase that denotes a person, object, place-like thing, concept, category, or other nominal referent, expressed as a lexical noun phrase rather than a proper name or a larger clause.
include_rules: Annotate common nouns and common noun phrases that denote concrete entities, roles, or abstract concepts, e.g. "website", "user", "style".
Annotate noun phrases with modifiers when the modifier is part of the same nominal referent, e.g. "new attraction".
Annotate nominal expressions that name linguistic, conceptual, or categorical entities, e.g. "relative clause" and "gerund-participial clause".
Annotate singular or plural common noun phrases whether they refer to physical things, people, abstract notions, or classificatory concepts.
Annotate count and mass nouns when they appear as standalone noun phrases or as the core of a larger noun phrase.
Annotate common-role nouns referring to participants in situations, e.g. "user".
Annotate abstract nouns referring to qualities, styles, or conceptual objects, e.g. "style".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate proper names or capitalized named entities such as company names, product names, place names, document labels, or person-like handles, e.g. items such as "Google", "Tehran", "GeoEye", "Fin-RACo" in the negative examples.
Do not annotate dates, times, percentages, citation markers, section headers, or other non-nominal metadata such as "Sunday", "June 21, 2009", "11:18am", "0.5--1%", or "Sec1".

Do not annotate noun material only because it appears inside long biomedical or technical terminology from the negative example when the expression is being used as a specialized named treatment or disease term rather than as a simple noun target.
Do not annotate pronouns, determiners, or quantifiers standing alone, such as "it", "their", or "some".
Do not annotate adjectives, adverbs, or standalone modifiers unless they are part of a noun phrase headed by a noun.
Do not annotate entire clauses, verb phrases, or gerundive constructions when they function as predicates or events rather than noun phrases.

Do not annotate repeated generic nouns embedded in references, titles, or structured formatting when they are part of bibliographic or markup content rather than ordinary sentence-level noun mentions.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun or noun phrase that names the thing, person, concept, or grammatical category, including descriptive modifiers that are part of the same referential unit, e.g. "new attraction", "relative clause", "gerund-participial clause".
Include determiners only when they are part of the annotated example span or needed for a fixed nominal expression; otherwise prefer the core noun phrase without surrounding function words.
Do not include surrounding verbs, prepositions, complementizers, punctuation, quotation marks, or clause material outside the noun phrase.
For coordinated noun phrases, annotate each noun phrase separately unless the coordination forms one single named nominal unit.
When a head noun is modified by adjectives or participial modifiers that are semantically part of the noun phrase, include them in the span, e.g. include "new" in "new attraction".

Use the minimal complete noun phrase: include the head noun and its internal modifiers, but exclude trailing relative clauses, appositives, or other post-nominal material that extends beyond the noun phrase core unless it is part of a fixed term.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate verbs, verbal clauses, or events merely because they describe actions involving a noun; annotate only the nominal expression itself.

Do not annotate proper names, dates, times, organizations, products, diseases, or other specialized named entities when the mention is functioning as a specific name rather than a common noun phrase; the negative examples show such named and technical references are outside this type.
Distinguish noun from adjective-only mentions: annotate "style" but not a standalone descriptive word unless it heads a noun phrase.
Distinguish noun from pronouns and determiners: references like "it", "their", "some" are not noun entities unless they are part of a larger noun phrase headed by a noun.

Distinguish noun from full technical descriptions of processes or treatments when the text is primarily listing domain-specific named concepts rather than simple noun mentions; only annotate if the target span is a common noun or noun phrase itself.

Grammatical labels such as "relative clause" and "gerund-participial clause" are included because they are noun phrases naming concepts, even though they refer to linguistic categories rather than physical objects.

number.definition: A number is an explicit expression of numeric value or quantity, written in digits or words, including counts and simple quantified expressions.
include_rules: Annotate explicit cardinal numbers written in digits, such as "5".
Annotate explicit cardinal numbers written in words, such as "five" or "Two".
Annotate quantified count expressions where a number directly modifies a counted item, such as "105 cases".
Annotate numbers appearing inside mathematical, logical, or comparative statements when they denote the numeric value itself, such as the "5" in "$i<5".
Annotate both singular and multiword number expressions when they directly express quantity or count.
Annotate numbers regardless of whether they refer to abstract values, counts of objects, cases, people, or thresholds, as long as they explicitly encode a number.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words or phrases that do not explicitly state a number, even if they imply amount or plurality.

Do not annotate alphanumeric codes, software versions, file names, variable names, property names, module names, or other technical identifiers containing digits, such as "D8", "D7", "$view_mode", or "Profile2", when they function as names rather than quantities.
Do not annotate single letters, symbols, operators, or full formulas unless there is a standalone numeric expression within them.
Do not annotate numbers that are embedded inside another token and are not separately functioning as a quantity expression.
Do not annotate surrounding context such as nouns, verbs, or adjectives when they are not part of the quantity span.
Do not annotate error messages or technical text with no explicit quantity expression used semantically as a number.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal span that expresses the numeric quantity.
Include the numeral or number word itself, e.g. "5" or "five".
Include an immediately attached counted unit or classifier when it forms one quantity expression, e.g. "105 cases".
Do not include surrounding function words, verbs, adjectives, or punctuation unless they are part of the number expression itself.
For comparative or mathematical context, annotate only the number token, not the full expression, e.g. in "$i<5" annotate "5" only.
For spelled-out numbers, include only the words making up the number phrase, not the noun outside the quantity unless the noun is part of the counted expression being annotated.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish number from identifiers, model names, and version-like strings: annotate only when the expression denotes a quantity, count, magnitude, order, or numeric value, not when it is part of a product/code name.

Distinguish number from generic technical tokens containing letters and digits such as "D8", "D7", variable names, or API/property names; these are not quantities unless the numeric part is explicitly used as a number.

Distinguish number from dates, times, and other specialized measurements only by semantic role: if the expression is used simply as a numeric value or count, annotate it; if it primarily names a calendar/time expression or another non-quantity entity, do not treat the whole expression as number.
Distinguish standalone numbers from surrounding mathematical expressions, conditions, and formulas: annotate the numeric value only, not operators, variables, or full equations.
Distinguish number from noun phrases that merely imply plurality without an explicit numeric expression; only annotate when a specific number is stated.

nutrient.definition: A nutrient is a chemical substance or nutrient class consumed by organisms for nourishment or normal physiological function, including vitamins, minerals, macronutrients, and essential nutrient groups.
include_rules: Annotate named vitamins, such as "Vitamin D".
Annotate minerals and trace elements when referred to as nutritional substances, such as "zinc".
Annotate nutrient classes and essential nutrient groups, such as "essential fatty acids".
Annotate macronutrients and basic nutrient substances, such as "protein" and "sugar".

Annotate nutrient mentions whether they appear in isolation or inside statements about concentration, interaction, composition, or physiological effect, as long as the span denotes the nutrient itself.
exclude_rules: Exclude proteins, compounds, or biomolecules when they are mentioned as structural, genetic, receptor, or mechanistic entities rather than as nutrients.
Exclude pharmaceuticals, thrombolytic agents, supplements framed purely as treatment agents, and other medical intervention substances if they are not referenced as nutrients.
Exclude foods, diets, organisms, tissues, sample names, and experimental group labels unless the nutrient itself is explicitly named.
Exclude generic biomedical or technical terms that are not nutritional substances, even if they appear in similar syntactic positions.
Exclude broader phrases that describe effects, measurements, or administration involving a nutrient when the nutrient word alone can be annotated.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the nutrient itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the nutrient name or class, such as letter/designation markers and essentiality descriptors: "Vitamin D", "essential fatty acids".
Include plural forms when they are part of the nutrient mention, e.g., "fatty acids".

Do not extend the span to surrounding verbs, quantities, comparison phrases, organisms, or experimental context, e.g., annotate "protein" alone in "higher in protein" and "sugar" alone in "contained more sugar".
Exclude adjacent words that denote receptors, molecules, tissues, treatments, devices, or other non-nutrient entities, e.g., annotate "Vitamin D" but not "vitamin D receptors".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate biological structures or molecules that are not nutrients, even if mentioned near a nutrient, such as "DNA molecules" or "vitamin D receptors"; only the nutrient substance itself is nutrient.

Do not annotate drugs, therapeutic agents, or medical intervention compounds when they are used as treatments rather than nutrients, as illustrated by non-annotated items like "nitroglycerin" and "urokinase".
Do not annotate foods, meals, or sample labels unless the text explicitly names the nutrient component; in "BF contained more sugar," annotate "sugar" but not "BF".
Do not annotate general biomedical, technical, or product terms that may contain everyday words resembling substances, such as "sounds," "engine," or brand/product names in non-biological contexts.
When a phrase contains both a nutrient and another entity, annotate only the nutrient portion, not the larger composite mention.

occupation.definition: A word or phrase that denotes a person's occupation, profession, job title, or formal work role.
include_rules: Annotate common nouns and noun phrases that name professions or jobs, such as "rancher", "geologist", and "officials".

Annotate specialized professional roles, including scientific, medical, governmental, and field-work occupations, such as "physician scientists", "translational researchers", and "federal wildlife biologist".
Annotate both singular and plural occupation mentions.
Annotate occupation mentions whether they refer to a specific individual, a generic class of workers, or people asked about their profession in a list.
Annotate modified occupational titles when the modifier is part of the job designation, e.g. "federal wildlife biologist" rather than only "biologist".
Annotate coordinated occupation mentions separately when each occupation is listed as its own noun phrase, e.g. "rancher", "hunter", "ski patroller".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate abstract factors, variables, outcomes, methods, models, or technical terms that are not person roles.
Do not annotate diseases, histological types, stages, treatments, or other biomedical entities.
Do not annotate generic references to people when no occupation or job role is expressed, such as family relationships, victims, patients, or women.
Do not annotate verbs or action descriptions that indicate what someone does without naming an occupation.
Do not annotate organizations, institutions, or fields of study by themselves, such as "rheumatology" or "hospital", unless they are embedded inside an occupation title.
Do not annotate adjectives or descriptors alone when the occupational head noun is missing.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal span that names the occupation itself.

Include modifiers that are part of the occupational title, including adjectival or institutional descriptors when they directly specify the role, as in "translational researchers" and "federal wildlife biologist".
Include multiword job titles in full, such as "ski patroller" or "physician scientists".
Exclude surrounding determiners, conjunctions, punctuation, and list markers such as "a", "an", commas, and question framing.
Do not extend the span to include organizations, specialties, activities, or affected people unless they are part of the occupational title itself.
For plural occupational nouns, annotate the full plural form, e.g. "officials", "researchers".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate a term as occupation only when it denotes a type of person defined by job, profession, official role, or work function.

Do not annotate diseases, medical conditions, tumors, prognostic factors, model terms, or other scientific/technical concepts even if they share suffixes or appear in similar noun phrases, e.g. "epithelial carcinoma", "random effects", "prognostic factor" are not occupations.
Do not annotate demographic or relational person terms that do not describe a job, such as "relatives" or "victims".

Do not annotate activities, hobbies, or participant roles unless the noun itself denotes a recognized occupation in context. For example, annotate "hunter" when used as a person role label, but not a verb or general activity mention.

If a phrase refers to people by employment or institutional role, annotate the role noun, e.g. "officials"; if it refers instead to a group only by status, condition, or relationship, do not annotate.
When a noun phrase contains both a field and a role word, annotate it only if the head denotes an occupation, e.g. "wildlife biologist" is an occupation because "biologist" is a profession.

operating system.definition: A named software operating system or operating-system distribution/platform, including official names and versioned releases, used as the system environment for devices or software.
include_rules: Annotate official names of operating systems and OS distributions/platforms, such as desktop, mobile, or device operating systems.
Annotate versioned OS mentions when a release number is given as part of the name, e.g., "Ubuntu 12.04".
Annotate branded variants or derivatives that function as named operating system distributions, e.g., "UbuntuStudio".
Annotate standalone OS names when they indicate the software platform on which an app, service, or content runs, e.g., "iOS" and "Android".
Annotate each distinct OS mention separately when multiple operating systems appear in the same sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text that refers only to generic concepts such as "operating system", "OS", "mobile platform", or "system" without a specific named OS.
Do not annotate applications, editors, plugins, configuration files, commands, or programming tools, even in technical contexts.
Do not annotate companies, developers, or product makers unless the mention itself is the operating system name.
Do not annotate devices, hardware families, or game/content names that are not operating systems.
Do not annotate descriptive phrases that merely indicate compatibility or environment without naming a specific OS.
Do not annotate unrelated proper nouns from non-software domains such as elections, places, people, or government bodies.
boundary_rules: Annotate the exact operating system name as it appears in text.
Include attached version or release identifiers when they are part of the OS mention, e.g., "Ubuntu 12.04".
Include product-family variants that are written as a single name, e.g., "UbuntuStudio".

Do not extend the span to surrounding descriptive text such as "based on", parentheses, appositions, or verbs; in "UbuntuStudio(based on Ubuntu 12.04)", annotate "UbuntuStudio" and "Ubuntu 12.04" separately.
Exclude preceding platform-role words when they are outside the name, such as "for" in "for iOS".
Exclude generic nouns following the OS name unless they are part of the official name, e.g., annotate "Android" not "Android phone".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate operating systems, not software applications or tools running on them; for example, annotate "iOS" but not "Vim".
Do not annotate configuration files, shell variables, plugins, or commands that may occur in technical text, such as ".vimrc", "$VIM/vimrc", "modeline", or "autocmd".
Do not annotate hardware, devices, game titles, or content merely described as working on an OS; in "PSP Games download in Android", only "Android" is the operating system.
Do not annotate organizations or vendors unless the text names the OS itself; for example, "Microsoft" is not an operating system, but "iOS" is.
Do not annotate geographic, political, or event names that are unrelated to software platforms, even if they contain words like "state" or other misleading context.

operation.definition: A term or phrase that names a specific action, procedure, processing step, or functional mode of operation, especially in technical or procedural contexts.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that explicitly name a mode or type of operation, especially when headed by "operation", e.g. "emergency operation" and "drive operation".
Annotate compact technical labels for processing actions or low-level computational operations, including abbreviated or hyphenated forms such as "bitwise-op".
Include operation names that describe how a device, circuit, system, or process functions, provided the span denotes the action/procedure rather than the object.
Annotate technical or procedural action terms even when modified by adjectives that specify condition, purpose, or subtype, as long as the whole phrase names the operation.
Annotate standalone operation terms when they can be interpreted as a specific procedural step in context.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general references to algorithms, recipes, programs, systems, or methods unless the text explicitly names them as an operation term.
Do not annotate broad everyday activities, states of existence, celebrations, campaigns, or social/political narratives that are not specific operational procedures.
Do not annotate the objects, components, or materials acted upon, such as power supplies, circuits, masks, contours, or networks.
Do not annotate generic verbs describing actions in context unless they appear as a noun-like or compact term naming the operation itself.
Do not annotate descriptive technical context surrounding an operation if it does not belong to the operation name, such as figure references, trigger statements, or explanatory clauses.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full operation expression, including modifiers that specify the kind or mode of operation when they are part of the name, e.g. "emergency operation" and "drive operation".
Include hyphenated or abbreviated operation terms as a single span when they directly denote an action or processing step, e.g. "bitwise-op".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding arguments, objects, or complements of the operation, such as "of the emergency power supplies 950" or "of a pixel circuit 2".
Do not include preceding determiners, discourse words, or verbs outside the operation phrase; annotate only the noun or compact label naming the operation itself.
When an operation word appears inside a longer noun phrase, select the minimal span that still names the operation type.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate operation only when the phrase denotes an action, procedure, mode of functioning, or processing step; do not annotate entities that are physical devices, systems, supplies, circuits, or other objects involved in that action.

Do not annotate method names that are presented as general mathematical or computational solutions unless they are explicitly referred to as an operation; for example, "algorithm" is not operation in the negative example.

Distinguish operation from broad events or life activities: expressions about living, celebrating, governing, or social existence are not operation unless they name a specific operational procedure or functional mode.
If a phrase refers to what a component does in a technical process, annotate the operation term itself, not the component performing it.
Operation is typically a process/action label rather than a person, organization, date, publication, or location mention; annotate only the procedural term.

operator.definition: A programming, query, or formal-language operator token or operator keyword that denotes an operation within an expression, condition, pattern, or object construction.
include_rules: Annotate explicit operator keywords used in programming, SQL, regex, or other formal-language contexts, such as LIKE, new, and AND.
Annotate operators when they are mentioned as the specific token needed, executed, or applied in code or query behavior.
Include both alphabetic operator words and, when present in similar contexts, symbolic operators if they function as operators in an expression.
Annotate the operator even when it appears inside explanatory prose rather than executable code, as long as the token itself is the operator being referred to.
Annotate operator mentions that express logical, construction, matching, arithmetic, comparison, or similar formal operations, provided the mention is the operator token itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate natural-language descriptions of operations when they are not operator tokens, such as operation, division, modulus, remainder, or prose uses of and.
Do not annotate variable names, class names, function names, method names, object names, or other identifiers even if they contain operator-like substrings.
Do not annotate language keyword inventories, syntax highlighter word lists, regex character classes, or metacode that names possible operators without using them as operator mentions in context.
Do not annotate punctuation or symbols that are not serving as operators in context.
Do not annotate full code expressions, comparisons, or statements; only the operator token qualifies.
Do not annotate generic discussions of efficiency or semantics of an operation unless a specific operator token itself is explicitly present and being referred to.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the exact token(s) that realize the operator, such as LIKE, new, or AND.
Do not include surrounding operands, punctuation, parentheses, quotes, assignment targets, or full expressions.
For symbolic operators, if annotated in other contexts, the span should cover only the operator symbol itself, not adjacent numbers or variables.
For word-based operators, include only the operator word, preserving its surface form and casing as written.
When an operator appears inside code or prose, annotate just the operator token, not the enclosing statement or sentence.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate words that merely describe an operation in natural language; annotate only the operator token used as a programming, query, or formal language construct. For example, prose mentions like bit operation or and operation are not operator entities.

Distinguish operator from keywords that declare structure, types, or control flow unless they are functioning as operators. new is included because it acts as an operator; generic language keywords listed as part of a syntax inventory are not annotated unless used as an actual operator mention.
Distinguish operator from function names, method names, commands, or identifiers. A callable or named program element is not an operator just because it performs an operation.
Distinguish operator from full expressions or statements. In x % 2 == 1, the entity type targets the operator token itself, not the whole comparison or arithmetic expression.

Distinguish operator from metalinguistic references to classes of symbols, such as isOperatorChar or a list of keywords in source code defining a lexer; those are mentions about language elements, not actual operator entities to annotate in this scheme.

option.definition: A word or phrase that names a selectable, configurable, or command-line option in software, systems, interfaces, or technical procedures.
include_rules: Annotate names of command-line or configuration options mentioned as options in technical text.
Annotate option names referenced in error messages, usage text, or diagnostics when they identify the specific option involved.
Annotate mount, boot, system, or application option names when the context indicates a configurable setting, e.g. 'root'.
Annotate menu items, UI choices, or slash-separated selectable labels when they function as a user-selectable option, e.g. 'Merge/Integrate'.
Annotate both single-word and multiword option names, as long as the span denotes the option label itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general technical vocabulary, object names, or component names unless they specifically function as an option name in context.
Do not annotate values, arguments, parameters, or settings supplied to an option; only the option label itself should be marked.
Do not annotate generic uses of words that happen to match option names when they are not being used as selectable/configurable options.
Do not annotate full error messages, instructions, or descriptive phrases when only one token within them is the option.
Do not annotate scientific, medical, patent, or other non-software terminology that is not presented as an option or menu choice.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the option name itself, not surrounding trigger words such as 'option', 'specified', 'argument', 'mount', 'system', or verbs describing its use.
When an option is expressed as a single token, select just that token, e.g. 'attach' or 'root'.
When an option name is a multiword or slash-linked menu/command choice that functions as one selectable option, include the full expression, e.g. 'Merge/Integrate'.
Do not include leading punctuation, bullets, asterisks, or trailing punctuation such as colons, commas, or periods.
If an option appears inside a longer noun phrase, include only the substring that names the configurable/selectable option, not the whole phrase.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate ordinary nouns, scientific terms, or domain concepts that are not selectable/configurable options; for example, biomedical uses of words like 'lipase' or 'insulin' are not options.

Do not confuse an option with the software system, device, file system, protocol, or resource it modifies or applies to. In 'NFS as root mount storage', annotate 'root' only if it names the mount option, not 'NFS' or 'storage'.
Do not annotate error messages or command descriptions as a whole; only the specific option name inside them is an option, e.g. 'attach' in 'option attach specified with no argument'.
Menu actions or UI choices should be annotated when they are presented as selectable options, even if they look like verbs or command labels, e.g. 'Merge/Integrate'.
If a term names a general action or process in prose rather than a specific selectable/configurable option, do not annotate it.

ordinal.definition: An ordinal is a word or numeric form that explicitly denotes ordered position, rank, or sequence of an entity within a set, such as first, seventh, 11th, or 35th.
include_rules: Annotate words that explicitly mark rank or ordered position in a sequence, competition, list, history, or chronology.
Annotate both spelled-out ordinals and numeric ordinals with suffixes, such as "first", "seventh", "eighth", "11th", and "35th".
Annotate ordinals used attributively before a noun, as in "first car" or "35th head coach".
Annotate ordinals used predicatively or adverbially to indicate placement, as in "dropped from seventh to eighth".
Annotate ordinals that indicate an entity's position within an ordered historical or institutional succession, such as "11th since the ACC was formed".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate cardinal numbers that express amount, total, age, year, date, time, score, measurement, or other non-ordered numeric information.
Do not annotate numbers appearing in code, commands, imports, class names, encodings, or other technical strings when they are not natural-language ordinals.
Do not annotate nouns or noun phrases that imply order without an explicit ordinal form, such as role names by themselves.
Do not annotate generic sequence words unless they function as true ordinals marking rank or ordered position in context.
Do not annotate surrounding entities or descriptions that the ordinal modifies, such as "car" in "first car" or "head coach" in "35th head coach".
boundary_rules: Annotate only the ordinal expression itself as the span: e.g., "first", "seventh", "eighth", "35th", "11th".
Do not include surrounding function words, nouns, or complements such as articles or head nouns: annotate "first" in "the first car", not "the first car".
For rank expressions in predicates or prepositional phrases, include just the ordinal token: annotate "seventh" and "eighth" in "from seventh to eighth", not the surrounding words.
If the ordinal is written as a single numeric token with an ordinal suffix, include the full token including the suffix, e.g., "35th", "11th".
Do not extend the span to include punctuation or adjacent modifiers unless they are part of the ordinal token itself.
