confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse ordinals with cardinal numbers. Numbers expressing quantity, years, ages, dates, counts, or identifiers are not ordinal unless they explicitly mark rank or ordered position.
Do not annotate date components or birth years such as "October 13, 1995", even though they contain numbers; these indicate time, not ordinal rank.
Do not annotate bare numbers in technical text, code, or version-like strings unless they function semantically as ordinals in natural language.
Distinguish ordinals from titles or role nouns: in "35th head coach", annotate "35th" only; "head coach" is not an ordinal.
Distinguish ordinals from sequence-related adjectives that do not explicitly mark ordered position. Only expressions that directly encode order or rank belong to this type.

organ.definition: A body organ or organ tissue of an organism, including named internal organs, sensory organs, and specific functional organ tissues when mentioned as anatomical structures.
include_rules: Annotate names of specific body organs, e.g., "liver", "lung", and "eye".
Annotate named organ tissues when they function as anatomical structures, e.g., "myocardium".
Annotate organ-denoting adjectives when the adjective itself refers to the organ in context, e.g., "Hepatic" for liver-related context.
Annotate organ mentions in biomedical, clinical, or anatomical descriptions regardless of whether they appear in normal, diseased, or experimental context.
Annotate each explicit organ mention independently when multiple organs are mentioned in the same sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-biological uses of organ-related strings or any text unrelated to anatomy.
Do not annotate diseases, lesions, symptoms, or injuries, even if they contain an organ adjective or occur near an organ mention.
Do not annotate physiological processes, functions, or experimental conditions such as "metabolism", "toxicity", or "perfused".
Do not annotate generic body references that do not name a specific organ.
Do not annotate organizations, websites, titles, films, books, places, or other proper names that are not anatomical structures.
Do not annotate modifiers alone if they only describe a pathology or another non-organ concept and do not themselves denote the organ.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the organ or organ tissue itself.
Include adjectival organ terms when they are used alone to denote the organ, e.g., "Hepatic".
Include the full organ noun phrase only up to the organ head noun, e.g., annotate "human myocardium" only as "myocardium" unless the modifier is part of the fixed anatomical name.
Exclude surrounding disease, injury, imaging, procedural, and experimental context words such as "failing", "isolated perfused", "toxicity", or "lesions".
For coordinated mentions, annotate each organ separately if each is explicitly named, e.g., "liver" and "lung".
Do not extend the span to prepositions, determiners, or neighboring non-anatomical words.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate organs and organ tissues, not diseases or pathological findings affecting them; for example, in "cerebellar lesions", annotate neither the pathology term nor the adjective because the organ itself is not named as a standalone span.

Distinguish organ from broader organism or person references: body-part or internal-structure terms such as "eye", "liver", and "myocardium" are organ, while references to humans, patients, or animals are not.
Distinguish organ from cells, molecules, or physiological processes: terms like "metabolism" or "toxicity" are not organs even when they appear near organ mentions.

Distinguish organ from anatomical location adjectives that merely modify another non-organ noun unless the adjective itself stands for the organ, as in "Hepatic metabolism" where "Hepatic" denotes the liver.
Distinguish organ from medical tests, images, or contrast terms; in phrases about imaging or measurement, annotate only the anatomical structure if explicitly named.

organism.definition: Names or designations of living organisms, including scientific names, common names used as organism identifiers, and organism-related source mentions when used to refer to the organism itself.
include_rules: Annotate scientific binomials and other formal organism names when they identify a species or other organism.
Annotate organism names appearing in descriptive biomedical text, including organisms mentioned as sources of biological material.
Annotate common organism names when they function as the organism identifier in context.
Annotate organism-related source phrases used to refer to the organism entity in the text, such as organism-associated protein/source expressions that clearly identify the organism.
Annotate generic organism-related terms only when the text uses them as the target organism mention in context.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general biological terms that are not specific organisms, such as 'genome' when used only as an abstract genetic concept.
Do not annotate proteins, genes, enzymes, or other biomolecules unless the phrase is being used to name the organism/source entity in context.
Do not annotate experimental materials, methods, diseases, or descriptive adjectives that merely relate to an organism.
Do not annotate partial organism names when the full mention continues into additional taxonomic words or identifiers.
Do not annotate text that only describes an organism without naming it.
Do not annotate non-living objects, software, interfaces, code, or other unrelated nouns.
boundary_rules: Include the full taxonomic or scientific name as written, preserving all words that together identify the organism.
If the mention includes a common-name modifier directly attached to an organism name (e.g., 'rabbit transferrin'), include the modifier when it is part of the organism identification in context.
Do not include surrounding prepositions, determiners, punctuation, or descriptive phrases that are not part of the organism name.
For organism group terms used as the referent of a biological sample or material (e.g., 'genome'), annotate the minimal organism-related noun phrase used by the text, even if it is generic.
Do not expand spans into adjacent assay, gene, protein, or experimental words unless they are syntactically part of the organism mention.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish organism from molecule/protein/gene mentions: annotate the living source or biological taxon, not the biomolecule name itself, unless the text uses the organism phrase as the identifying source name (e.g., 'rabbit transferrin').

Distinguish organism from strain/cell-line only labels when they are not explicitly organism names; annotate only if the phrase names a biological organism or organism-derived biological source used as the entity mention.

Distinguish organism from anatomy, tissue, or sample terms: do not annotate parts of organisms or specimen types unless the phrase directly denotes the organism or a biological source term used as its identifier.

Distinguish organism from general scientific concepts such as 'genome'; annotate such terms only when they are used as the organism-related entity in context, not when they refer to abstract genetics concepts.
If a mention contains both an organism and a non-organism modifier, keep only the span that denotes the organism entity and exclude unrelated functional descriptors.

organization.definition: An organization is a named formal collective entity such as a company, institution, government body, registry, broadcaster, media outlet, nonprofit, sports promotion, or other established group that acts as a unit.
include_rules: Annotate named organizations of any sector, including companies, broadcasters, media outlets, sports organizations, government or quasi-government bodies, registries, and institutional programs that function as formal entities.
Annotate both full names and well-known abbreviations or acronyms when they clearly refer to an organization, e.g. "UFC".
Annotate organization names that include punctuation or internet-style formatting when used as the entity's name, e.g. "KHBS/KHOG-TV" and "ESPN.com".
Annotate official titles of institutional bodies or registries when they denote a specific named entity, e.g. "National Register of Historic Places".
Annotate the organization mention even when it appears as part of a larger noun phrase describing members or representatives of that organization, e.g. "UFC" in "UFC officials".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic common nouns for groups or institutions when no specific name is given, such as "officials", "the database", or "the web server".

Do not annotate software products, services, technologies, functions, APIs, or platforms merely because they contain brand or company names, e.g. "Azure SQL" or "Microsoft SQL Server 2008" in technical discussion.
Do not annotate document titles, licenses, notes, blog posts, or standards unless the title itself is the name of an organization.

Do not annotate code artifacts such as package names, namespaces, import paths, class names, method names, or comments in source code unless they explicitly mention an organization in normal naming use.
Do not annotate person names, job titles, or authors associated with organizations unless the span itself names the organization.
Do not annotate partial name fragments when the full organization name is present and identifiable in the same mention.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full official name of the organization as it appears in context, including internal punctuation, slashes, hyphens, abbreviations, and suffixes that are part of the name, e.g. "KHBS/KHOG-TV" and "ESPN.com".
Include leading modifiers only when they are part of the established organization name, e.g. "National Register of Historic Places" as a whole.
Do not include surrounding verbs, prepositions, dates, descriptive appositives, or role nouns that are not part of the name, e.g. in "with KHBS/KHOG-TV in 2013", annotate only "KHBS/KHOG-TV".
For acronym-plus-common-noun mentions, annotate only the acronym or named portion if the common noun is not part of the formal name, e.g. annotate "UFC" in "UFC officials", not "UFC officials".
Do not extend the span to include URLs, package paths, code syntax, copyright boilerplate, or license text unless the exact span is being used as the organization name in normal prose.
If a domain-style string functions as the organization's name in context, include the full string, e.g. "ESPN.com".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse organizations with products, software platforms, programming libraries, functions, or technical systems. In the negative examples, "Azure SQL", "Microsoft SQL Server 2008", and "EnvelopeAngle" refer to software or functions, not organizations.
Do not annotate person names as organizations, even when they appear near organization mentions, e.g. "Isaac Kunen" is a person, not an organization.
Do not annotate legal or licensing document titles as organizations unless they denote the institution itself; e.g. "Apache License, Version 2.0" is a license, not an organization.

Do not annotate code namespaces, package names, imports, or class paths as organizations, even if they contain company-like strings, e.g. "com.couchbase.client.java.query.dsl.functions" and "org.junit.Assert" are code references, not organization mentions.

Distinguish organizations from facilities, places, or geographic entities: annotate the governing body, registry, company, broadcaster, sports promotion, or media outlet, not a physical location or region.

Titles of registries, networks, broadcasters, media outlets, and institutional bodies count as organizations when they name a formal collective entity, as in "National Register of Historic Places" and "KHBS/KHOG-TV".

outcome.definition: A word or phrase that names a result, endpoint, response, event, or survival status used to describe what happened or what was observed, especially as the effect or consequence of a condition, exposure, or intervention.
include_rules: Annotate terms that explicitly name an observed or assessed result, endpoint, or consequence.
Annotate clinical or evaluative response categories such as "partial response".
Annotate harmful or beneficial event outcomes such as "adverse events".
Annotate survival-related outcomes when the outcome term itself is stated, such as "survival".
Annotate outcome expressions whether they appear alone or within a larger sentence describing associations, definitions, or reported rates, as long as the span itself names the outcome.
Annotate plural or singular forms when they denote the outcome concept, e.g. "event" or "events" if used as the actual named outcome.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic narrative words like "result", "effect", or "outcome" when they are used abstractly and do not name a specific observed endpoint.
Do not annotate descriptive findings, symptoms, lesions, or patient states unless the phrase itself functions as the named outcome category.
Do not annotate causes, risk factors, treatments, procedures, or exposures that may lead to an outcome.
Do not annotate percentages, rates, durations, or other numeric summaries by themselves.
Do not annotate broad event context that is not an observed consequence, such as project activities, warfare topics, software behavior, or administrative actions from the negative examples.
Do not annotate explanatory or definitional text attached to an outcome if it describes criteria rather than the outcome name itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the outcome, such as "partial response", "adverse events", or "survival".
Include modifiers that are part of the established outcome name or category, such as degree/type words in "partial response".
Exclude surrounding verbs, copulas, and definitional material, e.g. annotate "partial response" not "was defined by a persistent but non-enhancing similar size lesion".
Exclude numeric values, percentages, and count expressions that quantify the outcome unless they are part of the outcome term itself; e.g. annotate "survival" not "68%" or "74%".
When multiple outcomes are coordinated, annotate each outcome term separately if they are distinct spans; do not force one span to include unrelated coordinated material.
Do not extend the span to include affected populations, interventions, time periods, or causal phrases; annotate only the outcome expression itself.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate diseases, lesions, injuries, or other clinical conditions merely because they are mentioned near an outcome; annotate the result term (e.g. "partial response") rather than the underlying finding used to define it.
Do not annotate interventions, exposures, or causal factors as outcomes; for example, "inadequate staffing levels" is not an outcome, while "adverse events" is.
Do not annotate organizations, projects, software names, weapons, or other concrete entities even if text describes what happened to them; the outcome type is limited to result/end-state concepts.
Distinguish outcome from measurement values: the named result category is the outcome, while percentages or statistics reporting its magnitude are not.

Distinguish outcome from process or action terms: words naming treatment, research activity, warfare, surveillance, or support are not outcomes unless they explicitly denote a resulting state or endpoint.

package.definition: A package is the name of a software package or package-like namespace identifier referenced in text or code, including standalone package names and multiword package names used as a single package unit.
include_rules: Annotate standalone software package names or namespace names mentioned in prose or code-like text.
Annotate the package portion of dotted or qualified identifiers when the package is referenced together with a class or member, e.g. "user" in "user.Info".
Annotate package names that modify software artifacts or configuration elements, e.g. "react" in "react specific properties".
Annotate multiword package names when the complete phrase functions as the package name, e.g. "top package".
Annotate lowercase, code-style, or namespace-like tokens when context indicates they denote a package rather than a person, project, or generic word.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic technical words that are not package names, even if they appear near code or software discussion.
Do not annotate classes, interfaces, methods, fields, properties, usernames, repositories, projects, organizations, or websites unless the mention specifically denotes a package.
Do not annotate biomedical, chemical, or other scientific terms that only resemble identifiers or contain punctuation.
Do not annotate generic noun uses of "package" or packaging terminology when they refer to physical packaging or manufacturing rather than a software package name.
Do not annotate full code expressions when only one subpart is the package; exclude the non-package segments.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the package itself, excluding surrounding syntax and context words.
In code-like mentions with dotted members or methods, include only the package segment and exclude the following class/member part, e.g. annotate "user" in "user.Info".
Include modifiers only when they are part of the package name used in context, e.g. annotate the full phrase "top package".
Do not include trailing generic nouns such as "interface", "properties", "project", or "account" unless they are part of the package name.
Do not include punctuation such as dots, parentheses, quotes, or possessive markers unless they are internal to the package name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a package with a class, interface, method, property, module member, or variable; when a dotted expression appears, annotate the package portion only if it denotes the package namespace.

Do not confuse a package with a software product, platform, website, repository host, or service name such as GitHub or TeamCity when the text refers to the service/tool rather than a package namespace.

Do not confuse a package with generic uses of the common noun "package" in physical, biological, shipping, or manufacturing contexts unless the phrase explicitly denotes a named package entity in the target sense.
Do not annotate user/account names, VCS usernames, email identifiers, or project owners as packages unless the context clearly identifies them as package names.
If a term could be either a package or a generic technology/topic word, annotate it only when it functions as the name of a package being referenced, imported, defined, or modified.

parameter.definition: A named variable, argument, setting, or measurable attribute used to define, control, compare, or describe a system, function, experiment, or analysis.
include_rules: Annotate terms that denote a configurable input, argument, option, or setting in technical or programming contexts.
Annotate terms that denote a measured or analyzed variable, characteristic, or attribute in scientific or medical contexts.
Include both single-word and multiword parameter names, such as "burnin", "function arguments", and "DNA ploidy".
Annotate category-style parameter phrases when the phrase itself is used as the variable under comparison or analysis, such as "histologic parameters".
Annotate attributes that function as dimensions of analysis or comparison, even if they are not explicitly called "parameters" in the sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate entire code blocks, interface names, method names, package names, comments, or legal/license text.
Do not annotate general descriptive nouns that are not specific variables or attributes, such as "data", "ability", "details", or "appearance".
Do not annotate techniques, instruments, or modalities such as "magnetic resonance imaging", "X-ray pelvimetry", or "splat operator" when they are methods/tools rather than parameters.
Do not annotate biological structures, patients, diseases, or outcomes unless the phrase explicitly denotes a measured attribute or comparison variable.
Do not annotate standalone numbers, thresholds, or parameter values such as "20" in "burnin of 20".
Do not annotate generic mentions of "parameter" or "parameters" when no specific parameter type is identified.
boundary_rules: Select the minimal noun phrase that names the parameter itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the parameter name or category, such as domain descriptors in "histologic parameters".
Include multiword parameter expressions when the whole phrase denotes the parameter, such as "function arguments".
Do not include associated values, settings, units, or counts unless they are part of the parameter name; in "burnin of 20", annotate only "burnin".
Do not include surrounding verbs, prepositions, or comparison phrases, such as "results of", "compared with", or "using".
When a generic head noun like "parameters" appears with a qualifying modifier that specifies the parameter type, annotate the full phrase; if it appears alone in a vague way, do not annotate it.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate concrete values or measurements as parameter; the configurable or descriptive variable name is the parameter, not its value.

Do not confuse a parameter with a method, procedure, or technique. In technical or scientific text, names of imaging methods, operators, or experimental procedures are not parameters unless the text refers to them specifically as an argument, setting, or measured variable.
Do not confuse a parameter with a general object, anatomy term, or material being studied. For example, tissues, placenta, cervix, or pelvis are entities under study, not parameters.
In programming contexts, annotate the variable role in an interface or call specification, such as "function arguments", not the syntax token or operator itself.
In scientific contexts, annotate measured, compared, or analyzed variables such as "DNA ploidy", not the overall outcome statement or study topic.

part.definition: A physical component, section, structural element, or subsystem that forms part of a larger object, machine, vehicle, building, or other tangible whole.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote physical components of machinery, vehicles, buildings, or other larger tangible objects, e.g., "bearing flange", "engine", "main pump".
Annotate named structural sections of buildings or objects when they function as constituent parts, e.g., "upper level", "side-gable roof", "dormers".
Annotate component references expressed by generic labels plus identifiers when they clearly refer to a specific part, e.g., "Component 36".
Annotate both specialized technical parts and ordinary physical subparts, as long as they are presented as belonging to a larger whole.
Include modifiers that distinguish one part from another when they are part of the referring expression, such as positional, functional, or type modifiers like "main", "upper", or "side-gable".
Annotate a part even when the larger whole is mentioned nearby or in a possessive/of-phrase construction, e.g., "engine" in "engine of the motor vehicle".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate references to complete standalone objects or artifacts when they are not framed as subparts of something larger, e.g., "house", "motor vehicle", "coach", "van".
Do not annotate broad domains, events, processes, or business/administrative content such as conferences, registration details, rates, dates, or contact information.
Do not annotate substances, utilities, or resources such as "natural gas", "hydropower", or "storage" when they are not physical components of a larger object.
Do not annotate generic environment or condition words like "interior" and "exterior" in sales text unless they clearly denote a specific structural part being referred to as a component.
Do not annotate occupant counts, dimensions, engine sizes, or similar specifications by themselves, e.g., "11 passengers", "2.7 litre".
Do not annotate brand names or product makers unless the brand term itself is also the name of a part mention.
Do not annotate mentions that only describe functionality or comfort features without naming a concrete part.
Do not annotate pronouns or anaphoric references to parts unless the actual part name is present.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest complete noun phrase that names the part, including necessary modifiers that identify which part it is, e.g., "bearing flange", "main pump", "side-gable roof", "upper level".
Include attached designators or labels when they are part of how the part is referenced in context, e.g., "Component 36".
Exclude surrounding prepositions, determiners, and verbs unless they are part of the established part name.
Include descriptive adjectives or positional modifiers only when they specify the part itself, e.g., "main pump", "upper level"; do not extend the span to unrelated descriptive context.
For coordinated mentions, annotate each distinct part separately when individually nameable; do not merge across conjunctions unless the full coordinated phrase names one single part.
Do not include larger host-object wording outside the part mention; for example, annotate "engine" rather than "engine of the motor vehicle".
Do not include functional clauses or repair/inspection instructions around the part mention, only the part name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate a mention as part when it denotes a component, section, structural element, or subsystem of a larger physical object, building, machine, or vehicle.
Do not annotate whole objects, devices, or vehicles as part just because they contain parts; for example, "motor vehicle", "house", and "coach" are hosts, not parts.
Do not annotate materials, fuels, or substances unless the text uses them as the name of a component; e.g., "natural gas" is not a part.

Do not annotate capacities, product specifications, or feature descriptions that describe an item but do not name a component, e.g., "11 passengers", "4 door", "manual transmission" in advertisement-style specs unless the expression clearly functions as a named component mention.
Distinguish part from abstract operational concepts or measurements: words about storage, supply, output, demand, rates, or registration are not parts.

If a noun can refer either to a whole artifact or a component, use the local context: annotate it as part only when it is presented as a subcomponent of something larger, as in "engine" being part of a vehicle.

particle.definition: A named subatomic or elementary particle, or a standard particle-radiation term used as a particle label in scientific context, such as electron, proton, alpha, or gamma.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of subatomic or elementary particles, including singular and plural forms, such as "electron", "electrons", "proton", and "protons".
Annotate standard particle/radiation labels when they are used as particle mentions in scientific contexts, such as "alpha" and "gamma" in scattering or decay notation.
Annotate each separate particle mention whenever it refers to a physical particle type rather than a general concept.
Annotate particle mentions appearing in prose, counts, comparisons, or experimental descriptions, as long as the span directly names the particle.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-particle scientific concepts such as resonances, reactions, experiments, or other phenomena unless the span itself is a particle name.
Do not annotate generic physics adjectives or descriptors that merely modify a particle-related concept, such as "dipole" or "neutral".
Do not annotate biological entities, animals, places, organizations, departments, permits, projects, measurements, or other non-physics objects.
Do not annotate generic words like "parts", "number", or "universe" even when they occur in a sentence that mentions particles.
Do not annotate symbols or notation characters by themselves when they are not standalone particle names.
Do not annotate larger phrases when only one token within them is the particle mention.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the particle itself, usually a single word such as "electron", "electrons", "proton", "protons", "alpha", or "gamma".
Include inflectional morphology that is part of the particle mention, such as plural endings in "electrons" and "protons".

Do not include surrounding punctuation, primes, commas, parentheses, or experiment-format notation unless the particle word itself contains the full mention; in "(alpha, alpha' gamma)", annotate only "alpha" and "gamma".

When a particle name appears inside a larger scientific phrase, annotate only the particle token, not the full phrase; for example, in "pygmy dipole resonance" no span is annotated, while in the same sentence "alpha" and "gamma" are annotated separately.
If multiple particle mentions occur adjacently, annotate each particle as its own span rather than merging them into one entity.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate subatomic particles and particle radiation terms as particle, not broader physical phenomena or experimental events; for example, "gamma" is annotated, but "pygmy dipole resonance" is not.
Do not annotate animal or biological uses of the same surface form; terms like "sea turtles" are organisms, not particle.
Do not annotate engineering, environmental, geographic, or administrative terms that may appear in the same document; these are outside the particle type.

Distinguish particle from materials, objects, or bulk matter: only the named fundamental or atomic-scale particle is annotated, not generic substances, land, water, buildings, or similar physical things.

When a term can refer either to a particle or to a broader process/context, annotate only when the local context indicates the particle itself, such as counts of electrons and protons or scattering/radiation notation involving alpha and gamma.

patent.definition: A specific patent reference or patent identifier mentioned in text, including formal patent citation strings and context-specific shorthand names used to denote particular patents.
include_rules: Annotate formal patent citations that uniquely identify a patent, including jurisdiction or country markers plus document numbers and kind codes, e.g. "DE 197 41 476 A1".
Annotate patent references introduced by words such as "Patent" or "Patent No." when they specify a unique patent, e.g. "Japanese Patent No. 4773332".
Annotate shorthand or nickname-style labels that the text explicitly uses to refer to particular patents, e.g. "Stein I" when the surrounding context states these are patents.
Include alphanumeric patent formatting elements that are part of the cited identifier, such as spaces within the number, document suffixes, and numbering markers.
Annotate each individually referenced patent mention wherever it appears, whether in technical, legal, or descriptive context, as long as it denotes a specific patent.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic mentions of patents that do not identify a specific one, such as "a patent", "the patents", or "patents" by themselves.
Do not annotate technical descriptions, inventions, prior art descriptions, or legal discussion surrounding a patent unless a specific patent reference is present.
Do not annotate ordinary numbers, dates, statistics, phone numbers, email addresses, catalog numbers, or sports scores that are not patent identifiers.
Do not annotate person names, company names, locations, or product names unless the text uses them as an explicit patent label.
Do not annotate incomplete fragments of a patent citation when the fragment alone is not the referenced patent name, such as a bare country code or a bare serial number outside patent context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full patent designation as it appears in text, including country or language identifiers, the word Patent/Patents if part of the name, numbering, and document kind codes, e.g. "DE 197 41 476 A1" and "Japanese Patent No. 4773332".
Include attached serial components that are part of the official or cited patent reference, such as "No.", roman numerals, and alphanumeric suffixes like "A1".

For shorthand patent names used as identifiers in context, annotate only the individual cited patent label, e.g. "Stein I"; do not automatically extend one span over coordinated mentions like "Stein I, II and III" unless each full item is explicitly repeated.
Do not include surrounding verbs, determiners, or descriptive context such as "disclosed in", "related art", or "the patents, as has been done by the parties, as".
Do not include punctuation outside the patent reference span unless it is internal to the citation format.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish patent from general numbers, scores, dates, phone numbers, and IDs: annotate only numbers presented as a patent citation or patent nickname, not standalone numerals such as "1973", "76", or contact numbers.
Distinguish patent from legal or technical subject matter terms: words like "related art", "machines", or "articles" are not patent entities unless they are part of the patent's cited name.

Distinguish patent from people or inventor-like names: a personal surname alone is not a patent, but a surname plus a patent-identifying marker can be, as in "Stein I" when the context says it refers to patents.

Distinguish patent from organizations and places: country adjectives or names such as "Japanese" or "DE" are included only when they function inside a patent citation, not when they refer independently to nationality, country, or organization.
Distinguish patent from generic mentions of intellectual property: annotate only specific identifiable patents, not generic phrases like "the patents" or "a patent" without a unique reference.

patient.definition: A mention of one or more human individuals identified as patients, i.e. people receiving medical care, participating in clinical care contexts, or referred to as a patient population.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of patients as individuals or groups, including singular and plural forms such as "patient" and "patients".
Annotate patient mentions modified by medical condition, immune status, body-system label, or similar descriptors, e.g. "immunocompromised patients" and "non-cardiac patients".
Annotate patient population mentions in clinical, hospital, or study contexts when the referent is clearly the people receiving care or being discussed as patients.
Annotate both specific and generic patient groups when the noun phrase explicitly denotes patients, even if no named disease or hospital is given.
Annotate patient mentions whether or not they include descriptive adjectives, counts, or other local modifiers, as long as the core referent is patients.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, treatments, or biological states by themselves when no patient-denoting head noun is present.
Do not annotate generic humans or groups such as "people", "subjects", "participants", or "parents" unless they are explicitly referred to as patients.
Do not annotate non-human organisms, animal models, species names, or biological materials.
Do not annotate cells, tissues, tumors, organs, or other non-person biomedical entities.
Do not annotate broad clinical or research activities such as "clinical trials", "epidemiological studies", or "day hospitals".
Do not annotate phrases where a medical condition appears without an explicit patient term, e.g. annotate neither "cancer" nor "tumor cells" as patient.
boundary_rules: Include the patient-denoting head noun such as "patient" or "patients".

Include premodifiers that describe the patient group when they are part of the same noun phrase, such as condition/status adjectives or compounds: "immunocompromised patients", "non-cardiac patients".

Include numeric quantifiers only when they are integral to the noun phrase span in context is not required; prefer the core patient mention over leading counts. For example, annotate "patients" rather than "2867 patients" unless annotation policy explicitly keeps counts inside entity spans.
Do not extend the span to surrounding verbs, prepositions, or study-design context, e.g. in "clinical trials ... in patients with cancer", do not include "in" or the larger clause.

If a postmodifying phrase is necessary to identify the patient mention and remains part of the noun phrase, it may be included only if it directly modifies the patient head; otherwise keep the span to the patient noun phrase core.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate humans receiving medical care or described as a patient population, not diseases or conditions. In "patients with cancer", the patient mention is of type patient, while "cancer" itself is not.

Do not annotate animals, species, or other organisms unless they are explicitly described as patients in a care context; biological subjects like frogs, tadpoles, or animal species are not patient mentions.

Do not annotate generic biomedical study terms such as "clinical trials", "studies", "cell culture", or "tumor cells"; these are experimental contexts or biological materials, not patient populations.

Distinguish patient from general person-role mentions: only annotate when the text explicitly frames the humans as patients, typically using the word "patient(s)" or a direct equivalent in care context.
When a modifier names a disease, body system, or vulnerability, annotate the whole patient phrase if the head is still "patient(s)"; the modifier does not change the entity type away from patient.

patient group.definition: A mention referring to a group, cohort, or population of human patients in a clinical or biomedical context, typically expressed with a noun phrase headed by "patients" and optionally modified by descriptors such as age, diagnosis, or enrollment status.
include_rules: Annotate explicit noun phrases that denote a set of human patients in a study, trial, or clinical discussion.
Include patient-group mentions whether generic or specifically characterized, such as plain "patients" and modified forms like "consecutive patients" or "pediatric patients".
Annotate patient cohorts described by demographic, temporal, clinical, or recruitment modifiers when those modifiers are part of the noun phrase.
Annotate mentions of patients in any study role when the phrase still denotes the patient population itself, such as eligible, enrolled, assessable, or randomized patients, subject to boundary rules.
Annotate singular or plural expressions only when they clearly refer to a collective patient population rather than an individual case.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-human or non-clinical entities, including proteins, genes, organisms, tissues, cells, or laboratory systems.
Do not annotate generic human groups that are not patients, such as controls, volunteers, subjects, or individuals, when they are not explicitly presented as patients.
Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, treatments, or procedures by themselves, even if they imply a patient population.
Do not annotate purely numeric expressions or arm-size counts without the patient-group noun.
Do not annotate broad article-level or discourse references to study populations when no explicit patient-group noun phrase is present.
Do not annotate patient abbreviations or diagnosis labels alone when they stand in for a group without explicitly naming patients, unless local annotation policy says otherwise.
boundary_rules: Select the noun phrase headed by a human-group term such as "patients" when it denotes a cohort or study population.
Include premodifiers that characterize the patient cohort, such as age, disease, selection, or enrollment descriptors: e.g., "consecutive patients", "pediatric patients".
Do not include preceding numerals or quantifiers when they merely count the group rather than name it: annotate "patients" in "72 patients".

Do not include surrounding study-design or status wording that is not part of the patient-group noun phrase, such as "eligible for randomization" in "72 patients eligible for randomization" unless the full corpus policy explicitly treats postmodifying eligibility clauses as part of the entity.
End the span at the patient-group head noun or its essential premodifiers; exclude following parenthetical arm sizes, comparative phrases, and clause-level context.
If multiple coordinated patient groups are mentioned separately, annotate each distinct patient-group mention separately.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate only groups of human patients, not biological materials, organisms, proteins, genes, cells, or experimental systems; terms from basic science contexts such as "cyanobacterial photosystem I" are never patient groups.

Do not annotate comparison groups that are not patients, such as "elderly controls" or "healthy controls"; these are human groups but not patient groups because they are controls rather than patients.
Disease names or conditions alone are not patient groups; annotate only when the phrase explicitly refers to patients, as in "pediatric patients" rather than the disease term by itself.
Individual participants are not a separate target here unless expressed as a patient-group mention; the type is for collective references like cohorts, populations, or study arms of patients.

Do not annotate subgroup labels that omit the patient head noun if they function primarily as diagnostic abbreviations or subject labels, such as "MCI" or "MCI subjects", unless project policy explicitly expands patient group beyond patient-headed mentions.

pattern.definition: An abstract arrangement, regular form, recurring structure, or formal matching expression explicitly referred to as a pattern, including technical pattern terms such as regular expressions.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of patterns, arrangements, or recurring structures when the text refers to them as a recognizable form or schema.
Annotate noun phrases headed by "pattern" or "patterns" when they denote a specific type of arrangement or regularity, including domain-specific varieties like "pharmacological patterns".
Annotate descriptive arrangement expressions that specify the form of the pattern, such as "zig zag pattern".
Annotate standalone technical terms that denote a formal matching pattern or pattern language, such as "regex".
Include both everyday and specialized scientific/technical pattern mentions, as long as the expression denotes the pattern itself rather than an example or operation on it.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate documents, citations, case names, dates, or other legal/reference formatting that may look formulaic but do not denote a pattern.
Do not annotate raw example strings, punctuation marks, delimiters, function names, or code operations unless the text explicitly names them as a pattern concept.
Do not annotate generic process descriptions or instructions about transforming text or data, such as removing text after parentheses.
Do not annotate objects that participate in or display a pattern when the pattern itself is not named, such as "blocks" alone.
Do not annotate related scientific findings, classifications, or traits that are not the pattern mention itself, such as "phenotypes" in the example.
Do not annotate overly generic uses of words like "results", "data", or "arranged" unless they are part of an explicit pattern expression.
boundary_rules: Include the lexical item(s) that name the pattern itself, whether a noun phrase or a compact technical term: e.g., "zig zag pattern", "regex", "pharmacological patterns".

Include descriptive modifiers that are part of the named pattern phrase when they specify the kind of pattern, such as "zig zag" in "zig zag pattern" and "pharmacological" in "pharmacological patterns".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding verbs, determiners, or reporting context, such as "opted for the", "some", or "were identified including".
When the head noun is present, include it if it is part of the pattern mention, as in "zig zag pattern" and "pharmacological patterns".
When a standalone technical term directly denotes a pattern formalism or matching pattern, annotate only that term, as in "regex".
Exclude trailing explanatory material that describes outcomes, instances, or related concepts rather than the pattern itself, such as "including two unusual phenotypes".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate strings that are merely character sequences, punctuation examples, or code snippets unless they are referred to as a pattern concept; for example, in SQL examples with parentheses and functions, no pattern entity is present.
Distinguish pattern from ordinary arrangement objects or physical items: annotate the abstract arrangement schema ("zig zag pattern"), not the blocks or objects arranged according to it.
Distinguish pattern from scientific observations or categories that are not themselves pattern mentions; annotate "pharmacological patterns" but not related result labels such as "phenotypes".
Distinguish pattern from legal citations, case names, dates, and document metadata; these may contain repetitive forms or formatting but do not denote patterns.

Distinguish pattern from general actions on text or data. Instructions like removing text after parentheses describe an operation, not a pattern entity, unless a specific pattern term is explicitly named.

people.definition: Mentions of human beings, whether individual persons or groups of people, including demonyms/ethnic groups and collective nouns that refer to human members.
include_rules: Annotate nouns and noun phrases that refer to human individuals or groups.
Annotate collective human nouns such as occupational, social, or situational groups when they denote people, e.g. "students", "crew".
Annotate demonyms, ethnicities, and nationality terms when used as nouns to mean people, e.g. "Americans" in "African-Americans".
Annotate plural or mass-like group references when the referent is clearly a set of humans rather than an institution or abstract category.
Annotate both specific and generic human group mentions if the text explicitly denotes people.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate organizations, institutions, facilities, schools, districts, companies, or brands, even when they are made up of people.
Do not annotate events, courses, classes, programs, studies, or other non-human activities/offers.
Do not annotate abstract concepts, data sources, documents, issues, methods, or fields such as "health care costs" or "Issue Brief".
Do not annotate adjectives or modifiers that imply relation to people when they are not themselves used as noun phrases referring to humans.
Do not annotate generic pronouns, determiners, or vague references unless the dataset explicitly treats them as named entities; this type is for explicit human-denoting mentions.
Do not annotate body counts, quantities, or surrounding descriptive text apart from the people-denoting noun itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the people or human group, usually the noun head: "Americans", "students", "crew".
Include modifiers that are part of the people-denoting expression when they are necessary to identify the group, such as attached demonyms or descriptors: "African-Americans" as one span.
Do not include surrounding quantifiers, numerals, determiners, or measure words unless they are fused into the people term itself: annotate "crew" not "All the 4 crew".
Do not include accompanying prepositional or relative phrases that only describe the group rather than form the core people mention.

For coordinated mentions, annotate each people-denoting span according to the text boundaries if they refer to distinct mentions; if a single coordinated phrase forms one continuous people expression, include only the people words, not extra role descriptions unless needed.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate people when the referent is a human individual or group, including nationalities/ethnic groups used as nouns and collective human nouns such as "students" or "crew".

Do not annotate organizations, businesses, schools, teams as institutions, or programs even if their names contain person-related words; only annotate when the text refers to human members rather than the institution itself.
Do not annotate activity groups or class labels when they denote events, programs, or offerings rather than the participants, e.g. "CrossFit Classes" in a schedule/advertising context.

Do not annotate abstract audiences or generic role slots unless the expression itself denotes actual people in context; prefer clear human-denoting nouns over vague references to information users or stakeholders.
Differentiate people from locations or political units: in demonyms such as "Americans", annotate the human group, not the country or place associated with it.

percentage.definition: A numeric expression that explicitly states a proportion or rate in percent units, using a number together with a percent marker such as "percent", "per cent", or "%" when intended as a percentage expression.
include_rules: Annotate expressions that explicitly give a percentage as a number plus a percent marker.
Annotate percentage expressions referring to shares, rates, proportions, ownership stakes, or substantiated portions when written in natural language.
Annotate both integer and decimal percentage expressions.
Annotate spelling variants of the percent marker, including "percent" and "per cent".
Annotate the percentage expression regardless of whether it is preceded by modifiers like "about" or "more than", but include only the core percent expression itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate standalone numbers that lack an explicit percent marker.
Do not annotate numbers expressing age, calendar years, counts of people or items, addresses, code constants, hexadecimal values, or money amounts.
Do not annotate scientific measurements, ranges, inequalities, or formula-like numeric expressions that are not written as percentages.
Do not annotate comparative or quantifier words by themselves, such as "more than", "less than", "about", or "around"; only annotate the percentage if present.
Do not annotate surrounding context such as "of them", "of individuals", or clause-level descriptions attached to the percentage.
Do not annotate examples from code, identifiers, or technical constants that merely contain numbers and symbols without percentage meaning.
boundary_rules: Include the numeric value together with the explicit percent marker as one span, e.g. "44 percent", "50 per cent", "89.5 percent".
Include decimal numbers when they are part of a percentage expression.
Include orthographic variants of the percent marker such as "percent", "per cent", and "%" when attached to a number.
Exclude approximators, hedges, and comparison words outside the percentage expression, such as "about", "around", "more than", or "less than".
Exclude any following noun or measured population from the span; stop at the percent marker, e.g. annotate "44 percent" not "44 percent of them".
Exclude surrounding punctuation unless it is part of the tokenization of the percentage symbol itself.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate plain counts, totals, ages, years, money amounts, or other numeric expressions unless they explicitly denote a percentage.
Do not annotate ratios, fractions, ranges, inequalities, or measurement units such as "0.65 cm(2)/m(2)", even when they describe proportional concepts, unless expressed as a percent.
Do not annotate parenthetical percentages used only as supplementary numeric notation in this guideline set; the negative examples indicate that forms like "63%" should be left unannotated here.
Do not extend the span to include the thing being compared or the group being described; only the percentage expression itself is the entity.
If a number appears in statistical or scientific context without an explicit percent marker, treat it as a non-percentage numeric value, not as this entity type.

person.definition: A person is any mention that refers to an individual human being or to a group/class of human beings, including named individuals and common-noun references to people.
include_rules: Annotate proper names that refer to individual people, including first names, last names, or other name parts when they alone identify the person in context.
Annotate surname-only references to people in citation or author contexts when they denote a human individual.
Annotate common nouns that explicitly denote humans, including age-group terms and kinless human classes such as "babies" and "children".
Annotate collective or demographic human-group nouns when they refer to people, such as "patients", "males", and "females".
Annotate singular or plural person-denoting nouns regardless of whether the specific individuals are named, as long as the mention refers to humans.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-human entities such as diseases, symptoms, treatments, procedures, drugs, anatomy, or biological substances.
Do not annotate legal, technical, or publication boilerplate text that contains no human referent.
Do not annotate dates, times, ages, percentages, counts, or other numeric expressions, even when they describe people.
Do not annotate pronouns or implicit references to people unless the mention itself is a lexical person-denoting noun or name.
Do not annotate adjectives or demographic descriptors when they do not stand alone as a noun referring to people.
Do not annotate eponymous or possessive disease names as person mentions when the full expression denotes a disease rather than a human.
Do not annotate animal, object, or abstract class nouns that are not humans.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that uniquely identifies the person mention.
Include only the person-denoting word or name itself, such as a given name, surname, or human-group noun.
Do not include surrounding punctuation, parentheses, quotation marks, or citation year markers; e.g., annotate "Koes" but not "(Koes, 1997)".
Do not include determiners, numerals, ages, or descriptive modifiers unless they are part of the person-denoting expression itself; e.g., annotate "children" rather than "the children".

For coordinated mentions, annotate each person mention separately when they appear as distinct names or nouns joined by conjunctions; e.g., "Michael" and "Donald" separately, and "babies" and "children" separately.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate person only for humans or groups of humans, not for diseases, treatments, body parts, biological materials, or medical procedures; e.g., do not annotate "Hodgkin's disease", "bone marrow", or "transplantation".
Do not annotate organizations, products, laws, licenses, or web addresses even if capitalized; e.g., "Apache License" and the URL are not person mentions.

A possessive form derived from a personal name inside a disease or eponymous term is not a person mention when the phrase refers to a condition or concept rather than the individual person; e.g., "Hodgkin's disease" is not person.

Generic human-denoting nouns can still be person mentions when they refer to people as a class or participant group; e.g., "babies", "children", "patients", "males", and "females" are person mentions if they denote humans in context.
Citation-style author surnames refer to people and should be annotated as person when they identify an individual source author; e.g., "Koes" in a parenthetical citation.

phenomenon.definition: A naturally occurring, observed, or theoretically recognized physical, perceptual, or biological effect, behavior, or process referred to as a phenomenon in scientific text, typically named as a specific event-like or effect-like concept rather than as a procedure, object, or measurement.
include_rules: Annotate specific named or clearly delimited physical phenomena, perceptual effects, or dynamic processes described as observed occurrences or effects.
Include illusion, scattering, vortex, wave, oscillation, resonance, instability, and similar event-like or effect-like terms when they denote a specific phenomenon mention.
Include multiword phenomenon names with their essential modifiers, especially when the modifier distinguishes the phenomenon type, as in "Coriolis illusion" and "dynamical scattering".
Include pluralized phenomenon terms when they refer to a recognized class or instance of phenomena, as in "vortex dipoles".
Include phenomena mentioned in titles, abstracts, or body text when they are the scientific effect or behavior being studied or observed.
Include both naturally occurring and experimentally observed phenomena, provided the span denotes the effect/process itself rather than the method used to observe it.
exclude_rules: Exclude medical or laboratory procedures, surgeries, diagnostic methods, and experimental techniques.
Exclude generic scientific processes or abstract mechanisms when mentioned only as broad background concepts rather than as specific phenomenon names.
Exclude diseases, injuries, symptoms, complications, or clinical outcomes unless the text presents them as a named phenomenon rather than a condition.
Exclude physical objects, materials, organisms, cells, molecules, receptors, and anatomical structures that participate in or host a phenomenon.
Exclude measurements, variables, properties, and readouts such as visual function, image contrast, duration, odds ratios, and confidence intervals.
Exclude broad topical phrases, article titles, or research aims that describe a domain of study without naming a specific phenomenon.
Exclude contexts that merely indicate exposure, response, or immune function without a concrete phenomenon term.
boundary_rules: Mark the smallest contiguous span that names the phenomenon itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the established phenomenon name, such as descriptive adjectives or domain qualifiers: "Coriolis illusion", "dynamical scattering".
Include plural forms when they denote the phenomenon: "vortex dipoles".
Do not extend the span to surrounding event, measurement, or effect context, such as "Changes in visual function during the Coriolis illusion"; annotate only "Coriolis illusion".

Do not include verbs, determiners, or explanatory relative clauses that describe what the phenomenon does or causes, e.g. in "dynamical scattering, which will seriously influence image contrast", annotate only "dynamical scattering".

When a phenomenon appears inside a larger noun phrase, exclude material naming the host system, specimen, or setting unless it is part of the phenomenon name itself, e.g. exclude "in an oblate Bose-Einstein condensate" from "vortex dipoles in an oblate Bose-Einstein condensate".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate procedures, interventions, or techniques as phenomenon. Terms like "laparoscopic surgery", "open surgery", "appendectomy", and "diagnostic laparoscopy" are actions/methods, not naturally occurring or observed phenomena.

Do not annotate biological processes merely because they are processes if they are discussed as general mechanisms or system functions rather than specific observed phenomena in context, e.g. "diversification", "selection", "expansion", "rearrangement", and "somatic modification" in the negative example.

Distinguish phenomenon from effects or outcomes measured in studies: "image contrast", "pain", "wound infections", and "intraabdominal abscesses" are outcomes, conditions, or measurements, not the phenomenon exemplified by "dynamical scattering".
Distinguish phenomenon from physical objects or systems in which it occurs. "Bose-Einstein condensate" is a material/system, while "vortex dipoles" is the phenomenon observed within it.

Distinguish phenomenon from named molecules, receptors, proteins, cells, or anatomical entities. Expressions such as "immunoglobulins", "T-cell antigen receptors", "FREPs", "VCBPs", "VLRs", and "NITRs" are entities or molecular classes, not phenomena.

Distinguish phenomenon from broad scientific topics or fields. Generic headings like "alternative mechanisms of immune receptor diversification" name a subject of discussion, not a specific phenomenon mention unless a concrete phenomenon term is explicitly stated.

physical object.definition: A physical object is a concrete, materially existing thing or physically instantiated entity, including man-made artifacts, components, containers, surfaces, and object-like physical phenomena, that can be treated as occupying the physical world rather than being an abstract concept, event, behavior, or purely symbolic construct.
include_rules: Annotate common nouns and noun phrases that denote concrete physical artifacts or components, such as devices, panels, sensors, appliances, and similar man-made objects.
Annotate everyday physical items and surfaces that are treated as discrete objects in context, such as "page".
Annotate object-like physical phenomena when they are referred to as real physical entities, such as "magnetic field".
Annotate physical settings or built/container-like entities when the text refers to them as material things, not as abstract domains.
Annotate both whole objects and their concrete parts when each is explicitly mentioned, such as "appliance" and its part "control panel".
Annotate singular or plural mentions if they denote actual physical things rather than symbolic names or abstractions.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate abstract concepts, properties, or calculated quantities, such as resistance, current, proportion, frequency, influence, or effect.
Do not annotate actions, processes, states, or behaviors, such as refreshing, administration, pregnancy, parturition, infanticide, or parental behavior.
Do not annotate symbolic labels, variables, formula names, or schematic identifiers, such as "R_3", "R_{36}", or other algebraic references.
Do not annotate organisms, species names, or animal mentions, such as "house mice", "mice", "female", "pup", or "Mus musculus".
Do not annotate chemicals, hormones, drugs, or other biochemical substances when they are referred to as substances rather than object mentions, such as "oxytocin" or "prostaglandin F2 alpha".
Do not annotate generic modifiers or relational words that do not independently denote an object in context.
Do not annotate purely textual, graphical, or schematic placeholders like "This is the circuit:" when no explicit concrete object mention is being targeted from the text itself.
boundary_rules: Select the smallest contiguous noun phrase that names the physical object or object-like thing; do not extend the span to surrounding verbs, prepositions, or explanatory clauses.
Include modifiers that are part of the object name when they help identify the object, as in "control panel" and "magnetic field".
Exclude determiners, quantifiers, and demonstratives unless they are inseparable from the conventional name of the object; annotate "page", not "the page", and "appliance", not "other appliance".
When an object is mentioned through a single common noun, annotate just that noun, as in "page" or "appliance".
If multiple object mentions appear in one phrase, annotate each separately when they refer to distinct objects rather than one larger fixed name, as in "household" and "appliance".
Do not annotate subparts that appear only as symbols, variable names, or mathematical labels, such as resistor identifiers like "R_3" or formula-derived names.
Do not include measurement values, units, or functional descriptions attached to the object unless they are part of the object name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate tangible or physically instantiated things, including devices, components, containers, surfaces, locations in a material sense, and physical phenomena treated as object-like entities; do not annotate abstract properties or processes.
Do not confuse physical objects with behaviors, events, or actions: "infanticide", "parental behavior", "pregnancy", and "parturition" are not physical objects.

Do not confuse physical objects with substances, hormones, chemicals, or biological molecules when the mention refers to a biochemical agent rather than a discrete object; examples like "oxytocin" and "prostaglandin F2 alpha" are excluded here.

Do not confuse physical objects with mathematical, electrical, or analytical abstractions such as "current", "voltage source" in a schematic role, "resistance", or "Thevenin equivalent resistance" when the mention is used as a circuit-analysis concept rather than as a concrete device mention.

Do not confuse physical objects with organisms or taxonomic mentions when the text refers to living beings as species or animals rather than inanimate objects; "house mice" and "Mus musculus" are excluded.
A field or similar physical phenomenon can be annotated when the text presents it as a real physical entity, as in "magnetic field", even though it is not a solid artifact.

physical property.definition: A measurable or observable physical attribute, state variable, or material/environmental characteristic of an object, substance, system, or surrounding conditions, such as pressure, texture, angle, coverage, or depth.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote a concrete physical attribute or state that can be measured, observed, compared, or controlled.
Include environmental and atmospheric properties such as coverage, angle, depth, and pressure when they refer to real-world physical conditions.
Include material or object characteristics such as "texture" that describe a physical quality of a substance or item.
Include property mentions whether they appear as standalone property names or with necessary qualifiers identifying the specific kind of property, e.g. "external pressure".
Annotate each distinct physical property mention separately when multiple properties occur in the same sentence.
Annotate the property even when it is mentioned as increasing, decreasing, determined, controlled, or compared, as long as the span itself names the property.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate physical objects, substances, locations, or components themselves; only annotate their properties.
Do not annotate processes, events, actions, or changes in a property, such as heating, processing, declining, or dropped.
Do not annotate generic technical terms, system parameters, or engineering concepts that are not clearly physical attributes in the material/environmental sense shown by the examples.
Do not annotate vague collective expressions like "environmental conditions" when no specific physical property is named.
Do not annotate device settings, configurations, controller choices, manufacturer information, or equipment types.
Do not annotate full clauses containing a property plus comparison or status language, such as "close to ambient external pressure"; only the property phrase is in scope.
Do not annotate abbreviations or signal labels from wireless communication text when they refer to transmission concepts rather than physical properties of matter or environment.
boundary_rules: Select the minimal noun phrase that names the property itself, including modifiers needed to specify which property is meant, as in "cloud cover", "solar zenith angle", "ozone column depth", and "external pressure".
Include adjectival or relational modifiers when they are part of the conventional property name or distinguish the measured property, e.g. include "external" in "external pressure".
Do not extend the span to surrounding verbs, measurement statements, comparison phrases, or affected objects; annotate "texture" alone in "The texture of ... carrot disks was determined."
Do not include parenthetical abbreviations, units, numeric values, or symbols unless they are part of the property name itself.
When a coordinated list contains multiple distinct properties, annotate each property separately, e.g. "cloud cover", "solar zenith angle", and "ozone column depth".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the attribute being measured or varied, not the physical object or material that has the attribute. For example, annotate "texture" rather than the object "carrot disks".

Do not annotate devices, systems, controllers, equipment, or wiring/configuration terms even if they influence a physical condition; these are artifacts or setup descriptions, not physical properties.

Do not annotate communication or signal-engineering descriptors such as "radio frequency signals", "modulation schemes", "amplitude", "phase", "bandwidth efficiency", or "data rate" in this type, because in the provided evidence this entity type is used for material/environmental state properties rather than signal or system design terminology.
Distinguish a physical property from a process or action: annotate "pressure" in a phrase like "external pressure", but not the event phrase "has dropped" or the control action "used to control".

Distinguish a physical property from a generic condition-setting phrase. Broad expressions like "environmental conditions" or "comfort level" are too unspecific unless a concrete property name is stated.

physical quantity.definition: A measurable physical, chemical, or biophysical property, amount, magnitude, or relation of a material system, such as a ratio, dose, gradient, or radioactivity.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote measurable physical, chemical, or biophysical properties or magnitudes.
Include quantity expressions describing relations between physical entities, such as ratios and gradients.
Include names of measurable signal or activity properties, such as "radioactivity".
Include dosage or dose terms when they denote an administered physical amount, as in "doses".
Include quantity terms whether singular or plural, and whether they appear alone or inside a larger noun phrase.
Include domain-specific quantity/property terms even when no explicit numeric value or unit is present.
exclude_rules: Exclude entities that are material substances, body parts, organisms, diseases, chemicals, devices, or locations rather than the quantity/property itself.
Exclude pure numbers, units, frequencies, dates, time spans, and counts when they appear without a physical quantity noun.
Exclude event or trend expressions such as "increase", "decrease", "growth", or "expansion" unless the quantity term itself is present and annotated separately.

Exclude broad epidemiological or demographic counts such as "numbers of cases", "390 million dengue infections per year", or "1 million cases"; these are not physical quantity mentions of the target type.
Exclude generic nouns like "level", "amount", or "number" when they are too unspecific or refer to abstract/statistical counts rather than a concrete physical quantity term.
Exclude non-domain uses of terms from programming or metadata text, such as "entities" referring to classes or objects.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the quantity concept itself, including essential head nouns and attached qualifiers that specify the quantity, as in "ATP/ADP ratio".
Include ratio, dose, gradient, concentration, pressure, volume, mass, intensity, radioactivity, and similar measurement/property terms when they denote a physical measurable quantity.
Do not include surrounding determiners, verbs, or event words unless they are part of the established quantity name; e.g., annotate "radioactivity" not "three zones of radioactivity".

Do not include the object being measured unless it is syntactically part of the quantity expression; e.g., include "ATP/ADP ratio" but not additional surrounding context like "mitochondrial" unless it is required as part of the named quantity expression in context.
For plural quantity nouns, annotate the plural form when that is the expression used, as in "doses".

When a quantity is modified by numeric values, units, or comparative/change language, annotate only the quantity term, not the number/unit/change phrase, unless the full phrase is the only way the quantity is expressed.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate substances, molecules, organisms, diseases, or objects being discussed; annotate only the measurable property or amount associated with them. For example, in "ATP/ADP ratio", the entity is the ratio, not ATP or ADP individually.
Do not annotate counts of cases, years, countries, infections, or travelers when they refer to population/event tallies rather than a physical quantity term of the target type.

Distinguish physical quantities from locations or regions: "zones" and "fraction" are not physical quantity entities in "three zones of radioactivity in the gels of the soluble fraction"; only "radioactivity" is.
Distinguish physical quantities from processes or changes: in phrases like "increase in the proton gradient", annotate "proton gradient" as the quantity, not "increase" as an event/change word.
Do not annotate generic software/game/programming uses of the word "entities" or class/object names; these are non-physical and outside this type.
Do not annotate generic abstract properties unless they denote a measurable physical/biophysical amount or relation in context.

place.definition: A place is a named or referential location: a geographic area, built location, site, or interior space that denotes where something is situated, happens, or can be found.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of geographic or built locations, such as regions, lands, centers, buildings, rooms, and other identifiable sites.
Annotate common-noun location mentions when they clearly denote a concrete place where something is located or occurs, such as "office".
Annotate named commercial, civic, or community sites used as destinations or locations, such as "Easton Town Center".
Annotate historically, culturally, or religiously named locations when they function as place names, such as "Land of Goshen".
Annotate both real and legendary/scriptural places if the text refers to them as locations.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate people, bands, teams, companies, or other organizations, even if their names contain place words or are derived from locations.
Do not annotate generic spatial words used non-locationally or as metaphors.
Do not annotate adjectives or relational forms of places when they describe something else rather than naming the location itself.
Do not annotate routes, travel status, or movement expressions such as "on the road" when they do not identify a specific place.
Do not annotate diseases, works, products, records, or other non-location entities appearing near place-like words.
Do not annotate a broader textual mention like "a place mentioned in the Hebrew Bible" when it is only a description rather than the actual place name.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal contiguous span that names the place, including internal modifiers that are part of the conventional name, e.g. "Easton Town Center" and "Land of Goshen".
Include the full multiword place name when all words are needed to identify the location; do not split off components such as "Town Center" or "Goshen" alone when the full phrase is used as the name.

For common-noun locations, annotate only the location noun phrase itself, e.g. "office"; exclude surrounding possessives, determiners, and prepositional context unless they are part of the place name.
Exclude descriptive material outside the place name, such as appositives or explanations, e.g. in "the Land of Goshen, a place mentioned in the Hebrew Bible," annotate only "Land of Goshen".
Do not extend the span to include organization ownership or affiliation markers when they are not part of a place mention.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate places, not people, groups, or creative works. For example, band names, athlete/team names, and person names are not place entities even if they contain words associated with locations.

Distinguish a place from an organization: annotate the span as place when it denotes a physical or geographic location that one can be in, go to, or locate something in, such as "Easton Town Center" or "office".
Do not annotate demonyms, residents, or community members; only the location itself is a place.
Do not annotate events, games, tours, or time expressions even when they occur at or are associated with a location.
When a word can refer either to a place or to an organization/team, annotate it only if the sentence uses it as a location rather than as an acting social entity.

planet.definition: A named planet: the proper name of a celestial body classified as a planet, including Earth when referred to as the world/astronomical body.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of planets when they refer to the astronomical body.
Annotate "Earth" when it refers to the planet, including in scientific, cosmological, or general world-reference contexts.
Annotate planet names even when modified by articles or descriptive words, as long as the referent is still the planet itself, e.g., "the Earth", "earliest Earth".
Annotate a planet name when it appears inside a larger noun phrase but still directly names the planet, e.g., "Earth" in "Earth disk".
Annotate repeated mentions of a planet name each time they occur if each mention refers to the planetary body.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic nouns or descriptions that are not proper planetary names, such as "planet", "world", or "disk" by themselves.
Do not annotate metaphorical, symbolic, fictional, or non-astronomical uses of a planet name unless the context clearly refers to the celestial body.
Do not annotate names of continents, countries, oceans, or other terrestrial places unless they are explicitly the planet name itself.
Do not annotate scientific, medical, or biological terms that merely co-occur in the sentence and are unrelated to planets.
Do not annotate parts of larger descriptive phrases beyond the planetary name, e.g., exclude "disk" in "Earth disk" and exclude "earliest" in "earliest Earth".
boundary_rules: Annotate only the planetary name itself as the span, e.g., "Earth".
Do not include surrounding prepositions, articles, or punctuation, e.g., annotate "Earth" in "On Earth" and "the Earth disk".

If a planet name is immediately followed by a generic noun or descriptive term, include only the name unless the full expression is an established single planetary proper name; in the example "Earth disk", annotate only "Earth".
Do not expand the span to include adjectives or temporal modifiers that describe the planet rather than name it, e.g., annotate only "Earth" in "the earliest Earth".
Use the shortest span that uniquely identifies the planet.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate planets as astronomical world bodies; do not confuse them with geographic regions, landforms, or locations on a planet.

Do not annotate common nouns for anatomical, biological, or dental entities even when they resemble other domain terms in the sentence, e.g., "primary teeth", "molars", and "Merkel cells" are not planets.
Do not annotate person, fictional character, or object names appearing near a planet mention; in "On Earth, Iron Man and the Punisher...", only "Earth" is the planet.
Do not annotate generic astronomical descriptors such as "disk" when paired with a planet name; only the planet name is the entity.
If a term can refer to both a planet and a non-astronomical concept, annotate it only when the context clearly indicates the celestial body.

plant.definition: A mention of a plant organism or plant taxon, including common names and scientific/proper names of trees, flowers, crops, and other living plants.
include_rules: Annotate common names of plants, including generic types such as "tree" when they refer to a living plant organism.
Annotate proper, scientific, or taxonomic plant names, such as "Petunia".
