Annotate crop and garden plant mentions, including multiword expressions like "tomato plants".
Annotate singular or plural mentions of living plants when the text refers to the organism itself rather than a product derived from it.
Annotate plant names in agricultural, horticultural, botanical, or ecological contexts when the span denotes the plant organism.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-plant entities such as people, organizations, government acts, places, software, diseases, or anatomical terms.
Do not annotate generic biological material or plant parts alone, such as "leaf discs", "leaf", "seed", "root", or "pollen", unless the span itself denotes the whole plant taxon.
Do not annotate chemicals, procedures, or experimental methods associated with plants, such as "Agrobacterium-mediated transformation".
Do not annotate ambiguous words when they refer to non-biological meanings, such as industrial "plant" or other metaphorical uses.
Do not annotate broad habitat or vegetation words unless they specifically denote a plant organism mention in context.
Do not annotate acronyms or abbreviations unless they unambiguously name a plant taxon.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the plant, e.g., "tree", "Petunia", "tomato plants".

Include common head nouns that are part of the plant mention, including plural forms and cultivation terms such as "plants" when they refer to the organism itself, e.g., "tomato plants" not just "tomato".
Include genus/species or cultivar wording when present as part of the plant name.
Do not include surrounding non-plant words such as pests, plant parts, procedures, measurements, or prepositions, e.g., in "Petunia leaf discs" annotate only "Petunia".
Do not extend the span to include determiners, adjectives, or context words unless they are part of the conventional plant name.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the plant organism, not a plant part or tissue sample: in "Petunia leaf discs", "Petunia" is plant, while "leaf discs" is not.
Do not annotate humans, organizations, places, laws, programs, or acronyms that may resemble capitalized names; the negative examples contain many proper names but none are plants.
Distinguish plant from nonliving industrial or mechanical senses of "plant"; only biological organisms count.
Distinguish plant from food/product mentions when the word refers to the edible item rather than the organism. Annotate only when the context indicates the living plant, as in "tomato plants".

Distinguish plant from microorganisms or pathogens unless the mention is clearly a plant taxon; context about transformation or infestation does not change the requirement that the span itself denote a plant.

plant part.definition: A physical anatomical structure, tissue, organ, or reproductive product of a plant, including named external or internal parts such as leaves, flowers, inflorescences, seeds, and grain.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote a discrete part of a plant, such as organs, tissues, structures, or reproductive units.
Annotate above-ground and reproductive structures such as "leaves", "flowers", and "inflorescences".
Annotate plant reproductive products and harvested plant parts when referred to as biological plant material, such as "seed", "hybrid seed", and "F2 grain".
Annotate both singular and plural mentions of plant parts.
Annotate modified plant-part mentions when the modifier is necessary to identify the specific plant-part expression, such as generation/class or type descriptors in "F2 grain" and "hybrid seed".
Annotate plant-part mentions regardless of whether they occur in descriptions of morphology, growth, breeding, cultivation, or crop production.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate mentions of whole plants, crops, species, varieties, hybrids, or organisms unless the span specifically names a plant part.
Do not annotate non-plant entities, even if they contain words that resemble plant-part terms or occur near the word "plant" in another sense.
Do not annotate qualities, symptoms, colors, counts, sizes, or conditions by themselves, such as "yellow", "droopy", or "black spots" when not part of the plant-part name.
Do not annotate actions or processes involving plant parts, such as "flowers" when used as a verb meaning blossoms, unless it clearly denotes the structure itself.

Do not annotate generic biological or agricultural context words that are not themselves plant structures, such as "seed" in abstract classification language if no concrete plant-part mention is intended.
Do not annotate age, study, medical, sports, or other unrelated text with no plant anatomy meaning.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the plant structure, tissue, or reproductive product.
Include modifiers that are part of the lexicalized plant-part expression, such as material/state/class descriptors that specify the plant part itself: "hybrid seed", "F2 grain".
Do not include surrounding determiners or quantifiers unless they are part of the fixed term; e.g., annotate "inflorescences" not "large inflorescences" and "flowers" not "65 flowers each".

Do not include attached descriptive adjectives that only describe appearance, size, condition, or color rather than identify the plant part, e.g., annotate "leaves" not "droopy leaves" if phrased that way.
When a coordinated list contains multiple plant parts, annotate each plant-part mention separately if they are separate spans.
Do not extend the span to include the whole plant or event/process words around the part, e.g., annotate "grain" in a noun phrase but not verbs such as "produced" or "grown".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate plant anatomical structures or products, not whole plants or plant taxa; for example, "leaves" is a plant part, but "plant" itself is not.

Annotate reproductive or harvested products when they denote a part/product of a plant, such as "seed" and "grain"; do not confuse these with generic agricultural commodities when no plant-part meaning is expressed.
Do not annotate human or animal body parts even if the same word can occur in other contexts; only plant-organ meaning qualifies.
Do not annotate disease symptoms, damage, or traits unless the plant structure itself is explicitly named; for example, annotate "leaves" but not "yellow" or "black spots" alone.
Do not annotate developmental processes or botanical events such as flowering unless the span names the structure produced, such as "flowers" or "inflorescences".
Do not annotate non-biological uses of plant-part words in unrelated domains or metaphorical uses, such as sports positions like "center fielder" or idiomatic expressions.

platform.definition: A named digital software platform, operating system, or application distribution marketplace that serves as an environment for running, publishing, downloading, or installing software or apps.
include_rules: Annotate named application marketplaces or software distribution stores, such as "App Store" and "Marketplace", when they refer to a specific platform for obtaining software.
Annotate named operating systems or software ecosystems that function as a target platform for apps or software, such as "iOS".
Annotate platform names when they are mentioned as places where software can be installed, downloaded, published, or made available.
Annotate named digital platforms even when mentioned without the owning company, as long as the reference is to the platform itself.
Annotate both single-word and multi-word proper platform names if they uniquely identify a software platform or app distribution environment.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic mentions of platforms or stores that are not specific names, such as "platforms", "app store" used descriptively, or "marketplace" when clearly generic.
Do not annotate ordinary websites, social sites, forums, or communities that are not identified as software/app platforms.
Do not annotate software code artifacts, libraries, headers, namespaces, file paths, macros, or technical identifiers such as those appearing in source code.
Do not annotate company names, developer names, or publishers unless the company name itself is being used as the platform name.
Do not annotate software titles, app names, versions, or devices unless they denote the platform itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the exact platform name only, including all words that are part of the official name: e.g., "App Store", "Marketplace", "iOS".
Do not include surrounding determiners, prepositions, punctuation, or descriptive words unless they are part of the platform name itself.
For multi-word store names, include the full fixed name rather than only the head noun: annotate "App Store", not just "Store".
When a generic platform noun is used without a distinctive name, do not annotate it: e.g., "other platforms" is not an entity, but "iOS" is.
Keep the span to the platform mention as written in text; do not expand to include associated products, versions, companies, or app names unless they are part of the platform name.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate digital distribution channels, operating systems, and software ecosystems used to publish, install, or run apps as platform entities.
Do not confuse a platform with a general website or site name; ordinary sites or communities are excluded unless they function as a named software/app platform.
Do not annotate companies or creators when they are mentioned instead of the platform; for example, the company that owns a store is different from the store platform itself.
Do not annotate apps, packages, or products being distributed through a platform; in "install ... from the Marketplace", annotate "Marketplace" but not the software being installed.
Do not annotate generic technical infrastructure terms, source code identifiers, file names, namespaces, or libraries as platforms unless they clearly name a user-facing software platform or OS.

policy.definition: A policy is a named or clearly described rule, regulatory measure, institutional intervention, funding action, or enforceable governance-related constraint that shapes behavior, administration, or public/social outcomes.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote formal or informal policies, rules, restrictions, funding measures, governmental interventions, or regulatory categories.
Annotate plural or singular references to policies when they refer to governing measures, e.g. 'policies'.
Annotate public-sector or institutional actions when they function as a policy instrument, e.g. 'governmental involvement' and 'funding'.
Annotate categories of prohibited or regulated behavior when expressed as an enforceable rule domain, e.g. 'traffic violations'.
Annotate policy expressions even when they are generic rather than official titled policies, as long as they clearly refer to a governance or regulation-related measure.
Annotate policy mentions whether they describe existing measures, causes of a situation, or elements of a proposed solution, provided the mention itself is a policy concept.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic actions or verbs that are not presented as a policy or institutional measure.
Do not annotate technical, software, architectural, migration, or implementation solutions, even if described as a 'solution' or 'constraints'.
Do not annotate scientific mechanisms, natural processes, or research methods.
Do not annotate entire programs, projects, or organizations unless the specific policy/intervention component is named.
Do not annotate broad contextual nouns like 'solution', 'crisis', 'program', or 'customer' unless they themselves denote a policy measure.
Do not annotate references to possible future actions, proposals, or concerns when no concrete policy or rule is expressed.
Do not annotate specific incidents, outcomes, or consequences instead of the governing rule or measure behind them.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the policy, regulatory measure, rule, or institutional intervention itself.
Include common nouns that denote the policy/action, such as 'policies', 'funding', 'governmental involvement', or 'traffic violations' when used as a rule-enforcement or regulatory concept.

Include necessary modifiers that specify the type of policy or intervention when they are part of the policy name or concept, e.g. 'governmental involvement' rather than only 'involvement', and 'traffic violations' rather than only 'violations'.
Do not include surrounding determiners, auxiliaries, prepositions, or explanatory clauses unless they are part of the policy expression itself.
For coordinated items, annotate each distinct policy expression separately if they refer to separate measures, e.g. 'governmental involvement' and 'funding'.
Do not extend the span to include effects, motivations, or outcomes of the policy, such as 'contributed to the creation of this crisis'.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate organizations, governments, or institutions unless the text refers to their intervention or policy action; annotate 'governmental involvement' but not 'government' by itself.
Do not annotate programs, projects, applications, technical solutions, or redesign plans; these are operational or technical artifacts, not policies.

Do not annotate scientific mechanisms, biochemical reactions, or physical processes such as 'reaction mechanism' or 'racemization reaction'; these are natural or technical processes, not social or institutional rules.
Distinguish policy from general events or activities: annotate only when the phrase denotes a governing measure, funding decision, regulatory constraint, or rule-enforcement concept.

Distinguish policy from violations as individual incidents: annotate phrases like 'traffic violations' only when they refer to the regulated offense category or rule domain, not a specific event of someone violating a rule.
Do not annotate abstract solutions, proposals, or strategies unless they explicitly denote a policy or formal institutional measure.

political group.definition: A political group is a named or clearly identifiable collective of people or institutions defined by political ideology, partisan alignment, governing role, or organized political identity, including ideological constituencies and governing bodies referred to as political actors.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote political constituencies or camps defined by ideology or partisan alignment, such as "conservatives", "Liberals", and "Communists".
Annotate references to governing political bodies when used as political actors, such as "government".
Annotate both formal and informal mentions of political collectives, including generic ideological groups and broad governing entities, even when no official organization name is given.
Annotate plural or singular mentions when they refer to a collective political identity rather than an individual person.
Annotate the mention whenever the text treats the entity as acting, being criticized, supported, opposed, or otherwise participating in political or ideological relations.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-political groups such as medical cohorts, research consortia, scientific groupings, patients, controls, or demographic populations.
Do not annotate diseases, disorders, genes, proteins, biological processes, or other technical terms, even if they occur near words like "group".
Do not annotate abstract political concepts or policy domains when they do not refer to a collective actor, e.g., generic references to education or the environment are not political groups.
Do not annotate individual persons, professions, or media roles unless the span itself denotes a political collective.
Do not annotate adjectives or descriptors that only modify another noun unless the full expression denotes the political group.
Do not annotate incidental uses of words like "liberal", "conservative", or "communist" when they describe style, attitude, or theory rather than a political constituency or actor.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal contiguous span that names the political group, ideology-based constituency, or governing body, such as "conservatives", "Liberals", "Communists", or "government".
Include capitalization only as written in text; both capitalized and lowercase forms can be entities when they denote a political group.
Do not extend the span to include surrounding verbs, determiners, descriptive clauses, or quoted commentary unless they are part of the group name itself.
For plural demonyms or ideology labels used as group nouns, annotate only the noun phrase denoting the group, e.g., "conservatives" not "against conservatives".
When a generic governing body is referenced as the political actor, annotate just the core term, e.g., "government" not "Get government out".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate political constituencies, ideological camps, parties, movements, ruling bodies, or state governing actors as political group when the mention refers to a collective political actor or category.

Do not annotate medical, scientific, biological, or patient groups merely because they are described as groups; examples such as "patients", "controls", or "Autism Sequencing Consortium" are not political groups.

Distinguish political group from ideology words used abstractly: annotate "Liberals" or "Communists" when referring to people/groups, but not abstract references to liberalism, communism, or conservatism as doctrines alone.

Distinguish political group from government as an institution in a purely administrative or abstract sense only when no political actor is denoted; annotate "government" when it refers to the governing political body or actor, as in the positive example.
Do not annotate general populations, social categories, or clinical cohorts unless the text presents them specifically as political actors or political affiliations.

population.definition: A mention of a human subgroup or study/target group defined by shared demographic, clinical, residential, social, or functional characteristics; typically a noun phrase referring to people as a population, residents, patients, or another defined subgroup.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that refer to groups of people defined by a shared characteristic, condition, treatment status, demographic profile, residence, or life stage.
Annotate study or clinical population mentions, especially phrases ending in words like 'population', 'residents', 'patients', or similar human-group nouns.
Annotate population mentions modified by medical or care-related context, such as treatment exposure or healthcare setting, e.g., 'dialysis population'.
Annotate demographic subgroup phrases that specify age, sex, disability, or living arrangement, e.g., 'elderly disabled nursing home residents' and 'female population'.
Annotate subgroup descriptors that denote reproductive or life-stage eligibility when used as a population-like category, as in 'childbearing potential'.
Annotate the phrase only when it denotes people, either explicitly ('residents', 'population') or elliptically through a clearly human subgroup label in context.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-human groups, objects, buildings, districts, institutions, or legal/administrative entities.
Do not annotate generic collective nouns that are not presented as a defined subgroup of people in context.
Do not annotate diseases, treatments, procedures, or outcomes unless the phrase explicitly refers to the people defined by them, e.g., annotate 'dialysis population' but not 'dialysis' alone.
Do not annotate pure numeric expressions, percentages, or counts such as '10-15%' or '1,500 participants' unless the span also includes a substantive human-group expression that defines a population.
Do not annotate locations alone, even when they describe where a population lives or is studied, e.g., 'Denmark' is not a population.
Do not annotate broad references to the public or people in general when no specific subgroup is identified.
Do not annotate role mentions such as 'experts', 'lawyers', or 'judges' when they are merely discourse participants rather than a defined population under discussion.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase that names the human group, including descriptive modifiers that define membership, e.g., 'dialysis population' and 'elderly disabled nursing home residents'.
Include demographic or status modifiers that are part of the population mention, such as sex, age, disability, residence, treatment status, or care setting.

When a broader population term is followed by an eligibility or life-stage qualifier introduced by 'of', include only the population term if the qualifier is separately meaningful as its own population-like subgroup, e.g., annotate 'female population' and separately annotate 'childbearing potential' rather than the entire combined phrase.
Do not include surrounding verbs, prevalence statements, percentages, or disease names unless they are part of the population noun phrase itself.
Do not extend the span to locations when the location merely specifies where the group is found, unless the location is embedded inside the noun phrase as an integral modifier.

If a mention is expressed as a bare abstract attribute rather than an actual group noun phrase, annotate only when it is used elliptically to denote a subgroup, as in 'childbearing potential' in the provided example.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate diseases, conditions, or infections; in 'Hepatitis B virus infection in the dialysis population', only 'dialysis population' is the population, while the disease mention is not.

Do not annotate organizations, facilities, or institutions by themselves. For example, 'nursing home' alone is not a population, but 'nursing home residents' is a population because it refers to people.
Do not annotate geographic places or districts. A location can modify a population mention, but the place name itself is not a population.

Do not annotate event participants, legal attendees, or counts of people unless the text clearly denotes them as a defined human group. In the negative example, 'participants attending the three-day convention' is not treated as a target population mention.

Distinguish population from generic collective or administrative group references. Terms like 'law enforcement', 'attorneys', or 'judges' are not automatically population mentions unless presented as a study/target subgroup noun phrase.

Distinguish population from attributes or categories. Sex, age, disability, or reproductive status terms alone are only annotated when they function as the name of a subgroup or are part of a larger population phrase.

position.definition: A named role, job slot, functional title, lineup role, or game/table seat that identifies the position an individual may hold or occupy within work, sport, or structured activities.
include_rules: Annotate occupational or training role names that denote a job slot or appointment, such as "trainee positions".
Annotate sports lineup or unit-position names and abbreviations that identify a player role, such as "OLs".
Annotate game, table, or turn-order seat names that denote a participant's designated place in play, such as "middle position" and "big blind".
Annotate both full forms and abbreviations when the expression clearly functions as a position name in context.
Annotate singular or plural mentions when they refer to the role/slot category rather than to people as individuals.
Annotate modifiers that are necessary to identify the specific position, including rank, training level, side, or relative seat descriptor.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic nouns that do not denote a specific role or seat in context.
Do not annotate physical positions, locations, orientations, or spatial arrangements when they refer only to where something is located rather than a role or designated slot.
Do not annotate technical, scientific, medical, or industrial terms that may contain words like "position", "stage", "cell", or similar but do not name a human role or structured seat.
Do not annotate actions, duties, recruitment events, or availability statements; only the position name itself is annotated.
Do not annotate persons, teams, organizations, or groups instead of their positions.
Do not annotate broad activity domains or units unless the text explicitly names a position within them.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the position itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the conventional position name, such as level, side, or seat descriptor: "trainee positions", "middle position", "big blind".
Include plural or singular morphology when it is part of the tokenized position term, e.g. "OLs".
Exclude surrounding verbs, determiners, numerals, and quantifiers unless they are inside the position name itself, e.g. annotate "trainee positions" not "Ten trainee positions".
Exclude organization, team, person, or event names that co-occur with the position.
For prepositional phrases introducing a position, annotate only the position phrase after the preposition when that phrase is the role/seat name, e.g. "from middle position" -> "middle position".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate people holding a position; annotate the role title or slot, not the individual name.
Do not annotate organizations, teams, or groups associated with a position; in "these two teams may have the worst OLs", only "OLs" is the position type.

Do not annotate physical locations or spatial positions unless the phrase denotes a conventional role/seat/category within an activity. "middle position" in poker is a position, but ordinary locative uses of "middle position" are not.
Do not annotate medical, biological, or technical process terms such as "macrophages", "type I interferon", or machinery/components; these are not roles, jobs, or play/seat positions.
Distinguish position from generic employment events or staffing counts: annotate the role slot name ("trainee positions"), not the fact that positions are available.
Distinguish position from tactical actions or game actions: annotate the seat/role name ("big blind") but not action words like "opened" or "defended".

problem.definition: A mention of a difficulty, error, malfunction, adverse condition, or troublesome situation that is presented as something wrong, failing, or needing to be handled or resolved.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that explicitly denote a problem, difficulty, trouble, error, or malfunction.
Annotate both generic and specific problem mentions, including broad forms like "trouble" and more specific forms like "network connection errors".
Annotate problem mentions whether they describe current, recurring, or potential issues, as long as the expression itself denotes the adverse condition.
Annotate problem expressions embedded in larger clauses when they are the semantic object of discussion, e.g. in questions or statements about handling them.
Annotate plural or collective problem mentions when they refer to a set of mistakes or failures, e.g. "errors".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate neutral or positive concrete entities such as foods, beverages, menu items, occupations, places, organizations, or people.
Do not annotate events, historical incidents, or activities unless the text explicitly frames them as a problem rather than simply describing that they occurred.
Do not annotate general subject matter, domains, or technologies by themselves when no difficulty or failure is expressed.
Do not annotate desired actions, solutions, or handling instructions; only annotate the problem being addressed.
Do not annotate vague surrounding context that implies concern without naming a specific troublesome condition.
Do not annotate causes or related circumstances alone if they are not themselves phrased as the problem.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the problem itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the problem expression, such as domain/specifying nouns or adjectives: "network connection errors".
Include the head noun that signals the problem, such as "trouble", "error", "errors", "failure", "issue", when it is used to denote an actual or possible problem.
Do not include surrounding reporting or framing words such as "about how to handle", "there is", quotation punctuation, or determiners unless they are inseparable from the problem mention.
Do not extend the span to include causes, consequences, locations, times, or agents unless they are syntactically part of the problem phrase itself.
When the problem is expressed as a plural/common noun phrase, include the full noun phrase but stop before unrelated following clauses or prepositional phrases not needed to identify the problem.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate problem mentions only when the span denotes a difficulty, error, malfunction, adverse condition, or trouble; do not annotate entities that are merely objects, foods, people, jobs, places, or historical events.

Do not confuse a topic with a problem: if a phrase names a subject area without indicating difficulty, it is not a problem. "network connection errors" is a problem because it names failures, not just networking.
Do not annotate ordinary activities or processes unless the wording explicitly marks them as problematic, e.g. annotate "errors" but not "writing the code".
Do not annotate generic narrative context or event descriptions unless the event itself is presented as a harmful/problematic condition by words like "trouble", "error", or similar.
If a sentence contains an event, product, or domain plus a problem word, annotate only the problem-denoting phrase, not the broader event or document topic.

procedure.definition: A medical, surgical, or clinical intervention or technique performed on a patient or body site, expressed as the name of an operation, treatment action, or method of carrying out such an intervention.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of medical or surgical procedures, operations, interventions, or treatment techniques.
Annotate procedural noun phrases headed by intervention terms such as debridement, closure, resection, excision, repair, or surgery names when they denote a performed or performable clinical act.

Annotate named methods of carrying out a clinical intervention, including phrases introduced by terms like "method of" when the full phrase refers to a procedural technique, e.g. "method of wound closure".
Annotate procedure mentions whether they are single-word names or multiword phrases, e.g. "Esophagectomy", "Sharp debridement", and "combined resection".
Annotate each distinct procedure mention separately when more than one procedure is named in the same sentence or title.
Include procedure mentions in titles, headings, and bracketed article titles when they denote the intervention discussed.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-medical uses of action words or commercial/promotional text that merely contains words like product, performance, engineered, or sale.
Do not annotate diseases, diagnoses, symptoms, wounds, tissues, organs, or body parts by themselves unless they are part of the procedure name.
Do not annotate people, roles, or participants involved in care, such as clinicians or "the nurse's role".
Do not annotate generic scientific or methodological language that does not itself name a clinical intervention, unless it explicitly denotes a procedure concept as in "method of wound closure".
Do not annotate outcomes, properties, benefits, or treatment goals such as comfort, support, pressure relief, or discounted product performance.
Do not annotate temporal, locational, or indication phrases attached to a procedure, such as "for T4 esophageal carcinoma", unless they are inseparable from the canonical procedure name.
boundary_rules: Start the span at the first word that names the procedure itself, including procedural modifiers that are part of the technique name, as in "Sharp debridement".
End the span at the last word that still denotes the procedure; exclude trailing purpose, indication, anatomy, disease, or participant phrases unless they are integral to the procedure name.
Include nominalized action heads such as "debridement", "closure", "resection", and surgery names such as "Esophagectomy" when they denote an actual medical/surgical procedure.
For analytic constructions like "method of wound closure", include the full procedural noun phrase when the head and its complement together identify the procedure concept.
Do not extend the span to surrounding discourse markers, punctuation, bracket characters, or descriptive context outside the procedure phrase.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the action or intervention itself, not the disease, condition, or wound being treated; for example, annotate "Sharp debridement" but not devitalized tissue alone.
Annotate the procedural act, not anatomical sites or organs unless they are embedded inside the procedure phrase, as in "method of wound closure".
Do not confuse procedure names with products, materials, or commercial items; texts about sales, foam products, or engineered formulas are not procedures.

Do not annotate general performance descriptions, roles, or study framing language unless a concrete procedure term is present; for example, annotate "the nurse's role" as non-procedure and "Sharp debridement" as procedure.
When a phrase contains both a procedure and a diagnosis/context, annotate only the procedure portion; in "for T4 esophageal carcinoma", the carcinoma is not part of the procedure entity.
If multiple coordinated procedure mentions appear, annotate each distinct procedure term separately when each independently denotes a procedure, as with "Esophagectomy" and "combined resection".

process.definition: A process is a named action, operation, procedure, or dynamic biological/physical/clinical activity that unfolds over time, including physiological functions, treatment or cleaning procedures, and measurable operational activities.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that explicitly denote a process, procedure, or dynamic activity occurring over time.
Include biological or physiological processes, such as cell behaviors or functional activities, e.g., "proliferation" and "cytotoxicity."
Include measurable clinical or experimental functional processes expressed as noun phrases, e.g., "inulin clearance."
Include operational or household procedures when the phrase names a recognized procedure, e.g., "normal laundering procedures."
Annotate process mentions whether they are natural, induced, observed, or evaluated, as long as the span itself names the process rather than the act of studying it.
Include both single-word and multi-word process names when the full phrase is needed to identify the specific process type.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate entities that are people, groups, organizations, places, classes, media items, or projects, even if they are involved in or promote an activity.
Do not annotate generic reporting or investigation words such as "experiments," "investigate," "observed," or "diagnosis" unless the phrase itself names the process being referred to.
Do not annotate static attributes, results, or qualities such as "yellowness," "stiffness," "natural frequencies," "mode shapes," or "limitation."
Do not annotate broad event nouns or abstract occurrences when they do not clearly denote a process type, such as "changes," "motion," or "experience" in the provided contexts.

Do not annotate failures, conditions, or abnormalities merely because they happen over time if the mention is used as a state/problem label rather than a process name, as in "interface failure" and "loosening" in the negative examples.
Do not annotate highly generic action words with no domain-specific process meaning in context, such as "help," "share," "making the rounds," or "brings attention."
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal contiguous span that names the process itself.
Include head nouns that denote an action, operation, procedure, or physiological/experimental process, such as "proliferation," "cytotoxicity," "clearance," and "procedures."
Include necessary modifiers that are part of the established process name or that specify the process type, such as the object or manner in "inulin clearance" and "normal laundering procedures."
Exclude surrounding verbs that merely report or study the process, such as "were investigated" or "were observed."
Exclude coordinating punctuation and conjunctions unless they are inside a single established process name; in lists, annotate each process mention separately.
Exclude participants, agents, targets, locations, or instruments unless they are syntactically part of the process term itself.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate physical objects, substances, or body parts involved in a process; annotate the action or operation term instead, e.g., "inulin clearance" not "inulin" alone.

Do not annotate research methods or analytical techniques when they are mentioned as tools or study designs rather than as the target process type; phrases like "finite element method," "computer simulation," and "modal analysis" are not process entities here based on the negative examples.
Do not annotate social artifacts or products such as projects, campaigns, videos, blogs, classes, or collections of stories; these are outcomes or works, not processes.

Do not annotate general events, states, properties, or failures unless the phrase explicitly denotes a process or procedure. For example, "changes," "loosening," and "failure" in the negative examples are not annotated here.

When a phrase names an ongoing biological, chemical, clinical, domestic, or operational process, annotate it; when it names evidence, diagnosis, result, limitation, or capability related to that process, do not annotate the non-process term.

profession.definition: A word or phrase that denotes a person's occupation, trade, or professional job category.
include_rules: Annotate common nouns or noun phrases that explicitly name a profession or occupational group.
Annotate professional specializations and licensed occupations when they refer to the job category, such as medical, legal, consulting, or other recognized professions.
Annotate both singular and plural mentions of professions.
Annotate profession mentions whether they refer to one person, multiple people, or a class of workers in general.
Annotate profession terms in ordinary prose even when modified by adjectives or followed by descriptive content, as long as the profession word itself is present.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that describe activities, methods, fields of study, or technical concepts rather than occupations, such as "algorithm", "rotation", or "translation".
Do not annotate generic human references that lack occupational meaning, such as "people", "someone", "group", or pronouns.
Do not annotate organization names, departments, teams, or networks even if they are composed of professionals.
Do not annotate modifiers, evaluative descriptions, or relative clauses attached to a profession mention unless they are part of a fixed profession title.
Do not annotate credentials, degrees, honorifics, or names of individuals unless the occupational title itself is explicitly present.
Do not annotate verbs or deverbal nouns that refer to work activity rather than the worker category itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the profession, typically the head noun alone: "consultants", "lawyers", "anesthesiologists".
Include multiword profession titles when all words are part of the occupational name, such as adjective+noun or specialty+noun combinations if present.
Do not include surrounding determiners, quantifiers, or modifiers that are not part of the profession name, such as "many" in "many consultants" or "the" in "the lawyers".
Do not include predicates, actions, or descriptive clauses attached to the profession mention, such as "known for generating millions of dollars in profit".
For coordinated professions, annotate each profession term separately unless the coordination forms a single fixed title.
Annotate plural and singular profession forms equally when they denote an occupation.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate profession when the span denotes an occupation or job category, not merely a person or group without occupational meaning.

Do not annotate technical or academic terms, mathematical expressions, algorithms, or domain terminology unless they explicitly name an occupation; the negative examples contain many specialized words but no profession entities.
Do not annotate organizational roles or participant labels unless they clearly function as profession names.
Distinguish profession from field or discipline: annotate the person-type occupational label (for example, "anesthesiologists"), not the area of practice or activity by itself.
Distinguish profession from descriptors of skill, status, or behavior; only the occupational title itself should be labeled.

program.definition: A named or generic software, academic, training, or institutional program entity, including code/software artifacts, educational rotations such as clerkships, and formally designated programs.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of software or code artifacts referred to as a program entity, including plural forms like "codes" when they denote implemented code.
Annotate educational or training program entities, including curricular units and rotations such as "clerkships" when referred to as the program/unit itself.
Annotate formally named institutional programs, especially when introduced with a title or acronym, such as "MBRS Program".
Annotate both proper names and generic program mentions when the noun itself denotes a discrete program-like entity rather than a general activity.
Include mentions whether singular or plural if they refer to identifiable program entities in the sentence context.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general activities, procedures, services, or tasks that are not organized programs or code artifacts.
Do not annotate institutions or places associated with a program, such as universities, ports, employers, or laboratories, unless the program itself is named.
Do not annotate diseases, syndromes, psychological conditions, or biological systems.
Do not annotate broad research contexts such as "rodent models", "behavioral researchers", or "security services"; these are not program entities.
Do not annotate descriptive mentions of work or employment such as "job" or "doing her job properly".
Do not annotate standalone common nouns that merely describe an area or domain unless they specifically refer to a program-like entity in context.
Do not annotate surrounding legal, medical, or procedural text that mentions no software, educational program, or organized institutional program.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest contiguous noun phrase that names the program-like entity.
Include the core head noun when it denotes the entity, such as "Program", "code", or "clerkships".
Include modifiers that are part of the official or conventional name of the program, such as acronym or title words before the head noun: "MBRS Program".
For generic references, annotate only the program term itself if that term is being used as the entity mention, e.g. "clerkships" or "codes".
Exclude surrounding prepositions, determiners, and verbs unless they are part of the fixed name.
Exclude trailing institutional affiliation or location unless it is clearly part of the official program name; in "MBRS Program at Mississippi Valley State University", annotate only "MBRS Program".
Do not extend the span to include explanatory clauses, purposes, lists, or descriptive text following the entity mention.
confusable_type_rules: Do not label organizations, employers, ports, universities, or other institutions as program; annotate only the program run by them if explicitly named.
Do not label jobs, occupations, or employment roles as program; a "security guard" or "job" is not a program.
Do not label medical conditions, disorders, syndromes, or biological axes as program, even when followed by the word "program" in broader discourse about research.

Distinguish program from procedure or methodological detail: experimental procedures, reporting variables, and laboratory conditions are not programs unless they are referred to as a named code, curriculum, clerkship, or organized program entity.

Distinguish program from survey, study, or document names: surveys and court documents are not programs unless the mention specifically denotes a training, academic, software, or organized institutional program.
When a term could denote either software text or a broader technical method, label it as program only if the context indicates actual code/software artifacts, as in "codes" being implemented.

programming concept.definition: A word or phrase that refers to an abstract concept used in programming or software development, especially a code-level construct, structural unit, or callable abstraction such as a function, method, or class.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote programming abstractions or constructs discussed as concepts rather than as specific code tokens.
Include common software-development concept terms such as "function", "method", and "class" when they refer to programming constructs.
Annotate concept mentions even when used generically or hypothetically, e.g. asking whether a "function" exists or whether a "method" or "class" has a property.
Include singular or plural mentions of programming concepts when they clearly refer to code-level ideas or structures.
Annotate concept terms appearing in explanatory, comparative, or instructional text, not only inside source code.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-programming uses of abstract nouns, even if the word could appear in technical contexts.
Do not annotate error labels, error-message wording, or diagnostic phrases such as "incompatible type" or "incomparable type".
Do not annotate domain subjects, academic disciplines, institutions, or general science terminology.
Do not annotate product, platform, framework, or language names unless they themselves are being used as programming concept terms.
Do not annotate concrete code instances such as specific variable names, literals, return values, operators, or entire code expressions.
Do not annotate highly generic words like "anything", "scenario", or "operation" unless they clearly denote a recognized programming concept in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the minimal span naming the concept itself, usually a single noun such as "function", "method", or "class".
Exclude surrounding determiners, adjectives, and quantifiers when they are not part of the concept name, e.g. annotate "function" not "a different function".
When concepts appear in coordinated or disjunctive phrases, annotate each concept term separately, e.g. in "function or anything like that", annotate only "function".
Do not extend the span to include verbs, arguments, examples, or descriptive clauses attached to the concept term.
If a phrase contains both a modifier and a core concept word, include the modifier only when it is necessary to identify a distinct programming concept rather than a generic description.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate programming abstractions or structural code notions such as "function", "method", and "class", not mentions of concrete code errors or outcomes.

Do not annotate general uses of "type" when it refers to a data category, comparison category, or error wording such as "incompatible type" or "incomparable type"; these are discussed as error/message phrasing in the negative example, not target concept mentions for this type.
Do not annotate programming language names, libraries, frameworks, or platforms; in "exists in wordpress", annotate "function" but not "wordpress".
Do not annotate literal values, variable names, operators, or code snippets unless the span itself is a named programming concept term.
Do not annotate broad academic or scientific subject terms such as "Materials Science" or "science"; these are non-programming domains even though they contain abstract terminology.

project.definition: A named project, initiative, or organized effort referred to by a specific proper name, including scientific, technical, restoration, or research projects.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of projects or initiatives, including technical, scientific, restoration, and research projects.
Include project names that appear with or without the generic word "project" when the name itself uniquely identifies the effort.
Annotate names containing letters, numbers, or hyphenation when they function as a project title, such as "Euro-REVES 2 project".
Annotate a shorter canonical name when a longer mention contains extra wording not part of the actual project name, as shown by "Karma-Runner" where the entity is "Karma".
Include named restoration or preservation efforts when a distinct proper name identifies the undertaking, such as "Lufthansa Super Star" in a restoration-project context.
Annotate project names even when embedded in descriptive sentences, as long as the mention refers to a specific organized effort rather than a generic task.
exclude_rules: Exclude generic nouns for projects or activities without a specific name, such as "project", "research", "study", or "restoration project" by themselves.
Exclude broad topical descriptions, subject headings, and article titles that describe a field of study rather than name a project.
Exclude names of organizations, funds, government bodies, statistical offices, and international institutions unless they are explicitly used as project names.
Exclude ordinary products, homes, listings, forms, contact fields, and other non-project text even if capitalized.
Exclude benefits, allowances, policies, and programs mentioned as social or administrative concepts unless they are clearly presented as named projects.
Exclude personal names and place names that appear near project mentions but are not part of the project name.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that identifies the project by name.
Include project designators such as "project" when they are part of the name or are needed to express the full named project, as in "Euro-REVES 2 project".
Exclude surrounding descriptive context that is not part of the name, such as preceding nouns or locations: in "restoration project", annotate only "Lufthansa Super Star", not the activity phrase.

If a longer surface form contains a generic suffix but the canonical project name is a shorter embedded name, annotate only the project name, as in "Karma-Runner" → "Karma" when context indicates the project is referred to by that name.
Include internal punctuation, hyphens, numbers, and capitalization that belong to the project name, such as "Euro-REVES 2".
Do not include determiners, verbs, or explanatory apposition outside the project name span.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a project with a general activity or undertaking: annotate only specifically named projects, not unnamed "restoration project", "research", or "study" mentions.
Do not confuse a project with an organization, company, or institution. If the mention refers to a group or institution rather than a distinct project effort, exclude it.
Do not confuse a project with a product, software package, vehicle, or artifact. Annotate such a name only when the context shows it is the name of a project rather than the object itself.
Do not annotate event-like or program-like descriptions unless they function as the proper name of a project.
Research contexts alone are insufficient: phrases like "our research" or topic descriptions are not project entities unless a specific project name is given.

pronoun.definition: A pronoun is a standalone referring word that substitutes for a person, group, or other discourse participant instead of naming it directly, such as personal pronouns like "we" and "they".
include_rules: Annotate personal pronouns that directly refer to discourse participants or entities, such as "we" and "they".
Annotate subject, object, or standalone pronoun forms when they function as the explicit referring mention in the sentence.
Annotate pronouns even when they appear inside contractions, as long as the pronoun portion is explicitly present in the surface form, e.g. "they" in "they'll" and "We" in "We've".
Annotate pronouns regardless of capitalization at sentence start or elsewhere.
Include first-person, second-person, or third-person standalone referring pronouns when they substitute for a named or understood entity rather than repeating a noun phrase.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate proper names, even when they refer to the same discourse participant as a pronoun.
Do not annotate lexical noun phrases or titles that describe a person or group instead of pronominally referring to them.
Do not annotate determiners that modify a following noun, such as possessive determiners in noun phrases like "our technology" or "his office manager".
Do not annotate relative pronouns, complementizers, or other function words when they are introducing clauses rather than acting as the target standalone referring pronoun.
Do not annotate numbers, abbreviations, punctuation, or any non-pronominal token in the sentence.
Do not annotate texts with no explicit pronoun mention, even if entities are implied by context.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the pronoun token itself, such as "they", "we", or "he".

Do not include auxiliary or clitic material beyond the pronoun; in contractions, annotate only the pronoun part when separable in the text, e.g. from "they'll" annotate "they" and from "We've" annotate "We".
Do not include surrounding punctuation, quotation marks, or possessive markers outside the pronoun token.
For multiword pronominal expressions, include only the pronoun word(s) that function as the referring expression, not adjacent modifiers or predicates.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse pronouns with person names, organization names, or titles; annotate "he", "she", "they", "we", etc., but not named mentions such as "Matthew Scott" or "Donald Penn".
Do not annotate common nouns used to refer to people or groups if they are not pronouns, e.g. "father", "brother", "commissioner", or "candidate".

Possessive determiners and articles that modify a noun phrase, such as "our" in "our technology" or "his" in "his office manager", are not annotated as pronoun entities under this guideline unless the token stands alone as the pronoun mention.

Do not annotate relative pronouns or complementizers used mainly to link clauses rather than refer independently, such as "whose" in "Scott, whose father..." or "that" in "That we won't be left..." when they are not the target referring mention.
A pronoun entity is a referring expression standing in for a participant or discourse entity; words that are merely part of a name, title, or nominal description are excluded.

property.definition: A property is a named characteristic, condition, measurable attribute, performance-related quantity, or outcome/state used to describe an entity, material, system, or process.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that denote an intrinsic or observed characteristic of an entity or process, such as physical, optical, mechanical, chemical, or performance-related properties.
Annotate phrases naming operating or environmental conditions when they function as a describable state or constraint, e.g. "stress conditions".
Annotate measurable rate- or quantity-type attributes expressed as noun phrases, e.g. "overall rate of turnover".
Annotate plural or singular property terms when they directly name the characteristic, e.g. "optical properties".
Annotate outcome or state terms when they denote a relevant system condition or performance result, e.g. "failure".
Annotate the property phrase even when it appears as the object of verbs like "measured", "dominate", "cause", or "decrease in".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate names of entities being described, such as materials, devices, compounds, organisms, or systems.
Do not annotate experimental methods, analytical techniques, reactions, treatments, or procedures unless the phrase itself denotes a characteristic rather than an action or method.
Do not annotate standalone numeric values, units, parameter settings, percentages, or coded constants, even if they correspond to measurements.
Do not annotate generic context words that only indicate investigation or observation of a property, such as "results", "analysis", "measured", or "characterized".
Do not annotate broad scientific nouns that refer to processes or events rather than attributes, unless they are clearly used as an outcome/state property as in "failure".
Do not annotate software, code, hardware flags, identifiers, or programming constants from source code snippets.
Do not annotate full clauses describing that a property changes; only the property noun phrase itself is eligible.
boundary_rules: Select the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the property itself, including essential modifiers that specify which property is meant, such as "stress conditions", "optical properties", or "overall rate of turnover".

Include adjectives or quantifiers that are part of the established property name when they restrict the measured characteristic, e.g. "optical" in "optical properties" and "overall" in "overall rate of turnover".
Include the head noun that expresses the property, such as "properties", "conditions", "failure", or "rate".

Exclude surrounding event or relation words that merely describe change in the property, such as "decrease in", "increase in", "measurement of", or "dominate"; annotate only the property phrase following them.

Exclude attached arguments introduced by prepositions if they identify the object or material associated with the property rather than the property itself, e.g. annotate "overall rate of turnover" but not "with choline".
Do not extend the span to include determiners or sentence-level context unless they are part of a fixed technical term.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate a property when the phrase denotes a characteristic, condition, performance measure, or outcome/state of an entity or process, not the entity or process itself.

Do not annotate physical objects, materials, devices, substances, or organisms even when they are being characterized; for example, in "pumping unit" and "two-dimensional semiconductors", the property is separate from the object.

Do not annotate procedures, treatments, reactions, or experimental methods; expressions like "photoelectrooxidation", "analysis", or "reaction kinetics" are not properties unless the phrase specifically names the characteristic being measured.

Distinguish a property from a numeric value or setting: values such as lamp power, current density, pH, treatment time, and percentages are measurement values or parameters, not property mentions by themselves.

States or outcomes such as "failure" should be annotated when they function as a describable condition or performance-related attribute of a system, not when they merely serve as a general verb or non-technical mention.

Generic technical terms like "feature bits" or software/hardware capability names in code are not properties in this annotation sense unless they denote a scientific or engineering characteristic being discussed in natural-language content.

protein.definition: A mention of a specific protein, peptide, enzyme, or named protein product, including full biochemical names and established protein names used to denote the protein molecule.
include_rules: Annotate specific named proteins and protein products, including common protein names such as "calcyclin" and "calmodulin".
Annotate enzymes when the mention refers to the protein/enzyme entity, including multiword biochemical names such as "glutathione S-transferase".
Annotate protein mentions that include contextual qualifiers as part of the named entity when they form a single protein phrase in text, e.g. "Serum glutathione S-transferase".
Annotate abbreviations or acronyms that denote a protein when used as the protein name in context, e.g. "CDPK" in "CDPK activity" if extracting all protein mentions from text.
Annotate protein mentions whether they appear as subjects, objects, appositives, or components of larger noun phrases, provided the span itself denotes the protein.
Annotate singular or plural protein names only when the phrase still denotes a specific protein or protein family mention rather than a wholly generic category.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic references to proteins without a specific name, such as "protein", "protein kinase inhibitors", or "activity" alone.
Do not annotate chemicals, drugs, stains, chelators, or inhibitors even when they interact with proteins, such as "W-7", "staurosporine", "phenylmethylsulphonyl fluoride", or "LDS-751".
Do not annotate antibodies, antibody labels, or reagent phrases such as "monoclonal antibodies", "fluorescein isothiocyanate", or "phycoerythrin-labelled antibody".

Do not annotate broad antigen categories or receptor categories when no specific protein is named, e.g. "surface antigens", "adhesion molecules", "immune complex receptors", "leucocyte surface antigens".
Do not annotate cell types, tissues, organisms, diseases, surgical terms, or clinical descriptors that are not themselves protein names.
Do not annotate experimental methods, instruments, channels, or measurements.
Do not annotate modifiers alone when they merely describe a protein mention; for example, "cell-cycle-regulated protein" alone is not a protein name if the specific name is given separately.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that uniquely names the protein mention in context.
Include descriptive modifiers that are part of the protein name when they directly specify the protein, such as source/body-fluid or biochemical class terms: "Serum glutathione S-transferase".
Include full multiword protein names, including hyphenation and capitalization as written in text.
Exclude surrounding appositive or explanatory words that are not part of the name, e.g. in "cell-cycle-regulated protein calcyclin", annotate "calcyclin" only, not the preceding description.
Exclude generic head nouns such as "protein", "inhibitor", "activity", "antibody", "antigens", or "molecules" unless they are integral to an established protein name.

When coordinated proteins are listed separately, annotate each protein span individually; do not merge across conjunctions. For example, annotate "calmodulin" alone in "The calmodulin and protein kinase inhibitors...".
Do not extend the span to include organism names, cell types, disease names, assay context, or verbs unless they are embedded within the protein name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish protein from chemicals or inhibitors: annotate the protein target itself, not small molecules or drugs mentioned with it. In "W-7 and staurosporine" these are inhibitors, not proteins, while "calmodulin" is a protein.
Distinguish protein from generic protein classes or activities: if the phrase refers only to a functional category or measurement without identifying a specific protein mention, do not annotate it.

Distinguish protein from antibodies and antigens used as assay reagents or surface markers: mentions such as "monoclonal antibodies", "surface antigens", and "function-associated antigens" are not proteins unless a specific protein name is stated.
Distinguish protein from genes or nucleic acids: annotate mentions referring to the protein molecule or protein product, not DNA/RNA entities or stains.

Distinguish protein from cellular components, tissues, organisms, and diseases: words like "serum", "liver", "rats", or disease/process terms are only included when they are part of the protein mention, not when they merely provide context.
Distinguish protein from procedures, instruments, and measurements: terms naming assays, cytometry channels, activity readouts, or surgical/clinical concepts are not protein entities.

protein domain.definition: A protein domain is a named or descriptively identified structural or functional subregion of a protein, including domains or modules defined by position, composition, binding property, or morphology.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of specific protein subregions explicitly labeled as "domain".
Annotate protein subregions labeled as "module" when the context shows they are parts of a protein.
Annotate domain mentions that include positional qualifiers, such as N-terminal or C-terminal.
Annotate domain mentions that include structural, compositional, or morphological descriptors, such as "collagen-like domain" or "globular domain".
Annotate domain mentions that include functional or binding descriptors, such as "RNA-binding domain" or "2xRNA-binding domain".
Annotate each distinct domain mention in enumerations of protein parts.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words from non-biological or unrelated text, even if they resemble domain terminology.
Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, lesions, tissues, or other clinical descriptions.
Do not annotate whole proteins, genes, protein families, or ribonucleoprotein classes unless the mention specifically names a subregion/domain of the protein.
Do not annotate abstract activities or functions without a structural-part term such as "domain" or contextually equivalent "module".
Do not annotate overly generic mentions like bare "domain" or "module" when they do not identify a specific protein subregion.
Do not annotate flanking descriptions that refer to what a domain does unless they are part of the domain name itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the complete domain/module phrase, including positional or descriptive modifiers that specify the part, e.g., "N-terminal domain", "short variable domain", "collagen-like domain", "C-terminal globular domain", "C-terminal module", "N-terminal module".
Include the head noun that marks the structural unit, such as "domain" or "module".
Include attached functional or structural descriptors immediately preceding the head noun, such as binding specificity, copy number, shape, or composition, e.g., "2xRNA-binding domain".
Do not include the parent protein/gene name unless it is grammatically part of the domain mention itself.
Do not include trailing punctuation, citation markers, surrounding prepositions, or appositive context outside the domain phrase.
When coordinated domain mentions are listed separately, annotate each full domain phrase individually rather than the whole list as one span.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a protein domain with a full protein or gene name. In "DNA- and RNA-binding protein JKTBP", annotate neither the protein name nor the whole protein description; only annotate the subregion mention "2xRNA-binding domain".

Do not confuse protein domains with general biological functions or activities. For example, "transcription activation function" is a function, not a structural subunit, so it is not a protein domain.
Do not confuse domain mentions with generic anatomical or everyday uses of words like "domain", "module", "terminal", or "globular" outside protein structure context.
Protein domain refers to a named or described structural/functional part of a protein; annotate the part itself, not the process, phenotype, disease, or cellular role associated with it.
Mentions of protein parts expressed as "module" should be annotated when they clearly denote a protein subregion analogous to a domain, as in "N-terminal module" and "C-terminal module".

protocol.definition: A communication, transmission, transfer, or security protocol, or an explicit protocol class/standard, mentioned as a technical standard governing data exchange between systems.
include_rules: Annotate specific named communication or transfer protocols, especially standard abbreviations such as "RSVP" and "SSH".
Annotate named protocol standards that include alphanumeric or hyphenated designations, such as "Eureka-147".
Annotate explicit generic mentions of protocol classes when the phrase itself denotes protocols, such as "standard transmission protocols".
Annotate protocol mentions in networking, transmission, secure transfer, or system communication contexts when the term denotes the governing standard rather than a device or service.
Annotate both full names and abbreviated forms when they directly refer to a protocol.
Annotate a protocol mention even if it appears inside a larger technical expression, provided the protocol term itself is the entity span supported by context or examples.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-technical names such as hotels, songs, people, places, organizations, or entertainment titles.
Do not annotate ordinary technical terms that describe setups, components, accounts, rooms, services, or infrastructure rather than protocols.
Do not annotate domain names, application areas, or technologies unless the text specifically identifies them as protocols.
Do not annotate verbs, events, or actions involving protocol use, such as "using" or "configured".
Do not annotate surrounding descriptive text that merely modifies a protocol mention but is not part of the protocol name or protocol-class phrase.
Do not annotate examples of transportation, travel, hospitality, or music content that contain no protocol meaning, even if they include capitalized words or numbers.
Do not automatically annotate every acronym in technical text; only annotate acronyms that denote a protocol or explicit protocol standard/class.
boundary_rules: For named protocols, annotate only the protocol name itself, excluding surrounding prepositions, verbs, and descriptive context: in "by using RSVP-TE", annotate "RSVP" only as shown by the example.

When a protocol is expressed as an abbreviation embedded in a longer hyphenated variant, include only the protocol token if that is the protocol mention evidenced by the example; e.g., annotate "RSVP" from "RSVP-TE" rather than the entire compound form.
For generic protocol category mentions, include the full noun phrase that denotes the protocol class when it explicitly refers to protocols, e.g., "standard transmission protocols".
Include protocol identifiers with letters, numbers, or hyphens when they function as the protocol name, e.g., "Eureka-147".
Exclude neighboring domain/application phrases that are not part of the protocol name, such as "for DAB" or "part of" context.

Do not extend the span to include determiners or modifiers unless they are part of the protocol-designating phrase itself; e.g., include "standard transmission protocols" but not preceding quantifiers like "Various".
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish protocol from a general technology or system: annotate the communication/transfer standard itself, not the broader setup, service, or account context. In "Since SFTP is part of SSH", only "SSH" is labeled, indicating the protocol mention rather than unrelated account/setup terms.
Distinguish protocol from application, product, or infrastructure names: if a term names a hotel, song, place, company, or other non-technical named entity, do not annotate it.
Distinguish protocol from domain/task abbreviations: terms like "DAB" are not protocol unless the text explicitly presents them as a protocol or protocol standard.
Distinguish protocol from document structure or engineering terms: items like "LSP" or "setup" are not protocol unless they explicitly name a transmission, network, or secure transfer protocol.
Distinguish protocol from purely descriptive wording: generic technical actions or capabilities are not protocol entities unless they denote a protocol class, as in "standard transmission protocols".

When a compound expression contains both a protocol and an extension or variant notation, prefer the span corresponding to the protocol name evidenced by the examples rather than automatically taking the full compound string.

province.definition: A named first-level or comparable subnational administrative region corresponding to a province, prefecture, or similarly interpreted province-level unit within a country.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of province-level administrative regions.
Include names of regions explicitly marked by province-level designators such as "Prefecture" when the full phrase names the administrative region, e.g. "Okayama Prefecture".
Include province names that appear without a designator when they clearly refer to a province-level unit, e.g. "Kampong Cham", "Kratie", "Prey Veng", and "Free State" in the provided contexts.
Annotate province names appearing in running text, lists, or metadata-like strings when they still denote a province entity.
Annotate historical or current province-level units if the mention functions as a proper geographic name of that administrative region.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic administrative nouns used without a specific proper name, such as "province", "prefecture", or "district" alone.
Do not annotate countries, cities, towns, villages, or other place types that are not province-level administrative regions.

Do not annotate non-geopolitical uses of similar words, including scientific, mathematical, or technical terms such as "crystal", "state" in an abstract sense, or institutional address text that does not name a province.
Do not annotate document structure text, category labels, titles, affiliations, postal codes, or email addresses unless they contain a standalone province name that refers to a province entity.
Do not annotate larger or smaller geographic expressions that only contain a province name incidentally when the full span refers to a different entity type.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full official province-level name as it appears in the text, including an attached administrative designator when it is part of the name, e.g. "Okayama Prefecture".
If the province name appears without a designator, annotate just the name token(s), e.g. "Kampong Cham", "Kratie", "Prey Veng", "Free State".
Do not include preceding generic labels, list markers, or document metadata such as "Category:", "in the", or surrounding punctuation/parentheses unless they are part of the official name.
Do not extend the span to include larger containing locations such as cities or countries mentioned nearby, e.g. in "Okayama City, Okayama Prefecture, Japan", annotate only "Okayama Prefecture".
For coordinated lists, annotate each province as a separate span.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish province from city: annotate the province-level administrative region, not a municipality or city. In "Kampong Cham city", the example evidence shows "Kampong Cham" can denote the province; if the text explicitly refers to the city entity instead, it should not be annotated as province.
Distinguish province from country: do not annotate sovereign states such as "Japan", "Germany", or "Slovak Republic" as province.
Distinguish province from sub-provincial units: terms such as "district" are lower-level administrative areas and should not be annotated as province.

Distinguish province from generic geographic or scientific uses of similar words: ordinary nouns like "state" or "crystal system" are not province unless they are part of a proper province name such as "Free State".

Distinguish province from organizations or institutions located in a province: universities, academies, research centers, and departments are not province entities even if their addresses mention places.

publication.definition: A publication is a named or generic written or issued media work or outlet, such as a magazine, journal, newspaper, or article, referred to as the document itself or the publishing outlet.
include_rules: Annotate names of specific publications such as magazines, newspapers, journals, and similar outlets.
Annotate generic mentions of publication types when they refer to written works or outlets, e.g. "journals" and "articles".
Annotate multiword publication titles in full, including embedded genre words like "Magazine" when they are part of the name.
Annotate publication mentions whether singular or plural, named or unnamed, if they denote the publication object rather than its topic.
Annotate publication entities in contributor or citation contexts, such as where someone writes for or contributes to a magazine or journal.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate subject matter, topics, or descriptors that merely modify a publication type without being part of the publication name.
Do not annotate standalone document content, article text, abstracts, or study narratives when no publication entity is being referred to explicitly.
Do not annotate titles or heading-like strings by default if they are presented only as the content of a work rather than as a publication mention.
Do not annotate organizations, hospitals, companies, or media platforms unless the text identifies the publication itself.
Do not annotate broad domains such as industries, economies, communications, or social systems that are not publications.
Do not annotate generic plural nouns unless they clearly denote publications, as in "articles" or "journals"; unrelated uses of similar words should be excluded.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal span that names the publication or publication type itself.
Include internal function words and leading articles when they are part of the title, e.g. "The Daily Jugantor".
Include the medium word when it is part of the named title or the generic publication mention, e.g. "Tag Magazine", "F Stop Magazine", "journals", "articles".
Do not include surrounding verbs, determiners, modifiers, or descriptive clauses unless they are part of the official title.

For generic mentions, annotate only the publication noun phrase that denotes written/issued works, not nearby topical modifiers unless they are part of the same publication expression; e.g. in "Anti-inflammatory drug journals", annotate "journals" rather than the full topical descriptor.
Do not extend the span to include sentence content that looks like an article title when the text is functioning as quoted or running prose rather than a publication mention.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate publication for newspapers, magazines, journals, articles, and similar issued written/media works or outlets, whether named or generic.
Do not annotate organizations, companies, or contributors just because they produce or host content; the entity must be the publication itself, not its publisher or staff.
Do not annotate creative works in general unless they are functioning specifically as publications; publication is narrower than any title-like text in the sentence.
Do not annotate events, industries, or subject matters discussed by a publication; only the publication name/type is included.

In scholarly or descriptive prose, distinguish article/journal mentions from research content: annotate the publication term only when it refers to the document or outlet, not when the text is merely the body or title of a study without an explicit publication mention.

quality.definition: A word or short phrase that denotes an attribute, property, condition, capability, or evaluative characteristic of an entity, process, or situation.
include_rules: Annotate nouns that directly name an attribute or evaluative property, such as "tolerability", "efficacy", and "order".
