Annotate adjectives or participial forms that state a condition or property of an entity, such as "viable" and "non-motile".
Annotate qualities that describe physical, functional, behavioral, structural, or evaluative characteristics of an entity or situation.
Annotate both positive and negative property expressions when they directly denote a quality, including polarity-bearing forms like "non-motile".
Annotate a quality whether it is objective/descriptive or evaluative, as long as the span itself denotes the property rather than the thing evaluated.
Annotate quality terms even when they appear inside larger prepositional or complement structures, provided the quality word itself is clearly identifiable.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate entities, substances, organisms, diseases, treatments, films, scenes, or other concrete/abstract objects that merely possess qualities.
Do not annotate actions, events, processes, or mechanisms unless the span itself names a property rather than an occurrence.

Do not annotate generic report language, survey labels, headings, or response categories such as "Overall Satisfaction", "Favorable", or "Unfavorable" when they function as document/reporting categories rather than direct quality mentions.
Do not annotate broad descriptive phrases whose head is not a quality term, such as treatment options, classes, spectra, mechanisms, administration routes, or development processes.

Do not annotate evidence statements about a quality when the quality is not itself the span, e.g. exclude phrases like "has been shown to be effective" unless only the adjective "effective" is being marked and the scheme allows it in context.

Do not annotate degree words, intensifiers, negation words standing alone, or comparison markers such as "more", "less", "very", or "comparable" unless they are part of a lexicalized quality expression.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that expresses the quality term itself, usually a noun or adjective, such as "tolerability", "efficacy", "viable", "non-motile", or "order".
Include derivational morphology and fixed polarity markers that are part of the quality word, e.g. include the full token in "non-motile".
Do not include surrounding function words, determiners, copulas, or support phrases, e.g. annotate "viable" not "were viable", and "order" not "in the order".
Do not include the entity being evaluated or the domain context unless it is part of the lexicalized quality expression, e.g. annotate "efficacy" not "efficacy of levofloxacin in APE".
When coordinated qualities appear, annotate each quality separately, e.g. "tolerability" and "efficacy" as two spans.
If a quality is expressed by a comparative or modified phrase, annotate only the quality head, not degree or comparison words unless they are built into the quality term.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate quality only when the span denotes a property, attribute, capability, status, or evaluative characteristic; do not annotate the object that has the property.
Do not annotate outcomes, results, or events merely because they imply a judgment; annotate the explicit quality word only if present.
Do not annotate broad discourse labels or summary categories such as survey headings or report sections even if they contain evaluative language; these are document descriptors, not quality entities.
Do not annotate treatment, organism, film, or other domain entities surrounding the quality term; for example, in "efficacy of levofloxacin" only "efficacy" is the quality.

Do not annotate generic scientific or operational descriptions unless they function as a direct property term. For example, "spectrum of activity", "modes of action", and "resistance development" describe mechanisms or processes rather than a standalone quality term in this schema.

A sentiment-laden adjective is quality only when it attributes a property to an entity in context; generic mentions of response categories like "Favorable" and "Unfavorable" in survey reporting are excluded because they function as labels rather than intrinsic quality mentions.

quantity.definition: A mention that expresses an amount, count, number, or quantitative extent of something, either as a numeral/cardinal expression or as a lexical quantity term.
include_rules: Annotate explicit cardinal numbers that denote how many units, items, people, rooms, or similar countable entities are involved.
Annotate number+noun expressions when the noun is part of the counted quantity mention, such as a count of people or objects.
Annotate lexical quantity terms that directly express amount or extent, such as "amount" or "amounts".
Annotate quantitative expressions whether they modify a following noun or stand alone, as long as they semantically indicate amount or count.
Annotate both exact numeric quantities and general amount terms when they refer to measurable quantity rather than identity.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate nouns that denote measurable properties or dimensions by themselves unless they explicitly express an amount or count.
Do not annotate generic descriptive words indicating scale, size, or degree without a quantity expression.
Do not annotate temporal durations, dates, ages, or frequencies when they function primarily as time expressions rather than quantities.
Do not annotate numbers used only as identifiers, citations, addresses, street numbers, section numbers, or parts of names unless they clearly denote amount/count.
Do not annotate entity mentions that lack an explicit quantitative meaning, even if they imply abundance or scarcity.
Do not annotate broad noun phrases when only a subpart is the quantity expression.
boundary_rules: Include the minimal span that expresses the quantity mention.
For pure numerals used as quantities, annotate only the numeral itself (e.g., "300").
When a number is directly tied to the counted unit and together they form the quantity expression, include both number and unit/noun (e.g., "14 patients").
For non-numeric quantity nouns, annotate the quantity word itself when it denotes an amount or extent (e.g., "amounts").
Do not include surrounding determiners, prepositions, verbs, adjectives, or location/event context unless they are part of the quantity expression itself.
Do not extend the span to include what the quantity modifies if that material is outside the quantity phrase.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse quantity with the thing being measured or counted: annotate the amount expression, not the broader object/event mention.

Do not confuse quantity with measurement attributes or physical properties mentioned generically without an explicit quantity expression; words like "mass", "frequency", or "stiffness" alone are not quantities in these examples.
Do not confuse quantity with time expressions; temporal mentions such as "eight weeks" indicate duration/time rather than quantity for this type.

Do not confuse quantity with identifiers, names, or labels; numbers that function as part of a proper name, model name, citation, or other identifier should not be annotated unless they denote amount/count.
Do not confuse quantity with descriptive adjectives of size or degree such as "small" or "major" when no explicit amount/count expression is present.

race.definition: A mention of a racial or ethnic group identity, including named groups and adjectival references to such groups when they denote people by race or ethnicity.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of racial or ethnic groups, such as "Hispanic".
Annotate adjectival references to racial groups when they denote people by race, such as "black" in a statement about group characteristics.
Annotate group identifiers whether they appear in demographic, social, historical, or comparative contexts.
Annotate both single-word and multiword mentions when the full span names a racial or ethnic group.
Annotate mentions even when the group is discussed in general rather than referring to a specific individual or set of individuals.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that describe color, appearance, disease states, or metaphorical labels rather than racial/ethnic identity.
Do not annotate scientific, medical, or technical terms, including molecules, cytokines, genes, procedures, or syndromes.
Do not annotate named events, works, organizations, or locations even if their wording could resemble a group label.
Do not annotate generic nouns referring to people without a specific racial/ethnic designation, such as "population" or "people" alone.
Do not annotate the standalone category word "race" unless it is part of a specific named racial/ethnic group mention.
Do not annotate nationality, regional origin, or language labels unless the context clearly uses them as racial or ethnic group identifiers.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the race or racial/ethnic group.
Include the core group term itself, whether it is a single word or a multiword proper name-like expression.
For adjectival group mentions used as noun phrases, annotate only the adjective/group word, e.g. "black".
Do not include surrounding function words such as articles, conjunctions, or prepositions, e.g. exclude "or Latino of any race" when annotating "Hispanic".
Do not extend the span to include generic class words like "race", "population", or "people" unless they are part of a fixed group name.
If a mention contains multiple distinct groups joined coordinatively, annotate each group separately when they refer to separate racial/ethnic categories.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse race with color terms used literally or medically; words like "blue" in "blue babies" are not race entities.
Do not confuse race with biomedical, chemical, or protein names containing Greek letters, hyphens, or abbreviations, such as "IL-2" or "TNF-alpha".
Do not confuse race with event names, titles, or competitions; if a phrase denotes a named event rather than a people group, it is not a race entity even if it contains words like "Mile".
Do not confuse race with geographic regions or demonyms when they refer to location or nationality rather than racial/ethnic identity.
Do not confuse race with broad generic references to categorization, such as the standalone word "race" when it does not name a specific group.

radio station.definition: A named radio broadcasting station, usually identified by its official call sign or full station name, referring to the broadcaster as an organization/outlet rather than to a frequency, network, airport code, or generic station mention.
include_rules: Annotate specific named radio stations mentioned in text.
Annotate official call signs used as the station name, especially short all-caps identifiers like "WWRC", "KQIK", and "KQDI".
Annotate the station name when the context indicates broadcasting activity, airing programming, receiving equipment, licensing, or call-sign changes.
Annotate the current or former call sign when it is explicitly used to identify a radio station.
Include mentions where the station is referred to as a broadcasting outlet, member station, or licensed radio service, provided the mention itself names the station.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic references such as "the station" or "commercial station" when no specific station name is given.
Do not annotate frequencies, channels, or technical descriptors alone, such as "90.5 FM".
Do not annotate names of television stations or other non-radio broadcasters.
Do not annotate airport codes, location codes, HTML/code tokens, or other strings that only resemble call signs but are not radio stations in context.
Do not annotate network, league, university, or program names that are merely carried by a radio station.
Do not annotate location names, licensing phrases, or jurisdiction descriptions associated with the station mention.
Do not annotate disambiguation-page headings or bare ambiguous strings when the context does not clearly identify a radio station mention.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the station name itself, typically the call sign, e.g. "WWRC", "KQIK", "KQDI".
Do not include preceding or following generic descriptors such as "station", "radio station", "commercial station", or format/frequency text.

If a call sign appears with a service-band suffix as part of the full station name, include the suffix only when the mention clearly refers to the radio station itself; if the context is ambiguous or not an actual mention of the station, do not annotate.
Do not include surrounding punctuation, list markers, parentheses, or licensing/location information.
When a sentence says a station changed its call sign, annotate the call sign that names the station, not the surrounding phrase such as "the current" or "call sign".
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish radio station from television station: annotate only entities that denote radio broadcasters; do not annotate call signs explicitly referring to television stations, even if the string resembles a radio call sign.
Distinguish radio station from airport/code identifiers: do not annotate ICAO or other transport/location codes such as "KLFT" when they refer to an airport rather than a broadcaster.

Distinguish radio station from generic media-network names: annotate the local station call sign (e.g. "KQIK"), not larger distribution or sports networks such as "Oregon Sports Network" unless the network itself is the station being referred to.
Distinguish radio station from generic nouns: words like "station" alone are not entities unless accompanied by the specific station name.

Distinguish actual mentions from disambiguation or reference-list entries: a bare string in a may-refer-to list or code list should not be annotated unless the context clearly uses it as the name of a radio station mention.

real estate.definition: Land, buildings, dwellings, premises, and physical property features or amenities that are part of a residential or commercial property being owned, rented, sold, or described.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote a real property unit, such as a house, home, or property.
Annotate physical parts, structures, and outdoor features of a property, such as yards, sheds, garages, and access features.
Annotate amenities or included features when they are presented as components of the property offering in a real-estate context, such as "Appliances".
Annotate generic property references when they clearly denote the premises being rented, sold, owned, or maintained, e.g. "home" and "property".
Annotate both complete properties and subordinate property features if they refer to tangible real-estate assets rather than agreements or activities.
Annotate property-related access or lot characteristics when they are described as part of the premises, e.g. "lane access".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-property objects or equipment mentioned outside a housing or property-description context.
Do not annotate legal, contractual, or financial terms related to real estate, such as "lease", "rent", or transactional processes.
Do not annotate people involved in housing contexts, such as buyers, tenants, landlords, or owners.
Do not annotate generic scientific or medical references to physical objects, lesions, apparatus, or laboratory structures that are not land/building property.
Do not annotate descriptive condition phrases alone unless they name a property feature; for example, annotate "fenced yard" but not just "good condition".
Do not annotate broad contextual words that only imply housing activity without naming a property item, such as "looking" or "making mistakes".
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the real-estate item or property feature, including essential modifiers that identify its type or physical character, e.g. "fenced yard", "oversized single garage".
Include attached descriptors that are part of the property feature name, such as size, structure, or access type, when they directly modify the real-estate item.
Exclude surrounding verbs, determiners, and possessives unless they are part of a fixed property name; annotate "home" not "a home", and "property" not "the property".
For coordinated property features, annotate each feature separately when they are distinct units, e.g. "fenced yard", "shed", and "oversized single garage" as separate spans.
Include access-related property features when they denote part of the premises or lot characteristics, e.g. "lane access".
Do not extend the span to transactional or legal language around the property, such as "rent", "lease", or tenant-related wording.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate physical real-estate units and property features, not legal or financial arrangements; for example, "property" is real estate, but "lease" and "rent" are not.
Annotate real-estate features sold or included with a property, even if they are movable household items in context, as shown by "Appliances" in a property listing context.
Do not annotate technical, medical, or scientific uses of words like "property", "object", "lesions", or "equipment" when they do not denote land, buildings, or property features.

Distinguish real estate from general locations: annotate a property-related location feature such as "lane access", but not broader places unless they specifically name the premises or a feature of it.
Distinguish real estate from people or roles involved with it; "tenants" and "buyers" are not real estate even when mentioned in housing contexts.
Distinguish real estate from household actions or conditions; annotate the physical asset ("home", "property") rather than events like buying, renting, or maintaining.

reference.definition: A textual label or title that explicitly refers to another informational item, such as a figure designation or the title of a referenced publication, list, or resource.
include_rules: Annotate explicit figure-style references that use a marker plus identifier, such as "FIG. 1" or "FIG. 28".
Annotate full titles of named resources, lists, reports, or publications when they are mentioned as referenced works, such as "IUCN Red List of Threatened Species".
Annotate human-readable reference expressions that point the reader to a specific external or internal informational object.
Include references whether they appear sentence-initially, sentence-finally, or embedded in running prose, as long as the span itself is the reference label/title.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate citation markup, bibliography anchors, or placeholder-style reference codes, such as bracketed citation IDs.
Do not annotate legal or patent citation strings like "U.S. Pat. No. 4,042,279" based on the provided negative examples.
Do not annotate generic nouns for materials without a specific reference label or title, such as "figure", "table", "patent", or "solution described" when no explicit named reference is present.
Do not annotate years, issue numbers, or numbering alone unless they are part of the full reference expression.
Do not annotate names of entities that are merely content topics within a sentence, such as species names, devices, conditions, or study subjects, unless they are part of a referenced title.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full citation label exactly as written, including standard abbreviations and numbering when present: e.g., "FIG. 1", "FIG. 28".
Include all words that are part of a named publication or catalog title, e.g., "IUCN Red List of Threatened Species".
Do not include surrounding verbs, punctuation, or descriptive context outside the reference name, such as "shown in" before "FIG. 28".
For figure/table-style references, include the marker plus its identifier; do not annotate only the number without the reference marker.
When a reference appears inside a longer sentence, select only the minimal span that uniquely names the referenced item.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse reference with a general document mention or legal/patent citation. Mentions such as "U.S. Pat. No. 4,042,279" are excluded here unless the task explicitly treats such document citations as named references; based on the examples, patent citations are not annotated.
Do not annotate bracketed bibliography keys or inline citation handles such as "[@b1-pnfs-21-310]"; these are citation markup, not human-readable reference names.
Do not confuse reference with ordinary numbers, dates, or identifiers. A bare year like "2006" is not a reference entity when it only modifies a title.
Do not confuse reference with the subject matter being discussed. For example, a species name or device name is not a reference unless it is itself the title/label of a referenced item.
Reference entities function as pointers to figures or named works/resources, rather than people, places, organisms, inventions, or generic objects mentioned in the text.

region.definition: A named or specifically described geographic area or functional subarea of an object, device, or surface, expressed as a region-level span rather than an individual country, city, or unrelated object.
include_rules: Annotate names of geographic regions that refer to multi-country, subcontinental, continental-part, or otherwise non-country areas, such as 'Central America' and 'Indian subcontinent'.

Annotate non-geopolitical region expressions when they identify a specific functional or structural area within an object, device, image, surface, or system, such as 'reflective region' and 'transmission region'.
Annotate region mentions whether they are proper names or descriptive noun phrases, as long as they denote a specific bounded area rather than a generic place.
Annotate each distinct region mention separately when multiple regions are mentioned in the same sentence.
Annotate the region phrase even when it appears in scientific, technical, or patent-style descriptions.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate individual countries, cities, states, or other administrative/political units unless the mention itself is a broader region expression.
Do not annotate generic mentions of space or area that are not specific region designations, such as vague uses of 'area', 'section', or 'part'.
Do not annotate numeric indices, reference numbers, or diagram labels attached to a region mention, such as '10' and '20'.
Do not annotate code tokens, SQL fragments, filenames, class names, command-line arguments, or other technical strings that do not denote a spatial or functional region.
Do not annotate entities from unrelated semantic types that merely contain location-like words or capitalization patterns.
boundary_rules: Include the full region name or region-designating noun phrase that identifies the area, such as 'Central America', 'Indian subcontinent', 'reflective region', and 'transmission region'.
For geographic regions, include directional or descriptive adjectives that are part of the conventional name, e.g. 'Central' in 'Central America' and 'Indian' in 'Indian subcontinent'.
For technical or diagrammatic regions, include the modifier plus the head noun 'region'; do not include trailing reference numerals or labels such as '10' or '20'.
Do not include surrounding prepositions, determiners, or verbs unless they are part of the fixed region name.
When a larger coordinated phrase contains multiple regions, annotate each region span separately, e.g. 'reflective region' and 'transmission region'.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish region from country or other administrative place names: annotate broader or subnational area expressions like 'Central America' and 'Indian subcontinent' as region, but not individual countries such as 'Mexico' or 'the United States'.

Distinguish region from generic location nouns: annotate only when the phrase denotes a specific area or a functionally defined subarea, not an unspecified use of words like 'area', 'zone', or 'part' without region reference.
Distinguish region from physical objects or components: in technical text, annotate the area designation ('reflective region') rather than the larger device or material it belongs to.
Distinguish region from identifiers or labels: numbers, figure markers, and test names are not region entities even when adjacent to a region term.
Distinguish region from organization, product, or software strings appearing in code or logs; such strings do not denote spatial areas and should not be annotated as region.

register.definition: A register is either a formally named registry/list used as an official record or designation, or a hardware/CPU register identifier used as an operand in low-level programming text.
include_rules: Annotate official, explicitly named registries or lists whose proper name includes or implies a register, such as "National Register of Historic Places".
Annotate the name of a register when text states that something is listed on, entered in, or recorded in that register.
Annotate hardware or CPU register names used in programming, assembly, or opcode descriptions, such as "al".
Annotate short alphanumeric register identifiers when they function as operands or storage locations in machine-level or assembly-like contexts.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate unrelated capitalized names such as competitions, organizations, agencies, software products, missions, or prizes.
Do not annotate code symbols that are not hardware registers, such as function names, class names, labels, temporaries, IR intrinsics, or compiler directives.
Do not annotate generic references like "the register", "a register", or "historic register" unless they clearly refer to a specific named registry.
Do not annotate buildings, districts, landmarks, or other items that may appear in relation to a register but are not themselves the register.
Do not annotate instruction mnemonics or operation names such as "CMP"; only annotate the register operand.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full official name of the register when it is expressed as a title, including leading modifiers that are part of the name, e.g. "National Register of Historic Places".
For hardware/CPU contexts, annotate only the register token itself, e.g. "al"; do not include surrounding instruction names, punctuation, operands, commas, or numeric literals.
Do not extend the span to include contextual verbs or prepositions such as "listed on the" before a named register.
If a register name is followed by dates, status words, or descriptive clauses, stop the span at the end of the register name.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a register with an organization, award, or program name just because it is capitalized; annotate it only when the phrase denotes an official registry/list or a hardware register operand.
In historic-preservation contexts, the annotated span is the registry itself, not the building, district, or place being listed on it.
In assembly or low-level programming contexts, annotate CPU register identifiers such as "al", but not opcodes, instructions, immediates, memory references, functions, or compiler/runtime symbols.
Do not annotate generic uses of the common noun "register" when it means sign up, record, device register, or a non-specific list unless it clearly names a specific register entity.

related art.definition: Technical concepts mentioned in a prior-art or background context, including technologies, devices, processes, materials, data/media objects, technical properties, and document or graphics concepts that are the subject matter of the art being described.
include_rules: Annotate names of technical devices, machines, or components discussed as prior art, e.g., "display device".
Annotate technical processes, methods, or operations, including spelled-out names and explicit abbreviations, e.g., "Chemical mechanical polishing" and "CMP".
Annotate materials, substances, or engineered layers used in the technical description, e.g., "silicon dioxide".
Annotate software, languages, protocols, or other named technologies, e.g., "PostScript".
Annotate technical media/data objects and outputs of processing, e.g., "processed image".
Annotate technical properties, qualities, or characteristics when they are explicitly discussed as objects of improvement or analysis, e.g., "sharpness" and "details".
Annotate document, layout, or graphics concepts when presented as technical subject matter, e.g., "page description language", "layout", "graphics", and "document".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-technical named entities such as persons, locations, organizations, nationalities, teams, events, dates, or scores.
Do not annotate purely contextual, rhetorical, or legal/patent boilerplate text such as section headings, discourse phrases, or references to "those in the art".
Do not annotate verbs, full actions, or result/purpose clauses unless they contain a standalone technical noun phrase that independently names the related art entity.
Do not annotate overly generic words that are not used as technical concepts in context.
Do not annotate pronouns, determiners, or repeated references that do not explicitly name the technical concept.
Do not annotate quantities, times, rankings, or other incidental factual details unrelated to technical subject matter.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the technical concept, artifact, material, process, property, or document component.

Exclude surrounding explanatory or definitional context such as articles, copulas, and purpose phrases: in "PostScript is a page description language for generating the layout...", annotate "PostScript" and "page description language", not the full clause.
For abbreviations introduced in parentheses, annotate the full term and the abbreviation separately when both appear explicitly, e.g., "Chemical mechanical polishing" and "CMP".
Do not include section headers or discourse markers such as "Description of the Related Art" unless they are themselves the technical term, which the examples do not support.
Include multiword technical names in full, e.g., "display device", "processed image", "silicon dioxide", "page description language".

When a general technical noun is used as a standalone object of discussion in the prior-art description, annotate just that noun phrase, e.g., "layout", "graphics", "document", "sharpness", "details".
confusable_type_rules: Do not restrict annotation to inventions, products, or methods only; this type also includes materials, image properties, document/graphics concepts, and other technical objects discussed in prior art.

Distinguish related art from named entities about real-world events, people, places, organizations, or dates. In the negative example, items such as countries, cities, competitions, people, and times are not related art.
Distinguish technical entities from section titles or rhetorical framing. "Description of the Related Art" is context, while the technical items described under it are related art.

Distinguish the technical concept itself from its functional description. Annotate "processed image" but not the larger purpose clause "to improve sharpness or details of an image" beyond the standalone technical/property terms that are explicitly named.
If both a specific named technology and its category are present, annotate each separately when each denotes a technical concept, e.g., "PostScript" and "page description language".

relationship.definition: An expression that explicitly names an abstract relation, relationship, or association holding between two or more people, entities, concepts, variables, or objects.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that explicitly denote a relationship or relation between entities, concepts, people, or objects.
Include interpersonal, conceptual, statistical, structural, and formal relation expressions when the text names the relation itself.
Annotate both singular and plural forms, such as "relationship", "relationships", and "relation".
Include modified relation phrases where adjectives or compound modifiers specify the type of relation, e.g. "doctor-patient relationship" or "adjacency relation".
Annotate relation expressions even when the related arguments are omitted or only implied, as long as the phrase itself denotes a relationship.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that merely contain the string "relation" or similar morphology when they refer to a software component, identifier, title, or proper name rather than an abstract relation.
Do not annotate unrelated legal, copyright, license, or boilerplate text.
Do not annotate participant entities alone when no relationship term is present.
Do not annotate implicit relationships that must be inferred from context; the relation must be explicitly named in the text.
Do not annotate verbs or clauses describing that items are related unless there is a noun phrase denoting the relation itself.
Do not annotate highly generic connective language such as "connection" or "association" unless it clearly functions as the named relationship expression in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase that denotes the relationship, including modifiers that are part of the expression, e.g. "doctor-patient relationship", "significant relationships", "adjacency relation".
Include the head noun that signals the relation, such as "relationship", "relationships", or "relation".
Include adjectival or domain-specific modifiers that specify the kind of relationship when they are attached to the relation term.

Exclude surrounding verbs, prepositions, and clausal material that merely describe the relationship, e.g. in "relation between vertices", annotate "adjacency relation" but not "between vertices" unless the argument phrase is syntactically integrated into the named relational noun phrase.
Do not extend the span to include determiners or quantifiers unless they are part of a fixed relational expression; usually annotate from the first descriptive word to the relation head noun.
If a word containing "relation" is used as part of a product, class, file, or tool name rather than as a semantic relation, do not annotate it.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate relationship only when the phrase denotes an abstract connection, association, or relational structure between entities, people, variables, or concepts.
Do not annotate names of software, products, APIs, classes, or files that happen to contain relation-like wording; for example, a tool name such as "PredicateEditor" is not a relationship.

Do not annotate the entities participating in the relationship by themselves; only annotate the phrase naming the relationship, such as "doctor-patient relationship" rather than separate spans for "doctor" and "patient".
Do not annotate general discussion of interaction or dependency unless it is explicitly realized as a relationship/relation noun phrase.
When a phrase describes a legal, organizational, or bibliographic document context without naming a relation, it is not a relationship entity.

religion.definition: Names of religions, denominations, sects, faith traditions, or religious bodies, including formal religious identities and named religious traditions or authorities used as established religion-specific terms.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of religions or faith traditions.
Annotate denominational or sectarian names, including multiword religious identity expressions such as "Orthodox Jewish".
Annotate named religious traditions, authorities, or normative sources that are specific to a religion, such as "Sunnah".
Annotate terms for religious bodies when used as names of the faith community or denomination, such as "Church" in a context referring to a religious body.
Annotate singular or multiword spans that identify a person's or group's religion, even when embedded in a larger noun phrase, provided the religion expression itself is explicit.
Annotate both adjectival and nominal forms when they function as the name of the religion or denomination.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic words for belief or worship that do not name a specific religion or religious tradition, such as bare uses of "religion".
Do not annotate non-religious capitalized terms, including court names, military units, battles, states, places, and personal names.
Do not annotate family, ethnic, national, or social descriptors unless they explicitly form a religious identity term.
Do not annotate buildings or physical sites of worship when the reference is to a location rather than a religious body.
Do not annotate roles, classes, or events associated with religion unless the span itself is the religion name.
Do not annotate broad narrative context that merely implies a religion without naming it explicitly.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that uniquely names the religion, denomination, sect, faith tradition, or religious body.
Include internal modifiers that are part of the religious identity name, such as doctrinal or denominational adjectives: "Orthodox Jewish".
Include singular institutional religious body names when they are used to mean a religion or church body rather than a building, e.g. "Church" in a religious-teaching context.
Exclude surrounding nouns that describe people or social units rather than the religion itself, e.g. in "Orthodox Jewish family", annotate only "Orthodox Jewish", not "family".
Exclude preceding articles, prepositions, and following explanatory material unless they are part of the fixed religious name.
For titles of religious law, custom, or normative tradition, annotate only the religious term itself, e.g. "Sunnah".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse religion with persons or groups of believers: annotate the religious identity term, not the people noun, e.g. annotate "Orthodox Jewish" but not "family".

Do not confuse religion with organizations when the mention clearly refers to a specific administrative institution rather than a faith tradition; annotate only when the term denotes a religious body or denomination.
Do not confuse religion with facilities or places of worship; words like "church" should be annotated only when they refer to the religious body or faith, not a building.
Do not confuse religion with legal, military, or geopolitical entities; capitalized words in case citations, wars, courts, regiments, states, or empires are not religion entities.
Do not confuse religion with generic academic or social uses of words like "religion classes"; annotate only the named religion-related entity, not the generic activity.

Religious concepts, customs, or authorities are included only when they function as names of a recognized religious tradition or normative religious source, as in "Sunnah"; generic moral terms are excluded.

religious group.definition: A named or clearly identified group of people organized by shared religion, denomination, sect, congregation, or adherent identity, including both formal religious units and terms for members of a religion.
include_rules: Annotate words or phrases that denote adherents of a religion or denomination, such as plural group names like "Christians".
Annotate self-identification or label terms for religious adherent groups when used as group names, such as "Neopagan" and "Pagan".
Annotate common nouns for organized religious communities or ecclesiastical units when they refer to the group itself, such as "parish".
Annotate both formal and informal names of religious groups, sects, denominations, and congregations when the referent is a collective of believers.
Annotate singular or plural forms when they stand for the religious group identity rather than an abstract adjective.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-religious groups, organizations, software terms, legal text, or any unrelated content.
Do not annotate religious objects, sacred symbols, texts, rituals, or beliefs by themselves.
Do not annotate generic words for people or collectives that are not themselves religious-group designations, such as "members", "persons", or "faiths or beliefs".
Do not annotate place names unless the expression refers to an ecclesiastical grouping rather than merely a location.
Do not annotate personal names, divine names, or titles of individuals unless they denote a group.
Do not annotate modifiers around the group mention that only describe attitudes, quantity, or evaluation rather than forming part of the group name.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the religious group or religious collective, e.g. "Christians", "Neopagan", "Pagan", "parish".
Include adjectival or demonym-like forms only when they explicitly denote adherents or a religious grouping in context, as in "Neopagan" and "Pagan".

Do not include surrounding determiners, quantifiers, or modifiers unless they are part of the group name itself, e.g. annotate "Christians" not "Not all members of faiths or beliefs regarded as Neopagan".
When a common noun denotes a religious unit or congregation, annotate just that noun, e.g. "parish".
Do not extend the span to associated religious objects, doctrines, places, or persons, e.g. in "Christians exalt the Cross of Christ", annotate only "Christians".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate religious communities, denominations, congregations, or adherent groups; do not annotate beliefs, doctrines, or abstract religion terms unless the word refers to a group of adherents in context.
Do not confuse a religious group with a religious object or symbol; for example, "Cross of Christ" is not a religious group.
Do not confuse a religious group with an individual religious figure or person name; only the collective group is annotated.
Do not confuse a religious group with a geographic place unless the place term itself denotes an ecclesiastical unit or congregation in context, as with "parish" in the example.
Do not annotate generic social collectives such as "members", "persons", or "people" unless the text explicitly identifies them by a religious group name.

research.definition: A phrase that explicitly denotes a research activity, investigation, or study/studies, including named study types or generic references to research as an undertaking.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that explicitly name a study or studies, including study-design modifiers, e.g., "descriptive study" and "qualitative studies".
Annotate explicit mentions of research as an activity or agenda, including temporal or planning modifiers, e.g., "future research".
Annotate singular or plural research expressions when they refer to formal investigation in scholarly, clinical, scientific, or evidence-producing contexts.
Include modifiers that specify the kind, approach, or stage of the research when they are syntactically part of the research phrase.
Annotate generic mentions of research even when no specific project title is given, as long as the phrase itself denotes research activity.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text that contains no explicit reference to research or studies.
Do not annotate general academic or clinical concepts that are not themselves research expressions, such as "findings", "clinical practice", "experiences", or "perspectives".
Do not annotate methods, actions, or procedures unless they are part of a phrase naming a study or research activity.
Do not annotate domains, subjects, participant groups, or phenomena being studied by themselves, such as donor groups, diseases, or outcomes.
Do not annotate unrelated uses of words from other senses or other document types, including news articles, product descriptions, stories, dates, and technical specifications.
Do not annotate overly broad surrounding text when only a smaller research phrase is present.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal contiguous span that names the research activity or study type, including modifiers that are part of that expression, e.g., "descriptive study", "future research", "qualitative studies".
Include adjectival or temporal modifiers when they directly specify the research expression, such as study design, methodology, or timing: "descriptive", "qualitative", "future".
Include the head noun that makes the expression a research mention, such as "study", "studies", or "research".

Do not extend the span to include surrounding verbs, determiners, purpose clauses, or topic descriptions unless they are part of the research expression itself. For example, in "synthesize qualitative studies on the experiences...", annotate only "qualitative studies".
Do not include participant groups, outcomes, diseases, or domains that are merely the subject of the research unless they are embedded inside a fixed research expression.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate research when the phrase denotes an investigation, study type, or research activity; do not annotate when similar words refer to general work, practice, writing, or non-research projects.

Distinguish research from topical content: in phrases like "studies on the experiences and perspectives of HSC donors", only the research term "studies" with its relevant modifier belongs to research, while the topic "experiences and perspectives of HSC donors" does not.

Distinguish research from future actions or goals: annotate "future research" because it explicitly names research activity, but do not annotate broader recommendations or practice statements such as "new directions for clinical practice".
Distinguish research from organizations, products, events, or technologies appearing in the same text; only phrases that semantically denote research/study activity are included.
Distinguish research from generic academic language: words like "findings", "evidence", or "screened" are not research entities unless they are part of an explicit research-expression span.

resource.definition: A resource is an informational, digital, or data-bearing artifact that can be stored, accessed, managed, transmitted, or used, such as information, databases, code, or files.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases referring to information content or digital/data artifacts.
Include stored or managed data collections, such as "databases".
Include software/code artifacts, such as "source code".
Include file-like digital objects, such as "sprite file".
Include standalone mentions of information when they denote content being conveyed, shared, or accessed, such as "information".
Include both singular and plural resource mentions when they refer to concrete informational assets rather than a general field or process.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate people, groups, clients, intermediaries, owners, operators, or job roles.
Do not annotate physical devices, infrastructure, vehicles, or material components, even if they store or transmit something, such as waveguides, fibers, cores, cladding, vessels, or departments.
Do not annotate abstract actions, methods, or analyses, such as "performed the statistical analysis"; only the resource object itself is eligible.
Do not annotate broad technical fields, disciplines, or networks, such as "optical telecommunications" or "telecommunication networks".
Do not annotate legal allegations, claims, findings, wages, dates, or locations.
Do not annotate generic mentions of signals, modes, radiation, or rays when they refer to physical phenomena rather than informational artifacts.
Do not annotate entire clauses or sentences that merely describe the use, transfer, or management of a resource.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the resource itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the resource name or subtype, such as material/content or file/data type descriptors: "source code", "sprite file".
Exclude determiners, quantifiers, and possessives unless they are inseparable from a fixed expression: annotate "information", not "the information"; "databases", not "the databases".
Exclude surrounding verbs and event phrases: annotate only "statistical analysis"? No—do not annotate analytic activities; for resources in such contexts, annotate only the object like "databases".

When the resource is expressed as a head noun with a necessary compound modifier, include the full compound span: "optical signal information" would only be annotated if treated as information content, but not the larger clause around it.
Do not extend the span to include users, owners, locations, or transmission context, such as "their clients" or "between different locations".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate resource for informational or data-bearing objects, not for people or organizations that create, manage, send, or receive them.

Annotate resource for stored or usable artifacts like "information", "databases", "source code", and "sprite file", not for processes or actions involving them such as "statistical analysis", "guide", or "carry".

Do not annotate physical infrastructure or vehicles as resource merely because they carry signals or people; examples like "optical waveguides", "optical fiber", "vessel", and "ocean freighter" are not resource here.
Do not annotate legal claims, employment roles, or court-case text as resource; those are discourse content or occupational/legal concepts rather than informational assets or data objects.
When a phrase mentions signals, radiation, or other physical phenomena, treat them as non-resource unless the expression explicitly denotes information content rather than the physical carrier.
If a term refers to a domain, field, system, or network rather than a discrete informational asset, do not label it as resource.

river.definition: A proper name that refers to a river or named flowing watercourse.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of rivers mentioned in text.
Annotate names introduced with the generic word "River" followed by the specific name, e.g. "River Vyrnwy".
Annotate standalone proper river names when context indicates the name refers to a river, e.g. "Tereshka" in a sentence about freezing and thawing.
Annotate each distinct river name separately when multiple rivers are mentioned in the same sentence.
Include named rivers whether they are the main topic or mentioned as related watercourses such as tributaries.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate common nouns for water features used generically, such as "river" or "tributary" without a specific name.
Do not annotate settlements, administrative areas, or other locations that are merely associated with or named after a river, such as "Southfleet" or "the village".
Do not annotate descriptive phrases about a river that are not part of its name, such as "a tributary of the River Vyrnwy" except for the embedded river name itself.
Do not annotate non-geographic terms from scientific, medical, or technical text, even if capitalized or acronym-like.
Do not annotate identifiers, variables, class names, or other strings in source code or markup.
boundary_rules: Include the full proper name of the river as it appears in text.
Include a leading generic term when it is part of the written name, e.g. "River Morda", "River Thames", "River Fleet".
If the river is referred to only by a unique proper name without a generic term, annotate just that name, e.g. "Tereshka".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding appositives or descriptors such as "a tributary of", "minor tributary of", or location phrases.
Do not include determiners or prepositions outside the name, such as "the" before "River Morda".
When a sentence contains multiple river names in a relation, annotate each river name separately.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate only flowing watercourse names that denote rivers; do not annotate names of villages, regions, or other places even when they are named after a river.
Do not confuse river names with generic hydrological descriptions; words like "tributary" are not river entities unless part of a proper river name.
Do not annotate person, organization, biological, chemical, technical, or software terms that may superficially resemble names; the entity must denote a river or named watercourse.
If a name appears in code, metadata, or scientific text but does not refer to a river, do not annotate it.
For water-related mentions, annotate the named river itself, not broader geographic units or adjacent places mentioned with it.

road.definition: A named road entity is the proper name or official route designation of a transportation thoroughfare for vehicles, such as a street, road, highway, or numbered route.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of roads used as transportation thoroughfares, including names ending in terms like "Street", "Road", or similar road-type words.
Annotate official numbered or letter-number route designations when they refer to roads, such as regional or highway route codes like "R26" and "R717".
Annotate highway names that combine a road-type term with a number, such as "Highway 7".
Annotate each distinct road name mentioned in navigation or location context, including when multiple roads appear in the same sentence.
Annotate roads whether they appear as destinations, connectors, or reference points in phrases like "connects ... to ..." or "at ... and ...".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic road terms used without a specific name or identifier, such as standalone mentions of "street", "road", "highway", or "route".
Do not annotate intersections, directions, or route descriptions unless they contain a specific road name; only the road names themselves should be marked.
Do not annotate non-road locations such as cities, neighborhoods, buildings, centers, ports, or geographic areas.
Do not annotate alphanumeric identifiers that refer to non-road objects, such as hull numbers, vehicle models, or other technical codes.
Do not annotate titles of works, organizations, ships, or other named entities that are unrelated to transportation thoroughfares.
boundary_rules: Mark the full road name as it appears in text, including the specific designator when it is part of the name: "Abdullah Ghosheh Street", "Airport Road", "Highway 7", "Jane Street".
Include alphanumeric route identifiers in full when they function as named roads, including any prefix letters and numbers: "R26", "R717".
Do not extend the span to include preceding prepositions, articles, or surrounding location/context words such as "at", "to", "near", "intersection with".
Do not include intersecting-road connectors or punctuation outside the road name; annotate each road separately in phrases like "Highway 7 and Jane Street".
If a road name contains multiple tokens, include all tokens that belong to the official or conventional name and stop at the first token that is not part of the name.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate transportation route names that denote roads, streets, highways, or numbered routes; do not annotate places merely located on or near them, such as "Wepener".

Do not confuse road names with broader place entities such as cities, regions, or facilities. In "Vaughan Metropolitan Centre is at Highway 7 and Jane Street", only the two road names are road entities.

Do not annotate names of ships, vehicles, military units, games, or organizations even if they contain words that can appear in road names or route codes; examples like "HMCS Quinte" and "Majin and the Forsaken Kingdom" are not roads.

A numbered code is a road only when it denotes a route identifier in road context, as with "R26" and "R717"; similar-looking alphanumeric codes for hull numbers, model numbers, or other identifiers are not roads.

Road entities are linear transport thoroughfares, not intersections or junction descriptions. In a phrase describing an intersection, annotate the component road names only, not the whole intersection phrase.

role.definition: A common-noun expression that denotes the functional role, capacity, or participant type of a person, group, or system component in an activity, organization, permission structure, or technical interaction.
include_rules: Annotate common nouns or short noun phrases that explicitly name a functional role played by a participant in a process, system, or organizational setting.
Include technical interaction roles such as communication endpoints or system-side participants, e.g. "client" and "server".
Include human or agentive roles that describe responsibility, access level, or job-like function in context, e.g. "worker" and "admin".
Include roles whether they refer to humans, software agents, machines, or abstract system participants, as long as the word denotes the participant's function rather than its identity.
Annotate singular or plural role terms when explicitly stated, provided they denote a role category in the sentence.
Include role mentions introduced by articles or embedded in prepositional phrases, but annotate only the role term itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate concrete objects, devices, products, plants, materials, or places, even if they participate in an event or system.
Do not annotate proper names of people, companies, products, or brands; the role type targets functional labels, not named entities of other kinds.
Do not annotate generic person references that are not functioning as a specific role label in context, such as broad mentions of people in narrative text.
Do not annotate activity names, tasks, permissions, or abstract concepts themselves; only the entity filling the role is annotated.
Do not annotate descriptive nouns that refer to physical parts or content rather than roles, such as "screen", "contacts", "messages", "memory", or "USB" in the examples.
Do not annotate role-like interpretations that are only implied by context if no explicit role term is present in the text.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun or noun phrase that names the role itself, usually a single common noun such as "worker", "admin", "client", or "server".
Do not include surrounding determiners, possessives, or quantifiers unless they are part of a fixed role expression; prefer "worker" over "the worker" and "client" over "the client".

Do not include descriptive modifiers that merely characterize the role bearer rather than form the role name; for example, annotate only the core role term unless the modifier is essential to the role meaning.
When a coordinated list contains multiple role nouns, annotate each role separately, e.g. "client" and "server" as separate spans.
If a role is expressed inside a longer prepositional or verbal phrase, annotate only the role word(s), not the full phrase.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate role nouns that describe a function, capacity, or participant type in a technical, organizational, or interactional context; do not confuse them with named individuals, organizations, devices, or software products.
Words like "client" and "server" are roles when they describe communication endpoints or interaction positions, not specific branded systems or host names.
Words like "admin" are roles when they denote permission level, responsibility, or user capacity, not when they are part of a team name, title string tied to a person name, or account identifier.

Do not annotate profession-like or person-denoting words unless they are used specifically as a functional role in the described system or activity; generic mentions of people such as "engineer" in news text are not automatically role entities for this type.
Do not annotate objects, artifacts, or natural entities that are merely participants in an event but not role labels, such as "smartphone", "car", "hammer", "cactus", "plant", or "water system".

room.definition: A room is a specific named or typed interior space or chamber intended for a particular use within a larger structure or enclosed environment.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that name a specific type of room or chamber, such as domestic rooms like "kitchen" and "Family room".
Annotate multiword room designations that include functional, stylistic, or size modifiers, such as "formal living/dining room" and "bonus/game room".
Annotate room names used in technical, fictional, or specialized settings when they denote a designated room, such as "Scanner Room".
Annotate singular or compound room mentions whether they are lowercase, capitalized, or title-like, as long as they refer to a room.
Annotate each distinct room mention in coordinated or listed descriptions separately.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic references to a whole dwelling or place of residence such as "home" unless the text clearly identifies a specific room.
Do not annotate buildings, houses, inns, or other whole structures; only the internal room-level space is included.
Do not annotate non-room locations such as countries, cities, or other geographic places.
Do not annotate common nouns that describe activities, furniture, or roles rather than spaces.
Do not annotate mentions that are only part of book titles, plot text, or other unrelated content unless they actually denote a room.
Do not annotate descriptive words alone without the room head noun, such as "formal" or "bonus" by themselves.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase naming the room, including modifiers that are part of the room designation, such as type, function, size, or style: "Family room", "formal living/dining room", "bonus/game room", "Scanner Room".
Include compound room names joined by slashes or multiword modifiers when they refer to a single room type: "living/dining room", "bonus/game room".
Include capitalization as written when the room name is used as a titled or labeled space, but capitalization alone does not determine annotation: "Scanner Room" is included because it names a room.
Do not extend the span to surrounding verbs, prepositions, or descriptive context outside the room noun phrase, e.g. annotate only "kitchen" in "hang them in the kitchen".
Do not include trailing explanatory text that describes the room rather than names it, e.g. in "Scanner Room Description" annotate only "Scanner Room".
When multiple room mentions appear in a list, annotate each room mention separately.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate rooms as indoor spaces or designated chambers within a building, structure, vehicle, base, or similar enclosed setting; do not confuse them with entire buildings, homes, or properties.
Do not annotate broad residence terms such as "home" when they refer to the household or dwelling generally rather than a specific room.
Do not annotate geopolitical places or countries such as "Turkey" or "Austria"; those are locations, not rooms.

Do not annotate organizations, products, or document section titles unless the text specifically names a room as a physical or designated space; "Scanner Room" qualifies because it denotes a room, not merely a product name.
Do not confuse room mentions with people’s roles or possessions; for example, "lady's maid" is a person role, not a room.

If a phrase could refer either to a room or to an abstract label, annotate it only when the local context supports a room interpretation, such as habitation, movement into it, or description of its function or upgrade.

route.definition: A word or phrase that denotes the pathway or mode by which a substance is administered to a body or by which an agent is transmitted or spread, especially in medical or biological context.
include_rules: Annotate terms that specify a medical administration route, such as oral, intravenous, or their abbreviations.
Annotate abbreviations commonly used to denote route of administration when they clearly have that meaning in context, such as "IV".
Annotate phrases that specify a transmission pathway or spread mechanism in biological or medical context, such as "fecal-oral route".
Annotate route mentions whether they appear alone or inside a larger descriptive phrase, as long as the span denotes the pathway itself.
Annotate both administration routes and infection/transmission routes when the text describes how something is delivered to or spread through an organism.
Annotate route expressions even when followed by generic words like "route" or "route of administration", but keep the span focused on the route term or fixed route phrase.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-medical or non-biological uses of route-related or directional language, such as interface placement terms like "left-end", "top", or "bottom".
Do not annotate general uses of "route" referring to travel, navigation, logistics, or abstract process flow.
Do not annotate organizations, places, events, art programs, or technical concepts that are unrelated to administration or transmission pathways.
Do not annotate full treatment expressions when only part of the text denotes route; exclude dose, schedule, and medication names.
Do not annotate generic surrounding descriptors if they do not add route meaning beyond the route term itself, such as "route of administration" when the specific route is separately identifiable.
Do not annotate body sites or anatomical locations unless they explicitly function as a named route expression in context.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal span that expresses the route itself.
Include modifiers that are part of a fixed transmission-route phrase, such as "fecal-oral route".
Include abbreviations when they directly denote route of administration, such as "IV".

Exclude surrounding trigger words that merely introduce the concept, such as "route", "route of administration", "given", or prepositions, when the route term itself can stand alone; e.g., annotate "oral" in "oral route of administration".
Do not extend the span to include dosage, drug names, frequency, or other treatment details; e.g., in "Daily 2g IV ceftriaxone", annotate only "IV".
Do not annotate unrelated uses of the word "route" when it refers to navigation, placement, or interface position rather than biological/medical transmission or administration.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the means/pathway of administration or transmission, not the substance being administered or transmitted; for example, in "IV ceftriaxone", "IV" is route, while "ceftriaxone" is not.
Annotate the route term itself, not the disease, pathogen, or condition associated with it.

When a phrase contains both a generic label and a specific route, prefer only the specific route expression if the generic label is optional; e.g., annotate "oral" rather than the full descriptive phrase "oral route of administration".

Do not confuse medical/biological route with physical directions, transport routes, software/UI placement terms, or artistic/programmatic uses of similar words such as "left", "right", or "open source" in non-medical contexts.
A route is a semantic pathway or mode by which something enters or spreads in an organism; if the text instead names a location, procedure, device, or treatment regimen, it is not route.

school.definition: A named or clearly identified school-level educational institution, or an explicit school-type unit such as elementary school, referring to a school as an institution rather than to a person, place, event, or general concept of education.
include_rules: Annotate official names of primary or secondary schools and similar school-level institutions.
Annotate school names that include modifiers indicating affiliation, population, or level, such as "Girl's", "Public", or "High" when they are part of the school name.
Annotate explicit school-type phrases that refer to a school as an institution, even when generic rather than uniquely named, such as "elementary school".
Annotate school mentions appearing inside larger sentence contexts about attendance, acting, graduation, or schooling when the span itself denotes the school.
Annotate the school portion of a mention even when it is followed by location information or punctuation.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-educational uses of the word sequence include/exclude or other technical text such as configuration files or code-like content.
Do not annotate names of ships, vessels, companies, government entities, or other organizations that are not schools.
Do not annotate standalone locations or addresses that accompany a school name, such as city names, roads, states, or countries.
Do not annotate generic education words that do not denote a school institution, such as "schooling" by itself.

Do not annotate activities, events, or objects merely associated with school, such as "school plays"; only the school expression within them may be annotated if it denotes the institution or school type.
Do not annotate person names, titles, dates, or historical descriptions surrounding a school mention.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the school or school-level institution itself.

Include official name components that are part of the school name, such as organization names, descriptors, and the head noun School, as in "Bangladesh Railway Girl's High School" and "Delhi Public School".
Include educational level modifiers when they are part of the institution mention or school-type phrase, e.g. "elementary school".
Exclude following location information, commas, and addresses unless they are part of the official school name: in "Delhi Public School, Mathura Road, New Delhi", annotate only "Delhi Public School".
Exclude surrounding verbs, prepositions, and event nouns such as "plays" in "elementary school plays"; annotate only the school expression.
Do not extend the span to nearby parent organizations, biographical context, dates, or other non-school material.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish school from geographic locations: cities, roads, regions, and countries near a school mention are not part of the school entity unless embedded in the official school name.

Distinguish school from organizations that are not educational institutions: ships, companies, railways, government bodies, and software project fields are not school entities unless they occur as part of a school's official name.
Distinguish school from educational activities or events: annotate the institution or school-type phrase itself, not performances, classes, admissions, or schooling events associated with it.

Distinguish school from broader education terms: generic references to schooling, education, or study are not school unless they explicitly denote a school institution or school-type unit such as "elementary school".
Distinguish school from address strings: when a school name is followed by a location or mailing address, annotate only the school and not the address.

science.definition: Scientific concepts expressed as physical quantities, natural phenomena, effects, or other domain-specific science terms that denote what is being measured, observed, or discussed in a scientific sense.
include_rules: Annotate terms that denote physical quantities or measurable scientific properties, such as "power" and "energy", including their specific variants like "output power" and "input power".
Annotate named scientific effects or phenomena, such as "prismatic effect".
Annotate natural physical entities or phenomena when referenced as scientific concepts, such as "light".
Annotate both single-word and multi-word science expressions when the phrase as used refers to a scientific concept rather than a general topic.
Annotate the concept term even when it appears inside a larger technical phrase, provided the scientific concept itself is clearly identifiable and is the intended referent.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate entities from non-scientific domains such as politics, salaries, legal or administrative reviews, organizations, or job titles.
Do not annotate biomedical disorders, injuries, surgeries, complications, or anatomical/pathological terms such as abscess, pseudoaneurysm, thrombosis, hemorrhage, or trauma.

Do not annotate generic discourse words, actions, or mathematical/relational language unless they themselves denote a scientific concept, e.g. exclude "changing", "controlled", "occupy", and "set values".
Do not annotate objects or systems merely because they appear in scientific text if they are not themselves the science term targeted by this type.
Do not annotate highly generic modifiers alone, such as "input" or "output", when the full scientific quantity is the intended entity.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the scientific quantity, physical phenomenon, or natural entity, such as "output power", "input power", "prismatic effect", "light", and "energy".
Include modifiers that are part of the scientific concept name when they distinguish the concept, as in "output power" versus "input power".
Exclude surrounding verbs, determiners, and relational material, e.g. annotate "energy" but not "transmon energy can only occupy".

Exclude trailing explanatory phrases or complements that describe states, values, locations, or behavior of the concept, e.g. do not extend from "energy" into "levels" unless the longer phrase itself is the scientific concept name.
When a coordinated list contains multiple distinct scientific entities, annotate each entity separately if each can stand alone as a science term.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate scientific concepts, quantities, effects, and natural phenomena as science, not the devices, organisms, people, or organizations associated with them.

Treat common nouns as science only when they denote a scientific entity in context; for example, "light" is science when referring to the physical phenomenon, but not when used in a non-scientific everyday sense.

Do not annotate medical conditions, injuries, procedures, or anatomical/pathological terms as science based on these examples; the negative biomedical passage shows such domain terms are outside this type.
Do not annotate economic, political, or administrative terms such as salaries, reviews, or offices; technical-sounding but non-scientific concepts in news or governance contexts are not science.

If a phrase mixes a non-scientific referent with a scientific quantity, annotate only the scientific part when it is the target concept, e.g. "energy" in "transmon energy" rather than the device name.

scientific concept.definition: A term or phrase that denotes a scientific phenomenon, process, state, property, measurement concept/value, or technical experimental/model-system concept used to describe scientific observations, conditions, or mechanisms.
include_rules: Annotate phrases naming scientific phenomena, mechanisms, transitions, or processes, such as "membrane lipid phase transition".
Annotate scientific states or conditions of biological, chemical, or physical systems, including adjectival forms like "oxic" and "hypoxic".
Annotate technical model-system or experimental-subject concepts used in scientific research, such as "phantoms" in an experimental context.
Annotate scientific measurement concepts and their explicit value expressions when they are presented as scientifically meaningful quantities, such as "field strengths", "4.7 T", and "1.0 T".
Annotate both general scientific quantity terms and specific instantiated values when both are mentioned and each carries independent scientific meaning.
Annotate noun phrases, adjectival phrases, or value-unit expressions that directly denote a scientific concept rather than merely describe non-scientific objects.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-scientific uses of words that may look technical, such as artistic, commercial, religious, or consumer-product language.
Do not annotate prices, product dimensions, shipping details, dates, or other everyday numeric expressions unless they denote a scientific measurement concept in context.
Do not annotate generic discourse words, experimental reporting language, or sentence framing, such as "results", "presented", or "assume", unless they themselves name a scientific concept.
Do not annotate ordinary object mentions unless they refer to a technical scientific model system or concept in the given context.
Do not annotate broad real-world entities like organizations, people, places, or events unless the span itself denotes a scientific concept.
Do not annotate isolated units or symbols without their value when the value-plus-unit expression is the actual scientific concept mentioned in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that expresses the scientific concept, state, process, model system, or measurement concept.
Include all modifiers that are part of the concept name, as in "membrane lipid phase transition" rather than only "phase transition" when the full phrase is used.
Include scientific state adjectives when they denote biological or physical conditions, e.g. "oxic" and "hypoxic".
Include numeric-symbol expressions when they function as scientific measurement concepts in context, e.g. "4.7 T" and "1.0 T" for field strength values.
Do not include surrounding function words, determiners, or verbs unless they are inside the concept phrase.
When a general scientific noun is paired with example values, annotate both the general concept and the values if each independently denotes a scientific concept, e.g. "field strengths" and "4.7 T".
Do not extend the span to include experimental framing or arguments, such as "Results from", "at", or "are presented".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate scientific abstractions, states, processes, measurement concepts, and experimental model-system terms as scientific concept, not only formal technical jargon.
A scientific concept can be a condition or property word such as "oxic" or "hypoxic" when it denotes a scientifically meaningful state rather than ordinary description.
Treat model or experimental system terms like "phantoms" as scientific concept when they refer to technical research objects used in experiments, not everyday objects.
Annotate measurement units with values such as "4.7 T" only when they denote a scientific quantity in context; do not treat unrelated numbers, prices, dimensions, or dates as scientific concept.

Scientific concept differs from general background or commercial language: product descriptions, religious text, decorative art terminology, and shopping information are not scientific concepts in this sense.
When a phrase names a scientific mechanism or phenomenon, such as "membrane lipid phase transition", annotate the full phenomenon phrase rather than isolated component nouns.

season.definition: A temporal entity that names a season of the year or an explicitly labeled season installment/designation, such as a natural season (e.g. spring, summer) or a numbered "Season" in a series.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of natural seasons of the year, such as "spring" and "summer".
Annotate season mentions regardless of whether they are modified by nearby words like "this", "last", or "next"; only the season term itself is the entity.
Annotate explicit installment-style season designations that use the word "Season" plus an identifier, such as "Season 2".
Annotate season mentions whether they are lowercase or capitalized, as long as they denote a season concept.
Annotate season expressions when they function as temporal references in a sentence, e.g. indicating when an event happened or will happen.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic time expressions that are not seasons, such as "annually", "some time", "days", "four working days", or "2008".
Do not annotate words that refer to scheduling, duration, or frequency unless they explicitly name a season.
Do not annotate non-temporal uses of similar words or capitalized tokens unless they clearly denote a season or season designation.
Do not annotate broader noun phrases when only the season term is the entity.
Do not annotate text with no explicit season mention, even if the topic is agriculture, weather, or yearly cycles.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the season or season designation itself.
For natural seasons, include only the season word, e.g. "spring", "summer", "fall", "autumn", "winter".
