For numbered or titled installments using the word "Season", include the full designation such as "Season 2"; include the number that identifies the season.
Do not include surrounding prepositions, determiners, or verbs, e.g. annotate "this spring" as "spring" and "this summer" as "summer".
Do not extend the span to include unrelated modifiers unless they are part of the season name/designation itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate as season when the expression denotes a part of the year or a numbered season of a series/product, not a general time expression.
Do not annotate generic duration or recurrence expressions such as "annually", "days", "some time", or "working days"; these are time-related but not seasons.
Do not annotate domain terms that merely contain seasonal context indirectly or refer to activities associated with a season without naming the season itself.
When "Season" is used as an installment label for media or similar content, treat it as season rather than a generic number or title fragment.
Do not confuse season with dates, years, or other calendar references unless the text explicitly names a season term or season designation.

section.definition: A named or clearly referential subdivision of a larger information resource or interface, such as a page, tab, forum, thread area, or labeled section within a website, application, project settings, or document structure.
include_rules: Annotate names of site or app sections that organize content, such as "events section".
Annotate discussion-area subdivisions such as "forums" when they refer to a platform section users can visit or subscribe to.
Annotate page, tab, panel, or settings-section labels in software or web interfaces, such as "Web".
Annotate section mentions whether they are common-noun labels or title-like UI names, as long as they denote a distinct subdivision of a larger system.
Annotate both explicitly marked sections (with words like "section" or "page") and implicit ones when context makes the structural role clear.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary prose, article bodies, screenshots, or long passages that are not referring to a structural subdivision.
Do not annotate organizations, political groups, universities, banks, telecom providers, or other real-world institutions mentioned in text.
Do not annotate generic nouns like "story", "account", "number", or "project" unless they specifically denote a section of a larger resource.
Do not annotate deadlines, legal topics, or subject matter references that are not interface/document sections.
Do not annotate mentions of user actions or functions, such as "editing" or "subscribe", unless the text explicitly names a section.
Do not annotate broad websites or apps when no internal subsection is being referenced.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the section itself.
Include a generic head noun when it is part of the section name or the only indicator that the mention is a content area, e.g. "events section" and "forums".
Include quoted or title-cased page/tab labels exactly as named, e.g. "Web".

Do not include surrounding UI or document words that introduce the section, such as "page", "section", "goto", or "Open the \"Properties\" of the web project and goto page" when the actual section name is only "Web".
Do not include verbs, determiners, or descriptive context outside the section name, e.g. annotate "forums" not "subscribe to forums".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate navigational or structural content areas of a site, app, document, or interface as section.
Do not annotate whole applications, websites, projects, or documents when the mention refers to the overall product rather than an internal section.
Do not annotate topical subjects or event types unless they denote a named subsection or area, e.g. annotate "events section" but not standalone "events" in a non-structural sense.
Do not annotate general page content, article text, or narrative passages that are not section labels or references to structural subdivisions.
If a term could be either a topic or a UI/page label, use section only when context shows it is a tab, page, forum, thread area, or similar subdivision.

sequence.definition: A mention of a molecular sequence as an informational biological entity, typically a nucleotide or amino acid sequence described explicitly with the word "sequence" or "sequences".
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that explicitly denote a biological molecular sequence.
Include mentions of nucleotide sequences.
Include mentions of amino acid sequences.
Annotate both singular and plural forms, such as "sequence" and "sequences".
Include necessary type modifiers that specify what kind of sequence it is, such as "nucleotide" or "amino acid".
Annotate sequence mentions even when they are followed by complements indicating source or ownership, as long as the sequence term itself is explicit.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general uses of the word "sequence" that do not refer to a molecular biological sequence.
Do not annotate texts with no explicit sequence mention, even if they discuss biology, molecules, or experiments.
Do not annotate biomolecule names, gene names, protein names, virus names, or organism names by themselves unless they are part of the sequence phrase headed by "sequence"/"sequences".
Do not annotate generic terms such as "data", "list", "Appendix", "study", or "proteins" when they are not themselves sequence mentions.
Do not annotate accession numbers, percentages of identity, counts of sequences, or other metadata surrounding a sequence mention.
Do not annotate implied sequences when the text refers only to analysis results or properties without explicitly naming a sequence.
boundary_rules: Select the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the sequence object itself, e.g. "amino acid sequences" or "nucleotide sequence".
Include the core sequence descriptor words such as "sequence" or "sequences" together with necessary modifiers that specify the molecular type, such as "amino acid" or "nucleotide".

Exclude names of organisms, genes, proteins, viruses, or samples that the sequence belongs to when they occur outside the core sequence phrase, e.g. in "the nucleotide sequence of rat ZP3", annotate only "nucleotide sequence".
Exclude surrounding determiners, adjectives of quantity, and discourse words unless they are part of the fixed sequence name, e.g. exclude "the", "total list of 297", and similar context.
When a longer phrase contains both an entity owner and the sequence term, end the span at "sequence"/"sequences" unless the owner term is grammatically inside a fixed sequence type expression.
Do not extend the span to include trailing complements such as "of rat ZP3", accession information, percentages, or experimental results.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate sequence only when the text denotes the molecular sequence as an informational object; do not annotate the underlying molecule or biomolecule itself if the mention refers to the protein, gene, virus, or other biological entity rather than its sequence.
Do not confuse sequence with collections or records that merely contain sequences, such as lists, appendices, papers, or databases; only the sequence term itself is annotated.
Do not annotate generic scientific content words like "data", "study", or "coat proteins" unless they explicitly form a sequence mention.

If a mention names a biological entity modified by sequence language, annotate only the sequence expression, not the biological entity name. For example, in contexts like "sequence of rat ZP3" the entity is the sequence mention, not "rat ZP3".

Do not annotate numerical identifiers, accession numbers, identity percentages, or other metadata associated with a sequence, because they describe or reference the sequence rather than naming the sequence entity itself.

service.definition: A service is a named or clearly delimited offering of assistance, care, access, support, or platform/subscription functionality provided to users, customers, or clients, including both generic service types and branded service names.
include_rules: Annotate phrases that denote a provided support or assistance function for users or customers, e.g. "customer service".
Annotate healthcare, social, or professional care offerings when mentioned as service types, e.g. "psychiatric services".
Annotate branded or named consumer-facing platforms, memberships, or subscription offerings that function as services, e.g. "Amazon Prime".
Annotate both common-noun service types and proper-name services when the mention refers to an actual offering available to recipients or users.
Annotate service mentions whether the recipient is explicit or implicit, as long as the phrase itself identifies a service offering.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic technical terms, data objects, or software artifacts that are not service offerings, such as "API response" or "JSON structure".
Do not annotate locations, settlements, or administrative regions, such as village or province names.
Do not annotate people, organizations, or institutions unless the mention specifically refers to the service they provide rather than the provider entity itself.
Do not annotate broad actions or verbal uses of "serve/service" when they do not denote a service offering.
Do not annotate content, products, or events merely associated with a service platform, such as movies or TV shows being distributed through it.
Do not annotate overly generic references like "service" or "services" when they do not identify a specific offering or type of service in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase that names the service, including descriptive modifiers that specify the service type, e.g. "customer service" and "psychiatric services".
For named platform or subscription services, include the complete official service name, e.g. "Amazon Prime".
Do not include surrounding verbs, prepositions, determiners, or purpose clauses unless they are part of the fixed service name.
Do not extend the span to include broader event or content descriptions around the service, e.g. in a media announcement annotate only "Amazon Prime", not the full sentence about titles being added.
If the text uses singular or plural common-noun forms to denote an actual service offering, annotate only that service expression itself.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish service from organizations or providers: annotate the service offering itself, not the company or institution behind it. In "Amazon Prime", the span refers to the service, not separately to a company.
Distinguish service from places: villages, provinces, countries, and other locations are not service entities, even when they appear in encyclopedia-style text.
Distinguish service from APIs, data formats, and technical artifacts: generic mentions such as "API response" or "JSON structure" are not services unless they explicitly name a service offering.

Distinguish service from general activities or abstract concepts: annotate when the phrase denotes a provided support, care, platform, or subscription offering, not when similar words are used in a non-entity or purely descriptive sense.
Distinguish service from products or content items: movies, TV shows, and other items delivered through a platform are not themselves services; only the delivery/platform service should be annotated.

set.definition: A mathematical expression that denotes a set or collection-valued object, including named classes of sets, descriptive set phrases, and symbolic variables used to refer to sets.
include_rules: Annotate common-noun phrases that explicitly denote a mathematical set, such as "filtered set" and "totally ordered set".
Annotate named or conventional families/classes of sets, such as "Cantor sets".
Annotate symbolic variables when the surrounding mathematical context shows they are being used as a set, such as "B" in "\sup B".
Annotate both singular and plural set-denoting mentions when they refer to mathematical collections.
Annotate set mentions whether they are concrete, abstract, or defined by a mathematical property, as long as the phrase denotes the set object itself.
Annotate the mention even when it appears inside a larger mathematical statement, provided the span directly names the set.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-mathematical uses of the same string, such as surnames, given names, or other named entities unrelated to set theory or collections.
Do not annotate generic common nouns like "formula", "theorem", "measure", or "example" unless they themselves denote a set.
Do not annotate adjectives or properties without the set noun when they only describe a set, e.g. exclude standalone "Dedekind complete".
Do not annotate relation words or structural phrases such as "subset of", "bijection between", "injective", or "surjective".
Do not annotate determiners, quantifiers, or vague modifiers around the set mention, such as "a", "certain", or "some" when they are not part of the set name.
Do not annotate symbols or expressions that are not clearly used as set-denoting objects in context.
Do not annotate every explicit set notation by default when the examples indicate exclusion of generic proof notations such as "A", "[n]", "[m]", or "\{0,\ldots,n-1\}" in broad relational statements.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that denotes the set itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the mathematical set name or description, such as adjectives and participles: "Cantor sets", "filtered set", "totally ordered set".
Include the head noun when present: annotate "filtered set", not just "filtered".
Annotate standalone symbolic names when they refer to a set, e.g. "B" in "\sup B".

Do not include preceding determiners or quantifiers unless they are part of the conventional set expression: annotate "filtered set", not "a filtered set"; annotate "Cantor sets", not "certain Cantor sets".
Do not include relational or operator context outside the set mention, such as "subset of", "sup", equality signs, or surrounding punctuation.
For plural mentions, include the plural noun as part of the span when it denotes sets as entities, e.g. "Cantor sets".
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish set from properties or predicates: annotate the object denoting the collection ("filtered set"), not abstract property words alone such as "filtered" or "Dedekind complete".
Distinguish set from numeric, order, or measure expressions: numbers, bounds, measures, and operators like "\sup" are not sets unless the expression itself denotes a collection.
Distinguish set from general uppercase symbols: annotate a symbol only when the context indicates it is a set or collection-valued mathematical object; do not automatically tag every capital letter.
Distinguish set from person or surname mentions: words like "Whittaker" in biographical or name lists are not mathematical sets.

Distinguish set from formulas or bracketed constructions used only as part of a proof pattern when they are not being annotated in this scheme; generic notations like "[n]" or "\{0,\ldots,n-1\}" should be excluded unless the task specifically treats such explicit extensional/set-builder expressions as set mentions.
Distinguish set from subset relations or membership statements: annotate the set term itself, not the surrounding relation phrase such as "subset of a finite set".

setting.definition: A named configurable option, parameter, preference, or settings field in a software, system, or technical context, including explicit UI setting names and parameter names that can be adjusted or assigned.
include_rules: Annotate names of configurable parameters in technical text, such as security, system, or application options.
Annotate explicit setting names that appear as standalone parameter labels or field contents, including single-word names like "strength".
Annotate strings that identify the default or current option in a system or interface when the string itself is the named setting-related item, such as "Userid" in a defaulted field/context.
Annotate multiword setting phrases when the phrase is the actual name of the settings group or option, such as "throwdown settings".
Annotate setting mentions whether singular or plural when they denote a concrete configurable menu, group, field, or parameter rather than configuration in general.

Annotate settings mentioned in command, instruction, or configuration contexts, especially near cues like "change", "defaulted", "setting of", or similar configuration language, if a specific option name is present.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate expressions that refer to mathematical quantities, variables, constraints, discriminants, or other technical terms that are not configurable options.
Do not annotate artworks, titles, places, people, organizations, or physical objects, even if they appear near descriptive words that could resemble settings language.
Do not annotate generic nouns like "algorithm", "installation", "training", or "matches" unless they are explicitly part of the setting name.
Do not annotate descriptive phrases about configuration without a concrete option name, such as "an appropriate setting" by itself; only the actual named option inside it is annotated.
Do not annotate values, outcomes, or enabled behaviors controlled by a setting unless the dataset treats that exact string as the setting name in context.
Do not annotate broad mentions of "settings" when they do not identify a specific configurable setting or settings group.
boundary_rules: Include the lexical item(s) that name the configurable option or settings field itself, such as a parameter name, UI setting name, or settings phrase.
When a specific option name appears alone as the setting, annotate only that name, e.g. "strength" or "Userid".
Include modifiers that are part of the established setting name, especially plural or domain qualifiers, e.g. "throwdown settings" rather than only "settings".
Exclude surrounding function words and context words such as "setting of", "defaulted to", "change", "accept", "with an appropriate", unless they are part of the setting name.

Do not extend the span to include the value, state, or action associated with the setting when it is separate from the setting name, e.g. in "throwdown settings to accept rank matches", annotate only "throwdown settings".
Exclude quotation marks and other punctuation unless they are internal to the setting string.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish setting from a value: annotate the configurable field or option name, not the selected value or content entered into it unless the value string itself is being used as the named setting target, as in the example "Userid".
Distinguish setting from an action or command: verbs like "change", "defaulted", "accept", or "used" are not settings; only the parameter or option being configured is annotated.

Distinguish setting from a general topic or property mention: annotate only when the term refers to a configurable software/system option or parameter, not when similar words describe non-configurable attributes in math, art, everyday scenes, or other non-configuration contexts.
Distinguish setting from whole systems, products, or installations: product names and version numbers such as software names are not settings unless they directly name a configurable option.

Distinguish setting from generic references to configuration in the abstract: annotate concrete setting names or setting phrases, not broad mentions like "configuration" or "algorithm" unless they function as the actual option label.

shape.definition: A word or phrase that explicitly denotes a geometric figure, spatial form, or the recognizable physical shape of an object or arrangement.
include_rules: Annotate nouns and noun phrases that directly name a geometric shape or spatial form, such as 'circle', 'loop', or 'cube/cubes'.
Annotate expressions describing the physical form of an object when the form itself is the relevant meaning, including modified shape phrases like 'closed loop'.
Annotate singular or plural shape-denoting terms when they refer to one or more entities characterized by that shape.
Annotate shape mentions in visual, physical, or spatial descriptions, including references to objects identified primarily by their form.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate code tokens, programming identifiers, or technical text where words are not used to denote geometric form.
Do not annotate abstract or domain-specific uses of 'structure' or similar terms when they refer to biological targets, molecules, systems, or conceptual organization rather than shape.
Do not annotate general object names unless the object term itself functions as a shape descriptor in context, e.g. 'cubes' is included because it directly denotes cube-shaped objects.
Do not annotate attributes that are not shape terms, such as color, size, position, material, or motion descriptions.
Do not annotate whole clauses or noun phrases when only a smaller subspan expresses the shape.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the shape, such as a single noun ('circle', 'cubes') or a compact noun phrase ('closed loop').
Include modifiers only when they are part of the shape expression itself and needed to identify the form, as in 'closed loop'.

Do not include surrounding prepositions, determiners, verbs, or locative phrases, e.g. annotate 'cubes' not 'the cubes around the screen', and 'circle' not 'a circle inside and surrounded by gray color'.
For plural shape terms, include the full plural noun when it denotes multiple objects of that shape, e.g. 'cubes'.
Do not extend the span to include material, color, function, or discourse description unless those words are part of the shape name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate shape words only when they denote geometric form or a physically described form; do not annotate terms used purely as object names without shape meaning unless the wording explicitly refers to the object's shape.
Do not annotate biological, medical, molecular, or technical 'structure' mentions when they refer to targets, molecules, data structures, or abstract constructs rather than geometric form.
Do not annotate generic object mentions, locations, or actions surrounding a shape mention; only the form-denoting expression is a shape entity.
If a word can denote either a concrete shape or an abstract configuration, annotate it only in the concrete geometric/form sense supported by the sentence.

ship.definition: A named watercraft vessel, especially a specific ship or boat identified by its proper name in context.
include_rules: Annotate proper names that refer to specific ships or seafaring vessels.
Annotate vessel names whether they are single-word or multiword names, e.g., "Enterprise", "Wexford", "Admiral Makarov".
Annotate a name when the surrounding context indicates a maritime vessel, such as phrases like "aboard", ship specifications, class/type descriptions, or naval/commercial sailing context.
Annotate named ships mentioned as military, merchant, or passenger vessels as long as the mention denotes a specific individual vessel.
Annotate historical or fictional ship names if they are presented as specific vessels.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic nouns for vessels such as "ship", "cruiser", "Indiamen", or "boat" when no specific proper name is given.
Do not annotate ship classes, designations, or technical types without an individual vessel name, e.g., "Kresta II-class cruiser".
Do not annotate person names, even if they resemble ship names, unless the context clearly identifies them as vessels.
Do not annotate building or hall names, institutional names, or place names that are not vessels.
Do not annotate vehicle, railway, locomotive, or equipment terms from non-maritime contexts.
Do not annotate adjectival or descriptive modifiers attached to a ship mention unless they are part of the official name.
Do not annotate pronouns, abbreviations, or references like "the ship" unless they themselves are the actual named vessel mention.
boundary_rules: Mark the exact vessel name only, including integral prefixes, honorific-style naming elements, or numerals that are part of the official ship name, e.g., "Admiral Makarov".
Include the full contiguous proper name of the ship; do not split multiword names.
Do not include surrounding descriptors or appositives such as ship class, type, or role, e.g., in "Kresta II-class cruiser, Admiral Makarov", annotate only "Admiral Makarov".
Do not include determiners or prepositions unless they are part of the official name.
Do not include verbs or contextual phrases like "aboard the" or "under the command of"; annotate only the vessel name following them.
If a bare name refers to the vessel in context, annotate that bare name alone, e.g., "Enterprise", "Wexford".
confusable_type_rules: Do not label person names as ship unless the context shows they are the names of vessels; "Admiral Makarov" is a ship when presented as a cruiser, while "Wilton E. Hall" is a person.
Do not label buildings, venues, or other facilities as ship; a name like "Wilton Hall" is not a ship unless the sentence explicitly indicates a vessel.
Do not label transportation systems or rail-related equipment as ship; terms from railway, locomotive, or brake-disc contexts are non-ship objects.
Do not label ship classes, models, or categories as ship. For example, "Kresta II-class cruiser" is the class/type description, not the named vessel.
Do not label organizations, military units, or fleets unless the mention denotes a specific vessel.

Use semantic role to decide: if the mention is the craft on which people serve, travel, or which has nautical specifications such as beam or draught, it is ship; if it denotes a human, place, technology, or generic object, it is not.

signal.definition: A signal is a biochemical, physical, optical, electronic, or otherwise functional information-bearing entity or signal-specific phenomenon that conveys, triggers, modulates, or represents communication, transmission, or amplification in a system.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that directly denote a signal or signaling entity, including biochemical, optical, electronic, or analogous information-bearing entities.
Annotate named or generic signaling molecules when they are used in a signaling sense, e.g. "cytokine".
Annotate phrases referring to physical or engineered signals, including modality-specific mentions such as "optical signals".
Annotate signal-related phenomena when the phrase explicitly names a transformation or function of a signal, e.g. "signal amplification".
Annotate both singular and plural mentions when they refer to actual signals rather than a general field or unrelated concept.
Annotate the mention even if it appears inside a larger compound expression, as long as the signal term itself is a valid standalone span, e.g. "cytokine" inside "cytokine receptor".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate objects, devices, or structures that generate, receive, guide, mount, or contain signals unless the mention itself is the signal, e.g. exclude "receptor" and "waveguides".
Do not annotate generic scientific, industrial, or mechanical terms that lack an information-bearing or signaling meaning in context.
Do not annotate chemicals, compounds, or biological entities when they are mentioned only as substances or components and not as signaling agents.
Do not annotate material flows or substances such as "gas stream", "off-gas", or lists of hydrocarbons when they are not described as signals.
Do not annotate unrelated actions or engineering outcomes such as mounting, alignment, reduction, positioning, or holding power.
Do not annotate broad contextual phrases or clauses that merely mention signal-related verbs unless there is a specific signal noun or fixed signal expression present.
Do not annotate entire compound nouns if only one component denotes the signal; for example, in "cytokine receptor", exclude "receptor".
boundary_rules: Include the full noun phrase or technical term that denotes the signal or signaling phenomenon, including descriptive modifiers that specify its type, medium, or function, e.g. "cytokine", "optical signals", "signal amplification".
For plural mentions, include the plural form when it is part of the entity mention, e.g. "signals" in "optical signals".
Include adjectival modifiers only when they directly characterize the signal itself, such as physical modality or domain, e.g. "optical" in "optical signals".
For process-like signal expressions, include the complete fixed phrase when the head concept is a signal-related phenomenon, e.g. "signal amplification".
Do not include surrounding determiners, prepositions, verbs, or larger event clauses, e.g. annotate "optical signals" not "guide optical signals to propagate".
Do not extend the span to receptor, device, pathway, plant, or other non-signal nouns that merely co-occur with the signal mention.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish signal from physical objects or structures: annotate the conveyed or functional informational/biochemical/physical signal, not the carrier or component around it. For example, in "cytokine receptor", annotate "cytokine" as the signaling molecule, not "receptor".

Distinguish signal from materials, chemicals, or compounds that are mentioned only as substances without a signaling role. A molecule name is a signal only when used as a signaling agent, as in "cytokine".

Distinguish signal from devices or engineering artifacts: words like "waveguides", "anchor bolts", "catalyst body", and similar equipment are not signals even if they transmit, amplify, or interact with signals.

Distinguish signal from general processes or system behavior: annotate terms like "signal amplification" when the process explicitly concerns a signal, but do not annotate unrelated processes such as reduction, mounting, positioning, or propagation unless the span itself names a signal entity.

Distinguish signal from streams, emissions, or flows of matter: gas streams and off-gas are not signals unless the text explicitly frames them as carrying information/signaling rather than material transport.
Distinguish signal from broad scientific topics or fields. Only annotate concrete signal entities or signal-specific phenomena mentioned in context.

size.definition: A word or phrase that specifies the physical dimension, standardized size label, or size-related capacity/attribute of an object, product, or resource.
include_rules: Annotate explicit apparel or product size labels, including letter sizes and named categories, e.g. "M", "Small".
Annotate measurements that state a dimension using a number and unit, e.g. "6 Inch".
Annotate noun phrases that explicitly name a size attribute or capacity-related size concept, e.g. "buffer size".
Annotate size expressions whether they appear before a product noun, after a verb, or standalone, as long as they denote size.
Annotate abbreviated, symbolic, or lexical forms of size when the local context clearly indicates a size meaning.
Annotate both physical object dimensions and technical size/capacity attributes when the phrase explicitly uses size terminology.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that describe suitability, comfort, or fit without naming a size value or size attribute, e.g. "fits well" or "good fit".
Do not annotate generic descriptive adjectives like "large" or "small" when they are only vague descriptions and not explicit size labels or named size expressions.
Do not annotate scientific classes, model codes, receptor labels, or other alphanumeric identifiers that resemble size notation but semantically indicate taxonomy, type, or naming rather than size.
Do not annotate drug names, chemical names, biological terms, or other domain entities that do not express dimension or size.
Do not annotate pure numbers or units when they indicate time, count, version, dosage, class, or other non-size meanings.

Do not annotate the object being sized if the size phrase can be isolated; for example, exclude surrounding nouns such as product names unless they are part of a lexicalized size attribute phrase like "buffer size".
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal contiguous span that expresses the size concept.
Include numeric value plus measurement unit when together they specify size, e.g. "6 Inch".
Include standalone standardized size labels when they denote size, e.g. "M", "Small".
Include common head nouns when the text names a size attribute or capacity-like dimension as a noun phrase, e.g. "buffer size".
Exclude surrounding product names, material names, and descriptive words that are not part of the size expression, e.g. in "Innovative 6 Inch Hand Seamers", annotate only "6 Inch".
Do not extend the span to include fit/evaluation language such as "fits well", "good fit", or comparative wording unless the size term itself is included.

If a unit or size code appears with punctuation or symbols that are part of the written size expression, include them only if they are attached to the size token itself; otherwise keep the span to the size words/numbers only.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate product names or object names as size; annotate only the part that states dimension or size class, e.g. "6 Inch" not "6 Inch Hand Seamers".
Do not annotate quantities, counts, rates, dates, years, or percentages unless they explicitly denote physical size or capacity.

Do not annotate single letters, numerals, or class labels when they refer to scientific classifications, receptor classes, versions, or other codes rather than size; examples like "class I", "class II", or isolated technical symbols are not size.
Do not annotate adjectives meaning amount or degree in a general sense, such as "large", unless they function as an explicit size designation of an item or attribute.
Distinguish size from performance or fit assessment: "Small" is size, but "good fit" and "fits well" describe suitability, not the size entity itself.
Distinguish size from generic attributes or technical parameters unless the phrase explicitly names the size attribute, as in "buffer size".

skill.definition: A word or phrase that denotes a learned ability, competency, proficiency, or task/domain-specific capability that a person or system can possess or apply.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of ability or competence words such as "skill" and "skills" when they refer to having capability.
Annotate phrases naming a specific learned capability, technique, or task-oriented proficiency, including domain-qualified forms such as "PHP Session Management".
Annotate performance or functional capability phrases when they describe skilled ability, as in rehabilitation or motor-performance contexts like "arm hand skilled performance".
Annotate both general and specialized skill mentions, including references to a person’s skill set or whether they have the skills to do something.
Annotate skill expressions whether they are standalone nouns or multiword noun phrases, provided the phrase denotes competence rather than merely a subject area.
Annotate capability mentions even when they appear in informal, hypothetical, or evaluative contexts, such as discussing whether someone has the skills needed for tasks.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate nouns that refer only to a field, topic, object, or technology without expressing competence or capability.
Do not annotate job titles, professions, or roles of people or organizations.
Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, treatments, body parts, or other medical concepts unless the span explicitly denotes a skilled capability.
Do not annotate generic action verbs or event descriptions that are not presented as abilities or competencies.
Do not annotate broad document subjects, question titles, or problem statements unless they directly name a skill.
Do not annotate incidental words like "difference", "speed", "screen", "picture", or "bootstrap" when they refer to objects, properties, or software rather than a competency.
boundary_rules: Include the minimal contiguous span that names the skill or competency expression.
Include modifiers that are part of the skill name or capability phrase, such as domain, modality, or task descriptors: "PHP Session Management", "arm hand skilled performance".
Include singular or plural head nouns that directly denote ability, such as "skill" or "skills", when they refer to competence rather than another object.
Exclude surrounding function words, determiners, auxiliaries, and predicates unless they are inside the conventional skill phrase; for example annotate "skills" not "the skills to".
When a skill is expressed as a noun phrase followed by an infinitival purpose clause, usually annotate only the skill-denoting portion unless the whole phrase is the established name of the skill.
Do not extend the span to nearby tools, problems, questions, or descriptive context unless those words are part of the actual skill expression.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate abilities or competencies, not merely topics, products, or technologies. For example, a software name alone is not a skill, but a capability phrase like "PHP Session Management" is a skill/task area.
Do not label occupations or practitioner titles as skill; a person role such as "chiropractor" is not a skill.
Do not label medical conditions, body conditions, or diseases as skill, even if they appear in instructional or treatment contexts.
Do not label general activities or document topics unless the text presents them as an ability, competency, or skill set.
Task or performance phrases can be skill when they describe learned functional capability, as in "arm hand skilled performance", rather than a one-time event or result.
Interface/layout problems, website plans, or troubleshooting requests are not skills unless the text explicitly names the competency required to perform them.

social group.definition: A social group is a mention of a human collective defined by shared social identity, caste, ancestry, community membership, or participation as a group in a social or research context.
include_rules: Annotate named or labeled caste/community groups, including formal social stratification terms such as 'Schedule Caste'.
Annotate noun phrases that denote a human collective in a social, demographic, or ethnographic sense, including caste- or ancestry-based groups such as 'warrior caste'.
Annotate collective mentions used for groups of people participating together in a study, discussion, or social setting, such as 'group' in 'focus group'.
Include both specific named groups and generic-but-contextually human group terms when the phrase clearly refers to a social unit rather than an arbitrary set.
Annotate the group-denoting phrase even when it appears inside a larger clause about population, interviews, ancestry, or demographics.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-human groups, collections, or categories, such as types of malware, plant equipment classes, or grouped technical components.
Do not annotate generic plural references to people when no specific social grouping is expressed, such as 'people' by itself in ordinary usage.

Do not annotate occupational roles, discourse participants, or functional actors unless the text presents them as a social group identity; examples like 'attacker', 'end user', and 'plant operators' are excluded.
Do not annotate locations, populations measured statistically, or demographic statements unless the span itself names the social group.

Do not annotate ancestry labels, ethnic labels, or descriptors when they are mentioned only as attributes of someone else and not as the actual group span to capture; for example, do not annotate 'Arora' in the example because the target group mention is 'warrior caste'.
Do not annotate abstract uses of 'group' that do not denote a human collective in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that denotes the social group, including essential classifying words such as 'caste', 'people', or 'group' when they are part of the reference.
Include modifiers that are part of the group's identity or subtype, such as 'warrior' in 'warrior caste' or a named qualifier in 'Schedule Caste'.

Do not extend the span to include surrounding determiners, verbs, percentages, locations, or descriptive clauses; e.g., annotate 'Schedule Caste' but not 'the people are from Schedule Caste' or 'Schedule Caste which constitutes 71.53%'.
When a generic collective noun itself is used to refer to a participant set or social unit, annotate just that noun, e.g., 'group' in 'focus group'.

If a broader phrase contains both a people name and a separate social-group descriptor, annotate only the part that itself denotes the social group targeted by this type, e.g., annotate 'warrior caste' rather than the surrounding ancestry statement.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse social groups with organizations or institutions: annotate human collectives defined by social identity or participant grouping, not formal bodies, companies, or administrative units.
Do not confuse social groups with populations tied only to geography; a place name or settlement is not a social group unless the span itself denotes a human group identity.
People labels can be included when they denote a collective social community, but individual persons, personal names, and roles such as 'attacker', 'operator', or 'end user' are not social groups.

Generic technical or functional uses of 'group' should only be annotated when they refer to a set of people in a social or study context; non-human, mathematical, or technical grouping terminology is excluded.
Class or caste terms are social groups when they describe social stratification or ancestry categories; descriptive adjectives alone are not enough unless paired with the group-denoting head noun.

software.definition: A software entity is any named or clearly software-referring mention of a computer program, application, operating system, framework, tool, package, or malware artifact, including both official product names and generic references when they denote a software artifact in context.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of software products, applications, frameworks, tools, packages, and operating systems.
Annotate names of malicious software or attack tools when they are referred to as software artifacts, such as "LOIC".
Annotate software used in technical, installation, development, or security contexts, such as "snort", "Android", "Cordova", and "Ionic Framework".
Annotate lowercase, uppercase, acronymic, and multiword software names if they identify a software artifact.
Annotate generic common-noun mentions like "program" when the local context clearly refers to a specific piece of software or code behavior.
Annotate mentions whether they appear as standalone names or in lists of software to install or use.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate entities that are not software, such as people, organizations, sports teams, venues, locations, dates, or publications.

Do not annotate domain content terms, technical concepts, or activities that merely relate to software use or analysis, such as "rules", "activity", "diagnosis", or "therapy", unless the term itself denotes a software artifact.
Do not annotate hardware, devices, protocols, file formats, programming languages, or code elements unless the text uses them specifically as software product names.

Do not annotate generic nouns like "system", "tool", "application", or "program" when they refer to an abstract concept, a non-software process, or are too vague to denote a software artifact in context.
Do not annotate surrounding descriptive text, installation actions, or document titles that mention software without themselves being software names.
Do not treat capitalization or technical context alone as sufficient evidence; the mention must denote software or a software artifact.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the software item, product, framework, operating system, tool, malware, or program reference.
Include integral modifiers that are part of the software name, such as product-form words in the official name: "Ionic Framework" includes "Framework".
For multiword software names, include all contiguous words belonging to the name: "Ionic Framework" and not just "Ionic" when the full name is written.
Include capitalization variants and acronyms exactly as written in text: "snort", "LOIC", "Android".
When the mention is a generic common noun referring to a specific software artifact in context, annotate only the noun phrase head that denotes the software, e.g. "program".
Do not include surrounding verbs, versionless installation commands, determiners, or punctuation unless they are part of the software name.
Do not extend the span to include descriptive context such as "article on", "rules to detect", or platform/location phrases like "in Ubuntu" beyond the software mention itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate software names and software-referential common nouns, not organizations, people, teams, venues, or media outlets; for example, in "The Spiderlabs Blog has an article on snort rules", annotate "snort" but not "The Spiderlabs Blog".
Annotate malware/hacking tools when they are software artifacts, as shown by "LOIC"; do not exclude a mention just because the software is malicious or security-related.
Annotate operating systems and development frameworks as software, e.g. "Android", "Cordova", and "Ionic Framework".

Do not annotate medical terms, anatomy, diagnoses, therapies, tests, or physiological phenomena from technical prose unless they denote software; terms like "diagnosis", "therapy", and "pulse" are not software.
Do not annotate sports teams, stadiums, persons, dates, or locations even if capitalized, since capitalization alone does not indicate software.

Generic words such as "program" should be annotated only when they clearly denote a software application or code artifact, not when they refer to a schedule, plan, broadcast program, or institutional program.

source.definition: A source is an entity, medium, document, provider, or origin from which information, materials, substances, or availability is obtained or said to originate.
include_rules: Annotate expressions that denote where something comes from, is obtained from, or is copied from.
Annotate media, channels, or providers when they serve as the provenance or supply source, e.g. "Internet" in an access/supply context.
Annotate noun phrases explicitly referring to origins or provenance, including generic source phrases when they are the stated origin, e.g. "various sources."
Annotate documents, posts, answers, pages, or similar informational artifacts when they are cited as the source of copied or referenced information, e.g. "meta.stackexchange answer."
Annotate source expressions for physical, informational, or abstract origins, as long as the phrase identifies the origin/provider rather than the item produced.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate entities that are merely locations, organizations, or products unless the text specifically presents them as the origin or provider of something.
Do not annotate generic nouns that do not actually denote provenance in context, such as incidental uses of "source" in code, titles, or abstract discussion without a concrete referent.
Do not annotate technical strings, filenames, UI labels, code identifiers, or markup terms from code snippets, even if they resemble document names or resources.
Do not annotate outcomes, test results, emissions values, pollutants, or measured variables; these are things derived from or analyzed, not the source itself.
Do not annotate surrounding descriptive context that only explains access or origin, such as "available via" or "copied from," apart from the source noun phrase itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the source itself, without adding surrounding prepositions or verbs, e.g. annotate "Internet" in "via Internet suppliers," not "via Internet suppliers".
Include modifiers that are part of the source expression when they help identify the source, e.g. "various sources" and "meta.stackexchange answer".

For source phrases headed by generic nouns such as "source," "sources," "supplier," "answer," or similar provenance terms, include the full noun phrase if it denotes the origin of information/material rather than a separate event or action.
Do not extend the span to include what is being obtained from the source, its downstream effects, or surrounding locative context unless those words are part of the source phrase itself.
When a platform, medium, website, document, or response is mentioned as the origin, annotate only that source-denoting expression, not the full clause describing copying, availability, or origin.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a source with a physical location or jurisdiction: a place is only annotated if it is explicitly used as the origin/provider; mere mentions of places such as "United States" or "Arizona" are not sources here.
Do not annotate substances, emissions, results, variables, or items being produced or measured; annotate only the origin from which they come.
Do not confuse source with communication content: in "meta.stackexchange answer," the source is the answer as an origin of copied information, not the quoted content itself.

Do not confuse source with software/code artifacts: strings such as "README", function names, file reads, or variable names are not sources unless the text explicitly uses them as provenance entities in natural language.

Generic media or channels can be sources when they function as providers or origins, as in "Internet"; however, generic technical mentions of a network, file system, or platform without an origin/provenance role should not be annotated.

species.definition: Names of specific organism species as mentioned in text, including formal scientific species names and vernacular common names that refer to a particular species-level organism taxon.
include_rules: Annotate scientific Latin species names, especially binomial names consisting of genus plus species epithet, e.g. "Hyparpax aurostriata".
Annotate common names that refer to a species, including eponymous forms with possessives, e.g. "Fischer's greenbul" and "Sharpe's greenbul".
Annotate common fish, bird, insect, or other organism names when they are used to denote the species itself, e.g. "walleyes" and "perch".
Annotate species mentions regardless of whether they appear in taxonomic discussion, ecological context, feeding context, or identification context.
Annotate both singular and plural surface forms when they still denote the species taxon or organism kind mentioned in text.
Annotate the species mention even when it appears inside a larger taxonomic statement, such as being described as a subspecies of another species.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate names of sports teams, organizations, products, brands, or other non-organism entities, even if their wording could resemble a biological name.
Do not annotate diseases, syndromes, infections, or medical abbreviations such as "acute respiratory infections" or "ARIs".
Do not annotate generic references to organisms that do not identify a species-level taxon, such as broad categories like "bacterial complications" or "children".
Do not annotate taxonomic rank terms by themselves, such as "subspecies", "species", "genus", or similar labels, unless they are part of the species name string.
Do not annotate descriptive biological phrases that refer to groups of organisms without naming the species, such as "pre-school children" or "one specific part of the upper respiratory tract".
Do not annotate author names, founder names, player names, or locations that appear near species-like wording but are not organism names.
boundary_rules: Include the full species mention as it appears in text, whether it is a scientific binomial name or a common-name expression.
For scientific names, include both genus and species epithet when present, e.g. "Hyparpax aurostriata".
For common names, include the complete animal or organism name, including possessive eponym parts when they are part of the established name, e.g. "Fischer's greenbul" and "Sharpe's greenbul".
Include pluralized common species mentions when they refer to the animal taxon, e.g. "walleyes" and "perch".
Do not include surrounding determiners, quantifiers, or context words such as "some", "the", "subspecies of", or habitat/action words like "in deep water".
Do not extend the span to include taxonomic rank words unless they are part of the surface species name itself; in "a subspecies of Sharpe's greenbul", annotate only "Sharpe's greenbul".
Do not split contiguous multiword species names; annotate the whole name as one span.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate species names only when they denote an organism taxon or organism kind, not when the same word sequence is used as part of an organization, team, product, or other non-biological name.
Distinguish species from higher-level taxonomic groups: broad group labels such as "bacterial", "children", or general biological classes are not species unless a specific organism name is given.
Distinguish species from diseases, infections, and medical conditions: terms like "acute respiratory infections" or "ARIs" are conditions, not organisms, and should not be annotated as species.
Distinguish species from taxonomic-rank expressions: words such as "subspecies" indicate rank, while the actual species mention is the organism name that follows or precedes it.
Distinguish species from person names embedded in organism names: in "Fischer's greenbul", the full phrase is a species name, not a person entity.
Distinguish species from generic prey/food references only by lexical identity: if the word itself names an organism taxon, such as "perch", annotate it even when mentioned as food or prey.

specific.definition: A specific technical, material, functional, or conceptual entity expressed as a common-noun term or noun phrase, referring to a distinct item, substance, component, mechanism, or named domain concept rather than a person, place, organization, or purely generic category.
include_rules: Annotate common-noun phrases that denote a distinct technical or domain-specific artifact, component, material, mechanism, or concept.
Annotate names of physical components or devices, including pluralized technical object terms, e.g. "circuit breakers" and "bimetal strip".
Annotate names of substances, fuels, or materials when the phrase identifies a specific kind, e.g. "liquid propane gas" and "natural gas".
Annotate noun phrases naming a specific functional concept or protection/operation concept when used as a term, e.g. "thermal protection".
Annotate named conceptual areas or subfields expressed as common nouns when they refer to a recognizable concept, e.g. "informal learning".
Include modifiers that are necessary to identify the exact subtype or formulation of the entity.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate proper names of people, organizations, political parties, places, institutions, offices, or documents.

Do not annotate generic category nouns or underspecified references such as "types", "devices", "area", "investigation", "deployments", or similar broad class words unless the phrase itself names a distinct specific concept.
Do not annotate long descriptive clauses, explanations, or full technical statements; only annotate the specific term within them if one is present.
Do not annotate verbs, adjectival descriptions, or action/process phrases unless they function as a noun phrase naming a specific concept.
Do not annotate context words that merely introduce or modify the entity without being part of its name, such as determiners, quantifiers, discourse markers, or purpose phrases.

Do not annotate highly general technical vocabulary in the negative example style, such as broad rendering-pipeline discussion terms, when they refer to system operations or abstract discussion rather than a discrete specific entity term.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the specific item, substance, component, protection mechanism, or named concept, including essential modifiers that make it specific, as in "bimetal strip", "thermal protection", "liquid propane gas", and "informal learning".

Include descriptive adjectives or material/state words when they are part of the established term or distinguish the specific concept from a broader class, e.g. include "liquid" in "liquid propane gas" and "natural" in "natural gas".
Include plural morphology when the entity is expressed in plural form, e.g. "circuit breakers" rather than "circuit breaker" if the text uses the plural.
Do not include surrounding function words, determiners, or appositive framing outside the term, such as "Some types of", "an area called", or "to provide".
When the term appears after a naming cue like "called", annotate only the named concept itself, e.g. in "an area called informal learning", annotate only "informal learning".
Do not extend the span to include broader explanatory context, purposes, or relational phrases unless they are part of the fixed term itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate domain-specific common-noun concepts even when they are not proper names; this type covers terms like devices, materials, and conceptual subfields, unlike entity types limited to named people, organizations, or places.

Do not annotate person names, political parties, institutions, offices, or locations; the negative example shows that proper names such as politicians, councils, parties, and parliaments are outside this type.

Do not annotate generic technical process descriptions just because they are specialized vocabulary. Only mark a phrase when it denotes a discrete specific concept or item, not a whole explanatory passage or a broad process description.

Distinguish this type from general category words: annotate "natural gas" or "bimetal strip" as specific terms, but not higher-level labels like "devices", "types", "area", or "lighting values" when they are used generically.

If a phrase denotes a function or protection method as a recognized concept, as in "thermal protection", annotate it; if it merely states what something does in context without forming a concept term, do not annotate it.

This type is semantic rather than syntactic: a common noun phrase can qualify if it refers to a specific technical/material/conceptual entity, while many noun phrases in descriptive text should still be excluded if they are only generic references.

sport.definition: A named sport, athletic discipline, sporting event category, or sport-denoting facility expression referring to a specific type of sport or competitive athletic activity.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of sports, such as "basketball" and "tennis".
Annotate specific sport disciplines or event categories, including specialized variants within a sport, such as "women's double sculls".
Annotate sport expressions that include category modifiers like gender, style, or format when those modifiers are part of the recognized sport/event name.
Annotate sport-related facility expressions when the context uses them as the available sport activity rather than merely as a place, as in "golf course" alongside playable amenities.
Annotate singular or multiword sport names whenever they denote a type of athletic activity rather than an organization or competition.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-sport text, including medical, scientific, legal, software, or document formatting content.
Do not annotate dates, measurements, rankings, awards, titles, or competition outcomes even when they appear near a sport mention.
Do not annotate organization names, team names, mascots, conferences, leagues, or national/college programs.
Do not annotate broad event containers or competitions such as the Olympics unless the mention itself is a sport discipline.
Do not annotate generic nouns like "event", "game", "match", or "title" when they do not by themselves name a sport.
Do not annotate equipment, venues, or facilities unless the expression is used to denote the sport itself in context.
Do not annotate bodily conditions, injuries, or medical terms associated with sports.
boundary_rules: Mark the smallest contiguous span that names the sport, discipline, event, or sport facility expression used as the sport mention.
Include modifiers that are part of the official event or discipline name, such as gender/category words and equipment/style terms: "women's double sculls".
For simple sport names, annotate only the sport word itself: "basketball", "tennis".
When a facility term is used metonymically to denote a sport activity, include the full expression if that is the natural mention in context: "golf course" in a recreational amenities listing.

Do not include surrounding prepositions, verbs, determiners, distances, dates, competition names, or organizations, e.g. exclude words outside "women's double sculls" in "competed in the women's double sculls event at the 2004 Summer Olympics".
If a generic head noun follows the sport name, exclude it unless it is required for the sport expression; for example annotate "women's double sculls" rather than "women's double sculls event".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate teams, clubs, or school athletic programs as sport; only the sport itself is annotated. In "Arkansas Razorbacks" the team name is not a sport, while "basketball" is.
Do not annotate competitions, tournaments, leagues, divisions, or titles as sport. "SEC West title" and "2004 Summer Olympics" are competitions/awards contexts, not the sport mention.
Do not annotate roles or participants such as athletes, coaches, or groups of competitors.
Distinguish sport from general physical actions: only annotate named sports or recognized sport disciplines/events, not generic verbs like "play" or "competed".

Distinguish sport from locations/facilities: annotate a facility term only when it stands for the sport activity in context, as with "golf course" in an amenities list; otherwise a venue by itself is not automatically a sport mention.
Do not annotate unrelated technical, medical, or document text that happens to contain ambiguous words; only clear sport meanings qualify.

sports event.definition: A named organized sports competition, tournament, championship, or finals event, including specific editions of recurring competitions and named stages/titles of major competitions.
include_rules: Annotate named sports competitions such as tournaments, cups, championships, olympics, and finals.
Annotate specific editions of recurring sports events when identified by year or equivalent edition marker, e.g. "1984 Summer Olympics," "2007 Rugby World Cup."
Annotate named championship rounds or title events when they function as the event name, e.g. "NBA Finals."
Annotate both international and domestic sports events as long as the mention names a particular competition or event title.
Annotate event names whether they are one-off events or recurring competitions, provided the mention refers to a concrete sports event rather than a general concept.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic references to sports competitions that are not named events, such as "the tournament," "the finals," or "the world cup" when used non-specifically.
Do not annotate sport names, leagues, or organizations by themselves unless the phrase denotes the event itself, e.g. "NBA" alone is not a sports event.
Do not annotate teams, clubs, national squads, players, or other participants in the event.
Do not annotate venues, host cities/countries, seasons, or date expressions unless they are part of the full event name.
Do not annotate non-sports uses of words like "finals," "cup," or other terms that may appear outside sports contexts.
Do not annotate broad institutional concepts such as the Olympic movement or a federation when the text does not refer to a specific competition event.
boundary_rules: Include the full official or commonly used event name as it appears in text, including sport/league/competition terms such as "Summer Olympics," "Rugby World Cup," and "NBA Finals."
Include edition identifiers that are part of the event name, such as year markers: "1984 Summer Olympics," "2007 Rugby World Cup."

Do not include surrounding prepositions, articles, or temporal/context words that are not part of the name, e.g. annotate "NBA Finals" not "the NBA Finals" unless the article is part of a fixed title.
Do not extend the span to include participant names, results, venues, or descriptive clauses, e.g. in "lost to Washington in the NBA Finals," annotate only "NBA Finals."
When a recurring event is mentioned without a year but still names a specific competition stage or title, annotate the named event span as given, e.g. "NBA Finals."
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a sports event with sports teams or national sides; "Azzurri" is a team nickname, while "2007 Rugby World Cup" is the competition event.

Do not confuse a sports event with a sports organization or league as an institution. Annotate the event occurrence or championship title ("NBA Finals"), not the governing body or league name by itself unless it is used as the event name.

Do not confuse a sports event with a generic sport or discipline. "Rugby" alone or "Olympics" used generically for the movement is not enough unless it refers to a named competition edition or event title.
Do not confuse a sports event with dates or time expressions. A year alone such as "1984" is not a sports event; it is included only when part of the event name.

Do not confuse a sports event with non-sports events or technical/medical uses of similar words. Terms like "ultrasonic" or other domain-specific expressions are outside this type even if they contain words that resemble event terminology.

sports league.definition: An organized named sports league, series, or competition structure in which teams or competitors participate on an ongoing basis, such as a professional or recognized league name.
include_rules: Annotate specific named sports leagues or series, including professional, national, regional, or recognized competition-league names.
Include league names even when they appear as modifiers before words like "side", "team", or "season", as long as the span refers to the league itself.
Include established league/series names that identify the organized competition for teams or individual competitors, including motorsport series such as "Formula One".
Annotate the league name whether it appears alone or inside a larger noun phrase, provided the league name is explicitly stated.
Include names containing words such as "League" or conventional series names even if they do not literally contain the word "league", when they clearly denote a sports league or series.
exclude_rules: Exclude generic references to leagues or competitions when no specific named league is given, such as "the league", "a racing series", or "teams and circuits".
Exclude names of sports teams, clubs, reserve sides, or franchises that participate in a league.
Exclude names of specific seasons, tournaments, matches, championships, or one-off events when the mention is not the league name itself.
Exclude sport names used generically rather than as league names.
Exclude country adjectives, temporal markers, and participant-role words that merely modify the league mention rather than belong to its name.
Exclude all non-sports entities, including films, political parties, religious processions/events, locations, and persons.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full official or conventional league name as a single span, including distinguishing sport/level words that are part of the name, e.g. "Formula One", "Jia-A League", "United Soccer League".
Include organizational designators when they are part of the league name, such as "League" in "Jia-A League" and "United Soccer League".
Do not include surrounding role nouns or descriptors that are not part of the league name, such as "season", "side", "team", "club", or "circuits".
Do not include geographic or organizational modifiers outside the name itself; for example, in "Chinese Jia-A League side", annotate only "Jia-A League".
If the league name appears inside a longer event expression, annotate only the league portion unless the entire expression is itself the established league name.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a sports league with a sports team or club. In phrases like "United Soccer League side Portland Timbers 2", annotate "United Soccer League" as the league, not the team name.

Do not confuse a sports league with a competition season or edition. In "1994 Formula One season", annotate only "Formula One"; the year and "season" refer to a specific season, not the league itself.
Do not confuse a sports league with a sport in the generic sense. Annotate "Formula One" as the organized racing league/series name, but not generic sport words when they are not naming a league.
Do not confuse a sports league with governing bodies, federations, or associations unless the text is clearly using the name to denote the league itself.
Do not annotate non-sports organizations, political parties, films, religious events, or place names even if they contain words like "league" or appear in organization-like contexts.

sports team.definition: A named athletic team or club that competes in sports, including professional, amateur, collegiate, minor-league, or other formally identified teams.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of specific sports teams identified by a unique team name, including city/location plus nickname, e.g., "Seattle Mariners".
Annotate shortened but still specific team names and nicknames when they clearly refer to a team, e.g., "The Dash" and "Potomac Nats".
Annotate team mentions regardless of sport if the mention denotes the competing team entity rather than the league or event.
Annotate both full official names and commonly used abbreviated team names when they function as the name of a specific sports team in context.
Annotate each distinct team mention separately when multiple teams appear in the same sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate sport names, league names, governing bodies, tournaments, seasons, or championships by themselves.
Do not annotate generic references to teams or clubs when no specific team name is given, such as "the team" or "the club".
Do not annotate cities, regions, or venues alone unless they are part of the team name actually mentioned.
Do not annotate organizations, companies, copyright holders, products, or institutions that are not sports teams.
Do not annotate people, fan groups, age groups, nationalities, or demographic groups.
Do not annotate abbreviations or acronyms that refer to leagues or sports rather than a specific team.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full team name as it appears in context, including location/city and mascot/nickname when both are part of the name, e.g., "Seattle Mariners", "Los Angeles Dodgers", "San Diego Chargers".
Include a leading article only when it is used as part of the referenced team mention in the sentence, e.g., "The Dash".
For shortened or nickname forms, annotate only the words actually used to refer to the team, e.g., "Potomac Nats".

Do not include surrounding league names, sport names, competition names, or explanatory abbreviations, e.g., in "Major League Baseball (MLB) for the Seattle Mariners", annotate only "Seattle Mariners".

Do not include adjacent scores, record information, possessive markers outside the name, or descriptive context such as "action", "championship", or "league" unless they are part of the official team mention.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a sports team with a sports league, tournament, or competition. "Major League Baseball" and "Carolina league championship" are competitions/leagues, not teams.

Do not confuse a sports team with a geographic place. In team names such as "Seattle Mariners" or "San Diego Chargers", the location word is included because it is part of the team name, not because the place alone is being annotated.
Do not confuse a sports team with an organization or company unrelated to athletic competition, such as "LocalNerve LLC".
Do not annotate demonyms, population groups, or biological groups such as "Taiwanese", "males", or "females"; these are not teams even if they appear in plural form.
Do not annotate sport names or generic group references like "baseball team", "the club", or "the team" unless they clearly function as a specific team name in context.

stage.definition: A named stage or stage category indicating a position within a medical, biological, developmental, or lifecycle progression, including formal clinical stage labels and biologically defined stage groupings.
include_rules: Annotate formal clinical stage expressions used to classify disease extent or status, including forms like "Stage I", "Stage II", "Stage III", "Stage IV", and lowercase variants such as "stage IV".
Annotate stage expressions that include a named staging system or classifier before the word "Stage", e.g. "Brodeur Stage I".
Annotate stage labels with letter or subtype suffixes when they are part of the designation, e.g. "stage IB".
Annotate biological or parasitological lifecycle stage terms when they refer to a defined class of stages, e.g. "parasitic stages".
Annotate both singular and plural forms when they denote recognized stage categories rather than incidental wording.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate unrelated words that happen to resemble stage terminology or occur in non-staging contexts.
Do not annotate generic mentions of progression without an actual stage expression, such as vague references to severity, advancement, or phases with no named stage label.
Do not annotate counts, outcomes, or statistics associated with a stage, such as survival percentages or number of patients.
Do not annotate verbs or nouns meaning performative or interface stage-like actions unless they denote an actual progression stage.
Do not annotate context text where no medical, biological, or developmental stage is being identified.
boundary_rules: Include the full stage expression, starting with any attached stage system or modifier and ending with the stage label or numeral, e.g. "Brodeur Stage I", "stage IB", "Stage IV".
Include Roman numerals, Arabic numerals, and letter suffixes that are part of the stage designation, e.g. "I", "II", "IV", "IB".
For biological or developmental references, include the adjective or noun directly modifying "stage"/"stages" when it names the type of stage, e.g. "parasitic stages".
Do not include surrounding prepositions, verbs, percentages, or patient-count language, e.g. annotate "stage IB" not "in stage IB", and "Stage II" not "four in Stage II".
Do not extend the span to include the disease, organism, or other entity unless it is syntactically part of the stage name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate stage designations themselves, not the disease, condition, or organism being staged.
Do not annotate general temporal sequence words such as "new town", "back button", or other uses of ordinary words that are unrelated to medical, biological, or lifecycle staging.
Differentiate stage from grades, scores, classes, or versions: only annotate expressions that explicitly denote a stage/state in a recognized progression or lifecycle.
When "stage" is part of a broader clinical phrase, annotate only the stage component rather than the entire clinical statement.
Plural mentions can be annotated when they refer to a meaningful class of lifecycle/developmental stages, as in "parasitic stages", not when "stages" is merely generic or metaphorical.

standard.definition: A named or explicitly referenced technical, procedural, formatting, calibration, or interoperability standard, specification, or standards class used as a normative reference or convention.
include_rules: Annotate formal names of standards or specifications, including issuing body plus document identifier, e.g., "ASTM Specification E960".
Annotate recognized standards acronyms when they are used to denote the standard itself, e.g., "DICOM".
Annotate explicit references to classes or categories of standards when the phrase denotes a meaningful standards grouping, e.g., "animal-specific standards".
Annotate standards mentioned as normative sources, calibration references, encoding formats, service protocols, or compliance targets.
Annotate both full names and abbreviated names if each independently refers to the standard in context.
exclude_rules: Exclude generic mentions of "standard" or "standards" when they do not identify a specific named standard or a clearly delimited standards class.
Exclude disease classifications, syndrome types, and other non-normative uses of words like "type", "specification", or alphanumeric codes in biomedical text.
Exclude methods, algorithms, or workflows unless they are explicitly presented as standards or specifications.
Exclude software products, toolkits, services, and implementations that merely use or support a standard rather than being the standard itself.
Exclude regulations, requirements, or recommendations unless the span explicitly names the underlying standard or specification.
Exclude organization names by themselves when they are not part of the cited standard title.
boundary_rules: Include the full name of the standard or specification, including the issuing body and identifier when present, e.g., "ASTM Specification E960".
Include adjectival or descriptive standard phrases only when they explicitly denote a class of standards, e.g., "animal-specific standards".
Include standalone acronyms only when they refer to a recognized standard or standards framework in context, e.g., "DICOM" in technical protocol or service context.
Exclude surrounding functional or descriptive words that are not part of the standard name, such as "Designed from", "Type I requirements", "toolkit", "services", or "for calibration".
Do not extend the span to include punctuation or conjunctions unless they are internal to the standard name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate disease subtype labels, clinical types, or syndrome names containing words like "type" or codes, such as "Autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome type 2"; these are medical conditions, not standards.
Do not annotate procedural descriptions like "This method" or general uses of "method" unless the phrase itself names a standard.

Distinguish standards from software or tools: annotate "DICOM" when it denotes the interoperability standard, but not accompanying non-standard artifacts like "toolkit" unless the toolkit name itself is the standard.
Do not annotate numeric thresholds, requirements classes, or implementation variants unless they are part of the formal standard name.

Distinguish standards from organizations: if an organization name appears only as an institution and not as part of a standard title, do not annotate it; if it is embedded in the official standard name, include it.

state.definition: A mention of a state, either as a named geopolitical state-level region or as an explicitly expressed condition/status phrase describing the state of an entity.
include_rules: Annotate full names of specific geopolitical states when they identify a U.S. state or equivalent state-level region, such as "Missouri" and "Delaware".
