Annotate state names that appear inside larger organization or location strings, as long as the state word itself is explicitly present.
Annotate phrases that express a condition or status state of a medical or other entity when the wording directly states that status, e.g. "positive rheumatoid factor".
Include adjectival or polarity modifiers that are essential to the expressed state, such as "positive" in a test/status phrase.
Annotate the state mention wherever it appears in running text, lists, headings, or institutional names, provided it directly denotes the target state concept.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic uses of the word "state" that do not name a specific state or express a target condition, e.g. "State drug violation".
Do not annotate countries, cities, counties, districts, courts, or other administrative or organizational entities unless the span specifically names the state portion.
Do not annotate software, class, model, namespace, or UI terms such as "View Data Class" or code identifiers.
Do not annotate legal case metadata, dates, sentence terms, or procedural statuses unless they are actual state mentions under this type.
Do not annotate standalone medical entities, biomarkers, or diagnoses when no state/status expression is present; the state mention is the condition phrase, not the base noun alone.
Do not annotate demonyms, abbreviations, or regional references unless they unambiguously function as the state mention in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the words that express the state itself, not surrounding institution or person names: in "Missouri Court of Appeals" annotate only "Missouri".
For geographic U.S. states, include the full state name as written; do not include preceding city names or following punctuation, as in "Wilmington, Delaware" -> "Delaware" only.

For condition/state expressions, include necessary modifiers that are part of the expressed state, such as polarity or status adjectives: annotate "positive rheumatoid factor" as a whole, not only "rheumatoid factor".
Do not extend the span to include generic legal or administrative words like "State" when they do not form a specific state name.
Exclude commas, periods, court designators, and other trailing context unless they are part of the state name itself.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish state from city or county locations: annotate the state-level unit ("Delaware"), not nearby lower-level places such as "Wilmington" or "Allegheny County".
Distinguish state from organizations or institutions: in names like "Missouri Court of Appeals," annotate only the state reference, not the court name.

Distinguish state from generic governmental usage of "State": a common noun use such as "a State drug violation" is not a state entity because it does not identify a specific state or condition-state mention intended by this type.

Distinguish state from diseases, tests, or substances: when a phrase denotes a status or condition, annotate it only if the full phrase expresses the state itself, as in "positive rheumatoid factor"; do not separately annotate the underlying medical term if the target is the status expression.
Distinguish state from temporal or legal status words such as "probation," "sentenced," or "expired" unless the phrase itself denotes the target state category exemplified by the positive examples.

statistic.definition: A term that explicitly names a statistical measure, summary metric, or statistical property of data, rather than the raw value, measured variable, or general quantity.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun-like terms that name statistical properties or summary measures of data, such as frequency, central tendency, variation, or between-group/data-pattern characteristics.
Include common statistic terms such as "mean", "prevalence", and "heterogeneity" when they refer to a statistical concept in context.
Annotate statistic terms whether they appear in running text, titles, headings, or short nominal phrases.
Annotate the statistic term even when no numeric value is given, as long as it denotes a statistical property or measure.
Annotate the statistic term when it is followed by an "of" phrase or other complement describing what is being measured, but limit the span to the statistic term itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate standalone numeric expressions, even when they report a result, if no statistical measure term is present.
Do not annotate raw counts, amounts, quantities, years, dates, page numbers, monetary amounts, case citations, or identifiers.
Do not annotate the variable, population, or event being measured, such as "time to union" in "The mean time to union was 5.4 months."
Do not annotate general adjectives or descriptive words unless they are part of the statistic term itself; for example, annotate "heterogeneity" but not merely "substantial".
Do not annotate broad methodological or study-design terms that are not themselves statistical measures or properties.
Do not annotate units, ranges, intervals, or parenthetical value expressions that accompany a statistic unless the statistic term itself is present and separately annotated.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span naming the statistical concept itself, usually a single noun such as "heterogeneity", "prevalence", or "mean".

Exclude surrounding measured variables, populations, outcomes, and complements. For example, in phrases like "prevalence of X" or "mean time to union", annotate only "prevalence" or "mean", not the full noun phrase.
Exclude numeric values, ranges, percentages, counts, units, and time expressions that may accompany the statistic, such as "5.4", "4-6", "%", "months", or similar value-bearing material.
If modifiers merely specify what kind of statistic it is and are part of its fixed name, include them only when necessary to preserve the statistic term; otherwise prefer the core statistic word.
Do not extend the span to include verbs or clauses describing the statistic, such as "was 5.4" or "showed substantial heterogeneity"; annotate only the statistic noun/term.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish statistic from raw numbers or measured values: annotate the summary measure term ("mean", "prevalence"), not the numerical result associated with it.

Distinguish statistic from quantities, counts, dates, years, money, and legal citations. Numbers like "48", "1,152", "$500", or "280" are not statistics unless they explicitly function as named statistical measures.
Distinguish statistic from the underlying variable or outcome being summarized. In "mean time to union", "time to union" is the variable; only "mean" is the statistic.

Distinguish statistic from general scientific or descriptive terminology. Annotate terms that denote statistical properties or summary measures, such as distributional or frequency concepts, not ordinary domain nouns.

Distinguish statistic from study actions or methods: words referring to analysis procedures, experiments, or clinical events are not statistics unless they directly name a statistical measure or statistical property.

status.definition: A word or phrase that denotes the current condition, standing, visibility, urgency level, or resultant state of an entity, object, or situation.
include_rules: Annotate words or phrases that explicitly state the condition or state of an entity, object, or situation.
Annotate visibility or availability states assigned to items, such as "hidden."
Annotate resultant states in passive or predicative constructions, such as something being "engulfed" or "dispelled."
Annotate named status categories or labels, including domain-specific labels in parentheses or as category names, such as "urgency" when it denotes the status category/value being referenced.
Annotate status mentions whether they appear after linking verbs, in appositive/category notation, or after markers like "marked as" or "considered."
exclude_rules: Do not annotate words that describe actions, processes, or events without naming a resulting state.
Do not annotate generic administrative or discourse language such as instructions, organization-related wording, or forum-management terms unless they explicitly function as a status value.
Do not annotate software code, class names, method names, validation logic, or technical labels that are not state values of a described entity.
Do not annotate the noun "status" by itself when it only introduces a field or concept and does not itself provide the status value.
Do not annotate surrounding entities, causes, agents, or time expressions associated with a status mention.
Do not annotate evaluative or emotional words like "excited" when they are not being used as a formal or contextual state/status label of the target entity in the annotation setting.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that expresses the status value itself, usually a single adjective, participle, or noun used as a state label.

Include the status term even when it appears after verbs such as "marked," "considered," "was," or similar copular/passive constructions; exclude those surrounding function words unless they are part of a fixed multiword state expression.

For constructions like "status was I (urgency)," annotate only the lexical label naming the status category if that is the intended status mention; do not include the word "status" itself or surrounding numerals unless the numeral is itself the only status label used.

When a status is expressed as a multiword phrase, include all words necessary to represent that single state and exclude trailing punctuation, determiners, and explanatory material not part of the label.

Do not extend the span to include the entity being described, the action causing the state, or temporal/modal context such as "until," "provided," or negation markers unless they are part of the fixed status phrase.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate events or actions; annotate the resulting condition only. In "considered engulfed until ORS is dispelled," "engulfed" and "dispelled" are states/statuses, not the swallowing or dispelling events themselves.

Do not annotate generic process, organizational, or discussion-control words that merely describe how something should be handled, such as "organized" in forum instructions, unless they explicitly denote the status of a target item.

Do not annotate technical class names, validator names, or programming identifiers that refer to software objects or constraints rather than a real-world state, such as code-related terms in source files.

Distinguish status from attributes or categories: annotate a label when it denotes the current condition or visibility/state of an item, such as "hidden," but not when a word only names a type, domain, or topic without asserting state.

If a term could be read as either a property name or a status value, annotate it only when context shows it is the value assigned to an entity's current condition, e.g., "marked as hidden" or "considered engulfed."

store.definition: A named retail store, shop brand/outlet, or named technical repository referred to as a store, where the mention identifies the specific store rather than the generic word "store" itself.
include_rules: Annotate specific proper names of retail stores or shop brands mentioned as places where goods are purchased, e.g. "Charlotte Russe" and "Wal-mart".
Annotate a named entity that functions as a technical store or repository when the context explicitly identifies it as a store, e.g. "ADAM" in "the ADAM store".
Include named stores whether they appear with or without a generic head noun like "store".
Include store names in consumer, shopping, or product-acquisition contexts when the name identifies the seller/source.
Include store-like names in computing or access-control contexts when the name identifies the specific storage repository being accessed or referenced.
exclude_rules: Exclude generic mentions of "store", "shop", "outlet", or "repository" when no specific store name is given.
Exclude common nouns and unrelated words that are orthographically similar or appear in unrelated contexts, such as "surgery", "Wall Street", or "border wall".
Exclude descriptive phrases for stores that do not contain a proper or specific name, such as "the local store" or "a grocery store".
Exclude surrounding business descriptors or category words when they are not part of the store name itself, such as the trailing word "store" in "Charlotte Russe store".
Exclude names of organizations, products, systems, or databases when the context does not indicate they are being referred to as a store or repository.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the name of the store or store-like repository, not the following generic head noun when it is merely descriptive, e.g. annotate "Charlotte Russe" in "Charlotte Russe store" and exclude "store".
Include the full canonical name as written when it is part of the store name, including internal punctuation or hyphenation, e.g. "Wal-mart".
When a technical store is expressed as "the X store", annotate only "X" if "store" is a generic common noun naming the type of repository, e.g. "ADAM" in "the ADAM store".
Do not extend the span to surrounding determiners, prepositions, or modifiers that are not part of the store name, such as "the", "both", or descriptive adjectives.
If the store mention is already a standalone proper name without a trailing generic noun, annotate the entire proper name and nothing more.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate generic common nouns like "store" when they refer to a retail outlet or repository type without a specific name; only the specific named store is a store entity.

Distinguish a store from a person, place, or organization mention by its role in context: annotate when the name denotes a retail outlet/brand used as a shopping source or a named data/authorization repository used for storage/access.
If a name appears in a technical context and refers to a named repository rather than a software product broadly, annotate it as store, as in "ADAM" in "ADAM store".
Do not annotate physical objects, medical instruments, historical terms, or other non-store nouns that happen to appear near the string "store" or in unrelated text.
Do not annotate financial-market expressions, locations, or institutions unless they specifically denote a named store or repository in the sentence context.

strain.definition: A strain is a specific named biological lineage, isolate, inbred line, or subtype within a species or broader organism group, identified by a proper name or alphanumeric designation.
include_rules: Annotate specific names of biological strains, inbred lines, isolates, or subtypes of organisms.
Annotate short proper-name strain labels used for laboratory animals, such as rat or mouse line names like 'Lewis' and 'AVN'.
Annotate alphanumeric strain identifiers used for microbial or other biological isolates, such as 'AL662T'.
Annotate named subtype labels used as biological strain/cultivar categories in context, such as 'Sativa' and 'Indica'.
Annotate the strain name when it appears with contextual trigger words like 'strain', 'inbred strain', or similar lineage-introducing phrases, but label only the name itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic words that merely indicate the concept of strain, such as 'strain', 'inbred strain', or 'Hybrid', without a specific strain name.
Do not annotate higher-level taxonomic names such as genus names, species names, or other broad organism categories unless they are part of a true strain designation.
Do not annotate non-biological uses of similar words or identifiers, including software/system terms like 'root' or organization names like 'Shasta'.
Do not annotate descriptive ratios, attributes, or comparisons such as '70/30 ratio', 'dominant', or 'greater in'.
Do not annotate institutions, teams, schools, leagues, places, or mascots even if they resemble proper names.
Do not annotate unnamed references to organisms when no distinct lineage or subtype label is given.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the specific strain designation, not surrounding category words such as 'strain', 'inbred strain', 'dominant Hybrid', 'gene', or 'genus'.
When a strain name appears after a cue word like 'strain', include only the identifier itself, e.g. annotate 'AL662T' in 'strain AL662T'.
For animal or plant subtype names used as strain/cultivar-like labels, annotate just the subtype term, e.g. 'Lewis', 'AVN', 'Sativa', 'Indica'.
Do not extend the span to include species names, higher taxonomic ranks, or descriptive modifiers unless they are part of the strain name itself.
Preserve alphanumeric and letter-number suffixes that are part of the strain identifier, including terminal letters such as 'T' in 'AL662T'.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate species, genus, or other taxonomic ranks unless the mention specifically refers to a strain-level designation. In 'belongs to the genus Thermococcus', 'Thermococcus' is not a strain.
Do not annotate breed, variety, or general category terms when they are used generically rather than as a specific strain label.
Do not annotate organization, league, school, team, software, operating-system, or user-account names; for example, 'root' in the negative example is a system account, not a biological strain.
A strain is a specific biological lineage, isolate, inbred line, or named subtype within a broader organism group. Annotate the lineage label itself, not the broader organism or context around it.
If a term could describe a general class or a specific subtype, annotate it only when the sentence uses it as a biological subtype/strain name, as with 'Sativa' and 'Indica' in the cannabis context.

street.definition: A named street-level roadway or thoroughfare, such as a street or road, identified by a specific proper name.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of specifically named streets and roads that denote a roadway/thoroughfare.
Include proper names followed by a street-type designator such as "Street" or "Road" when they function as the name of a roadway, e.g. "Scott Street", "Frances Hill Road".
Annotate each distinct street name separately when multiple streets are mentioned in the same context, such as an intersection.
Include street names that appear inside a larger address expression, but annotate only the street-name portion.
Include street names used as location references for where something is situated, served, or located, as long as the mention denotes the roadway itself.
Include historical or current street names equally, provided they identify a specific named thoroughfare.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic common nouns for roadways when they are not part of a specific proper name, such as isolated uses of "street", "road", or similar terms.
Do not annotate full addresses including house numbers, unit numbers, postal codes, cities, or states; only the named street portion qualifies.
Do not annotate names of facilities, landmarks, or properties that merely contain a street name as part of their own title, unless the street itself is explicitly being referred to.
Do not annotate non-road uses of similar words, including mechanical or technical terms such as "X-Y stage" and "Z stage".
Do not annotate broad location descriptions like neighborhoods or wards, even when they occur in the same phrase as a street mention.
Do not annotate unnamed intersections or vague references such as "the road", "the street", or "at the corner" without a specific street name.
boundary_rules: Annotate the street-name span itself, including the full specific name and road-type term when present, e.g. "Scott Street", "Elgin Street", "Frances Hill Road", "Market Street".
Exclude street numbers, building numbers, and other address components that precede the street name, e.g. annotate "Frances Hill Road" but not "16 Frances Hill Road".
Exclude surrounding prepositions and locative phrases such as "on", "at", "in", and trailing area names such as neighborhoods, cities, or states.

If a larger place or facility name contains a street name used only to name that facility, annotate only the street portion when it clearly denotes the street, e.g. in "Market Street Burial Ground", annotate "Market Street" not the full facility name.
Do not extend the span to intersections or coordinated phrases; annotate each street separately in expressions like "Scott Street at Elgin Street".
Include directional or descriptive words only when they are part of the official street name; otherwise exclude contextual direction words.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate buildings, houses, cemeteries, districts, stations, or other facilities named after a street unless the mention itself denotes the street; for example, "Market Street Burial Ground" is a facility name, while only "Market Street" is the street entity.
Do not annotate cities, neighborhoods, wards, states, or other administrative/place names that may appear in an address context; only the street-level thoroughfare is the target.
Do not annotate full postal addresses as a single span; extract only the street component from an address mention.

Do not confuse the target with generic engineering or geometric uses of "stage" or letter-direction terms such as "X-Y stage" or "Z stage"; these are not streets because they do not denote named roadways.
Do not annotate transportation lines or routes unless the mention is a named street; for example, a transit line mentioned near a street is not itself a street entity.

string.definition: A textual string mention: a specific sequence of characters, symbol/variable string, quoted literal text, or named technical term that is being referred to as a string-like textual item in discourse.
include_rules: Annotate explicit mentions of string literals or textual content being referred to directly, including quoted or unquoted examples such as "foo".
Annotate single-character or symbolic string names when they denote a specific textual/symbolic item, such as a variable/string label like "s".
Annotate multiword technical terms when the phrase itself is the named textual item under discussion, such as "magic methods".
Annotate the minimal phrase that identifies the string-like entity being discussed, especially in explanatory or metalinguistic contexts like "the text foo" or "documentation for the magic methods".

Include terms that are referred to as textual labels or names, even if they are not enclosed in quotation marks, as long as the mention clearly points to the string itself rather than a larger code structure.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate full URLs, URIs, HTTP headers, query strings, JSON/YAML content, log dumps, or other serialized text blocks.
Do not annotate programming-language syntax, full commands, or code snippets unless a specific substring is explicitly being named as the string of interest.
Do not annotate software objects or code entities such as classes, constructors, methods, exceptions, packages, or applications when they are referenced as program components rather than strings.
Do not annotate generic descriptive phrases that are not themselves specific named strings, such as "the documentation", "this code", or "the tutorial".
Do not annotate surrounding context words like "text", "string", "method", or "head" unless they are part of the actual string mention.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the exact token sequence that names the string, symbol, literal text, or technical term itself.
For single-letter or symbolic names, include just the symbol/letter used as the named string (e.g., "s"), without surrounding variables like "$" unless the notation makes the symbol inseparable.
For quoted literal text, annotate only the literal content if that is the named string (e.g., "foo"), not surrounding explanatory words such as "text" or the command syntax around it.
For multiword technical names, include the full established phrase when it functions as the named string/term (e.g., "magic methods").
Do not extend the span to include determiners, prepositions, verbs, or nearby descriptors unless they are part of the string itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate STRING when the mention is the content of text, a symbolic string/variable name, or a named technical term being referred to as a string-like item.
Do not annotate broader code expressions, commands, URLs, HTTP payloads, or source-code lines when they are shown as full program text rather than singled out as a specific string mention.
Do not annotate class names, method names, API names, or product/platform names unless the mention is explicitly the string content itself rather than the software entity.
Generic nouns like "documentation", "code", "constructor", or "tutorial" are not STRING unless a specific named text span is being cited.
Long serialized data, request parameters, logs, and configuration blobs should be excluded even though they contain many character strings; only short, explicitly referenced string mentions qualify.

struct.definition: A named program-defined structured type or class-like data type identifier, including explicit struct names and type-like object names referred to in code or code discussion.
include_rules: Annotate identifiers that denote user-defined or named structured data types in source code.
Annotate struct names when they appear in type annotations, return types, declarations, constructor-like expressions, or associated-item access.
Annotate class-like type identifiers even when they are single-letter names, if code syntax shows they are types.
Annotate a type-like identifier mentioned in surrounding explanatory text when the text refers to an object/temporary/value of that named type.
Annotate repeated mentions of the same struct name each time they appear as separate entity mentions.
Annotate names regardless of casing if context makes clear they are being used as struct/type names rather than ordinary variables.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary prose words, numbered list items, or generic technical terms that are not type identifiers.
Do not annotate infrastructure, configuration, deployment, network, image, stack, domain, path, or service names appearing outside programming-type semantics.
Do not annotate function names, method names, operators, macros, keywords, namespaces, modules, or field names unless the token itself is the struct name.
Do not annotate primitive, builtin, or standard syntax types solely because they appear in type position, e.g. "u8".
Do not annotate variable names, parameter names, argument names, or local bindings when they denote values rather than struct/type names.
Do not annotate punctuation, qualifiers, reference markers, array brackets, or lifetime markers attached to a type expression.
Do not annotate text where an uppercase or short token is not functioning as a type identifier in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the identifier token that names the struct, without surrounding type syntax, generics, references, pointers, array notation, lifetimes, namespaces, or function-call punctuation.
In declarations or signatures, include the full struct name exactly as written, e.g. annotate "BytesDeque" in "-> BytesDeque" and "Bytes" in "Bytes::copy_from_slice".
For single-letter struct names, annotate just the letter when it is used as a type or class-like object, e.g. "A" in "A &operator=(A &&)".
Do not extend the span to keywords such as "struct", "class", "pub", "fn", or to method/operator names such as "operator=".
When a constructor-like or associated-item expression uses the struct name followed by scope resolution or call syntax, annotate only the struct identifier, not the following "::..." or parentheses.
If a lowercase identifier is clearly being referred to as a struct/type name in technical discussion, annotate only that identifier, e.g. "foo" in "the lifetime of the foo temporary".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate struct names, not functions or methods. In "Bytes::copy_from_slice", annotate "Bytes" but not "copy_from_slice".
Annotate struct names, not variables or parameters used as values. For example, do not label "bytes" in "bytes: &[u8]".
Annotate the type/class-like identifier itself, not primitive or built-in types such as "u8".

Annotate the named composite/program-defined type, not module, package, service, container, network, or file names such as "traefik", "docker-compose.yml", or "traefik-net" when they are infrastructure labels rather than struct types.
Distinguish struct from generic technical nouns or ordinary prose tokens. Only annotate when the token denotes a concrete type/object definition or type-like entity in code or code discussion.

If an identifier could be read either as a variable or a struct, prefer struct only when the surrounding syntax or wording indicates a type/object identity, constructor/associated-item usage, or explicit discussion of a temporary/object of that named type.

structure.definition: A physical, built, material, or anatomical form or part that has a structural existence, including buildings, architectural elements, engineered supports, and body sites or parts.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that denote physical structures in the built environment, such as buildings and structural supports like columns.
Annotate anatomical structures or body sites when the phrase refers to a physical part or location in the body, such as "anatomical sites".
Annotate plural and singular mentions of structures when they refer to concrete structural entities rather than abstract categories.
Annotate both whole structures and named structural parts when they are explicitly mentioned as material or anatomical forms.
Annotate structure mentions even when they appear in technical, engineering, gaming, or medical contexts, as long as the referent is a physical or anatomical structure.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate abstract concepts, processes, methods, systems, inventions, or fields of study, even if they relate to physical objects.
Do not annotate people, professions, organizations, or groups of subjects.
Do not annotate diseases, pathologies, sounds, experiments, or other events and phenomena.
Do not annotate geographic or administrative locations unless the mention denotes a physical structure rather than a place name.
Do not annotate generic artifact names when the context is about the whole device or instrument rather than a structural component or structure type.
Do not annotate descriptive context around a structure mention, such as force, side, focus, or other relational/property words, unless those words are part of the structure name itself.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal noun phrase that names the structure, such as "buildings", "columns", or "anatomical sites".

Include modifiers only when they are part of the structural mention itself, as in "anatomical sites"; exclude surrounding descriptors that merely describe state, size, position, or quantity, such as "large/tall" in "large/tall buildings" or "tensed side of the columns" when only "columns" is the structure.
Exclude determiners and prepositions unless they are inseparable parts of the structural expression.
When a larger phrase contains both a structure and a non-structure region or property, annotate only the structural head if that is the true structure mention.
Use the exact span of the physical or anatomical structure term; do not extend the span to include actions, events, or explanatory context.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish structure from devices or instruments: annotate the physical structural component itself, not an entire artifact mentioned as an object of use unless the mention specifically refers to its structural aspect. For example, do not annotate musical instruments in the negative example.
Distinguish structure from locations: annotate built or anatomical structures like "buildings" or "anatomical sites", not cities or places such as "Megapolis" or "Paris".
Distinguish structure from body conditions or diseases: in medical text, annotate the anatomical part or site, not disorders, pathologies, or experiments.
Distinguish structure from methods, systems, and abstract concepts: do not annotate phrases naming inventions, enhancement systems, related art, means, methods, or scientific practices.

Distinguish structure from roles and participants: do not annotate people, organizations, or subject groups such as doctors, inventors, or "human subjects" unless the text names a body structure rather than a person or group.

study.definition: A mention of a scientific or clinical research investigation, either as a named study/project or as a study-design expression referring to a specific investigation or set of investigations.
include_rules: Annotate phrases that denote a research study, trial-like investigation, or epidemiologic/clinical investigation.
Annotate study-design noun phrases when they clearly refer to a research investigation, such as "pilot studies", "case-cohort study", and "cross-sectional study".
Annotate formally named studies or projects, especially capitalized names ending with or containing "Study", such as "Rotterdam Study".
Include both singular and plural study mentions when they refer to actual research investigations.
Annotate study mentions whether they refer to one specific study or multiple studies, as long as the phrase itself denotes research studies.
Annotate study mentions even when modified by methodological or scope descriptors, if the full phrase still names the investigation type.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate uses of "study" or related words that refer to education, learning, or the act of studying rather than research investigations.
Do not annotate non-research events or organized activities such as showcases, tournaments, practices, competitions, schedules, or meetings.
Do not annotate generic scientific activity terms like "research" or "investigation" unless they explicitly form a study mention matching a concrete investigation or study-design phrase.
Do not annotate surrounding population or setting descriptions by themselves, such as "population-based" or "prospective", unless they are part of the contiguous study mention.
Do not annotate institutions, departments, colleges, teams, websites, or organizations that co-occur with a study mention but are not themselves studies.
Do not annotate mentions of results, evidence, or findings without the study entity itself.
boundary_rules: Include the full study-design expression when the design adjective/modifier is part of the study name, e.g., "pilot studies", "case-cohort study", "cross-sectional study".
Include the complete formal study title when it is a named study, including a capitalized head noun such as "Study", e.g., "Rotterdam Study".
Do not extend the span to surrounding descriptive material that is not part of the study mention, such as "embedded in the prospective, population-based" around "case-cohort study".
Exclude determiners and reporting verbs unless they are inseparable parts of the named study mention; annotate "a case-cohort study" as "case-cohort study".
For plural mentions, include the pluralized study head if it refers to studies as research investigations, e.g., "pilot studies".
Do not include punctuation before or after the study mention unless it is internal to an official study title.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate research investigations or formally named studies, not the act of learning; "studied" in "She studied part-time" is not a study entity.
Do not annotate showcases, schedules, events, rosters, or competitions simply because they are organized activities; these are not research studies.
Distinguish study from general research-topic descriptions: annotate the investigation itself (e.g., "cross-sectional study"), not the disease, treatment, population, or outcome being investigated.
When a phrase names a specific project or cohort with "Study" as part of its official name, annotate it as study (e.g., "Rotterdam Study").
When a phrase describes the methodological design of an investigation without a separate proper name, annotate the full design phrase as study (e.g., "case-cohort study").

style.definition: An expression that denotes an aesthetic, visual, or formal manner of appearance, design, or presentation, including named looks, styling descriptions, and design categories.
include_rules: Annotate nouns or noun phrases that name a visual, aesthetic, or design style.
Annotate adjectival or mixed phrases that describe a recognizable style or look, including era-based or genre-based descriptions such as "retro" or "80's styling".
Annotate phrases headed by words like "look", "styling", "design", or similar when they refer to aesthetic presentation.
Annotate generic design-category terms when the context clearly treats them as the style itself, as in "designs" referring to the kind of look.
Annotate coordinated style descriptions when both modifiers jointly form the style expression, such as "odd, 80's styling".
Annotate style mentions whether they are formal labels or informal descriptive phrases.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate references to scientific, behavioral, epidemiological, or other non-aesthetic patterns, methods, variables, or models.
Do not annotate materials, shapes, components, or object parts unless the expression names a design/style category rather than the physical form itself.
Do not annotate generic descriptive adjectives that describe quality or age without denoting a style in context.
Do not annotate entire clauses or opinions about style; only the phrase naming the style should be marked.
Do not annotate actions or processes of styling/designing unless they are used metonymically to denote the resulting style.
Do not annotate broad contextual text around the style mention, such as evaluative language like "have a very" or reported speech outside the style phrase.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal contiguous span that expresses the style or stylistic category.
Include the head noun when it is part of the style expression, such as "styling", "look", or "designs" when those words denote an aesthetic style in context.
Include descriptive modifiers that specify the style, including coordinated adjectives or era markers, as in "odd, 80's styling" and "vintage look".

Do not extend the span to include determiners, intensifiers, or surrounding predicates unless they are part of the style phrase itself; for example, annotate "vintage look" not "a very retro and vintage look to them".
When a generic artifact noun is used to denote a stylistic design category in context, include that noun alone if no narrower style term is present, as in "designs".
Do not include trailing prepositional or comparative material that comments on the style rather than naming it.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate style expressions, not the physical object being described. For example, in "Marker line", the entity is the line type/style, not a concrete marker tool.

Do not confuse style with general attributes, behaviors, or scientific patterns. Expressions like "physical activity patterns" or methodological patterns are not aesthetic or formal styles and should be excluded.
Do not annotate domain categories, biological classes, or technical labels unless they explicitly denote a style or design aesthetic.

When a noun like "look", "design", or "styling" refers to visual or aesthetic manner, annotate it as style; when the same words refer to process, planning, or non-aesthetic organization, exclude them.
Era or genre words such as "retro" are style only when they describe an aesthetic mode; if they refer to time period alone without stylistic meaning, do not annotate.
Style spans may be broad category names rather than named proper nouns; do not require capitalization or formal titles.

subject.definition: A mention that denotes the matter or participant serving as the subject of discourse, especially a discourse participant pronoun, an academic discipline/field, or a named examination/topic being referred to.
include_rules: Annotate pronouns or noun phrases that refer to the discourse participant acting as the subject, such as "we".
Annotate named examinations or standardized test names when they are the subject matter being discussed, such as "MCAT" and "GRE".
Annotate academic disciplines, fields of study, or subject-matter terms when they denote what is being learned, taught, or discussed, such as "chemistry".
Annotate each distinct subject mention separately if multiple subjects appear in coordination or lists.
Include both common-noun subject names and acronym-based subject names when they clearly denote the topic/field/exam under discussion.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general activities, promotions, menu items, products, or entertainment phrases such as "Yoga", "Guacamole", or "Queso" when they are not treated as subject entities.
Do not annotate software names, file formats, or technical objects such as "Illustrator", "PDF", "artboard", or "rectangle" based on the provided evidence.
Do not annotate descriptive nouns, examples, or lists that are merely content inside an explanation, such as "elements", "earth", "wind", "water", and "air" in the example sentence.
Do not annotate verbs, actions, or goals surrounding the subject mention, such as "prepare", "transfer", or "export it as a PDF".
Do not annotate entire noun phrases when only the core subject term is the target; exclude modifiers unless they are part of the fixed name of the subject.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the subject itself: a single pronoun ("we"), a single discipline name ("chemistry"), or a single exam name/acronym ("MCAT", "GRE").

Do not include surrounding function words, determiners, auxiliaries, or punctuation. For example, annotate "we" not "We have", and "MCAT" not "the MCAT" unless the article is inseparable from the official name.
For coordinated subjects, annotate each subject mention separately when they are distinct items, e.g. "MCAT" and "GRE" as two entities rather than one combined span.
When the subject is expressed as a common noun naming a field or topic, include only the lexical item for the field/topic, not explanatory appositives or examples that follow it.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate event, activity, or product mentions just because they are topics of discussion; in the negative examples, "Yoga", "Frida Night", "PDF", and "Illustrator" are not treated as subject entities in this scheme.

Distinguish subject from organization or person references: annotate a pronoun like "we" only when it functions as the discourse subject/party being referred to, not because it names a specific organization or person.

Distinguish subject from object names used merely as software, file-format, venue, or brand references; exam names and academic disciplines are included as subjects, but general brand/tool names are not supported by the examples.

Distinguish subject from generic content words in descriptions. Only annotate terms that denote the matter, discipline, exam, or discourse participant under discussion, not every noun in the sentence.

substance.definition: A named material entity such as a chemical, biochemical, molecular compound, ligand, peptide, or other physical substance mentioned as matter or a molecular agent.
include_rules: Annotate names of chemicals and biomolecules, including endogenous compounds and laboratory-measured analytes, e.g. "cholesterol".
Annotate names of proteins, peptides, ligands, and related molecular substances, e.g. "fibrinogen", "ligands", "Peptides".
Annotate both specific compounds and broad substance classes when the term itself denotes a material entity.
Annotate substance mentions whether they occur in clinical, biological, pharmacological, or experimental context, as long as the mention refers to the material itself.
Annotate singular or plural substance names when they denote physical or molecular matter.
Annotate a substance mention even if it is not a drug and even if it is mentioned only as something measured, bound, activated, or used in treatment.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate diseases, injuries, symptoms, deficits, or other medical conditions.
Do not annotate procedures, methods, assays, trials, protocols, treatments, or routes of administration unless a specific material name appears within them.
Do not annotate doses, units, concentrations, time expressions, or numeric quantities by themselves.
Do not annotate general health, medicine, fitness, spirituality, nutrition, or business terms that do not denote a concrete material substance.
Do not annotate body parts, tissues, muscles, neurons, platelets, or other anatomical/cellular entities unless the mention is specifically a substance name.
Do not annotate brand-like study labels, scales, scores, abbreviations for assessments, or category names.
Do not annotate placebo or highly generic treatment placeholders such as "medical treatment" or "test-drug" unless a specific substance name is explicitly given.
boundary_rules: Mark the minimal span that names the substance itself, usually the head noun only: "cholesterol", "fibrinogen", "ligands", "Peptides".

Include modifiers only when they are part of the established substance name and necessary to identify the material; do not automatically include contextual words such as "serum", "levels", "activation", or "binding".
Exclude measurement, dose, quantity, concentration, and administration details from the span, e.g. do not include values or units around a substance mention.
Exclude surrounding experimental or clinical descriptors such as "test-drug", "placebo-controlled", "intravenously", or "method".
When a coordinated list contains multiple substance names, annotate each substance separately if each names a distinct material.
Do not extend the span to include trailing generic event or role words unless they are part of the substance mention itself.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate a term as substance when it denotes a chemical, biochemical, molecular, or material entity; do not annotate when the term denotes a process, action, assay, treatment regimen, or clinical intervention.
Do not label anatomical or biological structures, cells, tissues, injuries, or body parts as substance; only the material involved is included.
Do not label scales, scores, methods, trials, or protocols as substance, even if they occur near chemicals or drugs.
Do not label generic medical-care, fitness, or wellness terms as substance when they refer to practices, domains, or activities rather than material entities.
Drug administration expressions are substance only for the material name itself; route, schedule, and therapeutic context are not part of the substance mention.

A broad material class can still be substance if it directly names a type of material or molecule, as with "Peptides" or "ligands"; however, generic abstract nouns that do not denote matter should be excluded.

surname.definition: A family name or last name mentioned as a lexical surname form, typically in dictionary-style, genealogical, or surname-description context, rather than as part of a normal mention of a specific person.
include_rules: Annotate standalone family names explicitly identified as surnames, e.g. patterns like 'X is a surname'.
Annotate surname variants or counterparts when the text states they are forms of a surname, such as feminine or masculine variants.
Annotate surnames listed in surname-entry style contexts, including headings, glossary-like entries, and 'see also' related surname entries.
Annotate the family-name portion when it is presented in a list of people specifically to illustrate bearers of that surname, as in 'Notable people with the surname include ...'.
Annotate single-word or multiword surname forms when the mention denotes the surname as a name type rather than a specific person.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate full person names when the text is referring to a specific individual rather than discussing the surname itself.

Do not annotate surname strings that appear only as the last part of a person mention in news or narrative text without surname-focused context, e.g. 'Gutierrez' in mentions of Officer Reginald Gutierrez.
Do not annotate given names, middle names, initials, honorifics, nicknames, or roles/titles.
Do not annotate common nouns, article titles, social media terms, or other capitalized words that are not family names.
Do not annotate demographic or descriptive words around the surname, such as nationality adjectives or labels like 'Bulgarian', 'Armenian', 'Dutch cricketer', or 'American swimmer'.
Do not annotate pronouns, references to people as groups, or generic expressions like 'people with the surname' when no actual surname form is given.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the surname token(s) themselves, not surrounding descriptors such as 'surname', 'masculine surname', 'feminine counterpart', or 'notable people with the surname include'.

When a full personal name appears in running text, do not extend the span to include given names, nicknames, titles, or initials; only annotate the surname if it is independently being referred to as a surname entry.
Include inflected or gendered surname variants when they are explicitly presented as surname forms, e.g. 'Getova' as the feminine counterpart of 'Getov'.
If a surname appears as a standalone heading, list entry, or 'see also' related surname, annotate just that standalone word or surname phrase.
Do not include punctuation, parentheses, commas, bullets, or surrounding quotation marks in the entity span.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish surname from a person mention: 'Reginald "Jake" Gutierrez' and similar full-name mentions refer to a specific individual, so they are not annotated as surname here unless the surname alone is being discussed as a family-name form.

Distinguish surname from given names or nicknames: only family names such as 'Getov', 'Getova', 'Simonyan', 'Grevers', and 'Grever' qualify, not first names like 'Chantal' or 'Matt' or nicknames like 'Jake'.
Distinguish surname from organizations, places, and titles: names such as 'Tacoma Police Department' or 'Tacoma' are not surnames even if they are capitalized.
Distinguish surname from ordinary capitalized words in titles, headlines, or sentence starts, such as 'Information', 'Tweets', or 'Photo'; capitalization alone is not evidence of a surname.

Use contextual cues like 'is a surname', 'feminine counterpart', 'notable people with the surname', or dictionary-style surname entries to identify surname mentions rather than treating every last token of a person name as a surname annotation.

symbol.definition: A sign, icon, emblem, coat of arms, or other graphic/representational mark that stands for something or is used as a symbolic element.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of specific symbols, icons, emblems, coats of arms, or symbolic marks.
Annotate named heraldic or official emblems, e.g. "Commonwealth Coat of Arms".
Annotate described symbol categories when the phrase refers to the symbol itself, e.g. "diamond ring symbols".
Annotate mentions where the text explicitly identifies a form as a symbol used to represent something, such as symbols representative of numerical values.
Include both singular and plural mentions when they denote symbolic items rather than abstract symbolism.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text that contains no specific sign or emblem mention, even if it discusses symptoms, objects, or other concrete things.
Do not annotate generic uses of words like "symbol" or "symbols" when no particular symbol type or named emblem is identified.
Do not annotate legal or publication boilerplate such as copyright statements, license names, or distribution notices.
Do not annotate physical objects, products, or materials unless the text explicitly presents them as symbolic icons or emblematic marks.
Do not annotate properties, actions, or meanings associated with a symbol if they are outside the symbol name itself, e.g. "representative of numerical values".
boundary_rules: Annotate the full noun phrase that names the symbol or emblem, including descriptive modifiers that identify its depicted form, e.g. "diamond ring symbols" and "Commonwealth Coat of Arms".
Include the common noun "symbol"/"symbols" when it is part of the mention referring to the sign category or icon instance, as in "diamond ring symbols".
If the symbol is named by a fixed title, include the entire title and preserve internal capitalization, e.g. "Commonwealth Coat of Arms".
Do not extend the span to surrounding trigger words or event language such as "representative of numerical values", "obtaining", "featured", or location/context phrases like "at Reel Gems".

Do not annotate only a partial component of a multiword symbol name when the larger phrase identifies the symbol; for example, prefer "diamond ring symbols" over "diamond ring" alone in that sentence.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate ordinary objects unless they are explicitly referenced as symbols or emblems; "diamond ring" is not a symbol by itself, but "diamond ring symbols" is.

Do not annotate organizations, countries, or institutions when the mention is actually their emblem; annotate the emblem name itself, e.g. "Commonwealth Coat of Arms" as a symbol, not "Commonwealth" as a political entity.
Do not annotate letters, numbers, or values unless the text presents them as signs/glyphs/symbols rather than as quantities or codes.

Do not annotate legal marks, copyright notices, or license text as symbols when they function as document metadata rather than a referenced sign or emblem, as illustrated by the copyright/license negative example.
Do not annotate general descriptive language about representation unless a specific sign, icon, emblem, or symbolic mark is named.

symptom.definition: A symptom is a word or phrase describing a patient-reported complaint or a clinically observed manifestation/abnormal condition experienced by the patient, such as pain, breathing difficulty, bleeding, or ocular misalignment.
include_rules: Annotate explicit symptom mentions that describe subjective complaints, e.g. pain or breathing difficulty.
Annotate clinically observed manifestations or abnormal functional states presented as patient signs/symptoms, even if not self-reported, e.g. "hemoptysis", "hypotropias", "exohypotropias".
Annotate symptom terms whether singular or plural.
Annotate multiword symptom phrases when the full phrase is needed to express the manifestation, e.g. "chest pain".
Annotate symptom mentions appearing in lists of patient presentations, findings, or complaints.
Annotate symptom mentions regardless of whether they are introduced by phrases such as "reported", "presented with", or "were noted"; only the symptom span itself should be marked.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text outside a medical or patient-condition context.
Do not annotate diseases, disorders, infections, syndromes, injuries, or diagnostic labels when they name the underlying condition rather than the manifestation.
Do not annotate body parts, organs, or anatomical regions alone unless they are included in a symptom expression.
Do not annotate medications, procedures, devices, tests, or findings that are not symptoms.
Do not annotate generic severity, temporal, or frequency language by itself, such as "late", "isolated", or numeric prevalence information.
Do not annotate reporting/context phrases such as "were noted", "was recorded", or "presented with".
Do not annotate non-clinical technical text, patent text, software text, URLs, or similar content with no symptom meaning.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that expresses the symptom itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the established symptom phrase, such as body-site or directional qualifiers: "chest pain" includes "chest".
Include the full lexicalized symptom term even when morphologically complex or pluralized, e.g. "hypotropias", "exohypotropias".
Do not include preceding event/reporting words such as "presented with", "reported", "noted", or "recorded".
Do not include surrounding punctuation, coordinating conjunctions, percentages, counts, or patient descriptors.
If multiple symptoms are listed separately, annotate each symptom as its own span.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate symptoms as patient-experienced or clinically observed manifestations; do not confuse them with diseases, diagnoses, syndromes, or causes.
Do not annotate treatments, devices, procedures, or interventions even if they are mentioned in a medical context.
Do not annotate anatomical sites alone unless they are part of a symptom phrase; for example, annotate "chest pain" but not "chest" by itself.
Do not annotate measurement values, frequencies, or study statistics associated with a symptom, such as percentages or patient counts.
Do not annotate non-medical uses of words that may resemble symptom language when the context is technical, legal, or unrelated to patient condition.

system.definition: A system is a named or clearly identified organized technical, computational, networked, or operational system, including software/protocol systems and institutional operating systems used to carry out functions or manage activities.
include_rules: Annotate names of technical or operational systems, including software systems, protocol systems, network systems, and organized service systems.
Annotate common-noun system types when they denote a specific kind of organized system in context, e.g., "appointment systems".
Annotate proper names or acronyms of systems, especially when introduced as a system, e.g., "RADIUS".
Annotate infrastructure terms that refer to an organized connected system, e.g., "network".
Annotate plural mentions when the plural noun phrase itself denotes the system entity type being discussed, e.g., "appointment systems".
exclude_rules: Do not annotate references to natural languages, dialects, or language families.
Do not annotate code elements such as methods, classes, variables, threads, or programming modifiers, even when they contain words like "system" or describe behavior in software.
Do not annotate generic technical jargon or abstract concepts that do not denote a concrete system, such as programming paradigms, operations, or behaviors.
Do not annotate physical devices or hardware alone unless the mention denotes a system rather than an individual object.
Do not annotate explanatory descriptions, roles, or purposes adjacent to a system name if they are not part of the name itself.
Do not annotate very generic uses of words like "system" when they do not specify an identifiable system or system type in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the system itself.
Include the full system name, whether it is a common noun phrase or a proper/acronym name, e.g., "appointment systems", "network", "RADIUS".

Exclude surrounding determiners, possessives, and quantifiers unless they are part of the official system name, e.g., annotate "appointment systems" not "respondents' general practitioners operated appointment systems".
Exclude descriptive appositives or functional explanations outside the name span, e.g., annotate "RADIUS" not "RADIUS, a lightweight system for the purpose".
When a modifier is necessary to identify the system type, include it as part of the span, e.g., "appointment systems" rather than only "systems".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate methods, functions, program keywords, or code constructs as system entities; in the negative example, "synchronized method" and class/member names are software/code elements, not systems.
Do not annotate human languages or language varieties as systems; in the negative example, "Pahari Kinnauri language" refers to a natural language, not a technical or organizational system.
Distinguish a system from a device: annotate the organized infrastructure or platform (e.g., "network"), not a physical object such as a "computer" unless the text presents it as a named system.
Distinguish a system from a general process or purpose statement: annotate the actual system name, not nearby descriptions like "for the purpose" or generic activity words.
Distinguish a named or typed system from broad technical domains; annotate concrete systems and system types, not abstract fields like multithreading or programming in general.

table.definition: A named database table or relation: a specific table identifier used to refer to a structured data table in a database or data-storage context.
include_rules: Annotate explicit names of database tables or relations mentioned in technical or data-modeling context.
Annotate a table identifier when it appears after a generic cue such as "table" and names the specific table, e.g. "Users" in "My main table, Users".
Annotate table mentions that combine the designator with the identifier when both together function as the table name mention, e.g. "Table a".
Annotate table names whether they are simple words, letters, or mixed alphanumeric identifiers, as long as they denote a specific database table.
Annotate the table entity even when the sentence also discusses table type, storage backend, or usage, provided a specific table name is present.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic mentions of "table" or "tables" when no specific table name or identifier is given.
Do not annotate non-database uses of "table", including furniture, document tables, experimental tables, or other ordinary-language senses.
Do not annotate names of databases, database systems, query engines, schemas, or storage technologies unless they are themselves the table mention.
Do not annotate descriptive phrases about a table when they are not part of the table's name, such as "main table", "internal table", or "external table" used only as type descriptions.
Do not annotate biological, medical, geographic, or other domain entities that merely appear in text with no database-table meaning, as in the negative examples.
Do not annotate user groups, records, or content descriptions unless the context clearly identifies the phrase as the name of a database table.
boundary_rules: Annotate the table name span itself, including an attached leading type/designator when it is part of the identifier, as in "Table a".

Include the full contiguous table identifier, including internal capitalization or alphanumeric form, as in "BookIDin Readers" only when the table name to be marked is the table-designating component shown by context; in the example, annotate only "Readers" because it is the table name following descriptive words.
Include bare table names when they appear in apposition or after cues such as "table", "stores", "from", or similar database-description context, e.g. "Users" in "My main table, Users".
Do not include surrounding punctuation such as commas, parentheses, or trailing periods.

Do not extend the span to include descriptive modifiers that are not part of the table identifier, such as "main" in "main table" or "internal/external" when they describe table type rather than name.
If a common noun "table" precedes the identifier as part of the actual named mention, include it only when the mention is written as a named unit, e.g. "Table a".
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish table from a database, schema, or storage system name: annotate the specific relation/table being described, not the larger platform or container, e.g. annotate "Table a" but not "Hive" or "Cassandra".
Distinguish table from column/field names: if a token refers to an attribute inside a table rather than the table object itself, do not annotate it as table.
Distinguish table from generic nouns: the common noun "table" by itself is not a table entity unless it is part of a concrete table name mention.

Distinguish table from natural-language tabular references: mentions of physical tables, tables in papers/documents, thin-layer chromatography tables, or other non-database senses are not table entities.
Distinguish table from dataset/list descriptions: phrases like "comma separated list" or "information about users" are not table entities unless a specific database table name is explicitly present.

tag.definition: A tag is a user- or system-defined label string used to categorize, mark, or index content, code elements, or documentation entries, typically appearing as the specific value of a tagging field or label reference.
include_rules: Annotate strings that explicitly function as tag labels or tag values in the sentence.
Include standalone labels entered, assigned, or referenced as tags, even if they are common words or single characters, e.g. ENTER and A.
Include multi-token or punctuated strings when the punctuation is intrinsic to the tag label, e.g. realm-of-the-elderlings.
Annotate a tag when context words such as tag, Tag, add, usage, or for indicate that the string is being used as a categorization label.
Include tags appearing in prose, UI instructions, metadata descriptions, or structured documentation if the string itself is the label being named.
Include tags regardless of capitalization or length, provided the context identifies them as specific tag values rather than ordinary text.
exclude_rules: Exclude generic mentions of the concept of a tag when no specific tag value is given, e.g. Tag in Hit ENTER after each Tag.
Exclude descriptive text about a tag, its usage, or its meaning when that text is not itself the tag label.
Exclude scientific terms, organism names, proteins, chemicals, abbreviations, and other domain vocabulary that are not functioning as tags in context.
Exclude code comments, commands, compiler flags, file paths, filenames, and other software artifacts unless they are explicitly presented as tags.
Exclude surrounding structural syntax such as @return, braces, parentheses, bullets, slashes, or comment markers when they introduce or surround a tag-like value.
Exclude document content that is merely referenced or described by the tag, such as document fragment containing the DOM tree.
boundary_rules: Mark only the exact token or string that functions as the tag label.
Include internal punctuation that is part of the tag string, such as hyphens in realm-of-the-elderlings.
Do not include surrounding marker characters, prose, or field labels such as tag, Tag, @return, braces, quotes, or explanatory text.
When a tag appears as a standalone value in structured text, annotate only the value itself, e.g. A in @return {DocumentFragment} A document fragment containing the DOM tree.
Preserve the original casing of the tag span, e.g. ENTER versus enter if the text uses uppercase.
Do not extend the span to adjacent descriptions, counts, or usage notes such as Numbers in parentheses represent the Tag's usage.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate ordinary words merely because they are capitalized or isolated; annotate them only when the context indicates they are being used as a tag label, as with ENTER.
Do not annotate technical types, classes, or data types such as DocumentFragment; those are object/type names, not tags.
Do not annotate command-line flags, filenames, source code tokens, or generated-code text such as -Wall, linux/types.go, mkpost.go, or README.md unless the context explicitly identifies them as tags.
Do not annotate biological, chemical, or scientific terms in running text, such as abscisic acid, ABA, actin, or Triticum aestivum, because they are domain terms rather than tagging labels.
Do not annotate the generic noun tag/Tag when it refers to the concept of tagging rather than a specific tag value.
If a string could be interpreted either as a content label or as a command/key/code token, use the surrounding context: annotate it only when it is presented as a tag assigned to content or metadata.

team.definition: A named sports or esports team: a group of players competing together under a shared team name, including school teams, national teams, club-like teams, and esports teams.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of sports teams mentioned as competitors or as groups of players/coaches.
Annotate team nicknames and mascot-style names that function as the team's name, e.g. "All Blacks".
Annotate school, university, or regional names when they are used to refer to the athletic team, e.g. "Georgia Southern" in a sports context.
Annotate esports team names, including multiword branded names and game-specific team names, e.g. "Denial eSports StarCraft 2".
Annotate both professional and amateur team names if they denote an organized competitive squad.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate nonprofits, companies, research centers, libraries, schools, or other organizations that are not teams.
Do not annotate generic activity or art-group terms such as "theatre", "drama classes", or "Musical Theatre Class".
Do not annotate generic role nouns or collective references such as "players", "coaches", "roster", or "team" when they appear without a specific team name.
Do not annotate event names, tournaments, campaigns, seasons, or dates.
Do not annotate locations or institutions unless the mention specifically denotes the competing team rather than the place or organization itself.
Do not annotate descriptive phrases for a team when no actual team name is given.
boundary_rules: Annotate the contiguous span that names the team itself.
Include modifiers that are part of the official or commonly used team name, including geographic or organizational identifiers, e.g. "Georgia Southern" and "Denial eSports StarCraft 2".
Include esports game-title designators when they are part of the team name, e.g. "StarCraft 2" in "Denial eSports StarCraft 2".
Do not include following generic nouns such as "team", "players", or "coaches" when they are not part of the name, e.g. annotate "Georgia Southern" not "Georgia Southern players and coaches".
Do not include surrounding event, date, or competition context, e.g. annotate "All Blacks" only, not "the All Blacks on 21 September".
Exclude determiners or descriptive words before the name unless they are fixed parts of the team name; for example annotate "All Blacks" not "the All Blacks".
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate organizations whose function is educational, nonprofit, corporate, or research rather than being a sports or esports team; for example "Schumacher Center for a New Economics" is an organization, not a team.
Do not annotate artistic groups, classes, or activity labels unless they clearly denote a competitive team; for example "Musical Theatre Class" and mentions of "theatre" are not teams.

Distinguish a team from a school or institution name used in a non-team sense: annotate when the name refers to the athletic/esports side competing or having players/coaches, as in "Georgia Southern players and coaches," but not when the same string refers only to the institution.
Distinguish a team from a roster description: annotate the proper team name, not generic references like "current roster" or the trailing noun "team".
Distinguish a team from event names or competitions: in phrases like "World Cup campaign against the All Blacks," annotate only the competitor team, not the tournament.
A mascot-style nickname can be a team if it denotes the competing side, even without a geographic term, as in "All Blacks".

technique.definition: A named method, procedural approach, measurement method, or analytical procedure used to perform an experiment, make a measurement, process data, or determine a result.
include_rules: Annotate phrases that explicitly name an experimental, measurement, or analytical method.
Annotate instrumental or laboratory techniques, including adjectival method names such as "Gas-liquid chromatographic" when they refer to the analytical technique used.
Annotate named procedural expressions ending in words like "technique", "analysis", or "fitting" when they denote a specific methodological approach.
Annotate data-analysis methods and statistical procedures when they are presented as the means used to analyze data, such as "Gaussian curve fitting" and "multivariate statistical analysis".
Annotate a method phrase whether it appears as a noun phrase or as a modifier of an omitted generic head, provided the phrase clearly denotes the technique itself.
Annotate each distinct technique separately when multiple methods are listed in the same sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate biological entities, chemicals, tissues, diseases, organisms, genes, RNAs, proteins, or other study subjects mentioned near a method.
Do not annotate biological processes, functions, mechanisms, or regulatory phenomena such as gene expression regulation, signal transduction, differentiation, inflammation, or secretion.
Do not annotate generic topic terms, field labels, or broad scientific areas when they do not denote a concrete method used in the described work.
Do not annotate action verbs or event triggers alone, such as "measured", "performed", or "determination", unless they are part of a named technique span.
Do not annotate the purpose, result, or object of a technique, such as the compounds measured or the tissue analyzed.
Do not annotate unrelated nontechnical text, including legal, commercial, or narrative language that does not name a scientific or analytic method.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the technique itself.
Include descriptors that are part of the established method name, including adjectival modifiers before the head term, as in "Gas-liquid chromatographic" and "multivariate statistical analysis".

Include the generic method head when it is part of the named expression, such as "technique", "fitting", or "analysis" in "search-coil technique", "Gaussian curve fitting", and "multivariate statistical analysis".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding trigger words such as "measured by", "performed by", "determination of", or objects/purposes of the method.
Do not include materials, analytes, samples, organisms, or locations that the technique is applied to, such as "of clonidine and some analogues in rat brain tissue".
When multiple techniques are coordinated, annotate each technique span separately rather than one combined span.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate a phrase as technique only when it denotes a method, procedure, analytic approach, or measurement approach; do not annotate entities that are substances, body parts, organisms, diseases, or legal/business terms.

A research action noun is a technique only if it names a specific methodological approach, e.g. "curve fitting" or "statistical analysis"; generic scientific activities without a named method sense should not be annotated.
Do not confuse biological or regulatory processes with techniques. Phrases about gene regulation, signalling, differentiation, secretion, or networks describe phenomena, not methods.
Do not confuse targets or objects of study with techniques. In "determination of clonidine", the technique is the chromatographic method, while "clonidine" is only the analyte.
Do not annotate broad domain terms or document-topic phrases as techniques unless they explicitly denote a method used to obtain, measure, or analyze data.
Non-biomedical or unrelated text that lacks a procedural or methodological expression should contain no technique entities.

technology.definition: A named technological artifact, platform, service, component, or computing/engineering concept used as a specific technical term in context, including hardware components, software architecture terms, cloud platforms, and technical service types.
include_rules: Annotate named or clearly term-like technologies used in technical discussion, including hardware and system components such as "Microprocessors".
Annotate abbreviations and acronyms that denote technical concepts or components, such as "PV" and "SPA".
Annotate named cloud or software platforms/services, such as "Azure".
Annotate technical service or interface terms when they function as a concrete technology mention, such as "API services".
Annotate domain-specific technical terms that refer to a distinct computing or engineering artifact, architecture, or service rather than a general activity or object.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate personal names, team names, handles, social usernames, or geographic locations, even when they appear in technical data or metadata.
Do not annotate generic technical nouns that describe ordinary system parts or resources without serving as a specific technology name, such as "disk", "partition layer", or "file storage".
Do not annotate broad activities, operations, or problem descriptions such as "writing", "scraping", "merge", or error text.
Do not annotate file types, document artifacts, or content objects such as "pdf" when they refer to output/input files rather than a technology entity.
Do not annotate programming-language or library/package mentions in this guideline when they appear like "python" or "pypdf", since the provided evidence marks them as non-entities.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the technology itself: "PV", "Microprocessors", "SPA", "API services", "Azure".
Include modifiers only when they are part of the established technology name or term, as in "API services"; do not split off integral head/modifier words.

Exclude surrounding functional, descriptive, or relational context such as verbs, prepositions, and purpose clauses: in "SPA out of file storage with API services on Azure", annotate only the technology terms, not the full phrase.
For abbreviations and acronyms, annotate the acronym alone when it is the technology mention, e.g., "PV" and "SPA".
Do not extend the span to include generic category words unless they are part of the actual technology expression being used in context.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate technology names, computing components, architectural styles, platforms, and technical service terms; do not annotate people, teams, usernames, or locations such as "CoquiHackers", "Jesiniel Nieves Ortiz", or "Canovanas, PR".
Distinguish technology from ordinary files, documents, and content artifacts: "pdf" in the negative example is a file format/document reference and is not annotated here.
Distinguish technology from programming-language mentions in this dataset context: "python" and library/package names like "pypdf" are not annotated based on the provided evidence.
Distinguish technology from generic technical actions or processes such as "writing", "scraping", or "merge"; only the named technical artifact or platform should be annotated.

Distinguish technology from abstract infrastructure nouns used non-specifically. Annotate "Azure" because it refers to a named platform, but do not annotate generic words like "disk", "partition layer", "file storage", or "web pages" when they are merely descriptive system components or resources rather than named technologies.

term.definition: A domain-specific concept name or label, usually a technical, procedural, or specialized vocabulary item, mentioned as the name of a concept rather than as a specific person, place, organization, or event.
include_rules: Annotate single words or multiword expressions that explicitly denote a specialized concept, especially in technical or instructional contexts.
Annotate terms used as labels in definitions, glossaries, bullet points, or command-line/help-style descriptions.
Annotate computing or internet vocabulary such as protocol/object names and command-line concepts when referenced as concept names, e.g. "url" and "command".
Annotate a word when context indicates it is being introduced, defined, or discussed as a term of art rather than merely used descriptively.
