Annotate capitalized or uncapitalized terms equally when capitalization reflects formatting rather than named-entity status.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general nouns or content words used only in their ordinary sentence meaning without clear concept-label status.
Do not annotate legal, governmental, journalistic, or economic vocabulary in running prose when it is simply part of the topic being discussed.
Do not annotate names of people, places, organizations, courts, or case titles.
Do not annotate numeric identifiers, docket numbers, dates, citations, or document metadata.
Do not annotate full explanatory phrases, example descriptions, or clause content around a term; only the term itself qualifies.
Do not annotate filenames, script names, or other specific artifacts unless the target span is the conceptual term rather than the artifact name.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the minimal noun or noun phrase that names the technical or procedural concept itself, e.g. "url", "Flag", "command".
Do not include surrounding determiners, adjectives, or verbs unless they are part of the fixed term name.
When a term appears inside an explanatory definition, include just the term being defined, not the descriptive gloss around it; e.g. in "Flag: the letter or word...", annotate only "Flag".
For multiword terms, include all words that form the single concept name; exclude trailing punctuation such as colons, commas, or periods.
If a common noun is used generically in ordinary prose rather than as a named concept label, do not annotate it.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate words that refer to abstract technical, computing, command-line, formatting, or domain-specific concepts as terms, not the concrete objects or instances those concepts may denote.
Do not annotate organizations, courts, places, people, legal case names, dates, or document identifiers even if they contain capitalized or specialized words; these are not terms in this sense.
Do not annotate ordinary topical vocabulary from news or legal prose such as "tariffs", "report", or "order" when they are used as general language rather than as defined technical labels.
A term may be a common dictionary word, but it should be annotated only when context shows it is being used as a concept name, command-line label, or technical category, as with "command" or "Flag".
File names, product names, and document format names should not be annotated as term unless the context clearly treats them as the concept being defined rather than as a specific named item.

test.definition: A named test, testing method, assay, or evaluative procedure, including specifically described variants or method families used to detect, assess, or measure something.
include_rules: Annotate expressions that name a specific test or testing variant, especially when a modifier distinguishes the test from other tests, e.g. "standard tube" in "standard tube test".
Annotate phrases that refer to a deliberately created or designated test instance, e.g. "demo test".
Annotate named testing approaches or method families when they function as the means of detection, diagnosis, or evaluation, e.g. "immunologic methods".
Include laboratory, diagnostic, experimental, or software/testing procedure names when the phrase denotes the method itself rather than the surrounding activity.
Annotate both singular and plural mentions when they denote concrete testing methods or classes of tests.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic uses of words like "test", "method", or "procedure" when they are not part of a specific named or clearly identified testing expression.
Do not annotate programming code, class definitions, function names, or other software syntax, even if they could coincidentally contain words associated with testing.

Do not annotate clinical, scientific, or technical activities that are not themselves tests, such as product development, enrichment processes, preparation methods, correction strategies, or general analysis.
Do not annotate substances, specimens, patients, diseases, nutrients, or outcomes being tested or measured; only annotate the test or testing method.
Do not annotate regulatory, instructional, or publication-related phrases such as "instruction on application" or "methodical recommendations" unless they explicitly name a test.
boundary_rules: Include the full noun phrase that names the specific test, testing variant, or testing method, including descriptive modifiers that are part of its name, e.g. "standard tube", "demo test", "immunologic methods".
When the head word is explicitly present, include it together with its attached modifiers, e.g. include "demo test" rather than only "test".
When the mention refers to a method family used as a testing approach, include the complete expression, e.g. "immunologic methods".
Do not include surrounding verbs, determiners, prepositions, results, or measured materials outside the test name, e.g. exclude words like "detected", "using", "of the serums positive".

Do not extend the span to include implementation details, data sources, or APIs that are merely used to run or demonstrate the test, e.g. in "demo test using reqres.in fake data API", annotate only "demo test".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the procedure or method used to test, not the object being tested or analyzed. For example, in "standard tube test detected 98% of the serums", the test mention is the method name, not "serums".
Annotate the testing approach itself, not supporting tools, software, code, or external services. In "demo test using reqres.in fake data API", annotate "demo test" and not the API name.

Do not annotate administrative or instructional documents related to procedures, such as "instruction", "methodical recommendations", or "clinical approbation", unless they directly name a test or testing method.

Do not annotate broad scientific or technical activities unless they denote a concrete testing method. Generic research, analysis, enrichment, preparation, or correction processes are not test entities.

If an expression names a class of testing approaches, such as "immunologic methods", treat it as a test entity when it refers to a diagnostic or evaluative method rather than a general scientific field.

therapy.definition: A named medical, psychological, or procedural intervention used for treatment or therapeutic management of a condition, including specific therapy modalities, stimulation-based interventions, and drugs when mentioned as the treatment itself.
include_rules: Annotate specific named therapies or treatment modalities used to treat or manage a condition.
Annotate named psychological or behavioral therapies, including full multiword titles such as "Acceptance and Commitment Therapy".
Annotate named procedural or device-based therapeutic interventions, such as stimulation therapies like "spinal cord stimulation".
Annotate drug names when the text presents the drug as the therapeutic intervention being used, as in "Bleomycin" for treatment.
Annotate therapy mentions regardless of whether they appear in titles, abstracts, or running text, as long as the phrase denotes a treatment.
Annotate both conventional and nonconventional therapeutic modalities if they are explicitly named and used in a therapeutic sense.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate person names, fictional characters, organizations, products, software components, payment methods, or other non-medical entities.
Do not annotate generic treatment words without a specific named intervention, such as "therapy", "treatment", or "therapeutic modality" used alone.
Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, lesions, or diagnoses that are the target of treatment rather than the treatment itself.
Do not annotate anatomical structures, devices, or procedures unless the full phrase denotes a therapeutic intervention.
Do not annotate research framing language such as "review", "principles", "applications", or "findings".
Do not annotate substances when they are mentioned only as chemicals or materials and not as an administered therapy in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the therapy, treatment, or therapeutic modality.

Include the full official or commonly used therapy name, including key modifiers that identify the modality, such as route, target, or disease-relevant mechanism when they are part of the name (e.g., "spinal cord stimulation", "Acceptance and Commitment Therapy").
Include the drug name alone when the text uses it as the treatment mention rather than merely naming a chemical substance (e.g., "Bleomycin" in a therapeutic context).
Exclude surrounding generic category words when they are not part of the therapy name, such as standalone descriptors like "principles of", "modality", "treatment with", or "applications of".
Do not include disease names, patient groups, outcomes, or anatomical sites unless they are embedded within the established therapy name itself.
Do not extend the span to include punctuation, conjunctions, or trailing explanatory phrases.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate therapy when the mention denotes an intervention used to treat, manage, or therapeutically affect a condition; do not rely on capitalization alone.
Distinguish therapy from diseases or findings: conditions being treated, such as scars or cancer, are not therapy entities.
Distinguish therapy from anatomy or targets: body parts affected or stimulated, such as the spinal cord, are included only when they are part of the therapy name, not as separate therapy mentions.

Distinguish therapy from general research or procedural discussion: phrases describing principles, applications, findings, or procedure are not therapy unless they contain a specific intervention name.

Distinguish therapy from substances in non-treatment roles: a drug or agent should be annotated only when used as the therapeutic intervention, not when mentioned solely as a chemical, biomarker, or experimental reagent.
Distinguish therapy from generic care terms: broad words like "therapy", "treatment", or "therapeutic modality" alone are not annotated unless they refer to a specific named intervention.

time.definition: A word or phrase that explicitly denotes a point in time, a time period, a season, a historical era segment, or a relative temporal offset used to locate an event or state in time.
include_rules: Annotate explicit references to seasons or seasonal periods when they denote when something happens, such as "austral summer".
Annotate historical or calendar-like period expressions that denote eras, centuries, or portions of them, such as "late 17th century".
Annotate relative temporal expressions that state elapsed time or temporal offset from a reference point, such as "12 days later".
Annotate phrases consisting of a number or quantifier plus a time unit when they clearly function as a temporal expression.
Annotate temporal expressions that answer questions like when?, how long after?, or during what period? when the phrase itself directly denotes time.
Annotate modifiers that refine the temporal reference, including words like "late", "early", "mid", and directional/seasonal qualifiers that are part of the time phrase.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-temporal numbers or number-word expressions that indicate quantity, distance, score, dosage, or measurement rather than time.

Do not annotate rank, stage, class, or ability progression expressions such as "7th level", "each level", or "of that level" when they describe game mechanics, hierarchy, or classification rather than temporal meaning.
Do not annotate event nouns modified by time-derived adjectives when the full phrase names an event rather than a time expression, such as "a week-long bombing campaign".
Do not annotate generic temporal function words by themselves, such as isolated prepositions or conjunctions like "after" or "during" without the time expression they govern.
Do not annotate dates written as calendar dates in these guidelines, such as "October 8" or "October 7".
Do not annotate durations implied only by morphology or embedded inside another non-time expression unless the phrase itself directly denotes time.
boundary_rules: Include the full temporal phrase needed to express the time reference, including modifiers such as seasons, ordinals, and relative markers: "austral summer", "late 17th century", "12 days later".
Include premodifiers that are part of the temporal meaning, such as "late", "early", "mid", or domain-specific seasonal adjectives like "austral".
Include numeric value + time unit together when they form one expression, and include attached relative words such as "later", "earlier", or similar if present.

Do not extend the span to surrounding prepositions or conjunctions when they are not part of the temporal expression itself; for example, annotate the time phrase, not leading words like "in", "on", or "after" unless they are inseparable from the expression.
Do not include adjacent event descriptions or clauses; annotate only the words denoting the time reference.
If a sentence contains a date-like string that functions as a calendar date, do not annotate it as this type under these guidelines.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate expressions whose primary role is to locate something in time or express temporal distance; do not annotate expressions whose primary role is numeric measurement, rank, game level, spell level, or quantity.
Do not annotate bare ordinals or numbered levels when they describe status, progression, or classification rather than time, such as "7th level" or "each level".
Do not annotate physical measurements or distances such as "1,500 km"; these are quantities, not times.

Do not annotate durations that are used only as adjectival modifiers of an event or object when they are not treated as standalone time expressions in this scheme, such as "a week-long bombing campaign".
Do not annotate named entities of other semantic types even if they contain temporal-looking material; only the temporal expression itself is in scope.

Calendar-style dates such as "October 8" and "October 7" are excluded here, even though they are temporal in meaning, because the examples indicate they are not to be labeled as this type in this guideline set.

time period.definition: A span that denotes a temporal interval, era, phase, or period of time, including named or descriptively defined periods and calendar-based period endpoints, rather than a single exact timestamp.
include_rules: Annotate expressions that denote a bounded or interpretable stretch of time, such as month-year mentions used as interval points in temporal coverage statements.
Annotate named or descriptive eras, ages, phases, and developmental periods, such as "modern age".
Annotate phrases where a temporal modifier and domain noun together denote a historical or developmental period, such as "early assembly language development".
Annotate temporal expressions that answer "when in history or over what period" rather than "at what exact moment".
Annotate both formally named periods and lowercased descriptive periods when the phrase semantically refers to a distinct time period.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate exact timestamps, clock times, or full date-time strings, such as numeric dates with times or time zones.
Do not annotate isolated date components extracted from code, variables, or formatting strings, such as "Day", "Month", "Year", or generated date fields.
Do not annotate generic deictic or contextual time expressions that are only relative references and not period entities, such as "this year", "every day", or "current section".
Do not annotate event dates or schedule mentions when they function as exact occurrences rather than as time periods.
Do not annotate purely technical version numbers, release labels, file names, or identifiers that resemble dates or periods but are not temporal intervals.
Do not annotate broad sentence context around a period expression if the surrounding words describe actions, events, or locations rather than the time period itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that expresses the time period.
Include internal modifiers that are part of the period expression, such as adjectives or era markers: "early assembly language development", "modern age".
For month-year expressions used as temporal interval endpoints, include both the month and year together: "January 1998", "December 1999".
Do not include surrounding prepositions, conjunctions, or range markers unless they are part of the named period itself; exclude words such as "between", "and", "from", "to", "in", "on", "during".
When a larger noun phrase contains a temporal modifier plus a head describing a developmental phase or era, include the full phrase if it denotes a period, not just the modifier alone.
Do not extend the span to include unrelated event descriptions, clauses, or verbs outside the period expression.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish time period from specific calendar dates or timestamps: annotate spans that denote an interval, era, phase, or period-like temporal segment, not exact date-time mentions such as "11/29/2013, 2:00pm EST".
Distinguish time period from date ranges written as full event scheduling information; if a mention is only a concrete schedule or publication date rather than a period entity, do not annotate it.
Distinguish time period from durations expressed only quantitatively, such as counts of days, months, or years without identifying a specific period or era.

Distinguish time period from events or activities: in "early assembly language development", annotate the whole phrase only because it names a developmental period, not because "development" alone is an event.
Distinguish time period from generic temporal adverbs or frequency expressions like "every day", "this year", or "current" when they do not denote a standalone period entity.

tissue.definition: An anatomical tissue, organ, or tissue sample from an organism, including named body materials used as biological specimens or sites in biomedical context.
include_rules: Annotate mentions of anatomical tissues and organs, such as "bone marrow" and "liver".
Annotate tissue mentions whether they refer to a body site in situ or to harvested biological material/sample.
Annotate tissue phrases with attached organism, strain, or source modifiers when the full phrase denotes the tissue, e.g. "DBA/2 bone marrow".
Annotate tissue mentions appearing in prepositional phrases or experimental descriptions, e.g. "in the bone marrow".
Annotate canonical common-noun tissue names even when not capitalized.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-biological uses of words that happen to resemble body-related terms or occur in unrelated documents.
Do not annotate people, titles, organizations, events, or legal/governmental entities.
Do not annotate organism names by themselves unless they are part of a larger tissue phrase.
Do not annotate cell types, cell populations, or phrases whose head denotes cells rather than tissue.
Do not annotate biological processes, observations, diseases, or experimental actions that occur in or involve tissue but are not themselves tissue mentions.
Do not annotate generic sample descriptors with no anatomical tissue head.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the tissue or organ, e.g. "bone marrow" and "liver".
Include attached strain/species/source modifiers when they directly specify the tissue sample, e.g. "DBA/2 bone marrow".
Do not extend the span to surrounding prepositions, determiners, or verbs, e.g. in "in the bone marrow" annotate only "bone marrow".
Do not include coordinated non-tissue items outside the tissue phrase, e.g. in "DBA/2 bone marrow and spleen cells" annotate "DBA/2 bone marrow" but not the full coordination.

When a tissue word is followed by a cell-type term that makes the phrase denote cells rather than the tissue itself, end the span before the cell term unless the full phrase still primarily names a tissue sample.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish tissue from organism mentions: species or strain names alone such as "rat" or "DBA/2" are not tissue, but they may be included when modifying a tissue mention, as in "rat liver" or "DBA/2 bone marrow" if the full phrase denotes the tissue.
Distinguish tissue from cell mentions: annotate anatomical material such as "bone marrow" or "liver", not individual cell populations such as "spleen cells" or "neoplastic cells".
Distinguish tissue from diseases or pathological processes: words describing events or conditions in tissue, such as "erythrophagocytosis", are not tissue entities.

Distinguish tissue from organizations or institutions: words like "Children's Book Circle" or titles such as "Lord High Steward" may contain body-part words like "children's" or "steward" contextually unrelated to anatomy and must not be annotated.
Distinguish tissue from locations in a non-biological sense: only anatomical structures or tissue samples from organisms are included, not political, social, or ceremonial entities.

title.definition: A word or phrase that denotes a person’s formal role, rank, office, honorific, or status-based form of address, used as that person’s title in context.
include_rules: Annotate formal occupational or official titles attached to or referring to a specific person, such as judicial, legal, governmental, military, religious, or administrative titles.
Annotate multiword title phrases when the full phrase expresses the office or rank of the person.
Annotate titles used in apposition with a name or as a standalone reference to a person.
Annotate status-based or honorific forms of address when they function as a title for the person in the sentence.
Annotate both common modern titles and culture-specific or historical titles when they denote a person’s rank, status, or office.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate non-human uses of the word "title" or similar metadata labels.
Do not annotate document titles, article titles, book titles, study names, section headings, or other names of works.
Do not annotate generic profession or role nouns when they are not used as a specific person’s title in context.
Do not annotate names of people, even when adjacent to a title.
Do not annotate organizations, locations, licenses, technical configuration fields, or legal/administrative boilerplate text.
Do not annotate descriptive noun phrases about groups of people unless they function as an actual title for an individual.
Do not annotate age, status, or demographic descriptors such as "older women" unless they clearly function as a formal title, which they usually do not.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the words that make up the title itself.
Do not include the name of the person holding the title before or after the title span.
Include modifiers that are part of the fixed title, such as rank or office descriptors, when they directly form the title phrase (e.g., "Assistant Prosecuting Attorney", "District Judge").
When a title is followed or preceded by punctuation, exclude the punctuation unless it is orthographically part of the title.
For standalone honorific-like titles used as a form of address or status label, annotate only that single token if it functions as the title (e.g., "Agassi").
If multiple titles appear in coordination, annotate each title phrase separately rather than the whole coordinated sequence unless the coordination itself forms one single title expression.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse title with a person name: in "Crabtree, Assistant Prosecuting Attorney", annotate "Assistant Prosecuting Attorney" as title, not "Crabtree".
Do not confuse title with occupation mentions used generically; annotate only when the phrase denotes a role, rank, honorific, or office borne by a specific person in context.
Do not confuse title with organization names or institutional units; words like "Court", "Office", or similar are only included if they are part of the person’s title phrase.
Do not confuse title with document metadata or field labels such as "type:" in configuration or licensing text; these are not human titles.
Do not confuse title with study names, article headings, or descriptive topic phrases in scientific text; these are not titles borne by people.
Do not confuse title with legal case roles or participant categories unless they are expressed as formal person titles or offices.

tool.definition: A named software tool or utility, including applications, analyzers, or software products used to perform a task, computation, analysis, fabrication, or similar function.
include_rules: Annotate names of software tools, utilities, or applications used to carry out a task.
Annotate standalone product or command names that clearly function as tools, e.g., "clang-tidy".
Annotate multiword software names or descriptive tool mentions when the phrase refers to the software itself, e.g., "fabrication software" and "Network Analyst".
Annotate tool mentions in contexts of use, execution, computation, analysis, or building, as long as the span names the tool rather than the action or result.
Include both branded/proper names and descriptive names when they denote a concrete tool available to users.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic nouns for technology or methods when they do not refer to a specific tool mention, such as broad references to software, analysis, or options without a tool name-like expression.
Do not annotate ordinary actions, website interactions, or social-media terms such as posts, upvotes, or shares.
Do not annotate biological, medical, or scientific processes, treatments, or procedures, even if they are used strategically, such as IVF, cryopreservation, implantation, or transfer.
Do not annotate outputs, results, models, routes, roads, satellites, embryos, or other non-tool objects affected by a tool.
Do not annotate configuration details, parameters, or supplied options unless they are part of the tool name itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the tool itself.
Include generic head nouns such as "software" or "analyst" only when they are part of the actual tool name or the phrase used to identify the tool, e.g., "fabrication software" and "Network Analyst".
Do not include surrounding verbs, auxiliaries, or usage context, e.g., annotate "clang-tidy" not "clang-tidy also does it".
Do not include arguments, options, outputs, objects acted on, or purpose clauses, e.g., annotate "fabrication software" not "fabrication software" plus nearby task description.
Preserve original capitalization, hyphenation, and multiword form when they are part of the tool name, e.g., "clang-tidy" and "Network Analyst".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate a term as tool when it denotes a software application, utility, package, analyzer, or similar instrument used to perform a task.

Do not annotate scientific procedures, medical treatments, or laboratory methods as tool merely because they are instrumental; for example, "in-vitro fertilization" and cryopreservation strategies are processes, not tools.

Do not annotate devices, systems, projects, or objects being modeled unless the text specifically names the software or utility used with them; in "models of the MMS satellites using fabrication software," only "fabrication software" is a tool.
Do not annotate roles, people, or organizations that use tools; only the tool artifact itself is tagged.
Do not annotate generic activity descriptions or platform actions such as deleting posts or getting upvotes, because they do not name a distinct tool.

topic.definition: A topic is an explicit phrase naming the subject matter, domain, practice, medium, or category being discussed, rather than a specific person, organization, product, event, or technical instance.
include_rules: Annotate explicit noun phrases that denote a subject area, theme, or category of discussion.
Annotate named practices, fields, or domains of activity, especially when they can be discussed in terms of pros/cons, methods, or how-to guidance, e.g. "cord blood banking".
Annotate media/content categories when they are discussed as a type of content, e.g. "gig vlogs".
Annotate broad but explicit domains when they are the subject of discussion, e.g. "internet" in a how-to or advice context.
Include multiword topic phrases when the modifiers are necessary to identify the specific topic, e.g. "dental pulp banking" rather than just "banking".
Annotate each distinct topic separately when multiple topics are contrasted or listed in the same sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate names of companies, platforms, applications, accounts, APIs, protocols, or standards, even if they are central to the discussion.
Do not annotate full problem descriptions, research findings, or sentences that describe a subject without giving a compact topic phrase.

Do not annotate generic technical terms, components, or procedural elements such as "controller", "user info", "callback method", "signals", or "loads" unless they function as an explicit subject/category label.
Do not annotate inferred or implicit topics that are not directly named in the text.
Do not annotate overly broad abstract nouns like "life", "movement", or "conditions" when they are just part of a description rather than the named subject matter.
Do not annotate modifiers alone when the full topic phrase is available; for example, do not annotate "cord blood" alone in "cord blood banking".
Do not annotate specific instances or examples of use when the phrase refers to an entity rather than a subject area.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the topic, including essential modifiers that specify the subject, e.g. "cord blood banking", "dental pulp banking", "gig vlogs".
Include head nouns that make the topic complete, such as "banking" in "cord blood banking" or "vlogs" in "gig vlogs".
Include domain adjectives or material/type modifiers when they are part of the topic name, e.g. "cord blood" and "dental pulp".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding verbs, purpose clauses, or explanatory context, e.g. annotate "internet" not "make some income from internet".
Do not include determiners, conjunctions, or list-introducing words unless they are inside the topic phrase itself.
When two coordinated topic phrases are mentioned, annotate each topic separately rather than the whole coordinated string, e.g. "cord blood banking" and "dental pulp banking" separately.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate subject matter or content domains, not organizations, brands, products, or service providers. For example, the negative example contains platform names such as "OneLogin", "ClassLink", and "Clever", which are not topic mentions.

Do not annotate technical methods, system components, or experimental procedures merely because they are discussed in detail; the negative scientific abstract describes research content but does not contain a clearly named topic phrase of the kind shown in the positive examples.
Distinguish a topic from an event or activity instance: annotate the general subject/category "gig vlogs", not a specific performance, login attempt, or experiment.

Distinguish a topic from a broad document theme inferred from the whole passage. Only annotate explicit lexical mentions of the subject, not an implied theme like SSO implementation or muscle control research when no concise topic phrase is presented.

Distinguish a topic from generic objects or participants mentioned within a discussion. Terms such as users, loads, signals, callbacks, or APIs are not topic entities unless they are used as explicit subject labels.

tournament.definition: A named competitive sporting or esports event at the tournament level, identified by its official or widely used proper name.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of tournaments in sports or esports, including standalone names and names containing sponsor, brand, or location elements.
Annotate named recurring competition events referred to by their conventional title, even if the title is short or article-led, such as "The International".
Annotate tournament names that include format/type words as part of the title, such as "Classic" or "Sevens".
Annotate the tournament name whether it appears alone or followed by contextual information like the sport, league, or tour in parentheses.
Annotate tournament mentions when the context clearly indicates they are competitive events with participants or winners.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic event nouns without a specific proper name, such as "tournament", "classic", "sevens", or "championship" when used descriptively only.
Do not annotate leagues, tours, circuits, federations, or other organizing bodies unless the mention is specifically the name of a tournament event.
Do not annotate locations, venues, or geopolitical names that merely indicate where an event occurs and are not part of the tournament title.
Do not annotate descriptive phrases that characterize an event but are not its name, such as rankings, prize descriptions, or explanatory text.
Do not annotate non-event capitalized strings from unrelated domains, such as scientific terms, genes, proteins, study titles, or news locations.
Do not annotate attacks, concerts, meetings, or other non-tournament events even if they are named events.
boundary_rules: Annotate the exact tournament name span only, including distinctive words that are part of the official or conventional title, such as sponsor names, locations, numerals, and event-format terms: "Meijer LPGA Classic", "Hong Kong Sevens", "The International".
Include a leading article only when it is part of the established event name, as in "The International"; otherwise do not extend the span to include surrounding determiners.
Do not include parenthetical league/series information, as in "Meijer LPGA Classic (LPGA Tour)" where only "Meijer LPGA Classic" is annotated.
Do not include descriptive appositives or explanations outside the name, such as "the biggest DOTA 2 tournament in the world".
Do not include surrounding verbs, prepositions, or temporal phrases such as "since the 1980s" or "page".
If the tournament name appears inside a larger sentence fragment or headline, annotate only the contiguous words forming the event title.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate a tournament only when the mention denotes a competition event; do not annotate organizations, governing bodies, or tours/series that run events, such as "LPGA Tour".
Do not annotate sports venues, stadiums, cities, countries, or regions mentioned as locations of play unless they are part of the tournament's official name, as in "Hong Kong Sevens".

Do not annotate game titles, sports names, or disciplines by themselves; annotate the event built around them only when a specific tournament name is given, e.g. "The International" rather than generic "DOTA 2".

Do not annotate generic references to tournaments or competitions without a specific name, such as "a sports stadium" context or "the biggest DOTA 2 tournament in the world" when used descriptively rather than as the proper name.
Do not confuse a tournament with teams, players, winners, or participants; those are competitors in the event, not the event itself.

town.definition: A named populated settlement at the town level, including a town or town-like community referred to by its proper name.
include_rules: Annotate proper names that denote a populated settlement at the town level.
Annotate town names appearing in locative contexts, such as after prepositions like "at", "in", "from", or in distance/direction descriptions.
Annotate a settlement name even when it is modified by nearby geographic context, as long as the annotated span is only the town name.
Annotate town names whether they appear alone or followed by a larger administrative location, e.g., a state or country.
Annotate names of communities that function as town-level settlements in context, including small towns or town-like localities.
Annotate repeated mentions of the same town each time they appear with a town referent in context.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic settlement nouns without a proper name, such as "community" or "town" used alone.
Do not annotate suburbs, neighborhoods, or districts when they are explicitly described as such rather than as towns.
Do not annotate higher-level administrative units such as states, provinces, regions, or countries.
Do not annotate natural features or bodies of water, even when they help locate a town.
Do not annotate organizations, buildings, bridges, schools, products, charts, or other non-settlement entities that may contain a place name.
Do not annotate person names or other capitalized words that are not place names.
Do not annotate text with no settlement referent, including technical, legal, or patent language with no town mention.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the place-name token(s) that denote the town, e.g., "Walters", "Paksey", "Greenock".
Exclude higher-level administrative or geographic additions when they are not part of the town name itself, e.g., annotate "Walters" in "Walters, Oklahoma" but not ", Oklahoma".
Exclude preceding generic descriptors such as "town of", "community", or similar settlement-type nouns unless they are part of the official name.
Exclude following explanatory appositives or locational phrases such as "a suburb in south-western Sydney" or "on the southern shore of the Firth".
If a town name appears inside a larger institution or facility name, annotate it only when the sentence refers to the town itself, not when it functions merely as part of the larger name.
Use the smallest span that uniquely identifies the town; do not extend to surrounding punctuation or unrelated modifiers.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish town from larger administrative regions such as states or countries: annotate the town mention, not the broader location, e.g., "Walters" not "Oklahoma".

Distinguish town from natural geographic features: names of rivers, firths, shores, or similar features are not town entities, e.g., "Padma" and "Firth" are excluded while "Paksey" and "Greenock" are included.

Distinguish town from neighborhoods or suburbs when the type is explicitly given as a suburb rather than a town; do not annotate such mentions based on this type alone, as in "Campbelltown, a suburb in south-western Sydney".
Distinguish town from organizations or facilities that contain a place name, such as bridges, schools, labels, or charts; annotate the place only if it denotes the settlement itself in context.
Distinguish town from generic settlement words used descriptively without a proper name; words like "community" or "town" alone are not entities.
Distinguish town from street addresses, buildings, or site-specific locations; only populated settlement names belong to this type.

township.definition: A named township-level administrative division or municipality, including official names of townships and equivalent local government units at the township/municipal level.
include_rules: Annotate proper names that denote a township-level administrative unit, such as official townships or equivalent municipalities.
Annotate full named forms containing the administrative designator, e.g. names ending with "Township" when that is part of the official unit name.
Annotate municipality names that function as township-level entities even when no generic word like "Township" appears in the surface form.
Annotate local-language or jurisdiction-specific terms when they name a township-level municipal entity, as in "thesaban tambon".
Annotate each township mention separately when several township names appear in a list.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate unrelated proper nouns, such as person names, organizations, sports teams, awards, publications, or events.
Do not annotate higher-level geographic units such as countries, provinces, states, or large regions.
Do not annotate generic nouns like "township" or "village" when they are used descriptively and not as a specific named entity.
Do not annotate names of sub-parts or parent units mentioned only for geographic relation unless they themselves are township entities.
Do not annotate adjectival or demonym forms derived from township names.
Do not annotate explanatory translations or descriptions surrounding the entity unless they are themselves the official township-level label.
boundary_rules: Include the full official name of the township-level administrative unit, including the generic unit word when it is part of the name, e.g. "Lowell Township".
For named township-level municipalities whose official names do not contain a generic suffix, annotate the complete name only, e.g. "Hunting Valley", "Moreland Hills", "Pepper Pike", "Woodmere".
When a local-language administrative label itself is the township entity being referenced, include the full label phrase, e.g. "thesaban tambon".
Do not include preceding determiners, appositives, or explanatory text outside the name, such as "the", commas, or parenthetical glosses unless the parenthetical phrase itself is the township name.
Do not extend the span to larger geographic expressions or clauses; annotate only the township unit name.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish township from broader or different settlement/place types: annotate only names referring to a township-level municipality or equivalent local administrative unit, not provinces, states, countries, regions, or generic places.

Distinguish township from neighborhoods, villages, cities, or counties: if the mention refers specifically to a township-level administrative entity, annotate it; if it refers to another level or type of place, do not.
Distinguish township from facility, organization, sports team, or event names that may contain place words; for example, team and competition names are not township entities.

Distinguish township from generic administrative terminology: annotate terms like "township" only when they denote a specific township-level entity or official unit label in context, not when used merely as a common noun.
If a place name appears in a list of municipalities and functions as a township-level local government unit, annotate it even if the word "Township" is absent from the surface form.

trait.definition: A biological trait is any named characteristic, property, phenotype, fitness-related attribute, anatomical or reproductive feature, or biologically relevant entity treated in context as a characteristic-bearing unit of an organism or biological system.
include_rules: Annotate named biological characteristics or properties, including phenotypic and functional attributes such as "virulence" and "fitness".
Annotate named anatomical, morphological, or physiological trait expressions, including multiword expressions such as "leaf succulence".

Annotate biologically relevant entities when they are treated in context as the units whose properties or relations define a trait description, as in reproductive descriptions like "plant", "pollen", and "flower".
Annotate both concrete and abstract biological trait expressions if they denote a characteristic-bearing biological entity or property rather than an event.
Annotate singular or plural noun phrases that directly name the trait or biological feature under discussion.
Annotate a trait mention even when it appears inside definitional or explanatory language, provided the span itself names the trait.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate source code, library names, pass names, file names, licenses, URLs, or other software-engineering text.
Do not annotate generic discourse words that refer to categories of traits rather than specific traits, such as standalone "trait", "characteristic", or "feature" when no specific trait is named.
Do not annotate biological processes, events, or relations unless the text explicitly uses them as the name of a trait.
Do not annotate purely contextual scientific terms that are not themselves traits, such as taxonomic group labels, methodological terms, or explanatory phrases.
Do not annotate pronouns, determiners, or generic nouns that only refer back to a trait indirectly.
Do not annotate modifiers alone when they do not constitute the trait name by themselves.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the trait, property, characteristic, structure, function, or life-stage entity, without extra determiners or surrounding explanation.

Include modifiers that are part of the trait name or establish the specific property being described, e.g. "leaf succulence" rather than only "succulence" when the modifier is integral to the named trait.
For simple biological entities used as trait-like units in context, annotate just the head noun or noun phrase, e.g. "plant", "pollen", "flower".
Do not include framing words such as "trait", "example of", "as a", "type of", or clause material outside the entity span; in "As a trait, virulence...", annotate only "virulence".
Do not include verbs, prepositions, or relational context unless they are inside a fixed trait expression.
If coordinated traits are listed separately, annotate each trait span individually unless the coordinated phrase forms one established combined trait expression.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate trait when the expression denotes a biological characteristic, phenotype, structure, function, or organismal/reproductive unit being discussed as a property-bearing entity; do not rely only on whether it is a concrete object or an abstract property.

Do not annotate process or event expressions as trait merely because they occur in biological descriptions; only annotate the property or entity itself, not actions like pollination or selection unless the wording denotes a trait name.

Distinguish trait from generic scientific context words: terms such as "family", "example", "selection", or "correlates" are not trait unless they directly name the characteristic being measured or described.
Distinguish trait from broad discourse labels: in phrases like "As a trait, virulence...", the label "trait" is not the entity; the specific characteristic "virulence" is.

Distinguish trait from software/code tokens or technical artifacts outside biological trait semantics; source-code identifiers, library names, pass names, and license text are never trait in this schema.

transportation.definition: A vehicle type, public transit mode, or named transit service/line used to transport people or goods from one place to another.
include_rules: Annotate names of transportation modes and vehicle types used for travel, such as "bus" and "train".
Annotate abbreviations or short forms that clearly denote a transit system or transport mode in context, such as "MRT".
Annotate named public transit lines or services, such as rail, metro, subway, or district/circle lines, when they identify a specific transportation service.
Annotate both common nouns and proper names when they refer to an actual means of transportation rather than a metaphor or unrelated concept.
Annotate each distinct transportation mention separately, even when several appear in the same clause or list.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate people, organizations, or traveler groups associated with movement rather than the transport medium itself.
Do not annotate locations, stations, destinations, roads, or buildings unless the mention refers directly to a transportation service or line.
Do not annotate generic travel context words such as "downtown", "fare", "minutes away", or motion verbs.
Do not annotate highly generic infrastructure words like "transport", "transit", or "line(s)" when they appear without a specific transportation name.
Do not annotate abbreviations or technical strings that are not clearly transportation in context, such as biomedical acronyms or software/code tokens.
Do not annotate text from unrelated technical, scientific, or code examples when no transportation meaning is present.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the transportation mode, transit service, or line.
For common vehicle nouns, mark only the vehicle term itself, e.g. "bus", "train".

For named transit lines, include the unique line name but exclude the generic word "line" or "lines" when the name is understandable without it, e.g. annotate "Circle" and "District" in "Circle and District lines".
If multiple transportation entities are joined by conjunctions or punctuation, annotate each separately.
Exclude surrounding prepositions, fare or distance expressions, time phrases, and destination/location context, e.g. exclude "by", "for less than S$2 fare", and place names.
Do not expand the span to include verbs or service phrases such as "served by" or "took".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate transportation when the term denotes a vehicle mode or public transit service/line used for travel, not a place or destination.
Transit line names such as "Circle" and "District" are transportation because they refer to rail/subway lines, even though the words can look like ordinary nouns or locations.
Do not annotate nearby locations, buildings, or geographic names such as downtown or Mansion House unless the text explicitly uses them as the transportation service itself.
Do not annotate organizations or groups involved in travel, such as passengers, migrants, or senators; only the transport medium is in scope.
Do not annotate prices, durations, or route descriptions; these are attributes of travel, not transportation entities.
Do not annotate technical, biomedical, or software terms that may resemble abbreviations unless they clearly denote a transport system; for example, only "MRT" in a travel context is transportation.

treatment.definition: A therapeutic intervention, treatment modality, medication-based therapy, or therapeutic material used to prevent, manage, or treat a medical condition or injury.
include_rules: Annotate noun phrases that explicitly name a therapeutic procedure or modality, including therapies ending in words such as "therapy".
Annotate medication-based treatment expressions that denote therapeutic agents used in care, such as "antiseptic drugs".
Annotate therapeutic materials or applications used to manage or protect a treated site when they function as treatment in context, such as "bandages".
Annotate named complex or composite therapies when presented as a treatment label or title, such as "Medicinal Complex Therapy".
Include both standard and descriptive treatment names when the phrase identifies what is administered, applied, or performed for medical care.
exclude_rules: Exclude generic mentions of healthcare delivery, quality of care, programs, or impactful interventions when they do not name a specific therapeutic method or therapeutic material.
Exclude diseases, disorders, opportunistic diseases, symptoms, or patient states that are targets of treatment rather than the treatment itself.
Exclude drug names or chemical substances when they are mentioned only as interacting agents, study topics, or components and not as a treatment mention in context.
Exclude verbs or verbal phrases describing the act of treating, covering, administering, or managing unless there is a noun phrase naming the treatment.
Exclude non-medical technical uses of words that may resemble treatment-related terms, such as software, services, containers, or configuration items in the Docker/nginx example.
Exclude abbreviations or aliases when the full expression is not annotated in the document context under this guideline, as illustrated by unannotated "ART" in the negative example.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the treatment, including modifiers that are part of its established name, such as material, method, or form words: "Plasma exchange therapy", "Medicinal Complex Therapy", "antiseptic drugs".
Include the treatment head noun when present, such as "therapy", "drugs", or "bandages"; do not trim these words from the span.
Exclude surrounding function words, verbs, and syntax outside the treatment name, such as "with" in "treated with antiseptic drugs" or "covered with" in "covered with bandages".

Exclude indication, target condition, patient group, or procedural purpose attached by prepositions unless they are part of the treatment name itself, e.g. annotate "Plasma exchange therapy" but not "for endotoxin shock in puppies".
If multiple treatments are coordinated or separately mentioned, annotate each treatment span separately rather than one combined span.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate diseases, symptoms, or clinical conditions as treatment. In "Plasma exchange therapy for endotoxin shock in puppies", annotate the intervention "Plasma exchange therapy", not the condition "endotoxin shock".

Do not annotate care programs, service quality, resource management actions, or broad interventions when they are discussed at a system or policy level rather than as a concrete therapeutic modality; the negative example with "highly active antiretroviral treatment (ART)" is not annotated here and should not be treated as a treatment mention for this guideline.

Do not annotate drugs merely because they are chemicals or substances unless they are mentioned in their role as therapeutic agents, as in "antiseptic drugs". Substance names appearing only in interaction, composition, or other non-treatment contexts are not treatment by default.
Do not annotate medical actions expressed only as verbs, such as "treated" or "covered"; annotate the object naming the intervention or therapeutic material instead when present.
Do not confuse treatment with medical equipment, containers, or general objects unless the object itself is being applied as therapeutic management, as in "bandages".

tv network.definition: A television broadcasting network or branded TV channel/distributor that airs television programming.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of television networks or branded TV channels that broadcast or air programming.
Include both full names and standard abbreviations of TV networks when used as the broadcaster, such as "FOX", "NBC", and "HBO".
Annotate network names appearing in scheduling or airing context, especially after cues like "on" or near verbs such as "airs" and "premiere".
Annotate the network mention even when it is short, uppercase, or stands alone, if the context clearly identifies it as the broadcaster.
Include mentions of premium cable or broadcast television brands when they function as the network airing the content.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate abbreviations or capitalized strings that are not television broadcasters, even if they look like organization names.
Do not annotate TV shows, episode titles, season references, or other content titles.
Do not annotate times, dates, frequencies, or schedule phrases associated with broadcasts.
Do not annotate generic media terms such as "network", "channel", or "show" when they are not part of a specific official network name.
Do not annotate technical, scientific, or chemical symbols and codes such as "PO", "Mg2P2O7", or similar uppercase/domain-specific strings.
Do not annotate platforms or companies unless the mention clearly denotes the TV network/channel itself.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the network name itself, e.g., "FOX", "NBC", "HBO".
Do not include preceding or following function words or scheduling context such as "on" in "on FOX".
Do not include surrounding program information, dates, times, episode titles, or descriptive nouns unless they are part of the official network name.
If the official network name contains multiple tokens, include the full name and only the full name.
For abbreviated all-caps network names, annotate the exact abbreviation span only.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate tv networks, not individual TV shows, episodes, or seasons; in "The Simpsons ... on FOX", annotate "FOX" but not the show title.
Annotate the broadcasting/distribution brand, not airtimes or channels expressed only as numeric schedule information such as "at 10".
Do not annotate scientific, chemical, financial, or technical abbreviations that happen to resemble capitalized names, such as "PO" or crystallographic notation like "P2(1)/n".
Distinguish a tv network from a broader media company or studio when the mention refers specifically to the television broadcaster/carrier of programming.
If a name refers to the outlet airing content in a television context, treat it as a tv network; if it refers to unrelated organizations or domain-specific abbreviations, do not annotate it.

tv series.definition: A named television series or web series title, including a specific season mention when the text uses that full phrase to refer to the show installment.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of television series, including scripted, animated, reality, and web-distributed series.
Annotate a series title when it appears alone, e.g. "Game of Thrones".
Annotate a series title when it appears within a larger noun phrase referring to a season or installment of that show, e.g. "first season of Grimm".
Annotate titles of online video series when the context indicates an ongoing show rather than a single video, e.g. "Annoying Orange".
Annotate the title even when it appears in release, viewing, streaming, or popularity contexts such as premieres, online leaks, subscribers, or home-video releases.
Annotate multiword titles in full, preserving all words that belong to the series name.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic references to television content such as "the show", "series", "season", or "episode" when no specific title is given.
Do not annotate platform, format, or distribution terms such as "DVD", "Blu-ray", "YouTube channel", or dates unless they are part of the actual series title.
Do not annotate unrelated capitalized text from software code, product listings, forum posts, or advertisements when it is not a TV-series title.
Do not annotate names of channels, websites, networks, or accounts unless the mention itself is the title of a tv series.
Do not annotate character names, actor names, or creator names unless the same span is explicitly the series title.
Do not annotate broad franchise references when the context is about merchandise, branding, or a non-television property rather than the series itself.
boundary_rules: Mark the smallest span that uniquely identifies the tv series name in context.

Include season/series installment wording only when it is part of the referenced title span in context, as in "first season of Grimm" from the example; do not split off the series name if the mention is clearly to that installment of the show.
Include leading articles or integral words only if they are part of the commonly used title mention.
Exclude surrounding verbs, prepositions, release formats, dates, and other non-title context, e.g. exclude words like "hits DVD and Blu-ray on August 7th" around a show mention.

For channel or franchise names that are also used as the title of a show, annotate only the title string itself, not the platform descriptor, e.g. annotate "Annoying Orange" but not "YouTube channel".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a tv series with a TV episode, season number alone, or release event. Annotate the show title mention; only include season wording when the full mention refers to that installment of the show, as in "first season of Grimm".
Do not confuse a tv series with a film, book, song, video game, or other creative work. Only annotate titles that denote a television series or web series.

Do not confuse a tv series with a YouTube channel, streaming service page, network, or distributor. If the title string names the show and appears inside a channel/platform phrase, annotate only the show title, not the platform term.
Do not confuse a tv series with a franchise, brand, or merchandise line unless the context specifically refers to the television show itself.
Do not annotate person names, product names, software/class names, or generic capitalized phrases that are not titles of television series.

tv show.definition: A named television program or series title, including scripted, animated, reality, competition, news, or other broadcast/streamed TV show titles.
include_rules: Annotate proper names that refer to a specific television show or series title.
Include titles of animated television programs, as shown by "Bungo Stray Dogs" when referenced as a "TV anime".
Include titles of recurring television programs with episodes, as shown by "Southern Routes" in the context of "classic October episodes".
Include titles of television competition or reality programs, as shown by "The Voice".
Annotate the show title even when it appears with surrounding descriptive context about creators, seasons, episodes, or awards, as long as the mention denotes the TV program itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic references to television content that are not specific titles, such as "TV anime", "show", "series", "episode", or "talent competition" used descriptively.
Do not annotate seasons, episodes, or installment numbers by themselves, such as "season 15" or "October episodes".
Do not annotate names of people, production companies, networks, sports teams, clubs, or other organizations mentioned near a show title.
Do not annotate book, manga, film, song, or other non-television titles unless the context clearly identifies the mention as a TV show.
Do not annotate capitalized text that is not an entertainment title, such as medical article titles, competition names in sports, championships, or profile headings.
boundary_rules: Annotate the title only: include all words that are part of the TV show name, including leading articles when part of the official title, e.g., "The Voice".

Exclude medium descriptors and format words that are outside the title, such as "TV anime", "show", "series", "episode", "season", "talent competition", unless they are part of the official title itself.
When a title is followed or preceded by contextual words, select only the title span, e.g., annotate "Bungo Stray Dogs" in "Bungo Stray Dogs TV anime".
Do not include season or episode numbers unless they are officially embedded in the program title; in "season 15 of The Voice", annotate only "The Voice".
Do not include surrounding punctuation or explanatory appositives unless they are part of the official title.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the television program itself, not people associated with it; directors, actors, hosts, contestants, and winners are not tv show entities.

Do not confuse a tv show with an episode title: if the mention refers to a specific episode of a program rather than the series/program as a whole, do not annotate it as tv show unless the episode title is also the program title.
Do not confuse a tv show with a season reference; "season 15" is not a tv show, while "The Voice" is.
Do not confuse a tv show with a generic genre or format label such as "TV anime" or "American talent competition"; these describe the type of program, not its title.

Do not confuse a tv show with organizations, teams, publications, or medical/scientific text strings appearing as capitalized phrases; only annotate phrases that denote an entertainment television program title.

type.definition: A common-noun expression that denotes a kind, class, subtype, format, method, or other category of thing, rather than a specific named instance.
include_rules: Annotate expressions that explicitly name a category or subtype, especially in technical or classificatory contexts, such as topology variants.
Annotate common nouns that denote a format, medium, or content form, such as "thread" or "show" when used as labels for kinds of content.
Annotate common nouns that denote a document, instrument, or collection method type, such as "questionnaires".
Annotate subtype expressions that combine a modifier with a head category noun when the modifier is necessary to identify the type, e.g. "norm topology".
Annotate abbreviated or elliptical subtype mentions when the head category is omitted but understood from context, as with coordinated mentions like "weak" standing for a topology subtype.
Annotate singular or plural mentions when they refer to the category/type itself rather than a specific named instance.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate proper names of specific entities such as products, companies, people, or locations.
Do not annotate mentions of specific individual objects, beings, or items when they are not being used to denote a category or subtype.
Do not annotate broad topical nouns that describe a subject, issue, or domain rather than a type label, such as "technology" or "problem".
Do not annotate action/event nouns, analytic activities, or processes unless they clearly function as names of a type or format.
Do not annotate surrounding generic context words that are not themselves the type, such as "original", "broadcast", "tracks", "ages", or "menarche" in the examples.
Do not annotate words that merely describe features, components, or specifications of an item unless they denote a recognized type/category expression.
Do not annotate full noun phrases when only the head type word or subtype expression is the type mention.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the type itself, not the full larger noun phrase unless the modifiers are part of the type name.
Include descriptive modifiers when they distinguish a subtype or variant of a type, as in "norm topology".

If a symbolic or orthographic marker is attached to the type expression and changes the subtype, include it as part of the type mention when possible, e.g. "weak$^*$ topology"; if tokenization prevents this, annotate the core subtype term actually marked in the data.
For coordinated lists, annotate each type mention separately, e.g. "norm topology" and "weak" in "norm topology, the weak topology and the weak$^*$ topology."
Do not include determiners, prepositions, or surrounding explanatory text such as "the", "for", "of", or relative clauses.
For artifact or document-type nouns, annotate only the common noun naming the type, e.g. "questionnaires" not "from questionnaires".
For media/content labels, annotate the generic content/form type word alone when that is the type mention, e.g. "thread" and "show".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate common nouns that denote a category, class, format, method, or subtype, not individual named instances of things.
Do not confuse a type mention with a named product, organization, place, or person. In the negative example, "iPad", "Apple", and "San Francisco" are named entities of other kinds, not type mentions.

Do not annotate concrete individual objects when the text is about specific entities rather than their category. In the negative example, repeated mentions of "dogs" refer to actual animals in a situation, not a formal type label to extract here.

Differentiate type terms from events or issues: words like "problem", "study", or "analysis" describe situations or activities, not category/type labels unless they explicitly denote a recognized type/form.

Differentiate type terms from attributes or measurements: hardware specifications such as "9.7 inch", "1 gigahertz", or feature names in product descriptions are not the target unless they are used as a type label.
When a word could be either a generic noun or a type term, annotate it only if context shows it labels a kind, form, or subtype, as with topology names or document/media formats.

ui element.definition: A ui element is a named on-screen interface component or widget in a software application that users can see, click, tap, select, or otherwise interact with, such as buttons, controls, and similar interface parts.
include_rules: Annotate names of concrete interface controls and widgets, including standard GUI elements such as buttons and segmented controls.
Annotate both common-language UI element names and formal framework/class names when they denote an interface component, e.g. "button" and "UISegmentedControl".
Annotate multiword UI element mentions when the full phrase identifies the component type, e.g. "modal button".
Annotate a UI element whether it is mentioned in natural language description, bug report, or programming/development context, as long as it refers to the interface component itself.
Annotate singular or plural mentions of interface components if the text names the component directly.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate terms from unrelated domains, even if they are capitalized or look technical, such as medical procedures, scales, inventories, or article section headings.
Do not annotate actions, events, or behaviors involving an interface element, such as "clicked", "shows", or "happens".
Do not annotate generic software content or data shown inside an element, such as "text", unless the term explicitly names a UI component.
Do not annotate device, platform, OS, or version references such as "iOS 6" or "iOS 7" when they merely describe environment or style.
Do not annotate broad application areas, scripts, code, or programming concepts that are not interface widgets themselves.
Do not annotate highly generic nouns unless the context clearly indicates they refer to a concrete interface component; only label them when they function as UI element mentions, as with "button".
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the interface component, such as "button", "modal button", or "UISegmentedControl".
Include modifiers that are part of the UI element name or subtype, such as appearance/style or functional subtype words when they directly specify the element: "modal button".
Do not extend the span to include surrounding verbs, determiners, or event context, e.g. annotate "button" in "the button is clicked" but not "the button is clicked".
Do not include platform/version/product words unless they are part of the element's official name. In "iOS 6 UISegmentedControl", annotate only "UISegmentedControl".
When a technical class/control name appears as a single token or code-style identifier, annotate the full identifier exactly as written, e.g. "UISegmentedControl".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate UI components themselves, not user actions performed on them. For example, in click-related text, label "button" but not "clicked" or "click".
Do not annotate operating systems, versions, frameworks, or programming environments unless the token itself is the UI element. Thus "iOS 6" and "iOS 7" are not ui element mentions.
Do not annotate general software behavior, states, or content shown by an element, such as "text" in "shows the text", unless the word itself refers to a UI control name.
Distinguish ui elements from non-interface domain terms or document titles. Capitalized technical or scientific names are not ui elements unless they denote an on-screen control/widget.
If a phrase refers to a procedure, method, or abstract concept rather than a visible or interactive screen component, it is not a ui element.

unit.definition: A word, abbreviation, symbol, or fixed label that denotes a unit of measurement or an analogous unit-like label used to express the scale, rate, distance, concentration, or other measured/quantified value in context.
include_rules: Annotate standard measurement units and scientific unit abbreviations, such as frequency, distance, concentration, or similar units, e.g. "Hz", "mm", "mu M".
Annotate abbreviated or symbolic unit strings that accompany a numeric measurement, even when written in compact form.
Annotate unit-like labels that serve as the denomination or measurement label for a reported value in context, as in "Ki".

Annotate fixed labels functioning as units even when they are nonstandard or domain-specific names, including multiword labels such as "PS Rydia" and single-token labels such as "Citra" when used as unit names in context.
Annotate repeated occurrences whenever the token span denotes a unit or unit-like denomination in the sentence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate standalone numbers, ranges, percentages, dates, ages, or counts unless the token itself is the unit label.
Do not annotate full measurement expressions that combine number plus unit; only the unit portion should be tagged.
Do not annotate ordinary nouns that describe measured entities rather than units, such as materials, body parts, or objects.
Do not annotate generic text that is unrelated to quantification or unit-like labeling, even if it contains short abbreviations or capitalized tokens.
Do not annotate book titles, person names, locations, organizations, or other named entities unless they are explicitly functioning as unit-like labels in this annotation scheme.
Do not annotate mass terms written without a unit word, e.g. "60 pounds of marijuana" is not evidence to tag non-unit tokens like "marijuana".
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the unit itself, not the adjacent number or measured quantity: annotate "mu M" in "6 mu M", not "6 mu M".
Include prefixes and symbols that are part of the conventional unit expression, such as Greek-letter or micro prefixes rendered as text, e.g. "mu M".
For abbreviated units, include the full abbreviation exactly as written, e.g. "Hz", "mm", "Ki", "PS Rydia", "Citra".
Do not include surrounding prepositions, operators, punctuation, or descriptive nouns unless they are part of the unit name.
When a unit-like label is multiword, include all tokens that together function as the unit name, e.g. "PS Rydia".
If a sentence contains both a number and a unit, the boundary starts after the number and ends at the last token of the unit string.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse unit with numeric values: in "6 mu M" and "10 Hz", only "mu M" and "Hz" are unit, while "6" and "10" are not.

Do not confuse unit with physical quantities or variables being measured: in "Ki of 6 mu M", "Ki" is annotated only because it functions as the relevant unit-like label in this schema; surrounding measurement context words are not included unless they are themselves the unit string.
Do not confuse unit with ordinary objects, substances, or locations appearing near measurements, such as "marijuana" or "Vermont"; these are not units.
Do not annotate general nouns indicating countable items or collective groups unless they are used as a fixed unit name.

Strings that look like names or abbreviations should be annotated only when they function as a unit label in context, as shown by examples like "Citra" and "PS Rydia", not merely because they are capitalized.

unit of measurement.definition: A word, symbol, abbreviation, or compound expression that denotes a standardized unit used to quantify magnitude, amount, size, concentration, storage, distance, or similar measurable properties.
include_rules: Annotate standard measurement unit symbols and abbreviations such as storage, length, mass, volume, and similar unit markers, e.g. "KB".
Annotate spelled-out or lexicalized unit forms, including plural forms, when they denote measurable scale, e.g. "microns".
Annotate compound units formed with slashes or other connectors when the full expression denotes a measurement basis or concentration, e.g. "microg/l urine".
Annotate units that appear after a number in prose when the unit term itself is explicitly present, even if the number is excluded from the span.
Annotate very short unit strings if context shows they denote a measurement unit rather than another abbreviation.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate pure numbers or numeric ranges without the unit.
Do not annotate full measured quantities consisting of number plus unit; only the unit portion is the entity.
Do not annotate non-measurement abbreviations or acronyms, even if short and uppercase, such as profession titles or technical labels.
Do not annotate variable names followed by unit notation in formulaic assignments, e.g. voltage parameter expressions like "Vcg=12 [V]".
Do not annotate generic nouns for objects, materials, body specimens, or dimensions unless they are part of a fixed compound unit expression.
Do not annotate section numbers, figure numbers, reference numerals, or item counts.
Do not annotate words like "size", "deep", "wide", or "urine" by themselves when they are not part of the unit string.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that expresses the measurement unit itself.
Include attached symbols, prefixes, slashes, and denominator material when they are part of a compound unit, e.g. "microg/l urine".
Include pluralized unit words when they function as units, e.g. "microns".
Exclude preceding numbers, approximators, and comparison markers such as "0.05", "129", "1,000", "ca.", "about".
Exclude surrounding punctuation or brackets unless the bracketed content is exactly the unit token.

For expressions written as number plus bracketed unit, do not annotate the bracketed unit alone when the notation is part of a variable assignment or formula-like engineering notation, e.g. "12 [V]" in the negative example.
Do not extend the span to the measured object unless it is explicitly built into the unit expression after a slash or other unit linker, as in "l urine" within "microg/l urine".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a unit of measurement with a numeric quantity: annotate "KB" but not "129 KB"; annotate "microns" but not "30 microns".
Do not annotate abbreviations that are role labels, professions, variable names, or general acronyms rather than units, e.g. "RN", "Vcg", "Vs", "Vd".
Do not annotate physical attributes, substances, or objects being measured; only annotate the standardized unit expression attached to them.
Do not treat device, chemistry, or engineering notation as a unit entity when the token is functioning as part of a formula/parameter assignment rather than as a standalone measurement unit mention.
If a span combines a unit with a specimen or basis as part of a conventional concentration/rate unit, keep the full compound unit; otherwise, leave contextual nouns outside the unit span.

url.definition: A URL is a textual web or internet address that identifies the location of an online resource, typically expressed as a domain or full link such as an http/https address.
include_rules: Annotate explicit web addresses that point to online resources.
Include URLs beginning with a web protocol such as http:// or https://.
Include the full address when it contains a domain plus optional path segments, as in links to specific pages or files.
Annotate URLs appearing in running text, references, or instructions when they are written as literal address strings.
Include both top-level site URLs and deeper page/file URLs when they are syntactically valid web addresses.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary text, technical prose, or document content that does not express an internet address.
Do not annotate local section markers, bibliography anchors, markdown references, or other internal document IDs such as {#section...} or [@bibr...].
Do not annotate generic mentions of websites, online resources, or documents when no actual URL string is present.
Do not annotate isolated domain-unrelated strings, file-like names, or identifiers unless they clearly function as a URL.
Do not include adjacent explanatory text, labels, or verbs introducing the URL.
boundary_rules: Include the full URL string as written, starting from the protocol if present (e.g., http:// or https://) and continuing through the domain and any path components.
Include internal punctuation that is part of the URL, such as : / . - _ ? = # and similar standard URL characters when they occur inside the address.
