Exclude surrounding prose punctuation or spacing that is not part of the address, such as a trailing period, comma, or enclosing brackets, unless the character is clearly part of the URL itself.
Do not include preceding cue words such as visit, see, from, or for license information; only annotate the URL token sequence itself.
If a URL is followed by separated punctuation or whitespace, end the span at the last character belonging to the address.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate a string as url only when it denotes a web address or online resource locator, not merely a named website, organization, or document title.

Do not label citation anchors, section identifiers, or markup fragments as url when they are local document references rather than web addresses, even if they contain symbols like #, braces, or hyphens.
Do not annotate technical strings, codes, or alphanumeric identifiers unless they function as an actual URL.
If a span names a site in plain text without giving the address format, it is not url; the url type is for the address string itself.
When a sentence contains both descriptive text and a web address, annotate only the address, not the surrounding description of the resource.

user.definition: A person, user account, or username identified as the actor or access identity in a computing, software, database, or system context.
include_rules: Annotate explicit usernames or account names in technical contexts, such as login names, command-line user arguments, or ownership identities, e.g. "root".
Annotate mentions of a named user/account when the context directly identifies it as a user, even if the same string could also name something else, e.g. "the user sudo" -> "sudo".
Annotate common-noun mentions of people acting as system, database, or application users when they are described as accessing or using computing resources, e.g. "users access their data resources".
Annotate both singular and plural mentions when they semantically refer to users/accounts in a computing environment.
Annotate user mentions whether they refer to a real person in the role of user or to a system account, as long as the mention functions as the access identity.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate biomedical, legal, scientific, or other non-computing uses of words that happen to resemble usernames, such as "patient", "person", or unrelated common nouns in the negative examples.
Do not annotate devices, software, databases, services, commands, files, or groups unless the text explicitly states they are a user/account.
Do not annotate abstract references to access or control when no user or account is named, e.g. generic descriptions of a system without a user mention.
Do not annotate pronouns or determiners referring to users indirectly, such as "their" or "one's".
Do not annotate adjectives or compounds that merely modify other nouns unless they themselves denote users/accounts.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that names the user or user account, usually a single token such as "root" or "sudo".
Include plural or singular common-noun forms when they denote users as actors or account holders, e.g. "users".
Do not include surrounding command syntax, flags, punctuation, or service names; in "mysql -u root -p", annotate only "root".
Do not extend the span to include descriptive words unless they are part of a fixed username string.
When a username follows an appositive or explanatory noun, annotate only the username token, e.g. in "the user sudo", annotate only "sudo".
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish user from software, commands, or services: annotate the account or person identifier, not adjacent system terms such as "mysql", "sudo" as a command/group, or "service" unless the text explicitly says it is the user/account name.

Distinguish user from groups or roles: if a token names a group, role, or permission class rather than a user/account, do not annotate it; only annotate it when the context explicitly identifies it as a user.
Distinguish user from generic human references: annotate common nouns like "users" only when they clearly mean system/application/database users, not people in general.
Distinguish user from ordinary lexical uses of the same string: words like "root" should be annotated only when they denote a login/account name, not when used with another everyday meaning.
Distinguish user from data/resources/devices: entities that are accessed, controlled, recorded, or analyzed are not users unless they are the actor/account performing access.

user interface element.definition: A named on-screen or software interface object, control, widget, component, or structural part of a graphical or application interface that a user can see, interact with, or that organizes interaction.
include_rules: Annotate names of GUI controls, widgets, and component classes used in software or app interfaces, such as "EditText" and "TextViews".
Annotate named interface parts that structure or decorate a window or screen, such as "title bar".
Annotate pointer and cursor interface objects, such as "mouse cursor".
Annotate generic component terms when they explicitly refer to interface building blocks in context, such as "component".
Annotate form-related and interactive web/app elements, such as "form", "link", and "dropdownchoice".
Annotate both singular and plural mentions of interface elements when they refer to UI objects.
Annotate interface element mentions even when they appear in technical discussion about frameworks, layouts, or implementation, as long as the term denotes the UI element itself.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate general prose, article text, or ordinary nouns that are not referring to software or graphical interface components.
Do not annotate non-UI physical objects, astronomical objects, religious terms, historical entities, or other real-world entities appearing in surrounding text.
Do not annotate whole screens, windows, or applications unless the mention specifically names a sub-element or control within the interface.
Do not annotate purely positional or visual descriptions such as "top of the screen" unless they are part of the actual element name.
Do not annotate actions, states, or behaviors such as clicking, grouping, hiding, or rendering; only the interface object itself.
Do not annotate code-related or technical words unless they denote a UI class/component/control in context.
Do not annotate generic list markers, captions, headlines, or media labels from documents or web pages when they are not UI elements in the sentence context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal noun phrase that names the interface element itself: "EditText", "TextViews", "mouse cursor", "title bar", "form", "link", "dropdownchoice".
Include multiword element names when all words are part of the UI element name, such as "mouse cursor" and "title bar".
Do not include surrounding determiners, quantities, or descriptive context unless they are part of the element name; for example, annotate "title bar", not "the title bar".
Do not include app, framework, or product names attached to the element unless they are part of the element's canonical name.
When a generic head noun explicitly refers to a UI element in context, it may be annotated alone, as in "component".
If several UI element names appear in a list, annotate each element separately rather than the whole list or parenthetical expression.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate interface parts and controls, not entire software systems, applications, websites, or platforms.
Annotate visible or interactive UI objects and named widget classes, not user actions or events involving them.
Annotate UI-specific words only when they denote interface elements; do not label the same surface form when used with a non-UI meaning, such as ordinary document editing text.
Distinguish a UI element from layout/location language: words like "top of the screen" describe position, not an interface element.
Framework or programming vocabulary should be annotated only when it names a UI component class or control, as with "EditText" or "TextViews"; other technical terms are excluded.
Generic nouns should be annotated only when the sentence makes clear they refer to interface components; otherwise leave broad technical nouns untagged.

value.definition: A literal or enumerated value assigned to, selected for, or stated as the content of a parameter, property, setting, field, mode, or representation, including numeric values and fixed symbolic/textual values.
include_rules: Annotate explicit values given for parameters, properties, settings, fields, or options.
Annotate numeric values when they are stated as the actual value of something, including integers like "0".
Annotate textual or symbolic option values when they represent a selected mode, level, format, or enumeration, such as "Full".
Annotate fixed representational values or coded value tokens used as the content of a variable or setting, such as "RGB" in a conversion or representation context.
Annotate values introduced by constructions such as "is", "set to", "equals", "default ... is", or similar assignment/selection language.
Annotate both alphabetic and alphanumeric values if they function as the literal value rather than the name of the attribute.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary words, scientific concepts, or technical terminology that are mentioned descriptively rather than as a specific value.
Do not annotate section numbers, list numbering, citation markers, figure/table labels, patent formatting artifacts, or document structure markers.
Do not annotate measurement descriptions, formulas, or symbolic expressions when they are not being used as a concrete assigned value.
Do not annotate names of parameters, properties, menu items, commands, classes, or data structures unless the mention itself is the value.
Do not annotate broad qualitative mentions like "high", "late", or similar adjectives when they describe a property generally rather than giving an explicit set value.
Do not annotate standalone letters, abbreviations, or symbols that name a concept instead of serving as a literal option/value in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal span that expresses the value itself, without surrounding labels or descriptors.
Include only the token(s) that constitute the value, such as "RGB", "Full", or "0".
Exclude parameter names, option labels, units, punctuation, arrows, and surrounding syntax; for example, in "oplogging parameter is 0", annotate only "0".
When a value appears after configuration paths or UI labels, annotate only the final assigned setting, not the path leading to it.
If a value is part of a longer noun phrase, include only the portion functioning as the literal value, not the entire phrase.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate variable names, field names, parameter names, or property names; annotate only the value assigned to them.
Do not annotate general technical terms or domain concepts unless they are explicitly used as a value or setting in context.
Distinguish values from identifiers or labels: a value fills a slot such as a setting, parameter, code, mode, or representation, while an identifier names the slot itself.
Do not annotate quantities mentioned only descriptively in running text when they are not functioning as a specific assigned or stated value.
If a token could be both a concept and a value, annotate it only when the sentence makes clear it is the chosen/actual value of some attribute, option, or parameter.

variable.definition: A variable is a symbolic placeholder, identifier, or named value used in code, formulas, or formal reasoning to stand for data, an argument, or an abstract quantity.
include_rules: Annotate symbols, letters, or words that explicitly function as variables in mathematical, logical, or programming context.
Annotate single-letter placeholders used in expressions or discussion of formulas, such as "a" or "x".
Annotate identifiers that denote a value being passed, returned, evaluated, assigned, or otherwise manipulated in code-like context.
Annotate common-word variable names when they are clearly used as program variables or abstract placeholders, such as "code" in an evaluation context.
Annotate variables whether they appear standalone or inside larger notation, as long as the token itself is the variable name.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate ordinary natural-language words that are not being used as symbolic placeholders or identifiers.
Do not annotate entire documents, comments, licenses, dates, or narrative text with no variable usage.
Do not annotate constants, literal values, or fixed outputs such as numbers unless they are explicitly presented as variable names.
Do not annotate function names, method names, keywords, operators, or control-flow words when they are not variables.
Do not annotate object types, classes, libraries, or software names unless they are explicitly used as variable identifiers.
Do not annotate descriptive mentions of data or content when the term is generic rather than acting as a variable in context.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal token sequence that names the variable itself.
For indexed or function-like notation, tag only the variable symbol, not surrounding operators or delimiters; e.g. in "f(x)", annotate "x" only.
Do not include articles, determiners, punctuation, or nearby verbs unless they are part of the variable name itself.
For ordinary word-form variable names, annotate just the noun or identifier used as the placeholder or stored value; e.g. annotate "code" in "eval the code".
If a variable is expressed as a single letter or symbol in prose or formulas, annotate only that symbol.
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse a variable with a function name: in expressions like "f(x)", annotate the argument variable "x", not the function "f".

Do not annotate general topical nouns unless they are used as placeholders, identifiers, or values manipulated in an operation; "code" is a variable only when referred to as something being evaluated, assigned, returned, passed, etc.
Do not annotate pronouns or discourse references such as "it", "this", or "that" even when they refer to a variable-like thing indirectly.

Do not confuse variables with full mathematical expressions, statements, or return values; annotate the named placeholder only, not surrounding expression text such as "returns 3" or "not y" unless the token itself is the variable.
Do not annotate natural-language uses of letters or words when they are not serving as symbolic placeholders, parameters, or identifiers.

vehicle.definition: A vehicle is a physical conveyance designed or used to transport people or goods from one place to another, such as aircraft, trucks, or motorcycles.
include_rules: Annotate common nouns and noun phrases that explicitly denote vehicles used for transportation.
Include land, air, or other transport conveyances when the phrase clearly refers to the vehicle itself.
Annotate both singular and plural vehicle mentions.
Include subtype-specifying modifiers that are necessary to identify the vehicle class, such as "passenger" in "passenger plane" and "pickup" in "pickup truck".
Annotate vehicle mentions regardless of whether they refer to owned, used, targeted, or invented vehicles.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate abstract transportation actions or services, such as "ride", when no vehicle is named.
Do not annotate people associated with transportation, such as drivers, passengers, or travelers.
Do not annotate locations, routes, destinations, or facilities connected to travel.
Do not annotate non-vehicle objects, even if mobile or engineered, such as nanoparticles, firearms, or other equipment.
Do not annotate generic references to transport when the text does not name a specific vehicle type.
Do not annotate legal, scientific, or procedural terms that may resemble vehicle-related words but do not denote a conveyance.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous noun phrase that names the vehicle, including essential modifiers that specify the vehicle subtype, e.g., "passenger plane" and "pickup truck".
Include the head vehicle noun and adjectival or compound modifiers that are part of the conventional vehicle name or subtype.
Exclude surrounding determiners, numerals, and nonessential descriptive words unless they are part of the established vehicle name.
Do not extend the span to include attached equipment, cargo locations, or usage phrases outside the vehicle mention itself.
For plural mentions, annotate the full plural vehicle noun, e.g., "motorcycles".
confusable_type_rules: Annotate only physical means of transportation; do not annotate general movement events such as "ride".
Do not annotate weapons, machines, or other man-made objects unless they are specifically used as transport vehicles.
Do not annotate containers, particles, or technical objects such as "nanoparticles"; despite being physical objects, they are not vehicles.
If a phrase refers to a vehicle part, location on a vehicle, or accessory rather than the vehicle itself, do not annotate it unless the full vehicle name is explicitly present.
When a phrase contains both a vehicle and another entity, annotate only the vehicle portion if separable.

venue.definition: A venue is a specifically named physical place or facility where events, performances, games, or gatherings are held, such as a stadium, field, hall, arena, theater, or similar site.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of specific physical facilities used to host sports, performances, concerts, or similar events.
Include named stadiums, fields, halls, arenas, theaters, clubs, centers, and comparable event locations when they function as the place where something occurred or is scheduled.
Annotate the venue name whether it appears after location cues such as "at", "in", or verbs like "played" and "performed".
Annotate venues that are buildings or grounds associated with institutions or cities, as long as the mention refers to the specific named facility rather than the broader institution or place.
Annotate both indoor and outdoor named event sites if they are presented as the location of an activity or gathering.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate names of cities, states, countries, regions, or neighborhoods unless the place name itself refers to a specific venue.
Do not annotate organizations, teams, universities, newspapers, companies, websites, or government bodies, even if they are mentioned near an event location.
Do not annotate generic common nouns for places such as "stadium", "field", "hall", "arena", or "venue" when they are not part of a unique proper name.
Do not annotate event names, competitions, performances, or sports matches as venue.
Do not annotate addresses, mailing/shipping destinations, or vague location phrases that do not name a specific facility.
Do not annotate products, brands, or commercial text that happen to contain place-like wording but do not denote an event facility.
boundary_rules: Annotate the full proper name of the venue, including attached venue designators that are part of the official name, such as "Stadium", "Field", or "Hall".
Include hyphens, apostrophes, and capitalization that belong to the venue name, e.g., "Mercedes-Benz Stadium".
Do not include preceding prepositions or surrounding context words such as "at", "in", "throughout", "played", or "matches at".
Do not include the event, team, or organization associated with the venue unless it is part of the venue's official name.
If a larger location phrase contains both a venue and a city/area, annotate only the venue portion, not the broader place name unless the broader place itself is being used as the venue name.
When a generic venue noun appears without a unique name, do not annotate it.
confusable_type_rules: Distinguish venue from geographic locations: annotate "Carnegie Hall" and "Mercedes-Benz Stadium" as venue because they are specific facilities/buildings where events occur, but do not annotate broader places like "Brooklyn" or "New York City" as venue.
Distinguish venue from organizations or teams: names such as "Atlanta United" or "Chicago Fire" refer to sports teams, not the physical place where an event happens, so they are not venue.

Distinguish venue from institutions: if a name refers to a university, newspaper, company, or website rather than the physical event site, do not label it as venue unless the text clearly names a specific facility within that institution.
Distinguish venue from event descriptors: phrases like "men's soccer matches" or "throughout the season" describe events or time, not the location/facility, so they are not venue.
Distinguish venue from generic addresses or shipping destinations: broad phrases such as "any deal with on the planet" or website-related text are not specific event or performance venues.

verb.definition: A verb is a word functioning as a predicate that expresses an action, event, process, or state in the sentence.
include_rules: Annotate words that function as verbs in context, including finite verbs expressing an action or event, such as "dominated".
Annotate base-form verbs when they appear as verbs in examples, commands, quoted strings, or metalinguistic mention, such as "go".
Annotate lexical verbs in verbal idioms or semi-fixed constructions when they still function as verbs, such as "help" and "notice" in "can't help but notice".
Include main verbs regardless of tense, aspect, or agreement, as long as the token functions verbally in the sentence.
Annotate each verb separately when multiple verbs occur in the same clause or construction and each is a verbal head or complement verb.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate sentences or spans that contain no actual verb token to mark, even if they discuss actions or processes abstractly.
Do not annotate nouns, including common nouns, proper nouns, species names, market segments, or technical terms.
Do not annotate nominalized action words or abstract event nouns such as words ending in forms like -tion or other noun uses of actions.
Do not annotate adjectives, adverbs, determiners, pronouns, prepositions, conjunctions, or punctuation.
Do not annotate auxiliary-only material such as negation, tense markers, or support verbs when the target is the main lexical verb.
Do not annotate the infinitival marker "to" or particles/symbols attached to search or command syntax unless they are themselves verbs.
boundary_rules: Mark only the verb token(s) themselves, not surrounding auxiliaries, subjects, objects, complements, particles, punctuation, or quotation marks.

For multi-word verbal constructions, annotate only the lexical verb(s) that express the action or event unless the expression is written as separate verbs that each function verbally; e.g. annotate "help" and "notice" separately in "can't help but notice".
Do not include determiners, adjectives, or nouns adjacent to a verb.
If a verb appears inside quotation marks or code-like text, annotate only the verb form itself; e.g. annotate "go" in "\"go *\"" but exclude the asterisk and quotes.
Use the surface form exactly as it appears in the text, including inflection such as tense or agreement; e.g. "dominated" rather than its lemma "dominate".
confusable_type_rules: Do not confuse verbs with nouns derived from verbs or action names; annotate only words functioning as predicates/actions in context, not nominalizations such as "search" when used as a noun.
Do not annotate adjectives or participles when they describe a noun rather than function verbally.
Do not annotate auxiliary or modal words by themselves unless they are the main lexical verb; focus on the content verb expressing the event or state.
Do not annotate entity names, technical terms, or domain-specific labels unless the token is actually used as a verb in the sentence.
When a word can be both noun and verb, decide by syntactic role: annotate it only if it functions as the predicate or part of a verbal predicate in the sentence.

version.definition: A named software, programming language, protocol, or technical product version identifier, including product names used in explicit version context and formal release labels that denote a specific edition or version.
include_rules: Annotate names of software products or programming technologies when they appear as the item whose version is being discussed, e.g. 'PHP', 'CFML', 'postfix'.
Annotate formal version/release identifiers for languages or technologies, especially alphanumeric edition labels, e.g. 'ES2015'.
Annotate the minimal expression that uniquely identifies the versioned technology or release.
Include both abbreviated and full product/language names if they function as the version target in phrases like 'X version' or 'version of X'.

Annotate version mentions whether the exact numeric release is omitted, as long as the mention clearly refers to the version of a specific software/product rather than the generic concept of versioning.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic words like 'version' when they are not themselves the identifier.
Do not annotate descriptive modifiers such as 'latest', 'current', 'old', or 'newest' without a specific version name or identifier.
Do not annotate standalone numbers, years, or codes that are not used as software/language/product version identifiers.
Do not annotate operating system release mentions or other numbered product references when they function as environment details rather than the target version entity, e.g. 'Ubuntu 14.04 version'.
Do not annotate commands, environment variables, file paths, error codes, exit codes, or build/tool output such as 'ANDROID_HOME', 'PATH', or 'exit code 2'.
Do not annotate historical years, event titles, or other non-technical numbered expressions such as '1953 Ceylonese Hartal'.
Do not annotate broader software/project names mentioned without version semantics in the local context.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the token(s) that name the versioned release or product identifier itself, not surrounding trigger words such as 'version', 'latest version of', 'using', or 'language'.
Include alphanumeric version strings when the version designation is itself the release name, e.g. 'ES2015'.
When a product name appears in context explicitly indicating software/version reference, annotate only the product name, e.g. 'PHP', 'CFML', 'postfix'.
Do not extend the span to include adjectives or modifiers such as 'latest', 'current', or ordinal/descriptive text unless they are part of the official version string.
Do not include trailing punctuation or surrounding parentheses unless the parentheses are part of the tokenized version name; in '(Until ES2015)', annotate only 'ES2015'.
confusable_type_rules: Do not annotate operating system names, platforms, or software ecosystems unless they are the item being referred to as a versioned software/product name in context; for example, 'Ubuntu 14.04 version' is not annotated here.

Do not annotate numeric years or dates when they denote calendar time or historical periods rather than a software/language release; '1953' in a historical event is not a version, while 'ES2015' is a versioned language edition.
Distinguish version from generic product/software mentions by requiring explicit or clear version/release context, such as 'version of postfix' or a recognized release identifier like 'ES2015'.
Distinguish version from organizations, events, or places: names such as political parties, countries, and demonstrations are never versions even if they contain numbers or capitalization patterns.
If a mention is only a general technology/product reference with no release/version meaning in context, do not annotate it as version.

video game.definition: A named, specific video game title, including standalone games and installment titles, as referred to in text.
include_rules: Annotate names of specific video games mentioned in running text, such as standalone titles or installments in a franchise.
Include titles that contain common function words, abbreviations, numerals, or year-like sequel markers when they are part of the official title, e.g., "Gears of War", "God of War", and "KOF 2003".
Annotate a title even when it appears with nearby gameplay, release, or platform context, as long as the span itself denotes the game title.
Include shortened or acronym forms only when the context indicates they are being used as the name of a specific video game.
Annotate the title whether it refers to a current release, a past game, or a game discussed as part of a review, announcement, or comparison.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic nouns or categories such as "game", "fighting series", or "series" when they do not name a specific title.
Do not annotate platforms, consoles, operating systems, or devices associated with a game, such as "PS4".
Do not annotate release dates, store references, or promotional context around a game title, such as "due in stores everywhere on November 12".
Do not annotate unrelated capitalized expressions, scientific terminology, product names, or art/media terms that are not video game titles.
Do not annotate descriptive references to a game without the actual title, such as "it" or "the title".
Do not annotate franchise/group labels when the mention is about the series as a whole rather than a specific game entry.
boundary_rules: Mark the exact title of the video game and exclude surrounding context words such as platform names, release wording, or descriptive verbs.
Include all words, numbers, and internal function words that are part of the official game title, including short abbreviations and sequel/year markers, e.g., "KOF 2003".
Exclude preceding or following prepositions and platform/device mentions when they are not part of the title, e.g., in "God of War on PS4", annotate only "God of War".
Do not extend the span to include franchise-level mentions like "the series" unless the franchise name itself is the game title being referenced in that mention.

Preserve the surface form of the title as written in text, including capitalization variants, but do not include quotation marks or surrounding punctuation unless they are clearly part of the official title.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the game title itself, not the hardware or platform it appears on; a console name such as "PS4" is not a video game.

Distinguish a specific video game title from a broader game series or franchise mention: if the text names the playable work itself, annotate it; if it refers only to the franchise generically, do not annotate unless the franchise name is also being used as the title of a specific game in context.
Do not annotate company, studio, publisher, or developer names; only the name of the game product belongs to this type.
Do not annotate non-game media titles or technical terms that may look title-like; the entity must denote an interactive video game work.
Do not confuse abbreviations or acronyms with general terms; annotate them only when they clearly function as a video game title, as in "KOF 2003".

village.definition: A named small human settlement recognized as a village, including current or historical village names, when the mention refers to the settlement itself.
include_rules: Annotate proper names that refer to a village or small settlement.
Include village names introduced by cues such as 'the village', 'named', 'served by', or historical naming context when the referent is the settlement.
Annotate both single-word and multi-word village names.
Include historical or former village names when they denote the settlement itself.
Annotate a name even if it is ambiguous with another geographic feature, provided the sentence context makes clear the mention is the village.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic common nouns like 'village' when no specific village name is given.
Do not annotate names of larger or different place types unless the context indicates they are villages.
Do not annotate non-settlement entities such as railway stations, rivers, companies, products, diseases, industries, measurements, or technical terms.
Do not annotate adjectival, descriptive, or metonymic references that do not directly name the village.
Do not annotate larger spans that include an associated facility or feature name beyond the village name itself.
Do not annotate text in negative examples that contains no settlement name, even if capitalized terms appear.
boundary_rules: Annotate only the place name itself, excluding preceding generic descriptors such as 'village', 'hamlet', or similar settlement-type words when they are not part of the official name.
Include all tokens that belong to the official village name, including internal spaces and capitalization, as in 'Quintrell Downs'.
Do not extend the span to nearby infrastructure, administrative descriptors, or appositives, e.g., in 'Quintrell Downs railway station', annotate only 'Quintrell Downs'.
Do not include surrounding prepositions, articles, or verbs, e.g., annotate 'Morda' in 'named after the River Morda' only if it refers to the village name in context.
If the name appears as a single token, annotate that token alone, as in 'Calkinsville' or 'Morda'.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate as village only when the name denotes a settlement/locality; do not annotate the same string when it refers to a river, station, company, product, person, or other non-settlement entity.

Names that also occur inside larger place- or facility-names should be tagged only for the village span, not for the larger entity; e.g., 'Quintrell Downs' is a village, while 'Quintrell Downs railway station' is not a village span.
Do not annotate generic industry, scientific, medical, or technical terms that are capitalized or noun-like but are not settlement names.
When a name is historically mentioned as a post office name or former settlement name, annotate it if the context indicates it is the name of a village/settlement, as with 'Calkinsville'.
If context explicitly identifies the referent as another geographic feature such as a river, exclude it unless the sentence states the village shares that name and the mention refers to the village.

virus.definition: A virus entity is the name or standard abbreviation of a specific virus or viral taxon mentioned as a pathogen or viral agent.
include_rules: Annotate specific virus names written in full, such as "hepatitis C virus".
Annotate standard abbreviations or acronyms that clearly denote a virus in context, such as "IAV" and "BAV3".
Annotate virus mentions whether they appear in basic science, clinical, or experimental contexts, as long as the span refers to the virus itself.
Annotate viral names embedded in descriptions of constructs or reagents when the text explicitly contains the virus name as a recognizable standalone component.
Include both common-name virus expressions and alphanumeric virus designations when they identify a specific viral agent.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic mentions such as "virus", "viral infection", or "the pathogen" when no specific virus is named.
Do not annotate diseases, syndromes, or conditions unless the span explicitly names the virus itself.
Do not annotate proteins, genes, signaling molecules, vaccines, drugs, or treatments associated with a virus.
Do not annotate laboratory constructs, replication properties, or experimental descriptors as part of the entity when they are not themselves the virus name.
Do not annotate unrelated uppercase abbreviations or technical terms that are not viruses.
Do not annotate non-biomedical text or ordinary words that coincidentally resemble abbreviations.
boundary_rules: Select the minimal span that uniquely identifies the virus name.
Include the full virus name when expressed as a common scientific name, e.g., "hepatitis C virus".
Include uppercase or abbreviated virus designations when they refer to a virus, e.g., "IAV" and "BAV3".
Do not extend the span to surrounding words that describe experimental properties, constructs, or modifiers unless they are part of the virus name itself.
When a virus name appears inside a longer engineered construct or recombinant notation, annotate only the virus portion if it stands alone as a virus name, e.g., annotate "BAV3" within "BAV3.E3gD".
Exclude punctuation, conjunctions, parentheses, and trailing strain/construct separators unless they are integral to the virus name form in context.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate the virus itself, not a disease caused by the virus; for example, include "hepatitis C virus" because it names the pathogen, not just "hepatitis C" if that refers to the disease.
Do not annotate host organisms, patients, or animal species mentioned in infection or experiment context; only the viral agent is a virus entity.
Do not annotate treatments, signaling molecules, anesthetics, physiologic indices, or laboratory measurements even if they are discussed in relation to viral infection.

Distinguish virus names from engineered viral products or plasmid-like constructs: if the text names a modified construct containing a virus name, annotate only the embedded virus name when that is the actual virus reference.
Do not annotate generic biomedical nouns such as "virus" alone unless they are part of a specific virus name.

weapon.definition: Any term that denotes a weapon, weapon class, or explicitly weaponized means used to injure, kill, or attack, including conventional arms and named categories such as biological weapons.
include_rules: Annotate common nouns and noun phrases that directly name weapons or classes of weapons, e.g. "rifles", "firearms".
Annotate explicit weapon category phrases, including non-conventional categories, e.g. "biological weapons".
Annotate terms for weapon discharge or firing output when the term itself is conventionally used as a weapon-related lexical item, e.g. "gunfire".
Annotate both singular and plural forms of weapon names.
Annotate generic higher-level weapon class terms when they clearly denote arms rather than metaphorical or unrelated senses.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate text with no weapon meaning, even if it appears in long documents or formal titles.
Do not annotate laws, acts, schedules, administrative terms, housing terms, or other civic/government language.
Do not annotate body parts, injuries, victims, or attack events unless the specific term itself denotes a weapon or weapon discharge.
Do not annotate generic tools, objects, or substances unless they are explicitly presented as weapons.
Do not annotate broader scientific or biological terms by themselves when only the weaponized expression should be marked, e.g. exclude "zoonotic agents" if not part of "biological weapons".
Do not annotate pronouns, references, or implicit mentions of weapons when no actual weapon term is present.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that names the weapon or weapon class, including modifiers that are part of the weapon term, e.g. "biological weapons".
For plural or singular common nouns denoting weapons, include the full noun form, e.g. "rifles", "firearms".
Include the complete lexicalized attack/munition term when it directly denotes harmful weapon discharge or fire, e.g. "gunfire".
Do not extend the span to surrounding verbs, determiners, or explanatory context, e.g. annotate "rifles" not "used on rifles", and "gunfire" not "struck by gunfire".
When a modifier only describes circumstance, ownership, quantity, or evaluation rather than weapon type, exclude it from the span.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate physical weapons, weapon classes, or direct weaponized means of harm; do not annotate general violence or injury language unless the term itself denotes weapon discharge, as in "gunfire".
Distinguish weapon from disease, agent, or material: annotate the phrase only when the wording explicitly frames it as a weapon, e.g. "biological weapons" rather than standalone "zoonotic agents".
Do not annotate people, organizations, places, laws, or works even if the surrounding text discusses conflict or public safety.
Do not annotate non-weapon objects or infrastructure unless the text explicitly identifies them as weapons.
Treat broad category nouns as weapon when they denote classes of arms, e.g. "firearms"; do not require a specific model or brand.

weather.definition: Atmospheric conditions, climate patterns, temperature-related conditions, or specific meteorological phenomena mentioned as weather states or events.
include_rules: Annotate words or phrases that denote general atmospheric conditions, such as 'climate'.
Annotate temperature-related or condition-describing expressions that characterize the weather, such as 'colder days'.
Annotate named or specific meteorological events and phenomena, such as 'microburst'.
Annotate both broad long-term weather patterns and short-term weather events when they are explicitly mentioned.
Annotate literal references to weather conditions rather than implied or inferred weather.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate numbers, dates, durations, or generic time expressions unless they explicitly form part of a weather expression.
Do not annotate locations, regions, landforms, or environments that are merely associated with weather discussion.
Do not annotate vegetation, productivity, economics, policy, health, mathematics, or other non-meteorological concepts, even if they co-occur in the sentence.
Do not annotate generic descriptive words unless they denote an atmospheric condition in context.
Do not annotate causes, effects, or actions related to weather when the weather phenomenon itself is not named.
boundary_rules: Annotate the minimal contiguous span that directly names the weather concept or condition.
Include adjectives or modifiers when they are part of the weather expression, as in 'colder days'.
Include the head noun for atmospheric or seasonal conditions, such as 'climate'.
For specific meteorological events, annotate the event term itself, e.g. 'microburst'; exclude surrounding reporting words such as 'some sort of' or 'moved through the area'.
Do not extend the span to include unrelated location, time, or causal context unless those words are part of the weather expression itself.
If a generic time word becomes weather-descriptive through a modifier, include the full phrase, e.g. 'colder days' rather than only 'days'.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate weather only when the phrase denotes atmospheric conditions, temperature patterns, or meteorological phenomena; do not annotate entities that are merely affected by weather.
Distinguish weather from geographic or environmental entities: places such as river valleys or Barcelona are not weather, even if climate is discussed in relation to them.
Distinguish weather from seasons or calendar time unless the expression explicitly describes a weather condition; plain temporal mentions like 'days' are not weather, but 'colder days' is.
Distinguish weather from natural disasters or physical processes by scope: annotate the meteorological phenomenon itself when it is inherently a weather event, as with 'microburst'.
Do not annotate abstract non-weather uses of similar words; only literal atmospheric meaning counts.

website.definition: A named website, web platform, online forum, hosted channel, or named site-like online destination that users visit or interact with on the internet.
include_rules: Annotate proper names of websites and online platforms, including forums and Q&A sites, such as 'stackoverflow'.
Annotate named channels or site sections when the mention denotes a distinct online destination on a platform, such as 'YouTube channel'.
Annotate website names that appear without a domain suffix when they are clearly used as site names in context.
Annotate names of online communities or sites introduced by context words like 'site', 'sites like', 'on', 'at', or 'channel' when the name itself identifies the web destination, such as 'Gaming'.
Annotate both standalone platform names and hosted destination names when the reference is to an internet-accessible site or platform presence.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic words for online resources when they are not part of a specific name, such as 'sites', 'website', 'channel', 'forum', or 'page' by themselves.
Do not annotate domain-independent content categories, topics, or activities that are not being used as the name of an online destination.
Do not annotate organizations, institutions, products, journals, studies, or protocols unless the text explicitly refers to their website or online platform as the entity.
Do not annotate technical, scientific, or medical terminology such as diseases, devices, methods, wavelengths, or findings.
Do not annotate full descriptive phrases that merely describe a website without naming it.
Do not annotate mentions in text that contain no identifiable website name, even if the document is web-related.
boundary_rules: Annotate the smallest contiguous span that uniquely names the website or web platform mention in context.

Include modifiers that are part of the named website expression when they specify the site unit, such as 'YouTube channel' rather than 'YouTube' alone when the text explicitly names the channel as the entity mention.
Include the full site name even when it is a single word with no domain suffix, such as 'stackoverflow' or 'Gaming'.
Do not include surrounding generic nouns or verbs that are not part of the site name, such as 'sites like' before 'Gaming'.
Do not extend the span to include URLs prefixes, trailing punctuation, possessives outside the name, or descriptive context unless those tokens are part of the official website expression.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate website names and named sections/channels hosted on web platforms when they function as web destinations; do not switch to another type just because the name resembles a product, community, or media brand.
A platform-specific subsite or channel can still be website if the mention denotes an online destination, as in 'YouTube channel'.
Do not annotate general technical terms, medical terms, instruments, protocols, or research topics that are not online services or sites, even if they appear capitalized or noun-like.
If a word could denote either a general subject area or a website, annotate it only when the sentence context makes clear it refers to an online site/platform, as with 'sites like Gaming'.
Do not annotate organizations, institutions, or companies unless the mention refers specifically to their website or web platform presence rather than the real-world entity itself.

work of art.definition: A named creative or cultural work, production, or award title, including titles of artistic works such as albums, musicals, films, songs, and other named cultural productions or prizes.
include_rules: Annotate specific titles of creative works, including albums, musicals, films, songs, series, or similar artistic productions.
Annotate named cultural award titles when they refer to a specific prize or award, as in Juno.
Annotate titles introduced by context words such as work, album, musical, or awards if the following phrase is a specific proper title, as in work Maanagaram and musical Barbary Coast.
Annotate each distinct title mentioned in a coordinated list separately, as in Call My Name and Once In A Lifetime.
Annotate titles even when they appear without quotation marks or italics, provided the context clearly indicates a named work or award.
exclude_rules: Do not annotate generic common nouns for artistic media or products, such as work, album, musical, invention, study, or prior art, when they are not part of a specific title.

Do not annotate technical document headings, patent references, article headings, or scientific paper titles in non-creative contexts, such as Field of The Invention or Effects of commonly consumed beverages..., because they are not treated here as creative works.
Do not annotate names of ordinary consumer products, substances, or materials such as cola, coffee, tea, resin composites, or spray gun components.
Do not annotate award categories or descriptive honor phrases by themselves, such as Best Editor, when they describe a category rather than a named award title.
Do not annotate partial titles when the full title is present in the text.
Do not annotate years, dates, or contextual event descriptions surrounding a title.
boundary_rules: Annotate the exact title of the work or named award only, without surrounding trigger words such as work, album, musical, film, song, series, award, or production when those words are not part of the official name.
Include all contiguous words that belong to the title, including function words and internal capitalization, as in Call My Name, Once In A Lifetime, and Barbary Coast.
If a title appears after a possessive or descriptive phrase, start the span at the first word of the title, not at the descriptor; e.g., annotate Maanagaram in his work Maanagaram.
Do not include preceding determiners, punctuation, list numbers, section headers, or explanatory text unless they are part of the official title.

For named awards, include the award name itself as it appears in context when it refers to a specific cultural prize or award title; exclude accompanying category descriptions such as for Best Editor unless they are part of the formal award name.
When multiple works are listed in coordination, annotate each title separately rather than as one combined span.
confusable_type_rules: Annotate creative works and named cultural awards as work of art when they refer to identifiable artistic or cultural products or titles, such as albums, musicals, films, songs, or award names.

Do not annotate generic references to inventions, studies, prior art, products, materials, or technical objects; these are not creative works even if they appear in title case or in document headings.
Do not annotate names of beverages, materials, or other common products such as cola, coffee, tea, or Coke unless they are clearly being used as the official title of a creative work.
Do not annotate category labels, descriptive roles, or honors surrounding an award name, such as Best Editor, unless the full phrase is itself the official title of the award.

Distinguish work of art from people, organizations, places, and dates by checking whether the expression names a created cultural artifact or named artistic production rather than a creator, institution, location, or time expression.
